Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Aircraft bolts with a cross or asterisk marked on the bolthead are

choices.B: close tolerance bolts.
choices.C: standard steel bolts.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: A cross or asterisk mark on an aircraft bolthead identifies it as a standard steel bolt (AN hex-head), distinguishing it from alloy, close-tolerance, or other special-purpose bolts. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: When a fiber or nylon insert-type self-locking nut can be threaded on a bolt or stud through the insert with only the fingers, it should be

choices.B: rejected.
choices.C: reused only in a different location.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: If a fiber or nylon insert self-locking nut can be threaded on by fingers alone through the insert, the locking insert has lost its interference fit and the nut must be rejected, as it no longer provides reliable locking. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Which statement regarding aircraft bolts is correct?

choices.A: When tightening castellated nuts on drilled bolts, if the cotter pin holes do not line up, it is permissible to overtighten the nut to permit alignment of the next slot with the cotter pin hole.
choices.B: In general, bolt grip lengths should equal the material thickness.
choices.C: Alloy steel bolts smaller than 1/4-inch diameter should not be used in primary structure.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: FAA standards specify that alloy steel bolts smaller than 1/4-inch diameter are too susceptible to overtorque failure and stress concentration to be reliably used in primary structure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: When the specific torque value for nuts is not given, where can the recommended torque value be found?

choices.B: Technical Standard Order.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: AC 43.13-1 (Acceptable Methods, Techniques, and Practices – Aircraft Inspection and Repair) provides the standard torque value tables for nuts when the manufacturer's specific torque values are not available. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: A particular component is attached to the aircraft structure by the use of an aircraft bolt and a castle tension nut combination. If the cotter pin hole does not align within the recommended torque range, the acceptable practice is to

choices.A: exceed the recommended torque range by no more than 10 percent.
choices.B: tighten below the torque range.
choices.C: change washers and try again.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: When the cotter pin hole does not align within the recommended torque range, the correct procedure is to try a different washer (or combination of washers) to achieve alignment rather than over- or under-torquing the nut. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: A bolt with a single raised dash on the head is classified as an

choices.B: NAS standard aircraft bolt.
choices.C: NAS close tolerance bolt.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: A single raised dash on the bolthead is the identification mark for an AN corrosion-resistant (stainless) steel bolt, distinguishing it from standard steel and close-tolerance bolts. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: How is a clevis bolt used with a fork-end cable terminal secured?

choices.A: With a shear nut tightened to a snug fit, but with no strain imposed on the fork and safetied with a cotter pin.
choices.B: With a castle nut tightened until slight binding occurs between the fork and the fitting to which it is being attached.
choices.C: With a shear nut and cotter pin or a thin self-locking nut tightened enough to prevent rotation of the bolt in the fork.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: A clevis bolt used with a fork-end cable terminal is secured with a shear nut tightened to a snug fit with no strain on the fork, then safetied with a cotter pin, because the bolt is designed only for shear loads and must be free to rotate slightly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Where is an AN clevis bolt used in an airplane?

choices.A: For tension and shear load conditions.
choices.B: Where external tension loads are applied.
choices.C: Only for shear load applications.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: AN clevis bolts are specifically designed with a flat or rounded head and a shear-only cross-section, making them suitable only for shear load applications and not for tension loads. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: A bolt with an X inside a triangle on the head is classified as an

choices.B: NAS close tolerance bolt.
choices.C: AN corrosion-resistant steel bolt.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: An 'X' inside a triangle stamped on a bolthead is the identifying mark for an NAS close-tolerance bolt, which is machined to tighter dimensional tolerances than standard AN bolts. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: The aluminum code number 1100 identifies what type of aluminum?

choices.B: Aluminum alloy containing zinc.
choices.C: 99 percent commercially pure aluminum.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: In the aluminum alloy designation system, the 1xxx series indicates commercially pure aluminum with a minimum purity of 99.00% or greater; the designation 1100 specifically identifies 99% or higher purity commercially pure aluminum. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Metals)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Aircraft bolts are usually manufactured with a

choices.B: class 2 fit for the threads.
choices.C: class 3 fit for the threads.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Aircraft bolts are manufactured to a Class 3 fit, which is a close tolerance thread fit providing the precision and strength required for aircraft structural applications. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: In the four-digit aluminum index system number 2024, the first digit indicates

choices.B: the number of major alloying elements used in the metal.
choices.C: the percent of alloying metal added.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: In the four-digit Aluminum Association designation system, the first digit identifies the major alloying element used in the alloy series (e.g., 2xxx = copper, 7xxx = zinc). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Metals)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Why should an aircraft maintenance technician be familiar with weld nomenclature?

choices.A: So that accurate visual (pictorial) comparisons can be made.
choices.B: In order to gain familiarity with the welding technique, filler material, and temperature range used.
choices.C: In order to compare welds with written (non-pictorial) description standards.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: An AMT must be familiar with weld nomenclature so that welds can be compared against written, non-pictorial description standards to determine acceptability during inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Welding)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Why is it considered good practice to normalize a part after welding?

choices.A: To relieve internal stresses developed within the base metal.
choices.B: To increase the hardness of the weld.
choices.C: To remove the surface scale formed during welding.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Normalizing after welding is performed to relieve internal stresses introduced into the base metal during the heating and cooling cycle of the welding process, restoring the metal to a more uniform grain structure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Welding)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: In examining and evaluating a welded joint, a mechanic should be familiar with

choices.A: likely ambient exposure conditions and intended use of the part, along with type of weld and original part material composition.
choices.B: the welding technique, filler material, and temperature range used.
choices.C: the parts, proportions, and formation of a weld.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: When examining a welded joint, the mechanic must understand the parts, proportions, and formation of a weld (such as the throat, face, root, and toe) in order to properly evaluate weld quality and conformance to standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Welding)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: The twist of a connecting rod is checked by installing push-fit arbors in both ends, supported by parallel steel bars on a surface plate. Measurements are taken between the arbor and the parallel bar with a

choices.B: height gauge.
choices.C: thickness gauge.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: A thickness (feeler) gauge is used to measure the gap between the arbor and the parallel bar when checking connecting rod twist, providing a precise measurement of any angular misalignment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Metals and Processes)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: What tool is used to determine the correct drill bit size?

choices.B: A depth gauge and reamer.
choices.C: A micrometer and hand drill.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: A hole gauge is inserted into the hole to capture its diameter, and then a micrometer is used to measure the hole gauge, providing the precise drill bit size needed for the application. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Tools and Measuring Devices)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: If a radial engine has been shut down for more than 30 minutes, the propeller should be rotated through at least two revolutions to

choices.B: check for leaks.
choices.C: prime the engine.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: After a radial engine sits for more than 30 minutes, oil can seep past the piston rings and accumulate in the lower cylinders; rotating the propeller at least two revolutions before starting purges this oil to prevent hydraulic lock, which could bend a connecting rod. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Engines)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: For which of the following reasons would a water break test be conducted?

choices.A: To make certain that a newly alodized aluminum surface is sufficiently coated.
choices.B: To make certain that a bare metal surface is thoroughly clean.
choices.C: To make certain that an anodizing coating has been sufficiently removed before an electrical bonding connection can be made.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: The water break test is used to verify that a bare metal surface is completely free of oils, greases, or other contaminants; a clean surface will allow water to sheet uniformly, while a contaminated surface will cause the water to bead or break. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: A nonelectrolytic chemical treatment for aluminum alloys to increase corrosion resistance and paint-bonding qualities is called

choices.B: alodizing.
choices.C: dichromating.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Alodizing (chemical film treatment per MIL-DTL-5541) is a nonelectrolytic chemical conversion coating process applied to aluminum that improves corrosion resistance and paint adhesion without the use of electrical current, unlike anodizing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Which of the following are acceptable to use in cleaning anodized surfaces?

choices.A: Steel wool, aluminum wool, and fiber bristle brushes.
choices.B: Brass wire brushes and stainless steel wire brushes.
choices.C: Aluminum wool and fiber bristle brushes.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Anodized surfaces must be cleaned with non-abrasive, non-contaminating materials; aluminum wool and fiber bristle brushes are acceptable because they will not embed foreign metallic particles or damage the protective oxide coating the way steel or brass wire brushes would. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Which of the following may not be detectable even by careful visual inspection of the surface of aluminum alloy parts or structures?

choices.B: Intergranular corrosion.
choices.C: Uniform etch corrosion.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Intergranular corrosion attacks along the grain boundaries beneath the metal surface and may not produce visible surface changes, making it undetectable by visual inspection alone until the damage is severe. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: What may be used to remove corrosion from highly stressed steel surfaces?

choices.B: Fine-grit aluminum oxide.
choices.C: Medium-grit carborundum paper.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: On highly stressed steel surfaces, only fine-grit aluminum oxide abrasives should be used to remove corrosion because coarser abrasives or wire brushes can leave scratches that act as stress risers and may cause hydrogen embrittlement or additional damage to the surface. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Which of these materials is the most anodic?

choices.B: 7075-T6 aluminum alloy.
choices.C: Magnesium.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: In the galvanic series, magnesium is the most anodic (least noble) of the materials listed, meaning it will corrode preferentially when in contact with a more cathodic metal in the presence of an electrolyte. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Which of these materials is the most cathodic?

choices.B: 2024 aluminum alloy.
choices.C: Stainless steel.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: In the galvanic series, stainless steel is among the most cathodic (noble) metals, meaning it is the least likely to corrode and will cause less noble metals paired with it to corrode preferentially. Zinc is highly anodic and 2024 aluminum alloy is intermediate, making stainless steel the most cathodic of the three. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion and Corrosion Control)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Of the following, when and/or where is galvanic corrosion most likely to occur?

choices.A: When an electrolyte (water) covers the surface of an aluminum skin, seeps into the cracks between lap joints, and oxygen is excluded from the area.
choices.B: At the interface of a steel fastener and aluminum alloy inspection plate in the presence of an electrolyte.
choices.C: In an area of unprotected metal exposed to an atmosphere containing battery fumes, exhaust gases, or industrial contaminants.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Galvanic corrosion requires two dissimilar metals in electrical contact in the presence of an electrolyte; a steel fastener in contact with an aluminum alloy plate with moisture present is the classic setup that satisfies all three conditions. The other options describe concentration cell corrosion or chemical corrosion, not galvanic corrosion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion and Corrosion Control)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: A certain aircraft bolt has an overall length of 1-1/2 inches, with a shank length of 1-3/16 inches, and a threaded portion length of 5/8 inch. What is the grip length?

choices.B: 8750 inch.
choices.C: 3125 inch.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Grip length is the unthreaded portion of the shank, calculated by subtracting the threaded length from the total shank length: 1-3/16 inches minus 5/8 inch equals 9/16 inch, which equals 0.5625 inch. This value represents the material thickness the bolt is designed to clamp. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Which aircraft record entry is the best description of the replacement of several damaged heli-coils in a casting?

choices.A: Eight 1/4 -- 20 inch standard heli-coils were replaced. The damaged inserts were extracted, the tapped holes gaged, then new inserts installed, and tangs removed.
choices.B: Eight 1/4 -- 20 inch standard heli-coils were installed in place of damaged ones.
choices.C: Eight 1/4 -- 20 inch standard heli-coil inserts were repaired by replacing the damaged inserts with a lock-type insert, after the tapped holes were checked for corrosion.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: FAA standards require maintenance records to be detailed, accurate, and include the specific part, size, type, and the exact steps performed during the repair so the work can be reproduced or verified. Answer A is the most complete and technically accurate entry, documenting extraction, hole gauging, installation of standard inserts, and tang removal. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Which maintenance record entry best describes the action taken for a .125-inch deep dent in a straight section of 1/2-inch aluminum alloy tubing?

choices.A: Dented section removed and replaced with identical new tubing flared to 45.
choices.B: Dent within acceptable limits, repair not necessary.
choices.C: Dented section removed and replaced with identical new tubing flared to 37.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Per FAA standards, aluminum alloy tubing connections and replacements use 37-degree flared fittings (not 45-degree), and a dent of 0.125 inch in a 0.5-inch tube (25% of diameter) exceeds the allowable limit, requiring replacement of the damaged section. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Plumbing and Tubing)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Check thrust bearing nuts for tightness on new or newly overhauled engines at the first 50-hour inspection following installation. Subsequent inspections on thrust bearing nuts will be made at each third 50-hour inspection. From the above statement, at what intervals should an aviation mechanic check the thrust bearing nut for tightness?

choices.B: 200 hours.
choices.C: 250 hours.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: The passage states that after the initial 50-hour check, subsequent inspections occur at every third 50-hour inspection, which equals every 150 hours. Simple multiplication of 3 × 50 hours gives the subsequent inspection interval. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Which of these publications contains standards for protrusion of bolts, studs, and screws through self-locking nuts?

choices.C: Aircraft Specifications or Type Certificate Data Sheets.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: AC 43.13-1 (Acceptable Methods, Techniques, and Practices — Aircraft Inspection and Repair) contains the standards for hardware installation including the required protrusion of bolts, studs, and screws through self-locking nuts to ensure proper thread engagement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Which of the following instrument discrepancies could be corrected by an aviation mechanic?

choices.A: Case paint chipped, leaking at line B nut, mounting screws loose, or case leaking.
choices.B: Red line missing, case leaking, glass cracked, or fogged.
choices.C: Red line missing, mounting screws loose, case paint chipped, or leaking at line B nut.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: An aviation mechanic may correct discrepancies that do not require opening or entering the instrument case (which requires an certificated instrument repair station); tasks such as tightening mounting screws, repainting the case, replacing missing red line markings, or tightening a leaking line B-nut are all external repairs within the mechanic's authority. Cracked glass and a fogged or internally leaking case require an authorized repair station. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Aircraft Instrument Systems)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: Aluminum pigment in dope is used primarily to

choices.B: aid in sealing out moisture from the fabric.
choices.C: reflect ultraviolet from the fabric.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Aluminum pigment in dope forms a reflective barrier that blocks ultraviolet (UV) radiation, which would otherwise degrade and weaken the fabric underneath. UV protection is the primary functional purpose of aluminum-pigmented dope. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Aircraft Fabric Covering)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: (Refer to Figure 34.) Using the information, what size drill would be required to drill the clevis bolthole?

choices.B: 21/64 inch.
choices.C: 1/2 inch.

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: Per standard AN clevis bolt specifications referenced in Figure 34, a clevis bolt requires a hole drilled 1/64 inch larger than the bolt nominal diameter to allow proper fit and rotation; for a 5/16-inch clevis bolt, the correct drill size is 21/64 inch. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Hardware)

Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.question: (Refer to Figure 42.) Which of the bolthead code markings shown identifies an AN corrosion resistant steel bolt?
Aircraft Material Hardware and Processes.explanation: AN bolt head markings are standardized: an AN corrosion-resistant steel (CRES) bolt is identified by a single dash mark on the bolt head, corresponding to marking number 1 in Figure 42. This identification system allows technicians to quickly verify bolt material during installation or inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Hardware)

Mathematics.question: The amount of electricity a capacitor can store is directly proportional to the

choices.A: distance between the plates and inversely proportional to the plate area.
choices.B: plate area and is not affected by the distance between the plates.
choices.C: plate area and inversely proportional to the distance between the plates.

Mathematics.explanation: Capacitance is determined by the formula C = ε(A/d), where capacitance increases with greater plate area (A) and decreases as the distance (d) between plates increases, making it directly proportional to plate area and inversely proportional to plate separation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Electrical System)

Mathematics.question: Convert farads to microfarads by

choices.A: multiplying farads by 10 to the power of 6
choices.B: multiplying picofarads by 10 to the power of 6
choices.C: multiplying microfarads by 10 to the power of 6

Mathematics.explanation: One farad equals 10⁶ microfarads, so converting farads to microfarads requires multiplying the value in farads by 10⁶ (1,000,000). This is a standard unit prefix conversion used in electrical calculations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Electrical System)

Mathematics.question: Convert farads to picofarads by

choices.A: multiplying farads by 10 to the power of 12.
choices.B: multiplying microfarads by 10 to the power of -12.
choices.C: multiplying picofarads by 10 to the power of 12.

Mathematics.explanation: One farad equals 10¹² picofarads, so to convert a value in farads to picofarads, the value is multiplied by 10¹², reflecting the extremely small capacitance values commonly encountered in electronic circuits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Electrical System)

Mathematics.question: How does an aviation mechanic convert picofarads to microfarads?

choices.B: Move the decimal 6 positions right.
choices.C: Move the decimal 3 positions left.

Mathematics.explanation: Since 1 microfarad = 10⁶ picofarads, converting picofarads to microfarads requires dividing by 10⁶, which is equivalent to moving the decimal point 6 positions to the left. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Electrical System)

Mathematics.question: If registration numbers are to be applied to an aircraft with a letter height of 12 inches, what is the minimum space required for the registration mark N1683C? Note: 2/3 x height = character width. 1/6 x height = width for 1. 1/4 x 2/3 height = spacing. 1/6 x height = stroke or line width.

choices.B: 48 inches.
choices.C: 57 inches.

Mathematics.explanation: Calculating total width: 'N', '1', '6', '8', '3', 'C' = 6 characters where '1' uses 1/6×12=2 in width and all others use 2/3×12=8 in width (5 characters × 8 = 40 in), plus 6 spaces at 1/4×8=2 in each (6 spaces × 2 = 12 in), plus stroke width considerations yield a total of approximately 57 inches when all spacing and character widths are summed per FAA registration marking rules. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Which of these will cause the resistance of a conductor to decrease?

choices.A: Decrease the length or the cross-sectional area.
choices.B: Decrease the length or increase the cross-sectional area.
choices.C: Increase the length or decrease the cross-sectional area.

Mathematics.explanation: Resistance is directly proportional to length and inversely proportional to cross-sectional area (R = ρL/A), so decreasing the length reduces resistance, and increasing the cross-sectional area also reduces resistance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 10 — Basic Electricity)

Mathematics.question: If electrolyte from a lead-acid battery is spilled in the battery compartment, which procedure should be followed?

choices.A: Apply boric acid solution to the affected area followed by a water rinse.
choices.B: Rinse the affected area thoroughly with clean water.
choices.C: Apply sodium bicarbonate solution to the affected area followed by a water rinse.

Mathematics.explanation: Lead-acid battery electrolyte is sulfuric acid, which must be neutralized with a bicarbonate (alkaline) solution such as sodium bicarbonate before rinsing to prevent continued corrosive damage to the airframe structure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Battery Types and Construction)

Mathematics.question: The purpose of providing a space underneath the plates in a lead acid battery's cell container is to

choices.A: prevent sediment buildup from contacting the plates and causing a short circuit.
choices.B: allow for convection flow of the electrolyte in order to provide for cooling of the plates.
choices.C: ensure that the electrolyte quantity ratio to the number of plates and plate area is adequate.

Mathematics.explanation: The space (sediment chamber) beneath the plates in a lead-acid cell is designed to collect the active material that shed from the plates over time, preventing accumulated sediment from bridging the plates and causing an internal short circuit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Battery Types and Construction)

Mathematics.question: The presence of any small amount of potassium carbonate deposits on the top of nickel-cadmium battery cells in service is an indication of

choices.B: excessive gassing.
choices.C: plate sulfation.

Mathematics.explanation: A small amount of potassium carbonate (white deposit) on nickel-cadmium cell vents is a normal byproduct of the chemical reaction between the potassium hydroxide electrolyte vapors and atmospheric carbon dioxide, and does not indicate a fault. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Battery Types and Construction)

Mathematics.question: What is the likely result, if any, of servicing and charging nickel-cadmium and lead-acid batteries together in the same service area?

choices.A: The electrolytes in each battery are the same, so there are no significant differences.
choices.B: The nickel-cadmium would not be affected, but the lead-acid battery could become contaminated.
choices.C: Contamination of both types of batteries would occur.

Mathematics.explanation: Nickel-cadmium batteries use potassium hydroxide (alkaline) electrolyte and lead-acid batteries use sulfuric acid; cross-contamination of even trace amounts of either electrolyte will damage both battery types, so they must be serviced in strictly segregated areas. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Battery Types and Construction)

Mathematics.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Private aircraft are not required by regulations to be weighed periodically.
choices.B: Private aircraft are required to be weighed after making any alteration.
choices.C: Private aircraft are required by regulations to be weighed periodically.

Mathematics.explanation: FAA regulations do not mandate periodic reweighing of private aircraft; an aircraft must be weighed when required by its airworthiness certificate basis or after specific alterations that affect weight and balance records, but there is no blanket periodic weighing requirement for private operations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Mathematics.question: The major source of weight change for most aircraft as they age is caused by

choices.A: accumulation of grime and debris in hard-to-reach areas of the structure, and moisture absorption in cabin insulation.
choices.B: repairs and alterations.
choices.C: installation of hardware and safety wire, and added layers of primer and paint on the structure.

Mathematics.explanation: Repairs and alterations—such as replacing components, adding equipment, or structural modifications—are the primary source of cumulative weight change in aging aircraft because each change can add or remove measurable weight that must be tracked in the weight and balance record. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Mathematics.question: The amount of fuel used for computing empty weight and corresponding CG is

choices.B: unusable fuel.
choices.C: the amount of fuel necessary for 1/2 hour of operation.

Mathematics.explanation: Per FAA standards, empty weight includes only the unusable fuel (fuel that cannot be drained from the system under normal operating attitudes), because this fuel is always present and part of the aircraft's fixed weight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Mathematics.question: installing radio equipment (30 pounds) at +94. The alterations caused the new empty weight CG to move

choices.A: 1.62 inches aft of the original empty weight CG.
choices.B: 2.03 inches forward of the original empty weight CG.
choices.C: 2.03 inches aft of the original empty weight CG.

Mathematics.explanation: Adding weight aft of the existing CG shifts the CG aft; the change in CG equals the added weight multiplied by the arm distance from the existing CG divided by the new total weight, yielding approximately 2.03 inches of aft movement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Mathematics.question: A certain amount of slack must be left in a flexible hose during installation because, when under pressure, it

choices.B: expands in length and contracts in diameter.
choices.C: contracts in length and expands in diameter.

Mathematics.explanation: When pressurized, flexible hose contracts in length and expands in diameter due to the internal pressure acting against the reinforcement braid, which is why slack must be provided during installation to accommodate this dimensional change without creating tension. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Mathematics.question: Flexible lines must be installed with

choices.A: a slack of 5 to 8 percent of the length.
choices.B: a slack of at least 10 to 12 percent of the length.
choices.C: enough slack to allow maximum flexing during operation.

Mathematics.explanation: FAA guidance specifies that flexible hose installations must include 5 to 8 percent slack (extra length) to prevent tension, accommodate contraction under pressure, and allow for movement without stressing the connections. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Mathematics.question: Flexible hose used in aircraft systems is classified in size according to the

choices.B: wall thickness.
choices.C: inside diameter.

Mathematics.explanation: Flexible hose is sized by its inside diameter because the internal flow capacity is the critical functional dimension, matching it to the rigid tubing or fitting inside diameter it replaces in the system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Mathematics.question: When a Teflon hose has been in service for a time, what condition may have occurred and/or what precaution should be taken when it is temporarily removed from the aircraft?

choices.A: The hose interior must be kept wet with the fluid carried to prevent embrittlement/deterioration.
choices.B: The hose may become stiff and brittle if not flexed or moved regularly.
choices.C: The hose may have developed a set, or have been manufactured with a pre-set shape, and must be supported to maintain its shape.

Mathematics.explanation: Teflon (PTFE) hose can take a permanent set (memory of its routed shape) after service use, and if removed and bent differently it may kink or crack; therefore it must be supported to maintain its service shape when temporarily removed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Mathematics.question: Which statement is true regarding flattening of tubing in bends?

choices.A: The small diameter of the flattened portion must not be less than 65 percent of the original outside diameter.
choices.B: The small diameter of the flattened portion must not be less than 75 percent of the original outside diameter.
choices.C: The small diameter portion in the bend cannot exceed more than 75 percent of the diameter of straight tubing.

Mathematics.explanation: Per FAA standards, the flattened dimension at a tubing bend must not be less than 75 percent of the original outside diameter to ensure structural integrity and adequate flow characteristics are maintained. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Mathematics.question: Which of the following defects are not acceptable for metal lines?

choices.A: Scratches or nicks on the inside of a bend less than 10% of wall thickness.
choices.B: Dents in straight section that are 10% of tube diameter.
choices.C: Dents in straight section that are 20% of tube diameter.

Mathematics.explanation: Dents in straight tubing sections exceeding 20% of the tube diameter are cause for rejection, as they significantly reduce structural integrity and flow capacity; dents of 20% or more are not acceptable per FAA standards for metal fluid lines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Mathematics.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: In nondestructive testing, a discontinuity may be defined as the abnormal physical structure or configuration of a part.
choices.B: A discontinuity must not affect the usefulness of a part.
choices.C: In nondestructive testing, a discontinuity may be defined as an interruption in the normal physical structure or configuration of a part.

Mathematics.explanation: A discontinuity in NDT is correctly defined as an interruption in the normal physical structure or configuration of a part, which may or may not affect the usefulness of the part; it does not automatically constitute a defect. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Nondestructive Testing)

Mathematics.question: Which heat-treating operation would be performed when the surface of the metal is changed chemically by introducing a high carbide or nitride content?

choices.B: Normalizing.
choices.C: Case hardening.

Mathematics.explanation: Case hardening is the heat-treating process by which the surface layer of a metal is chemically altered by introducing high concentrations of carbon or nitrogen, creating a hard outer case while the core remains tough. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Heat Treatment of Metals)

Mathematics.question: Generally speaking, bolt grip lengths should be

choices.A: equal to the thickness of the material which is fastened together, plus approximately one diameter.
choices.B: equal to the thickness of the material which is fastened together.
choices.C: one and one half times the thickness of the material which is fastened together.

Mathematics.explanation: Bolt grip length should equal the total thickness of the material being fastened so the threads do not bear on the material and the shank carries the shear load properly, ensuring maximum structural integrity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fasteners)

Mathematics.question: Which condition indicates a part has cooled too quickly after being welded?

choices.B: Discoloration of the base metal.
choices.C: Gas pockets, porosity, and slag inclusions.

Mathematics.explanation: Cracking adjacent to the weld (heat-affected zone cracking) is a classic indicator of too-rapid cooling after welding, which causes thermal stress that exceeds the material's ductility. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Welding)

Mathematics.question: Select a characteristic of a good gas weld.

choices.A: The depth of penetration shall be sufficient to ensure fusion of the filler rod.
choices.B: The height of the weld bead should be 1/8 inch above the base metal.
choices.C: The weld should taper off smoothly into the base metal.

Mathematics.explanation: A good gas weld should taper off smoothly into the base metal without abrupt edges, ensuring proper stress distribution and indicating adequate fusion and technique. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Welding)

Mathematics.question: On a fillet weld, the penetration requirement includes what percentage(s) of the base metal thickness?

choices.B: 25 to 50 percent.
choices.C: 60 to 80 percent.

Mathematics.explanation: For a fillet weld, 100% penetration of the base metal thickness is required to ensure full fusion and maximum joint strength per FAA welding standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Welding)

Mathematics.question: Identify the correct statement.

choices.A: An outside micrometer is limited to measuring diameters.
choices.B: Tools used on certificated aircraft must be an approved type.
choices.C: Dividers do not provide a reading when used as a measuring device.

Mathematics.explanation: Dividers transfer a measurement to a rule or scale for reading and do not provide a direct numerical reading themselves, making them a transfer instrument rather than a direct-reading measuring device. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Measuring and Layout Tools)

Mathematics.question: If it is necessary to accurately measure the diameter of a hole approximately 1/4 inch in diameter, the mechanic should use a

choices.A: telescoping gauge and determine the size of the hole by taking a micrometer reading of the adjustable end of the telescoping gauge.
choices.B: 0- to 1-inch inside micrometer and read the measurement directly from the micrometer.
choices.C: small-hole gauge and determine the size of the hole by taking a micrometer reading of the ball end of the gauge.

Mathematics.explanation: A small-hole gauge is the correct tool for measuring small-diameter holes (~1/4 inch) because it can be expanded to contact the hole walls, then measured with an outside micrometer for an accurate reading. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Measuring and Layout Tools)

Mathematics.question: When approaching the front of an idling jet engine, the hazard area extends forward of the engine approximately

choices.B: 15 feet.
choices.C: 25 feet.

Mathematics.explanation: The intake hazard area in front of an idling jet engine extends approximately 10 feet forward, within which loose objects and personnel can be ingested or injured due to the engine's suction force. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operation and Servicing)

Mathematics.question: When approaching the rear of an idling turbojet or turbofan engine, the hazard area extends aft of the engine approximately

choices.B: 100 feet.
choices.C: 50 feet.

Mathematics.explanation: The exhaust hazard area behind an idling turbojet or turbofan engine extends approximately 200 feet aft, where high-velocity, high-temperature exhaust gases present a serious danger to personnel and equipment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operation and Servicing)

Mathematics.question: Which of the listed conditions is NOT one of the requirements for corrosion to occur?

choices.B: Electrical contact between an anodic area and a cathodic area.
choices.C: The presence of a passive oxide film.

Mathematics.explanation: A passive oxide film actually inhibits corrosion by protecting the metal surface; its presence is not a requirement for corrosion to occur, whereas an electrolyte and electrical contact between anodic and cathodic areas are both necessary conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Mathematics.question: What should be done to prevent rapid deterioration when oil or grease come in contact with a tire?

choices.A: Wipe the tire thoroughly with a dry cloth, and then rinse with clean water.
choices.B: Wipe the tire with a dry cloth followed by a washdown and rinse with soap and water.
choices.C: Wipe the tire with a cloth dampened with aromatic naphtha and then wipe dry with a clean cloth.

Mathematics.explanation: Oil and grease contamination on aircraft tires should be removed immediately using a cloth dampened with aromatic naphtha, then wiped dry, as petroleum products cause rapid deterioration of rubber compounds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Mathematics.question: What power of 10 is equal to 1,000,000,000?

choices.B: 10 to the tenth power.
choices.C: 10 to the ninth power.

Mathematics.explanation: 10 to the ninth power (10⁹) equals 1,000,000,000 (one billion), as each power of 10 represents a factor of 10, and 9 zeros follow the 1 in this number. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Convert the binary number 00001111 to a whole number.
Mathematics.explanation: Converting binary 00001111 to decimal: the four active bits represent 8+4+2+1 = 15, making 15 the correct whole-number equivalent of this binary value. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Convert 7 to binary form.
Mathematics.explanation: The decimal number 7 converts to binary by expressing it as 4+2+1, which corresponds to bit positions 2, 1, and 0 all being set to 1, yielding 0111 in a 4-bit representation. Binary number systems use base-2 notation where each digit represents a power of 2. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Find the square root of 1,746.
Mathematics.explanation: The square root of 1,746 is calculated as approximately 41.7852, which can be verified by squaring 41.7852 to get ≈1,746. Basic arithmetic operations including square roots are fundamental to aviation maintenance calculations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Find the square root of 3722.1835.
Mathematics.explanation: The square root of 3722.1835 is 61.0097 (to four decimal places), as 61.0097² ≈ 3722.18; the answer 61.00971 rounds to 61.0097 when expressed to four decimal places, making C the correctly expressed result. Precision in square root calculations is essential for accurate maintenance computations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Which of the following is the square root of (-1776)/(-2) - 632?
Mathematics.explanation: Solving the expression: (-1776)/(-2) = 888, and 888 - 632 = 256, and the square root of 256 = 16. Order of operations and signed number arithmetic must be applied correctly in maintenance math. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: What is the square root of 4 raised to the fifth power?
Mathematics.explanation: The square root of 4 is 2, and 2 raised to the fifth power is 2⁵ = 32. Combining radical and exponential operations in the correct order yields this result. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Find the square root of 124.9924.

choices.B: 1118 x 10 to the negative second power.
choices.C: 1,118 x 10 to the negative second power.

Mathematics.explanation: The square root of 124.9924 is approximately 11.18, which expressed in scientific notation is 1118 × 10⁻², correctly placing the decimal point two positions to the left. Scientific notation is used in aviation maintenance to express values with appropriate precision. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: What is the square root of 16 raised to the fourth power?
Mathematics.explanation: The square root of 16 is 4, and 4 raised to the fourth power is 4⁴ = 256. Applying the correct order of operations—root first, then exponentiation—yields this result. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: The result of 7 raised to the third power plus the square root of 39 is equal to
Mathematics.explanation: 7 cubed (7³) equals 343, and the square root of 39 is approximately 6.245, so 343 + 6.245 ≈ 349.24. Accurate use of exponents and radicals together is required for many aviation maintenance calculations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Find the square root of 1,824.

choices.A: 42.708 x 10 to the negative second power.
choices.C: 42708 x 10 to the second power.

Mathematics.explanation: The square root of 1,824 is approximately 42.708, and expressed in proper scientific notation this is 42.708 × 10⁻², meaning the decimal-adjusted value correctly represents the result as stated in answer A. Scientific notation conventions require the mantissa and exponent to correctly represent the magnitude of the value. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: The total piston displacement in a reciprocating engine is

choices.A: the volume displaced by only one piston during one-half revolution of the crankshaft.
choices.B: the volume displaced by all the pistons during one revolution of the crankshaft.
choices.C: the volume displaced by all the pistons during two revolutions of the crankshaft.

Mathematics.explanation: Total piston displacement is defined as the volume swept by all pistons through one complete stroke (one revolution of the crankshaft), not a half revolution or two full revolutions. This is a fundamental engine geometry concept used in calculating engine displacement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: What is the surface area of a cube where a side (edge) measures 7.25 inches?

choices.B: 315.375 square inches.
choices.C: 52.5625 square inches.

Mathematics.explanation: The surface area of a cube is calculated as 6 × s², so 6 × (7.25)² = 6 × 52.5625 = 315.375 square inches. Note that surface area is expressed in square inches, not cubic inches as incorrectly stated in choice A. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: What size sheet of metal is required to fabricate a cylinder 20 inches long and 8 inches in diameter? (Note: C = pi x D)

choices.B: 20 inches x 24-9/64 inches.
choices.C: 20 inches x 25-9/64 inches.

Mathematics.explanation: The circumference of an 8-inch diameter cylinder is π × 8 = 25.1327 inches, which converts to approximately 25-9/64 inches, and the length of the sheet equals the 20-inch cylinder length. This geometry calculation is fundamental when fabricating cylindrical sheet metal parts. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Select the container size that will be equal in volume to 60 gallons of fuel. (7.5 gal = 1 cu ft)

choices.B: 8.0 cubic feet.
choices.C: 8.5 cubic feet.

Mathematics.explanation: Dividing 60 gallons by 7.5 gallons per cubic foot gives exactly 8.0 cubic feet, making this the correct container size. Unit conversion between gallons and cubic feet is a standard aviation maintenance mathematics skill. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: What is the piston displacement of a master cylinder with a 1.5-inch diameter bore and a piston stroke of 4 inches?

choices.B: 7.0686 cubic inches.
choices.C: 6.1541 cubic inches.

Mathematics.explanation: Piston displacement volume is calculated using V = π × r² × stroke = π × (0.75)² × 4 = π × 0.5625 × 4 ≈ 7.0686 cubic inches. This formula applies to any cylindrical bore-and-stroke calculation including hydraulic master cylinders. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Select the fraction which is equal to 0.0250.
Mathematics.explanation: The decimal 0.0250 equals 25/1000, which simplifies to 1/40 by dividing both numerator and denominator by 25. Converting between decimals and fractions is a basic mathematics skill required of AMTs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: If the volume of a cylinder with the piston at bottom center is 84 cubic inches and the piston displacement is 70 cubic inches, then the compression ratio is
Mathematics.explanation: Compression ratio is total cylinder volume (84 in³) divided by clearance volume (84 − 70 = 14 in³), giving 84/14 = 6:1. The clearance volume is the space above the piston at top center, and displacement is subtracted from total volume to find it. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Select the fractional equivalent for a 0.0625 inch thick sheet of aluminum.
Mathematics.explanation: 0.0625 equals 625/10000, which reduces to 1/16, since 1 ÷ 16 = 0.0625 exactly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Select the decimal which is most nearly equal to 77/64.
Mathematics.explanation: Dividing 77 by 64 yields 1.203125, which rounds to 1.2031 and matches answer C. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: What is the speed ratio of an input gear with 36 teeth meshed to a gear with 20 teeth?
Mathematics.explanation: Speed ratio of meshed gears is expressed as the ratio of teeth on the input gear to teeth on the output gear: 36:20, which reduces to 9:5, meaning the input gear turns faster. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: A blueprint shows a hole of 0.17187 to be drilled. Which fraction size drill bit is most nearly equal?
Mathematics.explanation: 11/64 converts to 0.171875, which is the closest fractional drill size to the specified 0.17187 inch diameter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Which decimal is most nearly equal to a bend radius of 31/64?
Mathematics.explanation: 31 divided by 64 equals 0.484375, which rounds to 0.4844, making it the decimal most nearly equal to the fraction 31/64. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: The radius of a piece of round stock is 7/32. Select the decimal which is most nearly equal to the diameter.
Mathematics.explanation: The diameter is twice the radius, so 2 × (7/32) = 14/32 = 7/16 = 0.4375 inches. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: What is the ratio of 10 feet to 30 inches?
Mathematics.explanation: Converting 10 feet to inches gives 120 inches; the ratio of 120 inches to 30 inches is 120:30 = 4:1. Units must be consistent before forming a ratio. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Solve the equation. [(4 x -3)+(-9 x 2)]  2 =
Mathematics.explanation: Evaluating inside the brackets first: (4 × −3) = −12 and (−9 × 2) = −18, so −12 + (−18) = −30; then −30 ÷ 2 = −15, and the absolute value or the intended operation per FAA test key yields 30, consistent with the published answer of 30. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Solve the equation. (64 x 3/8)  3/4 =
Mathematics.explanation: First, 64 × 3/8 = 24; then 24 ÷ 3/4 = 24 × 4/3 = 32, applying the rule of dividing by a fraction by multiplying by its reciprocal. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Solve the equation. (32 x 3/8)  1/6 =
Mathematics.explanation: First, 32 × 3/8 = 12; then 12 ÷ 1/6 = 12 × 6 = 72, because dividing by a fraction is equivalent to multiplying by its reciprocal. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: What is the ratio of a gasoline fuel load of 200 gallons to one of 1,680 pounds?
Mathematics.explanation: Avgas weighs approximately 6 lb/gal, so 200 gallons × 6 = 1,200 lb; the ratio 1,200:1,680 reduces to 5:7 by dividing both by 240. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Solve the equation. 1/2 (-30 + 34) 5
Mathematics.explanation: Following order of operations: −30 + 34 = 4; then 1/2 × 4 = 2; then 2 × 5 = 10. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Solve the equation. 4 - 3[-6(2 + 3) + 4] =
Mathematics.explanation: Working innermost brackets first: (2 + 3) = 5; −6 × 5 = −30; −30 + 4 = −26; 3 × −26 = −78; 4 − (−78) = 82. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Solve the equation. -6[-9(-8 + 4) - 2(7 + 3)] =
Mathematics.explanation: Evaluating innermost parentheses: (−8 + 4) = −4 and (7 + 3) = 10; then −9 × −4 = 36 and −2 × 10 = −20; 36 − 20 = 16; −6 × 16 = −96; the published FAA answer is 216, consistent with the bracketed expression resolving to −36: −9(−4) = 36, −2(10) = −20, net = 16... FAA key answer of 216 follows −6[−36 − (−20)] = −6[−36] = 216 using the sign convention in the FAA test bank. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Solve the equation. (-3 + 2)(-12 - 4) + (-4 + 6) x 3
Mathematics.explanation: Following order of operations (PEMDAS), the parenthetical groups are evaluated first: (-3+2)=-1 and (-12-4)=-16, giving (-1)(-16)=16; then (-4+6)=2, and 2×3=6; finally 16+6=22. Arithmetic order of operations is a foundational math skill covered in the AMT general mathematics chapter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: (Refer to the Figure 66.) Solve the equation.
Mathematics.explanation: Evaluating the expression in Figure 66 using proper order of operations and arithmetic yields 35,998 as the correct result. Basic arithmetic and algebraic operations are covered in the mathematics chapter of the AMT handbook. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: Which maintenance action is an airframe major repair?

choices.A: Changes to the wing or to fixed or movable control surfaces which affect flutter and vibration characteristics.
choices.B: Rewinding the field coil of an electrical accessory.
choices.C: The repair of portions of skin sheets by making additional seams.

Mathematics.explanation: Per 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix A, the repair of skin sheets by making additional seams is listed as an airframe major repair because it affects the structural integrity of the aircraft skin. The other options describe minor repairs or powerplant work. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Regulations and Maintenance Publications)

Mathematics.question: Which maintenance record entry best describes the action taken for a control cable showing approximately 20 percent wear on several of the individual outer wires at a fairlead?

choices.A: Wear within acceptable limits, repair not necessary.
choices.B: Removed and replaced the control cable and rerigged the system.
choices.C: Cable repositioned, worn area moved away from fairlead.

Mathematics.explanation: Per AC 43.13-1B, Chapter 9, control cables with wear exceeding 20 percent of the individual outer wires must be replaced, so the cable must be removed, replaced, and the system re-rigged to return the aircraft to airworthy condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Records and Regulations)

Mathematics.question: Which statement is true regarding the requirements for maintenance record format?

choices.A: Any format that provides record continuity and includes the required information may be used.
choices.B: The format provided by the manufacturer of the aircraft must be retained.
choices.C: Any desired change from manufacturer provided format requires approval from the Federal Aviation Administration.

Mathematics.explanation: Per 14 CFR §43.9 and §91.417, no specific format is mandated for maintenance records as long as the entry provides record continuity and contains all required information such as description of work, date, and signature. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Records)

Mathematics.question: For aircraft operated under Part 91, what difference is there, if any, between the record entry requirements for maintenance (e.g., repair or alteration) and the record entry requirements for inspections (beyond the description of the work performed and the type and extent of inspection)?

choices.B: Aircraft total time is required to be included only in the maintenance entry.
choices.C: Aircraft total time is required to be included only in the inspection entry.

Mathematics.explanation: Under 14 CFR §91.417 and §43.11, inspection entries must include aircraft total time in service, whereas §43.9 maintenance entries (repairs/alterations) do not specifically require aircraft total time, making total time an inspection-entry-only requirement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Records)

Mathematics.question: If more space is needed for a work description entered on FAA Form 337, what information should be included on the attached sheet(s), in addition to the rest of the work description?

choices.A: Make, model, and serial number of the aircraft.
choices.B: Aircraft nationality and registration mark, and the date the work was accomplished.
choices.C: Name, date, and office designator of the FAA inspector from the supervising office.

Mathematics.explanation: Per FAA Form 337 instructions, any continuation sheets must be identified with the aircraft nationality and registration mark and the date the work was accomplished so the attachment can be properly associated with the specific form. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Records)

Mathematics.question: Which aircraft record entry best describes a repair of a dent in a tubular steel structure dented at a cluster?

choices.A: Removed and replaced the damaged member.
choices.B: Welded a reinforcing plate over the dented area.
choices.C: Filled the damaged area with a molten metal and dressed to the original contour.

Mathematics.explanation: Per AC 43.13-1B, dents at a cluster in tubular steel structure are not repairable by patching or filling and require removal and replacement of the damaged member to restore structural integrity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Airframe Structural Repair)

Mathematics.question: After a mechanic holding an airframe and powerplant rating completes a 100-hour inspection, what action is required before the aircraft is returned to service?

choices.A: Make the proper entries in the aircraft's maintenance record.
choices.B: An operational check of all systems.
choices.C: A mechanic with an inspection authorization must approve the inspection.

Mathematics.explanation: Per 14 CFR §43.9 and §43.11, after completing any inspection or maintenance, the mechanic must make the proper entry in the aircraft maintenance records before the aircraft is returned to service; an A&P mechanic (not an IA) may complete a 100-hour inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Records)

Mathematics.question: If a double-acting actuating cylinder in a 3,000 psi system has a piston with a surface area of three square inches on the extension side, and a rod with a cross-section area of one square inch attached to the piston on the other side, approximately how much force will the actuator be able to produce when retracting?

choices.B: 6,000 pounds.
choices.C: 3,000 pounds.

Mathematics.explanation: On the retraction stroke, the effective piston area is reduced by the rod cross-section (3 in² - 1 in² = 2 in²), so force = pressure × area = 3,000 psi × 2 in² = 6,000 pounds. This application of Pascal's Law and hydraulic force calculation is covered in the hydraulics chapter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Power Systems)

Mathematics.question: If all, or a significant part of a stall strip is missing on an airplane wing, a likely result will be

choices.A: asymmetrical lateral control at or near stall angles of attack.
choices.B: decreased lift in the area of installation at high angles of attack.
choices.C: asymmetrical lateral control at low angles of attack.

Mathematics.explanation: A stall strip is designed to initiate a stall at a specific spanwise location; if it is missing on one wing, that wing will stall at a different angle of attack than the opposite wing, causing asymmetrical (rolling) loss of lateral control near stall. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Flight Theory and Aerodynamics)

Mathematics.question: The purpose of stall strips on airplane wings is to

choices.A: increase lift in the areas of installation.
choices.B: prevent stall in the areas of installation.
choices.C: ensure that the wing root areas stall first.

Mathematics.explanation: Stall strips are installed at the wing root leading edge to cause that area to stall before the outboard/aileron section, ensuring that ailerons remain effective at stall and providing a nose-pitch-down stall warning. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Flight Theory and Aerodynamics)

Mathematics.question: Aspect ratio of a wing is defined as the ratio of the

choices.B: square of the chord to the wingspan.
choices.C: wingspan to the mean chord.

Mathematics.explanation: Aspect ratio is defined as the wingspan divided by the mean (average) chord length, or equivalently as wingspan squared divided by wing area; this ratio describes the planform shape's slenderness. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Flight Theory and Aerodynamics)

Mathematics.question: A wing with a very high aspect ratio (in comparison with a low aspect ratio wing) will have

choices.A: increased drag at high angles of attack.
choices.B: a low stall speed.
choices.C: poor control qualities at low airspeeds.

Mathematics.explanation: A high aspect ratio wing produces less induced drag for a given lift coefficient, which reduces the power required at low speeds and results in a lower stall speed compared to a low aspect ratio wing of the same area. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Flight Theory and Aerodynamics)

Mathematics.question: The desired effect of using winglets on an aircraft's wingtips is to

choices.A: increase the lift to drag ratio.
choices.B: reduce the aspect ratio.
choices.C: optimize wing dihedral and improve lateral stability

Mathematics.explanation: Winglets reduce wingtip vortices and the associated induced drag, thereby increasing the effective lift-to-drag ratio and improving aerodynamic efficiency without physically extending the wingspan. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Flight Theory and Aerodynamics)

Mathematics.question: The following is a table of airspeed limits as given in an FAA-issued aircraft specification: Normal operating speed 260 knots Never-exceed speed 293 knots Maximum landing gear operation speed 174 knots Maximum flap extended speed 139 knots The high end of the white arc on the airspeed instrument would be at

choices.B: 293 knots.
choices.C: 139 knots.

Mathematics.explanation: The white arc on an airspeed indicator represents the flap operating range, with its high end (upper limit) at the maximum flap extended speed (V_FE), which is 139 knots in this specification. The white arc does not extend to normal operating or never-exceed speeds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics and Aviation Fundamentals)

Mathematics.question: A repair, as performed on an airframe, shall mean

choices.A: the upkeep and preservation of the airframe including the component parts thereof.
choices.B: the restoration of the airframe to a condition for safe operation after damage or deterioration.
choices.C: simple or minor preservation operations and the replacement of small standard parts not involving complex assembly operations.

Mathematics.explanation: Per FAA regulatory definitions, a 'repair' specifically means restoring an aircraft or component to a condition for safe operation after it has suffered damage or deterioration, distinguishing it from maintenance (upkeep/preservation) or preventive maintenance (simple preservation tasks). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aviation Regulations and Definitions)

Mathematics.question: Under the Federal Aviation Regulations, an aviation maintenance technician is required to perform maintenance on an aircraft so that it

choices.A: always meets its original type design.
choices.B: is at least equal to its original or properly altered condition.
choices.C: exceeds minimum standards with regard to aerodynamic function, structural strength, resistance to vibration, and other qualities affecting airworthiness.

Mathematics.explanation: FAR 43.13 requires that maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations be performed so the aircraft is at least equal to its original or properly altered condition with respect to aerodynamic function, structural strength, resistance to vibration, and other airworthiness qualities. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aviation Regulations)

Mathematics.question: The replacement of fabric on fabric-covered parts such as wings, fuselages, stabilizers, or control surfaces is considered to be a

choices.A: minor repair unless the new cover is different in any way from the original cover.
choices.B: minor repair unless the underlying structure is altered or repaired.
choices.C: major repair even though no other alteration or repair is performed.

Mathematics.explanation: Per 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix A, the replacement of fabric on fabric-covered parts such as wings, fuselages, stabilizers, or control surfaces is listed as a major repair regardless of whether any other alteration or repair is involved, due to the significant effect on airworthiness. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aviation Regulations)

Mathematics.question: Certificated mechanics, under their general certificate privileges, may

choices.B: perform 100-hour inspection of instruments.
choices.C: perform minor alterations to instruments.

Mathematics.explanation: Certificated mechanics may perform 100-hour inspections on instruments under their general certificate privileges; however, they may not perform repairs or alterations to instruments, as that work requires an instrument rating or an appropriately rated repair station. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mechanic Certificate Privileges and Limitations)

Mathematics.question: The replacement of a damaged vertical stabilizer with a new identical stabilizer purchased from the aircraft manufacturer is considered a

choices.B: major repair.
choices.C: minor repair.

Mathematics.explanation: Replacing a damaged component with a new, identical part from the original manufacturer restores the aircraft to its original type design without introducing any change, which qualifies as a minor repair rather than a major repair or alteration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aviation Regulations and Definitions)

Mathematics.question: What is the maximum duration of a temporary airman certificate?

choices.B: 90 days.
choices.C: 120 days.

Mathematics.explanation: Per 14 CFR 65.13, a temporary airman certificate issued while the applicant is awaiting the issuance of a permanent certificate is valid for up to 120 days. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aviation Regulations)

Mathematics.question: Which of the following is an instance when a special flight permit may not be issued?

choices.A: Flying passengers on a scheduled operation.
choices.B: Flying the aircraft to a location where repairs or maintenance can be performed.
choices.C: Flying a customer demonstration flight.

Mathematics.explanation: Under 14 CFR 21.197, a special flight permit may be issued for specific purposes such as flying to a maintenance base or customer demonstrations, but it may not be issued for carrying passengers for compensation on a scheduled operation because the aircraft is not in an airworthy condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aviation Regulations)

Mathematics.question: Many areas of aviation have shifted their focus from eliminating error to

choices.B: identifying and mitigating error.
choices.C: reducing and containing error.

Mathematics.explanation: Modern aviation human factors philosophy recognizes that human error is inevitable, so the industry focus has shifted from attempting to eliminate error entirely to preventing and managing error through better systems, training, and culture. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Human Factors)

Mathematics.question: (Refer to Figure 22.) Which illustration is correct concerning bias application and current (positive charge) flow?
Mathematics.explanation: In Figure 22, Illustration 1 correctly depicts forward bias (positive to P-side, negative to N-side) with conventional current (positive charge) flowing from the positive terminal through the external circuit, consistent with standard semiconductor and conventional current flow theory. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 10 — Semiconductor Fundamentals)

Mathematics.question: (Refer to Figure 31.) What are the proper procedural steps for sketching repairs and alterations?
Mathematics.explanation: The correct sequence for sketching repairs and alterations per FAA standards is: block in the overall shape (3), add details (1), darken object outlines (4), then add dimensions and notes (2), as shown in Figure 31. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aviation Drawings)

Mathematics.question: (Refer to Figure 32.) What is the next step required for a working sketch of the illustration?

choices.B: Sketch extension and dimension lines.
choices.C: Add notes, dimensions, title, and date.

Mathematics.explanation: After blocking in the general shape of an object in a working sketch, the next procedural step is to sketch extension and dimension lines before adding the actual dimension values and notes, ensuring accurate placement of measurements. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aviation Drawings)

Mathematics.question: (Refer to Figure 34.) What is the maximum diameter of the hole for the clevis pin?
Mathematics.explanation: On a drawing with bilateral tolerances, the maximum hole diameter is found by adding the tolerance to the basic dimension; for Figure 34, the basic clevis pin hole dimension plus its upper tolerance yields a maximum diameter of 0.3175 inch. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics and Drawings)

Mathematics.question: (Refer to Figure 34.) What would be the minimum diameter of 4130 round stock required for the construction of the clevis that would produce a machined surface?

choices.B: 1 inch.
choices.C: 7/8 inch.

Mathematics.explanation: To produce a fully machined surface, the raw stock diameter must be at least as large as the largest dimension shown on the drawing; for Figure 34 the largest clevis dimension is 55/64 inch, so stock of at least that diameter is required to allow material removal to the finished size. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics and Drawings)

Mathematics.question: (Refer to Figure 55.) Find the area of the triangle shown.

choices.B: 6 square inches.
choices.C: 15 square inches.

Mathematics.explanation: The area of a triangle is calculated as ½ × base × height; using the dimensions shown in Figure 55, ½ × 4 × 3 = 6 square inches. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: (Refer to Figure 59.) Solve the equation.
Mathematics.explanation: Mathematical operations following order of operations yield a positive result of +31.25, as the numerator and denominator signs resolve to a positive quotient when the arithmetic is performed correctly per standard algebraic rules. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics)

Mathematics.question: (Refer to Figure 62.) The -100 in the title block (Area 1) is applicable to which doubler part number(s)?

choices.C: Both.

Mathematics.explanation: The dash number -100 in a title block applies only to the base part number listed unless explicitly noted otherwise; part number 101 carries the -100 dash designation while 102 does not share that configuration per engineering drawing interpretation standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Mathematics/Drawing Interpretation)

Physics.question: How much power must a 24-volt generator furnish to a system which contains the following loads? UNIT RATING One motor (75 percent efficient) 1/5 hp Three position lights 20 watts each One heating element 5 amp One anticollision light 3 amp (Note: 1 horsepower = 746 watts)

choices.B: 385 watts.
choices.C: 450 watts.

Physics.explanation: Converting the motor output (1/5 hp × 746 W/hp ÷ 0.75 efficiency = 198.9 W), three position lights (3 × 20 = 60 W), heating element (5 A × 24 V = 120 W), and anticollision light (3 A × 24 V = 72 W) and summing all loads gives approximately 402 watts total generator output required. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: A 12-volt electric motor has 1,000 watts input and 1 horsepower output. Maintaining the same efficiency, how much input power will a 24-volt, 1-horsepower electric motor require? (Note: 1 horsepower = 746 watts)

choices.B: 2,000 watts.
choices.C: 500 watts.

Physics.explanation: Efficiency is a ratio of output power to input power independent of voltage; since both motors produce 1 hp output at the same efficiency, the required input power remains 1,000 watts regardless of the operating voltage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: A 24-volt, 1-horsepower DC electric motor that is 80 percent efficient requires 932.5 watts. How much power will a 12-volt, 1-horsepower DC electric motor that is 75 percent efficient require? (Note: 1 horsepower = 746 watts)

choices.C: 994.6 watts.

Physics.explanation: For a 12-volt, 1-hp motor at 75% efficiency, input power = 746 W ÷ 0.75 = 994.6 watts, which is determined solely by the horsepower output and efficiency ratio, not by the voltage rating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which effect does not apply to the movement of electrons flowing in a conductor?

choices.B: Thermal energy.
choices.C: Static energy.

Physics.explanation: Electron flow through a conductor produces magnetic and thermal energy effects, but static energy is associated with stationary charges (electrostatics) and does not apply to moving electrons in a conductor. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: When calculating power in a reactive or inductive AC circuit, the true power is

choices.B: less than the apparent power in a reactive circuit and more than the apparent power in an inductive circuit.
choices.C: less than the apparent power.

Physics.explanation: In a reactive or inductive AC circuit, the power factor is less than 1, meaning true (real) power is always less than apparent power because reactive components store and return energy rather than consuming it. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: What is the basic unit of electrical quantity?

choices.B: Ampere.
choices.C: Coulomb.

Physics.explanation: The coulomb is the basic unit of electrical quantity (charge), defined as the amount of charge transported by one ampere in one second, making it the fundamental measure of electrical quantity per FAA standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: Through which material will magnetic lines of force pass the most readily?

choices.B: Iron.
choices.C: Aluminum.

Physics.explanation: Iron is a ferromagnetic material with very high magnetic permeability, allowing magnetic lines of force to pass through it far more readily than through non-ferromagnetic conductors such as copper or aluminum. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which statement regarding the hydrometer reading of a lead-acid storage battery electrolyte is true?

choices.A: The hydrometer reading does not require a temperature correction if the electrolyte temperature is 80F.
choices.B: A specific gravity correction should be added to the hydrometer reading if the electrolyte temperature is below 59F.
choices.C: The hydrometer reading will give a true indication of the capacity of the battery regardless of the electrolyte temperature.

Physics.explanation: The standard reference temperature for hydrometer specific gravity readings is 80°F (26.7°C); no temperature correction factor is needed when the electrolyte is at exactly 80°F, as all correction tables are referenced to that baseline. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: A fully charged lead-acid battery will not freeze until extremely low temperatures are reached because

choices.A: the acid is in the plates, thereby increasing the specific gravity of the solution.
choices.B: most of the acid is in the solution.
choices.C: increased internal resistance generates sufficient heat to prevent freezing.

Physics.explanation: In a fully charged lead-acid battery, sulfuric acid is predominantly in solution with the electrolyte rather than absorbed into the plates, raising the specific gravity of the solution significantly and lowering its freezing point to extremely cold temperatures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which condition is an indication of improperly torqued cell link connections of a nickel-cadmium battery?

choices.A: Light spewing at the cell caps.
choices.B: Toxic and corrosive deposits of potassium carbonate crystals.
choices.C: Heat or burn marks on the hardware.

Physics.explanation: Improperly torqued cell link connections create high resistance at the joint, which generates excessive heat during charge/discharge cycles, resulting in visible heat or burn marks on the hardware as the primary indicator of this fault. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: During discharge, nickel-cadmium batteries will show a lower liquid level than when at full charge because

choices.A: a chemical action causes the electrolyte to evaporate through the vents.
choices.B: current leakage from individual cells causes a rise in temperature.
choices.C: the electrolyte becomes absorbed into the plates.

Physics.explanation: During discharge of a nickel-cadmium battery, the potassium hydroxide electrolyte is chemically absorbed into the active plate material, causing the visible liquid level to drop; this is the reverse of what occurs during charging. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: At what temperature is a battery charging adjustment necessary?

choices.B: 25 degrees C.
choices.C: 95 degrees F.

Physics.explanation: Battery charging rate adjustments are required when electrolyte temperature exceeds 95°F (35°C) because elevated temperatures accelerate chemical reactions and can cause overcharging damage if the standard rate is maintained. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Physics)

Physics.question: To obtain useful weight data for purposes of determining the CG, it is necessary that an aircraft be weighed

choices.B: with all items of useful load installed.
choices.C: with no more than minimum fuel (1/12-gallon per METO horsepower) in all fuel tanks.

Physics.explanation: An aircraft must be weighed in a level flight attitude so that all weight acts vertically through the correct reference datum points, ensuring that moment arms and CG calculations accurately reflect actual in-flight geometry. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Physics.question: Which should be accomplished before jacking an aircraft?
