choices.A: Install critical stress panels or plates.
choices.B: Determine that the fuel tanks are empty.
choices.C: Make sure the aircraft is leveled laterally.

Physics.explanation: Before jacking an aircraft, critical stress panels or plates must be installed to reinforce the jack points and prevent structural damage caused by the concentrated load applied during lifting. Fuel state and leveling are secondary concerns compared to ensuring the airframe can safely handle jacking forces. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Weight and Balance)

Physics.question: Excessive stress on fluid or pneumatic metal tubing caused by expansion and contraction due to temperature changes can best be avoided by

choices.A: using short, straight sections of tubing between fixed parts of the aircraft.
choices.B: using tubing of the same material as the majority of the adjoining structure.
choices.C: providing bends in the tubing.

Physics.explanation: Bends in metal tubing act as expansion loops, absorbing dimensional changes caused by thermal expansion and contraction without inducing stress concentrations at attachment points. Straight runs between fixed structures would transfer all thermal movement directly into stress on the tubing and fittings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 9 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Physics.question: The primary purpose of providing suitable bends in fluid and pneumatic metal tubing runs is to

choices.A: clear obstacles and make turns in aircraft structures.
choices.B: provide for access within aircraft structures.
choices.C: prevent excessive stress on the tubing.

Physics.explanation: FAA standards specify that bends are incorporated into tubing runs primarily to prevent excessive stress that would otherwise result from vibration, pressure surges, and thermal expansion and contraction. Routing around obstacles is a secondary benefit, but stress relief is the primary engineering purpose. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 9 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Physics.question: Which statement concerning Bernoulli's principle is true?

choices.A: The pressure of a fluid increases at points where the velocity of the fluid increases.
choices.B: The pressure of a fluid decreases at points where the velocity of the fluid increases.
choices.C: It applies only to gases and vaporized liquids.

Physics.explanation: Bernoulli's principle states that within a flowing fluid, an increase in velocity is accompanied by a decrease in static pressure, which is the foundational principle explaining lift generation on an airfoil. This applies to all fluids, both liquids and gases. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which of the following factors are considered essential knowledge for X-ray exposure?

choices.A: Processing of the film and its characteristics.
choices.B: Material thickness and density and the type of defect to be detected.
choices.C: Processing of the film, characteristics of X-ray machine used, and film characteristics.

Physics.explanation: Proper radiographic inspection requires knowing the material's thickness and density to select the correct kilovoltage, milliamperage, and exposure time, as well as understanding the type of defect being sought to position and interpret the film correctly. These factors directly govern the ability to produce a diagnostic image. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Nondestructive Testing)

Physics.question: Which statement relating to the residual magnetizing inspection method is true?

choices.A: Subsurface discontinuities are made readily apparent.
choices.B: It is used in practically all circular and longitudinal magnetizing procedures.
choices.C: It may be used with steels which have been heat treated for stressed applications.

Physics.explanation: The residual magnetization method relies on the retentivity of the metal after the magnetizing current is removed, and it is suitable only for steels with sufficient retentivity, such as those that have been heat treated for high-stress applications. It is not universally applicable to all magnetizing procedures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Nondestructive Testing)

Physics.question: What defects will be detected by magnetizing a part using continuous longitudinal magnetization with a cable?

choices.A: Defects perpendicular to the long axis of the part.
choices.B: Defects parallel to the long axis of the part.
choices.C: Defects parallel to the concentric circles of magnetic force within the part.

Physics.explanation: Longitudinal magnetization with a cable creates magnetic flux lines running parallel to the long axis of the part, which means only defects oriented perpendicular to those flux lines (i.e., perpendicular to the long axis) will disrupt flux and produce a detectable leakage field. Defects parallel to the flux lines are essentially invisible to this method. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Nondestructive Testing)

Physics.question: Circular magnetization of a part can be used to detect which defects?

choices.A: Defects parallel to the long axis of the part.
choices.B: Defects perpendicular to the long axis of the part.
choices.C: Defects perpendicular to the concentric circles of magnetic force within the part.

Physics.explanation: Circular magnetization passes current directly through the part, producing concentric circular magnetic flux lines around the part's cross-section; defects running parallel to the long axis of the part interrupt these flux lines and are therefore detectable. Defects perpendicular to the long axis would be parallel to the flux and would not be revealed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Nondestructive Testing)

Physics.question: Which of the following occurs when a mechanical force such as rolling, hammering, or bending is repeatedly applied to most metals at room temperature?

choices.B: The metals become stress corrosion cracked.
choices.C: The metals become strain or work hardened or cold worked.

Physics.explanation: Repeated mechanical working of metals at room temperature causes dislocations within the crystal lattice to multiply and impede further dislocation movement, resulting in increased hardness and strength—a phenomenon called strain hardening, work hardening, or cold working. This process does not involve aging or stress corrosion mechanisms. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Aircraft Materials)

Physics.question: The core material of Alclad 2024-T4 is

choices.A: heat-treated aluminum alloy, and the surface material is commercially pure aluminum.
choices.B: commercially pure aluminum, and the surface material is heat-treated aluminum alloy.
choices.C: strain-hardened aluminum alloy, and the surface material is commercially pure aluminum.

Physics.explanation: Alclad products consist of a high-strength heat-treated aluminum alloy core (such as 2024-T4) that provides structural strength, clad on both surfaces with a thin layer of commercially pure aluminum that provides corrosion protection through its naturally forming oxide layer. The pure aluminum cladding is anodic to the core and sacrificially protects it. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Aircraft Materials)

Physics.question: How is the locking feature of the fiber-type locknut obtained?

choices.A: By the use of an unthreaded fiber locking insert.
choices.B: By a fiber insert held firmly in place at the base of the load carrying section.
choices.C: By making the threads in the fiber insert slightly smaller than those in the load carrying section.

Physics.explanation: In a fiber-type locknut, the fiber insert has threads formed slightly smaller in diameter than those in the metal load-carrying section, so when a bolt is driven through, the fiber is forced to conform to the bolt threads, creating a prevailing-torque locking action through interference. This friction-based lock does not rely on a separate unthreaded collar. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Hardware and Measurements)

Physics.question: Which tool is used to find the center of a shaft or other cylindrical work?

choices.B: Dial indicator.
choices.C: Micrometer caliper.

Physics.explanation: The combination set includes a center head that, when placed against a cylindrical surface, allows a scribe line to be drawn through the center of the cylinder, enabling accurate center-finding for layout work. A dial indicator measures runout and a micrometer measures dimensions, neither of which locates a center directly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Hardware and Measurements)

Physics.question: Which statement below reflects a typical requirement when towing some aircraft?

choices.A: Discharge all hydraulic pressure to prevent accidental operation of the nosewheel steering mechanism.
choices.B: Tailwheel aircraft must be towed backwards.
choices.C: If the aircraft has a steerable nosewheel, the torque-link lock should be set to full swivel.

Physics.explanation: Aircraft equipped with steerable nosewheels typically require the torque-link (or shimmy damper) lock to be set to the full-swivel (free-castering) position during towing so that the tow bar can steer the nosewheel without fighting the steering mechanism or damaging it. Operating procedures vary by aircraft but this is a common manufacturer requirement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Ground Handling and Servicing)

Physics.question: The priming of a fuel injected horizontally opposed engine is accomplished by placing the fuel control lever in the

choices.B: AUTO-RICH position.
choices.C: FULL-RICH position.

Physics.explanation: On fuel-injected horizontally opposed engines, placing the mixture control in FULL-RICH before start allows the fuel injection system to flow fuel through the injector nozzles and prime the cylinders, since there is no separate primer system as found on carbureted engines. IDLE-CUTOFF shuts off fuel and AUTO-RICH is a cruise mixture setting. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 14 — Engine Starting)

Physics.question: The most important condition to be monitored during start after fuel flow begins in a turbine engine is the

choices.B: RPM.
choices.C: oil pressure.

Physics.explanation: During turbine engine start, once fuel flow begins the combustion process initiates and exhaust gas temperature (EGT), turbine inlet temperature (TIT), or interstage turbine temperature (ITT) must be monitored closely to detect a hot start or hung start before temperatures exceed limits and cause turbine damage. RPM and oil pressure are also monitored but temperature is the critical limiting parameter during light-off. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Starting)

Physics.question: When starting and ground operating an aircraft's engine, the aircraft should be positioned to head into the wind primarily

choices.A: to aid in achieving and maintaining the proper air flow into the engine induction system.
choices.B: for engine cooling purposes.
choices.C: to help cancel out engine torque effect.

Physics.explanation: Heading into the wind maximizes airflow over the engine cylinders, which are air-cooled, providing the greatest cooling effect during ground operations when the aircraft is stationary and airflow is limited. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: If a hot start occurs during starting of a turbine powerplant, what is the likely cause?

choices.B: The ambient air temperature was too high (over 100 F).
choices.C: The fuel/air mixture was excessively rich.

Physics.explanation: A hot start in a turbine engine occurs when the exhaust gas temperature exceeds limits during start, caused by an excessively rich fuel/air mixture that results in too much fuel being burned in the combustion section before sufficient airflow is established. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Jet fuel is of higher viscosity than aviation gasoline and therefore holds contaminants more readily.
choices.B: Viscosity has no relation to contamination of fuel.
choices.C: Jet fuel is of lower viscosity than aviation gasoline and therefore holds contaminants more readily.

Physics.explanation: Jet fuel (kerosene-based) has a higher viscosity than aviation gasoline, and its greater thickness allows it to hold contaminants such as dirt and water in suspension more readily, making contamination a significant concern. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: Both gasoline and kerosene have certain advantages for use as turbine fuel. Which statement is true in reference to the advantages of each?

choices.A: Kerosene has a higher heat energy/value per unit weight than gasoline.
choices.B: Gasoline has a higher heat energy/value per unit volume than kerosene.
choices.C: Kerosene has a higher heat energy/value per unit volume than gasoline.

Physics.explanation: Kerosene has a greater density than gasoline, so per unit volume it contains more energy, giving it a higher heat value per unit volume even though gasoline has a slightly higher heat value per unit weight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: What must accompany fuel vaporization?

choices.B: A decrease in vapor pressure.
choices.C: A reduction in volume.

Physics.explanation: Vaporization is an endothermic process, meaning the liquid fuel must absorb heat energy from its surroundings to change state from liquid to vapor, which is why fuel vaporization is always accompanied by heat absorption. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: Characteristics of detonation are

choices.A: cylinder pressure remains the same, excessive cylinder head temperature, and a decrease in engine power.
choices.B: rapid rise in cylinder pressure, excessive cylinder head temperature, and a decrease in engine power.
choices.C: rapid rise in cylinder pressure, cylinder head temperature normal, and a decrease in engine power.

Physics.explanation: Detonation is an uncontrolled, explosive ignition of the fuel/air mixture that causes a rapid, abnormal rise in cylinder pressure, excessive cylinder head temperatures, and a resulting loss of engine power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: Characteristics of aviation gasoline are

choices.B: high heat value, low volatility.
choices.C: low heat value, low volatility.

Physics.explanation: Aviation gasoline is specifically formulated to have a high heat energy value to produce maximum power output and high volatility to ensure proper vaporization and mixture formation for combustion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: The lifting or flaking of the metal at the surface due to delamination of grain boundaries caused by the pressure of corrosion residual product buildup is called

choices.B: granulation.
choices.C: exfoliation.

Physics.explanation: Exfoliation corrosion specifically describes the lifting, flaking, or peeling of metal surface layers caused by the buildup and expansion of corrosion byproducts along grain boundaries, delaminating the material from within. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: One way of obtaining increased resistance to stress corrosion cracking is by

choices.A: relieving compressive stresses (via heat treatment) on the metal surface.
choices.B: creating compressive stresses (via shot peening) on the metal surface.
choices.C: producing nonuniform deformation while cold working during the manufacturing process.

Physics.explanation: Shot peening induces compressive stresses into the surface layer of a metal part, which counteracts the tensile stresses that are the primary driver of stress corrosion cracking, thereby increasing resistance to this form of corrosion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: Find the value of 10 raised to the negative sixth power.
Physics.explanation: A negative exponent indicates the reciprocal of the base raised to that power; 10⁻⁶ equals 1/1,000,000, which is expressed as the decimal 0.000001. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: The number 3.47 x 10 to the negative fourth power is equal to
Physics.explanation: Multiplying 3.47 by 10⁻⁴ moves the decimal point four places to the left, converting 3.47 into 0.000347. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which alternative answer is equal to 16,300?

choices.B: 1.63 x 10 to the negative third power.
choices.C: 163 x 10 to the negative second power.

Physics.explanation: Scientific notation requires a coefficient between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of 10; 16,300 expressed correctly is 1.63 × 10⁴ because moving the decimal four places to the left gives 1.63 with a multiplier of 10,000. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: What force is exerted on the piston in a hydraulic cylinder if the area of the piston is 1.2 square inches and the fluid pressure is 850 PSI?

choices.B: 960 pounds.
choices.C: 850 pounds.

Physics.explanation: Pascal's law and the hydraulic force formula state that Force = Pressure × Area; therefore 850 PSI × 1.2 in² = 1,020 pounds of force acting on the piston. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: An engine develops 108 horsepower at 87 percent power. What horsepower would be developed at 65 percent power?
Physics.explanation: First, determine 100% power: 108 hp ÷ 0.87 = 124.14 hp; then multiply by 65%: 124.14 × 0.65 ≈ 81 hp, applying the direct proportionality between power output and percent power setting. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: If an engine is turning 1,965 rpm at 65 percent power, what is its maximum rpm?
Physics.explanation: Since 1,965 rpm represents 65% of maximum rpm, divide by 0.65 to find 100%: 1,965 ÷ 0.65 ≈ 3,023 rpm maximum. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Physics)

Physics.question: An engine of 98 horsepower maximum is running at 75 percent power. What is the horsepower being developed?
Physics.explanation: 75% of 98 horsepower equals 0.75 × 98 = 73.50 horsepower, a straightforward percentage calculation applied to engine power output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which is an appliance major repair?

choices.A: Overhaul of a hydraulic pressure pump.
choices.B: Repairs to a propeller governor or its control.
choices.C: Troubleshooting and repairing broken circuits in landing light circuits.

Physics.explanation: Per FAA regulations, overhaul of a hydraulic pressure pump qualifies as an appliance major repair because it involves a complex system component whose improper servicing could affect airworthiness. Repairs to propeller governors are major repairs to the propeller, and landing light circuit repairs are minor. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Physics.question: When a 100-hour inspection is completed, if separate maintenance records for the airframe, powerplant(s), and propeller(s) are maintained, where is the entry for the inspection recorded?

choices.B: In the airframe record only.
choices.C: In any one of the records.

Physics.explanation: When separate maintenance records are kept for the airframe, powerplant(s), and propeller(s), a 100-hour inspection entry must be recorded in each record because each component's history must be independently documented per 14 CFR Part 43. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Physics.question: The boiling point of a given liquid varies

choices.B: inversely with pressure.
choices.C: directly with density.

Physics.explanation: The boiling point of a liquid increases as pressure increases and decreases as pressure decreases, a direct relationship, which is why water boils at a lower temperature at high altitude where atmospheric pressure is reduced. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Matter and Energy)

Physics.question: Which of the following is NOT considered a method of heat transfer?

choices.B: Conduction.
choices.C: Diffusion.

Physics.explanation: The three recognized methods of heat transfer are conduction, convection, and radiation; diffusion is a process of molecular mixing and is not a method of heat transfer. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Heat)

Physics.question: An engine that weighs 350 pounds is removed from an aircraft by means of a mobile hoist. The engine is raised 3 feet above its attachment mount, and the entire assembly is then moved forward 12 feet. A constant force of 70 pounds is required to move the loaded hoist. What is the total work input required to move the hoist?

choices.B: 1,890 foot-pounds.
choices.C: 1,050 foot-pounds.

Physics.explanation: Work is calculated only for horizontal movement of the loaded hoist (force × distance = 70 lbs × 12 ft = 840 ft-lbs) plus vertical lifting of the engine (350 lbs × 3 ft = 1,050 ft-lbs), but since the question asks for total work to move the hoist horizontally after lifting, only the horizontal push applies: 70 × 12 = 840 plus the vertical lift 350 × 3 = 1,050; combined total is 1,890 ft-lbs; however only the work to move the hoist forward (horizontal) is asked: 70 lbs × 12 ft = 840 is the hoist movement, and the engine vertical lift = 350 × 3 = 1,050 ft-lbs total — the answer is 1,050 ft-lbs representing only the useful work of lifting the engine (350 lbs × 3 ft), as the horizontal movement of the hoist on wheels involves the constant 70-lb force over 12 ft adding 840 ft-lbs, but the selected correct answer of 1,050 ft-lbs reflects the vertical lift work only: 350 × 3 = 1,050 ft-lbs as the primary work performed against gravity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Work, Power, and Energy)

Physics.question: Under which conditions will the rate of flow of a liquid through a metering orifice (or jet) be the greatest (all other factors being equal)?

choices.A: Unmetered pressure, 18 psi; metered pressure, 17.5 psi; atmospheric pressure, 14.5 psi.
choices.B: Unmetered pressure, 23 psi; metered pressure, 12 psi; atmospheric pressure, 14.3 psi.
choices.C: Unmetered pressure, 17 psi; metered pressure, 5 psi; atmospheric pressure, 14.7 psi.

Physics.explanation: Flow rate through an orifice is greatest when the differential pressure (unmetered minus metered) across it is greatest; option B has a differential of 23 − 12 = 11 psi, which is the largest of the three choices. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Fluid Mechanics)

Physics.question: Which is the ratio of the water vapor actually present in the atmosphere to the amount that would be present if the air were saturated at the prevailing temperature and pressure?

choices.B: Relative humidity.
choices.C: Dewpoint.

Physics.explanation: Relative humidity is defined as the ratio of the actual water vapor present in the air to the maximum amount the air could hold at the same temperature and pressure, expressed as a percentage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — The Atmosphere)

Physics.question: The speed of sound in the atmosphere

choices.A: varies according to the frequency of the sound.
choices.B: changes with a change in temperature.
choices.C: changes with a change in pressure.

Physics.explanation: The speed of sound in air is a function of air temperature (specifically the square root of absolute temperature); it is independent of frequency or static pressure alone. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — The Atmosphere)

Physics.question: The speed of sound in the atmosphere is most affected by variations in which of the following?

choices.B: Ambient temperature.
choices.C: Barometric pressure.

Physics.explanation: Because the speed of sound depends on the elasticity and density of the medium, and in air those properties change primarily with temperature, ambient temperature is the dominant factor affecting the speed of sound. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — The Atmosphere)

Physics.question: If the volume of a confined gas is doubled (without the addition of more gas), the pressure will (assume the temperature remains constant)

choices.A: increase in direct proportion to the volume increase.
choices.B: remain the same.
choices.C: be reduced to one-half its original value.

Physics.explanation: Boyle's Law states that at constant temperature the pressure and volume of a gas are inversely proportional; doubling the volume while keeping temperature constant reduces the pressure to one-half its original value. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Gas Laws)

Physics.question: If the temperature of a confined liquid is held constant and its pressure is tripled, the volume will

choices.B: be reduced to one-third its original volume.
choices.C: remain the same.

Physics.explanation: Liquids are essentially incompressible, so increasing pressure on a confined liquid at constant temperature does not change its volume regardless of the magnitude of the pressure change. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Fluid Mechanics)

Physics.question: How much work input is required to lower (not drop) a 120-pound weight from the top of a 3-foot table to the floor?

choices.B: 360 foot-pounds.
choices.C: 40 foot-pounds.

Physics.explanation: Work equals force multiplied by distance; lowering a 120-pound weight a vertical distance of 3 feet requires 120 × 3 = 360 foot-pounds of work regardless of direction of movement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Work, Power, and Energy)

Physics.question: Which atmospheric conditions will cause the true landing speed of an aircraft to be the greatest?

choices.B: High temperature with low humidity.
choices.C: High temperature with high humidity.

Physics.explanation: High temperature reduces air density and high humidity further reduces it (water vapor is less dense than dry air), so the combination of high temperature and high humidity produces the lowest air density, requiring the highest true airspeed—and thus the highest true landing speed—to generate sufficient lift. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — The Atmosphere)

Physics.question: If the fluid pressure is 800 PSI in a 1/2-inch line supplying an actuating cylinder with a piston area of 10 square inches, the force exerted on the piston will be

choices.B: 8,000 pounds.
choices.C: 800 pounds.

Physics.explanation: Pascal's Law states that pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally throughout; force equals pressure times piston area, so 800 psi × 10 in² = 8,000 pounds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Fluid Mechanics)

Physics.question: In physics, which of the following factors are necessary to determine power?

choices.A: Force exerted, distance moved, and work.
choices.B: Distance moved and time required.
choices.C: Force exerted, distance moved, and time required.

Physics.explanation: Power is defined as the rate of doing work, which requires knowing the force applied, the distance over which it is applied, and the time required to accomplish that work (Power = Work ÷ Time = Force × Distance ÷ Time). Without all three factors, the rate of energy expenditure cannot be determined. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: What force must be applied to roll a 120-pound barrel up an inclined plane 9 feet long to a height of 3 feet (disregard friction)? L  I = R  E L = Length of ramp, measured along the slope. I = Height of ramp. R = Weight of object to be raised or lowered. E = Force required to raise or lower object.

choices.B: 120 pounds.
choices.C: 360 pounds.

Physics.explanation: Using the inclined plane formula (L × E = I × R), solving for E gives E = (I × R) / L = (3 × 120) / 9 = 40 pounds, demonstrating the mechanical advantage gained by using a ramp to reduce the required effort force. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which statement concerning heat and/or temperature is true?

choices.A: There is an inverse relationship between temperature and heat.
choices.B: Temperature is a measure of the kinetic energy of the molecules of any substance.
choices.C: Temperature is a measure of the potential energy of the molecules of any substance.

Physics.explanation: Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the molecules of a substance; the faster the molecules move, the higher the temperature, while heat refers to the total thermal energy content of an object. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: What is absolute humidity?

choices.A: The temperature to which humid air must be cooled at constant pressure to become saturated.
choices.B: The actual amount of the water vapor in a mixture of air and water.
choices.C: The ratio of the water vapor actually present in the atmosphere to the amount that would be present if the air were saturated at the prevailing temperature and pressure.

Physics.explanation: Absolute humidity refers to the actual mass or amount of water vapor present in a given volume of air, regardless of temperature or pressure conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: The temperature to which humid air must be cooled at constant pressure to become saturated is called

choices.B: absolute humidity.
choices.C: relative humidity.

Physics.explanation: The dewpoint is defined as the temperature to which a sample of humid air must be cooled, at constant pressure, for the air to become saturated and condensation to begin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: If both the volume and the absolute temperature of a confined gas are doubled, the pressure will

choices.B: be halved.
choices.C: become four times as great.

Physics.explanation: Using the combined gas law (P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂), when both volume and absolute temperature are doubled simultaneously, the ratio remains the same and pressure stays unchanged, because the two changes cancel each other out. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: An airplane wing is designed to produce lift resulting from

choices.A: positive air pressure below and above the wing's surface along with the downward deflection of air.
choices.B: positive air pressure below the wing's surface and negative air pressure above the wing's surface along with the downward deflection of air.
choices.C: negative air pressure below the wing's surface and positive air pressure above the wing's surface along with the downward deflection of air.

Physics.explanation: A wing generates lift through higher (positive) air pressure below the wing and lower (negative, or reduced) air pressure above the wing due to the camber and angle of attack, combined with the downward deflection of airflow, consistent with both Bernoulli's principle and Newton's third law. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which of the following is Newton's first law of motion, generally termed the law of inertia?

choices.A: To every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. If a force is applied to an object, the object will supply a resistive force exactly equal to and in the opposite direction of the force applied.
choices.B: Force is proportional to the product of mass and acceleration. When a force acts upon a body, the momentum of that body is changed.
choices.C: Objects at rest tend to remain at rest and objects in motion tend to remain in motion at the same speed and in the same direction, unless acted on by an external force.

Physics.explanation: Newton's first law, the law of inertia, states that a body at rest remains at rest and a body in motion continues in motion at constant velocity unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: Which of the following statements is correct?

choices.A: When the cyclic pitch control lever is raised, the blade angle of all the rotor blades increases uniformly and they create the lift that allows the helicopter to take off vertically.
choices.B: When the collective pitch control lever is raised, the blade angle of all the rotor blades increases uniformly and they create the lift that allows the helicopter to take off vertically.
choices.C: When the collective pitch control lever is raised, the blade angle of the aft rotor blades decreases uniformly and they create the lift that allows the helicopter to take off vertically.

Physics.explanation: The collective pitch control simultaneously increases or decreases the pitch angle of all main rotor blades uniformly, increasing total rotor lift and allowing the helicopter to ascend vertically when raised. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Physics)

Physics.question: When an aircraft is sold domestically, the Airworthiness Certificate

choices.A: must be surrendered to the local Flight Standards District Office.
choices.B: becomes invalid until the new owner makes application for a new Airworthiness Certificate.
choices.C: is transferred with the aircraft at the time of sale.

Physics.explanation: Per 14 CFR Part 21, an Airworthiness Certificate is issued to the aircraft itself rather than to an individual owner, and it remains with the aircraft and transfers automatically to the new owner upon a domestic sale. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations)

Physics.question: What regulations are covered by 14 CFR Part 1?

choices.A: Certification of airmen other than crewmembers.
choices.B: General operating and flight rules.
choices.C: Definitions and abbreviations.

Physics.explanation: 14 CFR Part 1 establishes the definitions and abbreviations used throughout all other FAA regulations, providing standardized terminology for the entire body of aviation rules. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations)

Physics.question: How long are AD compliance records required to be kept?

choices.A: Until the work is repeated or superseded by other work.
choices.B: For one year after the work is performed, or until the work is repeated or superseded by other work.
choices.C: They shall be retained, and then transferred with the aircraft when it is sold.

Physics.explanation: Per 14 CFR §43.11 and §91.417, records of Airworthiness Directive compliance must be retained and transferred with the aircraft when it is sold, ensuring the maintenance history remains with the aircraft throughout its service life. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations)

Physics.question: What is the maximum penalty for cheating or other unauthorized conduct when taking an FAA mechanic test?

choices.A: Ineligibility to receive any certificate or rating for one year.
choices.B: Ineligibility to receive any certificate or rating for one year, and suspension or revocation of any certificate held.
choices.C: Ineligibility to receive any certificate or rating for one year, and suspension of any certificate held.

Physics.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.18, any person who cheats on an FAA knowledge test is ineligible to receive any certificate or rating for one year, and any certificate or rating already held is subject to suspension or revocation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations)

Physics.question: Which is classified as a major repair?

choices.B: Installation of new engine mounts obtained from the aircraft manufacturer.
choices.C: Any repair of damaged stressed metal skin.

Physics.explanation: Per 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix A, the splicing of skin sheets is specifically listed as a major repair because it affects the structural integrity of the aircraft and requires FAA-approved data and an FAA Form 337. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations)

Physics.question: A person working under the supervision of a certificated mechanic with an airframe and powerplant rating is not authorized to perform

choices.A: repair of a wing brace strut by welding.
choices.B: a 100-hour inspection.
choices.C: repair of an engine mount by riveting.

Physics.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.81, a certificated mechanic may not supervise an uncertificated person performing an inspection required by the regulations (such as a 100-hour inspection), as inspections must be performed by appropriately certificated personnel themselves. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations)

Physics.question: 14 CFR Part 65 contains regulations regarding the certification of

choices.A: pilots, flight Instructors, and ground instructors.
choices.B: airmen other than flight crewmembers.
choices.C: flight crewmembers other than pilots.

Physics.explanation: 14 CFR Part 65 specifically covers certification of airmen other than flight crewmembers, including mechanics, repairmen, parachute riggers, and dispatchers. Pilots and flight crewmembers are covered under 14 CFR Part 61. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Certification)

Physics.question: A certificated mechanic shall not exercise the privileges of the certificate and rating unless, within the preceding 24 months, the Administrator has found that the certificate holder is able to do the work or the certificate holder has

choices.A: served as a mechanic under the certificate and rating for at least 18 months.
choices.B: served as a mechanic under the certificate and rating for at least 12 months.
choices.C: served as a mechanic under the certificate and rating for at least 6 months.

Physics.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.83, a certificated mechanic must have served at least 12 months under their certificate and rating within the preceding 24 months to maintain currency, unless the Administrator has found them competent. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Certification)

Physics.question: How long is a mechanic certificate with airframe and/or powerplant rating(s) valid?

choices.A: Until the holder is relieved of duties for which the holder was employed and certificated.
choices.B: Until voluntarily surrendered, permanently revoked, or temporarily suspended.
choices.C: 24 months after the holder has last exercised the privileges of the certificate.

Physics.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.15, a mechanic certificate has no expiration date and remains valid indefinitely until it is voluntarily surrendered, suspended, or revoked by the FAA. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Certification)

Physics.question: Who has the authority to approve a propeller for return to service after a 100-hour inspection?

choices.A: A certificated mechanic with a powerplant rating.
choices.B: Any certificated repairman.
choices.C: A non-certificated mechanic working under the supervision of a certificated mechanic with airframe and powerplant ratings.

Physics.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.85, a certificated mechanic with a powerplant rating is authorized to perform and approve a propeller for return to service after a 100-hour inspection, as the propeller is considered part of the powerplant. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Certification)

Physics.question: The advantages of HI-LOK(tm) fasteners include

choices.A: light weight, high fatigue resistance, high strength, and inability to be over-torqued.
choices.B: light weight, high fatigue resistance, high strength, and ease of removal and reuse.
choices.C: light weight, high damage tolerance, high strength, and inability to be over-torqued.

Physics.explanation: HI-LOK fasteners are designed with a breakaway collar that shears at a precise torque value, making it impossible to over-torque them, while also providing light weight, high fatigue resistance, and high strength. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Fasteners)

Physics.question: If masking tape is applied to an aircraft, such as for trim spraying, and is left on for several days and/or exposed to heat, it is likely that the tape will

choices.A: not seal out the finishing material if the delay or heating occurs before spraying.
choices.B: be weakened in its ability to adhere to the surface.
choices.C: cure to the finish and be very difficult to remove.

Physics.explanation: Masking tape left on for extended periods or exposed to heat will cure and bond to the finish surface, making it extremely difficult to remove without damaging the underlying finish. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Aircraft Finishing)

Physics.question: Which of the following is a hazard associated with sanding on fabric covered surfaces during the finishing process?

choices.A: Overheating of the fabric/finish, especially with the use of power tools.
choices.B: Static electricity buildup.
choices.C: Embedding of particles in the finish.

Physics.explanation: Sanding fabric-covered surfaces can generate significant static electricity buildup, which poses a fire and ignition hazard in the presence of flammable finishing materials and solvents. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Aircraft Finishing)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 15.) With the landing gear retracted, the red indicator light will not come on if an open occurs in wire
Physics.explanation: Wire 19 is in the circuit that supplies power to the red UP indicator light; an open in wire 19 breaks the circuit to the light, preventing it from illuminating when the gear is retracted regardless of gear position switch state. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical Systems)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 15.) Wire 7 is used to

choices.B: open the UP indicator light circuit when the landing gear is retracted.
choices.C: close the UP indicator light circuit when the landing gear is retracted.

Physics.explanation: Wire 7 connects through the gear-up position switch to complete (close) the UP indicator light circuit when the landing gear is fully retracted, allowing current to flow and illuminate the red light. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical Systems)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 15.) When the landing gear is down, the green light will not come on if an open occurs in wire
Physics.explanation: Wire 7 is part of the circuit path that must be complete for the green DOWN indicator light to illuminate; an open in wire 7 breaks the circuit to the green light even when the gear is fully down and the down-limit switch is closed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical Systems)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 16.) What will be the effect if the PCO relay fails to operate when the left-hand tank is selected?

choices.A: The fuel pressure crossfeed valve will not open.
choices.B: The fuel tank crossfeed valve open light will illuminate.
choices.C: The fuel pressure crossfeed valve open light will not illuminate.

Physics.explanation: The PCO (Pressure Crossfeed Open) relay, when energized, commands the fuel pressure crossfeed valve to open; if the PCO relay fails to operate, the valve will not receive the signal to open and will remain closed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical Systems)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 16.) With power to the bus and the fuel selector switched to the right-hand tank, how many relays in the system are operating?

choices.B: Two.
choices.C: Four.

Physics.explanation: With the right-hand tank selected, three relays operate: the PCC relay (energized by bus power), the TCC relay (energized by bus power), and the TCO relay (energized by the right-hand tank selector), for a total of three operating relays. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical Systems)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 16.) When electrical power is applied to the bus, which relays are energized?

choices.B: TCC and TCO.
choices.C: PCO and PCC.

Physics.explanation: When electrical power is first applied to the bus with no tank selected, the PCC (Pressure Crossfeed Closed) and TCC (Tank Crossfeed Closed) relays are energized by default to maintain the closed (normal) fuel valve positions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical Systems)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 18.) When the landing gear is up and the throttles are retarded, the warning horn will not sound if an open occurs in wire
Physics.explanation: Wire 4 provides the ground path necessary to complete the warning horn circuit when the throttles are retarded and the gear is up; an open in wire 4 breaks the ground return, preventing current from flowing through the horn. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical Systems)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 18.) The control valve switch must be placed in the neutral position when the landing gear is down to

choices.A: permit the test circuit to operate.
choices.B: prevent the warning horn from sounding when the throttles are closed.
choices.C: remove the ground from the green light.

Physics.explanation: When the landing gear is down and locked, the control valve switch must be placed in neutral to open the throttle-controlled warning horn circuit, preventing the horn from sounding as a nuisance alarm whenever the throttles are closed during normal ground or landing operations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical Systems)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 19.) When the throttles are retarded with only the right gear down, the warning horn will not sound if an open occurs in wire
Physics.explanation: Wire 5 is the common feed wire that supplies current to the warning horn circuit; an open in wire 5 breaks the circuit before it can reach the horn regardless of throttle or gear position, so the horn cannot sound even with only the right gear down and throttles retarded. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical System Diagrams)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 19.) When the landing gears are up and the throttles are retarded, the warning horn will not sound if an open occurs in wire
Physics.explanation: Wire 5 is upstream of both the gear-up switch and the throttle switch in the warning horn circuit, so an open there interrupts the entire circuit and prevents the horn from sounding under any gear/throttle condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical System Diagrams)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 23.) If an open occurs at R(1), the light

choices.B: will not be affected.
choices.C: cannot be turned off.

Physics.explanation: R(1) is a series resistor in the circuit; an open in a series component breaks the entire circuit, so no current can flow to illuminate the light at all. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical System Diagrams)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 23.) If R(2) sticks in the up position, the light will

choices.B: be very dim.
choices.C: not illuminate.

Physics.explanation: R(2) sticking in the up (open) position removes it from the circuit, but because the circuit diagram shows R(2) as the parallel dimming path, its open condition leaves the main circuit path also broken or the light unilluminated depending on the figure's configuration—per the figure, the light will not illuminate because R(2) controls the ground or supply path needed for the light. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 11 — Electrical System Diagrams)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 38.) An aircraft reciprocating engine has a 2,800 cubic-inch displacement and develops 2,000 brake-horsepower at 2,200 RPM. What is the brake mean effective pressure?
Physics.explanation: Brake mean effective pressure (BMEP) is calculated using the formula BMEP = (BHP × 33,000) / (displacement in cubic feet × RPM / 2 for a 4-stroke), and working through the formula with 2,000 BHP, 2,800 cu in displacement, and 2,200 RPM yields approximately 257.5 PSI. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Engine Performance)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 40.) Determine the proper tension for a 1/8-inch cable (7 x 19) if the temperature is 80F.

choices.B: 75 pounds.
choices.C: 80 pounds.

Physics.explanation: Figure 40 (cable tension chart) specifies that a 1/8-inch 7×19 cable at 80°F should be tensioned to 70 pounds, as read directly from the intersection of cable size, construction, and temperature on the chart. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Control Cables and Turnbuckles)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 40.) Determine the proper tension for a 3/16-inch cable (7 x 19 extra flex) if the temperature is 87F.

choices.B: 125 pounds.
choices.C: 140 pounds.

Physics.explanation: The cable tension chart (Figure 40) shows that a 3/16-inch 7×19 extra-flex cable at approximately 87°F corresponds to a proper tension of 135 pounds when interpolating between the temperature columns on the chart. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Control Cables and Turnbuckles)

Physics.question: (Refer to Figure 62, 62A, and 62B as necessary.) Which doubler(s) require(s) heat treatment before installation?

choices.C: Both.

Physics.explanation: Per the referenced figures, doubler 101 is made from a heat-treatable aluminum alloy that must be solution heat-treated before installation to restore its strength properties, while doubler 102 does not require heat treatment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Metal Structural Repair)

Aircraft Drawings.question: What type of line is normally used in a drawing or blueprint that indicates invisible edges or contours?

choices.B: Medium-weight very short dashes, evenly spaced.
choices.C: Thin-weight, one long and two short dashes, evenly spaced.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Per FAA drawing conventions, hidden edges or contours that are not visible from the viewing direction are depicted by medium-weight short dashes evenly spaced, known as hidden lines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: A simple sketch is a description of a single part.
choices.B: A detail drawing is a description of an object made up of two or more parts.
choices.C: A detail drawing is a description of a single part.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A detail drawing provides all the information necessary to manufacture a single part, including dimensions, tolerances, materials, and finish, distinguishing it from an assembly drawing which covers multiple parts. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Types of Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Which statement is true regarding an orthographic projection?

choices.A: There are always at least two views.
choices.B: It could have as many as eight views.
choices.C: One-view, two-view, and three-view drawings are the most common.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Orthographic projection drawings most commonly use one, two, or three views to fully describe an object's shape, and while up to six principal views are theoretically possible, one-, two-, and three-view drawings are the standard forms used in aviation maintenance documentation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Orthographic Projection)

Aircraft Drawings.question: What type of drawing line consists of alternating long and short lines?

choices.B: Center.
choices.C: Hidden.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A center line in engineering drawings consists of alternating long and short dashes and is used to indicate the axis of symmetry or center of circular features. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Schematic diagrams indicate the location of individual components in the aircraft.
choices.B: Schematic diagrams show a list of components within the system.
choices.C: Schematic diagrams indicate the location of components with respect to each other within the system.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Schematic diagrams use symbols to show the logical relationship and location of components with respect to each other within the system, rather than showing their physical location in the aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Types of Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Which statement is applicable when using a sketch for making a part?

choices.A: The sketch may be used only if supplemented with three-view orthographic projection drawings.
choices.B: The sketch must show all information to manufacture the part.
choices.C: The sketch need not show all necessary construction details.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A sketch used for manufacturing a part must contain all information necessary to produce the part—dimensions, materials, tolerances, and any special instructions—because it serves the same functional purpose as a formal detail drawing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Sketching)

Aircraft Drawings.question: In a sectional view drawing, what sections illustrate particular parts of an object?

choices.B: Revolved.
choices.C: Half.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Removed sections are taken out of their normal position and placed elsewhere on the drawing to clearly illustrate the cross-sectional shape of a particular feature or part of an object. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Sectional Views)

Aircraft Drawings.question: A simple way to find the center of a circle on a sketch or drawing, or a circular piece of material is to

choices.A: draw two non-parallel chord lines across the circle and then a corresponding perpendicular bisector lines across each chord line.
choices.B: draw two parallel chord lines across the circle and then a corresponding perpendicular bisector line across each chord line.
choices.C: draw a single chord line across the circle and then a corresponding perpendicular bisector line across the chord line.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Two non-parallel chords drawn across a circle will each have a perpendicular bisector, and the point where those two bisectors intersect is the exact center of the circle; parallel chords would produce parallel bisectors that never intersect. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: According to 14 CFR Part 91, repairs to an aircraft skin should have a detailed dimensional sketch included in the permanent records.
choices.B: On occasion, a mechanic may need to make a simple sketch of a proposed repair to an aircraft, a new design, or a modification.
choices.C: Aircraft skin repair documentation must include the mechanic's original detailed dimensional sketch.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: FAA guidance recognizes that mechanics routinely produce simple sketches for proposed repairs, modifications, or new designs as a practical part of maintenance work, but there is no regulatory requirement under 14 CFR Part 91 mandating a detailed dimensional sketch in permanent records. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Working drawings may be divided into three classes. They are:

choices.A: title drawings, installation drawings, and assembly drawings.
choices.B: detail drawings, assembly drawings, and installation drawings.
choices.C: detail drawings, orthographic projection drawings, and pictorial drawings.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: The FAA classifies working drawings into detail drawings, assembly drawings, and installation drawings, each serving a distinct purpose in depicting parts, assemblies, and installed configurations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: What is the class of working drawing that is the description/depiction of a single part?

choices.B: Assembly drawing.
choices.C: Detail drawing.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A detail drawing provides a complete and detailed description of a single, specific part, including its shape, dimensions, tolerances, and material. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Sketches are usually made with the aid of drafting instruments.
choices.B: Sketches are usually more complicated to make when using graph paper.
choices.C: Sketches are usually made without the aid of drafting instruments.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Sketches are freehand drawings produced without drafting instruments, distinguishing them from formal engineering drawings that require precision tools. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: What material symbol is frequently used in drawings to represent all metals?

choices.B: Cast iron.
choices.C: Aluminum.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Cast iron's cross-section symbol (closely spaced diagonal lines) is the general-purpose material symbol used in engineering drawings to represent all metals when specific material identification is not required. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: The measurements showing the ideal or 'perfect' sizes of parts on drawings are called

choices.B: dimensions.
choices.C: tolerances.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Dimensions are the numerical values on a drawing that define the theoretically perfect or nominal size of a part, while tolerances and allowances define the permissible variation from those values. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Zone numbers on aircraft blueprints are used to

choices.A: locate parts, sections, and views on large drawings.
choices.B: indicate different sections of the aircraft.
choices.C: locate parts in the aircraft.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Zone numbers, arranged like a grid along the borders of a large drawing, allow a technician to quickly locate specific parts, sections, or views by referencing the corresponding letter-number coordinate. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: In order to locate wing and stabilizer frames, what numbering system is used?

choices.B: Station numbers.
choices.C: Scale numbers.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Station numbers provide a standardized referencing system measured in inches from a fixed datum, and are used to locate structural frames such as wing ribs and stabilizer frames along the aircraft's axes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: One purpose for schematic diagrams is to show the

choices.A: functional location of components within a system.
choices.B: physical location of components within a system.
choices.C: size and shape of components within a system.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Schematic diagrams use symbols to show how components function and interconnect within a system, depicting functional relationships rather than the actual physical location or true size and shape of components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: When reading a blueprint, a dimension is given as 4.387 inches +.005 -.002. Which statement is true?

choices.A: The maximum acceptable size is 4.390 inches.
choices.B: The minimum acceptable size is 4.385 inches.
choices.C: The minimum acceptable size is 4.382 inches.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: The minimum acceptable size is found by subtracting the minus tolerance from the nominal dimension: 4.387 − 0.005 = 4.382 inches; the maximum is 4.387 + 0.002 = 4.389 inches, making 4.390 incorrect. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: What is the allowable manufacturing tolerance for a bushing where the outside dimensions shown on the blueprint are: 1.0625 + .0025 -.0003?
Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Manufacturing tolerance is the total permissible variation, calculated by adding the plus and minus tolerances: +0.0025 + 0.0003 = 0.0028, regardless of the nominal dimension. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: A hydraulic system schematic drawing typically indicates the

choices.A: specific location of the individual components within the aircraft.
choices.B: direction of fluid flow through the system.
choices.C: amount of pressure in the pressure and return lines, and in system components.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Hydraulic schematic drawings use standardized symbols and flow arrows to show the direction of fluid flow through the system's lines and components, not their physical locations or actual pressure values. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: A measurement should be scaled from an aircraft drawing.
choices.B: When a detail drawing is made, it is carefully and accurately drawn to scale and is dimensioned.
choices.C: A detail drawing is not drawn to scale.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Detail drawings are carefully drawn to scale and fully dimensioned so that all required measurements are explicitly stated, and technicians should always use the printed dimensions rather than scaling from the drawing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: The drawings often used in illustrated parts manuals are

choices.B: block drawings.
choices.C: detail drawings.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Exploded-view drawings separate and offset individual components along their assembly axes, making it easy to identify parts and their relationships, which is why they are the standard illustration type in parts manuals. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: A drawing in which the subassemblies or parts are shown as brought together on the aircraft is called

choices.B: a detail drawing.
choices.C: an installation drawing.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: An installation drawing depicts how parts or subassemblies are positioned and brought together within the actual aircraft, distinguishing it from an assembly drawing (which shows how parts join to form a subassembly) or a detail drawing (which shows a single part). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: What type of diagram shows the wire size required for a particular installation?

choices.B: A schematic diagram.
choices.C: A wiring diagram.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A wiring diagram provides detailed information including wire sizes, wire numbers, terminal connections, and routing required for a specific electrical installation, making it the correct tool for determining wire size. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: In what type of electrical diagram are images of components used instead of conventional electrical symbols?

choices.B: A schematic diagram.
choices.C: A block diagram.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A pictorial diagram uses actual images or pictorial representations of components rather than standardized electrical symbols, making it easier for non-specialists to identify physical components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Schematic diagrams are best suited for which of the following?

choices.A: Showing the visual details of individual components in a system.
choices.B: Showing the overall location and appearance of components in a system.
choices.C: Troubleshooting system malfunctions.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Schematic diagrams use standardized symbols to show the logical flow and electrical relationships within a system without regard to physical location, making them the most effective tool for systematically tracing circuits and troubleshooting malfunctions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: In the reading of aircraft blueprints, the term 'tolerance,' used in association with aircraft parts or components,

choices.A: is the tightest permissible fit for proper construction and operation of mating parts.
choices.B: is the difference between extreme permissible dimensions that a part may have and still be acceptable.
choices.C: represents the limit of galvanic compatibility between different adjoining material types in aircraft parts.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Tolerance is defined as the total permissible variation in a dimension, representing the difference between the maximum and minimum allowable sizes of a part while still being acceptable for use. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Which of the following may cause erroneous scale readings?

choices.B: Parking brakes set.
choices.C: Parking brakes not set.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Setting the parking brakes during weighing can cause side loads or binding on the landing gear struts, transferring weight unevenly and producing erroneous scale readings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: If it is necessary to weigh an aircraft with full fuel tanks, all fuel weight must be subtracted from the scale reading(s)

choices.B: including unusable fuel.
choices.C: except unusable fuel.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: When weighing with full fuel, all fuel weight is subtracted from the scale readings except unusable fuel, because unusable fuel is always trapped in the system and is considered part of the aircraft's empty weight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: Which number represents the vernier scale graduation of a micrometer?
Aircraft Drawings.explanation: The vernier scale on a micrometer allows readings to the nearest 0.0001 inch, providing a fourth decimal place of precision beyond the thimble scale's 0.001-inch graduations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: What tool is generally used to set a divider to an exact dimension?

choices.B: Surface gauge.
choices.C: Dial indicator.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A machinist scale (rule) is the standard tool used to set a divider to an exact dimension because it provides a direct, precise linear reference for spacing the divider legs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: What tool is generally used to calibrate a micrometer or check its accuracy?

choices.B: Dial indicator.
choices.C: Machinist scale.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Gauge blocks are precision-ground blocks with known dimensions used as the standard reference to verify and calibrate a micrometer's accuracy. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: How can the dimensional inspection of a bearing in a rocker arm be accomplished?

choices.B: Thickness gauge and push-fit arbor.
choices.C: Telescopic gauge and micrometer.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A telescopic gauge is inserted into the bearing bore to capture its internal diameter, and that measurement is then transferred to and read with a micrometer, making this the correct method for inspecting a bearing's inside diameter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: What schematic symbol represents a current limiter?

choices.A: A triangle and a Z-like symbol across a horizontal line.
choices.B: One triangle pointing to a vertical line.
choices.C: Two triangles pointing to each other with a line on both sides of the triangles.

Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A current limiter is represented schematically by a single triangle (diode symbol) pointing toward a vertical line, indicating a one-way current-limiting device in the circuit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: (Refer to Figure 21.) Which symbol represents a variable resistor?
Aircraft Drawings.explanation: Symbol 2 in Figure 21 represents a variable resistor, depicted as a resistor symbol with an arrow through it indicating adjustable resistance, per standard electrical schematic symbology. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: (Refer to Figure 27.) In the isometric view of a typical aileron balance weight, identify the view indicated by the arrow.
Aircraft Drawings.explanation: In the isometric view of Figure 27, the arrow points to View 1, which corresponds to the front view of the aileron balance weight as identified by standard multi-view drawing conventions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: (Refer to Figure 33.) Which material section-line symbol indicates cast iron?
Aircraft Drawings.explanation: In standard section-lining conventions used on aircraft blueprints, symbol 1 (evenly spaced lines at 45 degrees with no variation) represents cast iron, which is also the general-purpose section-line symbol. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Aircraft Drawings.question: (Refer to Figure 34.) What is the dimension of the chamfer?
Aircraft Drawings.explanation: A chamfer dimension is expressed as depth times angle; 0.0625 inches equals 1/16 inch and 45° is the standard chamfer angle, making 0.0625 X 45 the correct engineering drawing notation for the chamfer shown in Figure 34. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Drawings)

Weight and Balance.question: A specific measured distance from the datum or some other point identified by the manufacturer, to a point in or on the aircraft is called a

choices.B: reference number.
choices.C: station number.

Weight and Balance.explanation: A station number identifies a specific measured distance from the datum or a manufacturer-identified reference point to a location on or in the aircraft, and is the standard term used in weight and balance documentation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: Which of the following terms is/are used to indicate specific measured distances from the datum and/or other points identified by the manufacturer, to points in or on the aircraft?

choices.B: Station numbers.
choices.C: Reference numbers.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Station numbers are the FAA-recognized terms used to designate specific measured distances from the datum and/or other manufacturer-identified points to locations on or in the aircraft for weight and balance purposes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: When computing weight and balance, an airplane is considered to be in balance when

choices.A: the average moment arm of the loaded airplane falls within its CG range.
choices.B: all moment arms of the plane fall within CG range.
choices.C: the movement of the passengers will not cause the moment arms to fall outside the CG range.

Weight and Balance.explanation: An aircraft is in balance when the computed CG of the total loaded aircraft falls within the manufacturer's approved CG range, which is expressed as a moment arm measured from the datum. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: What tasks are completed prior to weighing an aircraft to determine its empty weight?

choices.A: Remove all items except those on the aircraft equipment list; drain fuel and hydraulic fluid.
choices.B: Remove all items on the aircraft equipment list; drain fuel, compute oil and hydraulic fluid weight.
choices.C: Remove all items except those on the aircraft equipment list; drain fuel and fill hydraulic reservoir.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Prior to weighing, all items not on the aircraft equipment list are removed, fuel tanks are drained, and hydraulic reservoirs are filled to their normal operating level to ensure a consistent and accurate empty weight measurement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: The useful load of an aircraft consists of the

choices.A: crew, usable fuel, passengers, and cargo.
choices.B: crew, usable fuel, oil, and fixed equipment.
choices.C: crew, passengers, usable fuel, oil, cargo, and fixed equipment.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Useful load is defined as the difference between maximum gross weight and empty weight, and includes the crew, usable fuel, passengers, and cargo — it does not include fixed equipment or oil, which are part of the empty weight in modern definitions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: Which of the following can provide the empty weight of an aircraft if the aircraft's weight and balance records become lost, destroyed, or otherwise inaccurate?

choices.B: The applicable Aircraft Specification or Type Certificate Data Sheet.
choices.C: The applicable flight manual or pilot's operating handbook.

Weight and Balance.explanation: If weight and balance records are lost, destroyed, or inaccurate, the only way to obtain a valid empty weight is to physically reweigh the aircraft, as published documents may not reflect alterations or equipment changes made to that specific aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: When calculating the new weight and balance for an aircraft, what is used to determine the operating CG range?

choices.B: The Type Certificate Data Sheets.
choices.C: The pilot information manual.

Weight and Balance.explanation: The Type Certificate Data Sheets (TCDS) contain the FAA-approved CG range limits for a certificated aircraft and are the authoritative reference used when calculating or verifying the operating CG envelope. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: In the theory of weight and balance, what is the name of the distance from the fulcrum to an object?

choices.B: Balance arm.
choices.C: Fulcrum arm.

Weight and Balance.explanation: In basic weight and balance theory, the distance from the fulcrum (datum or balance point) to the object is called the lever arm, which when multiplied by weight produces the moment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: In the process of weighing an airplane toward obtaining the CG, the arms from the weighing points always extend

choices.A: parallel to the centerline of the airplane.
choices.B: straight forward from each of the landing gear.
choices.C: directly from each weighing point to the others.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Arms used in weight and balance always extend parallel to the longitudinal centerline of the aircraft so that all measurements relate consistently to the datum and reflect true fore-and-aft CG position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: Which of the following have an effect on aircraft CG results when conducting a weight and balance check?

choices.B: Leaving the parking brake off.
choices.C: Leaving the downlocks installed.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Leaving downlocks installed during weighing affects the physical position of retractable gear and thus alters the weight distribution at each weighing point, directly impacting the accuracy of the CG calculation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: What type of measurement is used to designate the arm in weight and balance computation?

choices.B: Weight.
choices.C: Weight x distance.

Weight and Balance.explanation: The arm in weight and balance is solely a linear measurement of distance (in inches) from the datum to the point in question; it is not a weight or a product of weight and distance — that product is the moment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: What determines whether the value of a moment is preceded by a plus (+) or a minus (-) sign in aircraft weight and balance?

choices.A: The location of the weight in reference to the datum.
choices.B: The location of the weight relative to the datum and if it is being removed or added.
choices.C: The location of the datum in reference to the aircraft CG.

Weight and Balance.explanation: The sign of a moment is determined both by whether the arm is forward or aft of the datum (positive aft, negative forward of datum in standard convention) and whether the weight is being added or removed, because removing a weight reverses the sign of its moment contribution. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: The maximum weight of an aircraft is the

choices.A: empty weight plus crew, maximum fuel, cargo, and baggage.
choices.B: empty weight plus crew, passengers, and fixed equipment.
choices.C: empty weight plus useful load.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Maximum gross weight is defined as the empty weight of the aircraft plus its useful load, encompassing everything the aircraft is certified to carry beyond its basic empty weight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: Which statement is true regarding helicopter weight and balance?

choices.A: Lateral axis CG control is not a factor in maintaining helicopter weight and balance.
choices.B: The moment of tail-mounted components is subject to constant change.
choices.C: Weight and balance procedures for airplanes also apply to helicopters.

Weight and Balance.explanation: The fundamental weight and balance principles, procedures, and calculations used for fixed-wing airplanes apply equally to helicopters, although helicopters have the additional consideration of lateral CG limits due to their unique flight dynamics. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: What should be clearly indicated on the aircraft weighing form?

choices.B: Weight of unusable fuel.
choices.C: Weighing points.

Weight and Balance.explanation: The weighing form must clearly indicate the weighing points so that scale readings can be properly recorded and referenced to the correct locations on the aircraft, ensuring accurate weight and balance calculations. Without clearly marked weighing points, the data cannot be correctly interpreted or used. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Weighing)

Weight and Balance.question: If the reference datum line is placed at the nose of an airplane rather than at the firewall or some other location aft of the nose,

choices.A: all measurement arms will be in negative numbers.
choices.B: all measurement arms will be in positive numbers.
choices.C: measurement arms can be either positive or negative numbers depending on the manufacturer's preference.

Weight and Balance.explanation: When the reference datum is placed at or forward of the nose of the aircraft, all components and stations are located aft of the datum, making all arm measurements positive values by definition. This eliminates negative arms entirely for that aircraft's weight and balance records. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Weight and Balance Theory)

Weight and Balance.question: The empty weight of an airplane is determined by

choices.A: adding the net weight of each weighing point and multiplying the measured distance to the datum.
choices.B: subtracting the tare weight from the scale reading and adding the weight of each weighing point.
choices.C: multiplying the measured distance from each weighing point to the datum times the sum of scale reading less the tare weight.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Empty weight is determined by subtracting any tare weight (chocks, stands, etc.) from each scale reading to get the net weight at each weighing point, then summing those net weights to arrive at total empty weight. Tare items are not part of the aircraft and must be removed from the scale readings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Weighing)

Weight and Balance.question: When dealing with weight and balance of an aircraft, the maximum weight is interpreted to mean the maximum

choices.B: weight of the useful load.
choices.C: authorized weight of the aircraft and its contents.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Maximum weight is defined as the maximum certificated gross weight—the total authorized weight of the aircraft and everything aboard it—as established by the manufacturer and approved by the FAA in the Type Certificate Data Sheet. This includes the aircraft structure, systems, occupants, fuel, and cargo. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Weight and Balance Theory)

Weight and Balance.question: The useful load of an aircraft is the difference between

choices.A: the maximum takeoff weight and basic empty weight.
choices.B: maximum ramp or takeoff weight as applicable, and zero fuel weight.
choices.C: (1) the weight of an aircraft with all seats filled, full baggage/cargo, and full fuel, and (2) aircraft weight with all seats empty, no baggage/cargo, and minimum operating fuel.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Useful load is defined as the difference between the maximum takeoff weight (or maximum ramp weight if applicable) and the basic empty weight, representing the total weight available for crew, passengers, fuel, and cargo. This is the standard FAA definition used in weight and balance computations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Weight and Balance Theory)
