Weight and Balance.question: When determining the empty weight of an aircraft, certificated under current airworthiness standards (14 CFR Part 23), the oil contained in the supply tank is considered

choices.A: a part of the empty weight.
choices.B: a part of the useful load.
choices.C: the same as the fluid contained in the water injection reservoir.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Under 14 CFR Part 23 airworthiness standards, full engine oil is included in the basic empty weight of the aircraft, unlike older standards where oil was considered part of the useful load. This means the oil tank is considered full when computing empty weight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Weight and Balance Theory)

Weight and Balance.question: Improper loading of a helicopter which results in exceeding either the fore or aft CG limits is hazardous due to the

choices.A: reduction or loss of effective cyclic pitch control.
choices.B: Coriolis effect being translated to the fuselage.
choices.C: reduction or loss of effective collective pitch control.

Weight and Balance.explanation: In a single-rotor helicopter, the cyclic control system compensates for CG displacement by tilting the rotor disc; if the CG exceeds fore or aft limits, the cyclic reaches its mechanical limit and the pilot loses the ability to control rotor disc attitude, making the aircraft uncontrollable. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 10 — Helicopter Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: The maximum weight as used in weight and balance control of a given aircraft can normally be found

choices.A: by adding the weight of full fuel, pilot, passengers, and maximum allowable baggage to the empty weight.
choices.B: in the Aircraft Specification or Type Certificate Data Sheet.
choices.C: by adding the empty weight and payload.

Weight and Balance.explanation: The maximum certificated gross weight for a specific aircraft is an FAA-approved value established during type certification and is officially published in the Aircraft Specifications or Type Certificate Data Sheet (TCDS), which is the authoritative reference for such limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Weight and Balance Theory)

Weight and Balance.question: radio equipment weighing 35 pounds was installed at +95. What is the new empty weight CG?
Weight and Balance.explanation: Adding the new equipment's weight and moment (35 lb × 95 in = 3,325 in-lb) to the existing empty weight and moment, then dividing the new total moment by the new total weight yields a new CG of +34.65 inches. This standard weight and balance addition formula is used whenever equipment is installed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Record Keeping)

Weight and Balance.question: The CG range in single-rotor helicopters is

choices.B: approximately the same as the CG range for airplanes.
choices.C: more restricted than for airplanes.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Single-rotor helicopters have a very narrow CG envelope compared to fixed-wing aircraft because the cyclic control has limited authority to compensate for CG displacement, and even small CG deviations can result in loss of effective control. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 10 — Helicopter Weight and Balance)

Weight and Balance.question: An aircraft as loaded weighs 4,954 pounds at a CG of +30.5 inches. The CG range is +32.0 inches to +42.1 inches. Find the minimum weight of the ballast necessary to bring the CG within the CG range. The ballast arm is +162 inches.

choices.B: 30.58 pounds.
choices.C: 57.16 pounds.

Weight and Balance.explanation: To bring the CG to the forward limit of +32.0 inches, the required ballast is calculated using the formula: Ballast = [Aircraft Weight × (CG limit − Current CG)] / (Ballast Arm − CG limit), which yields approximately 61.98 pounds at +162 inches. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Record Keeping)

Weight and Balance.question: As weighed, the total empty weight of an aircraft is 5,862 pounds with a moment of 885,957. However, when the aircraft was weighed, 20 pounds of potable water were on board at +84, and 23 pounds of hydraulic fluid were in a tank located at +101. What is the empty weight CG of the aircraft?
Weight and Balance.explanation: Potable water and hydraulic fluid in a reservoir are not part of the aircraft's empty weight; they must be subtracted from the as-weighed weight and their moments removed from the total moment before dividing to find the true empty weight CG, yielding 150.700. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Weighing)

Weight and Balance.question: radio equipment weighing 30 pounds was installed at +30. What is the new empty weight CG?
Weight and Balance.explanation: Adding the installed equipment weight and moment (30 lb × 30 in = 900 in-lb) to the existing empty weight and moment, then dividing the new total moment by the new total weight produces a new CG of +32.69 inches, following the standard equipment installation weight and balance formula. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Record Keeping)

Weight and Balance.question: In a balance computation of an aircraft from which an item located aft of the datum was removed, use

choices.B: (-)weight X (-)arm = (+)moment.
choices.C: (+)weight X (-)arm = (-)moment.

Weight and Balance.explanation: When an item is removed from the aircraft, its weight is treated as negative (subtracted); if the item was located aft of the datum, the arm is positive, so the resulting moment is negative (−weight × +arm = −moment), which correctly reduces the total moment in the weight and balance computation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Record Keeping)

Weight and Balance.question: All other things being equal, if an item of useful load located aft of an aircraft's CG is removed, the aircraft's CG change will be

choices.A: aft in proportion to the weight of the item and its location in the aircraft.
choices.B: forward in proportion to the weight of the item and its location in the aircraft.
choices.C: forward in proportion to the weight of the item, regardless of its location in the aircraft.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Removing a weight that is located aft of the CG eliminates a moment that was pulling the CG rearward, so the CG shifts forward by an amount proportional to both the weight removed and its distance from the original CG. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Record Keeping)

Weight and Balance.question: Removable ballast (146 pounds at +380). What is the empty weight CG of the aircraft described above?

choices.B: 60.31 inches.
choices.C: 58.54 inches.

Weight and Balance.explanation: To find the empty weight CG, the removable ballast moment (146 lbs × 380 in = 55,480 in-lbs) must be included in the total moment, and the total moment divided by the total weight yields the CG of approximately 62.92 inches. Removable ballast is included in the empty weight calculation when it is installed and accounted for in the weight and balance records. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Theory)

Weight and Balance.question: When making a rearward weight and balance check to determine that the CG will not exceed the rearward limit during extreme conditions, the items of useful load which should be computed at their minimum weights are those located forward of the

choices.B: datum.
choices.C: rearward CG limit.

Weight and Balance.explanation: When performing a rearward CG check, items forward of the rearward CG limit are computed at minimum weights because reducing forward weight/moment produces the most adverse (rearward) CG condition, confirming the worst-case scenario does not exceed the limit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Adverse Loading)

Weight and Balance.question: When, or under what condition(s) are adverse loading checks conducted?

choices.A: At or below the maximum gross weight of the aircraft.
choices.B: Anytime a repair or alteration causes EWCG to fall outside the CG range.
choices.C: At specified flight hour or calendar time intervals.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Adverse loading checks are conducted to verify the aircraft CG remains within limits under the most unfavorable loading conditions, and these checks must be performed at or below the maximum certificated gross weight to remain within the approved operating envelope. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Adverse Loading)

Weight and Balance.question: When accomplishing loading computations for a small aircraft, necessary information obtained from the weight and balance records would include

choices.A: unusable fuel weight and distance from datum.
choices.B: weight and location of permanent ballast.
choices.C: current empty weight and empty weight CG.

Weight and Balance.explanation: When computing operational loading for a small aircraft, the current empty weight and empty weight CG from the weight and balance records serve as the baseline from which all useful load additions are calculated. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Records)

Weight and Balance.question: When an empty aircraft is weighed, the combined net weight at the main gears is 3,540 pounds with an arm of 195.5 inches. At the nose gear, the net weight is 2,322 pounds with an arm of 83.5 inches. The datum line is forward of the nose of the aircraft. What is the empty CG of the aircraft?
Weight and Balance.explanation: The empty CG is found by summing all moments (3,540 × 195.5 = 692,070 + 2,322 × 83.5 = 193,887; total = 885,957) and dividing by total weight (5,862 lbs), yielding approximately 151.1 inches from the datum. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Theory)

Weight and Balance.question: radio equipment weighing 25 pounds was installed at +85. What is the new empty weight CG?
Weight and Balance.explanation: The new empty weight CG is calculated by adding the new item's moment (25 lbs × 85 in = 2,125 in-lbs) to the existing total moment, then dividing by the new total weight, resulting in a CG of approximately +30.30 inches. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Computations)

Weight and Balance.question: The following alteration was performed on an aircraft: A model B engine weighing 175 pounds was replaced by a model D engine weighing 185 pounds at a -62.00-inch station. The aircraft weight and balance records show the previous empty weight to be 998 pounds and an empty weight CG of 13.48 inches. What is the new empty weight CG?

choices.B: 14.25 inches.
choices.C: 12.73 inches.

Weight and Balance.explanation: The weight change is +10 lbs at -62.00 inches, producing a moment change of -620 in-lbs; adding this to the original total moment (998 × 13.48 = 13,453.04) and dividing by the new weight (1,008 lbs) yields a new CG of approximately 13.96 inches. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Computations)

Weight and Balance.question: If the empty weight CG of an airplane lies within the empty weight CG limits,

choices.A: it is necessary to calculate the CG at gross weight.
choices.B: it is not necessary to calculate the CG location at gross weight.
choices.C: minimum fuel should be used in both forward and rearward CG checks.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Even if the empty weight CG lies within the empty weight CG limits, the distribution of useful load can shift the loaded CG outside the approved range, so a gross weight CG calculation is always required before flight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weight and Balance Checks)

Weight and Balance.question: When computing the maximum forward loaded CG of an aircraft, minimum weights, arms, and moments should be used for items of useful load that are located aft of the

choices.B: forward CG limit.
choices.C: datum.

Weight and Balance.explanation: For a forward CG check, items aft of the forward CG limit are computed at minimum weights and moments because reducing aft moment moves the CG further forward, creating the most adverse forward CG condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Adverse Loading)

Weight and Balance.question: Find the empty weight CG location for the following tricycle-gear aircraft. Each main wheel weighs 753 pounds, nosewheel weighs 22 pounds, distance between nosewheel and main wheels is 87.5 inches, nosewheel location is +9.875 inches from datum, with 1 gallon of hydraulic fluid at -21.0 inches included in the weight scale.

choices.B: +95.61 inches.
choices.C: +96.11 inches.

Weight and Balance.explanation: The empty weight CG is found by computing total weight (753 + 753 + 22 = 1,528 lbs, less the hydraulic fluid tare), determining each component's moment from the datum (nosewheel at +9.875, main wheels at +9.875 + 87.5 = +97.375), summing all moments, and dividing by total weight, yielding approximately +96.11 inches. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Weighing the Aircraft)

Weight and Balance.question: An aircraft's LEMAC and TEMAC are defined in terms of distance

choices.B: from each other.
choices.C: ahead of and behind the wing center of lift, respectively.

Weight and Balance.explanation: LEMAC (Leading Edge of Mean Aerodynamic Chord) and TEMAC (Trailing Edge of Mean Aerodynamic Chord) are both defined as distances measured from the aircraft datum, establishing their fixed reference positions for CG and MAC calculations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Mean Aerodynamic Chord)

Weight and Balance.question: If an aircraft CG is found to be at 24 percent of MAC, that 24 percent is an expression of the

choices.B: distance from the LEMAC.
choices.C: distance from the Datum.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Percent MAC is measured from the Leading Edge of the Mean Aerodynamic Chord (LEMAC), so a CG at 24% MAC means the CG is located 24% of the MAC length aft of LEMAC. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Mean Aerodynamic Chord)

Weight and Balance.question: An aircraft Type Certificate Data Sheet contains

choices.A: maximum fuel grade to be used.
choices.B: control surface adjustment points.
choices.C: location of the datum.

Weight and Balance.explanation: The Type Certificate Data Sheet (TCDS) specifies the location of the datum as a reference for all weight and balance measurements, along with other type design data required by the FAA. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Type Certificate Data Sheets)

Weight and Balance.question: What information is generally contained in Aircraft Specifications or Type Certificate Data Sheets?

choices.B: Useful load of aircraft.
choices.C: Control surface movements.

Weight and Balance.explanation: Aircraft Specifications and Type Certificate Data Sheets include control surface movement limits (deflection ranges) as part of the type design data, which are standardized values applicable to all aircraft of that type. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 4 — Type Certificate Data Sheets)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: What is used to indicate that a surface must be machine finished?

choices.B: Leader lines.
choices.C: Finished marks.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Finish marks (also called surface finish symbols) are placed on engineering drawings to indicate that a surface requires machining to a specified finish, distinguishing it from surfaces left in an as-cast or as-forged condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Drawing Symbols and Conventions)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: A primary reason why ordinary or otherwise nonapproved cleaning compounds should not be used when washing aircraft is because their use can result in

choices.B: hydrogen embrittlement in nonmetallic materials.
choices.C: a general inability to remove compound residues.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Non-approved cleaning compounds can cause hydrogen embrittlement in metal structures by introducing atomic hydrogen into the metal during the cleaning process, weakening the material and potentially causing cracking under stress. FAA standards require only approved cleaning agents to prevent this hazard. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: How may magnesium engine parts be cleaned?

choices.A: Soak in a 20 percent caustic soda solution.
choices.B: Spray with methyl ethyl ketone (MEK).
choices.C: Wash with a commercial solvent, decarbonize, and scrape or grit blast.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Magnesium parts must not be exposed to caustic solutions or aggressive solvents like MEK that can attack the base metal; instead, they are cleaned with approved commercial solvents, decarbonized, and then have deposits removed by scraping or grit blasting to avoid damaging the material. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: When an anodized surface coating is damaged in service, it can be partially restored by

choices.A: applying a thin coat of zinc chromate primer.
choices.B: chemical surface treatment.
choices.C: use of a suitable mild cleaner.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Anodizing is an electrochemical process that cannot be fully replicated in the field, but chemical surface treatments (such as Alodine/Bonderite) can partially restore corrosion protection to damaged anodized surfaces by forming a conversion coating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Select the solvent recommended for wipedown of cleaned surfaces just before painting.

choices.B: Dry-cleaning solvent.
choices.C: Aromatic naptha.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Aliphatic naphtha is the FAA-recommended solvent for final wipedown of surfaces immediately before painting because it evaporates cleanly, leaves no residue, and does not attack most finishes or substrates the way aromatic naphtha can. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Which of the following are acceptable to use when utilizing chemical cleaning agents on aircraft?

choices.A: Cotton fiber wiping cloths when using a flammable agent and atomizing spray equipment.
choices.B: Microfiber wiping cloths when using a flammable agent and atomizing spray equipment.
choices.C: Synthetic fiber wiping cloths when using a flammable agent.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Synthetic fiber cloths are acceptable with flammable agents because they do not generate the static electricity hazard that cotton fiber cloths can, and atomizing spray equipment must not be used with flammable agents due to the risk of ignition from mist dispersion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Select the solvent used to clean acrylics and rubber.

choices.B: Methyl ethyl ketone.
choices.C: Aromatic naphtha.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Aliphatic naphtha is the correct solvent for cleaning acrylics and rubber because it is mild enough not to attack or craze these materials, whereas MEK and aromatic naphtha are too aggressive and will damage them. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Fayed surfaces cause concern in chemical cleaning because of the danger of

choices.B: entrapping corrosive materials.
choices.C: corrosion by imbedded iron oxide.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Fayed surfaces—overlapping or mating surfaces—create capillary spaces where chemical cleaning agents can become trapped and continue to attack the metal long after the cleaning operation is complete, making entrapment of corrosive materials the primary concern. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Caustic cleaning products used on aluminum structures have the effect of producing

choices.B: improved corrosion resistance.
choices.C: corrosion.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Caustic (alkaline) cleaning products chemically attack aluminum by breaking down its natural protective oxide layer and reacting with the base metal, leading to active corrosion rather than protection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Fretting corrosion is most likely to occur

choices.A: when two surfaces fit tightly together but can move relative to one another.
choices.B: only when two dissimilar metals are in contact.
choices.C: when two surfaces fit loosely together and can move relative to one another.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Fretting corrosion occurs when two tightly fitting surfaces experience slight relative motion, causing microscopic abrasion that removes protective oxide films and produces fine metallic debris that oxidizes, creating a characteristic reddish-brown powder on ferrous metals. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: The rust or corrosion that occurs with most metals is the result of

choices.A: a tendency for them to return to their natural state.
choices.B: blocking the flow of electrons in homogenous metals, or between dissimilar metals.
choices.C: electron flow in or between metals from cathodic to anodic areas.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Most metals exist in nature as stable compounds (ores) and corrosion is the natural electrochemical process by which refined metals revert to their lower energy, combined state, driven by their thermodynamic tendency to return to a natural ore-like condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Which of the following are the desired effects of using Alodine(r) (also known as Bonderite(r)) on aluminum alloy?

choices.A: A smooth painting surface and increased corrosion resistance.
choices.B: Relieved surface stresses.
choices.C: A slightly rough surface and increased corrosion resistance.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Alodine (Bonderite) chemical conversion coating produces a smooth, adherent chromate film on aluminum that provides both an excellent base for paint adhesion and significantly increased corrosion resistance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Intergranular corrosion in aluminum alloy parts

choices.A: may be detected by surface pitting, and white, powdery deposit formed on the surface of the metal.
choices.B: commonly appears as threadlike filaments of corrosion products under a dense film of paint.
choices.C: cannot always be detected by surface indications.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Intergranular corrosion attacks along grain boundaries beneath the surface and can be well advanced before any visible surface indication appears, making it particularly dangerous because it may not be detectable by visual inspection alone. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: A primary cause of intergranular corrosion is

choices.B: dissimilar metal contact.
choices.C: improper application of primer.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Improper heat treatment can cause alloying elements to precipitate out along grain boundaries, creating localized galvanic cells that make those boundaries preferentially susceptible to intergranular corrosion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Corrosion should be removed from magnesium parts with a

choices.B: silicon carbide brush.
choices.C: carborundum abrasive.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Magnesium is extremely susceptible to damage from metallic tools and abrasives; a stiff nonmetallic brush is used to remove corrosion products without embedding foreign metallic particles that would accelerate further galvanic corrosion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Which of the following is an acceptable first step procedure to help prevent scratching when cleaning a transparent plastic surface?

choices.A: Gently wipe the surface with a clean, dry, soft cloth.
choices.B: Flush the surface with clean water.
choices.C: Gently wipe the surface with a clean, soft cloth moistened with de-mineralized or distilled water.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: The first step when cleaning transparent plastics is to flush the surface with clean water to float away abrasive particles and grit that would scratch the soft acrylic surface if wiping were performed first. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Cleaning and Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Galvanic corrosion is likely to be most rapid and severe when

choices.A: the surface area of the cathodic metal is smaller than surface area of the anodic metal.
choices.B: the surface areas of the anodic and cathodic metals are approximately the same.
choices.C: the surface area of the anodic metal is smaller than the surface area of the cathodic metal.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: When the anodic (less noble) metal has a smaller surface area than the cathodic metal, the corrosion current is concentrated over a smaller area, causing accelerated and more severe metal loss at the anode. This large-cathode/small-anode ratio drives a high current density at the anodic site, intensifying galvanic attack. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Corrosion caused by galvanic action is the result of

choices.B: contact between two unlike metals.
choices.C: excessive etching.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Galvanic corrosion requires an electrolyte and two dissimilar metals in electrical contact, forming an electrochemical cell in which the more anodic metal corrodes. The difference in electrochemical potential between the two metals drives the corrosive current flow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: The interior surface of sealed structural steel tubing would be best protected against corrosion by which of the following?

choices.A: Charging the tubing with dry nitrogen prior to sealing.
choices.B: Evacuating moisture from the tubing before sealing.
choices.C: A coating of linseed oil.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Charging sealed steel tubing with dry nitrogen displaces oxygen and moisture, eliminating the electrochemical environment necessary for corrosion to occur inside the sealed cavity. Dry nitrogen is inert and will not support oxidation or moisture-driven corrosion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: In the corrosion process, it is the cathodic area or dissimilar cathodic material that corrodes.
choices.B: In the Galvanic or Electro-Chemical Series for metals, the most anodic metals are those that will give up electrons most easily.
choices.C: In the Galvanic or Electro-Chemical Series for metals, the most cathodic metals are those that will give up electrons most easily.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: In the galvanic/electrochemical series, anodic (active) metals are those that most readily give up electrons, making them the metals that corrode; cathodic (noble) metals resist electron loss and are protected at the expense of the anode. It is the anodic area, not the cathodic area, that corrodes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Corrosion Control)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: What is used to slow the drying time of some finishes and to prevent blush?

choices.B: Retarder.
choices.C: Rejuvenator.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: A retarder is a slow-evaporating solvent added to finishes to extend drying/flash time, which allows trapped moisture to escape and prevents the milky, whitish condition known as blushing. A reducer simply thins material without specifically addressing drying rate for blush prevention. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Which type of coating typically includes phosphoric acid as one of its components at the time of application?

choices.B: Epoxy primer.
choices.C: Zinc chromate primer.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Wash primer (also called pretreatment or etch primer) contains phosphoric acid as a key component that chemically etches the bare metal surface, promoting adhesion and providing a degree of corrosion inhibition before topcoat application. Epoxy and zinc chromate primers do not rely on phosphoric acid as a constituent. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Which properly applied finish topcoat is the most durable and chemical resistant?

choices.B: Acrylic lacquer.
choices.C: Polyurethane.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Polyurethane topcoats form a cross-linked polymer film that provides superior hardness, gloss retention, UV resistance, and resistance to aviation fuels, hydraulic fluids, and other chemicals compared to synthetic enamels or acrylic lacquers. The FAA recognizes polyurethane as the most durable and chemically resistant finish topcoat. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: A correct use for acetone is to

choices.B: remove grease from fabric.
choices.C: thin dope.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Acetone is an effective degreasing solvent suitable for cleaning grease from fabric prior to finishing, but it is too aggressive to use as a thinner for zinc chromate primer or dope and would attack those coatings improperly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: When humidity is low, what is likely to occur if unhydrated wash primer is applied to unpainted aluminum and then, about 30 to 40 minutes later, a finish topcoat is applied?

choices.B: A glossy, blush-free finish.
choices.C: A dull finish due to the topcoat 'sinking in' to primer that is still too soft.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Wash primer requires moisture (humidity) to fully hydrate its phosphoric acid component and properly etch and bond to the metal; when humidity is low and the primer remains unhydrated, applying a topcoat traps the unreacted acid against the metal surface, causing corrosion rather than protection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Fungicidal dopes are used in aircraft finishing as the

choices.A: first, full-bodied, brushed-on coat to prevent fungus damage.
choices.B: first coat to prevent fabric rotting and are applied thin enough to saturate the fabric.
choices.C: final, full-bodied, brushed-on coat to reduce blushing.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Fungicidal dope is applied as the first coat on aircraft fabric, thinned sufficiently to fully penetrate and saturate the fabric fibers, where it inhibits fungal growth and fabric rot before subsequent coats build up the finish. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Before applying a protective coating to any unpainted clean aluminum, the aviation mechanic should

choices.A: wipe the surface with methyl ethyl ketone (MEK).
choices.B: remove any contaminants from the surface with a paper towel.
choices.C: avoid touching the surface with bare hands.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Skin oils and perspiration from bare hands contaminate cleaned aluminum surfaces, preventing proper adhesion of primers and coatings and potentially initiating corrosion under the finish; the mechanic must avoid bare-hand contact to maintain a contamination-free surface. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: When humidity is low, what is likely to occur if hydrated wash primer is applied to unpainted aluminum and then, 30 to 40 minutes later, a finish top coat is applied?

choices.B: A glossy, blush-free finish.
choices.C: A dull finish due to the topcoat 'sinking in' to primer that is still too soft.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: When wash primer is properly hydrated (sufficient moisture has reacted with the phosphoric acid) and topcoated within the correct window, the primer has etched the surface and formed a stable bond, yielding a glossy, blush-free finish because conditions are controlled and the primer chemistry is complete. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: What is the usual cause of runs and sags in aircraft finishes?

choices.A: Too much material applied in one coat.
choices.B: Material is being applied too fast.
choices.C: Low atmospheric humidity.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Runs and sags result from applying an excessive amount of coating material in a single pass, causing the wet film to exceed its ability to remain in place before it can flash off or cure. Proper technique calls for thin, even coats to avoid this condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Which defect in aircraft finishes may be caused by adverse humidity, drafts, or sudden changes in temperature?

choices.B: Blushing.
choices.C: Pinholes.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Blushing is a milky or cloudy appearance caused by moisture condensing in or on the finish film, which occurs when high humidity, cold drafts, or sudden temperature changes cause rapid solvent evaporation that chills the film surface below the dew point. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.question: Which statement is true regarding paint system compatibility?

choices.A: Old-type zinc chromate primer may not be used directly for touchup of bare metal surfaces.
choices.B: Acrylic nitrocellulose lacquers may be used over old nitrocellulose finishes.
choices.C: Old wash primer coats may be overcoated directly with epoxy finishes.

Cleaning and Corrosion Control.explanation: Acrylic nitrocellulose lacquers are compatible with and can be applied directly over existing nitrocellulose lacquer finishes because both use similar solvent and resin systems that bond without lifting or crazing the old coat. Incompatible combinations such as epoxy over old wash primer or certain primers over bare metal require additional preparation steps. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Painting and Finishing)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: What is meant by the term "residual fuel"?

choices.A: A known amount of fuel left in the tanks, lines, and engine.
choices.B: The fuel remaining in the tanks, lines, and engine after draining.
choices.C: The fuel remaining in the tank, lines, and engine before draining.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Residual fuel is specifically defined as the fuel that remains in the tanks, lines, and engine after draining has occurred, distinguishing it from unusable fuel (which cannot be drained) and total fuel on board before draining. This post-drain remainder is what is accounted for in weight and balance calculations as residual fuel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Weight and Balance)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: Maximum zero fuel weight is the

choices.A: dry weight plus the weight of full crew, passengers, and cargo.
choices.B: basic operating weight without crew, fuel, and cargo.
choices.C: maximum permissible weight of a loaded aircraft (passengers, crew, and cargo) without fuel.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Maximum zero fuel weight is the maximum allowable weight of the aircraft loaded with passengers, crew, and cargo but excluding any usable fuel, meaning it is the heaviest the aircraft may be with zero fuel on board. Any weight above this value must consist solely of usable fuel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Weight and Balance)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: Most modern aircraft are designed so that if all seats are occupied, full baggage weight is carried, and all fuel tanks are full, what will be the weight condition of the aircraft?

choices.A: It will be in excess of maximum takeoff weight.
choices.B: It will be at maximum basic operating weight.
choices.C: It will be at maximum taxi or ramp weight.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Aircraft are intentionally designed so that simultaneously filling all seats, loading maximum baggage, and filling all fuel tanks would exceed the certified maximum takeoff weight, forcing operators to make trade-offs between fuel, passengers, and cargo loads. This design philosophy ensures flexibility in loading configurations without structural overload in normal operations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Weight and Balance)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: During starting of a turbine powerplant using a compressed air starter, a hung start occurred. Select the proper procedure.

choices.B: Re-engage the starter.
choices.C: Shut the engine down.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: A hung start occurs when the engine lights off but RPM stabilizes below idle speed, meaning the engine is running but not accelerating to self-sustaining speed, which can cause overtemperature and damage; the correct response is to shut the engine down completely to prevent further damage. Simply re-engaging the starter or cutting only fuel does not safely address the running but under-speed condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Starting)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: A hung start in a jet engine is often caused by

choices.B: the starter cutting off too soon.
choices.C: an excessively rich fuel/air mixture.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: A hung start typically results from the starter cutting out or losing sufficient torque before the engine reaches a self-sustaining RPM, leaving the engine running but unable to accelerate through the critical speed threshold. Insufficient starter energy is the most common cause, not ignition failure or fuel mixture issues. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Starting)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: Which of the following conditions has the most potential for causing engine damage when starting or attempting to start a turbine engine?

choices.B: Cold start.
choices.C: Hot start.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: A hot start occurs when the exhaust gas temperature (EGT) exceeds the maximum allowable limit during starting, which can cause immediate and severe damage to turbine blades, combustion liners, and other hot-section components. While a hung start is also dangerous, a hot start presents the greatest potential for serious engine damage due to extreme thermal stress on critical components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Starting)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: How is a flooded engine, equipped with a float-type carburetor, cleared of excessive fuel?

choices.A: Crank the engine with the starter or by hand, with the mixture control in cutoff, ignition switch off, and the throttle fully open, until the fuel charge has been cleared.
choices.B: Turn off the fuel and the ignition. Discontinue the starting attempt until the excess fuel has cleared.
choices.C: Crank the engine with the starter or by hand, with the mixture control in cutoff, ignition switch on, and the throttle fully open, until the excess fuel has cleared or until the engine starts.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: To clear a flooded float-type carburetor engine, the ignition is left ON so the engine can fire and burn off the excess fuel charge while cranking with the throttle fully open and mixture in cutoff, allowing the excess fuel to be expelled and potentially allowing the engine to start. Turning the ignition off would only purge the fuel without the possibility of the engine starting and self-clearing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 10 — Engine Starting)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: Generally, when an induction fire occurs during starting of a reciprocating engine, the first course of action should be to

choices.A: discharge carbon dioxide from a fire extinguisher into the air intake of the engine.
choices.B: continue cranking and start the engine if possible.
choices.C: close the throttle.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: An induction fire during reciprocating engine starting is typically located within the intake system and can often be drawn into the engine and extinguished by continuing to crank and start the engine, which pulls the flames inward where they are consumed. Applying external extinguisher to the intake or closing the throttle may not clear the fire as effectively as allowing the engine to ingest and extinguish it. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 10 — Engine Starting)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: If a fire develops in an engine during the starting procedure, the aviation mechanic should

choices.A: discontinue the start attempt and allow the fireguard to extinguish the fire.
choices.B: continue cranking to start the engine and extinguish the fire.
choices.C: continue cranking and allow the fireguard to extinguish the fire.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: When a fire develops during the engine start procedure, the recommended action is to continue cranking to start the engine, which will draw the fire into the engine and extinguish it by consuming the fuel in the induction system. This is the standard procedure for an induction fire on a reciprocating engine where the engine's suction can clear the fire. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 10 — Engine Starting)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: What effect, if any, will aviation gasoline mixed with jet fuel have on a turbine engine?

choices.B: The tetraethyl lead in the gasoline forms deposits on the turbine blades.
choices.C: The tetraethyl lead in the gasoline forms deposits on the compressor blades.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Aviation gasoline contains tetraethyl lead (TEL) as an anti-knock additive, and when burned in a turbine engine the lead compounds deposit on the turbine blades, causing erosion, corrosion, and significant damage to the hot section. Turbine engines are designed for unleaded kerosene-based fuels and cannot tolerate leaded fuels without serious consequences. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuels and Fuel Systems)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: When towing a large aircraft

choices.A: a person should be in the cockpit to watch for obstructions.
choices.B: persons should be stationed at the nose, each wingtip, and the empennage at all times.
choices.C: a person should be in the cockpit to operate the brakes.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: When towing a large aircraft, a qualified person must be in the cockpit to operate the brakes in the event the tow bar fails or an emergency stop is required, ensuring the aircraft can be controlled and stopped safely. Personnel stationed at wingtips alone are insufficient without brake control capability in the cockpit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: Weathervaning tendency is greatest when taxiing

choices.A: both nosewheel and tailwheel-type airplanes in a quartering tailwind.
choices.B: a tailwheel-type airplane in a direct crosswind.
choices.C: a nosewheel-type airplane in a quartering headwind.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Weathervaning tendency is greatest with a quartering tailwind because the wind acts on the large tail surface area and pushes the tail downwind, causing the nose to swing into the wind, and this effect is most pronounced when the tailwind component is present for both nosewheel and tailwheel aircraft. A direct crosswind or quartering headwind produces less weathervaning moment than a quartering tailwind. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: When taxiing an airplane with a quartering tailwind, the elevators and

choices.A: upwind aileron should be held in the up position.
choices.B: upwind aileron should be held in the down position.
choices.C: both ailerons should be kept in the neutral position.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: With a quartering tailwind, the upwind aileron should be held down (away from the wind) to prevent the wind from lifting the upwind wing, while the elevator is held in a neutral or down position to prevent the tailwind from lifting the tail and nosing the aircraft over. Holding the upwind aileron down deflects it to reduce lift on that wing against the tailwind. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: When taxiing (or towing) an aircraft, a flashing red light from the control tower means

choices.A: stop and wait for a green light.
choices.B: move clear of the runway/taxiway immediately.
choices.C: return to starting point.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: A flashing red light signal from the control tower to an aircraft on the ground means the aircraft must stop and wait for a green light before proceeding, indicating an unsafe condition exists for movement. This is a standard ATC light gun signal codified in FAA regulations and guidance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: When taxiing (or towing) an aircraft, a flashing white light from the control tower means

choices.A: move clear of the runway/taxiway immediately.
choices.B: OK to proceed but use extreme caution.
choices.C: return to starting point.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: A flashing white light signal from the control tower directed at an aircraft on the ground means the aircraft should return to its starting point on the airport, used when ATC needs the aircraft to go back to where it began taxiing. This is a standard FAA light gun signal for ground operations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: When taxiing (or towing) an aircraft, an alternating red and green light from the control tower means

choices.A: move clear of the runway/taxiway immediately.
choices.B: OK to proceed but use extreme caution.
choices.C: return to starting point.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: An alternating red and green light gun signal means 'exercise extreme caution' and that the aircraft may proceed, serving as a cautionary clearance rather than a stop or return command. This light gun signal standard is established for ground operations when radio communication is unavailable. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: When stopping a nosewheel-type airplane after taxiing (or towing), the nosewheel should be left

choices.B: turned at a small angle towards uphill if the parking area is not perfectly flat.
choices.C: pointed straight ahead.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: After taxiing or towing, the nosewheel should be left pointing straight ahead to prevent stress on the nose gear assembly and to ensure the aircraft is properly aligned for the next operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: When first starting to move an aircraft while taxiing, it is important to

choices.B: closely monitor the instruments.
choices.C: notify the control tower.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Testing the brakes immediately after beginning to move confirms they are operational before the aircraft gains speed, which is a critical safety check during taxi operations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: The color of 100LL fuel is

choices.B: colorless or straw.
choices.C: red.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: 100LL (low lead) aviation gasoline is dyed blue to allow visual identification and to prevent misfueling with other fuel grades, as color-coding is the standard identification method for aviation fuels. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Fuel and Oil)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: How are aviation fuels, which possess greater antiknock qualities than 100 octane, classified?

choices.A: According to the milliliters of lead.
choices.B: By reference to normal heptane.
choices.C: By performance numbers.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Fuels with antiknock qualities exceeding the 100-octane scale cannot be measured by the octane rating system alone and are therefore classified by performance numbers, which reflect the fuel's power output relative to isooctane under supercharged conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Fuel and Oil)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: Why is ethylene dibromide added to aviation gasoline?

choices.A: To remove zinc silicate deposits from the spark plugs.
choices.B: To scavenge lead oxide from the cylinder combustion chambers.
choices.C: To increase the antiknock rating of the fuel.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Tetraethyl lead (TEL) is added to avgas as an antiknock agent, but it produces lead oxide deposits; ethylene dibromide is added as a scavenger to combine with lead oxide and form lead bromide, which is then expelled through the exhaust. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Fuel and Oil)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: A fuel that vaporizes too readily may cause

choices.B: detonation.
choices.C: vapor lock.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Fuel with excessively high volatility vaporizes in the fuel lines before reaching the carburetor, creating vapor bubbles that interrupt fuel flow and cause vapor lock. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Fuel and Oil)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: Jet fuel number identifiers are

choices.A: performance numbers to designate the volatility of the fuel.
choices.B: performance numbers and are relative to the fuel's performance in the aircraft engine.
choices.C: type numbers and have no relation to the fuel's performance in the aircraft engine.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Jet fuel designations such as Jet-A, Jet-A1, and Jet-B are type numbers assigned for identification and specification purposes and do not indicate performance levels or octane/performance ratings as reciprocating engine fuel grades do. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Fuel and Oil)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: The main differences between grades 100 and 100LL fuel are

choices.B: volatility, lead content, and color.
choices.C: lead content and color.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Grades 100 and 100LL have the same volatility and octane/performance rating, but differ in lead content (100LL contains less tetraethyl lead) and color (100 is green, 100LL is blue), making lead content and color the distinguishing characteristics. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Fuel and Oil)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: A fuel that does not vaporize readily enough can cause

choices.B: detonation.
choices.C: hard starting.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Fuel with insufficient volatility does not vaporize adequately to form a combustible mixture, particularly in cold conditions, making it difficult or impossible to start the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Fuel and Oil)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: Why is it important not to rotate the crankshaft after the corrosion preventive mixture has been put into the cylinders on engines prepared for storage?

choices.A: Fuel may be drawn into one or more cylinders and dilute or wash off the corrosion preventive mixture.
choices.B: The seal of corrosion preventive mixture will be broken.
choices.C: Engine damage can occur from hydraulic lock.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: After applying corrosion preventive mixture to cylinders, rotating the crankshaft would break the oil film seal that coats and protects the cylinder walls and other internal surfaces from corrosion during storage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations and Servicing)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: A rectangular-shaped fuel tank measures 60 inches in length, 30 inches in width, and 12 inches in depth. How many cubic feet are within the tank?
Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Volume in cubic feet is calculated by converting all dimensions to feet first: 60 in = 5 ft, 30 in = 2.5 ft, 12 in = 1 ft; then 5 × 2.5 × 1 = 12.5 cubic feet. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations and Servicing)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: How many gallons of fuel will be contained in a rectangular-shaped tank which measures 2 feet in width, 3 feet in length, and 1 foot 8 inches in depth? (7.5 gal = 1 cu ft)
Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: The tank volume is 2 ft × 3 ft × 1.667 ft = 10.0 cubic feet; multiplying by 7.5 gal/ft³ gives 75 gallons. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations and Servicing)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: A rectangular shaped fuel tank measures 27-1/2 inches in length, 3/4 foot in width, and 8-1/4 inches in depth. How many gallons will the tank contain? (231 cu in = 1 gal)
Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Converting width to inches (0.75 ft × 12 = 9 in), the volume is 27.5 × 9 × 8.25 = 2,041.875 cubic inches; dividing by 231 cu in/gal yields approximately 7.366 gallons. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations and Servicing)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: A rectangular-shaped fuel tank measures 37-1/2 inches in length, 14 inches in width, and 8-1/4 inches in depth. How many cubic inches are within the tank?
Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Cubic inches are found by multiplying all dimensions: 37.5 × 14 × 8.25 = 4,331.25 cubic inches, a straightforward volume calculation with all dimensions already in inches. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations and Servicing)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: When we think of aviation safety in a contemporary way, human error is

choices.B: the ending point.
choices.C: the intervention point.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: In contemporary aviation safety thinking, human error is the starting point of analysis because it initiates the chain of events leading to accidents, and understanding its root causes drives system-level safety improvements. Rather than treating human error as the final cause, modern safety philosophy uses it as the beginning of a deeper investigation into organizational and systemic factors. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Human Factors)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: (Refer to Figure 16.) The TCO relay will operate if 24-volts DC is applied to the bus and the fuel tank selector is in the

choices.B: crossfeed position.
choices.C: left-hand tank position.

Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Tracing the circuit in Figure 16, the TCO relay is energized only when the fuel tank selector is in the right-hand tank position, because that switch position completes the ground path necessary to energize the relay coil with 24V DC applied to the bus. The other selector positions do not complete the required circuit path for TCO relay operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Drawings and Diagrams)

Ground Operations and Servicing.question: (Refer to Figure 51.) Which marshalling signal should be given if a taxiing aircraft is in imminent danger of striking an object?
Ground Operations and Servicing.explanation: Signal 3 (arms crossed above the head) is the emergency-stop signal, given to halt an aircraft immediately when it is in imminent danger of striking an object. It directs the pilot to stop all movement at once. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Ground Operations and Servicing)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: Which coupling nut should be selected for use with 1/2-inch aluminum oil lines which are to be assembled using flared tube ends and standard AN nuts, sleeves, and fittings?
Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: AN-818 coupling nuts are dash-numbered based on the outside diameter of the tubing in 1/16-inch increments, so 1/2-inch tubing (which equals 8/16 inch) corresponds to an AN-818-8 nut. The dash number directly represents the tube OD in sixteenths of an inch. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: Metal tubing fluid lines are sized by wall thickness and

choices.B: inside diameter in 1/16 inch increments.
choices.C: outside diameter in 1/32 inch increments.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: Aircraft metal tubing is sized by outside diameter (OD) measured in 1/16-inch increments, with the dash number on AN fittings and tubing designations corresponding to the OD in sixteenths of an inch. Wall thickness is specified separately and determines pressure ratings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: Hydraulic tubing, which is damaged in a localized area to such an extent that repair is necessary, may be repaired

choices.A: by cutting out the damaged area and utilizing a swaged tube fitting to join the tube ends.
choices.B: only by replacing the tubing section run (connection to connection) using the same size and material as the original.
choices.C: by cutting out the damaged section and soldering in a replacement section of tubing.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: FAA standards permit repair of locally damaged hydraulic tubing by cutting out the damaged area and installing a swaged (or flared) tube fitting union to rejoin the tube ends, provided the repair meets all applicable specifications for the system. Soldering is not approved for hydraulic tubing repair, and full replacement is not always required for localized damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: What is an advantage of a double flare on aluminum tubing?

choices.B: More resistant to damage when the joint is tightened.
choices.C: Can be applied to any size and wall-thickness of tubing.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: A double flare on aluminum tubing folds the tube wall back on itself, creating two layers of material at the seating surface, which makes it significantly more resistant to damage (cracking or deformation) when the joint is tightened compared to a single flare. This added durability is the primary advantage of the double flare. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: The term 'cold flow' is generally associated with

choices.A: the effects of low temperature gasses or liquids flowing in hose or tubing.
choices.B: impressions left in natural or synthetic rubber hose material.
choices.C: flexibility characteristics of various hose materials at low ambient temperatures.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: "Cold flow" refers to the permanent impressions or deformations left in natural or synthetic rubber hose material by clamps, supports, or fittings over time, and is not related to fluid temperature effects. These impressions can compromise hose integrity and are a key inspection concern. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: What is the color of an AN steel flared-tube fitting?

choices.B: Blue.
choices.C: Red.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: AN steel flared-tube fittings are identified by their black color (or black with yellow stripe for certain types), which distinguishes them from AN aluminum fittings (blue) and allows technicians to quickly identify material composition during maintenance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: AN fittings have an identifying shoulder between the end of the threads and the flare cone.
choices.B: AC and AN fittings are considered identical except for material composition and identifying colors.
choices.C: AN fittings are generally interchangeable with AC fittings of compatible material composition.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: AN fittings are distinguished from AC fittings by an identifying shoulder (a machined shoulder) between the end of the threads and the flare cone, which prevents AN and AC fittings from being inadvertently interchanged even when thread dimensions appear similar. This shoulder is the key identifying feature that makes them non-interchangeable with AC fittings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: The maximum distance between end fittings to which a straight hose assembly is to be connected is 50 inches. The minimum hose length to make such a connection should be

choices.B: 51-1/2 inches.
choices.C: 52-1/2 inches.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: FAA standards require that flexible hose assemblies be installed with approximately 5–8% extra length (slack) to account for pressure expansion and movement; for a 50-inch straight-line distance, adding approximately 5% yields about 52.5 inches, making 52-1/2 inches the correct minimum hose length. This slack prevents hose stress under pressurization and vibration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: In most aircraft hydraulic systems, two-piece tube connectors consisting of a sleeve and a nut are used when a tubing flare is required. The use of this type connector eliminates

choices.A: the flaring operation prior to assembly.
choices.B: the possibility of reducing the flare thickness by wiping or ironing during the tightening process.
choices.C: wrench damage to the tubing during the tightening process.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: The two-piece sleeve-and-nut connector is designed so the sleeve contacts the flare and the nut rides on the sleeve rather than directly on the flare, which eliminates the wiping or ironing (thinning) of the flare material that can occur when a single-piece nut bears directly on the flare during tightening. This preserves flare integrity and sealing capability. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: During installation, Military Standard (MS) flareless fittings

choices.A: are normally tightened by turning the nut a specified amount, unless the manufacturer lists torque values.
choices.B: should be assembled by applying an anti-seize compound to both surfaces of the fitting and flare.
choices.C: are normally tightened by applying torque in small increments to prevent over-heating the tube mating surfaces.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: MS flareless fittings are installed by finger-tightening and then turning the nut a specific additional amount (such as 1/6 turn) per the standard procedure, unless the manufacturer specifies torque values, because the preset bite of the ferrule into the tubing is what creates the seal rather than a specific torque value. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: When flaring aluminum tubing for use with AN fittings, the flare angle must be
Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: AN fittings require a 37-degree flare angle on all tubing materials including aluminum, as this is the standardized angle machined into AN flare cones and fittings; using any other angle (such as 45 degrees used in automotive/plumbing applications) will result in an improper seal and potential fitting failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: Which of the following hose materials are compatible with phosphate-ester base hydraulic fluids?

choices.B: Neoprene and Teflon.
choices.C: Teflon and Buna-N.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: Phosphate-ester base hydraulic fluids (such as Skydrol) are highly aggressive and incompatible with most rubber compounds, but butyl rubber and Teflon (PTFE) are specifically rated as compatible with phosphate-ester fluids, making them the correct hose materials for such systems. Neoprene and Buna-N are degraded by phosphate-ester fluids. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 6 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: When installing bonded clamps to support metal tubing,

choices.A: paint removal from tube is not recommended as it will inhibit corrosion.
choices.B: paint clamp and tube after clamp installation to prevent corrosion.
choices.C: remove paint or anodizing from tube at clamp location.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: Bonded clamps must make direct metal-to-metal contact with the tube to provide an effective electrical bond, so paint or anodizing must be removed at the clamp location to ensure proper conductivity and grounding. Without this removal, the insulating coating would prevent the bonding function from working. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: A gas or fluid line marked with the letters PHDAN is

choices.A: a dual-purpose pneumatic and/or hydraulic line for normal and emergency system use.
choices.B: used to carry a hazardous substance.
choices.C: a pneumatic or hydraulic system drain or discharge line.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: Military Standard line identification coding uses letter combinations to describe line function; 'PHDAN' decodes to Pneumatic/Hydraulic, Drain/Discharge, meaning the line is a pneumatic or hydraulic system drain or discharge line. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: If a flared tube coupling nut is overtightened, where is the tube most likely to be weakened/damaged?

choices.A: Along the entire length of the sleeve and tube interface.
choices.B: At the edge of the sleeve and straight portion of the tube.
choices.C: At the sleeve, fitting nut, or tube flare.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: Overtightening a flared tube coupling nut concentrates stress at the weakest points of the assembly — the sleeve, the fitting nut, or the tube flare — where the material is already work-hardened and thin, making cracking or deformation most likely at those locations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Unbonded clamps are used for support when installing metal tubing.
choices.B: Unbonded clamps are used for support when installing wiring.
choices.C: Bonded clamps are used when installing wiring.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: Unbonded clamps are lined with a nonmetallic material (rubber or plastic) to cushion and isolate wiring bundles from vibration and chafing without creating an unwanted electrical ground path, making them the correct choice for supporting wiring. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: A 3/8 inch aircraft high pressure flexible hose as compared to 3/8 inch metal tubing used in the same system will

choices.B: have equivalent flow characteristics.
choices.C: usually have interchangeable applications.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: A flexible hose and a metal tube of the same nominal size (3/8 inch) are designed to carry the same internal bore diameter, so their flow characteristics are considered equivalent when used in the same system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: Each person performing an annual or 100-hour inspection shall use a checklist that contains at least those items in the appendix of

choices.B: 14 CFR Part 65.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix D, specifically lists the minimum checklist items that must be used when performing an annual or 100-hour inspection, and each person performing such inspections is required by regulation to use a checklist containing at least those items. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Fluid Lines and Fittings.question: The main rotor system on a helicopter is classified in three groups. Which of those listed is NOT one of these groups?

choices.B: The flexible-beam rotor system.
choices.C: The semi-rigid rotor system.

Fluid Lines and Fittings.explanation: The three primary helicopter main rotor classifications recognized by the FAA are fully articulated, semi-rigid, and rigid; the 'flexible-beam' rotor system is not one of the three standard FAA-defined groups. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 7 — Fluid Lines and Fittings)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: Magnetic particle inspection is used primarily to detect

choices.B: deep subsurface flaws.
choices.C: flaws on or near the surface.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: Magnetic particle inspection is most effective at detecting discontinuities at or near the surface of ferromagnetic materials because the magnetic flux leakage caused by surface and near-surface flaws attracts the ferromagnetic particles, making defects visible. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Inspection Concepts and Techniques)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: Liquid penetrant inspection methods may be used on which of the following?

choices.B: Porous and nonporous plastics and nonferrous metals.
choices.C: Ferrous and nonferrous metals and nonporous plastics.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: Liquid penetrant inspection can be used on any nonporous material — ferrous metals, nonferrous metals, and nonporous plastics — because the method relies on capillary action into surface-open defects, not on magnetic or electrical properties. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Inspection Concepts and Techniques)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: Which inspection method can be used on a variety of materials?

choices.B: Magnetic particle inspection.
choices.C: Ultrasonic inspection.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: Ultrasonic inspection can be applied to metals, composites, plastics, ceramics, and other materials regardless of magnetic or electrical properties, making it the most versatile NDT method among the choices listed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Inspection Concepts and Techniques)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: What nondestructive testing method requires little or no part preparation, is used to detect surface or near-surface defects in most metals, and may also be used to separate metals or alloys and their heat-treated conditions?

choices.B: Ultrasonic inspection.
choices.C: Magnetic particle inspection.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: Eddy current inspection requires minimal part preparation, detects surface and near-surface defects, and can also differentiate between metals, alloys, and heat-treat conditions by measuring changes in the eddy current field — capabilities not shared by magnetic particle or ultrasonic methods alone. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Inspection Concepts and Techniques)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: The testing medium that is generally used in magnetic particle inspection utilizes a ferromagnetic material that has

choices.B: low permeability and high retentivity.
choices.C: high permeability and high retentivity.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: The indicating medium used in magnetic particle inspection needs high permeability to be easily magnetized and attracted to flux leakage fields at defects, and low retentivity so the particles do not clump together and remain mobile enough to migrate to discontinuities. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Inspection Concepts and Techniques)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: A mechanic has completed a bonded honeycomb repair using the potted compound repair technique. What nondestructive testing method is used to determine the soundness of the repair after the repair has cured?

choices.B: Metallic ring test.
choices.C: Ultrasonic test.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: After a potted compound repair on bonded honeycomb structure has cured, the coin-tap (metallic ring) test is the standard method to check for voids or unbonded areas, as a solid bond produces a clear ring while a disbond produces a dull thud. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Inspection Concepts and Techniques)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: What two types of indicating mediums are available for magnetic particle inspection?

choices.B: Wet and dry process materials.
choices.C: High retentivity and low permeability material.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: Magnetic particle inspection uses two forms of indicating medium: dry powder particles (dry process) and particles suspended in a liquid carrier (wet process), with each suited to different applications and sensitivity requirements. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Inspection Concepts and Techniques)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: Which type crack can be detected by magnetic particle inspection using either circular or longitudinal magnetization?

choices.B: Longitudinal.
choices.C: Transverse.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: A crack oriented at 45 degrees to the part axis is neither purely longitudinal nor purely transverse, so it will produce flux leakage under both circular and longitudinal magnetization, allowing detection by either method. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 8 — Inspection Concepts and Techniques)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: Which of the following methods may be suitable to use to detect cracks open to the surface in aluminum forgings and castings?

choices.A: Dye penetrant inspection and eddy current inspection.
choices.B: Dye penetrant inspection and magnetic particle inspection.
choices.C: Magnetic particle inspection and metallic ring inspection.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: Aluminum is a non-ferromagnetic material, making magnetic particle inspection ineffective; dye penetrant and eddy current inspections are both valid methods for detecting surface-open cracks in non-ferrous materials like aluminum forgings and castings. Magnetic particle inspection is only suitable for ferromagnetic materials. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Inspection Methods)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: To detect a minute crack using dye penetrant inspection usually requires

choices.A: that the developer be applied to a flat surface.
choices.B: a longer-than-normal penetrating time.
choices.C: the surface to be highly polished.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: A minute or tight crack requires extended penetrant dwell time to allow the penetrant fluid sufficient opportunity to seep into the very narrow discontinuity through capillary action. Insufficient dwell time is the most common cause of missed small defects in penetrant inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Dye Penetrant Inspection)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: Which of the following is a main determinant of the dwell time to use when conducting a dye or fluorescent penetrant inspection?

choices.A: The size and shape of the discontinuities being looked for.
choices.B: The size and shape of the part being inspected.
choices.C: The type and/or density of the part material.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: Dwell time in penetrant inspection is primarily determined by the type and size of the discontinuity being sought, as larger or more open defects require less dwell time while tighter or smaller cracks require longer dwell times to ensure adequate penetrant entrapment. The nature of the flaw drives the dwell time selection more than the part geometry or material density. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Dye Penetrant Inspection)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: In magnetic particle inspection, a flaw that is perpendicular to the magnetic field flux lines generally causes

choices.A: a large disruption in the magnetic field.
choices.B: a minimal disruption in the magnetic field.
choices.C: no disruption in the magnetic field.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: When a flaw is oriented perpendicular to the magnetic flux lines, the discontinuity maximally interrupts the field, causing the flux lines to leak out of the part and create a strong, easily detectable magnetic particle indication. This is why parts must be magnetized in multiple directions to ensure all flaws regardless of orientation are detectable. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Magnetic Particle Inspection)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: If dye penetrant inspection indications are not sharp and clear, the most probable cause is that the part

choices.A: was not correctly degaussed before the developer was applied.
choices.B: has no appreciable damage.
choices.C: was not thoroughly washed before the developer was applied.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: If residual penetrant remains on the part surface due to inadequate washing before developer application, it bleeds out broadly and masks or blurs the indication from any actual defect, producing unclear and indistinct patterns. Thorough intermediate cleaning/washing is a critical step to ensure sharp, well-defined indications. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Dye Penetrant Inspection)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: The pattern for an inclusion is a magnetic particle buildup forming

choices.B: a single line.
choices.C: parallel lines.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: Inclusions are irregularly shaped foreign materials embedded within the metal, and their complex, branching geometry causes magnetic particle buildup in a characteristic fernlike or feathery pattern rather than a clean straight line. This distinguishes inclusions from cracks in magnetic particle inspection interpretation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Magnetic Particle Inspection)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: A part which is being prepared for dye penetrant inspection should be cleaned with

choices.B: the penetrant developer.
choices.C: water-base solvents only.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: A volatile petroleum-base solvent effectively removes oils, greases, and contaminants from the part surface without leaving residues that could interfere with penetrant absorption into surface discontinuities. Proper pre-cleaning is essential to ensure the penetrant can freely enter any cracks or defects. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Dye Penetrant Inspection)

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.question: In performing a dye penetrant inspection, the developer

choices.A: seeps into a surface crack to indicate the presence of a defect.
choices.B: acts as a blotter to produce a visible indication.
choices.C: thoroughly cleans the surface prior to inspection.

Inspection Concepts and Techniques.explanation: The developer acts as a blotter or absorbent medium, drawing the entrapped penetrant out of discontinuities and spreading it slightly to create a visible, color-contrasted indication against the developer background. It does not enter cracks itself nor clean the surface. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Dye Penetrant Inspection)

Human Factors.question: Under magnetic particle inspection, a part will be identified as having a fatigue crack under which condition?

choices.B: The discontinuity is found in a nonstressed area of the part.
choices.C: The discontinuity is found in a highly stressed area of the part.
