Human Factors.explanation: Fatigue cracks initiate and propagate in highly stressed areas of a part due to cyclic loading; a discontinuity found in a high-stress region is therefore consistent with a fatigue crack, whereas a flaw in a non-stressed area is more likely a manufacturing defect such as an inclusion or cold shut. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 5 — Magnetic Particle Inspection)

Human Factors.question: All of the following are consequences of human error, except

choices.B: catastrophic.
choices.C: personal injury.

Human Factors.explanation: Mental stressors are contributing factors or preconditions that can lead to human error, not consequences resulting from it; consequences of human error include outcomes such as catastrophic events, personal injury, or property damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Human Factors)

Human Factors.question: The positive aspects of human factor issues are referred to as the

choices.B: Dirty Dozen.
choices.C: MEDA.

Human Factors.explanation: The "Magnificent Seven" refers to the positive human performance attributes or factors that counteract the negative influences described by the "Dirty Dozen," representing traits and behaviors that promote safe maintenance practices. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Human Factors)

Human Factors.question: The SHEL model is another human factors tool. The goal is to determine not only what the problem is, but also

choices.B: how we prevent the problem.
choices.C: how many factors contribute to the error.

Human Factors.explanation: The SHEL model (Software, Hardware, Environment, Liveware) is used to analyze the interfaces between human and system components to identify not only what the human factors problem is, but also where in the system it exists and why it occurs, enabling targeted corrective action. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Human Factors)

Human Factors.question: The three types of human error are

choices.B: active, latent, and stressor.
choices.C: mental, situational, and physiological.

Human Factors.explanation: The three recognized types of human error are errors of omission (failing to perform a required action), commission (performing an action incorrectly), and extraneous (performing an unnecessary action not required by the task). These categories encompass the full range of procedural human errors in aviation maintenance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Human Factors)

Forms and Regulations.question: Where is the record of compliance with Airworthiness Directives or manufacturers' service bulletins normally indicated?

choices.B: Aircraft maintenance records.
choices.C: Flight manual.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Records of compliance with Airworthiness Directives and manufacturers' service bulletins are required to be documented in the aircraft's maintenance records, as this provides a traceable, permanent record accessible to owners, operators, and inspectors verifying airworthiness. FAA Form 337 is used specifically for major repairs and alterations, not routine AD compliance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Forms and Regulations)

Forms and Regulations.question: If work performed on an aircraft has been done satisfactorily, the signature of an authorized person on the maintenance records for maintenance or alterations performed constitutes

choices.A: approval of the aircraft for return to service.
choices.B: approval for return to service only for the work performed.
choices.C: verification that the maintenance or alterations were performed referencing approved maintenance data.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per FAA regulations, an authorized person's signature on maintenance records constitutes approval for return to service only for the specific work that was performed, not a blanket airworthiness approval of the entire aircraft. This distinction limits the scope of the approval to the documented task. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 3 — Forms and Regulations)

Forms and Regulations.question: During an annual inspection, if a defect is found which makes the aircraft unairworthy, the person disapproving must

choices.B: submit a Malfunction or Defect Report.
choices.C: provide a written notice of the defect to the owner.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 43.11, when an aircraft is disapproved for return to service after an inspection, the person performing the inspection must provide the owner or lessee with a written notice of the aircraft's unairworthy condition; the Airworthiness Certificate is not voided by the mechanic, and a Malfunction or Defect Report is a separate, voluntary/required reporting action unrelated to this obligation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Record Entries)

Forms and Regulations.question: What is the means by which the FAA notifies aircraft owners and other interested persons of unsafe conditions and prescribes the condition under which the product may continue to be operated?

choices.B: Aviation Maintenance Alerts.
choices.C: Aviation Safety Data.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Airworthiness Directives (ADs) are legally enforceable regulations issued by the FAA under 14 CFR Part 39 to notify owners and operators of unsafe conditions and to prescribe corrective actions or operating limitations required to restore airworthiness. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Airworthiness Directives)

Forms and Regulations.question: Where should the aviation mechanic find this entry? 'Removed right wing from aircraft and removed skin from outer 6 feet. Repaired buckled spar 49 inches from tip in accordance with figure 8 in the manufacturer's structural repair manual No. 28-1.'

choices.B: Aircraft minor repair and alteration record.
choices.C: FAA Form 337.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: A spar repair is classified as a major structural repair, and per 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix A, major repairs and alterations must be documented on FAA Form 337 (Major Repair and Alteration form), not in engine records or a minor repair log. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — FAA Form 337)

Forms and Regulations.question: For aircraft operated under Part 91, when is aircraft total time required to be recorded in aircraft maintenance records?

choices.A: After satisfactorily completing maintenance, preventive maintenance, rebuilding, and alteration (excluding inspections).
choices.B: After satisfactorily completing inspections.
choices.C: After satisfactorily completing airframe, component, or propeller maintenance.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 91.417(a)(1), for Part 91 operations, aircraft total time in service is specifically required to be recorded in the maintenance records after the completion of inspections, not after every maintenance or alteration action. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: A person installing a product, part, or appliance on a type certificated product must make certain that the item's records document the

choices.A: product, part, or material meets FAA airworthiness standards.
choices.B: source from which the part came.
choices.C: product or material was not produced under an FAA production approval.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 43.13 and related FAA standards, a person installing a part on a type-certificated product must ensure the item's records confirm that it meets FAA airworthiness standards, which helps verify the part is not counterfeit or unapproved. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Approved Parts)

Forms and Regulations.question: Who is responsible for making the entry in the maintenance records after an annual, 100-hour, or progressive inspection?

choices.A: The owner or operator of the aircraft.
choices.B: The person approving or disapproving for return to service.
choices.C: The designee or inspector representing the FAA Administrator.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 43.11, the responsibility for making the maintenance record entry after an annual, 100-hour, or progressive inspection lies with the person who approves or disapproves the aircraft for return to service, as they are certifying the results of that inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Record Entries)

Forms and Regulations.question: For aircraft operated under Part 91, which of the following records must be retained for at least one year, or until the work is repeated or superseded?

choices.A: Records of time since overhaul of items requiring overhaul on a time specified basis.
choices.B: Records of maintenance, alterations, preventive maintenance, 100-hour, annual, and progressive inspections.
choices.C: Records of the current inspection status of the aircraft, including time since last required inspection.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 91.417(b), records of maintenance, alterations, preventive maintenance, and inspections (100-hour, annual, progressive) must be retained for at least one year or until the work is repeated or superseded by other work; records of total time and overhaul status must be retained permanently. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: A certificated mechanic without an inspection authorization who signs the appropriate block on FAA Form 337 is doing what?

choices.A: Certifying that the work was done in accordance with the requirements of 14 CFR Part 43.
choices.B: Approving the work for return to service.
choices.C: Certifying the maintenance information used as FAA-approved data.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: A certificated mechanic (without an IA) who signs the appropriate block on FAA Form 337 is certifying that the work described was performed in accordance with the requirements of 14 CFR Part 43; only an IA or authorized representative can approve a major repair or alteration for return to service. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — FAA Form 337)

Forms and Regulations.question: For aircraft operated under Part 91, which of the following records must be retained and transferred with the aircraft when it is sold?

choices.A: Records of maintenance, alterations, preventive maintenance, 100-hour, annual, and progressive inspections.
choices.B: Records of inspections performed in accordance with 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix D.
choices.C: Records of the current status of applicable ADs, and date and time when recurring ADs are next due.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 91.417(b)(1), records that must be retained permanently and transferred with the aircraft upon sale include the current status of applicable ADs, including the method of compliance and the date/time when any recurring AD is next due; these are considered long-term aircraft history records. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: If an aircraft owner is provided a list of discrepancies on an aircraft that was not approved for return to service after an annual inspection, who may correct the discrepancies?

choices.A: Only a mechanic with an inspection authorization.
choices.B: An appropriately rated mechanic.
choices.C: Any certificated repair station.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 43.3 and 91.409, once the owner receives the written list of discrepancies, the repairs may be performed by any appropriately rated (certificated) mechanic, not exclusively an IA; an IA is required to perform the subsequent inspection approving the aircraft for return to service. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Inspection Authorization)

Forms and Regulations.question: In order to reconstruct lost or destroyed aircraft maintenance records, what is it necessary to establish?

choices.A: Dates of all maintenance, preventive maintenance and alterations.
choices.B: Dates and/or times of all 100-hour, annual, or progressive inspections.
choices.C: Total time-in-service of the airframe.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 91.417(c), when reconstructing lost or destroyed records, the regulations require that total time-in-service of the airframe be established, as this is the foundational reference point for all time-based maintenance requirements. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: When approving for return to service after maintenance or alteration, the approving person must enter in the maintenance record of the aircraft

choices.A: the date the maintenance or alteration was begun, a description (or reference to acceptable data) of work performed, the name of the person performing the work (if someone else), signature, and certificate number.
choices.B: a description (or reference to acceptable data) of work performed, date of completion, the name of the person performing the work (if someone else), signature, and certificate number.
choices.C: a description (or reference to acceptable data) of work performed, date of completion, the name of the person performing the work (if someone else), signature, certificate number, and kind of certificate held.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 43.9(a), the maintenance record entry for an approval for return to service must include a description of the work performed, the date of completion, the name of the person who performed the work (if different), and the approving person's signature, certificate number, and kind of certificate held. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Record Entries)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which of the following actions is/are appropriate concerning minor repairs performed on a certificated aircraft?

choices.B: Entries must be made in the aircraft's maintenance record.
choices.C: The owner of the aircraft must submit a record of all minor repairs to the FAA at least annually.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 43.9, minor repairs do not require an FAA Form 337, but all maintenance performed on certificated aircraft must be documented with an entry in the aircraft's maintenance record, including a description of the work, date, and the approving person's signature and certificate information. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Maintenance Record Entries)

Forms and Regulations.question: When work is performed on an aircraft that necessitates the use of FAA Form 337, who should prepare the form?

choices.A: The person who performs or supervises the work.
choices.B: The person who approves for return to service.
choices.C: The aircraft owner or operator.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 43.9 and the instructions on FAA Form 337, the form must be prepared by the person who performs or supervises the major repair or alteration, since that individual has direct knowledge of the work accomplished and the data used. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — FAA Form 337)

Forms and Regulations.question: What is the status of data used as a basis for approving major repairs or alterations for return to service?

choices.A: Data must be least FAA-acceptable when it is used for that purpose.
choices.B: Data must be FAA-approved prior to its use for that purpose.
choices.C: Data may be FAA-approved after its use for that purpose.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR 43.13(b) and FAA Order 8300.16, data used as the basis for approving major repairs or alterations for return to service must be FAA-approved prior to its use; using unapproved data after the fact does not satisfy the regulatory requirement for an approved basis for the work. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Approved Data)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which statement is true regarding the use of FAA Form 337?

choices.A: FAA Form 337 is authorized for use with both U.S. and foreign registered aircraft.
choices.B: FAA Form 337 is authorized for use with U.S. registered aircraft, and foreign registered aircraft when located in the United States.
choices.C: FAA Form 337 is not authorized for use with other than U.S. registered aircraft.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: FAA Form 337 is exclusively authorized for U.S.-registered aircraft; it has no provision for use on foreign-registered aircraft regardless of their location. Foreign-registered aircraft are governed by their own national aviation authority documentation requirements. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: When performing a major repair or alteration, for which aircraft must an aviation mechanic submit Form 337 to the FAA?

choices.A: Form 337 is required for both U.S. and foreign-registered aircraft.
choices.B: Form 337 is required for U.S.-registered aircraft and may be requested for foreign aircraft.
choices.C: Form 337 is required for all aircraft operated in U.S. airspace.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Under 14 CFR Part 43, Form 337 is mandatory for major repairs and alterations on U.S.-registered aircraft, and while it may be requested or used as documentation for foreign-registered aircraft operating in the U.S., it is not required for them. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: After making a certain repair to an aircraft engine that is to be returned to service, an FAA Form 337 is prepared. How many copies are required and what is the disposition of the completed forms?

choices.A: Two; one copy for the aircraft owner and one copy for the FAA.
choices.B: Two; one copy for the FAA and one copy for the permanent records of the repairing agency or individual.
choices.C: Three; one copy for the aircraft owner, one copy for the FAA, and one copy for the permanent records of the repairing agency or individual.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §43.9 and the Form 337 instructions, two copies are required: one is given to the aircraft owner to be kept with the aircraft records, and one is forwarded to the local FAA Flight Standards District Office. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: Who is responsible for upkeep of the required maintenance records for an aircraft?

choices.A: The maintaining repair station or authorized inspector.
choices.B: The maintaining certificated mechanic.
choices.C: The aircraft owner.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §91.417, the responsibility for maintaining and retaining required aircraft maintenance records rests with the aircraft owner or operator, not the mechanic or repair station performing the work. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: An aircraft was not approved for return to service after an annual inspection and the owner wanted to fly the aircraft to another maintenance base. Which statement is correct?

choices.A: The owner must obtain a special flight permit.
choices.B: The owner may fly without restriction up to 10 hours.
choices.C: The owner may fly in the restricted category up to 10 hours.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: When an aircraft does not meet airworthiness standards and cannot be approved for return to service following an annual inspection, the owner must obtain a special flight permit (ferry permit) under 14 CFR §21.197 to legally fly the aircraft to another facility for maintenance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: An FAA Form 337 is used to record and document

choices.A: preventive and unscheduled maintenance and special inspections.
choices.B: major and minor repairs, and major and minor alterations.
choices.C: major repairs and major alterations.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: FAA Form 337 is specifically designated under 14 CFR Part 43 for documenting only major repairs and major alterations; minor repairs and minor alterations are recorded in the aircraft maintenance records, not on Form 337. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: A certificated airframe and powerplant mechanic is required to have an Inspection Authorization rating to perform which of the following inspections and approve the aircraft for return to service?

choices.B: An annual and a progressive inspection.
choices.C: A progressive inspection only.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.95, an Inspection Authorization (IA) is required for a mechanic to perform and approve an aircraft for return to service after an annual inspection or a progressive inspection; a 100-hour inspection may be performed and signed off by an A&P without an IA. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: Airworthiness Directives are issued primarily to

choices.A: provide information about malfunction or defect trends.
choices.B: present recommended maintenance procedures for correcting potentially hazardous defects.
choices.C: correct an unsafe condition.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Airworthiness Directives (ADs) are legally enforceable regulations issued under 14 CFR Part 39 specifically to correct unsafe conditions found in aircraft, engines, propellers, or appliances. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: What does the acronym TSO mean regarding a type certificated aircraft part, material, component and/or process?

choices.B: Type Supplement Original.
choices.C: Technical Standard Order.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: TSO stands for Technical Standard Order, which is a minimum performance standard issued by the FAA for specified materials, parts, processes, and appliances used on civil aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: Primary responsibility for compliance with Airworthiness Directives lies with the

choices.B: certificated mechanic holding an Inspection Authorization who conducts appropriate inspections.
choices.C: certificated mechanic who maintains the aircraft.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §91.409 and §39.7, the aircraft owner or operator bears the primary legal responsibility for ensuring compliance with all applicable Airworthiness Directives, regardless of who performs the actual maintenance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: The issuance of an Airworthiness Certificate is governed by

choices.B: 14 CFR Part 21.
choices.C: 14 CFR Part 39.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: The issuance of Airworthiness Certificates is governed by 14 CFR Part 21, which establishes certification procedures for products and articles, including the requirements for obtaining standard and special airworthiness certificates. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: Where are technical descriptions of certificated propellers found?

choices.B: Aircraft Specifications.
choices.C: Propeller Type Certificate Data Sheets.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Technical descriptions, specifications, and approved configurations of certificated propellers are found in Propeller Type Certificate Data Sheets (TCDS), which are the official FAA documents defining approved design data for propellers. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: Placards required on an aircraft are specified in

choices.B: The Federal Aviation Regulations under which the aircraft was type certificated.
choices.C: Aircraft Specifications or Type Certificate Data Sheets.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Required placards for an aircraft are specified in the Aircraft Specifications or Type Certificate Data Sheets (TCDS), which are the controlling documents that define the approved configuration and required markings for a certificated aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which of the following are sometimes used as authorization to deviate from an aircraft's original type design?

choices.A: FAA Form 337, Supplemental Type Certificate, and Technical Standard Order.
choices.B: Supplemental Type Certificate, Airworthiness Directive, and Technical Standard Order.
choices.C: FAA Form 337, Supplemental Type Certificate, and Airworthiness Directive.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: FAA Form 337, Supplemental Type Certificates (STCs), and Technical Standard Orders (TSOs) are all recognized methods of authorizing deviations from an aircraft's original type design; ADs mandate corrections rather than authorizing design deviations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: The Federal Aviation Regulations do not require approval after compliance with the data of a Supplemental Type Certificate.
choices.B: An installation of an item manufactured in accordance with the Technical Standard Order system requires no further approval for installation in a particular aircraft.
choices.C: The Federal Aviation Regulations require approval after compliance with the data of a Supplemental Type Certificate.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Although an STC approves a design change, 14 CFR Part 43 still requires that each specific installation on an individual aircraft be properly accomplished and approved for return to service; similarly, a TSO authorization approves the article itself but does not automatically approve its installation in a specific aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 2 — Forms and Records)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which regulation provides information regarding instrument range markings for an airplane certificated in the normal category?

choices.B: 14 CFR Part 25.
choices.C: 14 CFR Part 23.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: 14 CFR Part 23 establishes the airworthiness standards for normal, utility, acrobatic, and commuter category airplanes, including requirements for instrument markings and range markings. Part 21 covers certification procedures and Part 25 applies to transport category airplanes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which regulation provides the airworthiness standards for an airplane certificated in the normal category?

choices.B: 14 CFR Part 25.
choices.C: 14 CFR Part 23.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: 14 CFR Part 23 prescribes the airworthiness standards for normal, utility, acrobatic, and commuter category airplanes, while Part 25 applies to transport category and Part 27 applies to normal category rotorcraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Propellers are NOT included in the Airworthiness Directive system.
choices.B: A certificated powerplant mechanic may make a minor repair on an aluminum propeller and approve for return to service.
choices.C: A certificated powerplant mechanic may make a major repair on an aluminum propeller and approve for return to service.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR Part 65, a certificated powerplant mechanic may perform and approve minor repairs on aluminum propellers for return to service, but major repairs to propellers require an appropriately rated repair station or someone holding an Inspection Authorization. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: An aircraft mechanic is privileged to perform major alterations on U.S. certificated aircraft; however, the work must be done in accordance with FAA-approved technical data before the aircraft can be returned to service. Which is NOT approved data?

choices.C: Supplemental Type Certificates.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: AC 43.13-2A is advisory circular data and is considered acceptable data, not FAA-approved data; approved data must be specifically approved by the FAA such as STCs, ADs, and Type Certificate Data Sheets. Advisory circulars provide guidance but do not carry the force of approved technical data for major alterations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: What is the maintenance recording responsibility of the person who complies with an Airworthiness Directive?

choices.A: Advise the aircraft owner/operator of the work performed.
choices.B: Make an entry in the maintenance record of that equipment.
choices.C: Advise the FAA office of the work performed, by submitting an FAA Form 337.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §43.9, the person who performs maintenance, including AD compliance, is required to make an entry in the maintenance record of the equipment describing the work done, the date, and their certificate information. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Manufacturer's data and FAA publications such as Airworthiness Directives, Type Certificate Data Sheets, and advisory circulars are all approved data.
choices.B: FAA publications such as Technical Standard Orders, Airworthiness Directives, Type Certificate Data Sheets, and Aircraft Specifications and Supplemental Type Certificates are all approved data.
choices.C: FAA publications such as Airworthiness Directives, Type Certificate Data Sheets, and advisory circulars are all approved data.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: TSOs, ADs, Type Certificate Data Sheets, Aircraft Specifications, and STCs are all FAA-approved data, whereas advisory circulars and manufacturer's data are acceptable but not automatically approved data. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Aviation Maintenance Alerts

choices.A: provide mandatory procedures to prevent or correct serious aircraft problems.
choices.B: provide information about aircraft problems and suggested corrective actions.
choices.C: provide temporary emergency procedures until Airworthiness Directives can be issued.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Aviation Maintenance Alerts (AMAs), published by the FAA, are informational publications that relay service difficulty reports and suggest corrective actions; they are not mandatory like ADs and do not have the force of regulation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: An aircraft has a total time in service of 468 hours. The Airworthiness Directive given was initially complied with at 454 hours in service. How many additional hours in service may be accumulated before the Airworthiness Directive must again be complied with? The following is the compliance portion of an Airworthiness Directive. 'Compliance required as indicated, unless already accomplished. I. Aircraft with less that 500-hours' total time in service: Inspect in accordance with instructions below at 500-hours' total time, or within the next 50-hours' time in service after the effective date of this AD, and repeat after each subsequent 200 hours in service. II. Aircraft with 500-hours' through 1,000-hours' total time in service: Inspect in accordance with instructions below within the next 50-hours' time in service after the effective date of this AD, and repeat after each subsequent 200 hours in service. III. Aircraft with more than 1,000-hours' time in service: Inspect in accordance with instructions below within the next 25-hours' time in service after the effective date of this AD, and repeat after each subsequent 200 hours in service.'
Forms and Regulations.explanation: The AD was initially complied with at 454 hours total time, so the next inspection interval is 200 hours after that compliance, placing the next due time at 654 hours; since the aircraft is currently at 468 hours, 654 minus 468 equals 186 additional hours before the next required compliance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: The action required by an AD may take which of the following forms?

choices.A: Inspection, part(s) replacement, design modification, and/or change in operating procedure(s).
choices.B: Inspection, part(s) replacement, design modification, and/or overall change in the content, form, and disposition of aircraft maintenance records.
choices.C: Inspection, part(s) replacement, design modification, change in operating procedure(s), and/or overall change in the content, form, and disposition of aircraft maintenance records.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: ADs may require inspections, parts replacement, design modifications, and/or changes in operating procedures as the means of correcting unsafe conditions; changes to the overall content and disposition of maintenance records are not a form of AD action. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which of the following includes all the regulatory definitions of 'maintenance'?

choices.A: Overhaul, repair, parts replacement, and preservation, and preventive maintenance.
choices.B: Overhaul, repair, parts replacement, preservation, inspection, and preventive maintenance.
choices.C: Overhaul, repair, parts replacement, inspection, and preservation.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §1.1, 'maintenance' is defined to include overhaul, repair, parts replacement, preservation, inspection, and preventive maintenance, making this the complete regulatory definition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Certificated mechanics with a powerplant rating may perform

choices.A: any inspection required by the Federal Aviation Regulations on a powerplant or propeller or any component thereof, and may release the same to service.
choices.B: 100-hour and/or annual inspections required by the Federal Aviation Regulations on powerplants, propellers, or any components thereof, and may release the same to service.
choices.C: 100-hour inspections required by the Federal Aviation Regulations on powerplants, propellers, or any components thereof, and may release the same to service.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.85, a certificated mechanic with a powerplant rating may perform and approve 100-hour inspections on powerplants, propellers, and their components, but only an IA holder may perform annual inspections. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: The 100-hour inspection required by Federal Aviation Regulations for certain aircraft being operated for hire may be performed by

choices.A: persons working under the supervision of an appropriately rated mechanic, but the aircraft must be approved by the mechanic for return to service.
choices.B: appropriately rated mechanics only if they have an inspection authorization.
choices.C: appropriately rated mechanics and approved by them for return to service.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.85, a certificated mechanic with the appropriate airframe or powerplant rating may perform and approve a 100-hour inspection on the equipment covered by that rating without needing an Inspection Authorization. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: An Airworthiness Directive requires that a propeller be altered. Certificated mechanics could

choices.A: perform and approve the work for return to service if it is a minor alteration.
choices.B: not perform the work because it is an alteration.
choices.C: not perform the work because they are not allowed to perform and approve for return to service, repairs or alterations to propellers.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.85, certificated mechanics may perform and approve minor alterations to propellers within the scope of their powerplant rating, and an AD-required alteration that qualifies as minor falls within this authority. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: FAA certificated mechanics may

choices.A: approve for return to service a major repair for which they are rated.
choices.B: supervise and approve a 100-hour inspection.
choices.C: approve for return to service a minor alteration they have performed appropriate to the rating(s) they hold.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.85, certificated mechanics may approve for return to service minor alterations they have performed that are within the scope of their rating, but major repairs and major alterations require an IA or approved repair station. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: A certificated mechanic with a powerplant rating may perform the

choices.A: annual inspection required by the Federal Aviation Regulations on a powerplant or any component thereof and approve and return the same to service.
choices.B: 100-hour inspection required by the Federal Aviation Regulations on a powerplant or any component thereof and approve and return the same to service.
choices.C: 100-hour inspection required by the Federal Aviation Regulations on an airframe, powerplant or any other component thereof and approve and return the same to service.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.85(a), a mechanic with a powerplant rating is authorized to perform and approve 100-hour inspections on powerplants and their components, but not annual inspections, which require an Inspection Authorization. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: What part of the Federal Aviation Regulations prescribes the requirements for issuing mechanic certificates and associated ratings and the general operating rules for the holders of these certificates and ratings?

choices.B: 14 CFR Part 91.
choices.C: 14 CFR Part 65.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: 14 CFR Part 65 specifically governs the certification of airmen other than flight crewmembers, including mechanics, establishing requirements for issuing mechanic certificates and ratings as well as the operating rules for certificate holders. Parts 43 and 91 address maintenance performance and general operating rules respectively, but neither governs mechanic certification. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: 14 CFR Part 65 Subpart D contains regulations regarding the certification of

choices.B: pilots.
choices.C: mechanics.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: 14 CFR Part 65 Subpart D is titled 'Mechanics' and contains all certification requirements specific to mechanic certificates, while Subpart E covers repairmen and Subparts B/C cover other airmen such as pilots. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Under Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations, what is the maximum penalty for falsification, alteration, or fraudulent reproduction of certificates, logbooks, reports, and records?

choices.A: Ineligibility to receive any certificate or rating for one year.
choices.B: Imprisonment for one year and a $5,000 fine.
choices.C: Suspension or revocation of any certificate held.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Under 14 CFR §65.20, the FAA may suspend or revoke any certificate held by a person who falsifies, alters, or fraudulently reproduces certificates, logbooks, reports, or records, which is the maximum administrative penalty prescribed under this regulation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which of the following statements is true for a certificated and appropriately rated mechanic regarding repairs and alterations?

choices.A: He or she may perform an airframe major repair or major alteration, but cannot approve the work for return to service.
choices.B: He or she may perform airframe minor repairs and minor alterations and approve the work for return to service, but cannot perform an airframe major repair or major alteration.
choices.C: He or she may perform an airframe major repair or major alteration and approve the work, but not the entire aircraft, for return to service.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR §65.81, a certificated mechanic with an airframe rating may perform and approve major repairs or major alterations to airframe components, but this approval does not extend to approving the entire aircraft for return to service — that authority belongs to an appropriately rated repair station or inspector. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Certificated mechanics with an airframe rating may perform a minor repair to an airspeed indicator providing they have the necessary equipment available.
choices.B: Certificated mechanics with a powerplant rating may perform a minor repair to a propeller providing they have the necessary equipment available.
choices.C: Certificated mechanics with a powerplant rating may perform a major repair to a propeller providing they have the necessary equipment available.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Propellers fall under the powerplant category per 14 CFR Part 65, so a mechanic holding a powerplant rating may perform minor repairs to propellers; airframe-rated mechanics do not have authority over propellers, and major propeller repairs require an appropriately rated repair station. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: The replacement of a damaged engine mount with a new identical engine mount purchased from the aircraft manufacturer is considered a

choices.A: major or minor repair, depending upon the complexity of the installation.
choices.B: major repair.
choices.C: minor repair.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Per 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix A, replacing a structural member with an identical replacement part from the original manufacturer is classified as a minor repair because it does not involve a change in design, material, or method of construction from the approved type design. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Forms and Regulations.question: Instrument repairs may be performed

choices.B: by an FAA-approved instrument repair station.
choices.C: on airframe instruments by mechanics with an airframe rating.

Forms and Regulations.explanation: Instrument repairs require specialized equipment and expertise; therefore, 14 CFR Part 43 and associated FAA guidance restrict instrument repairs to FAA-approved repair stations with the appropriate instrument rating, as mechanics alone are not authorized to perform such repairs regardless of their airframe or powerplant ratings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-30B, Ch 1 — Regulations and Publications)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Laminated wood spars may be substituted for solid rectangular wood spars

choices.A: only in certain instances where the primary load is shared by one or more other original structural member.
choices.B: if the same quality wood is used in both.
choices.C: only upon specific approval by the manufacturer or the FAA.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Laminated wood spars are not a simple drop-in replacement for solid rectangular spars; substitution requires specific approval from the manufacturer or the FAA because the structural equivalence must be formally verified. Unauthorized substitution could compromise airworthiness. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Any wooden member that has been overstressed is subject to which type of failure?

choices.B: Compression.
choices.C: Torsion.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: When wood is overstressed, the fibers buckle and crush internally, making compression failure the characteristic result; unlike metals, wood does not stretch or yield plastically under overload but instead fails in compression. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which statement about wood decay is correct?

choices.A: Decay that occurs before the wood is seasoned does not affect the strength of the finished piece.
choices.B: A limited amount of certain kinds of decay is acceptable in aircraft woods since decay affects the binding between the fibers and not the fibers themselves.
choices.C: Decay is not acceptable in any form or amount.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Decay destroys the structural integrity of wood fibers and their binding, and no amount or form of decay is considered acceptable in aircraft wood structures per FAA standards, as even minor decay compromises load-carrying capability. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which statement concerning wood decay is correct?

choices.A: Decay that appears to be mineral streaks is acceptable.
choices.B: A limited amount of decay along the beveled edges of unrouted beams is acceptable.
choices.C: Any form or amount of decay is not permitted.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: FAA standards are absolute on this point: decay in any form or quantity renders wood unairworthy for aircraft use because it degrades the fibers and their bonds, reducing structural strength unpredictably. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which of the following conditions will determine acceptance of wood with mineral streaks?

choices.A: Careful inspection fails to reveal any decay.
choices.B: They produce only a small effect on grain direction.
choices.C: Local irregularities do not exceed limitations specified for spiral and diagonal grain.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Mineral streaks may be acceptable only when careful inspection reveals no associated decay, since mineral streaks themselves are not the hazard but are often accompanied by decay that would disqualify the wood. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Pin knot clusters are permitted in wood aircraft structure provided

choices.A: they produce a small effect on grain direction.
choices.B: they have no mineral streaks.
choices.C: no pitch pockets are within 12 inches.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Pin knot clusters are permissible as long as they produce only a small effect on grain direction, because significant grain deviation reduces the wood's strength along its primary load axis. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Laminated wood is sometimes used in the construction of highly stressed aircraft components. This wood can be identified by its

choices.B: similarity to standard plywood construction.
choices.C: perpendicular grain construction.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Laminated wood used in highly stressed components is built with all laminations having parallel grain orientation to maximize strength along the primary load axis, distinguishing it from plywood which intentionally uses alternating perpendicular grain layers. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When patching a plywood skin, abrupt changes in cross-sectional areas which will develop dangerous stress concentration should be avoided by using

choices.B: square patches.
choices.C: doublers with any desired shaped patches.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Circular or elliptical patches eliminate sharp corners that create stress concentrations, distributing loads smoothly around the repair and preventing crack initiation at abrupt geometric transitions in the plywood skin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Glue deterioration in wood aircraft structure is indicated

choices.A: when a joint has separated and the glue surface shows only the imprint of the wood with no wood fibers clinging to the glue.
choices.B: when a joint has separated and the glue surface shows pieces of wood and/or wood fibers clinging to the glue.
choices.C: by any joint separation.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Glue deterioration is confirmed when a separated joint shows only the wood grain imprint on the glue surface with no wood fibers adhering, indicating the glue bond failed adhesively rather than the wood failing cohesively. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Compression failures in wood aircraft structures are characterized by buckling of the fibers that appear as streaks on the surface

choices.A: at right angles to the growth rings.
choices.B: parallel to the grain.
choices.C: at right angles to the grain.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Compression failures in wood appear as fiber buckling streaks oriented at right angles to the grain, resulting from the fibers folding transversely when longitudinal compressive loads exceed the wood's strength. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Wood Structure)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When and how is finishing tape applied on a fabric-covered aircraft?

choices.A: Sewed or laced on before dope is applied.
choices.B: Doped on immediately prior to the finish coat.
choices.C: Doped on after the first or second coat of dope.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Finishing tape is doped on after the first or second coat of dope so that subsequent dope coats bond the tape firmly to the fabric surface, providing protective coverage over structural edges and seams. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 6 — Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: The determining factor(s) for the selection of the correct weight of textile fabric to be used in covering any type of aircraft is the

choices.B: speed of the aircraft.
choices.C: speed of the aircraft and the maximum wing loading.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Both the aircraft's never-exceed speed and its maximum wing loading must be considered together when selecting fabric weight, as each factor independently determines the aerodynamic and structural demands placed on the covering material. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 6 — Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: How many fabric thicknesses will be found in a French-fell seam?

choices.B: Three.
choices.C: Four.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: A French-fell seam folds and stitches the fabric such that four thicknesses of material are present at the seam, providing a strong, finished edge suitable for aircraft fabric work. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 6 — Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Finishing tape (surface tape) is used for what purpose?

choices.A: To help prevent 'ripple formation' in covering fabric.
choices.B: To provide additional wear resistance over the edges of fabric forming structures.
choices.C: To provide additional anti-tear resistance under reinforcement tape.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Finishing tape (surface tape) is applied over the edges of ribs, seams, and structural members to provide additional wear resistance and protect the base fabric from abrasion and deterioration at those high-wear locations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 6 — Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Moisture, mildew, chemicals, and acids have no effect on

choices.B: cotton fabric.
choices.C: polyester fabric.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Fiberglass (glass fabric) is inherently inert to moisture, mildew, chemicals, and acids due to its inorganic silica-based composition, unlike organic natural or synthetic fibers that can absorb moisture or degrade chemically. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 6 — Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Fungicide and mildewicide additives are important for fabric covered aircraft, such

choices.B: cotton and linen.
choices.C: glass and linen.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Cotton and linen are natural organic fibers that are susceptible to mold, mildew, and fungal degradation, making fungicide and mildewicide additives essential for their preservation; synthetic fibers like Dacron and glass are inherently resistant to biological attack. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: The strength classification of fabrics used in aircraft covering is based on

choices.B: shear strength.
choices.C: tensile strength.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: The FAA classifies aircraft covering fabric strength by tensile strength, measured in pounds per inch in both the warp and fill directions, because this directly reflects the fabric's ability to withstand the aerodynamic loads placed upon it. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Fabric rejuvenator is used to

choices.A: restore the condition of dope coatings.
choices.B: restore fabric strength and tautness to at least the minimum acceptable level.
choices.C: penetrate the fabric and restore fungicidal resistance.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Fabric rejuvenator is a solvent-based product designed to soften and reconstitute deteriorated dope coatings, restoring their flexibility and protective qualities, but it does not restore the underlying fabric's structural strength. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Machine-sewn seams in aircraft covering fabrics may be of which of the following types?

choices.B: Folded-fell, French-fell, or plain lapped.
choices.C: French-fell or plain lapped.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: FAA-accepted machine-sewn seam types for aircraft fabric covering include the folded-fell, French-fell, and plain lapped seams, as each provides adequate strength and is recognized in approved fabric covering processes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When testing the strength of Grade A cotton fabric covering an aircraft that requires only intermediate grade, the minimum acceptable strength the fabric must have

choices.B: 70 percent of the original strength for intermediate fabric.
choices.C: 56 pounds per inch warp and fill.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: When Grade A cotton fabric is used on an aircraft that only requires intermediate-grade fabric, the minimum acceptable strength is 56 pounds per inch warp and fill, which is 70% of the original 80 lb/in strength of intermediate-grade fabric, not 70% of Grade A's higher original strength. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When dope-proofing the parts of the aircraft structure that come in contact with doped fabric, which of the following provide an acceptable protective coating?

choices.A: Resin impregnated cloth tape and cellulose tape.
choices.B: Aluminum foil and resin impregnated cloth tape.
choices.C: Aluminum foil and cellulose tape.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Aluminum foil and cellulose tape are FAA-accepted materials for dope-proofing structural members in contact with doped fabric, as they prevent the solvents in dope from deteriorating rubber, plastic, or other susceptible materials. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Fabric Covering)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Hole filling fasteners (for example, MS20470 rivets) should not be used in composite structures primarily because of the

choices.B: increased possibility of fretting corrosion in the fastener.
choices.C: difficulty in forming a proper shop head.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Hole-filling fasteners displace material radially as they are installed, and in composite laminates this outward force can cause interlaminar separation (delamination), which severely weakens the surrounding structure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Metal fasteners used with carbon/graphite composite structures

choices.A: may be constructed of any of the metals commonly used in aircraft fasteners.
choices.B: must be constructed of material such as titanium or corrosion resistant steel.
choices.C: must be constructed of high strength aluminum-lithium alloy.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Carbon/graphite fiber is highly electrochemically active and will cause galvanic corrosion in common aluminum fasteners; titanium and corrosion-resistant steel (CRES) are compatible with carbon fiber and are therefore required. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Sandwich panels made of honeycomb construction are used on aircraft because this type of construction

choices.A: has a high tensile strength and is non-flammable.
choices.B: has a low weight and is corrosion resistant.
choices.C: saves weight while not compromising strength.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Honeycomb sandwich construction achieves an exceptionally high strength-to-weight ratio by separating two stiff face sheets with a lightweight core, maintaining bending stiffness and strength without a proportional weight penalty. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which of these methods may be used to inspect fiberglass/honeycomb structures for entrapped water?

choices.B: X-ray and acoustic emission monitoring.
choices.C: X-ray and backlighting.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: X-ray inspection can detect the presence and location of entrapped water within honeycomb panels due to the density difference, and backlighting can reveal water-saturated areas in translucent or semi-transparent structures; acoustic emission monitoring is used for detecting active damage growth, not water entrapment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When balsa wood is used to replace a damaged honeycomb core, the plug should be cut so that

choices.A: the grain is parallel to the skin.
choices.B: it is about 1/8 inch undersize to allow sufficient bonding material to be applied.
choices.C: the grain is perpendicular to the skin.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: When balsa wood is used as a honeycomb core replacement, the grain must be oriented perpendicular to the skin so that the end grain, which has the highest compressive strength, carries the through-thickness loads just as honeycomb cell walls do. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When repairing puncture-type damage to a metal-faced laminated honeycomb panel, the edges of the doubler should be tapered to

choices.A: two times the thickness of the metal.
choices.B: 100 times the thickness of the metal.
choices.C: whatever is desired for a neat, clean appearance.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Tapering the doubler edges to 100 times the metal thickness provides a gradual load transition that minimizes stress concentration at the repair boundary, ensuring a structurally sound repair on metal-faced honeycomb panels. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: One of the best ways to assure that a properly prepared batch of matrix resin has been achieved is to

choices.B: have mixed enough for a test sample.
choices.C: test the viscosity of the resin immediately after mixing.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Mixing a test sample (witness or test coupon) from the same batch allows the technician to verify proper cure, color, hardness, and workability, serving as a practical quality check that confirms the resin-to-hardener ratio was correct. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: How does acoustic emission testing detect defects in composite materials?

choices.A: By picking up the 'noise' of any deterioration that may be present.
choices.B: By analyzing ultrasonic signals transmitted into the parts being inspected.
choices.C: By creating sonogram pictures of the areas being inspected.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Acoustic emission testing works by placing sensors on the structure that detect the high-frequency stress waves (noise) naturally released when cracks propagate, fibers break, or delaminations grow under load. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When conducting a tap test on a composite panel, which of the following sounds would indicate delamination?

choices.B: Dull thud.
choices.C: Sharp thud.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: A dull or hollow thud during a tap test indicates that the plies have separated (delaminated) and are not transmitting energy efficiently, whereas a solid, well-bonded area produces a sharp, ringing tone. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: One method of inspecting a laminated fiberglass structure that has been subjected to damage is to

choices.A: strip the damaged area of all paint and shine a strong light through the structure.
choices.B: use dye-penetrant inspection procedures, exposing the entire damaged area to the penetrant solution.
choices.C: use an eddy current probe on both sides of the damaged area.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Fiberglass is translucent, so shining a strong light through the structure after stripping paint allows the inspector to identify delaminations, voids, and damage by observing variations in light transmission through the laminate. This visual light-transmission method is a standard inspection technique for fiberglass structures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When inspecting a composite panel using the ring test/tapping method, a dull thud may indicate

choices.A: less than full strength curing of the matrix.
choices.B: separation of the laminates.
choices.C: an area of too much matrix between fiber layers.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: The tap/ring test detects delamination because separated laminate layers produce a dull thud rather than the crisp, sharp sound of a well-bonded structure, indicating a loss of adhesion between plies. This acoustic difference is caused by the air gap or unbonded area between the separated layers. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which of the following are benefits of using microballoons when making repairs to laminated honeycomb panels?

choices.A: Improved strength-to-weight ratio, less density, and lower stress concentrations.
choices.B: Greater concentrations of resin in edges and corners.
choices.C: Less density and improved strength-to-weight ratio.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Microballoons are hollow spherical fillers that reduce the density of the resin mixture while improving the strength-to-weight ratio, making them ideal for filling and fairing repairs on honeycomb panels without adding excessive weight. They do not significantly concentrate resin or add strength beyond the weight savings benefit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: The length of time that a catalyzed resin will remain in a workable state is called the

choices.B: work life.
choices.C: plyable life.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Pot life specifically refers to the length of time a catalyzed resin remains usable in its container before it begins to gel and can no longer be applied, which is distinct from work life or other time-related terms. This term is standard in composite repair terminology per FAA guidance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: The classification for high tensile strength fiberglass used in aircraft structures

choices.B: S-glass.
choices.C: G-glass.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: S-glass (structural glass) is specifically formulated for high tensile strength applications and is used in aircraft structures where strength-to-weight performance is critical, making it superior in tensile strength to the more common E-glass. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Superficial scars, scratches, surface abrasion, or rain erosion on fiberglass laminates can generally be repaired by applying

choices.A: a piece of resin-impregnated glass fabric facing.
choices.B: one or more coats of suitable resin (room-temperature catalyzed) to the surface.
choices.C: a sheet of polyethylene over the abraded surface and one or more coats of resin cured with infrared heat lamps.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Superficial surface damage that has not penetrated the structural plies can be restored by applying one or more coats of room-temperature catalyzed resin to seal and protect the surface fibers without requiring a full patch repair. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: The classification for fiberglass reinforcement material that has high resistivity and is the most common is

choices.B: S-glass.
choices.C: G-glass.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: E-glass (electrical glass) is the most commonly used fiberglass reinforcement material and is characterized by its high electrical resistivity, making it the standard choice for general-purpose composite applications in aviation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Composite fabric material is considered to be the strongest in what direction?

choices.B: Warp.
choices.C: Bias.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: The warp direction runs the length of the fabric (along the roll) and contains the highest fiber density and straightest fiber alignment, which gives it the greatest tensile strength compared to the fill or bias directions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: What reference tool is used to determine how the fiber is to be oriented for a particular ply of fabric?

choices.B: Bias clock (or compass).
choices.C: Warp clock (or compass).

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: A warp clock (or compass) is the reference tool used to specify and verify the angular orientation of each ply's warp direction relative to a datum, ensuring plies are laid at the correct fiber angles for the intended load path. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: The strength and stiffness of a properly constructed composite buildup depends primarily on

choices.A: a 60 percent matrix to 40 percent fiber ratio.
choices.B: the orientation of the plies to the load direction.
choices.C: the ability of the fibers to transfer stress to the matrix.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: In composite structures, the fibers carry the structural loads and the matrix only transfers load between fibers, so the orientation of each ply relative to the applied load direction is the primary determinant of the laminate's strength and stiffness. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which fiber to resin (percent) ratio for advanced composite wet lay-ups is generally considered the best for strength?
Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: A 60 percent fiber to 40 percent resin ratio is considered optimal for advanced composite wet lay-ups because a higher fiber volume fraction maximizes the load-carrying capability of the laminate while the resin provides just enough matrix for fiber support and load transfer. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: What is the material layer used within the vacuum bag pressure system to absorb excess resin during curing called?

choices.B: Breather.
choices.C: Release.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: The bleeder layer is placed directly over the repair plies to absorb excess resin squeezed out during vacuum bagging, helping to achieve the correct fiber-to-resin ratio in the cured laminate. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Proper prepreg composite lay-up curing is generally accomplished by

choices.A: adding a catalyst or curing agent to the resin.
choices.B: applying external heat and pressure.
choices.C: room temperature exposure and applying pressure.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Prepreg materials already contain the pre-measured resin system but require elevated temperature and pressure (typically applied via autoclave or heat bonder) to activate the resin, complete cross-linking, and consolidate the plies properly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When repairing large, flat surfaces with polyester resins, warping of the surface is likely to occur. One method of reducing the amount of warpage is to

choices.A: add an extra amount of catalyst to the resin.
choices.B: use short strips of fiberglass in the bonded repair.
choices.C: use less catalyst than normal so the repair will be more flexible.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Using less catalyst slows the cure rate, which reduces the exothermic reaction and allows internal stresses to dissipate more gradually, thereby minimizing the differential shrinkage that causes warping in large flat polyester resin repairs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When necessary, what type of cutting fluid is usually acceptable for machining composite laminates?

choices.B: Water displacing oil.
choices.C: Water only.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Water is the preferred cutting fluid for machining composite laminates because it cools the cutting area and suppresses hazardous dust without introducing oils or chemicals that could contaminate the composite material and degrade adhesive bonds in subsequent repairs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Nonmetallic Structures)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Fiberglass laminate damage not exceeding the first layer or ply can be repaired by

choices.A: filling with a putty consisting of a compatible resin and clean, short glass fibers.
choices.B: sanding the damaged area until aerodynamic smoothness is obtained.
choices.C: trimming the rough edges and sealing with paint.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Surface damage limited to the first ply of a fiberglass laminate is considered minor and can be restored by filling with a resin-and-short-glass-fiber putty mixture that bonds compatibly with the surrounding structure. This approach restores the surface contour and maintains structural integrity without requiring full ply replacement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Fiberglass Repair)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Fiberglass damage that extends completely through a laminated sandwich structure

choices.B: must be filled with resin to eliminate dangerous stress concentrations.
choices.C: may be filled with putty which is compatible with resin.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Even damage that penetrates completely through a laminated sandwich composite structure is repairable using approved methods such as core replacement and ply layup restoration, provided proper procedures and materials are followed. The FAA acknowledges that full-depth damage does not automatically render a composite structure irreparable. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Fiberglass Repair)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Repairing advanced composites using materials and techniques traditionally used for fiberglass repairs is likely to result in

choices.B: slightly stronger strength to weight ratio.
choices.C: an unairworthy repair.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Advanced composites such as carbon/graphite and aramid fiber laminates have specific resin systems, fiber orientations, and structural properties that are not replicated by standard fiberglass repair techniques, so applying fiberglass methods to them will not restore the required strength and will result in an unairworthy repair. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Advanced Composite Repair)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which of the following, when added to wet resins, provide strength for the repair of damaged fastener holes in composite panels?

choices.B: Microballoons.
choices.C: Microballoons and flox.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Flox (flocked cotton fibers) and chopped fiberglass fibers mixed into wet resin create a high-strength filler that is appropriate for repairing damaged fastener holes in composite panels because they provide the structural reinforcement needed to restore bearing strength. Microballoons alone create a lightweight but low-strength filler unsuitable for load-bearing areas. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Advanced Composite Repair)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which of the following are generally characteristic of aramid fiber (Kevlar) composites?

choices.B: High tensile strength and stiffness.
choices.C: Corrosive effect in contact with aluminum and ability to conduct electricity.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Aramid fiber (Kevlar) composites are characterized by exceptionally high tensile strength combined with flexibility and toughness, making them resistant to impact and penetration; however, they have relatively poor compressive and stiffness properties compared to carbon/graphite fibers. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which of the following are generally characteristic of carbon/graphite fiber composites?

choices.A: Flexibility, high compressive strength, and corrosive effect in contact with aluminum.
choices.B: Stiffness, high compressive strength, and corrosive effect in contact with aluminum.
choices.C: Ability to conduct electricity.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Carbon/graphite fiber composites are known for their high stiffness, high compressive strength, and ability to cause galvanic corrosion when in direct contact with aluminum due to their electrochemical incompatibility. These properties distinguish them from aramid and fiberglass composites. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Composite Materials)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which of the following drill bit types work best when drilling an aramid fiber (Kevlar) composite laminate?

choices.B: Diamond dust coated.
choices.C: Carbide W-point.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Aramid (Kevlar) fibers are tough and tend to deflect and fray rather than cut cleanly, so carbide W-point (brad-point) drill bits are recommended because their geometry shears the fibers cleanly rather than pushing them aside. Standard twist drills tend to cause delamination and fuzzing in Kevlar laminates. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Advanced Composite Repair)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which is correct concerning the use of a file?

choices.A: Apply pressure on the forward stroke, only, except when filing very soft metals such as lead or aluminum.
choices.B: A smoother finish can be obtained by using a double-cut file than by using a single-cut file.
choices.C: The terms 'double-cut' and 'second-cut' have the same meaning in reference to files.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: A file cuts only on the forward stroke, so pressure should be applied on the push stroke and relieved on the return stroke to prevent dulling the teeth; an exception is made for very soft metals like lead or aluminum where some drag on the return stroke can be tolerated. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Hand Tools and Their Use)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which procedure is correct when using a reamer to finish a drilled hole to the correct size?

choices.A: Turn the reamer in the cutting direction when enlarging the hole and in the opposite direction to remove from the hole.
choices.B: Turn the reamer only in the cutting direction.
choices.C: Apply considerable pressure on the reamer when starting the cut and reduce the pressure when finishing the cut.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: A reamer must always be turned in the cutting direction, both when advancing through the hole and when withdrawing, because reversing rotation will dull or break the cutting edges. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Hand Tools and Their Use)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: When straightening members made of 2024-T4, the aviation mechanic should

choices.B: straighten cold and anneal to remove stress.
choices.C: apply heat to the inside of the bend.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: 2024-T4 is a heat-treated aluminum alloy that cannot be re-heat-treated in the field, so bent members must be straightened cold; the bent area is then reinforced to compensate for any work-hardening stress introduced during straightening. Applying heat would destroy the temper. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Metals)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Clad aluminum alloys are used in aircraft because they

choices.A: are naturally corrosion resistant, so they do not require corrosion resistant materials for protection.
choices.B: have surface layers of pure aluminum or aluminum alloy bonded to the core material to inhibit corrosion.
choices.C: are highly corrosion resistant because an oxide film forms on their surfaces upon contact with air.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Clad (Alclad) aluminum alloys consist of a high-strength aluminum alloy core bonded with thin outer layers of pure aluminum or a more corrosion-resistant alloy, and it is these surface layers that provide corrosion protection for the stronger but less corrosion-resistant core. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Metals)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Parts fabricated from Alclad 2024-T3 aluminum sheet stock must have all

choices.A: bends made with a small radius to develop maximum strength.
choices.B: bends 90 to the grain.
choices.C: scratches, kinks, tool marks, nicks, etc., must be held to a minimum.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Alclad 2024-T3 sheet has a very thin pure-aluminum cladding layer that provides corrosion protection; surface damage such as scratches, nicks, tool marks, and kinks must be minimized because they penetrate the protective cladding and expose the less corrosion-resistant core alloy. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Aircraft Metals)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: The left brake is dragging excessively on an airplane on which no recent brake service work has been performed. The most probable cause is

choices.A: foreign particles stuck in the master cylinder compensating port.
choices.B: excessively worn brake linings.
choices.C: low fluid supply in the brake system reservoir.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: A clogged or restricted compensating port in the master cylinder prevents hydraulic fluid from returning to the reservoir after brake release, trapping pressure in the brake line and causing the brake to drag; this is the most probable cause when no recent service work has been performed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Brake Systems)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Which characteristics apply to aircraft hydraulic systems?

choices.A: About 80 percent operating efficiency (20 percent loss due to fluid friction).
choices.B: Minimum maintenance requirements and simple to inspect.
choices.C: Minimum maintenance requirements, lightweight, and simple to inspect.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Aircraft hydraulic systems are valued for their combination of minimum maintenance requirements, relatively light weight for the power transmitted, and ease of inspection, which collectively make them well-suited for aircraft applications. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Nonmetallic Structures.question: Materials which are Skydrol compatible or resistant include

choices.A: epoxy paints and most common aircraft metals.
choices.B: polyurethane and epoxy paints.
choices.C: common aircraft metals, polyurethane, and epoxy paints.

Nonmetallic Structures.explanation: Skydrol phosphate-ester hydraulic fluid is compatible with common aircraft metals, polyurethane coatings, and epoxy paints, but it attacks natural rubber, many petroleum-based sealants, and some other polymer materials, so components in Skydrol systems must be verified compatible. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)
