Landing Gear.question: If it is determined that spongy brake action is not caused by air in the brake system, what is the next most likely cause?

choices.B: Internal leakage in the master cylinder.
choices.C: Deteriorated flexible hoses.

Landing Gear.explanation: Deteriorated flexible brake hoses can balloon internally under pressure, effectively increasing the volume of the system and producing a spongy pedal feel similar to air entrapment, making them the next most likely cause once air has been ruled out. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: A brake debooster valve is installed in systems where the high pressure of the hydraulic system (3,000 psi) is used to operate brakes

choices.A: that are designed to work with lower pressure.
choices.B: that are used in conjunction with an antiskid system.
choices.C: that are used on aircraft having high landing speeds.

Landing Gear.explanation: A debooster valve steps down the aircraft's high system pressure (typically 3,000 psi) to a lower value compatible with brake assemblies that are designed and rated for lower operating pressures, protecting those components from damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: A stripe or mark applied to a wheel rim and extending onto the sidewall of a tube-type tire is a

choices.B: wheel-to-tire balance mark.
choices.C: wheel weight reference mark.

Landing Gear.explanation: A slippage mark is a paint stripe applied across the tire sidewall and onto the wheel rim so that any relative movement (slippage) between the tire and rim—which would alter valve stem position and cause tube damage—can be immediately detected during preflight or inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Tires)

Landing Gear.question: Overinflated aircraft tires may cause damage to the

choices.B: wheel hub.
choices.C: wheel flange.

Landing Gear.explanation: An overinflated tire has a smaller contact footprint and sits high on the tread center, concentrating stress on the wheel flanges during taxi and landing loads, which can crack or break the flange. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Tires)

Landing Gear.question: Debooster valves are used in brake systems primarily to

choices.A: ensure rapid application and release of the brakes.
choices.B: reduce brake pressure and maintain static pressure.
choices.C: reduce the pressure and release the brakes rapidly.

Landing Gear.explanation: Debooster valves reduce incoming high hydraulic pressure to a level appropriate for the brake assemblies and maintain that reduced static pressure to keep the brakes applied without creep, ensuring consistent and safe braking force. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: The repair for an out-of-tolerance toe-in condition of main landing gear wheels determined not to be the result of bent or twisted components consists of

choices.A: shimming the axle in the oleo trunnion.
choices.B: inserting, removing, or changing the location of washers or spacers at the center pivotal point of the scissor torque links.
choices.C: placing shims or spacers behind the bearing of the out-of-tolerance wheel or wheels.

Landing Gear.explanation: When toe-in is out of tolerance but no structural damage is present, adding, removing, or repositioning shims or spacers behind the wheel bearing changes the angular relationship of the wheel to the axle, correcting the alignment to within specification. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Alignment)

Landing Gear.question: Power boost brake systems are used on aircraft that have

choices.B: low normal hydraulic system pressure.
choices.C: more than one brake assembly per axle.

Landing Gear.explanation: Power boost brake systems supplement the pilot's pedal input with hydraulic system pressure, providing the braking force necessary to decelerate large, heavy aircraft that land at high speeds where pedal effort alone would be insufficient. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Aircraft Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: What is the purpose of the torque links attached to the cylinder and piston of a landing gear oleo strut?

choices.B: Hold the strut in place.
choices.C: Maintain correct wheel alignment.

Landing Gear.explanation: Torque links (scissors) connect the outer cylinder to the inner piston of an oleo strut and prevent relative rotation between the two, ensuring that the wheel and tire maintain correct directional alignment with the aircraft centerline throughout strut travel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Shock Struts)

Landing Gear.question: The removal, installation, and repair of landing gear tires by the holder of a private pilot certificate on an aircraft owned or operated is considered to be

choices.A: a violation of the Federal Aviation Regulations.
choices.B: a minor repair.
choices.C: preventive maintenance.

Landing Gear.explanation: 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix A, identifies tire removal, installation, and repair as preventive maintenance that a certificated private pilot may perform on an aircraft they own or operate, without requiring an AMT certificate. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Maintenance)

Landing Gear.question: Aircraft brakes requiring a large volume of fluid to operate the brakes generally

choices.B: do not use brake system accumulators.
choices.C: use power brake control valves.

Landing Gear.explanation: Large-volume brake systems (such as those on transport-category aircraft) require a power brake control valve fed by the main hydraulic system to supply the high fluid volume needed, rather than small independent master cylinders which cannot supply sufficient volume. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: What is one effect a restricted compensator port of a master cylinder will have on a brake system?

choices.B: The reservoir will be filled by reverse flow.
choices.C: The restriction will cause slow release of the brakes.

Landing Gear.explanation: The compensator port allows fluid to return to the reservoir when brake pressure is released; if it is restricted, fluid cannot return quickly enough, causing the brakes to release slowly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: When an air/oil type of landing gear shock strut is used, the initial shock of landing is cushioned by

choices.B: the fluid being forced through a metered opening.
choices.C: compression of the fluid.

Landing Gear.explanation: In an air/oil shock strut, the initial landing impact is absorbed by hydraulic fluid being forced through a metered orifice, which dissipates energy as heat; the air charge acts as a spring for the secondary cushioning and extension stroke. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear)

Landing Gear.question: Internal leakage in a brake master cylinder unit can cause

choices.B: slow release of brakes.
choices.C: the pedal to slowly creep down while pedal pressure is applied.

Landing Gear.explanation: Internal leakage past the master cylinder piston allows fluid to bypass under sustained pedal pressure, causing the pedal to slowly creep or sink toward the floor while pressure is applied. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: A sleeve, spacer, or bumper ring is incorporated in a landing gear oleo shock strut

choices.A: limit the extension of the torque arm.
choices.B: limit the extension stroke.
choices.C: reduce the rebound effect.

Landing Gear.explanation: A sleeve, spacer, or bumper ring inside the strut acts as a positive mechanical stop that prevents the strut from extending beyond its design limit, protecting the internal components and seals. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear)

Landing Gear.question: Which statement is true with respect to an aircraft equipped with hydraulically operated multiple-disc type brake assemblies?

choices.A: There are no minimum or maximum disc clearance checks required due to the use of self-compensating cylinder assemblies.
choices.B: Do not set parking brake when brakes are hot.
choices.C: No parking brake provisions are possible for this type of brake assembly.

Landing Gear.explanation: Setting the parking brake while the brakes are hot can cause warping of discs and brake components or result in brake dragging after cooling due to thermal contraction, so the FAA advises against this practice. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: When servicing an air/oil shock strut with MIL-5606 the strut should be

choices.A: collapsed and fluid added at the filler opening.
choices.B: fully extended and fluid added at the filler opening.
choices.C: partially extended and fluid added at the filler opening.

Landing Gear.explanation: When servicing an air/oil strut with hydraulic fluid, the strut must be fully collapsed so that the air charge is released first and the correct fluid volume can be added without air being trapped above the fluid. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear)

Landing Gear.question: Instructions concerning the type of fluid and amount of air pressure to be put in a shock strut are found

choices.B: in the aircraft operations limitations.
choices.C: in the aircraft manufacturer's service manual.

Landing Gear.explanation: Shock strut servicing specifications, including approved fluid type and required air pressure, are aircraft-specific and are found in the aircraft manufacturer's service manual, which is the authoritative maintenance reference. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear)

Landing Gear.question: The purpose of a relief valve in a brake system is to

choices.B: prevent the tire from skidding.
choices.C: compensate for thermal expansion.

Landing Gear.explanation: A relief valve in the brake system protects against thermally induced pressure buildup caused by heat expansion of trapped brake fluid, venting excess pressure back to the reservoir to prevent brake drag or line failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Aircraft tire pressure should be checked

choices.A: using only a push on stick-type gauge having one-pound increments.
choices.B: at least once a week or more often.
choices.C: as soon as possible after each flight.

Landing Gear.explanation: Tire pressure should be checked at least once a week or more frequently because pressure loss occurs naturally over time and maintaining correct inflation is critical to safe aircraft operation and tire longevity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Tires)

Landing Gear.question: If the extended longitudinal axis of the main landing gear wheel assemblies intersects aft of the aircraft, the wheels can be termed as having

choices.B: toe-in.
choices.C: negative camber.

Landing Gear.explanation: When the extended longitudinal axes of both main gear wheels converge and meet aft of the aircraft, the leading edges of the tires point inward toward each other, which is the definition of toe-in. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Alignment)

Landing Gear.question: What is the purpose of a compensating port or valve in a brake master cylinder of an independent brake system?

choices.A: Permits the fluid to flow toward or away from the reservoir as temperature changes.
choices.B: Assists in the master cylinder piston return.
choices.C: Prevents fluid from flowing back to the reservoir.

Landing Gear.explanation: The compensating port connects the brake master cylinder to the reservoir, allowing fluid to flow in either direction to accommodate volumetric changes caused by temperature fluctuations and to ensure the system is always fully primed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Brake Systems)

Landing Gear.question: If an aircraft shock strut (air/oil type) bottoms upon initial landing contact, but functions correctly during taxi, the most probable cause is

choices.B: low air charge.
choices.C: a restricted metering pin orifice.

Landing Gear.explanation: Bottoming on initial hard landing contact but functioning normally during the lighter loads of taxi indicates adequate fluid but insufficient air pressure to resist the high dynamic impact load; low fluid would cause bottoming during taxi as well. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear)

Landing Gear.question: What is the function of a cam incorporated in a nose gear shock strut?

choices.B: Straightens the nosewheel.
choices.C: Provides steering of aircraft during ground operation.

Landing Gear.explanation: A cam mechanism in the nose gear strut engages as the strut compresses upon touchdown, rotating the nosewheel and fork to the straight-ahead (centered) position so the aircraft tracks properly on landing rollout. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear)

Landing Gear.question: Extension of an oleo shock strut is measured to determine the

choices.A: amount of oil in the strut.
choices.B: physical condition of the strut itself.
choices.C: proper operating position of the strut.

Landing Gear.explanation: The exposed length of the strut piston (extension) is directly related to how much fluid is inside; measuring extension against the manufacturer's specification is the standard method used to verify correct fluid level. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear)

Landing Gear.question: Debooster cylinders are used in brake systems primarily to

choices.A: reduce brake pressure and maintain static pressure.
choices.B: relieve excessive fluid and ensure a positive release.
choices.C: reduce the pressure to the brake and increase the volume of fluid flow.

Landing Gear.explanation: Debooster cylinders serve a dual purpose: they reduce incoming hydraulic pressure to a level appropriate for the brakes while simultaneously increasing the volume of fluid delivered, ensuring smooth and controlled braking without excessive line pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: If a shock strut bottoms after it has been properly serviced, the

choices.A: strut should be disassembled and the metering pin orifice plate replaced.
choices.B: air pressure should be increased.
choices.C: strut should be removed, disassembled, and inspected.

Landing Gear.explanation: If a properly serviced strut bottoms out (fully compresses) during landing, it indicates an internal mechanical defect beyond simple servicing issues, requiring removal, disassembly, and inspection to identify the root cause. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: A high-speed aircraft tire with a sound cord body and bead may be recapped

choices.B: only by the tire manufacturer.
choices.C: an indefinite number of times.

Landing Gear.explanation: FAA standards allow high-speed aircraft tires with a structurally sound cord body and bead to be retreaded an indefinite number of times, provided each retread meets applicable specifications and the tire passes inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: If an airplane equipped with master cylinders and single-disc brakes has excessive brake pedal travel, but the brakes are hard and effective, the probable cause is

choices.A: the master cylinder one-way cup is leaking.
choices.B: worn brake linings.
choices.C: worn brake disc causing excessive clearance between the notches on the perimeter of the disc and the splines or keys on the wheel.

Landing Gear.explanation: Worn brake linings increase the distance the piston must travel to bring the friction material into contact with the disc, resulting in excessive pedal travel; however, once contact is made the brakes still function effectively because the hydraulic system itself is intact. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: The correct inflation pressure for an aircraft tire can be obtained from

choices.B: the aircraft service manual.
choices.C: the information stamped on the aircraft wheel.

Landing Gear.explanation: The aircraft service manual is the authoritative source for correct tire inflation pressures because it accounts for the specific aircraft's weight, load ratings, and operational requirements for each wheel position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: What should be checked when a shock strut bottoms during a landing?

choices.B: Packing seals for correct installation.
choices.C: Fluid level.

Landing Gear.explanation: A strut that bottoms during landing despite correct air pressure indicates insufficient hydraulic fluid, as the fluid provides the incompressible cushion needed to supplement the air charge and absorb landing loads. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: The hydraulic packing seals used in a landing gear shock strut are

choices.A: generally designed to be compatible with more than one type of fluid.
choices.B: kept from direct contact with fluid by teflon or nylon backup rings.
choices.C: used only with a specific type of fluid.

Landing Gear.explanation: Hydraulic packing seals in shock struts are manufactured to be compatible only with a specific hydraulic fluid type (e.g., MIL-PRF-5606 or MIL-PRF-87257), because mixing fluid types can cause seal degradation and system failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: A close inspection of a fusible plug reveals the core has experienced some deformation. What is the appropriate maintenance procedure?

choices.B: Replace the affected fusible plugs.
choices.C: Replace with new wheel assembly.

Landing Gear.explanation: Because fusible plugs on a wheel assembly are all exposed to the same heat event simultaneously, industry and FAA practice require replacing all fusible plugs as a set when any one shows signs of heat deformation, ensuring no compromised plug remains in service. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Chines are used on some aircraft nose wheel tires to help

choices.A: nose gear extension at higher air speeds.
choices.B: reduce the possibility of hydroplaning.
choices.C: deflect water away from the fuselage.

Landing Gear.explanation: Chines are molded ridges on nose wheel tires specifically designed to deflect water, slush, and debris outward and away from the fuselage-mounted engines or airframe during ground operations in wet conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: The best safeguards against heat buildup in aircraft tires are

choices.A: proper tire inflation, minimum braking, and ground rolls into the wind.
choices.B: short ground rolls, slow taxi speeds, minimum braking, and proper tire inflation.
choices.C: minimum braking, proper tire inflation, and long ground rolls.

Landing Gear.explanation: Heat buildup in aircraft tires is best minimized by combining short ground rolls, slow taxi speeds, minimum brake application, and maintaining proper tire inflation, all of which reduce frictional and flexing heat generation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: The fusible plugs installed in some aircraft wheels will

choices.B: prevent overinflation.
choices.C: melt at a specified elevated temperature.

Landing Gear.explanation: Fusible plugs are designed with a low-melting-point alloy core that melts at a specific elevated temperature caused by wheel brake overheat, allowing the tire to deflate in a controlled manner and preventing a potentially dangerous tire explosion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: What action, if any, should be taken when there is a difference of more than 5 pounds of air pressure in tires mounted as duals?

choices.B: Correct the discrepancy and enter in the aircraft records.
choices.C: Replace the tire with the lowest pressure.

Landing Gear.explanation: A pressure difference greater than 5 psi between dual-mounted tires causes unequal load sharing, accelerated wear, and handling issues, so the discrepancy must be corrected and documented in the aircraft maintenance records. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: How long should the aviation mechanic wait after a flight before checking tire pressure?

choices.A: At least 2 hours (3 hours in hot weather).
choices.B: At least 3 hours (4 hours in hot weather).
choices.C: At least 4 hours (5 hours in hot weather).

Landing Gear.explanation: FAA guidance specifies waiting at least 3 hours after flight before checking tire pressure (4 hours in hot weather) to allow heat generated during taxi, takeoff, and landing to dissipate so an accurate cold-inflation reading can be obtained. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: What is the minimum amount of time to wait for tires to cool before checking tire pressure in hot weather?

choices.B: 2 hours.
choices.C: 1 hour.

Landing Gear.explanation: In hot weather conditions, a minimum of 3 hours must be allowed for tires to cool before checking inflation pressure, since ambient heat combined with operational heat takes longer to dissipate and an inaccurate reading could lead to underinflation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Excessive wear in the shoulder area of an aircraft tire is an indication of

choices.B: excessive toe-in.
choices.C: underinflation.

Landing Gear.explanation: Underinflation causes the tire sidewalls to flex excessively, shifting the contact patch load toward the shoulders rather than the center tread, resulting in accelerated shoulder wear and potential structural damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Excessive wear in the center of the tread of an aircraft tire is an indication of

choices.B: underinflation.
choices.C: overinflation.

Landing Gear.explanation: Overinflation causes the tire to bulge in the center, concentrating contact and wear in the middle of the tread, while the shoulders remain relatively unworn. Underinflation causes wear on both edges, and misalignment causes wear on one side. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: When servicing an empty shock strut with fluid, the strut should be completely compressed and extended several times to ensure

choices.A: the piston rod and wiper are lubricated with hydraulic fluid.
choices.B: all excess hydraulic fluid is forced out of the strut.
choices.C: proper packing ring seating and removal of air bubbles.

Landing Gear.explanation: Compressing and extending the strut several times during servicing ensures that air pockets (bubbles) are expelled and that the packing seals seat properly, which is critical for correct strut operation and leak prevention. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: In shock struts, chevron seals are used to

choices.B: prevent oil from escaping.
choices.C: serve as a bearing surface.

Landing Gear.explanation: Chevron (V-shaped) seals in shock struts are dynamic seals specifically designed to prevent hydraulic fluid from escaping past the piston rod under pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: On most aircraft, the oil level of an air and oil shock strut is checked by

choices.A: removing the oil filler plug and inserting a gauge.
choices.B: measuring the length of the strut extension with a certain air pressure in the strut.
choices.C: releasing the air and seeing that the oil is to the level of the filler plug.

Landing Gear.explanation: On most air-oil shock struts, the correct procedure for checking fluid level is to fully deflate (release) the air charge and then verify the oil is at the level of the filler plug opening, ensuring an accurate reading unaffected by air pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: An electric motor used to raise and lower a landing gear would most likely be a

choices.B: split field shunt-wound motor.
choices.C: split field series-wound motor.

Landing Gear.explanation: A split field series-wound motor is used for landing gear actuation because it provides high torque, can be reversed by switching field polarity, and the series winding delivers the strong starting torque needed to raise and lower heavy gear assemblies. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Nose gear centering cams are used in many retractable landing gear systems. The primary purpose of the centering device is to

choices.A: align the nosewheel prior to touchdown.
choices.B: engage the nosewheel steering.
choices.C: center the nosewheel before it enters the wheel well.

Landing Gear.explanation: Nose gear centering cams mechanically align (center) the nosewheel in the straight-ahead position during retraction so it will fit properly into the wheel well without contacting the structure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: A special bolt in a landing gear attachment requires a torque value of 440 inch-pounds. How many foot-pounds are required?
Landing Gear.explanation: Converting inch-pounds to foot-pounds requires dividing by 12; 440 ÷ 12 = 36.666…, which rounds to 36.8 foot-pounds as listed in answer A. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: To be eligible for recharging, a DOT 3HT oxygen cylinder must have been hydrostatically tested every three years and be retired from service after

choices.B: 15 years or 10,000 filling cycles.
choices.C: 10 years or 5,000 filling cycles.

Landing Gear.explanation: DOT 3HT cylinders must be hydrostatically tested every three years and are required to be permanently retired from service after 24 years of use or 4,380 filling cycles, whichever comes first, per DOT regulations referenced in FAA maintenance standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 15 — Oxygen Systems)

Landing Gear.question: If it is necessary to enter an aircraft's fuel tank, which procedure should be avoided?

choices.A: Continue purging the tank during the entire work period.
choices.B: Station an assistant outside the fuel tank access to perform rescue operations if required.
choices.C: Conduct the defueling and tank purging operation in an air-conditioned building.

Landing Gear.explanation: Conducting defueling and tank purging inside an air-conditioned (enclosed) building creates an extreme fire and explosion hazard by concentrating flammable fuel vapors in a confined space; such operations must be performed outdoors or in well-ventilated areas. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Which motor would be most likely to have an armature brake?

choices.B: Landing light retraction motor.
choices.C: Inverter drive motor.

Landing Gear.explanation: Landing light retraction motors require an armature brake to hold the light door positively in the open or closed position and prevent back-driving from aerodynamic loads when the motor is not energized. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: How does the magnetic brake used to stop rotation of an electric motor armature operate?

choices.A: Centrifugal force releases a rotating brake cog from a stationary notch when the armature reaches a certain speed and magnetic force re-engages the cog when the electrical power is turned off.
choices.B: A friction brake is applied by a magnet and released by a spring.
choices.C: A friction brake is applied by a spring and released by a magnet.

Landing Gear.explanation: In a magnetic armature brake, a spring mechanically applies the friction brake to stop rotation, and when electrical power is supplied to the motor, the electromagnet overcomes the spring force to release the brake and allow the armature to spin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: An antiskid system is designed to

choices.A: solely sense the deceleration rate of every main landing gear wheel.
choices.B: release then reapply pressure at a slightly lower value only when a skid is detected.
choices.C: sense the deceleration rate of every main landing gear wheel and release then reapply pressure at a slightly lower value when a skid is detected.

Landing Gear.explanation: An antiskid system performs both functions simultaneously: it continuously monitors the deceleration rate of each main gear wheel and, when an impending skid is detected, releases and then reapplies hydraulic brake pressure at a slightly reduced value to maintain maximum braking efficiency without a full skid. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: In a brake antiskid system, when an approaching skid is sensed, an electrical signal is sent to the skid control valve which

choices.A: acts as a bypass for the debooster cylinders.
choices.B: relieves the hydraulic pressure on the brake.
choices.C: equalizes the hydraulic pressure in adjacent brakes.

Landing Gear.explanation: When the skid control valve receives the electrical signal indicating an impending skid, it opens to relieve (dump) hydraulic pressure from the affected brake, allowing the wheel to spin up and recover traction before pressure is reapplied. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: An antiskid system is

choices.B: an electrohydraulic system.
choices.C: an electrical system.

Landing Gear.explanation: An antiskid system is electrohydraulic because it uses electrical sensors and control units to detect wheel skid conditions and then modulates hydraulic brake pressure through electrically controlled skid control valves. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Antiskid braking systems are generally armed by

choices.B: a switch in the cockpit.
choices.C: the rotation of the wheels above a certain speed.

Landing Gear.explanation: Antiskid systems are armed by a dedicated cockpit switch that the flight crew activates, enabling the system to become operational; wheel speed or brake application alone does not arm the system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: When an airplane is slowed below approximately 20 mph, the antiskid system automatically activates to give the pilot full control of the brakes for maneuvering and parking.
choices.B: An antiskid system consists of two components; wheel speed sensors and control valves.
choices.C: When an airplane is slowed below approximately 20 mph, the antiskid system automatically deactivates to give the pilot full control of the brakes for maneuvering and parking.

Landing Gear.explanation: The antiskid system automatically deactivates below approximately 20 mph so the pilot has full manual brake control for low-speed maneuvering and parking, preventing the system from interfering with ground handling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: In an antiskid system, wheel skid is detected by

choices.B: a discriminator.
choices.C: a sudden rise in brake pressure.

Landing Gear.explanation: Wheel skid is detected by an electrical sensor (wheel speed transducer/generator) that monitors wheel rotational speed and sends signals to the control unit to detect deceleration indicative of a skid. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Which of the following functions does a skid control system perform?

choices.A: Normal skid control, normal braking, fail safe protection, locked wheel skid control.
choices.B: Normal skid control, fail safe protection, locked wheel skid control, touchdown protection.
choices.C: Normal skid control, normal braking, touchdown protection, takeoff protection.

Landing Gear.explanation: A complete antiskid system performs four functions: normal skid control, fail-safe protection, locked wheel skid control, and touchdown protection; normal braking and takeoff protection are not standard antiskid functions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: In the air with the antiskid armed, current cannot flow to the antiskid control box because the landing gear

choices.B: down and lock switch is open.
choices.C: antiskid valves are open.

Landing Gear.explanation: The squat switch (weight-on-wheels switch) is open when the aircraft is airborne, breaking the circuit to the antiskid control box to prevent inadvertent brake application in flight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: At what point in the landing operation does normal skid control perform its function?

choices.A: When wheel rotation deceleration indicates an impending skid.
choices.B: When wheel rotation indicates hydroplaning condition.
choices.C: Anytime the wheel is rotating.

Landing Gear.explanation: Normal skid control continuously monitors wheel deceleration and intervenes when the rate of deceleration indicates an impending skid, modulating brake pressure before the wheel actually locks up. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Which of the following conditions is most likely to cause the landing gear warning signal to sound?

choices.A: Landing gear locked down and throttle advanced.
choices.B: Landing gear locked down and throttle retarded.
choices.C: Landing gear not locked down and throttle retarded.

Landing Gear.explanation: The gear warning horn is designed to alert the crew when the throttle is retarded (approach configuration) and the landing gear is not in the down-and-locked position, signaling a potentially unsafe condition for landing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Where is the landing gear safety switch usually located?

choices.A: On the main gear shock strut.
choices.B: On the landing gear drag brace.
choices.C: On the pilot's control pedestal.

Landing Gear.explanation: The landing gear safety (squat) switch is located on the main gear shock strut so that it can sense whether the aircraft's weight is on the gear, activating or deactivating systems accordingly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: What safety device is actuated by the compression and extension of a landing gear strut?

choices.B: Downlock switch.
choices.C: Ground safety switch.

Landing Gear.explanation: The ground safety switch is actuated by the compression and extension of the landing gear strut, sensing weight-on-wheels to prevent inadvertent gear retraction on the ground and control other related systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Which repair would require a landing gear retraction test?

choices.B: Red warning light bulb.
choices.C: Gear downlock microswitch.

Landing Gear.explanation: A gear downlock microswitch directly affects the gear position indicating and warning system logic, so replacing or adjusting it requires a full retraction test to verify proper system operation and switch rigging. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: Landing gear warning systems usually provide which of the following indications?

choices.A: Red light for unsafe gear; green light for gear up.
choices.B: Green light for gear up and down; red light for unsafe gear.
choices.C: Red light for unsafe gear; green light for gear down; no light for gear up.

Landing Gear.explanation: Standard landing gear indication uses a red light to show an unsafe (in-transit or unlocked) condition, a green light to confirm all gear are down and locked, and no light to indicate gear is up and locked. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: When a landing gear safety switch on a main gear strut closes at liftoff, which system is deactivated?

choices.B: Antiskid system.
choices.C: Aural warning system.

Landing Gear.explanation: When the squat switch opens at liftoff (strut extends), it deactivates the antiskid system so that it does not interfere with wheel spin-up or provide unwanted brake commands while the aircraft is airborne. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: The proper fire-extinguishing agent to use on an aircraft brake fire is

choices.B: carbon dioxide.
choices.C: dry powder chemical.

Landing Gear.explanation: Dry powder chemical extinguishing agent is recommended for aircraft brake fires because brakes involve extremely high temperatures and burning metals where water can cause dangerous steam explosions and CO2 is ineffective on metal fires. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: An amber light in a gear position indicator system typically indicates what condition?

choices.A: At least one gear is in transition.
choices.B: The airspeed is too high for selecting gear down.
choices.C: All landing gear are down and locked.

Landing Gear.explanation: An amber light in a gear position indicator system indicates that at least one gear is in transition (neither up-and-locked nor down-and-locked), alerting the crew to an intermediate or unsafe gear state. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: (Refer to Figure 10.) The trunnion nut on an aircraft landing gear requires a torque of 320 inch-pounds. To reach the nut, a 2-inch straight adapter must be used on an 18-inch torque wrench. How many foot-pounds will be indicated on the torque wrench when the required torque of the nut is reached?
Landing Gear.explanation: Using the extension formula TW = (TN × L) / (L + E) = (320 × 18) / (18 + 2) = 5760 / 20 = 288 inch-pounds, which converts to 288 / 12 = 24 foot-pounds indicated on the torque wrench. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: (Refer to Figure 19.) Upon completion of the landing gear extension cycle, the green light illuminated and the red light remained lit. What is the probable cause?

choices.A: Short in the down limit switch.
choices.B: Short in the gear safety switch.
choices.C: Short in the up limit switch.

Landing Gear.explanation: If the green (down) light illuminates but the red light stays on after gear extension, it indicates the up-limit switch circuit is still closed (shorted), falsely signaling that the gear is still in the up position even though it is down and locked. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: (Refer to Figure 19.) What is the indication of the red landing gear position light under the following conditions? Aircraft on jacks. Landing gear in transit. Warning horn sounding.

choices.B: flashing.
choices.C: illuminated.

Landing Gear.explanation: When the aircraft is on jacks and the landing gear is in transit with the warning horn sounding, the red gear-in-transit light is extinguished because the squat switch (weight-on-wheels switch) opens the circuit to the red indicator when the aircraft is on the ground/jacks, preventing the light from illuminating regardless of gear position. The squat switch logic removes power from the red light circuit under ground conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: (Refer to Figure 20.) What will illuminate the amber indicator light?

choices.A: Closing the nosewheel gear full retract switch.
choices.B: Retarding one throttle and closing the left wheel gear locked down switch.
choices.C: Closing the nose, left and right wheel gear full retract switches.

Landing Gear.explanation: Closing the nosewheel gear full retract switch completes the circuit that illuminates the amber indicator light, signaling that the nose gear has reached its fully retracted position as designed in the gear indication circuit shown in Figure 20. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Landing Gear.question: (Refer to Figure 20.) What is the minimum circumstance that will cause the landing gear warning horn to indicate an unsafe condition?

choices.A: All gears up and one throttle retarded.
choices.B: Any gear up and both throttles retarded.
choices.C: Any gear not down and locked, and one throttle retarded.

Landing Gear.explanation: The warning horn is designed to alert the crew to an unsafe landing condition whenever any single gear is not down and locked combined with at least one throttle being retarded (indicating a possible approach to landing), making this the minimum condition required to trigger the unsafe indication. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Which statement is true regarding a cantilever wing?

choices.B: No external bracing is needed.
choices.C: It requires only one lift strut on each side.

Flight Controls.explanation: A cantilever wing is internally braced by its own structural members (spars, ribs, stringers) and requires no external struts, wires, or bracing to support the aerodynamic loads. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Structures)

Flight Controls.question: If it is necessary to compute a bend allowance problem and bend allowance tables are not available, the neutral axis of the bend can be

choices.A: represented by the actual length of the required material for the bend.
choices.B: found by adding approximately one-half of the stock thickness to the bend radius.
choices.C: found by subtracting the stock thickness from the bend radius.

Flight Controls.explanation: The neutral axis of a bend is located at a distance equal to the inside bend radius plus approximately one-half the stock thickness, representing the theoretical line within the material that neither stretches nor compresses, and is used to calculate the true developed length of material needed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Aircraft Metal Structural Repair)

Flight Controls.question: Unless otherwise specified, the radius of a bend is the

choices.A: inside radius of the metal being formed.
choices.B: inside radius plus one-half the thickness of the metal being formed.
choices.C: radius of the neutral axis plus one-half the thickness of the metal being formed.

Flight Controls.explanation: By FAA and industry convention, the bend radius specified in engineering drawings and repair manuals refers to the inside radius of the bend, measured from the inside curve of the bent material. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Aircraft Metal Structural Repair)

Flight Controls.question: When bending metal, the material on the outside of the curve stretches while the material on the inside of the curve compresses. That part of the material which is not affected by either stress is the

choices.B: bend tangent line.
choices.C: neutral line.

Flight Controls.explanation: The neutral line (or neutral axis) is the theoretical line within the thickness of the metal that experiences neither tensile nor compressive stress during bending, making it the basis for calculating bend allowance and developed length. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Aircraft Metal Structural Repair)

Flight Controls.question: The oxyacetylene flame for silver soldering should be

choices.B: neutral.
choices.C: carburizing.

Flight Controls.explanation: A neutral oxyacetylene flame is used for silver soldering because it provides sufficient heat without introducing excess oxygen (which would cause oxidation) or excess acetylene (which would carburize the base metal or filler), ensuring a clean, strong bond. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Welding)

Flight Controls.question: The oxyacetylene flame used for aluminum welding should

choices.B: be slightly oxidizing.
choices.C: contain an excess of acetylene and leave the tip at a relatively low speed.

Flight Controls.explanation: Aluminum welding requires a neutral, soft flame to deliver adequate heat without oxidizing the metal or introducing carbon; a hard flame can blow through the thin oxide layer and damage the base metal due to aluminum's narrow melting range. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Welding)

Flight Controls.question: Filing or grinding a weld bead is considered to be

choices.A: an acceptable means to achieve a smoother surface.
choices.B: a way to bring about strength reduction in joint.
choices.C: frequently necessary to avoid adding excess weight to a weld joint.

Flight Controls.explanation: Filing or grinding a weld bead removes material from the weld and creates stress concentrations and notches that reduce the cross-sectional area and introduce points of weakness, thereby reducing the strength of the joint. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Welding)

Flight Controls.question: Acetylene at a line pressure above 15 psi is

choices.B: used when a reducing flame is necessary.
choices.C: usually necessary when welding metal over 3/8-inch thick.

Flight Controls.explanation: Acetylene becomes chemically unstable and potentially explosive at pressures above 15 psi because the molecular structure of acetylene is inherently unstable under compression, and above this threshold it can decompose spontaneously without the presence of oxygen. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Welding)

Flight Controls.question: How should a welding torch flame be adjusted to weld stainless steel?

choices.B: Slightly oxidizing.
choices.C: Neutral.

Flight Controls.explanation: Stainless steel is welded with a slightly carburizing (excess acetylene) flame to prevent chromium oxidation and carbide precipitation at the weld zone, preserving the corrosion resistance and mechanical properties of the stainless steel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Aircraft Welding)

Flight Controls.question: Wing dihedral, a rigging consideration on most airplanes of conventional design, contributes most to stability of the airplane about its

choices.B: vertical axis.
choices.C: lateral axis.

Flight Controls.explanation: Wing dihedral (upward angle of the wings from root to tip) provides lateral stability by creating a restoring rolling moment when the aircraft slips, thus contributing primarily to stability about the longitudinal axis (roll axis). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Structures)

Flight Controls.question: Other than the manufacturer maintenance manual what other document could be used to determine the primary flight control surface deflection for an imported aircraft that is reassembled after shipment?

choices.B: Import manual for the aircraft.
choices.C: The certificate of airworthiness issued by the importing country.

Flight Controls.explanation: The Type Certificate Data Sheet (TCDS) is an FAA-approved document that lists the approved aircraft configuration, limitations, and rigging data including control surface deflection limits, making it an authoritative reference when the manufacturer's maintenance manual is unavailable. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: An airplane which has good longitudinal stability should have a minimum tendency to

choices.B: pitch.
choices.C: yaw.

Flight Controls.explanation: Longitudinal stability refers to the aircraft's tendency to resist and return from pitching motions about the lateral axis, so an airplane with good longitudinal stability will have a minimum tendency to pitch. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Aircraft Structures)

Flight Controls.question: The angle of incidence is that acute angle formed by

choices.A: the angular difference between the setting of the main airfoil and the auxiliary airfoil (horizontal stabilizer) in reference to the longitudinal axis of the aircraft.
choices.B: a line parallel to the wing chord and a line parallel to the longitudinal axis of the aircraft.
choices.C: a line parallel to the wing from root to tip and a line parallel to the lateral axis of the aircraft.

Flight Controls.explanation: The angle of incidence is specifically defined as the acute angle between the wing chord line and the longitudinal axis of the aircraft, representing the fixed built-in angle at which the wing is attached to the fuselage. This is a fixed geometric relationship established during manufacture. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Aerodynamics)

Flight Controls.question: An airplane's center of lift is usually located aft of its center of gravity

choices.A: so that the airplane will have a tail-heavy tendency.
choices.B: so that the airplane will have a nose-heavy tendency.
choices.C: to improve stability about the longitudinal axis.

Flight Controls.explanation: When the center of lift is located aft of the center of gravity, the aircraft naturally pitches nose-down (nose-heavy tendency), which is the preferred design condition because it makes the aircraft stable and recoverable — the horizontal stabilizer then provides a downward force to maintain level flight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Aerodynamics)

Flight Controls.question: An airplane is controlled directionally about its vertical axis by the

choices.B: elevator(s).
choices.C: ailerons.

Flight Controls.explanation: The rudder controls yaw, which is rotation about the vertical axis, allowing the pilot to direct the nose of the aircraft left or right. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Controls)

Flight Controls.question: The elevators of a conventional airplane are used to provide rotation about the

choices.B: lateral axis.
choices.C: vertical axis.

Flight Controls.explanation: The elevators control pitch, which is rotation about the lateral axis (the axis running wingtip to wingtip), causing the nose to move up or down. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Controls)

Flight Controls.question: Washing-in the left wing of a monoplane, for purposes of rigging corrections after flight test, will have what effect on the lift and drag of that wing?

choices.A: Both drag and lift will decrease due to decreased angle of attack.
choices.B: Both drag and lift will increase due to increased angle of attack.
choices.C: The drag will decrease due to the effect of the lift increase.

Flight Controls.explanation: Washing-in a wing increases its angle of incidence, which raises the angle of attack on that wing, directly increasing both lift and induced drag on that wing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Rigging and Alignment)

Flight Controls.question: What type of flap system increases the wing area and changes the wing camber?

choices.B: Slotted flaps.
choices.C: Split flaps.

Flight Controls.explanation: Fowler flaps are designed to slide rearward and downward, which physically increases the total wing area as well as increasing camber, providing greater lift with relatively less drag compared to other flap types. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — High-Lift Devices)

Flight Controls.question: If the right wing of a monoplane is improperly rigged to a greater angle of incidence than designated in the manufacturer's specifications, it will cause the

choices.A: airplane to be off balance both laterally and directionally.
choices.B: airplane to pitch and roll about the lateral axis.
choices.C: right wing to have both an increased lift and a decreased drag.

Flight Controls.explanation: If one wing is rigged to a greater angle of incidence than the other, it generates more lift and more drag than the opposite wing, causing the aircraft to roll toward the low-lift wing (lateral imbalance) and yaw toward the high-drag wing (directional imbalance). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Rigging and Alignment)

Flight Controls.question: Improper rigging of the elevator trim tab system will affect the balance of the airplane about its

choices.B: longitudinal axis.
choices.C: vertical axis.

Flight Controls.explanation: The elevator trim tab system controls pitch trim, which is the balance of the aircraft about its lateral axis; improper rigging will cause the aircraft to be out of pitch trim, requiring constant elevator pressure to maintain level flight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Controls)

Flight Controls.question: An airplane that has a tendency to gradually increase a pitching moment that has been set into motion has

choices.B: good lateral stability.
choices.C: poor lateral stability.

Flight Controls.explanation: Longitudinal stability refers to the aircraft's tendency to return to level flight after a pitch disturbance; an aircraft that progressively increases a pitching moment rather than correcting it demonstrates poor longitudinal (pitch) stability. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Aerodynamics)

Flight Controls.question: Buffeting is the intermittent application of forces to a part of an airplane. It is caused by

choices.B: an unsteady flow from turbulence.
choices.C: incorrect rigging of ailerons.

Flight Controls.explanation: Buffeting results from turbulent, unsteady airflow impacting aircraft surfaces — such as turbulent wake from a stalled surface striking a downstream component — rather than from control surface rigging errors. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Aerodynamics)

Flight Controls.question: Movement of an airplane along its lateral axis (roll) is also movement

choices.A: around or about the longitudinal axis controlled by the elevator.
choices.B: around or about the lateral axis controlled by the ailerons.
choices.C: around or about the longitudinal axis controlled by the ailerons.

Flight Controls.explanation: Roll is movement about the longitudinal axis (the axis running nose to tail), and it is controlled by the ailerons, which create differential lift between the two wings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Controls)

Flight Controls.question: Rigging and alignment checks should not be undertaken in the open. However, if this cannot be avoided, the aircraft should be positioned with the

choices.B: left wing into the wind.
choices.C: nose into the wind.

Flight Controls.explanation: When rigging and alignment checks must be performed outdoors, positioning the aircraft with its nose into the wind minimizes the aerodynamic forces acting on the control surfaces, reducing the risk of inaccurate readings caused by wind-induced deflection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Rigging and Alignment)

Flight Controls.question: The vast majority of aircraft control cables are terminated with swaged terminals, that must be

choices.A: corrosion treated to show compliance with the manufacturer's requirements after the swaging operation.
choices.B: pull tested to show compliance with the manufacturer's requirements after the swaging operation.
choices.C: checked with a go-no-go gauge before and after, to show compliance with the manufacturer's requirements after the swaging operation.

Flight Controls.explanation: After swaging, terminals must be pull-tested to the manufacturer's specified load to verify that the swaged connection has the required mechanical strength and integrity before being placed in service. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Cable and Terminal Assembly)

Flight Controls.question: What nondestructive checking method is normally used to ensure that the correct amount of swaging has taken place when installing swaged-type terminals on aircraft control cable?

choices.A: Check the surface of the swaged portion of the terminal for small cracks which indicate incomplete swaging.
choices.B: Measure the finished length of the terminal barrel and compare with the beginning length.
choices.C: Use a terminal gauge to check the diameter of the swaged portion of the terminal.

Flight Controls.explanation: A terminal (go-no-go) gauge is used to verify the finished outer diameter of the swaged barrel meets specifications; if the diameter is within tolerance, the correct amount of material compression has occurred during swaging. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Cable and Terminal Assembly)

Flight Controls.question: When inspecting a control cable turnbuckle for proper installation, determine that

choices.A: no more than four threads are exposed on either side of the turnbuckle barrel.
choices.B: the terminal end threads are visible through the safety hole in the barrel.
choices.C: the safety wire ends are wrapped a minimum of four turns around the terminal end shanks.

Flight Controls.explanation: Proper turnbuckle installation requires that the threaded terminal ends are inserted far enough that no threads are visible from outside, but the terminal threads must be visible through the inspection (safety) hole in the barrel to confirm minimum thread engagement is achieved. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Cable and Terminal Assembly)

Flight Controls.question: If all instructions issued by the swaging tool manufacturer are followed when swaging a cable terminal, the resultant swaged terminal strength should be

choices.A: the full rated strength of the cable.
choices.B: 80 percent of the full rated strength of the cable.
choices.C: 70 percent of the full rated strength of the cable.

Flight Controls.explanation: When a swaged terminal is installed correctly per the manufacturer's instructions, it achieves 100% of the cable's rated breaking strength, making it as strong as the cable itself. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: The purpose of the vertical fin is to provide

choices.B: longitudinal stability.
choices.C: lateral stability.

Flight Controls.explanation: The vertical fin (vertical stabilizer) provides directional stability by resisting yawing motions about the vertical axis, keeping the aircraft aligned with the relative wind. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: How are changes in direction of a control cable accomplished?

choices.B: Bell cranks.
choices.C: Fairleads.

Flight Controls.explanation: Pulleys are used in cable control systems to change the direction of cable travel, allowing cables to be routed around structural members and through the aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What is the smallest size cable that may be used in aircraft primary control systems?

choices.B: 5/16 inch.
choices.C: 1/8 inch.

Flight Controls.explanation: FAA standards specify that the minimum cable size permitted for use in primary flight control systems is 1/8 inch diameter, ensuring adequate strength and reliability. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: After repairing or re-covering a rudder, the surface should be rebalanced

choices.B: in its normal flight position.
choices.C: to manufacturer's specifications.

Flight Controls.explanation: After any repair or re-covering of a control surface, it must be rebalanced to the aircraft manufacturer's specifications to ensure proper control response and to prevent flutter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Placing a piece of cloth around a stainless steel control cable and running it back and forth over the length of the cable is generally a satisfactory method of inspecting for

choices.B: broken strands.
choices.C: excessive wear.

Flight Controls.explanation: Running a cloth along a control cable causes the cloth to snag on broken wire strands, making this a reliable field method for detecting broken wires that might not be visible to the naked eye. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: The cable-operated control system of an all-metal aircraft, not incorporating a temperature compensating device, has been rigged to the correct tension in a heated hangar. If the aircraft is operated in very cold weather, the cable tension will

choices.A: decrease when the aircraft structure and cables become cold.
choices.B: increase when the aircraft structure and cables become cold.
choices.C: be unaffected if stainless steel cable is installed.

Flight Controls.explanation: When an all-metal aircraft and its cables cool down, the aluminum structure contracts more than the steel cables, reducing the effective length of the airframe and thereby increasing cable tension. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Excessive wear on both of the sides of a control cable pulley groove is evidence of

choices.B: cable misalignment.
choices.C: excessive cable tension.

Flight Controls.explanation: Wear on both sides of a pulley groove indicates that the cable is pressing laterally against both walls due to excessive cable tension forcing the cable to ride hard against the groove flanges. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Fairleads should never deflect the alignment of a cable more than
Flight Controls.explanation: Fairleads are designed only to guide cables and must never deflect a cable more than 3 degrees from its original line of travel, as greater deflection causes excessive wear and friction. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Where does the breakage of control cable wires occur most frequently?

choices.A: Breakage usually occurs where cables pass over pulleys and through fairleads.
choices.B: Breakage sites are unpredictable and usually occur randomly anywhere along the length of a cable.
choices.C: Breakage usually occurs where cables are swaged to turnbuckle and ball terminals.

Flight Controls.explanation: Control cable wire breakage occurs most frequently where cables bend over pulleys and pass through fairleads because repeated flexing at these points causes fatigue failure of individual wires. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: With which system is differential control associated?

choices.B: Aileron.
choices.C: Elevator.

Flight Controls.explanation: Differential aileron control is a design in which the up-going aileron deflects more than the down-going aileron to reduce adverse yaw, and is specifically associated with the aileron system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Which statement concerning the 100-hour inspection of an airplane equipped with a push-pull tube-type control system is true?

choices.A: The threaded rod ends should not be adjusted in length for rigging purposes because the rod ends have been properly positioned and staked during manufacture.
choices.B: The terminal end threads of the turnbuckles should be visible through the safety hole in the barrel.
choices.C: The threaded rod ends should be checked for the amount of thread engagement by means of the inspection hole provided.

Flight Controls.explanation: Push-pull tube rod ends have an inspection hole that allows a technician to verify that adequate thread engagement exists, which is the proper inspection method for these components during a 100-hour inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: If control cables are adjusted properly and the control surfaces tend to vibrate, the probable cause is

choices.B: oil can effects on the control surfaces.
choices.C: excessive cable tension.

Flight Controls.explanation: Control surface vibration despite correct cable tension is most likely caused by worn attachment fittings (hinges, horns, or brackets) that allow the surface to move loosely and flutter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Aircraft flight control trim systems must be designed and installed so that the

choices.A: pilot can determine the relative position of the trim tab from the cockpit.
choices.B: operating control and the trim tab will always move in the same direction.
choices.C: trim system will disengage or become inoperative if the primary flight control system fails.

Flight Controls.explanation: 14 CFR and FAA standards require that trim tab systems allow the pilot to determine the trim tab's relative position from the cockpit at all times to ensure the pilot maintains situational awareness of the trim setting. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Stability about the axis which runs parallel to the line of flight is referred to

choices.B: lateral stability.
choices.C: directional stability.

Flight Controls.explanation: Lateral stability is defined as stability about the longitudinal axis, which runs parallel to the line of flight from nose to tail, and involves resistance to rolling motions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 1 — Aircraft Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: The purpose of spring tabs or servo tabs is to

choices.A: assist the pilot in moving the control surfaces.
choices.B: contribute to the static balance of the control surface.
choices.C: make in-flight trim adjustments possible.

Flight Controls.explanation: Spring tabs and servo tabs are aerodynamically operated auxiliary surfaces that generate a force assisting the pilot in deflecting the primary control surface, reducing the physical effort required especially at higher airspeeds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: If the control stick of an aircraft with properly rigged flight controls is moved rearward and to the left, the right aileron will move

choices.A: down and the elevator will move down.
choices.B: up and the elevator will move down.
choices.C: down and the elevator will move up.

Flight Controls.explanation: Moving the stick rearward raises the elevator (producing nose-up pitch), and moving it left raises the left aileron and lowers the right aileron; however, since the question states the stick moves rearward AND left, the right aileron moves down and the elevator moves up. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Movement of the flight deck control toward the nosedown position during a ground operational check of the elevator trim tab system will cause the trailing edge of the trim tab to move in which direction?

choices.B: Upward regardless of elevator position.
choices.C: Downward if the elevator is in the UP position and upward if the elevator is in the DOWN position.

Flight Controls.explanation: A trim tab moves opposite to its primary control surface to generate a corrective aerodynamic moment; moving the flight deck trim control toward nose-down commands the elevator to deflect trailing-edge down, which drives the trim tab trailing edge upward regardless of elevator position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: If the control stick of an aircraft with properly rigged flight controls is moved forward and to the right, the left aileron will move

choices.A: up and the elevator will move down.
choices.B: down and the elevator will move up.
choices.C: down and the elevator will move down.

Flight Controls.explanation: Moving the stick forward deflects the elevator trailing edge down (nose-down pitch), and moving it right raises the right aileron and lowers the left aileron, meaning the left aileron moves up and the elevator moves down. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: If the travel of an airplane's controls is correct but the cables are rigged exceptionally tight, what probable effect will this have when flying the airplane?

choices.A: The airplane will tend to fall off on one wing.
choices.B: The airplane will be heavy on the controls.
choices.C: The pilot will be unable to fly the airplane hands-off.

Flight Controls.explanation: Excessively tight cables increase friction and the force required to deflect control surfaces, making the controls feel heavy and stiff to the pilot even though full travel range is available. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: During inspection of the flight control system of an airplane equipped with differential-type aileron control, side-to-side movement of the control stick will cause

choices.A: each aileron to have a greater up travel (from the streamlined position) than down travel.
choices.B: each aileron to have greater down travel (from the streamlined position) than up travel.
choices.C: the left aileron to move through a greater number of degrees (from full up to full down) than the right aileron.

Flight Controls.explanation: Differential aileron control is designed so that the up-traveling aileron moves through a greater arc than the down-traveling aileron, reducing adverse yaw by equalizing drag on both wings during a turn. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: A universal propeller protractor used to measure the degrees of aileron travel should be zeroed

choices.A: with the aileron in the NEUTRAL position.
choices.B: with the aileron in the DOWN position.
choices.C: when the aircraft is in a level flight attitude.

Flight Controls.explanation: When using a universal propeller protractor to measure control surface travel, the instrument must be zeroed with the surface in the neutral (streamlined) position so that subsequent deflection measurements accurately reflect actual degrees of travel from neutral. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: The universal propeller protractor can be used to measure

choices.B: aspect ratio of a wing.
choices.C: degrees of flap travel.

Flight Controls.explanation: The universal propeller protractor is a versatile angle-measuring tool that can be used to measure the degrees of travel of movable surfaces such as flaps, in addition to its primary propeller-related uses. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: A tension regulator in the flight control cable system of a large all-metal aircraft is used primarily to

choices.A: increase the cable tension in cold weather.
choices.B: provide a means of changing cable tension in flight.
choices.C: retain a set tension.

Flight Controls.explanation: Tension regulators (also called tensioners) are spring-loaded devices installed in large all-metal aircraft cable systems to automatically compensate for temperature-induced expansion and contraction of the airframe, maintaining a constant preset cable tension. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Differential control on an aileron system means that

choices.A: the down travel is more than the up travel.
choices.B: the up travel is more than the down travel.
choices.C: one aileron on one wing travels further up than the aileron on the opposite wing to adjust for wash-in and wash-out.

Flight Controls.explanation: Differential aileron control means the up-moving aileron travels through a greater angle than the down-moving aileron from the neutral position, which reduces the parasite drag on the rising wing and minimizes adverse yaw. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What would be the effect if the piston return spring broke in a brake master cylinder?

choices.B: The brake travel would become excessive.
choices.C: The brakes would drag.

Flight Controls.explanation: The return spring in a master cylinder is responsible for retracting the piston after brake application, releasing hydraulic pressure; if it breaks, the piston cannot fully retract, keeping residual pressure on the brakes and causing them to drag. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Many brake types can be adapted to operate mechanically or hydraulically. Which type is not adaptable to mechanical operation?

choices.B: Single-servo type.
choices.C: Expander-tube type.

Flight Controls.explanation: Single-disk spot-type brakes rely on hydraulic clamping force applied perpendicular to a rotating disk and have no practical mechanical linkage equivalent to generate the required clamping force, making them unsuitable for mechanical operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Flight Controls.question: When bleeding aircraft brakes, one of the indications that the air has been purged from the system is

choices.B: full brake pedal travel.
choices.C: firm brake pedals.

Flight Controls.explanation: Air in a hydraulic brake system is compressible and causes spongy, soft pedal feel; once all air is purged and only incompressible fluid remains, the brake pedals become firm, indicating a fully bled system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Flight Controls.question: On all aircraft equipped with retractable landing gear, some means must be provided

choices.A: retract and extend the landing gear if the normal operating mechanism fails.
choices.B: extend the landing gear if the normal operating mechanism fails.
choices.C: prevent the throttle from being reduced below a safe power setting while the landing gear is retracted.

Flight Controls.explanation: FAA regulations and design standards require that all retractable landing gear aircraft have an emergency extension system so that the gear can be extended by an alternate means if the normal operating system fails, ensuring a safe landing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Flight Controls.question: How is it determined in a master cylinder brake system that all of the air has been purged from the system?

choices.A: By operating a hydraulic unit and watching the system pressure gauge for smooth, full scale deflection.
choices.B: By depressing the brake and noting that the brake is firm and not spongy.
choices.C: By measuring the amount of fluid return to the master cylinder upon brake release.

Flight Controls.explanation: In a master cylinder brake system, complete air purging is confirmed when the brake pedal feels firm and solid under foot pressure rather than spongy, because incompressible fluid transmits force immediately without pedal travel loss. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Lockout deboosters are primarily pressure reducing valves that

choices.A: allow full debooster piston travel without fluid from the high pressure side entering the low pressure chamber.
choices.B: cannot allow full debooster piston travel without fluid from the high pressure side entering the low pressure chamber.
choices.C: must be bled separately after brake bleeding has been completed.

Flight Controls.explanation: A lockout debooster is designed so that the debooster piston can travel its full stroke using only the fluid trapped on the low-pressure (brake) side, preventing high-pressure system fluid from entering the low-pressure chamber and thus protecting brake components from excessive pressure. This isolation is the defining characteristic that makes it a 'lockout' debooster. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Flight Controls.question: A pilot reports that the brake pedals have excessive travel. A probable cause is

choices.B: lack of fluid in the brake system.
choices.C: oil or some foreign matter on the brake rotors and linings.

Flight Controls.explanation: Lack of fluid (air in the system) causes the brake pedals to travel excessively because air is compressible and must be fully displaced before hydraulic pressure builds enough to apply the brakes, resulting in a spongy or low pedal. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Flight Controls.question: A flexible hydraulic hose identified as MIL-H-8788 will have a stripe running the length of the hose. This stripe is

choices.B: for high pressure fluids with a flexing range.
choices.C: suitable for a wide temperature range.

Flight Controls.explanation: The lay line (stripe) on MIL-H-8788 flexible hydraulic hose is used as a reference to ensure the hose is installed without twisting, since a twisted hose under pressure can fail prematurely. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Flight Controls.question: To protect seals from damage when installed over a threaded section, the threaded section should be

choices.B: covered with tape.
choices.C: covered with a suitable sleeve.

Flight Controls.explanation: A suitable protective sleeve (such as a cone-shaped tool or installation sleeve) should be used to cover threads when sliding seals over them, preventing the sharp threads from cutting or nicking the seal material during installation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What function does the absolute pressure regulator perform in the pneumatic power system?

choices.A: Regulates the compressor outlet air pressure to stabilize the system pressure.
choices.B: Regulates the pneumatic system pressure to protect the moisture separator from internal explosion.
choices.C: Regulates the compressor inlet air to provide a stabilized source of air for the compressor.

Flight Controls.explanation: The absolute pressure regulator in a pneumatic system controls the inlet air pressure to the compressor, ensuring the compressor receives a stable, regulated supply of air regardless of altitude-induced pressure variations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Pneumatic Power Systems)

Flight Controls.question: If a rigid tube is too short for the flare to reach its seat before tightening, pulling it into place by tightening

choices.B: may distort the flare.
choices.C: may distort the cone.

Flight Controls.explanation: If a rigid tube is too short, forcing the flare into its seat by overtightening the fitting can distort the flare, causing leaks or stress cracking; the tube must be replaced with one of the correct length. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Flight Controls.question: The internal resistance of a fluid which tends to prevent it from flowing is called

choices.B: viscosity.
choices.C: stability.

Flight Controls.explanation: Viscosity is defined as the internal resistance of a fluid to flow, and it is a critical property of hydraulic fluids because it affects the efficiency and responsiveness of the hydraulic system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Flight Controls.question: The primary function of the flap overload valve is to

choices.A: prevent the flaps from being lowered at airspeeds which would impose excessive structural loads.
choices.B: cause the flap segments located on opposite sides of the aircraft centerline to extend and retract together so that the aircraft will not become aerodynamically unbalanced to the extent that it becomes uncontrollable.
choices.C: boost normal system pressure to the flaps in order to overcome the air loads acting on the relatively large flap area.

Flight Controls.explanation: The flap overload valve prevents flap extension at airspeeds that would impose loads exceeding the structural design limits of the flap system by relieving or blocking hydraulic pressure when aerodynamic loads become excessive. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Flight Controls.question: A crossflow valve which is designed to bypass fluid from one side of an actuating cylinder to the other side, under certain conditions, may be found in some aircraft installed in the

choices.B: engine cowl flap system.
choices.C: landing gear system.

Flight Controls.explanation: A crossflow valve in an engine cowl flap system allows hydraulic fluid to bypass from one side of the actuating cylinder to the other, permitting the cowl flaps to move freely in response to aerodynamic forces without causing hydraulic lock. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Although dents in the heel of a bend are not permissible, they are acceptable in the remainder of a hydraulic tube providing they are less than what percent of the tube diameter?

choices.B: 10 percent.
choices.C: 20 percent.

Flight Controls.explanation: FAA standards permit dents in hydraulic tubing (outside the heel of a bend) only if they are less than 5 percent of the tube's outer diameter, as deeper dents create stress concentrations that can lead to fatigue failure.
