(Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Flight Controls.question: If the liquid level gauge in a vapor-cycle cooling system indicates a low freon charge, the system should

choices.A: be operated and a pressure check performed.
choices.B: be operated for a period of time to reach a stable condition and then the freon level rechecked.
choices.C: not be operated until freon and oil have been added.

Flight Controls.explanation: When a low refrigerant charge is suspected, the system should be operated and a pressure check performed to confirm the diagnosis before adding refrigerant, since operating pressures are the standard method of verifying charge level in a vapor-cycle system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 17 — Environmental Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Oxygen systems in unpressurized aircraft are generally of the

choices.B: pressure-demand type only.
choices.C: portable-bottle type only.

Flight Controls.explanation: Unpressurized aircraft typically use continuous-flow systems (for lower altitudes) and pressure-demand systems (for higher altitudes), providing oxygen on demand or continuously depending on altitude and crew/passenger needs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 17 — Environmental Systems)

Flight Controls.question: How should the aviation mechanic determine the amount of oxygen in a portable, high-pressure cylinder?

choices.A: Weigh the cylinder and its contents.
choices.B: Read the pressure gauge mounted on the cylinder.
choices.C: Measure the pressure at the mask.

Flight Controls.explanation: For high-pressure gaseous oxygen cylinders, the quantity of oxygen is determined by reading the pressure gauge, since the amount of gas present is directly proportional to the pressure indicated (unlike liquid oxygen systems, which are measured by weight). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 17 — Environmental Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Which instrument condition is acceptable and would not require correction?

choices.B: Case paint chipped.
choices.C: Fogged.

Flight Controls.explanation: Chipped case paint is a cosmetic defect that does not affect instrument accuracy, calibration, or airworthiness, making it an acceptable condition; fogging and missing red lines directly impair readability and accuracy, rendering those instruments unairworthy. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Cabin Atmosphere Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What is the primary purpose of an autopilot?

choices.A: To relieve the pilot of control of the aircraft during long periods of flight.
choices.B: To allow for longer flights and more precise courses flown by the pilot.
choices.C: To stabilize flight control in turbulent conditions.

Flight Controls.explanation: The primary purpose of an autopilot system is to relieve the pilot of the physical and mental workload of manually controlling the aircraft during prolonged flight, reducing fatigue and improving overall flight management. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Flight Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: In an autopilot, which signal nullifies the input signal to the ailerons?

choices.B: Course signal.
choices.C: Follow-up signal.

Flight Controls.explanation: The follow-up signal is generated by the servo or feedback mechanism and is equal and opposite to the input (error) signal, nullifying it once the correction has been made to prevent overcorrection. This feedback loop is fundamental to autopilot operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Autopilot Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What will occur if an aircraft attitude is changed by its autopilot system in order to correct for an error and the involved control surfaces are returned to streamline by the time the aircraft has reached its correct position?

choices.B: Undershoot and oscillation.
choices.C: Normal operation.

Flight Controls.explanation: If the control surfaces are returned to streamline (neutral) at the moment the aircraft reaches the correct position, the aircraft's momentum carries it past the desired attitude, causing overshoot and subsequent oscillation. A follow-up or damping signal is needed to begin the correction before the aircraft reaches the target position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Autopilot Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What component of an autopilot system applies torque to the control surfaces of an aircraft?

choices.B: Controller.
choices.C: Gyro.

Flight Controls.explanation: The servo is the output component of the autopilot that converts electrical command signals into mechanical torque or force applied directly to the aircraft's control surfaces. The gyro senses attitude and the controller sets desired parameters, but only the servo physically moves the controls. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Autopilot Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What is the main purpose of a servo in an autopilot system?

choices.A: Correct for displacement of the aircraft about its axis.
choices.B: Change mechanical energy to electrical energy.
choices.C: Move the control surface as commanded.

Flight Controls.explanation: The servo's primary function is to translate the electrical command signals from the autopilot computer into mechanical movement of the control surfaces as directed. It acts as the actuator between the autopilot electronics and the flight control surfaces. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Autopilot Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Which channel of an autopilot detects changes in pitch attitude of an aircraft?

choices.B: Aileron.
choices.C: Rudder.

Flight Controls.explanation: The elevator channel uses pitch-sensing gyros to detect changes in the aircraft's pitch attitude and commands elevator movement to correct deviations from the desired pitch. Each autopilot axis channel corresponds to the control surface governing that axis of rotation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Autopilot Systems)

Flight Controls.question: The elevator channel of an autopilot controls the aircraft about which axis of rotation?

choices.B: Longitudinal.
choices.C: Lateral.

Flight Controls.explanation: The elevator controls rotation about the lateral axis (nose up or nose down), which is the definition of pitch movement. The autopilot elevator channel therefore governs the aircraft's motion around the lateral axis. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Autopilot Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Dutch roll, a combination yawing and rolling oscillation that affects many sweptwing aircraft, is counteracted with

choices.B: an aileron damper system.
choices.C: a yaw damper system.

Flight Controls.explanation: Dutch roll is a coupled yaw-roll oscillation common in swept-wing aircraft, and it is counteracted by a yaw damper system that senses yaw rate and automatically applies rudder corrections to damp the oscillation. A flight director only provides guidance cues and does not automatically damp oscillations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Autopilot Systems)

Flight Controls.question: When installing coaxial cable, it should be secured firmly along its entire length

choices.B: wherever the cable sags.
choices.C: at two-foot intervals.

Flight Controls.explanation: FAA standards require coaxial cable to be secured firmly at one-foot intervals to prevent excessive movement, chafing, and signal degradation caused by flexing or vibration. Proper support spacing ensures the cable maintains its impedance characteristics and physical integrity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Communication and Navigation Systems)

Flight Controls.question: In the landing configuration GPWS typically monitors the radio (radar) altimeter, air data computer, instrument landing system, and

choices.B: landing gear and flap positions.
choices.C: spoiler, slat, and stabilizer positions.

Flight Controls.explanation: In the landing configuration, GPWS monitors landing gear and flap positions along with radio altimeter, air data computer, and ILS data to assess whether the aircraft is properly configured for landing and to trigger warnings if it is not. These configuration inputs are essential for detecting unsafe approach conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Warning Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What characteristics of the installation of a rigid antenna on a vertical stabilizer should be evaluated?

choices.B: Impedance and interference.
choices.C: Flutter and vibration.

Flight Controls.explanation: A rigid antenna mounted on the vertical stabilizer is subject to aerodynamic forces that can induce flutter and vibration, which can cause structural fatigue and antenna failure, so these characteristics must be evaluated during installation. Impedance and polarization are electrical considerations addressed separately during antenna selection and matching. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Communication and Navigation Systems)

Flight Controls.question: How much clearance from the seat bottom is required when installing radio equipment under a seat?

choices.B: No set minimum as long as the equipment receives adequate cooling and damage protection.
choices.C: 1 inch with the seat occupied and subjected to maximum downward seat spring deflection.

Flight Controls.explanation: When installing radio equipment under a seat, there must be at least 1 inch of clearance with the seat occupied and subjected to maximum downward seat spring deflection to ensure the equipment is not crushed or damaged under load. This ensures both equipment protection and passenger safety. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Communication and Navigation Systems)

Flight Controls.question: The primary purpose of an aircraft's fuel jettison system is to quickly achieve a

choices.B: balanced fuel load.
choices.C: reduced fire hazard.

Flight Controls.explanation: The primary purpose of a fuel jettison system is to rapidly reduce the aircraft's gross weight to at or below the maximum allowable landing weight so that a safe emergency or precautionary landing can be made. Reducing fire hazard and balancing fuel are secondary considerations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Which of the following is employed to maintain lateral stability when jettisoning fuel?

choices.B: Crossfeed system.
choices.C: Two interconnected systems.

Flight Controls.explanation: Two separate independent fuel jettison systems are used so that fuel can be jettisoned symmetrically from both sides of the aircraft, maintaining lateral stability and preventing asymmetric fuel loads from developing during the jettison process. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Normal fuel crossfeed system operation in multiengine aircraft

choices.A: calls for jettisoning of fuel overboard to correct lateral instability.
choices.B: reduces contamination and/or fire hazards during fueling or defueling operations.
choices.C: provides a means to maintain a balanced fuel load condition.

Flight Controls.explanation: The crossfeed system allows any engine to draw fuel from any tank, providing a means to transfer or balance fuel loads between tanks to maintain the aircraft's lateral and longitudinal balance. This capability directly supports fuel load management rather than jettison or contamination control. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What flight safety-related advantage does a pressure fueling system provide?

choices.A: Keeps the aircraft within weight and balance limitations.
choices.B: Reduces the chances for fuel contamination.
choices.C: Reduces the time required for fueling.

Flight Controls.explanation: Pressure fueling is a closed system that transfers fuel through sealed connections, which significantly reduces the exposure of fuel to the atmosphere and the possibility of contamination from water, dirt, or other foreign matter compared to open gravity fueling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Flapper valves are used in fuel tanks to

choices.B: prevent a negative pressure.
choices.C: act as check valves.

Flight Controls.explanation: Flapper valves in fuel tanks act as one-way check valves, allowing fuel to flow in one direction while preventing backflow, which helps maintain fuel availability to the engine and prevents sloshing from uncovering the outlet. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Fuel Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What is the principal advantage of the series-wound DC motor?

choices.B: Suitable for constant speed use.
choices.C: Low starting torque.

Flight Controls.explanation: In a series-wound DC motor, the field and armature windings are in series, producing very high magnetic flux at startup and therefore very high starting torque, making it ideal for applications requiring large initial mechanical force. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — DC Motors)

Flight Controls.question: Oil canning of the sides of aluminum or steel electrical junction boxes is considered to be

choices.A: normal operation in vibration prone areas.
choices.B: a shorting hazard.
choices.C: acceptable operation.

Flight Controls.explanation: Oil canning (flexing or buckling of the metal sides) of electrical junction boxes can cause internal wiring or components to contact the box walls, creating a shorting hazard that can result in electrical faults or fires. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical Wiring and Circuit Protection)

Flight Controls.question: What protection to wires and cables does conduit provide when used in aircraft installations?

choices.B: Mechanical.
choices.C: Structural.

Flight Controls.explanation: Conduit used in aircraft electrical installations provides mechanical protection to wires and cables, shielding them from abrasion, physical damage, and environmental hazards such as fluids and sharp edges. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Wiring Installation)

Flight Controls.question: The primary considerations when selecting electric cable size are

choices.A: current-carrying capacity and allowable voltage drop.
choices.B: the voltage and amperage of the load it must carry.
choices.C: the system voltage and cable length.

Flight Controls.explanation: Cable size selection must ensure the wire can carry the required current without overheating (current-carrying capacity) and that the voltage drop across the cable length does not exceed acceptable limits for proper equipment operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical Wire and Cable)

Flight Controls.question: Which of the following copper electrical cable sizes should be selected to replace a No. 6 aluminum electrical cable?

choices.B: No. 6.
choices.C: No. 8.

Flight Controls.explanation: Aluminum wire has higher resistance and lower conductivity than copper, so a No. 6 aluminum cable must be replaced with a No. 4 copper cable to provide equivalent current-carrying capacity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical Wire and Cable)

Flight Controls.question: What is the advantage of a circuit breaker when compared to a fuse?

choices.B: It controls current flow.
choices.C: It is resettable and reusable.

Flight Controls.explanation: Unlike a fuse, which must be replaced after it opens, a circuit breaker can be reset and reused after the fault is cleared, reducing maintenance downtime and the need to carry spare fuses. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Circuit Protection Devices)

Flight Controls.question: When considering an alteration, the criteria upon which the selection of electric cable size should be based are

choices.A: applied voltage and allowable voltage drop.
choices.B: current-carrying capacity and allowable voltage drop.
choices.C: current-carrying capacity and applied voltage.

Flight Controls.explanation: When selecting cable size for an alteration, the two critical criteria are current-carrying capacity (to prevent overheating) and allowable voltage drop (to ensure proper system operation), consistent with FAA wiring standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical Wire and Cable)

Flight Controls.question: Which of the following must be accomplished when installing an anticollision light?

choices.A: Install a switch independent of the position light switch.
choices.B: Use shielded electrical cable to assure fail-safe operation.
choices.C: Connect the anticollision light to the aircraft position light switch.

Flight Controls.explanation: FAA regulations require that the anticollision light be connected to the aircraft position light switch so that both systems operate together, ensuring the aircraft is always visible when position lights are in use. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Aircraft Lighting Systems)

Flight Controls.question: A typical takeoff warning indication system, in addition to throttle setting, monitors the position of which of the following?

choices.A: Ailerons, elevators, speed brake, and steerable fuselage landing gear.
choices.B: Elevators, speed brake, flaps, and stabilizer trim.
choices.C: Aerodynamically actuated slats, elevators, flaps, and speed brake.

Flight Controls.explanation: Takeoff warning systems monitor throttle position along with flight-critical configuration items including elevators (stabilizer trim), speed brake, flaps, and stabilizer trim to alert the crew of an unsafe takeoff configuration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Warning and Fire Protection Systems)

Flight Controls.question: When an airplane's primary flight control surfaces are set for a particular phase of flight, such as landing or takeoff, the corresponding control-surface indicating system will show

choices.B: speed break position.
choices.C: trim position.

Flight Controls.explanation: Control surface indicating systems for landing and takeoff phases primarily display flap and slat position, since these are the surfaces configured for those specific phases of flight and must be verified as properly set. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Flight Control and Warning Systems)

Flight Controls.question: What landing gear warning device(s) is/are incorporated on retractable landing gear aircraft?

choices.A: A visual indicator showing gear position.
choices.B: A light which comes on when the gear is fully down and locked.
choices.C: A horn or other aural device and a red warning light.

Flight Controls.explanation: Retractable landing gear aircraft are required to incorporate both an aural warning device (horn) and a red warning light to alert the flight crew when the gear is not in the proper down-and-locked position for landing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Flight Controls.question: Which of the following are some uses for a DC selsyn system?

choices.A: Indicate the position of retractable landing gear, cowl flaps position, cooler door position, fuel quantity, and position of wing flaps.
choices.B: Indicate the position of retractable landing gear, angle of incidence, and altitude.
choices.C: Indicate the fuel quantity, rate of climb, and position of wing flaps.

Flight Controls.explanation: DC selsyn (synchro) systems are used to remotely indicate the position of mechanical components such as retractable landing gear, cowl flaps, cooler doors, fuel quantity indicators, and wing flaps by transmitting angular position electrically. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 10 — Aircraft Instrument Systems)

Flight Controls.question: When air samples contain carbon monoxide, portable carbon monoxide detectors containing yellow silica gel will turn which color?

choices.B: Green.
choices.C: Red.

Flight Controls.explanation: Portable carbon monoxide detectors containing yellow silica gel change from yellow to green when exposed to carbon monoxide, providing a visual warning of CO contamination in the air sample. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Warning and Fire Protection Systems)

Flight Controls.question: A contaminated carbon monoxide portable test unit may be returned to service by

choices.A: cleaning the indicating element with soap and water.
choices.B: removing the indicating element and installing a new indicating element.
choices.C: removing the indicating element from the exposed area for 24 hours.

Flight Controls.explanation: Once the indicating element of a portable CO detector has been contaminated (changed color), it cannot be restored and must be replaced with a new indicating element to ensure accurate detection capability. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Warning and Fire Protection Systems)

Flight Controls.question: The waste water tanks hold

choices.B: gray water.
choices.C: drinking water.

Flight Controls.explanation: Waste water (gray water) consists of drainage from sinks, galleys, and lavatories (excluding toilet waste), distinguishing it from potable/drinking water systems onboard aircraft. Gray water is collected in waste water tanks separate from the potable water supply. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Cabin Environmental Systems)

Flight Controls.question: (Refer to Figure 2.) Select the preferred drawing for proper countersinking.
Flight Controls.explanation: The preferred countersink drawing shows the countersink depth stopping short of the lower sheet so that the rivet head bears on the upper sheet material, preventing 'knife-edging' of the hole and maintaining structural integrity. Figure 2 depicts this correct practice per FAA standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Riveting)

Flight Controls.question: (Refer to Figure 8.) Identify the cable that is used in primary control systems and in other places where operation over pulleys is frequent.
Flight Controls.explanation: 7x19 cable (extra flexible, cable 2 in Figure 8) is the standard for primary flight control systems and wherever frequent pulley operation occurs because its higher strand count provides superior flexibility and fatigue resistance compared to less flexible cable constructions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Cable and Terminal Hardware)

Flight Controls.question: (Refer to Figure 9.) When the outside air temperature is 80F, select the acceptable 3/16 cable tension range.

choices.B: 117 pounds minimum, 143 pounds maximum.
choices.C: 120 pounds minimum, 140 pounds maximum.

Flight Controls.explanation: Using the cable tension chart in Figure 9, a 3/16-inch cable at 80°F has an acceptable tension range of 130 pounds minimum to 140 pounds maximum, which is read directly from the intersection of cable size and temperature on the chart. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Rigging and Control Systems)

Flight Controls.question: (Refer to Figure 14.) One hour after an oxygen system was charged for a leakage check, the oxygen pressure gauge read 460 psi at 63F; 6 hours later the temperature was 51F. (A 5 psi change is the maximum allowable in a 6-hour period.) What pressure gauge readings would be acceptable to remain within the allowable limits?

choices.B: 446 to 450 psi.
choices.C: 455 to 460 psi.

Flight Controls.explanation: Using Figure 14, the corrected pressure for the temperature drop from 63°F to 51°F is calculated, and then subtracting the maximum allowable 5 psi leakage from that corrected value yields an acceptable range of 445 to 450 psi, confirming the system is within limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 17 — Oxygen Systems)

Airframe Inspection.question: A rivet set used to drive MS20470 rivets should

choices.A: have the same radius as the rivet head.
choices.B: have a slightly greater radius than the rivet head.
choices.C: be nearly flat on the end, with a slight radius on the edge to prevent damage to the sheet being riveted.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: A rivet set with a slightly greater radius than the universal (MS20470) rivet head ensures full, even seating of the head without pinching or marring the sheet surface, producing a properly driven rivet. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Riveting)

Airframe Inspection.question: Where would the aviation mechanic find precise information to perform a symmetry alignment check for a particular aircraft?

choices.A: Aircraft Specification or Type Certificate Data Sheet.
choices.B: Manufacturer's service bulletins.
choices.C: Aircraft service or maintenance manual.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: The aircraft service or maintenance manual contains the precise alignment dimensions, reference datums, and step-by-step procedures specific to each aircraft model required to perform a symmetry alignment check correctly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Aircraft Maintenance Publications)

Airframe Inspection.question: Which statement about Airworthiness Directives (ADs) is true?

choices.A: ADs are information alert bulletins issued by the airframe, powerplant, or component manufacturer.
choices.B: Compliance with an AD is not mandatory unless the aircraft affected is for hire.
choices.C: Compliance with an applicable AD is mandatory and must be recorded in the maintenance records.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: Airworthiness Directives are legally enforceable regulations issued under 14 CFR Part 39, and compliance is mandatory for all affected aircraft regardless of commercial use; the action must also be recorded in the aircraft maintenance records per 14 CFR Part 43. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Airworthiness Directives)

Airframe Inspection.question: What is the primary purpose of a special inspection?

choices.A: To perform routine maintenance tasks at scheduled intervals.
choices.B: To address specific conditions or events that may affect airworthiness.
choices.C: To replace components based on manufacturer-recommended timelines.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: Special inspections are triggered by specific events or conditions—such as hard landings, lightning strikes, or overweight operations—that may compromise airworthiness and require examination outside the normal scheduled maintenance intervals. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Inspection Concepts and Techniques)

Airframe Inspection.question: Which statement is correct regarding an aircraft that is found to be unairworthy after an annual inspection, due to an item requiring a major repair (assuming approved data is used to accomplish the repair)?

choices.A: An appropriately rated mechanic may accomplish the repair, and an IA may approve the aircraft for return to service.
choices.B: An appropriately rated mechanic or repair station may repair the defect and approve the aircraft for return to service.
choices.C: Only the person who performed the annual inspection may approve the aircraft for return to service, after the major repair.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: Under 14 CFR Part 43, an appropriately rated mechanic may perform a major repair using approved data, but only an Inspection Authorization (IA) holder may approve the aircraft for return to service following work that was identified during an annual inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Maintenance Regulations and Practices)

Airframe Inspection.question: An aircraft may be flown beyond the 100-hour inspection requirement to reach a place where the inspection can be accomplished, provided it

choices.B: has a special flight permit.
choices.C: does not exceed 15 flight hours.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: Per 14 CFR §91.409, an aircraft may exceed the 100-hour inspection limit by no more than 10 flight hours solely to fly to a location where the inspection can be performed, and that excess time is counted toward the next 100-hour interval. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Inspection Requirements)

Airframe Inspection.question: Where would the aviation mechanic find the recommended statement for recording the approval or disapproval for return to service of an aircraft after a 100-hour or annual inspection?

choices.B: 14 CFR Part 43.
choices.C: 14 CFR Part 91.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix B, provides the recommended format and required content for maintenance record entries documenting approval or disapproval for return to service after 100-hour and annual inspections. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Maintenance Records)

Airframe Inspection.question: The maximum time a 100-hour inspection may be extended is

choices.B: 10 hours with a special flight permit.
choices.C: 12 hours with a special flight permit.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: Per 14 CFR §91.409, the 100-hour inspection interval may be exceeded by a maximum of 10 hours only to ferry the aircraft to a place where the inspection can be done; no special flight permit is required or mentioned for this provision. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Inspection Requirements)

Airframe Inspection.question: Which statement is correct when an aircraft has not been approved for return to service after an annual inspection because of several items requiring minor repair?

choices.A: Only the person who performed the annual inspection may approve the aircraft for return to service.
choices.B: An appropriately rated mechanic may repair the defects and approve the aircraft for return to service.
choices.C: An appropriately rated mechanic may repair the defects, but an IA must approve the aircraft for return to service.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: When an annual inspection is incomplete due to discrepancies, only an IA holder has the authority to approve the aircraft for return to service after an annual inspection, even if an appropriately rated mechanic performed the actual repair work. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Maintenance Regulations and Practices)

Airframe Inspection.question: An aircraft that is due an annual inspection may be flown

choices.A: if a special permit has been issued for the aircraft.
choices.B: for the purpose of performing maintenance.
choices.C: for a period of time not to exceed 10 hours.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: An aircraft with an expired annual inspection is not airworthy and may not be operated except under a special flight permit (ferry permit) issued under 14 CFR Part 21, which authorizes flight to a location where the inspection or maintenance can be accomplished. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 3 — Inspection Requirements)

Airframe Inspection.question: Where would the aviation mechanic find the operating conditions that make a 100-hour inspection mandatory?

choices.B: 14 CFR Part 43.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: 14 CFR Part 91 (specifically §91.409) defines the operating conditions—such as carrying passengers for hire or giving flight instruction for hire—that make a 100-hour inspection mandatory, making it the regulatory authority on when this inspection is required. Part 43 governs how inspections are performed, not when they are required. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Airframe Inspection)

Airframe Inspection.question: Large airplanes and turbine-powered multiengine airplanes operated under 14 CFR Part 91, General Operating and Flight Rules, must be inspected

choices.A: in accordance with an inspection program authorized under Part 91, Subpart E.
choices.B: in accordance with a continuous airworthiness maintenance program (camp program) authorized under Part 91, Subpart E.
choices.C: in accordance with the progressive inspection requirements of Section 91.409(d).

Airframe Inspection.explanation: 14 CFR Part 91, Subpart E (§91.409(e)) requires that large airplanes and turbine-powered multiengine airplanes operated under Part 91 be inspected in accordance with an inspection program authorized under that subpart, which includes options such as continuous airworthiness, progressive inspection, or manufacturer programs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Airframe Inspection)

Airframe Inspection.question: A certificated mechanic may perform

choices.B: 100-hour inspections of instruments.
choices.C: instrument overhaul.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: A certificated mechanic is authorized to perform 100-hour inspections, including of instruments, as part of an annual or 100-hour inspection under 14 CFR Part 43; however, instrument repair and overhaul require an instrument rating or FAA-approved repair station authorization. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Airframe Inspection)

Airframe Inspection.question: Which of the following should be accomplished in the installation of aircraft wiring?

choices.A: Support the bundle to structure and/ or solid fluid lines to prevent chafing damage.
choices.B: Provide adequate slack in the wire bundle to compensate for large changes in temperature.
choices.C: Locate the bundle above flammable fluid lines and securely clamp to structure.

Airframe Inspection.explanation: Wire bundles must be routed above flammable fluid lines and securely clamped to structure so that any fluid leak drains away from the wiring, preventing fire hazards and chafing; routing below fluid lines would expose wiring to contamination and ignition risk. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 7 — Airframe Inspection)

Environmental Systems.question: Why is it necessary to use flux in all silver soldering operations?

choices.A: To chemically clean the base metal of oxide film.
choices.B: To prevent overheating of the base metal.
choices.C: To increase heat conductivity.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Flux is required in silver soldering to chemically remove and prevent oxide film formation on the base metal surfaces, which would otherwise inhibit proper bonding and flow of the solder. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: In gas welding, the amount of heat applied to the material being welded is controlled by the

choices.B: size of the tip opening.
choices.C: cutting oxygen valve lever.

Environmental Systems.explanation: In gas welding, the tip size determines the volume of gas mixture burned and therefore the heat output delivered to the work; larger tips produce more heat while smaller tips produce less, giving the welder control over heat input independent of regulator pressure settings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: If a pilot reports that an airplane flies left wing heavy, this condition may be corrected by

choices.A: increasing the angle of incidence of the left wing, or decreasing the angle of incidence of the right wing, or both.
choices.B: increasing the dihedral angle of the left wing, or decreasing the dihedral angle of the right wing, or both.
choices.C: adjusting the dihedral angle of the left wing so that differential pressure between the upper and lower wing surfaces is increased.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Increasing the angle of incidence on the heavy (left) wing increases its lift, and/or decreasing the angle of incidence on the opposite (right) wing reduces its lift, thereby correcting a wing-heavy condition by balancing the lift produced by each wing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Which section of a turbine engine provides high pressure bleed air to an air cycle machine for pressurization and air conditioning?

choices.B: Compressor inlet.
choices.C: C-D inlet compressor duct.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Bleed air for air conditioning and pressurization is extracted from the low or high pressure compressor stages of a turbine engine, where air has already been compressed to the pressure levels needed to drive the air cycle machine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: At which component in an air-cycle cooling system does air undergo a pressure and temperature drop?

choices.B: Primary heat exchanger.
choices.C: Refrigeration bypass valve.

Environmental Systems.explanation: In an air-cycle cooling system, the expansion turbine is where compressed air expands rapidly, causing both pressure and temperature to drop significantly as the air does work spinning the turbine wheel, producing the cooling effect. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: In a freon vapor-cycle cooling system, where is cooling air obtained for the condenser?

choices.B: Ambient air.
choices.C: Pressurized cabin air.

Environmental Systems.explanation: In a vapor-cycle (Freon) cooling system, ambient (outside) air is used to cool the condenser, allowing the refrigerant to release its heat and condense back into a liquid before re-entering the evaporator cycle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Turbine engine air used for air conditioning and pressurization is generally called

choices.B: ram air.
choices.C: bleed air.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Air tapped from turbine engine compressor stages for use in cabin air conditioning and pressurization is standardly referred to as bleed air throughout FAA and industry documentation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: In the combustion heater, combustion air system, what prevents too much air from entering the heaters as air pressure increases?

choices.A: Either a combustion air relief valve or a differential pressure regulator.
choices.B: Only a differential pressure regulator can be used.
choices.C: Only a combustion air relief valve can be used.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A combustion air relief valve or a differential pressure regulator is used to prevent excessive combustion air pressure from entering the heater as ram air pressure increases with airspeed, protecting the combustion process from being disrupted or over-pressured. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: How is the cabin pressure of an aircraft maintained in flight?

choices.A: By controlling the rate of the air flowing into the cabin.
choices.B: By inflating the door seals and recirculating the conditioned cabin air.
choices.C: By controlling the rate of the air flowing out of the cabin.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Cabin pressurization is maintained by controlling the rate at which air flows out through the outflow valve; the compressors or bleed air continuously supply air in, and the outflow valve modulates the exit flow to maintain the desired cabin pressure differential. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What controls the operation of the cabin pressure regulator?

choices.B: Bleed air pressure.
choices.C: Compression air pressure.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The cabin pressure regulator senses cabin altitude and modulates the outflow valve accordingly to maintain a pre-set cabin altitude, making cabin altitude the controlling parameter for regulator operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The basic air-cycle cooling system consists of

choices.A: a source of compressed air, heat exchangers, and a turbine.
choices.B: heaters, coolers, and compressors.
choices.C: ram air source, compressors, and engine bleeds.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The basic air-cycle cooling system requires a source of compressed air (bleed air), one or more heat exchangers to pre-cool the air, and an expansion turbine to produce the final temperature drop for cooling the cabin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Environmental Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What component might possibly be damaged if liquid refrigerant is introduced into the low side of a vapor-cycle cooling system when the pressure is too high or the outside air temperature is too low?

choices.B: Condenser.
choices.C: Evaporator.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Liquid refrigerant introduced into the low (suction) side of the system can cause liquid slugging, which can hydraulically damage the compressor because compressors are designed to compress gas, not incompressible liquid. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Vapor Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: How can it be determined that a vapor-cycle cooling system is charged with the proper amount of freon?

choices.A: Air bubbles in the sight glass disappear.
choices.B: The compressor loads up and RPM decreases.
choices.C: Air bubbles appear in the sight glass.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A properly charged vapor-cycle system will show a clear sight glass with no bubbles; bubbles indicate insufficient refrigerant, and their disappearance confirms the system has reached the correct charge level. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Vapor Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Frost or ice buildup on a vapor-cycle cooling system evaporator would most likely be caused by

choices.B: moisture in the evaporator.
choices.C: inadequate airflow through the evaporator.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Inadequate airflow across the evaporator reduces heat transfer from the cabin air, causing the evaporator coil temperature to drop below freezing and allowing moisture in the air to freeze on its surface. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Vapor Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Which oxygen system employs a regulator that operates only when the user inhales?

choices.B: Emergency-flow system.
choices.C: Demand-flow system.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A demand-flow oxygen system delivers oxygen only when the user creates a negative pressure by inhaling, making it more efficient than continuous-flow systems by not wasting oxygen between breaths. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 15 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The component that determines the pressure level in the cabin is the cabin air pressure

choices.B: safety valve.
choices.C: outflow valve.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The outflow valve controls the rate at which pressurized air exits the cabin, thereby directly regulating the differential pressure and maintaining the desired cabin altitude. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Hot compressor bleed air operates the conditioned air system on some turbine aircraft, cold air supplied by the

choices.B: vapor cycle air conditioning.
choices.C: air cycle air conditioning.

Environmental Systems.explanation: In an air-cycle machine (ACM) system, hot compressor bleed air is cooled by passing through heat exchangers and an expansion turbine, with the air-cycle unit providing the final cold air supply to the conditioned air distribution system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Air Cycle Air Conditioning)

Environmental Systems.question: What is the main cause of contamination in a gaseous oxygen system?

choices.B: Dust.
choices.C: Nitrogen.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Moisture is the primary contaminant in gaseous oxygen systems because it can cause corrosion of system components and, at high pressures, can combine with oxygen to create hazardous conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 15 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Where does the last stage of cooling in an air-cycle air conditioning system occur?

choices.B: Secondary heat exchanger.
choices.C: Expansion turbine.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The expansion turbine is where air expands rapidly, dropping sharply in temperature as it does work driving the turbine wheel, producing the final and coldest stage of cooling in an air-cycle system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Air Cycle Air Conditioning)

Environmental Systems.question: The point at which freon flowing through a vapor-cycle cooling system gives up heat and changes from a gas to a liquid is the

choices.B: evaporator.
choices.C: expansion valve.

Environmental Systems.explanation: In the condenser, high-pressure refrigerant gas releases its latent heat to the ambient airstream and condenses into a liquid, making it the point of heat rejection and phase change from gas to liquid. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Vapor Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The point at which freon flowing through a vapor-cycle cooling system absorbs heat and changes from a liquid to a gas is the

choices.B: evaporator.
choices.C: expansion valve.

Environmental Systems.explanation: In the evaporator, low-pressure liquid refrigerant absorbs heat from the warm cabin air and vaporizes into a gas, producing the cooling effect delivered to the aircraft cabin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Vapor Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: How is the cabin pressure of a pressurized aircraft usually controlled?

choices.A: By a pressure-sensitive switch that causes the pressurization pump to turn on or off as required.
choices.B: By an automatic outflow valve that dumps all the pressure in excess of the amount for which it is set.
choices.C: By a pressure-sensitive valve that controls the output pressure of the pressurization pump.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Cabin pressure is maintained by an automatic outflow valve that continuously modulates to release excess pressurized air, keeping the cabin differential pressure at the selected value. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Which is considered a good practice concerning the inspection of heating and exhaust systems of aircraft utilizing a jacket around the engine exhaust as a heat source?

choices.A: Supplement physical inspections with periodic operational carbon monoxide detection tests.
choices.B: All exhaust system components should be removed periodically, and their condition determined by the magnetic-particle inspection method.
choices.C: All exhaust system components should be removed and replaced at each 100-hour inspection period.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Because exhaust shroud-type heaters carry a risk of carbon monoxide entering the cabin if the exhaust develops cracks, the FAA recommends supplementing visual inspections with operational CO detection tests to catch leaks that may not be visually apparent. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Heating Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: On some cabin pressurization systems, pressurization on the ground is restricted by the

choices.B: cabin pressure regulator.
choices.C: negative pressure-relief valve.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A squat switch (weight-on-wheels switch) on the main landing gear is used to prevent or restrict cabin pressurization while the aircraft is on the ground, protecting ground crew and structure from inadvertent pressurization. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The cabin pressure control setting has a direct influence upon the

choices.B: pneumatic system pressure.
choices.C: inflow valve opening.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The cabin pressure control setting signals the outflow valve to open or close to maintain the selected cabin pressure differential, so the control setting directly governs outflow valve position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The function of the evaporator in a freon cooling system is to

choices.A: liquefy freon in the line between the compressor and the condenser.
choices.B: lower the temperature of the cabin air.
choices.C: transfer heat from the freon gas to ambient air.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The evaporator's primary function is to absorb heat from the cabin air passing over its coils, thereby lowering the temperature of the air before it is distributed into the cabin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Vapor Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What is the purpose of a mixing valve in a compressor bleed air air conditioning system?

choices.A: Control the supply of hot, cool, and cold air.
choices.B: Distribute conditioned air evenly to all parts of the cabin.
choices.C: Combine ram air with conditioned air.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The mixing valve blends hot compressor bleed air with cooled (and sometimes cold) air to achieve the desired cabin temperature, giving the system the ability to control the supply of hot, cool, and cold air to the distribution system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Air Conditioning Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: If the cabin rate of climb is too great, the control should be adjusted to cause the

choices.B: outflow valve to close faster.
choices.C: cabin compressor speed to decrease.

Environmental Systems.explanation: If the cabin rate of climb is too great, adjusting the outflow valve to close more slowly reduces the rate at which cabin pressure changes, thereby limiting the cabin climb rate to a more comfortable level. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The position of the thermostatic expansion valve in a vapor-cycle cooling system is determined by temperature and pressure of the

choices.B: air in the outlet of the condenser.
choices.C: freon in the outlet of the evaporator.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The thermostatic expansion valve (TXV) senses the temperature and pressure of the refrigerant at the evaporator outlet to ensure proper superheat, modulating refrigerant flow into the evaporator accordingly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The function of the condenser in a freon cooling system is to

choices.A: transfer heat from the freon gas to ambient air.
choices.B: change liquid freon into a gas before it enters the compressor.
choices.C: transfer heat from the cabin air to the liquid freon.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The condenser receives high-pressure refrigerant vapor from the compressor and rejects its heat to the ambient airstream, condensing the vapor back into a liquid. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The function of an expansion valve in a freon cooling system is to act as a metering device and to

choices.A: reduce the pressure of the gaseous freon.
choices.B: increase the pressure of the liquid freon.
choices.C: reduce the pressure of the liquid freon.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The expansion valve meters high-pressure liquid refrigerant and reduces its pressure, causing a drop in temperature as it enters the evaporator where it can absorb cabin heat. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Which prevents a sudden loss of pressurization in the event that there is a loss of the pressurization source?

choices.B: Cabin pressure outflow valve.
choices.C: Delivery air duct check valve.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Delivery air duct check valves prevent pressurized cabin air from escaping back through the ducting if the pressurization source is lost, thereby preventing a sudden cabin depressurization. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: When servicing an air conditioning system that has lost all of its freon, it is necessary to

choices.A: check oil and add as necessary, evacuate the system, relieve vacuum, and add freon.
choices.B: check oil and add as necessary, evacuate the system, and add freon.
choices.C: check oil and add as necessary, and add freon.

Environmental Systems.explanation: When all refrigerant has been lost, moisture and air can enter the system; therefore, after checking and correcting the oil level, the system must be evacuated (to remove moisture and non-condensables), a vacuum relief performed to verify integrity, and then recharged with refrigerant. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The primary function of the cabin pressurization system outflow valve is to

choices.B: maintain the desired cabin pressure.
choices.C: maintain the same cabin air pressure at all altitudes.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The outflow valve is the primary pressure-regulating component; by modulating how much air exits the cabin, it maintains the cabin at the desired pressurization schedule set by the pressurization controller. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: One purpose of a jet pump in a pressurization and air conditioning system is to

choices.A: produce a high pressure for operation of the out-flow valve.
choices.B: provide for augmentation of airflow in some areas of the aircraft.
choices.C: assist in the circulation of freon.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A jet pump uses a high-velocity air jet to entrain and augment airflow in areas of the aircraft that require additional ventilation or cooling but where duct sizing alone is insufficient. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Air Conditioning and Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What does a steady stream of bubbles indicate when servicing a vapor cycle air conditioning system?

choices.B: The system is properly charged.
choices.C: The system is under charged.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A steady stream of bubbles in the sight glass indicates that vapor is present in the liquid line, meaning insufficient refrigerant is in the system and it is undercharged; a properly charged system shows a clear sight glass. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: An aircraft pressurization cycle is normally considered to be

choices.A: one complete series of events or operations that recur regularly.
choices.B: one take off and one landing.
choices.C: when the fuselage reaches its maximum pressure differential one time.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A pressurization cycle is defined as one complete series of recurring events—from unpressurized through pressurization and back—used to track structural fatigue life of the pressure vessel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Which best describes cabin differential pressure?

choices.A: Difference between cabin flight altitude pressure and mean sea level pressure.
choices.B: Difference between the ambient and internal air pressure.
choices.C: Difference between cabin pressure controller setting and actual cabin pressure.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Cabin differential pressure (ΔP) is the difference between the pressure maintained inside the cabin and the ambient (outside) atmospheric pressure at altitude, which determines the structural load on the fuselage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The cabin pressurization modes of operation are

choices.B: differential, unpressurized, and isobaric.
choices.C: ambient, unpressurized, and isobaric.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Pressurization systems operate in three modes: isobaric (constant cabin altitude), differential (cabin pressure maintained at a fixed differential above ambient), and maximum differential (a safety limit preventing structural overstress). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: A pressurization controller uses

choices.A: bleed air pressure, outside air temperature, and cabin rate of climb.
choices.B: barometric pressure, cabin altitude, and cabin rate of change.
choices.C: cabin rate of climb, bleed air volume, and cabin pressure.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A pressurization controller schedules cabin altitude by referencing barometric (ambient) pressure and a selected cabin altitude, while also regulating the cabin rate of change to limit passenger discomfort. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Pressurization Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What is the condition of the refrigerant as it enters the condenser of a vapor-cycle cooling system?

choices.B: Low-pressure liquid.
choices.C: High-pressure vapor.

Environmental Systems.explanation: After the compressor raises the refrigerant's pressure and temperature, the refrigerant enters the condenser as a high-pressure superheated vapor, where it is cooled and condensed into a liquid. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What is the condition of the refrigerant as it enters the evaporator of a vapor-cycle cooling system?

choices.B: Low-pressure liquid.
choices.C: High-pressure vapor.

Environmental Systems.explanation: After passing through the expansion valve, the refrigerant pressure drops sharply, converting it to a low-pressure liquid that absorbs heat as it vaporizes in the evaporator. This phase change from liquid to vapor is what produces the cooling effect. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The evacuation of a vapor-cycle cooling system removes any water that may be present by

choices.B: raising the boiling point of the water and drawing out the vapor.
choices.C: lowering the boiling point of the water and drawing out the vapor.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Pulling a deep vacuum on the system lowers the boiling point of any residual water to below ambient temperature, causing it to vaporize so it can be drawn out by the vacuum pump. This prevents ice formation and corrosion within the system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What is the condition of the refrigerant as it leaves the evaporator of a vapor-cycle cooling system?

choices.B: Low-pressure vapor.
choices.C: High-pressure vapor.

Environmental Systems.explanation: As the refrigerant travels through the evaporator it absorbs heat and fully vaporizes, leaving the evaporator as a low-pressure vapor before being drawn into the compressor. This completed phase change is essential to the refrigeration cycle's heat-transfer process. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What is the condition of the refrigerant as it leaves the condenser of a vapor-cycle cooling system?

choices.B: High-pressure liquid.
choices.C: High-pressure vapor.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The condenser dissipates heat from the high-pressure refrigerant vapor, causing it to condense into a high-pressure liquid that then flows to the receiver-dryer and expansion valve. The refrigerant remains at high pressure because the compressor has already raised it before it enters the condenser. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: In what position should the bottle be placed when adding liquid freon to a vapor-cycle cooling system?

choices.A: Vertical with the outlet at the top.
choices.B: Horizontal with the outlet to the side.
choices.C: Vertical with the outlet at the bottom.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Positioning the bottle vertically with the outlet at the bottom ensures that liquid refrigerant (not vapor) is introduced into the high-pressure side of the system, which is required when charging with liquid freon. Adding vapor instead of liquid would result in an improper charge. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: When purging a freon air conditioning system, it is important to release the charge at a slow rate. What is the reason for the slow-rate discharge?

choices.A: Prevent the large amount of freon from contaminating the surrounding atmosphere.
choices.B: Prevent excessive loss of refrigerant oil.
choices.C: Prevent condensation from forming and contaminating the system.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Releasing refrigerant too rapidly creates a pressure drop that causes the refrigerant oil to be carried out of the system along with the freon, resulting in an oil-depleted system that can damage the compressor. A slow discharge rate allows the oil to separate and remain in the system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: When a vapor-cycle cooling system is not in operation, what is an indication that the system is leaking freon?

choices.B: Bubbles in the sight glass.
choices.C: An ozone-like odor in the immediate area.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Refrigerant oil circulates throughout the vapor-cycle system with the freon, so when the system is static and a leak is present, oil will seep out at the leak point and leave a visible residue. This oil seepage is the primary visual indicator of a freon leak when the system is not operating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The purpose of a subcooler in a vapor-cycle cooling system is to

choices.A: augment the cooling capacity during periods of peak demand.
choices.B: aid in quick cooling a hot aircraft interior.
choices.C: cool the freon to prevent premature vaporization.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A subcooler further reduces the temperature of the high-pressure liquid refrigerant leaving the condenser, ensuring it remains fully liquid and preventing flash vaporization (premature vaporization) before it reaches the expansion valve. This maximizes the efficiency of the refrigeration cycle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: A small amount of water in a vapor cycle cooling system can freeze

choices.A: in the receiver-dryer and stop the entire system operation.
choices.B: in the expansion valve and stop the entire system operation.
choices.C: but the system is designed to continue operation.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Water in the system can freeze at the expansion valve orifice, which is the point of greatest pressure drop and lowest temperature, completely blocking refrigerant flow and shutting down the entire cooling system. The receiver-dryer contains desiccant specifically to prevent this by removing moisture. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What type of oil is suitable for use in vapor-cycle cooling system?

choices.A: Low viscosity engine oil with the inability to absorb water.
choices.B: Special high grade refrigeration oil.
choices.C: Highly refined synthetic oil, free from impurities with special water absorbing additives.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Vapor-cycle systems require special refrigeration-grade oil that is compatible with the refrigerant, free of moisture, and will not break down under the pressures and temperatures of the system. Standard engine or synthetic oils are not suitable because they can contaminate the refrigerant and damage system components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: When an aircraft's oxygen system has developed a leak, the lines and fittings should be

choices.B: inspected using a special oxygen system dye penetrant.
choices.C: bubble tested with a special soap solution manufactured specifically for this purpose.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Oxygen systems must be leak-checked using a soap solution specifically approved for oxygen service because standard soaps or solvents may contain oils or contaminants that are combustion hazards in a high-pressure oxygen environment. Any unapproved substance introduced into an oxygen system creates a serious fire or explosion risk. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What controls the amount of oxygen delivered to a mask in a continuous-flow oxygen system?

choices.B: Pressure reducing valve.
choices.C: Pilot's regulator.

Environmental Systems.explanation: In a continuous-flow oxygen system, a calibrated orifice (fixed or adjustable) meters a constant, predetermined flow of oxygen to each mask regardless of the user's breathing cycle. This simple, passive device is what regulates delivery without requiring any demand mechanism. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: In the diluter demand oxygen regulator, when does the demand valve operate?

choices.A: When the diluter control is set at normal.
choices.B: When the user demands 100 percent oxygen.
choices.C: When the user breathes.

Environmental Systems.explanation: In a diluter demand regulator, the demand valve is actuated by the slight negative pressure (vacuum) created each time the user inhales, supplying oxygen only on demand rather than continuously. This on-demand operation conserves oxygen supply compared to continuous-flow systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What is used in some oxygen systems to change high cylinder pressure to low system pressure?

choices.B: Calibrated fixed orifice.
choices.C: Diluter demand regulator.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A pressure reducer valve (pressure-reducing valve) steps down the very high storage pressure from the oxygen cylinder (up to 1,800–2,000 psi) to a lower, usable system pressure before it is distributed to the regulators and masks. This component protects downstream system components from damage due to high cylinder pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The purpose of the airflow metering aneroid assembly found in oxygen diluter demand regulators is to

choices.A: regulate airflow in relation to oxygen flow when operating in emergency or diluter demand positions.
choices.B: regulate airflow in relation to cabin altitude when in diluter demand position.
choices.C: automatically put the regulator in emergency position if the demand valve diaphragm ruptures.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The airflow metering aneroid assembly is an altitude-sensitive device that automatically adjusts the ratio of cabin air diluting the oxygen supply as cabin altitude changes, providing a richer oxygen mixture at higher altitudes and a more diluted mixture at lower altitudes. This ensures the user always receives an physiologically appropriate oxygen-to-air ratio. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: A contaminated oxygen system is normally purged with

choices.B: compressed air.
choices.C: nitrogen.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A contaminated oxygen system is purged with oxygen itself because oxygen is the system's operating medium and will not introduce contaminants such as moisture or hydrocarbons that compressed air or nitrogen might carry. Using oxygen ensures compatibility with system seals and components while flushing out contaminants. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 17 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What may be used as a lubricant on oxygen system tapered pipe thread connections?

choices.B: Glycerine.
choices.C: Teflon tape.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Teflon tape is the only approved thread sealant/lubricant for oxygen system tapered pipe thread connections because it is non-combustible, non-reactive with oxygen, and leaves no hydrocarbon residue that could cause a fire or explosion hazard. Oils, greases, and silicone compounds are strictly prohibited in oxygen systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 17 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: On transport category aircraft what might be an indication of an over pressure event of the aircraft oxygen system?

choices.A: The green thermal expansion disk missing.
choices.B: The green thermal expansion disk in the cockpit missing.
choices.C: The green thermal expansion disk on the oxygen regulator missing.

Environmental Systems.explanation: On transport category aircraft, an overpressure event in the oxygen system is indicated by the green thermal expansion (blow-out) disk being missing from the exterior of the fuselage, as it is designed to rupture and vent overboard when system pressure exceeds safe limits. The question asks generically where the disk would be missing, and the correct answer encompasses all locations (cockpit and regulator are subsets, not the complete answer). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 17 — Oxygen Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The operation of an angle of attack indicating system is based on detection of differential pressure at a point where the airstream flows in a direction

choices.A: not parallel to the true angle of attack of the aircraft.
choices.B: parallel to the angle of attack of the aircraft.
choices.C: parallel to the longitudinal axis of the aircraft.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The angle-of-attack indicating system detects differential pressure at a sensing port when the local airstream direction is parallel to the aircraft's true angle of attack, allowing the system to accurately measure the angle between the aircraft's longitudinal axis and the oncoming relative wind. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Ice and Rain Control Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: A fuel heater cannot use engine bleed air as a source of heat.
choices.B: A fuel heater can use engine lubricating oil as a source of heat.
choices.C: A fuel heater cannot use engine lubricating oil as a source of heat.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Fuel heaters can use engine lubricating oil as a heat source because engine oil operates at temperatures sufficient to prevent fuel icing and the oil-to-fuel heat exchanger is a standard design used on many aircraft. Engine bleed air can also be used, making option A false. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 15 — Ice and Rain Control Systems / Fuel Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: A fuel pressure gauge is not a differential pressure indicator.
choices.B: A fuel pressure gauge indicates the pressure of the fuel entering the carburetor.
choices.C: A fuel pressure gauge indicates the pressure of the fuel entering the combustion chamber.

Environmental Systems.explanation: A fuel pressure gauge on a reciprocating engine aircraft indicates the pressure of fuel entering the carburetor (at the carburetor inlet), which is a direct reading of the fuel pump output pressure delivered to the carburetor. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: Electric circuits are protected from overheating by

choices.B: AN/MS connectors.
choices.C: fuses.

Environmental Systems.explanation: Fuses are overcurrent protection devices designed to open an electrical circuit when current exceeds a safe level, thereby preventing wiring and components from overheating and causing fires or damage. Thermocouples measure temperature and AN/MS connectors are simply standardized electrical connectors with no protective function against overcurrent. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: The angle-of-attack detector operates from differential pressure when the airstream

choices.A: is parallel to the longitudinal axis of the aircraft.
choices.B: is not parallel to the true angle of attack of the aircraft.
choices.C: is parallel to the angle of attack of the aircraft.

Environmental Systems.explanation: The angle-of-attack detector senses differential pressure between two ports when the airstream direction is parallel to the aircraft's true angle of attack, meaning the pressure differential across the sensing vane or probe reflects the actual AOA of the aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Ice and Rain Control Systems)

Environmental Systems.question: What system component aids in the prevention of carburetor icing?

choices.B: Bleed air valve.
choices.C: Air diverter butterfly.

Environmental Systems.explanation: An air diverter butterfly valve redirects warm air from around the engine into the carburetor air intake, raising the temperature of incoming air above the freezing point and preventing the formation of carburetor ice. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Ice and Rain Control Systems)

Rotorcraft.question: The auxiliary (tail) rotor of a helicopter permits the pilot to compensate for and/or accomplish which of the following?

choices.B: Airspeed and trimmed flight.
choices.C: Torque and directional control.

Rotorcraft.explanation: The tail rotor produces a side thrust that directly counteracts the torque reaction of the main rotor on the fuselage, and by varying tail rotor pitch the pilot controls yaw, providing directional (heading) control of the helicopter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Rotorcraft)

Rotorcraft.question: The vertical flight of a helicopter is controlled by

choices.B: cyclic pitch changes.
choices.C: increasing or decreasing the RPM of the main rotor.

Rotorcraft.explanation: Vertical flight in a helicopter is controlled by collective pitch changes, which simultaneously increase or decrease the pitch angle of all main rotor blades equally, changing the total lift produced by the rotor system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Rotorcraft)

Rotorcraft.question: A reduction in anti-torque thrust will cause the

choices.A: tail to pivot in the opposite direction of torque rotation around the main rotor axis.
choices.B: tail to pivot in the direction of torque rotation around the main rotor axis.
choices.C: anti-torque system to become more efficient in the hover mode.

Rotorcraft.explanation: When anti-torque thrust is reduced, the tail rotor produces less side force to counteract the main rotor torque reaction, so the fuselage pivots in the same direction as the torque reaction (the direction the main rotor torque tries to rotate the fuselage). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Rotorcraft)

Rotorcraft.question: In rotorcraft external-loading, the ideal location of the cargo release is where the line of action passes

choices.A: aft of the center of gravity at all times.
choices.B: forward of the center of gravity at all times.
choices.C: through the center of gravity at all times.

Rotorcraft.explanation: For external-load operations, the cargo release mechanism must be positioned so that the line of action of the load passes through the helicopter's center of gravity at all times, ensuring stable, controllable flight without inducing unwanted pitch or roll moments. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Rotorcraft)

Rotorcraft.question: A helicopter in forward flight, cruise configuration, changes direction by

choices.A: varying the pitch of the main rotor blades.
choices.B: changing rotor RPM.
choices.C: tilting the main rotor disk in the desired direction.

Rotorcraft.explanation: A helicopter changes direction in forward flight by tilting the main rotor disk in the desired direction of travel via cyclic pitch inputs, which shifts the resultant rotor thrust vector to produce a horizontal force component in that direction. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Rotorcraft)

Rotorcraft.question: The purpose in checking main rotor blade tracking is to determine the

choices.A: relative position of the blades during rotation.
choices.B: flight path of the blades during rotation.
choices.C: extent of an out of balance condition during rotation.

Rotorcraft.explanation: Main rotor blade tracking checks determine the relative position (flight path plane) of each blade tip during rotation to ensure all blades are traveling in the same plane; blades that are out of track will cause vibration and uneven loading. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Rotorcraft)

Rotorcraft.question: In a hovering helicopter equipped with a tail rotor, directional control is maintained by

choices.B: tilting the main rotor disk in the desired direction.
choices.C: varying the pitch of the tail rotor blades.

Rotorcraft.explanation: In a hover, directional (yaw) control is achieved by varying the pitch angle of the tail rotor blades, which changes the amount of thrust produced by the tail rotor to counteract or augment main rotor torque. Changing RPM is not the design method used; pitch change provides the necessary thrust variation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Helicopter Systems)

Rotorcraft.question: If a single-rotor helicopter is in forward horizontal flight, the angle of attack of the advancing blade is

choices.B: equal to the retreating blade.
choices.C: less than the retreating blade.

Rotorcraft.explanation: In forward flight, the advancing blade moves into the relative wind, increasing its relative velocity, which allows it to produce the same lift at a lower angle of attack compared to the slower retreating blade, which must fly at a higher angle of attack to compensate. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Aerodynamics of Flight)

Rotorcraft.question: Main rotor blades that do not cone by the same amount during rotation are said to be out of

choices.B: collective pitch.
choices.C: track.

Rotorcraft.explanation: Tracking refers to the path each rotor blade tip travels in the same plane of rotation; blades that cone unequally are out of track because they are not all following the same circular path. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 4 — Rotor System Maintenance)

Rotorcraft.question: One purpose of the freewheeling unit required between the engine and the helicopter transmission is to

choices.A: automatically disengage the rotor from the engine in case of an engine failure.
choices.B: disconnect the rotor from the engine to relieve the starter load.
choices.C: permit practice of autorotation landings.

Rotorcraft.explanation: The freewheeling (overrunning clutch) unit automatically disconnects the engine from the rotor system when engine power is lost, allowing the rotor to continue spinning freely and enabling autorotation for a safe landing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Drive Systems)

Rotorcraft.question: Which statement is correct concerning torque effect on helicopters?

choices.A: Torque direction is the same as rotor blade rotation.
choices.B: As horsepower decreases, torque increases.
choices.C: Torque direction is the opposite of rotor blade rotation.

Rotorcraft.explanation: Per Newton's Third Law, the fuselage reacts to the rotor torque in the opposite direction of rotor blade rotation; this torque reaction is what the tail rotor must counteract. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Aerodynamics of Flight)

Rotorcraft.question: What is the purpose of the free-wheeling unit in a helicopter drive system?

choices.A: It disengages the engine from the main rotor when engine RPM is less than rotor RPM.
choices.B: It allows the engines to be started without any load from the transmission.
choices.C: It allows the rotors to turn slowly as the engine RPM increases.

Rotorcraft.explanation: The freewheeling unit is an overrunning clutch that automatically disconnects the engine from the rotor whenever engine RPM drops below rotor RPM, permitting autorotative flight after engine failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Drive Systems)

Rotorcraft.question: Movement about the longitudinal axis (roll) in a helicopter is effected by movement of the

choices.B: cyclic pitch control.
choices.C: tail rotor pitch control.

Rotorcraft.explanation: The cyclic pitch control changes the pitch of each rotor blade individually as it rotates, tilting the rotor disk laterally or longitudinally; lateral cyclic input produces roll (movement about the longitudinal axis). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Controls)

Rotorcraft.question: Movement about the lateral axis (pitch) in a helicopter is effected by movement of the

choices.B: cyclic pitch control.
choices.C: tail rotor pitch control.

Rotorcraft.explanation: Forward or aft cyclic input tilts the rotor disk fore or aft, producing pitch movement (rotation about the lateral axis); the cyclic control is responsible for both roll and pitch attitude changes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 2 — Flight Controls)

Rotorcraft.question: The rotor in an Autosyn remote indicating system uses

choices.B: a permanent magnet.
choices.C: neither an electromagnet nor a permanent magnet.

Rotorcraft.explanation: In an Autosyn system, the rotor is an electromagnet (excited by AC current) whose magnetic field aligns with the stator windings, transmitting positional information to a remote indicator. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Instrument Systems)

Rotorcraft.question: The rotor in a Magnesyn remote indicating system uses

choices.B: an electromagnet.
choices.C: an electromagnet and a permanent magnet.
