Rotorcraft.explanation: The Magnesyn system uses a permanent magnet rotor whose physical position induces voltages in the surrounding toroidal stator winding, transmitting that position to a remote indicator without requiring rotor excitation current. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 5 — Instrument Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Exposure to and/or storage near which of the following is considered harmful to aircraft tires?

choices.A: Helium, fuel, oil, and low humidity.
choices.B: Low humidity, helium, and ozone.
choices.C: Fuel, oil, ozone, electrical equipment, hydraulic fluid, and solvents.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Aircraft tires are susceptible to deterioration from hydrocarbon fluids (fuel, oil, hydraulic fluid, solvents) and ozone generated by electrical equipment, all of which degrade the rubber compounds; these are the recognized harmful agents per FAA standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Landing Gear Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Aside from an external leak in the line, what will cause parking brakes to continually bleed off pressure?

choices.A: An internal leak in the master cylinder.
choices.B: Insufficient hydraulic fluid in the reservoir.
choices.C: Glazed brake linings.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: An internal leak in the master cylinder allows pressurized brake fluid to bypass the piston and return to the reservoir, causing the parking brake pressure to bleed off even with no external leak present. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Brake Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: An automatic damping action occurs at the steering damper if for any reason the flow of high-pressure fluid is removed from the

choices.B: inlet of the steering damper.
choices.C: replenishing check valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: When high-pressure supply fluid is removed from the inlet of the steering damper, the unit's internal check valves close and trap fluid on both sides of the piston, automatically providing hydraulic dampening resistance to shimmy or sudden steering inputs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Landing Gear Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The purpose of a sequence valve in a hydraulic retractable landing gear system is

choices.A: prevent heavy landing gear from falling too rapidly upon extension.
choices.B: provide a means of disconnecting the normal source of hydraulic power and connecting the emergency source of power.
choices.C: ensure operation of the landing gear and gear doors in the proper order.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Sequence valves are used to ensure that hydraulic actuators operate in a predetermined order — for example, requiring gear doors to fully open before the gear begins to extend or retract — preventing mechanical interference and structural damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Landing Gear Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The pressure source for power brakes is

choices.B: the power brake reservoir.
choices.C: a master cylinder.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Power brakes are actuated directly from the aircraft's main hydraulic system pressure, providing greater braking force than independent master cylinders; the main system supplies the high-pressure fluid required for power brake operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Brake Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What type of valve is used in the brake actuating line to isolate the emergency brake system from the normal power brake control valve system?

choices.B: An orifice check valve.
choices.C: A shuttle valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A shuttle valve automatically shifts to connect the emergency brake system to the brake actuating line while simultaneously blocking the normal power brake control valve, isolating the two systems so emergency pressure reaches the brakes without backflowing into the normal system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Brake Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If a brake debooster is used in a hydraulic brake system, its position in the system will be

choices.A: between the pressure manifold of the main hydraulic system and the power brake control valve.
choices.B: between the brake control valve and the brake actuating cylinder.
choices.C: in the brake pressure line between the brake pedal and the brake accumulator.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A brake debooster is placed downstream of the brake control valve and upstream of the brake actuating cylinder to reduce high main system pressure to a lower pressure suitable for the brake assembly, protecting brake components from excessive hydraulic force. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Brake Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: When installing a chevron-type seal in an aircraft hydraulic cylinder, the open side of the seal should face

choices.A: opposite the direction of fluid pressure.
choices.B: up or forward when the unit is installed in a horizontal position.
choices.C: the direction of fluid pressure.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Chevron (V-type) seals are pressure-energized; the open or concave side must face the fluid pressure so that system pressure forces the lips of the seal outward against the cylinder walls, creating an effective seal. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Seals)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What device in a hydraulic system with a constant-delivery pump allows circulation of the fluid when no demands are on the system?

choices.B: Shuttle valve.
choices.C: Pressure regulator.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A pressure regulator (also called an unloading valve) allows a constant-delivery pump to circulate fluid back to the reservoir at low pressure when system demands are satisfied, preventing continuous high-pressure output and relieving stress on the pump. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A fully charged hydraulic accumulator provides

choices.A: air pressure to the various hydraulic components.
choices.B: a source for additional hydraulic power when heavy demands are placed on the system.
choices.C: positive fluid flow to the pump inlet.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A fully charged accumulator stores hydraulic fluid under pressure and supplements the pump output during peak or heavy demands, acting as an auxiliary power source to maintain system pressure and fluid flow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A hydraulic system referred to as a 'power pack' system will

choices.A: have an engine-driven pump for greater pressure.
choices.B: have all hydraulic power components located in one unit.
choices.C: have a pressurized reservoir.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A power pack system integrates all hydraulic components—pump, reservoir, actuators, valves, and filters—into a single self-contained unit, simplifying installation and maintenance on smaller aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: An O-ring intended for use in a hydraulic system using MIL-H-5606 (mineral base) fluid will be marked with

choices.B: one or more white dots.
choices.C: a white and yellow stripe.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: O-rings compatible with MIL-H-5606 mineral-base hydraulic fluid are color-coded with a blue stripe or dot to distinguish them from seals designed for other fluid types, preventing installation of an incompatible seal that would deteriorate and fail. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Seals)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What condition would most likely cause excessive fluctuation of the pressure gauge when the hydraulic pump is operating?

choices.B: Inadequate supply of fluid.
choices.C: System relief valve sticking closed.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Low accumulator air precharge pressure means the accumulator cannot adequately dampen pressure fluctuations in the system, causing the pressure gauge to oscillate excessively as the pump cycles and demand varies. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A filter incorporating specially treated cellulose paper is identified as a

choices.B: CUNO filter.
choices.C: Micronic filter.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A Micronic filter uses specially treated cellulose paper as its filtering element and is capable of removing very fine contaminant particles, making it one of the most efficient filtration methods used in aircraft hydraulic systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The purpose of an orifice check valve is to

choices.A: relieve pressure to a sensitive component.
choices.B: restrict flow in one direction and allow free flow in the other.
choices.C: relieve pressure in one direction and prevent flow in the other direction.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: An orifice check valve permits unrestricted fluid flow in one direction but restricts (meters) flow through a calibrated orifice in the opposite direction, controlling actuator speed or cushioning movement in that direction. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which of the following is the most commonly used seal to prevent internal and external leakage in both directions of a hydraulic unit?

choices.B: V-ring.
choices.C: U-ring.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The O-ring is the most universally used seal in hydraulic systems because its symmetrical cross-section allows it to prevent leakage in both directions under static and dynamic conditions, and it is compatible with a wide range of hydraulic fluids and housing geometries. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Seals)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which of the following allows fluid to flow unimpeded in one direction but prevents fluid flow in the other direction?

choices.B: Sequence valve.
choices.C: Relief valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A check valve is a one-way flow device that opens to allow free fluid flow in one direction and closes to prevent any flow in the opposite direction, protecting system components from reverse flow and backpressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Select the valve used in a hydraulic system that directs pressurized fluid to one end of an actuating cylinder and simultaneously directs return fluid to the reservoir from the other end.

choices.B: Shuttle.
choices.C: Selector.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A selector valve directs pressurized fluid to one port of an actuating cylinder while simultaneously opening the opposite port to the return line, enabling controlled bidirectional movement of the actuator. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: One of the distinguishing characteristics of an open-center selector valve used in a hydraulic system is that

choices.A: fluid flows through the valve in the OFF position.
choices.B: fluid flows in three directions in the ON position.
choices.C: a limited amount of fluid flows in one direction and no fluid flows in the opposite direction.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: In an open-center hydraulic system, the selector valve is designed so that fluid continuously flows through the valve and back to the reservoir when no system function is selected (OFF position), keeping the pump unloaded and fluid circulating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Power Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What type of packings should be used in hydraulic components to be installed in a system containing Skydrol?

choices.A: AN packings made of natural rubber.
choices.B: Packing materials made for ester-base fluids.
choices.C: AN packings made of neoprene.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Skydrol is a phosphate ester-base fluid that is chemically incompatible with natural rubber and neoprene, so only packing materials specifically formulated for ester-base fluids (such as ethylene-propylene or EPDM compounds) must be used to prevent seal degradation and system leakage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids and Seals)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Relief valves are used in pneumatic systems

choices.B: to reduce the rate of airflow.
choices.C: as damage-preventing units.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Relief valves in pneumatic systems are safety devices designed to protect system components from overpressure by venting excess pressure when a predetermined limit is reached, thereby preventing damage to lines, fittings, and components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: An aircraft pneumatic system, which incorporates an engine-driven multistage reciprocating compressor, also requires

choices.B: a surge chamber.
choices.C: a moisture separator.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Reciprocating compressors are lubricated internally, which means oil vapor is introduced into the compressed air supply, so an oil separator is required downstream of the compressor to remove oil contamination before the air enters system components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The removal of air from an aircraft hydraulic system is generally accomplished

choices.A: through automatic bleed valves on individual components during system operation.
choices.B: by operating the various hydraulic components through several cycles.
choices.C: by allowing the system to remain inoperative for several hours.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Air trapped in a hydraulic system is removed by cycling the various hydraulic actuators and components through several complete operations, which forces the entrained air back to the reservoir where it can escape. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Pneumatic systems utilize

choices.B: relief valves.
choices.C: shuttle valves.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Like hydraulic systems, pneumatic systems require relief valves to protect against overpressure conditions and prevent damage to system components and plumbing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The component in the hydraulic system that is used to direct the flow of fluid is the

choices.B: orifice check valve.
choices.C: selector valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Selector valves are directional control valves used to direct hydraulic fluid to and from actuating units, allowing the operator to control the direction of movement of hydraulic components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What type of selector valve is one of the most commonly used in hydraulic systems to provide for simultaneous flow of fluid into and out of a connected actuating unit?

choices.B: Three-port, four-way valve.
choices.C: Two-port, open-center valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A four-port, closed-center selector valve is the most common type used because its four ports allow simultaneous pressure supply to one side of an actuator and return flow from the other side, and the closed center stops fluid flow when in the neutral position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What is the purpose of using backup rings with O-rings in hydraulic systems above 1,500 psi?

choices.A: Prevent internal and external leakage of all moving parts within a hydraulic system.
choices.B: Provide a seal between two parts of a unit which move in relation to each other.
choices.C: Prevent high pressure from extruding the seal between the moving and stationary part.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: At pressures above 1,500 psi, hydraulic pressure is sufficient to force (extrude) O-ring material into the clearance gap between mating parts, so backup rings are installed on the low-pressure side of the O-ring to block that gap and prevent extrusion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The purpose of the pressure regulator in a hydraulic system is to

choices.A: maintain system operating pressure within a predetermined range and to unload the pump.
choices.B: regulate the amount of fluid flow to the actuating cylinders within the system.
choices.C: prevent failure of components or rupture of hydraulic lines under excessive pressure.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The pressure regulator maintains hydraulic system pressure within a designed operating range and unloads the pump by allowing it to circulate fluid at low pressure back to the reservoir once the desired system pressure is reached, reducing wear and heat buildup. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The installation of a new metal hydraulic line should be made with

choices.A: a straight tube to withstand the shocks and vibration to which it will be subjected.
choices.B: a straight tube to permit proper alignment of the fitting and thereby reduce fluid loss through leakage.
choices.C: enough bends to allow the tube to expand and contract with temperature changes and to absorb vibration.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Metal hydraulic tubing must be installed with sufficient bends to accommodate thermal expansion and contraction as well as airframe vibration; a straight rigid installation would be subject to stress cracking and fatigue failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What is one advantage of piston-type hydraulic motors over electric motors?

choices.A: They are considerably quieter in operation.
choices.B: There is no fire hazard if the motor is stalled.
choices.C: They work satisfactorily over a wider temperature range.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: If a hydraulic motor is stalled under load, it simply stops turning without generating heat, sparks, or risk of fire, whereas an electric motor that is stalled will overheat and may create a fire or burn-out hazard. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Generally, the first step in removing an accumulator from an aircraft is to

choices.B: discharge the preload.
choices.C: drain the reservoir.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Before removing an accumulator, all hydraulic system pressure must be relieved first to prevent injury from pressurized fluid or a sudden release of stored energy from the accumulator's gas precharge. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If a hydraulic brake system uses neoprene rubber packing materials, the correct hydraulic fluid to service the system is

choices.B: synthetic-base oil.
choices.C: phosphate ester-base oil.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Neoprene rubber seals and packings are compatible with mineral-base (MIL-H-5606/MIL-H-6083) hydraulic fluids; using synthetic or phosphate ester-base fluids with neoprene seals would cause the seals to deteriorate and swell. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What is the viscosity of hydraulic fluid?

choices.A: The increase in volume of a fluid due to temperature change.
choices.B: The fluid's ability to resist oxidation and deterioration for long periods.
choices.C: The internal resistance of a fluid which tends to prevent it from flowing.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Viscosity is defined as a fluid's internal resistance to flow; it is a critical property of hydraulic fluid because it affects the efficiency of the system and the lubrication of components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which is a characteristic of petroleum-base hydraulic fluid?

choices.B: Compatible to natural rubber seals and packings.
choices.C: Nonflammable under all conditions.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Petroleum-base hydraulic fluids (such as MIL-H-5606) are flammable under normal conditions, which is a key safety consideration when selecting fluid type for a particular aircraft system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: When servicing aircraft hydraulic systems, use the type fluid specified in the

choices.B: instruction plate affixed to the unit.
choices.C: aircraft manufacturer's maintenance manual or instruction plate affixed to the unit.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Both the aircraft manufacturer's maintenance manual and any instruction plate affixed to the hydraulic unit are authoritative sources for the correct fluid type, and either or both should be consulted to ensure the proper fluid is used. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Petroleum-base hydraulic fluid is which color?

choices.B: Blue.
choices.C: Red.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Petroleum-base hydraulic fluid (MIL-H-5606) is dyed red to allow easy identification and to distinguish it from other fluid types used in aircraft systems. Color-coding prevents accidental mixing of incompatible fluids. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which of the following is adversely affected by atmospheric humidity if left unprotected?

choices.B: MIL-H-5606 hydraulic fluid.
choices.C: Skydrol hydraulic fluid.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Skydrol (phosphate ester-base fluid) is hygroscopic and will absorb moisture from the atmosphere, which degrades its fire-resistant properties and overall performance if left unprotected. MIL-H-5606 (petroleum base) does not share this vulnerability to atmospheric humidity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which is a characteristic of synthetic-base hydraulic fluid?

choices.B: High flash point.
choices.C: Low flash point.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Synthetic-base hydraulic fluids such as Skydrol are formulated to have a high flash point, which is a key safety advantage making them fire-resistant compared to petroleum-base fluids. This property is essential for use in areas of high fire risk on transport-category aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Two types of hydraulic fluids currently being used in civil aircraft are

choices.A: mineral base, and phosphate ester base.
choices.B: mixed mineral base and phosphate ester base.
choices.C: petroleum base and mixed mineral base.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The two hydraulic fluid types currently approved for use in civil aircraft are mineral (petroleum) base and phosphate ester base, each requiring specific and incompatible seal materials and system components. Mixing these two types will contaminate the system and cause seal failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which of the following lists only desirable properties of a good hydraulic fluid that has chemical stability?

choices.A: High viscosity, low flash point, high fire point.
choices.B: High flash point, low viscosity, low fire point.
choices.C: Low viscosity, high flash point, high fire point.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A chemically stable hydraulic fluid should have low viscosity for efficient flow, a high flash point to resist ignition, and a high fire point to minimize fire hazard — all of which are desirable safety and performance properties. Low viscosity also reduces heat buildup and improves system responsiveness. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Characteristics of MIL-H-8446 hydraulic fluid are

choices.A: blue color, phosphate ester base, fire resistant, butyl rubber seals.
choices.B: light purple color, phosphate ester base, fire resistant, butyl rubber seals.
choices.C: light green color, phosphate ester base, fire resistant, butyl rubber seals.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: MIL-H-8446 is a phosphate ester-base, fire-resistant hydraulic fluid identifiable by its light green color and requiring butyl rubber seals due to its incompatibility with natural and neoprene rubber. This distinguishes it from other military-spec hydraulic fluids used in aviation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Where can information be obtained about the compatibility of fire-resistant hydraulic fluid with aircraft materials?

choices.B: Aircraft manufacturer's specifications.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Because fire-resistant hydraulic fluid compatibility with aircraft materials can vary by formulation and aircraft design, the fluid manufacturer's technical bulletins provide the most current and specific compatibility data. Aircraft manufacturer specs address system design but may not reflect the latest fluid formulations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Characteristics of MIL-H-5606 hydraulic fluid are

choices.A: light purple color, phosphate ester base, fire resistant, uses butyl rubber seals.
choices.B: blue color, will burn, uses natural rubber seals.
choices.C: red color, petroleum base, will burn, uses synthetic rubber seals.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: MIL-H-5606 is a petroleum-base fluid that is red in color, flammable (will burn), and requires synthetic rubber (neoprene) seals to maintain compatibility and prevent seal degradation. Its petroleum base makes it incompatible with phosphate ester-base fluids and butyl rubber seals. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Characteristics of MIL-H-7644 hydraulic fluid are

choices.A: red color, petroleum base, will burn, synthetic rubber seals.
choices.B: light purple color, phosphate ester base, fire resistant, butyl rubber seals.
choices.C: blue color, vegetable base, will burn, natural rubber seals.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: MIL-H-7644 is a vegetable-base hydraulic fluid that is blue in color, flammable, and uses natural rubber seals, making it distinct from both petroleum-base and phosphate ester-base fluids. It is an older fluid type and largely obsolete in modern aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If an aircraft hydraulic system requires mineral-base hydraulic fluid, but phosphate ester-base hydraulic fluid is used, what will be the effect on the system?

choices.B: System will be contaminated, fluids will not blend, and the seals will fail.
choices.C: System will be contaminated, fluids will not blend, but there will be no seal problem.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Petroleum-base and phosphate ester-base hydraulic fluids are chemically incompatible — they will not blend and will contaminate the system — and the seal materials designed for one fluid type will be attacked and destroyed by the other. This makes cross-contamination a serious maintenance error requiring complete system flushing and seal replacement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What is used to flush a system normally serviced with MIL-H-5606 hydraulic fluid?

choices.B: Naphtha or varsol.
choices.C: Lacquer thinner or trichlorethylene.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Systems serviced with MIL-H-5606 (petroleum-base) fluid are flushed with petroleum-compatible solvents such as naphtha or varsol, which are chemically compatible and will not damage the system's seals or components. Solvents like lacquer thinner or MEK are inappropriate and could damage seals or leave harmful residues. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Components containing phosphate ester-base hydraulic fluid may be cleaned with

choices.B: Naphtha.
choices.C: Carbon tetrachloride.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Carbon tetrachloride is compatible with phosphate ester-base (Skydrol) hydraulic fluid and can be used to clean components without damaging them, whereas petroleum-base solvents like Stoddard solvent or naphtha are incompatible and would contaminate or degrade the system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: How can the proper hydraulic fluid to be used in an airplane be determined?

choices.A: By referring to the Pilot Operating Handbook.
choices.B: By consulting the aircraft's Type Certificate Data Sheet.
choices.C: By consulting the aircraft manufacturer's service manual.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The aircraft manufacturer's service manual specifies the correct hydraulic fluid type approved for that specific aircraft's system, including seal and component compatibility requirements. The Type Certificate Data Sheet and POH do not typically contain this level of maintenance-specific fluid detail. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Phosphate ester-base hydraulic fluid is very susceptible to contamination from

choices.B: water in the atmosphere.
choices.C: icing conditions.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Phosphate ester-base hydraulic fluids (e.g., Skydrol) are hygroscopic, meaning they readily absorb water vapor from the atmosphere, which degrades their fire-resistant properties and can cause corrosion and system component damage. Containers must be kept sealed when not in use to prevent moisture contamination. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The hydraulic component that automatically directs fluid from either the normal source or an emergency source to an actuating cylinder is called a

choices.B: shuttle valve.
choices.C: crossflow valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A shuttle valve automatically shifts to direct fluid from an alternate or emergency source to an actuating cylinder when normal system pressure is lost, ensuring continued operation of critical systems such as brakes or landing gear. It requires no manual intervention to switch fluid sources. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What is commonly used to connect an emergency source of power, and at the same time disconnect the normal hydraulic source from critical parts of a landing gear or wheel braking system for operation (usually when the normal source system fails)?

choices.B: Shuttle valve.
choices.C: Sequence valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A shuttle valve automatically shifts to connect an emergency pressure source while simultaneously isolating the normal hydraulic source, ensuring critical systems like landing gear and brakes remain operable when the normal system fails. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The primary purpose of a hydraulic actuating unit is to transform

choices.A: fluid motion into mechanical pressure and back again.
choices.B: fluid pressure into useful work.
choices.C: energy from one form to another.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Hydraulic actuating units convert stored fluid pressure (potential energy) into mechanical force and motion, which constitutes useful work such as moving a flight control surface or extending landing gear. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A unit which transforms hydraulic pressure into linear motion is called

choices.B: an accumulator.
choices.C: a hydraulic pump.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: An actuating cylinder converts hydraulic pressure into straight-line (linear) mechanical motion by allowing pressurized fluid to act on a piston, producing a push or pull force along a single axis. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If it is necessary to adjust several pressure regulating valves in a hydraulic system, what particular sequence, if any, should be followed?

choices.A: Units most distant from the hydraulic pump should be adjusted first.
choices.B: Units with the highest pressure settings are adjusted first.
choices.C: Units are independent of each other, and therefore, no particular sequence is necessary.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: When adjusting multiple pressure regulating valves, those with the highest pressure settings must be adjusted first because lower-pressure valves downstream may open prematurely and bleed off pressure before the higher-pressure valve can be properly set. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If an aircraft's constant-pressure hydraulic system cycles more frequently than usual and no fluid leakage can be detected, the most probable cause is

choices.A: a too high relief valve setting.
choices.B: pump volume output too high.
choices.C: low accumulator air preload.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: When an accumulator has insufficient air preload pressure, it has very little fluid storage capacity, causing the pump to cycle on and off more frequently to maintain system pressure even without any external fluid leakage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Unloading valves are used with many engine-driven hydraulic pumps to

choices.B: relieve the pump pressure.
choices.C: relieve system pressure.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Unloading valves sense when system pressure has reached its upper limit and divert pump output back to the reservoir at low pressure, relieving the pump itself of the load and reducing heat buildup and wear. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What safety device is usually located between the driving unit and hydraulic pump drive shaft?

choices.B: Pump motor safety switch.
choices.C: Pump drive coupling shear section.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A shear section (or shear neck) in the pump drive coupling is designed to break before the pump or driving unit is damaged if a hydraulic pump seizes, protecting both the engine accessory drive and the pump. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which valve installed in a hydraulic system will have the highest pressure setting?

choices.B: Main relief valve.
choices.C: Thermal relief valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A thermal relief valve is set above the main relief valve pressure to protect localized areas of the system from excessive pressure buildup caused by thermal expansion of trapped fluid, so its setting must exceed all other valve settings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Excluding lines, which components are required to make up a simple hydraulic system?

choices.A: Actuator, pressure reservoir, accumulator, and selector valve.
choices.B: Pump, reservoir, selector valve, and actuator.
choices.C: Pump, reservoir, relief valve, and shuttle valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A basic functional hydraulic system requires a reservoir to store fluid, a pump to pressurize it, a selector valve to direct flow, and an actuator to convert pressure into work; a relief valve is a safety addition and an accumulator is supplemental, not required for basic operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Most variable displacement aircraft hydraulic pumps in use

choices.A: must be driven at a nearly constant speed in order to be practical for use.
choices.B: are not practical for use with a closed-center hydraulic system.
choices.C: contain a built-in means of system pressure regulation.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Variable displacement pumps vary their own output volume to maintain constant system pressure through an internal compensator mechanism, eliminating the need for a separate pressure regulator or unloading valve in many systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: In a gear-type hydraulic pump, a mechanical safety device incorporated to protect the pump from overload is the

choices.B: check valve.
choices.C: shear pin.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A shear pin in a gear-type hydraulic pump is designed to fail under excessive torque loads, protecting the pump gears and housing from damage due to hydraulic lock or mechanical overload. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: After installation of a rebuilt hydraulic hand pump, it is found that the handle cannot be moved in the pumping direction (pressure stroke). The most likely cause is an incorrectly installed

choices.B: inport/outport orifice check valve.
choices.C: hand pump outport check valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: If the outport check valve is installed backwards, it blocks fluid from leaving the pump on the pressure stroke, making the handle immovable in the pumping direction; an incorrectly installed inport check valve would instead prevent fluid from entering the pump on the intake stroke. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If two actuating cylinders which have the same cross-sectional area but different lengths of stroke are connected to the same source of hydraulic pressure, they will exert

choices.A: different amounts of force but will move at the same rate of speed.
choices.B: equal amounts of force but will move at different rates of speed.
choices.C: equal amounts of force and will move at the same rate of speed.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Per Pascal's Law, the same pressure applied to actuating cylinders of identical cross-sectional area produces the same force regardless of stroke length, and since bore area is equal, fluid velocity and therefore piston speed will also be identical. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Using a hand pump, pressure of 100 psi has been built up in a hydraulic system. The hand pump piston is 1 inch in diameter. A 1/2-inch line connects the hand pump to an actuating cylinder 2 inches in diameter. What is the pressure in the line between the hand pump and the actuator?

choices.B: 150 psi.
choices.C: 200 psi.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Pascal's Law states that pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished throughout the system, so the pressure everywhere in the line remains the same 100 psi regardless of line or cylinder diameter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Heat exchanger cooling units are required in some aircraft hydraulic systems because of

choices.B: high pressures and high rates of fluid flow.
choices.C: the high heat generated from braking.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: High operating pressures combined with high fluid flow rates generate significant heat through friction and fluid shear in modern hydraulic systems, necessitating heat exchangers to keep fluid temperatures within safe limits and prevent fluid degradation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A hydraulic system operational check during ground runup of an aircraft indicates that the wing flaps cannot be lowered using the main hydraulic system, but can be lowered by using the emergency hand pump. Which is the most likely cause?

choices.A: The flap selector valve has a severe internal leak.
choices.B: The pressure accumulator is not supplying pressure to the system.
choices.C: The fluid level in the reservoir is low.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: When the main hydraulic pump cannot lower the flaps but the hand pump (which draws directly from the reservoir's standpipe or bottom sump) can, the most likely cause is a low fluid level — the main pump's inlet is uncovered while enough fluid remains at the bottom of the reservoir for the hand pump to draw from. A leaking selector valve or failed accumulator would not selectively disable the main system while leaving the hand pump functional in this manner. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Many hydraulic reservoirs contain a small quantity of fluid which is not available to the main system pump. This fluid is retained to

choices.B: supply fluid to the auxiliary pump.
choices.C: supply fluid to the pressure accumulator.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Hydraulic reservoirs are designed with a standpipe or baffle so that a small quantity of fluid at the bottom is unavailable to the main system pump but is reserved to supply the auxiliary (emergency hand) pump, ensuring emergency operation remains available even if the main system loses fluid. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The unit in a hydraulic system that requires a certain action to be completed before another action can begin is called a

choices.B: sequence valve.
choices.C: snubber valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A sequence valve controls the order of operation in a hydraulic system by requiring a specified pressure level — indicating completion of one actuator's travel — to be reached before allowing fluid to flow to the next actuator in the sequence. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The purpose of a hydraulic pressure regulator is to

choices.A: prevent the system pressure from rising above a predetermined amount due to thermal expansion.
choices.B: boost the pressure in portions of the system.
choices.C: relieve the pump of its load when no actuating units are being operated.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A hydraulic pressure regulator (also called an unloading valve) unloads the pump by bypassing fluid back to the reservoir once system pressure reaches a set value, relieving the pump of its load when no actuating units are being operated and allowing it to operate under reduced pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Severe kickback of the emergency hydraulic hand pump handle during the normal intake stroke will indicate which of the following?

choices.A: The hand pump inlet check valve is sticking open.
choices.B: The main system relief valve is set too high.
choices.C: The hand pump outlet check valve is sticking open.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: If the hand pump outlet check valve sticks open, high-pressure fluid from the system can flow back through the outlet port during the intake stroke, causing severe kickback of the pump handle because the piston is being forced backward by system pressure rather than drawing in low-pressure fluid. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What type of valve in an aircraft hydraulic system permits fluid to flow freely in one direction, but restricts the rate at which fluid is allowed to flow in the other direction?

choices.B: Orifice restrictor.
choices.C: Orifice check valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: An orifice check valve allows unrestricted free flow in one direction but includes a calibrated orifice that restricts (meters) the flow rate in the opposite direction, combining the functions of a check valve and a flow restrictor in a single unit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The main system pressure relief valve in a simple hydraulic system equipped with a power control valve should be adjusted

choices.A: with the power control valve held in the CLOSED position.
choices.B: while one or more actuating units are in operation.
choices.C: with the power control valve in the OPEN position.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The main system relief valve must be adjusted with the power control valve (unloading valve) in the open (bypassing) position so that full pump output flows through the relief valve during adjustment, ensuring the relief valve setting accurately reflects system relief pressure rather than being influenced by the unloading valve's action. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which of the following safeguards ensures proper system operation and mitigates damage to non-metallic components of the hydraulic system?

choices.A: The manual bleed valve should be closed prior to servicing hydraulic fluid to prevent fluid loss as the cap is being removed.
choices.B: Before assembly of any hydraulic components, seals and gaskets should be inspected and replaced only if they show signs of wear or leakage.
choices.C: When adding fluid to a system, use the type specified in the aircraft manufacturer's maintenance manual or on the instruction plate affixed to the reservoir or unit being serviced.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Using only the hydraulic fluid type specified by the manufacturer or placarded on the reservoir is critical because different fluid types (petroleum-base, phosphate ester, etc.) are chemically incompatible with seals and other non-metallic components designed for a specific fluid, and mixing fluids can cause seal degradation and system failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A hydraulic accumulator is charged with an air preload of 1,000 psi. When a hydraulic system pressure of 3,000 psi is developed, the pressure on the air side of the accumulator will be
Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The accumulator uses a flexible or movable separator; when hydraulic system pressure of 3,000 psi acts on the fluid side, it compresses the air until pressure equalizes across the separator, so the air side pressure rises to equal the fluid side pressure of 3,000 psi. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: How is the air in a hydraulic accumulator prevented from entering the fluid system?

choices.A: By forcing the oil/air mixture through a centrifugal separating chamber that prevents the air from leaving the accumulator.
choices.B: By physically separating the air chamber from the oil chamber with a flexible or movable separator.
choices.C: By including a valve that automatically closes when the fluid level lowers to a preset amount.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Accumulators use a physical separator — either a flexible bladder, diaphragm, or floating piston — to keep the air (or nitrogen) charge completely isolated from the hydraulic fluid, preventing air from entering and aerating the fluid system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: After a hydraulic accumulator has been installed and air chamber charged, the main system hydraulic pressure gauge will not show a hydraulic pressure reading until

choices.A: at least one selector valve has been actuated to allow fluid to flow into the fluid side of the accumulator.
choices.B: the air pressure has become equal to the fluid pressure.
choices.C: the fluid side of the accumulator has been charged.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The hydraulic system pressure gauge senses fluid-side pressure; until the fluid side of the accumulator has been charged by the hydraulic pump building pressure against the pre-charged air side, there is no hydraulic pressure in the system for the gauge to register. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which must be done before adjusting the relief valve of a main hydraulic system incorporating a pressure regulator?

choices.A: Eliminate the action of the unloading valve.
choices.B: Adjust all other system relief valves which have a lower pressure setting.
choices.C: Manually unseat all system check valves to allow unrestricted flow in both directions.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Before adjusting the main system relief valve in a system with a pressure regulator (unloading valve), the unloading valve must be disabled or bypassed so it cannot divert pump flow before system pressure reaches the relief valve setting, allowing accurate adjustment of the relief valve to true system pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which seals are used with petroleum-base hydraulic fluids?

choices.B: Butyl rubber.
choices.C: Buna-N.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Buna-N (nitrile) rubber seals are compatible with petroleum-base hydraulic fluids (MIL-H-5606, MIL-H-6083) and are the standard seal material used in systems utilizing these fluids; butyl rubber and polyester seals are used with phosphate ester (Skydrol-type) fluids. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Seals used with a mineral-base hydraulic fluid are identified by which color code?

choices.B: Blue dot or stripe.
choices.C: Yellow dot or stripe.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Per FAA standards, seals compatible with mineral-base (petroleum-base) hydraulic fluid are identified by a blue dot or stripe color code, allowing technicians to quickly identify the correct seal type during maintenance and replacement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The air that is expended and no longer needed when an actuating unit is operated in a pneumatic system is

choices.B: returned to the compressor.
choices.C: charged or pressurized for use during the next operating cycle.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Unlike hydraulic systems where fluid is recirculated, pneumatic systems are open systems — the compressed air that actuates a component is exhausted overboard or vented to atmosphere after its work is done, because returning it to the compressor would be impractical. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Some hydraulic systems incorporate a device which is designed to remain open to allow a normal fluid flow in the line, but closed if the fluid flow increases above an established rate. This device is generally referred to as a

choices.B: flow regulator.
choices.C: metering check valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A hydraulic fuse is specifically designed to allow normal fluid flow but automatically closes when flow rate exceeds a predetermined limit, protecting the system from catastrophic fluid loss due to a downstream line rupture or actuator failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: When hydraulic system pressure control and relief units fail to function properly, how are most systems protected against overpressure?

choices.A: A shear section on the main hydraulic pump drive shaft.
choices.B: One or more hydraulic fuses installed in the pressure and return lines.
choices.C: A shuttle valve interconnecting the main and emergency systems.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Most hydraulic systems protect against overpressure caused by pump drive failures by incorporating a shear section on the pump drive shaft, which will shear before pump or system damage occurs if pressures become dangerously high. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A worn hydraulic pump shaft seal can normally be detected by

choices.A: hydraulic fluid flowing from the pump drain line.
choices.B: evidence of hydraulic fluid combined in the engine oil.
choices.C: the presence of hydraulic fluid around the pump mounting pad.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A worn shaft seal on a hydraulic pump allows fluid to leak past the seal and exit through the pump case drain line, which is the normal indicator of shaft seal deterioration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Pumps)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If an engine-driven hydraulic pump of the correct capacity fails to maintain normal system pressure during the operation of a cowl flap actuating unit, the probable cause

choices.A: mechanical interference to the movement of the cowl flap.
choices.B: a partial restriction in the inport of the selector valve.
choices.C: restriction in the pump outlet.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: If an engine-driven hydraulic pump of correct capacity cannot maintain normal system pressure while operating a cowl flap, the most probable cause is mechanical interference resisting the cowl flap's movement, increasing the load demand beyond what pressure alone would indicate as a pump or valve fault. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Troubleshooting)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Before removing the filler cap of a pressurized hydraulic reservoir, in order to service the system, the aviation mechanic must

choices.B: pressurize all hydraulic components in the system.
choices.C: relieve the air pressure.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A pressurized hydraulic reservoir uses air or nitrogen pressure above the fluid; before removing the filler cap, the air pressure must be relieved first to prevent the cap from being ejected violently or causing injury. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Reservoirs)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What happens to the output of a constant-displacement hydraulic pump when the hydraulic system pressure regulator diverts the fluid from the system to the reservoir?

choices.A: The output pressure remains the same, but the volume reduces.
choices.B: The output pressure reduces, but the volume remains the same.
choices.C: The output pressure and volume remain the same.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A constant-displacement pump always moves the same volume of fluid per revolution regardless of system demand; when the pressure regulator bypasses flow back to the reservoir, the pump continues producing the same pressure and volume output since no mechanical change occurs at the pump itself. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Pumps)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Hydraulic system accumulators serve which of the following functions?

choices.B: Dampen pressure surges and ensure a continuous supply of fluid to the pump.
choices.C: Supplement the system pump when demand is beyond the pump's capacity and store power for limited operation of components if the pump is not operating.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Accumulators store pressurized hydraulic fluid that can supplement the pump during peak demand periods and provide limited system operation if the pump is not running, in addition to dampening pressure surges. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Accumulators)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Chattering of the hydraulic pump during operation is an indication

choices.B: that the main system relief valve is sticking open.
choices.C: that air is entering the pump.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Chattering or cavitation noise from a hydraulic pump is a classic symptom of air being ingested into the pump inlet, which causes the fluid to cavitate and produces a distinctive chattering or knocking sound. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Troubleshooting)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Quick-disconnect couplings in hydraulic systems provide a means of

choices.A: easily replacing hydraulic lines in areas where leaks are common.
choices.B: quickly connecting and disconnecting hydraulic lines and eliminate the possibility of contaminates entering the system.
choices.C: quickly connecting and disconnecting hydraulic lines without loss of fluid or entrance of air into the system.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Quick-disconnect couplings are designed with self-sealing valves on both halves so that when disconnected, fluid loss is prevented and air cannot enter the system, allowing rapid servicing or component replacement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic System Components)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which seal/material is used with phosphate ester-base hydraulic fluids?

choices.B: Butyl rubber.
choices.C: Neoprene rubber.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Phosphate ester-base hydraulic fluids (such as Skydrol) are incompatible with natural rubber and neoprene but are compatible with butyl rubber, which is the approved seal material for use with these fluids. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Fluids and Seals)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A hydraulic pump is a constant-displacement type if it

choices.B: produces a continuous positive pressure.
choices.C: delivers a uniform rate of fluid flow.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A constant-displacement pump delivers a fixed volume of fluid per revolution regardless of system pressure, meaning it produces a uniform rate of fluid flow at all times the pump is operating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Pumps)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A hydraulic motor converts fluid pressure to

choices.B: rotary motion.
choices.C: angular motion.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A hydraulic motor is the rotary actuator equivalent of a hydraulic pump operating in reverse; it converts hydraulic fluid pressure and flow into rotary mechanical motion (torque and RPM). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Actuators and Motors)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Hydraulic fluid filtering elements constructed of porous paper are normally

choices.B: discarded at regular intervals and replaced with new filtering elements.
choices.C: not approved for use in certificated aircraft.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Porous paper filter elements cannot be effectively cleaned or restored to their original filtering efficiency, so FAA standards require they be discarded and replaced at regular maintenance intervals. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Filters)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A pilot reports that when the hydraulic pump is running, the pressure is normal. However, when the pump is stopped, no hydraulic pressure is available. This is an indication of a

choices.B: low accumulator fluid preload.
choices.C: leaking accumulator air valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: When pump-on pressure is normal but pressure drops to zero when the pump stops, the accumulator is not holding its charge; a leaking air valve allows the nitrogen/air preload to escape, so the accumulator cannot store hydraulic pressure between pump cycles. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Accumulators)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If fluid is added to a reservoir in a constant pressure hydraulic system while the system is pressurized, what will result?

choices.A: Fluid will spray violently out of the reservoir when the filler neck cap is removed.
choices.B: The fluid level will increase when system pressure is reduced.
choices.C: Air will be drawn into the system, when the filler neck cap is removed.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: In a pressurized constant-pressure hydraulic system, the reservoir is under system pressure; removing the filler cap while pressurized will cause fluid to spray violently outward due to the stored pressure behind it. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic Reservoirs)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: In a hydraulic system that has a reservoir pressurized with turbine-engine compressor bleed air, which unit reduces the air pressure between the engine and reservoir?

choices.B: Air bleed relief valve.
choices.C: Air pressure regulator.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: An air pressure regulator is specifically designed to reduce and regulate the high-pressure bleed air from the turbine engine down to the lower pressure required to pressurize the hydraulic reservoir, whereas a relief valve only bypasses excess pressure rather than continuously regulating it. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What is the main purpose of a pressurized reservoir in a hydraulic system?

choices.B: Prevent hydraulic pump cavitation.
choices.C: Prevent hydraulic fluid from foaming.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Pressurizing the reservoir ensures a positive pressure head on the inlet side of the hydraulic pump, which prevents cavitation (the formation of vapor bubbles due to insufficient inlet pressure) especially at high altitudes where ambient pressure is low. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: One of the main advantages of Skydrol is its

choices.B: high operating pressure.
choices.C: inability to mix with water.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Skydrol, a phosphate-ester-based fire-resistant hydraulic fluid, is known for its wide usable operating temperature range, making it suitable for the extreme temperature variations encountered in aviation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Hydraulic fluid reservoirs are sometimes designed with a standpipe in one of the outlet ports in order to assure emergency supply of fluid. The outlet port with the standpipe in it furnishes fluid to the

choices.A: emergency pump when the fluid supply to the normal system has been depleted.
choices.B: emergency pump at any time it is required.
choices.C: normal system power pump.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The standpipe in the reservoir sits elevated above the tank bottom, so fluid below the standpipe level is reserved for emergency use; the outlet at the bottom of the tank (below the standpipe) supplies the normal system power pump, while the standpipe outlet retains a trapped reserve. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: An emergency supply of fluid is often retained in the main hydraulic system reservoir by the use of a standpipe. The supply line is connected to the

choices.A: inlet of the main hydraulic system.
choices.B: inlet of the emergency pump.
choices.C: outlet of the main system pump.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The standpipe reserves a quantity of fluid at the bottom of the reservoir below the normal system pickup; this lower, reserved supply is connected to the inlet of the emergency pump so fluid remains available for emergency operations even if the normal system has been depleted. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: To check the air charge in a hydraulic accumulator,

choices.A: reduce all hydraulic pressure, then observe the reading on the accumulator air gauge.
choices.B: observe the first reading on the hydraulic system gauge while operating a component in the system.
choices.C: read it directly from the auxiliary pressure gauge.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: To accurately read only the air (pneumatic) pre-charge pressure in an accumulator, all hydraulic system pressure must first be relieved so the gauge reflects only the nitrogen or air charge rather than a combined hydraulic and air pressure reading. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: How would the air pressure charge in the accumulator be determined if the engine is inoperative, but the system still has hydraulic pressure?

choices.A: Read it directly from the main system pressure gauge with all actuators inoperative.
choices.B: Build up system pressure with the emergency pump and then read the pressure on a gauge attached to the air side of the accumulator.
choices.C: Operate a hydraulic unit slowly and note the pressure at which a rapid pressure drop begins as it goes toward zero.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: When hydraulic pressure is still present and no external gauge is on the air side, the air pre-charge can be determined by slowly operating a hydraulic component and noting the pressure at which a sudden rapid drop toward zero occurs, indicating the hydraulic fluid side has been exhausted and only the air charge remains. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: How many of these seals are used with petroleum-base hydraulic fluids?

choices.B: Neoprene, Buna-N.
choices.C: Natural rubber, Butyl rubber.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Neoprene and Buna-N (nitrile) are synthetic rubber compounds that are compatible with petroleum-base hydraulic fluids, while natural rubber, butyl, and ethylene-propylene seals are used with phosphate-ester fluids such as Skydrol. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Hydraulic system thermal relief valves are set to open at a

choices.A: lower pressure than the system relief valve.
choices.B: higher pressure than the system relief valve.
choices.C: lower pressure than the system pressure regulator.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Thermal relief valves protect localized portions of a hydraulic system from pressure buildup caused by thermal expansion of trapped fluid; they must be set higher than the normal system relief valve so they only open under abnormal thermal overpressure conditions, not during normal operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Chatter in a hydraulic system is caused by

choices.B: insufficient system pressure.
choices.C: air in the system.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Air entrained in hydraulic fluid is compressible and causes erratic, oscillating pressure fluctuations that produce a characteristic chattering or spongy behavior in system components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If hydraulic fluid is released when the air valve core of the accumulator is depressed, it is evidence of

choices.B: a leaking check valve.
choices.C: a ruptured diaphragm or leaking seals.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: If hydraulic fluid appears at the air valve core when it is depressed, the dividing membrane (diaphragm) or piston seals within the accumulator have failed, allowing hydraulic fluid to migrate into the air chamber. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If the hydraulic system pressure is normal while the engine-driven pump is running, but there is no pressure after the engine has been shut off, it indicates

choices.A: the system relief valve setting is too high.
choices.B: no air pressure in the accumulator.
choices.C: the pressure regulator is set too high.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The accumulator stores pressurized hydraulic fluid to maintain system pressure after the pump shuts down; if no pressure remains when the engine (and thus the pump) stops, the accumulator has no air pre-charge to hold the stored pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The purpose of restrictors in hydraulic systems is to

choices.A: control the rate of movement of hydraulically operated mechanisms.
choices.B: allow the flow of fluid in one direction only.
choices.C: lower the operating pressure of selected components.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Restrictors (orifices or flow control valves) limit the flow rate of hydraulic fluid to a component, thereby controlling the speed at which actuators or other mechanisms extend or retract. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A common cause of slow actuation of hydraulic components is

choices.B: restricted orifices.
choices.C: internal leakage in the actuating unit.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Restricted orifices or clogged filters reduce fluid flow to actuating components, directly causing slow or sluggish operation of those components even when system pressure is normal. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A loud hammering noise in a hydraulic system that has an accumulator usually indicates

choices.B: too much preload in the accumulator.
choices.C: too low or no preload in the accumulator.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: When the accumulator has little or no pneumatic pre-charge, it cannot dampen the pressure surges and fluid hammer generated by the pump and system cycling, resulting in loud hammering or banging noises in the lines and components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: In a typical high-pressure pneumatic system, if the moisture separator does not vent accumulated water when the compressor shuts down, a likely cause is a

choices.B: malfunctioning pressure transmitter.
choices.C: malfunctioning solenoid dump valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The moisture separator's dump valve is a solenoid-operated valve that automatically opens to expel accumulated water when the compressor unloads or shuts down; if this valve malfunctions, water will not be expelled. A saturated chemical dryer affects moisture absorption downstream, not the dump cycle itself. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Pneumatic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What component of a pressurization system prevents the cabin altitude from becoming higher than airplane altitude?

choices.B: Negative pressure relief valve.
choices.C: Positive pressure relief valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The negative pressure relief valve opens when outside ambient pressure exceeds cabin pressure, preventing the cabin altitude from rising above aircraft altitude and protecting the fuselage from reverse pressure differential. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Pressurization Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: After cleaning or replacing the filtering element in a combustion heater fuel system, the system should be pressurized and

choices.B: the fuel filter bypass valve reset to the filter position.
choices.C: a sample of fuel taken downstream from the filter to ensure proper operation of the new filtering element.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: After any maintenance on a fuel system component such as a filter, the system must be pressurized and all connections inspected for fuel leaks before returning the system to service, per standard fuel system maintenance practice. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Combustion Heaters)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What unit in a vapor-cycle cooling system serves as a reservoir for the refrigerant?

choices.B: Evaporator.
choices.C: Condenser.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The receiver-dryer stores liquid refrigerant from the condenser and also contains a desiccant to remove moisture, effectively serving as the system's refrigerant reservoir. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 15 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: In an operating vapor-cycle cooling system, if the two lines connected to the expansion valve are essentially the same temperature, what does this indicate?

choices.B: The expansion valve is not metering freon properly.
choices.C: The compressor is pumping too much refrigerant.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Normally there should be a significant temperature drop across the expansion valve as high-pressure liquid refrigerant expands into low-pressure vapor; if both sides are the same temperature, the valve is not restricting flow and therefore not metering refrigerant properly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 15 — Vapor-Cycle Cooling Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: In a high-pressure oxygen system, if the pressure reducer fails, what prevents high-pressure oxygen from entering the system downstream?

choices.B: Pressure relief valve.
choices.C: Manifold control valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A pressure relief valve installed downstream of the pressure reducer will open and vent excess pressure if the reducer fails in the open position, protecting low-pressure system components from being exposed to high-pressure oxygen. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Oxygen Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: In a gaseous oxygen system, which of the following are vented to blow out plugs in the fuselage skin?

choices.B: Filler shutoff valves.
choices.C: Pressure reducer valves.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Pressure relief valves in gaseous oxygen systems are vented overboard through blow-out plugs in the fuselage skin to safely discharge oxygen in the event of an overpressure condition, preventing it from accumulating inside the aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Oxygen Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which of the following operating mechanisms would be found in a hydraulic pressure gauge?

choices.B: Pressure diaphragm.
choices.C: Evacuated bellows.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Hydraulic pressure gauges use a Bourdon tube mechanism, which straightens proportionally as internal fluid pressure increases, translating that mechanical movement into a pressure reading on the gauge dial. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Hydraulic Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which statement regarding an aircraft instrument vacuum system is true?

choices.A: Dry-type vacuum pumps with carbon vanes are very susceptible to damage from solid airborne particles and must take in only filtered air.
choices.B: Vacuum systems are generally more effective at high altitudes than positive pressure systems.
choices.C: If the air inlet to each vacuum instrument is connected to a common atmospheric pressure manifold, the system generally will be equipped with individual instrument filters only.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: When vacuum instruments share a common atmospheric manifold, individual filters at each instrument are sufficient because the manifold itself provides a common air source, eliminating the need for a single large central filter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Vacuum Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What does a reciprocating engine manifold pressure gauge indicate when the engine is not operating?

choices.B: The differential between the manifold pressure and the atmospheric pressure.
choices.C: The existing atmospheric pressure.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A manifold pressure gauge is an absolute pressure instrument referenced to a perfect vacuum, so when the engine is not running it reads the existing atmospheric (ambient) pressure rather than zero or a differential value. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Engine Instruments)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which condition would be most likely to cause excessive vacuum in a vacuum system?

choices.B: Vacuum relief valve improperly adjusted.
choices.C: Vacuum relief valve spring weak.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The vacuum relief valve regulates system suction by allowing atmospheric air to bleed in; if it is improperly adjusted to open at too high a vacuum level (or not open enough), the system will develop excessive vacuum beyond the instruments' design range. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Vacuum Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Fuel jettisoning is usually accomplished

choices.A: through a common manifold and outlet in each wing.
choices.B: by gravity flow into the outboard wing tanks and overboard through a common outlet in each wing.
choices.C: through individual outlets for each tank.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Fuel jettison systems on large aircraft typically route fuel from multiple tanks through a common manifold to a single outlet point in each wing, allowing rapid and controlled dumping to reduce landing weight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: How many engine driven fuel pumps per engine are required for engines requiring fuel pumps?

choices.A: Two engines can share one fuel pump.
choices.B: One fuel pump for each engine.
choices.C: Two fuel pumps for each engine.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: FAA regulations and design standards require one engine-driven fuel pump per engine to ensure each engine has a dedicated, reliable source of fuel pressure without depending on another engine's pump. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A typical large transport aircraft fuel manifold system allows which of the following?

choices.A: Tanks must be serviced through multiple connections.
choices.B: Engines can be fed only from a specified tank.
choices.C: A damaged tank can be isolated from the rest of the fuel system.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A fuel manifold system on large transport aircraft is designed with isolation valves so that a damaged or compromised tank can be shut off and isolated, preventing fuel loss or contamination from spreading to the rest of the system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Fuel heaters are used with fuel systems for turbine engines to prevent ____________ in the fuel from clogging system filters.

choices.B: ice crystals
choices.C: contamination

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Turbine engine fuels can contain dissolved water that precipitates as ice crystals at altitude and low temperatures; fuel heaters raise fuel temperature above freezing to prevent these ice crystals from accumulating and blocking fuel filters. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What is used in many aircraft to prevent bubbles in the fuel after it leaves the tank when atmospheric pressure is lower than fuel vapor pressure?

choices.B: Anti-foaming additives.
choices.C: Boost pumps.
