Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Boost pumps are submerged in the fuel tank and provide positive fuel pressure at the tank outlet, preventing fuel vapor formation (cavitation/bubbling) when ambient atmospheric pressure drops below the fuel's vapor pressure at altitude. This pressurized flow ensures the fuel remains in a liquid state as it travels to the engine-driven pump. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Before fueling an aircraft by using the pressure fueling method, what important precaution should be observed?

choices.A: The truck pump pressure must be correct for that refueling system.
choices.B: The truck pump pressure must be adjusted for minimum filter pressure.
choices.C: The aircraft's electrical system must be on to indicate quantity gauge readings.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Pressure fueling systems on aircraft are rated for a specific maximum inlet pressure, and exceeding that pressure can damage tanks, bladders, and fueling components; the truck pump pressure must be verified correct for that aircraft's system before fueling begins. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: How is the outlet fuel pressure regulated on a submerged, single-speed, centrifugal-type fuel pump?

choices.A: By the engine-driven pump's design and internal clearance.
choices.B: By the first check valve downstream from the pump.
choices.C: By the pump's design and internal clearances.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A submerged centrifugal-type boost pump is a non-positive-displacement pump whose output pressure is determined by the impeller design and internal clearances, not by an external relief valve, because excess fuel simply recirculates internally within the pump housing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The purpose of a diaphragm in a vane-type fuel pump is to

choices.A: equalize fuel pressure at all speeds.
choices.B: vary fuel pressure according to throttle setting.
choices.C: compensate fuel pressures to altitude changes.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The diaphragm in a vane-type fuel pump is referenced to the intake manifold pressure (or ambient), allowing the pump's output pressure to rise and fall with altitude changes so that a relatively constant pressure differential above manifold pressure is maintained regardless of altitude. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Entrained water in aviation turbine fuel is a hazard because of its susceptibility to freezing as it passes through the filters. What are common methods of preventing this hazard?

choices.A: Micromesh fuel strainers and fuel heater.
choices.B: High-velocity fuel pumps and fuel heater.
choices.C: Anti-icing fuel additives and fuel heater.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Entrained (dissolved or suspended) water in turbine fuel can freeze on fuel filters at altitude, blocking flow; the FAA-accepted methods to prevent this hazard are anti-icing additives (such as FSII/DiEGME) that lower the freezing point of water and fuel heaters that raise fuel temperature above freezing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A drip gauge may be used to measure

choices.A: the amount of fuel in the tank.
choices.B: system leakage with the system shut down.
choices.C: fuel pump diaphragm leakage.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A drip gauge (also called a drip stick or float-type gauge) is a direct-reading mechanical device that is inserted into the fuel tank to measure the actual fuel level and quantity in the tank. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What unit would be adjusted to change the fuel pressure warning limits?

choices.B: Pressure-sensitive mechanism.
choices.C: Fuel pressure relief valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Fuel pressure warning systems use a pressure-sensitive mechanism (such as a pressure switch or transducer) whose actuation point can be adjusted to set the upper and lower warning limits at which the cockpit alert is triggered. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Select one means of controlling the fuel temperature on turbine-powered aircraft.

choices.A: Engine bleed air to the fuel filter.
choices.B: Engine bleed air to the fuel tank.
choices.C: Engine bleed air to a heat exchanger.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: On turbine-powered aircraft, fuel temperature is commonly controlled by routing hot engine bleed air through a heat exchanger (fuel heater) in the fuel line, warming the fuel to prevent ice crystal formation on filters without contaminating the fuel with bleed air. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What is the purpose of flapper-type check valves in integral fuel tanks?

choices.A: To allow defueling of the tanks by suction.
choices.B: To prevent fuel from flowing away from the boost pumps.
choices.C: To allow the engine-driven pumps to draw fuel directly from the tank if the boost pump fails.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Flapper-type check valves are installed between bays of an integral fuel tank to act as one-way baffles that allow fuel to flow toward the boost pump sump area but prevent it from sloshing away from the boost pumps during maneuvers, ensuring a continuous fuel supply. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: In some aircraft with several fuel tanks, the possible danger of allowing the fuel supply in one tank to become exhausted before the selector valve is switched to another tank is prevented by the installation of

choices.A: a fuel pressure warning signal system.
choices.B: a fuel pressure relief valve.
choices.C: an engine fuel pump bypass valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A fuel pressure warning signal system alerts the flight crew when fuel pressure drops to a dangerously low level, giving them time to switch selector valves to another tank before the engine is starved of fuel due to a tank running dry. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Gas-turbine-engine fuel systems are not susceptible to the formation of ice in the fuel filters.
choices.B: A fuel heater operates as a heat disperser to cool the fuel.
choices.C: Gas-turbine-engine fuel systems are very susceptible to the formation of ice on the fuel filters.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Gas turbine engines operate at high altitudes where cold temperatures cause water entrained in jet fuel to precipitate and freeze, making turbine fuel filters highly susceptible to ice blockage; this is why fuel heaters and anti-icing additives are standard equipment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A fuel pressure relief valve is required on an aircraft

choices.B: centrifugal boost fuel pump.
choices.C: positive-displacement fuel pump and centrifugal boost fuel pump.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A positive-displacement pump (such as a gear or vane pump) produces a fixed volume of output per revolution regardless of downstream demand, so a pressure relief valve is mandatory to bypass excess fuel and protect the system from overpressure; a centrifugal pump is non-positive-displacement and self-limiting, so no relief valve is required. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Fuel-boost pumps are operated

choices.A: to provide a positive flow of fuel to the engine.
choices.B: primarily for fuel transfer.
choices.C: automatically from fuel pressure.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Fuel boost pumps are operated primarily to ensure a positive, uninterrupted flow of fuel under pressure to the engine-driven fuel pump, preventing vapor lock and cavitation, especially during high-altitude operations, engine start, and takeoff. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The location of leaks and defects within the internal portions of the fuel system can usually be determined by

choices.A: visual inspection for evidence of wet spots and stains, and feeling for unusually warm components.
choices.B: performing a fuel flow check.
choices.C: observing the pressure gauge and operating the selector valves.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Internal fuel system leaks are typically identified by visual inspection for telltale wet spots, fuel stains, or discoloration on components, combined with feeling for components that are unusually warm due to frictional heat caused by internal leakage or bypass flow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What type of fuel-booster pump requires a pressure relief valve?

choices.B: Sliding vane.
choices.C: Centrifugal.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A sliding vane pump is a positive-displacement pump that will continue to build pressure against a closed or restricted downstream line, making a pressure relief valve mandatory to prevent pump, line, or component damage from overpressure; centrifugal pumps are non-positive-displacement and do not require a relief valve. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: To prevent vapor lock in fuel lines at high altitude, some aircraft are equipped with

choices.B: direct-injection-type carburetors.
choices.C: booster pumps.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Booster pumps maintain positive fuel pressure in the fuel lines, preventing fuel from vaporizing at high altitude where low ambient pressure would otherwise allow vapor lock to occur. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The type of fuel-boost pump that separates air and vapor from the fuel before it enters the line to the carburetor is the

choices.B: centrifugal-type pump.
choices.C: sliding vane-type pump.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The centrifugal-type boost pump uses a vane impeller that naturally separates lighter air and vapor from heavier fuel by centrifugal action, allowing only liquid fuel to pass to the carburetor. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 12 — Fuel Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: A CSD unit drives a generator through the use of

choices.B: an infinitely variable mechanical gearing system.
choices.C: a variable hydraulic pump and hydraulic motor.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: A Constant Speed Drive (CSD) unit uses a variable-displacement hydraulic pump coupled with a hydraulic motor to continuously vary the drive ratio, maintaining constant output shaft speed regardless of engine speed changes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: If the Integrated Drive Generator (IDG) scavenge oil filter is contaminated with metal, the aviation mechanic should

choices.A: do an oil analysis to diagnose the problem.
choices.B: remove and replace the IDG.
choices.C: replace the oil and filter at 25 hour intervals.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Metal contamination in the IDG scavenge oil filter indicates internal mechanical failure within the unit; because the IDG is not field-repairable, the entire unit must be removed and replaced. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: The pneumatic (reed) type stall warning system installed in some light aircraft is activated by

choices.B: positive air pressure.
choices.C: negative air pressure.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The pneumatic reed-type stall warning system is activated by negative (suction) air pressure created at a slot near the leading edge of the wing as the angle of attack increases toward stall, drawing air through the reed to produce the warning sound. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 15 — Ice and Rain Control Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: In most modern hydraulically actuated landing gear systems, the order of gear and fairing door operation is controlled by

choices.B: shuttle valves.
choices.C: microswitches.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: Sequence valves are hydraulic control valves that ensure landing gear doors open before the gear extends or retracts, and close after gear movement is complete, controlling the precise order of operations based on hydraulic pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Landing Gear Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: What controls the inflation sequence in a pneumatic deicer boot system?

choices.B: Vacuum pump.
choices.C: Distributor valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The distributor valve directs pressurized air to the individual deicer boot sections in the correct timed sequence, ensuring leading edge sections inflate and deflate in the proper order to break off ice accumulation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 15 — Ice and Rain Control Systems)

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.question: Which of the following regulates the vacuum of the air pump to hold the deicing boots deflated when the pneumatic deicing system is off?

choices.B: Pressure regulator.
choices.C: Suction relief valve.

Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems.explanation: The suction relief valve regulates the vacuum level produced by the air pump, holding the deicing boots firmly deflated against the wing surface when the deicing system is not in operation to prevent aerodynamic disruption. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 15 — Ice and Rain Control Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: When charging a vapor-cycle cooling system after evacuation, the low-pressure gauge fails to come out of a vacuum. What is indicated?

choices.B: The expansion valve failed to close.
choices.C: The compressor is not engaging.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: If the low-pressure gauge fails to rise out of a vacuum when refrigerant is introduced after evacuation, it indicates a blockage in the system that is preventing refrigerant from flowing through to equalize pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Cabin Atmosphere Control Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: What component in a vapor-cycle cooling system would most likely be at fault if a system would not take a freon charge?

choices.B: Condenser.
choices.C: Receiver-dryer.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: The receiver-dryer contains desiccant that can become saturated with moisture; excessive moisture absorption causes the desiccant to swell and block the unit, preventing refrigerant from entering the system during charging. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Cabin Atmosphere Control Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: The air-cycle cooling system produces cold air by

choices.A: extracting heat energy across a compressor.
choices.B: passing air through cooling coils that contain a refrigerant.
choices.C: extracting heat energy across an expansion turbine.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: In an air-cycle cooling system, bleed air is expanded across a turbine, and the energy extracted during expansion causes a significant temperature drop in the air, producing the cooling effect. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Cabin Atmosphere Control Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: A synchro transmitter is connected to a synchro receiver

choices.B: electromagnetically without wires.
choices.C: electrically with wires.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Synchro transmitters and receivers are connected by electrical wires carrying AC signals; the transmitter rotor induces voltages in its stators that are transmitted electrically to the receiver stators, causing the receiver rotor to align with the transmitter rotor position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 10 — Aircraft Instrument Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: Fuel flow transmitters are designed to transmit data

choices.B: electrically.
choices.C: utilizing fluid power.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Fuel flow transmitters convert mechanical flow rate measurement into electrical signals that are sent to cockpit indicators, as electrical transmission is reliable, lightweight, and compatible with modern aircraft instrument systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 10 — Aircraft Instrument Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: Installed radio equipment is protected from damage due to jolts and vibration by

choices.B: spring and/or viscous damper mounted racks.
choices.C: rubber or foam cushioning material between circuit chassis and case.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Spring and/or viscous damper mounted racks absorb and isolate vibration and shock loads from the airframe before they reach the radio equipment, providing superior protection compared to simple cushioning materials. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communication and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: Radio equipment is bonded to the aircraft in order to

choices.B: minimize radio interference from static electrical charges.
choices.C: provide a low-impedance ground and minimize radio interference from static electrical charges.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Bonding radio equipment to the aircraft structure simultaneously provides a low-impedance ground path for proper electrical circuit completion and minimizes static charge buildup that would cause radio frequency interference, so both purposes are served. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communication and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: When must the radio station license be displayed in an aircraft equipped with a two-way radio?

choices.A: When the aircraft is operated outside the United States.
choices.B: When the aircraft is returned to service.
choices.C: When the aircraft is certified for IFR flight.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: FCC regulations require that a radio station license be aboard the aircraft and available for inspection only when operating outside the United States; domestic operations do not require the license to be displayed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: When would a U.S. resident NOT be required to hold a Federal Communications Commission (FCC) Restricted Radio Telephone Operator Permit to operate two-way aircraft VHF radio equipment?

choices.A: When flying to or communicating with destinations outside the United States.
choices.B: When flying or communicating within the United States.
choices.C: When the radio equipment is operated in aircraft certified for VFR flight only.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: FCC rules require a Restricted Radiotelephone Operator Permit only when operating to, from, or in foreign countries; purely domestic flight operations within the United States do not require the permit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: Part of the ADF system used on aircraft includes

choices.B: marker beacon antenna.
choices.C: sense and loop antennas.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: An ADF system uses two antennas working together: a loop antenna that provides directional bearing information and a sense antenna that resolves the 180° ambiguity inherent in the loop alone. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Automatic Direction Finder)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: How may the operation of an installed emergency locator transmitter (ELT) be verified during aircraft inspection?

choices.A: By moving the deactivating switch from the DISARM position to the ARM position while monitoring the civil emergency frequency with a communications receiver at five minutes after the hour.
choices.B: By activating the five-G switch and turning the unit on at five minutes after the hour.
choices.C: By tuning a communications receiver to the civil emergency frequency and activating the ELT momentarily within five minutes after the hour.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: An installed ELT can be tested by briefly activating it and monitoring the civil emergency frequency (121.5 MHz) within the first five minutes after any hour, which is the FAA-approved method that minimizes false-alarm confusion with actual emergencies. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Emergency Locator Transmitter)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: Static dischargers help eliminate radio interference by dissipating static electricity into the atmosphere at

choices.B: high voltage level.
choices.C: high current levels.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Static dischargers (wicks) work by providing a path of controlled, low-current discharge that bleeds off accumulated precipitation static at levels too low to cause significant broadband radio interference, unlike an uncontrolled high-voltage arc. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Static Dischargers)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: VHF radio signals are commonly used in

choices.B: VOR navigation.
choices.C: both VOR navigation and ATC communications.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: VHF frequencies (30–300 MHz) are used for both VOR navigation (108.0–117.95 MHz) and ATC voice communications (118.0–136.975 MHz), making VHF the backbone of civil aviation radio operations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — VHF Communications and Navigation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: On modern large aircraft, what electronic device typically monitors flight parameters and performs autopilot functions?

choices.B: Transponder.
choices.C: Control/display unit.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: On modern transport-category aircraft, the flight management computer (FMC) integrates flight parameter monitoring, navigation, performance management, and autopilot command functions into a single system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Flight Management Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: When an antenna is installed, it should be fastened

choices.A: to the primary structure at the approximate intersection of the three aircraft axes.
choices.B: with a reinforcing doubler on each side of the aircraft skin.
choices.C: so that loads imposed are transmitted to the aircraft structure.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Antenna installations must be accomplished so that any aerodynamic, vibration, or inertial loads imposed on the antenna are properly transmitted into and carried by the aircraft structure, ensuring airworthiness. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Antenna Installation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: After an automatic direction finding antenna has been installed, the

choices.B: loop must be calibrated.
choices.C: transceiver must be compensated.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: After an ADF loop antenna is installed, it must be calibrated (a compensation card is created) to correct for quadrantal error caused by the airframe's effect on the antenna's directional sensing accuracy. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Automatic Direction Finder)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: Doublers are used when antennas are installed to

choices.B: prevent oil canning of the skin.
choices.C: reinstate the structural strength of the aircraft skin.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Antenna mounting holes cut in the aircraft skin reduce structural strength, so reinforcing doublers are installed around the cutout to restore the original load-carrying capability of the skin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Antenna Installation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: One antenna can be used for the radio range and standard broadcast bands in light aircraft because the

choices.B: antenna is omnidirectional.
choices.C: antenna length may be electronically adjusted.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: An antenna coupler or antenna tuner can electronically adjust the effective electrical length of a single antenna, allowing it to be resonant at both the radio range (low/medium frequency) and standard broadcast band frequencies. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Antenna Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: A gasket or sealant is used between the antenna mast and fuselage skin

choices.A: to prevent the entry of moisture.
choices.B: for aircraft pressurization only.
choices.C: to prevent abrasion between the antenna mast and fuselage skin.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: A gasket or sealant between the antenna mast base and the fuselage skin provides a weatherproof seal that prevents moisture intrusion, which could cause corrosion of the airframe structure and antenna hardware. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Antenna Installation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: The preferred location of a VOR antenna on light aircraft is on

choices.A: the bottom of the fuselage and as far forward as possible.
choices.B: top of the cabin with the apex of the V pointing forward.
choices.C: top of the vertical stabilizer.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: A V-dipole VOR antenna mounted on top of the cabin with the apex pointing forward provides the best line-of-sight reception to VOR ground stations while minimizing airframe shielding effects. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — VOR Antenna Installation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: The purpose of a localizer is to

choices.A: set the airplane on the proper approach angle to the runway.
choices.B: indicate the distance the airplane is from the end of the runway.
choices.C: align the airplane with the center of the runway.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: The ILS localizer transmitter provides lateral guidance by producing a course that aligns the aircraft with the centerline of the instrument runway, while the glideslope provides vertical guidance separately. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Instrument Landing System)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: A DME antenna should be installed on the aircraft in a position that will

choices.A: not be blanked out by the wing when the aircraft is banked.
choices.B: not be blanked out by the vertical stabilizer.
choices.C: facilitate cleaning, inspection, and maintenance.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: A DME antenna is installed on the underside of the fuselage in a location where the wing will not block the antenna's line-of-sight to ground transponder beacons during normal banked flight maneuvers. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 14 — Distance Measuring Equipment)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: When installing a DME antenna, it should be aligned with the

choices.B: chord line.
choices.C: centerline on the airplane.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: The DME antenna must be installed along the aircraft centerline to ensure omnidirectional signal reception and proper interrogation of ground-based DME stations regardless of aircraft heading. Alignment with the centerline prevents signal shadowing or asymmetric radiation patterns. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: The addition of avionics and associated antenna systems forward of the CG limit will affect

choices.B: CG limits and useful load.
choices.C: useful load and maximum gross weight.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Adding avionics equipment increases the aircraft's empty weight, which directly reduces the available useful load by an equal amount; CG limits are type-certificated values that cannot be changed by adding equipment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: The purpose of a glideslope system is to

choices.A: provide for automatic altitude reporting to air traffic control.
choices.B: indicate the distance the airplane is from the end of the runway.
choices.C: assist the pilot in making a correct angle of descent to the runway.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: The glideslope component of the ILS provides vertical guidance to the pilot, indicating whether the aircraft is above, below, or on the correct 3-degree descent path to the runway threshold. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: A transducer in a fuel pressure system serves what function?

choices.A: Transmits an electrical signal to the transmitter for fluid pressure.
choices.B: Transmits electrical signal proportional to the fluid pressure.
choices.C: Converts fluid pressure directly through a transmitter to the indicator.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: A transducer converts one form of energy to another; in a fuel pressure system it generates an electrical signal whose magnitude is proportional to the sensed fluid pressure, which is then sent to an indicator. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: A transmitter in a fuel pressure warning system serves what function?

choices.A: Transmits an electrical signal to fluid pressure.
choices.B: Converts fluid pressure to an electrical signal.
choices.C: Transmits fluid pressure directly to the indicator.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: A transmitter in a fuel pressure warning system converts the mechanical fluid pressure into a corresponding electrical signal that can be transmitted to a cockpit warning light or indicator. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: What is the color and orientation of the position lights for navigation on civil airplanes?

choices.A: Left side, green; right side, red; rear aft, white.
choices.B: Left side, red; right side, green; rear aft, white.
choices.C: Left side, white; right side, green; rear aft, red.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Per FAA and 14 CFR Part 23/25 standards, navigation position lights are red on the left (port) wingtip, green on the right (starboard) wingtip, and white on the tail, following international convention for all civil aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: What is the most accurate type of frequency measuring instrument?

choices.A: Integrated circuit chip having a clock circuit.
choices.B: Electrodynamometers using electromagnetic fields.
choices.C: Electromagnets using one permanent magnet.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: An integrated circuit chip with a crystal-controlled clock circuit provides the highest accuracy for frequency measurement because the crystal oscillator maintains an extremely precise and stable reference frequency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: Grounding is electrically connecting a conductive object to the primary structure. One purpose of grounding is to

choices.B: allow static charge accumulation.
choices.C: prevent development of radio frequency potentials.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Proper bonding and grounding of conductive structures ensures all components are at the same electrical potential, which prevents the buildup of radio frequency voltages that could cause interference with avionics and communications systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: If one switch is used to control all navigation lights, the lights are most likely connected

choices.A: in series with each other and parallel to the switch.
choices.B: in series with each other and in series with the switch.
choices.C: parallel to each other and in series with the switch.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: When multiple lights are controlled by a single switch, each light must receive full circuit voltage to operate at rated brightness, requiring a parallel connection; the switch is placed in series with the parallel bank to control all lights simultaneously. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: The navigation lights of some aircraft consist of a single circuit controlled by a single switch which has an ON position and an OFF position, with no additional positions possible. This switch is referred to as a

choices.B: single-pole, double-throw (SPDT), two-position switch.
choices.C: single-pole, single-throw (SPST), two-position switch.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: A single-pole, single-throw (SPST) switch has one input, one output, and two positions (ON and OFF), making it the correct description for a simple on/off navigation light switch with no additional switching positions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: The purpose of a rectifier in an electrical system is to change

choices.B: direct current to alternating current.
choices.C: alternating current to direct current.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: A rectifier uses diodes or similar components to allow current flow in only one direction, thereby converting the bidirectional flow of alternating current into the unidirectional flow of direct current. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: The basic difference between an autosyn and a magnesyn indicating system is the

choices.B: transmitter.
choices.C: receiver.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: The autosyn system uses an AC-energized rotor (electromagnet), while the magnesyn system uses a permanent magnet rotor; this difference in rotor construction is the fundamental distinction between the two synchro-type remote indicating systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: Which of the following are fire precautions which must be observed when working on an oxygen system?

choices.A: Display 'No Smoking' placards and keep all tools and oxygen servicing equipment free from oil or grease.
choices.B: Display 'No Smoking' placards and provide adequate fire-fighting equipment.
choices.C: Display 'No Smoking' placards, provide adequate fire-fighting equipment, keep all tools and oxygen servicing equipment free from oil or grease, and avoid checking aircraft radio or electrical systems.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Oxygen supports combustion vigorously, so all precautions must be combined: no-smoking placards, fire-fighting equipment on hand, oil/grease-free tools, and avoidance of electrical sparks from radio or electrical system checks near oxygen servicing operations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: When testing a 406 MHz emergency locator transmitter (ELT),

choices.A: testing inside of a metal hangar will prevent detection of an emergency ELT signal.
choices.B: the unit sends a test signal that will not generate an emergency signal.
choices.C: care must be taken as the unit could send an emergency signal, activating search and rescue (SAR) agencies.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Unlike older 121.5 MHz ELTs, a 406 MHz ELT transmits a digitally coded distress signal that is immediately detected and processed by the COSPAS-SARSAT satellite system, meaning an inadvertent transmission can trigger an actual search and rescue response. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: When testing an emergency locator transmitter (ELT), a 50-ohm dummy load

choices.A: is connected in parallel with the antenna to reduce transmissions.
choices.B: is connected in place of the antenna to prevent the signal from being radiated into space.
choices.C: amplifies the ELT output for better analysis of the signal.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: A 50-ohm dummy load presents the correct impedance match to the ELT transmitter output, allowing the unit to operate normally during testing while absorbing and dissipating the RF energy so no signal is radiated from an antenna. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: Which of the following is a two-way text communication link between an airliner in flight and the airline's main ground facilities?

choices.B: Aircraft Communication Addressing & Reporting System (ACARS).
choices.C: Cospas-Sarsat.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: ACARS is a digital datalink system that provides two-way text messaging between aircraft and airline ground stations, used for operational communications such as weather, clearances, and maintenance data. WAAS and Cospas-Sarsat serve entirely different navigation and distress functions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Communications and Navigation Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 13.) Determine what unit is located immediately downstream of the expansion valve in a freon refrigeration system.

choices.B: Compressor.
choices.C: Evaporator coils.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: In a vapor-cycle refrigeration system, refrigerant exits the expansion valve as a low-pressure, low-temperature liquid/vapor mixture and immediately enters the evaporator coils, where it absorbs heat and vaporizes. The condenser and compressor are located upstream in the cycle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Air Conditioning Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 15.) What is the approximate drag load on an antenna with a frontal area of .125 square feet installed on an aircraft with a speed of 225 mph?

choices.B: 2.073 pounds.
choices.C: 2.080 pounds.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Using the antenna drag load chart (Figure 15), at 225 mph with a frontal area of 0.125 square feet, the interpolated drag value is approximately 2.069 pounds, which matches the calculated result from the drag formula provided in the figure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Antenna Installation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 15.) What is the approximate drag load on an antenna with a frontal area of .137 square feet installed on an aircraft with a speed of 275 mph?

choices.B: 3.741 pounds.
choices.C: 3.592 pounds.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Using Figure 15's drag load chart at 275 mph with a frontal area of 0.137 square feet, the correct interpolated drag load calculates to approximately 3.387 pounds based on the dynamic pressure and area values shown. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Antenna Installation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 16.) Which of the antennas shown is a typical DME antenna?
Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: DME antennas are characteristically blade-type antennas designed to operate in the UHF frequency range (962–1213 MHz), and Figure 16, Antenna 1 depicts this blade configuration typical of DME installations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Distance Measuring Equipment)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 16.) Which of the antennas shown is a typical glideslope antenna?
Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Glideslope antennas are typically small, fin-shaped or blade antennas tuned to the 329.15–335.00 MHz frequency range, and Antenna 2 in Figure 16 represents the standard glideslope antenna profile used on transport-category aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Instrument Landing System)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 31.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A marker beacon receiver antenna.
choices.C: A satellite weather antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 31 depicts a flat, rectangular loop-style antenna flush-mounted to the aircraft fuselage, which is the characteristic form of a marker beacon receiver antenna designed to receive 75 MHz vertically polarized signals from ground stations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Marker Beacon System)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 22.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.A: This is a glide slope antenna.
choices.B: This is a DME or a radar beacon transponder.
choices.C: This is a loop and sense antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 22 shows a blade-style antenna operating in the UHF range, which is characteristic of both DME and ATC radar beacon transponder antennas, as both systems share similar blade antenna designs in the 960–1215 MHz frequency band. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Transponder and DME Antennas)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 32.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A marker beacon receiver antenna.
choices.C: A radar beacon transponder antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 32 depicts a flat, low-profile rectangular antenna mounted flush to the aircraft skin, which is the standard configuration for a marker beacon receiver antenna tuned to receive 75 MHz signals from ILS marker transmitters. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Marker Beacon System)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 33.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A marker beacon receiver antenna.
choices.C: A radar beacon transponder antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 33 shows a combined VOR/glideslope antenna, which integrates reception of VOR signals (108–118 MHz) and glideslope signals (329–335 MHz) in a single antenna unit commonly installed on the vertical stabilizer or fuselage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — VOR and Glideslope Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 34.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A marker beacon receiver antenna.
choices.C: A radar beacon transponder antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 34 depicts a VOR/glideslope combination antenna, identifiable by its dual-band design that handles both the VHF VOR frequencies and the UHF glideslope frequencies, commonly mounted on the aircraft nose or tail. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — VOR and Glideslope Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 23.) What is this antenna used for?

choices.A: This is an altitude encoder used with transponders.
choices.B: This is a TCAS receiver used with monitoring and alerting systems.
choices.C: This is an aircraft radar beacon transponder used for location identification.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 23 depicts an altitude encoder (also called an encoding altimeter or blind encoder), which converts pressure altitude into a Gray code signal transmitted to the ATC transponder to report altitude; it is not itself a transponder or TCAS component. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Transponder Systems)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 24.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A Marconi antenna.
choices.C: A loop antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 24 shows a dipole antenna, which consists of two equal-length conductive elements extending in opposite directions from a center feedpoint, representing the fundamental half-wave dipole antenna design. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Antenna Theory and Types)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 25.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A canted antenna.
choices.C: A Marconi antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 25 depicts a dipole antenna oriented at an angle (canted), but its fundamental structure of two equal radiating elements identifies it as a dipole; however, per FAA references and the correct answer, it is classified as a dipole antenna type. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Antenna Theory and Types)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 26.) Where is the strongest signal for this antenna?

choices.B: Above the antenna.
choices.C: Ahead of the antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 26 illustrates a vertically polarized antenna with a radiation pattern that is strongest below the antenna, which is the designed characteristic for antennas such as those used in ADF or communications systems mounted on the underside of the fuselage to maximize ground-directed signal reception. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 13 — Antenna Radiation Patterns)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 27.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A VOR and localizer antenna.
choices.C: A radar beacon transponder antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: The V-shaped or dipole-style antenna depicted in Figure 27 is characteristic of a VOR and localizer antenna, designed to receive VHF signals in the 108–118 MHz range used by both systems. Its shape and mounting location on the aircraft are distinguishing features covered in antenna identification sections of the AMT handbook. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 28.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A DME antenna.
choices.C: A VHF communications antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: The antenna shown in Figure 28 is a wire or whip-type antenna characteristic of an ELT, which transmits on 121.5 MHz and 406 MHz and is typically mounted on the aft fuselage. ELT antennas are specifically identified by their simple monopole design and dedicated emergency frequency operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 29.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A VOR and localizer antenna.
choices.C: A radar beacon transponder antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: Figure 29 depicts a VOR and localizer antenna, identifiable by its blade or dual-element configuration suited for receiving VHF nav signals in the 108–118 MHz band used by both VOR and ILS localizer systems. Antenna identification by shape and function is a key competency for AMTs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation)

Communications and Navigation Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 30.) What kind of antenna is this?

choices.B: A VOR and localizer antenna.
choices.C: A satellite weather antenna.

Communications and Navigation Systems.explanation: The antenna shown in Figure 30 is a lightning detector (Stormscope) antenna, which has a distinctive loop or sense antenna design used to detect electrical discharges associated with thunderstorms. Its unique form differentiates it from navigation or communication antennas. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: For use in pressurized aircraft, which is generally the least complicated and requires the least maintenance?

choices.B: High-pressure oxygen systems.
choices.C: Low-pressure oxygen systems.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Chemical oxygen generator systems are the least complicated and require the least maintenance because they have no high-pressure storage tanks, regulators, or plumbing—they generate oxygen through a chemical reaction initiated by a firing pin, making them virtually maintenance-free until expended. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 16 — Cabin Atmosphere Control Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: The function of a symbol generator (SG) in an EFIS is to

choices.A: display alphanumeric data and representations of aircraft instruments.
choices.B: allow the pilot to select the appropriate system configuration for the current flight situation.
choices.C: receive and process input signals from aircraft and engine sensors and send the data to the appropriate display.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: The symbol generator (SG) in an EFIS receives raw input data from aircraft and engine sensors, processes that data, and distributes it to the appropriate display units (PFD, MFD, etc.) for presentation to the flight crew. It acts as the central data-processing and routing component of the EFIS architecture. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: What must be done to an instrument panel that is supported by shock mounts?

choices.A: Bonding straps must be installed across the instrument mounts as a current path.
choices.B: The instrument mounts must be grounded to the aircraft structure as a current path.
choices.C: The instrument mounts must be tightened to the specified torque required by the maintenance manual.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: When an instrument panel is mounted on shock mounts (which are electrically isolating), bonding straps must be installed across the mounts to provide a continuous electrical ground path, preventing static buildup and ensuring proper grounding of instruments. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: When must the emergency locator transmitter (ELT) battery be replaced (other than reading the replacement date)?

choices.A: When the transmitter has been in use for more than one cumulative hour.
choices.B: Must be replaced annually or if the five-G switch has been activated.
choices.C: When the transmitter has been tested more than ten times.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Per FAA regulations (14 CFR 91.207) and manufacturer guidance, an ELT battery must be replaced when the transmitter has been in use for more than one cumulative hour, in addition to the marked replacement date, because battery capacity may be significantly depleted after that duration of operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: An emergency locator transmitter (ELT) battery must be capable of furnishing power for signal transmission for at least

choices.B: 48 hours.
choices.C: 72 hours.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Per 14 CFR 91.207, an ELT battery must be capable of furnishing power for signal transmission for at least 48 hours at the operating temperature extremes, ensuring the unit can sustain a search-and-rescue signal long enough for detection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: How may the battery replacement date be verified for an emergency locator transmitter (ELT)?

choices.A: By removing the batteries and testing them under a measured load to determine if 50 percent of the useful life remains.
choices.B: By observing the battery replacement date marked on the outside of the transmitter.
choices.C: By activating the transmitter and measuring the signal strength.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: The battery replacement date for an ELT is verified by observing the replacement date marked on the outside of the transmitter, as required by 14 CFR 91.207(c), making it immediately accessible without removing or testing the battery. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 11 — Communications and Navigation)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: If a generator is equipped with a vibrator-type voltage regulator, the actual time the voltage regulator points remain open

choices.A: depends on the load carried by the generator.
choices.B: is controlled by the reverse-current cutout relay point clearance.
choices.C: is increased when the external load is greater than the generator output.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: In a vibrator-type voltage regulator, the points cycle open and closed to insert resistance into the field circuit; the actual time the points remain open varies with generator load because higher loads cause greater voltage drop, requiring longer open periods to maintain regulated voltage output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: What is a cause of generator brush arcing?

choices.B: Carbon dust particles.
choices.C: Low spring tension.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Low brush spring tension causes inadequate contact pressure between the brush and commutator, resulting in the brush bouncing or losing consistent contact, which produces arcing and accelerated wear on both the brush and commutator surface. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: What is required when operating two aircraft AC generators in parallel?

choices.A: Amperes and frequency must both be equal.
choices.B: Frequency and voltage must both be equal.
choices.C: Amperes and voltage must both be equal.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: When paralleling two AC generators, both frequency and voltage must be equal (and in phase) to prevent circulating currents between the units, which could damage the generators or cause uneven load sharing; amperage is a result of load, not a paralleling requirement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: The only practical method of maintaining a constant voltage output from an aircraft generator under varying conditions of speed and load is to vary the

choices.B: number of conductors in the armature.
choices.C: speed at which the armature rotates.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Varying the strength of the magnetic field (by adjusting field current through a voltage regulator) is the only practical method of controlling generator output voltage under varying speed and load conditions, since changing armature conductors or speed is not feasible in service. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: The pole pieces or shoes used in a DC generator are a part of the

choices.B: field assembly.
choices.C: brush assembly.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Pole pieces (shoes) are laminated iron cores around which the field windings are wound, making them part of the field assembly; they concentrate and direct the magnetic flux needed to induce current in the rotating armature. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: How many cycles of AC voltage are produced in a six-pole alternator of the revolving-field type for each revolution of the rotor?

choices.B: Three.
choices.C: Six.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: In a revolving-field alternator, the number of AC cycles per revolution equals the number of pairs of poles; a six-pole alternator has three pairs of poles, producing three complete cycles per revolution. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: If the reverse current cutout relay contact points fail to open after the generator output has dropped below battery potential, current will flow through the generator armature

choices.A: in the normal direction and through the shunt field opposite the normal direction.
choices.B: and the shunt field opposite the normal direction.
choices.C: opposite the normal direction and through the shunt field in the normal direction.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: If the reverse current cutout relay fails to open, the battery drives current backward through the generator armature (opposite normal direction) while the shunt field, connected directly across the bus, continues to receive current in its normal direction, which can cause the generator to motorize. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Aluminum wire must be stripped very carefully because

choices.A: high resistance will develop in stripping nicks.
choices.B: stripping nicks can cause short circuits.
choices.C: individual strands will break easily after being nicked.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Aluminum wire strands are significantly less ductile than copper and will fracture at nicks created during stripping, making careful stripping essential to prevent strand breakage and loss of current-carrying capacity. (Ref: FAI-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: The commutator of a generator

choices.A: changes direct current produced in the armature into alternating current as it is taken from the armature.
choices.B: changes alternating current produced in the armature into direct current as it is taken from the armature.
choices.C: reverses the current in the field coils at the proper time in order to produce direct current.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: The commutator acts as a mechanical rectifier; its segments and brushes are arranged so that the alternating current naturally generated in the rotating armature windings is converted to unidirectional (direct) current as it is delivered to the external circuit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: An ammeter in a battery charging system is for the purpose of indicating the

choices.B: total amperes being used in the airplane.
choices.C: rate of current used to charge the battery.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: In a battery charging circuit, the ammeter is placed in series to measure the rate of current actually flowing into the battery during charging, allowing the technician to monitor charging progress and detect abnormal conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Which of the following is not one of the purposes of interpoles in a generator?

choices.B: Overcome armature reaction.
choices.C: Reduce arcing at the brushes.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Interpoles (commutating poles) are small auxiliary poles wired in series with the armature to counteract armature reaction and reduce brush arcing; they play no role in reducing main field strength, which is the function of a voltage regulator. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: To test generator or motor armature windings for opens,

choices.A: place armature in a growler and connect a 110V test light on adjacent segments; light should light.
choices.B: check adjacent segments on commutator with an ohmmeter on the high resistance scale.
choices.C: use a 12/24V test light between the armature core segments and the shaft.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Checking adjacent commutator segments with an ohmmeter on a high-resistance scale detects open armature windings because an open circuit between two segments will show infinite or very high resistance rather than the near-zero resistance of a good winding. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: To what depth is the mica insulation between the commutator bars of a DC generator undercut?

choices.A: One-half the width of the mica.
choices.B: Equal to twice the width of the mica.
choices.C: Equal to the width of the mica.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: FAA standards specify that mica between commutator bars must be undercut to a depth equal to the width of the mica itself, preventing the mica from riding up and causing brush bounce or arcing as the commutator wears. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: A voltage regulator controls generator output by

choices.A: introducing a resistance in generator-to-battery lead in the event of overload.
choices.B: shorting out field coil in the event of overload.
choices.C: varying current flow to generator field coil.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: A voltage regulator maintains a constant generator output voltage by varying the amount of current supplied to the generator's field coil, which in turn controls the strength of the magnetic field and thus the output voltage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Which type of DC generator is not used as an airplane generator?

choices.B: Series wound.
choices.C: Compound wound.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: A series-wound generator has very poor voltage regulation because its output voltage varies drastically with load current, making it entirely unsuitable for aircraft electrical systems that require stable voltage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: During ground operation, aircraft generator cooling is usually accomplished by

choices.A: auxiliary air cooled through an air-fuel heat exchanger.
choices.B: an integral fan.
choices.C: an external motor-driven fan.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Most aircraft DC generators incorporate an integral cooling fan mounted on the rotor shaft that draws air through the generator housing during operation, providing self-contained cooling without external equipment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: When a diode is checked for an open circuit or a short circuit, it should be

choices.B: checked with a milliamp ammeter.
choices.C: disconnected from the circuit.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: A diode must be disconnected from the circuit before testing because other circuit paths can provide alternate current routes that will give false readings, masking an actual open or short condition within the diode. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Which of the following is most likely to cause thermal runaway in a nickel-cadmium battery?

choices.B: Excessive current draw from the battery.
choices.C: Constant current charging of the battery to more than 100 percent of its capacity.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Overcharging a nickel-cadmium battery beyond 100 percent of its capacity causes excessive heat buildup; because internal resistance drops as temperature rises, the battery accepts even more current, creating a self-reinforcing thermal runaway cycle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Which of the following factors must be taken into consideration when determining the wire size to use for an aircraft installation?

choices.A: Allowable power loss, permissible voltage drop, current carrying capability of the conductor, type of load.
choices.B: Mechanical strength, allowable power loss, resistance of current return path through the aircraft structure, type of load.
choices.C: Allowable power loss, resistance of current return path through the aircraft structure, type of load.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: FAA guidance requires that wire sizing account for allowable power loss, permissible voltage drop, current-carrying capacity (to prevent overheating), and type of load (continuous vs. intermittent), as all four factors together determine safe and adequate wire selection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: When selecting hardware for attaching bonding connections to an aircraft structure, which of the following should be considered?

choices.A: Mechanical strength, ease of installation, amount of current to be carried.
choices.B: Permissible voltage drop, amount of current to be carried, type of load.
choices.C: Mechanical strength, allowable power loss, ease of installation.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: When selecting bonding hardware, mechanical strength ensures the connection remains secure under vibration, ease of installation ensures correct assembly, and the amount of current to be carried determines the required conductor and connector size to prevent excessive resistance or heating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: The voltage output of an alternator may be regulated by controlling the

choices.B: voltage output of the dc exciter.
choices.C: resistance in the rotor windings.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: In an alternator, output voltage is controlled by varying the DC exciter current supplied to the rotor field windings, which changes the magnetic field strength and thus the induced voltage in the stator. Alternator speed is held relatively constant by the engine, so voltage regulation is accomplished through field current control, not speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Alternating Current Generators)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Circuits that must be operated only in an emergency or whose inadvertent activation could endanger a system frequently employ

choices.B: push-pull-type circuit breakers only (no switches).
choices.C: spring-loaded to off toggle or rocker switches.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Guarded switches incorporate a physical barrier or cover that must be lifted or moved before the switch can be actuated, preventing inadvertent operation of critical or emergency circuits. This design safeguard is required for circuits where accidental activation could endanger the aircraft or its systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Switches)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: During inspection of the terminal strips of an aircraft electrical system, it should be determined that

choices.A: only locknuts have been used for terminal attachment to the studs.
choices.B: the terminal studs are anchored against rotation.
choices.C: only plain nuts and lockwashers have been used for terminal attachment to the studs.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Terminal studs on terminal strips must be anchored against rotation to prevent loosening of connections during vibration and to maintain secure, low-resistance electrical connections. A stud that can rotate will cause the terminals to loosen over time, creating a potential open-circuit or high-resistance fault. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Wiring Installation)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: If the (+) terminal of a voltmeter is connected to the (-) terminal of the source voltage and the (-) terminal of the meter is connected to the (+) terminal of the source voltage, the voltmeter will read

choices.B: low voltage.
choices.C: backwards.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: A voltmeter is a polarized instrument, and connecting its positive terminal to the negative source and its negative terminal to the positive source causes the meter movement to deflect in the reverse direction, resulting in a backward (off-scale negative) reading. This reversed polarity does not give a correct low reading but instead drives the needle the wrong way. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical Measuring Instruments)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Aircraft electrical junction boxes located in a fire zone are usually constructed

choices.B: carbon steel.
choices.C: stainless steel.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Junction boxes located in fire zones must be constructed of stainless steel to withstand the high temperatures and fire conditions that may be present, whereas aluminum would melt and carbon steel lacks sufficient corrosion and heat resistance for this application. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Wiring Installation)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: To help minimize radio interference a capacitor will largely eliminate and provide a steady direct current if the capacitor is connected to the generator in

choices.B: series.
choices.C: series/parallel.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: A capacitor connected in parallel with a generator acts as a filter by absorbing voltage spikes and smoothing out ripple, thereby minimizing radio frequency interference while allowing steady DC to pass to the load. A series connection would block DC flow entirely. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical System Components)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: In installations where the ammeter is in the generator or alternator lead, and the regulator system does not limit the maximum current that the generator or alternator can deliver, the ammeter can be redlined at what percent of the generator or alternator rating?
Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: FAA standards specify that when the ammeter is in the generator/alternator lead and the regulator does not limit maximum output current, the ammeter redline should be set at 75 percent of the generator or alternator's rated output to protect the system from sustained high-current operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical Measuring Instruments)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Bonding connections should be tested for

choices.B: amperage value.
choices.C: reactance.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Bonding connections are verified by measuring their resistance value, with FAA standards requiring the bonding resistance to be less than 0.003 ohm to ensure effective electrical continuity and static charge dissipation. Amperage or reactance measurements are not the appropriate test parameters for bonding integrity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Bonding and Shielding)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: A circuit breaker is installed in an aircraft electrical system primarily to protect the

choices.A: circuit and should be located as close to the source as possible.
choices.B: circuit and should be located as close to the unit as possible.
choices.C: electrical unit in the circuit and should be located as close to the source as possible.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: A circuit breaker is primarily installed to protect the wiring (circuit) from overcurrent damage, and it should be located as close to the power source as possible to minimize the length of unprotected wire between the source and the breaker. Placing it near the unit would leave a long run of wire unprotected. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Circuit Protection Devices)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: How should a voltmeter be connected?

choices.B: In parallel with the load.
choices.C: In series with the load.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: A voltmeter measures potential difference and must be connected in parallel (across) the load so that it samples the voltage without significantly altering circuit current; connecting it in series would insert its very high internal resistance into the circuit and essentially interrupt current flow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Electrical Measuring Instruments)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: A circuit protection device called a current limiter is essentially a slow-blow fuse and is designed to be used in

choices.B: heavy power circuits.
choices.C: starter-generator circuits.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Current limiters are slow-blow protective devices designed for heavy power circuits where momentary high inrush currents are normal; their delayed response prevents nuisance interruptions while still protecting against sustained overcurrent faults. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Circuit Protection Devices)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Which of the following are considered circuit-protective devices that are used in aircraft electrical systems?

choices.A: Circuit breakers, relays, and current limiters.
choices.B: Circuit breakers, fuses, and current limiters.
choices.C: Circuit breakers, capacitors, and current limiters.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: FAA-recognized circuit protective devices in aircraft electrical systems are circuit breakers, fuses, and current limiters; relays are switching devices and capacitors are filtering components, neither of which serves as overcurrent protection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Circuit Protection Devices)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: In the American Wire Gauge (AWG) system of numbers used to designate electrical wire sizes, the number assigned to a size is related to its

choices.B: current-carrying capacity.
choices.C: cross-sectional area.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: In the AWG system, each gauge number corresponds directly to a specific cross-sectional area of the conductor; as the gauge number increases, the cross-sectional area decreases. Current-carrying capacity and resistance are derived properties that result from the cross-sectional area. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Wire)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: What is the purpose of the selection of derated switches for known continuous load current applications?

choices.A: To calculate the voltage drop across the circuit.
choices.B: To prevent short circuits in the motor field windings.
choices.C: To obtain reasonable switch efficiency and service life.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Switches are derated below their nameplate rating for continuous load applications because sustained current causes heat buildup that degrades contact materials over time; derating ensures the switch operates within safe thermal limits to achieve reasonable efficiency and extended service life. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Switches)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: In aircraft electrical systems, automatic reset circuit breakers

choices.A: should not be used as circuit protective devices.
choices.B: are useful where only temporary overloads are normally encountered.
choices.C: must be used in all circuits essential to safe operation of the aircraft.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Automatic reset circuit breakers continuously reset themselves during an overload condition, potentially allowing a fault to persist and causing wire overheating or fire; FAA standards therefore prohibit their use as circuit protective devices in aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Circuit Protection Devices)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: The circuit breaker in the instrument lighting system protects the

choices.B: wiring from too much current.
choices.C: wiring from too much voltage.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Circuit breakers are designed to protect wiring from overcurrent conditions that could cause overheating and fire; the device being powered (the lights) has its own design limits, but the breaker's primary role is wire protection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: Certain transport aircraft use AC electrical power for all normal operation and battery furnished DC electrical power for standby emergency use. In aircraft of this type that operate no DC generators, the batteries are kept charged by

choices.A: inverters which use the aircraft's AC generators as a source of power.
choices.B: alternators which use the aircraft's generators as a source of power.
choices.C: rectifiers which use the aircraft's AC generators as a source of power.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Rectifiers convert AC power from the aircraft's AC generators into DC power suitable for charging the batteries, eliminating the need for DC generators in all-AC primary power aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: If the positive field lead between a generator and a generator control panel breaks and is shorted while the engine is running, a voltmeter connected to generator output would indicate

choices.B: residual voltage.
choices.C: normal voltage.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: When the field circuit is broken, the generator loses its externally supplied field excitation and can only produce the small residual voltage generated by the remaining residual magnetism in the field poles. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: What is a method used for restoring generator field residual magnetism?

choices.B: Reseat the brushes.
choices.C: Energize the armature.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: Flashing the fields restores lost residual magnetism by momentarily applying external DC power directly to the field windings, re-magnetizing the field poles. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: The inductor-type inverter output voltage is controlled by the

choices.A: number of poles and the speed of the motor.
choices.B: voltage regulator.
choices.C: dc stator field current.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: In an inductor-type inverter, output voltage is regulated by a voltage regulator that controls the DC input or field excitation, maintaining a stable AC output regardless of load variations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: When using an ohmmeter to check the continuity of a generator field coil, the coil should

choices.A: be removed from the generator housing.
choices.B: show high resistance when the meter prods are connected to the terminals of the coil.
choices.C: show very low resistance if it is a series field coil.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: A series field coil is wound with few turns of heavy wire to carry the full armature current, resulting in very low resistance that an ohmmeter will confirm; high resistance or an open circuit would indicate a fault. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: A voltage regulator controls generator voltage by changing the

choices.A: resistance in the generator output circuit.
choices.B: current in the generator output circuit.
choices.C: resistance of the generator field circuit.

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: A voltage regulator maintains constant generator output voltage by varying the resistance in the field circuit, which changes field current and thus the strength of the magnetic field and the resulting output voltage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Aircraft Electrical Systems.question: The overvoltage control automatically protects the generator system when excessive voltage is present by

choices.B: opening and resetting the field control relay.
choices.C: breaking a circuit to the trip coil of the field control relay.&&&&%'wer

Aircraft Electrical Systems.explanation: The overvoltage control protects the system by breaking the circuit to the trip coil of the field control relay, which de-energizes the relay and removes the generator from the bus before damage occurs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-31B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)
