Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Ignition is timed to occur before TDC so that combustion pressure peaks just after TDC at the beginning of the power stroke, maximizing the force applied to the piston during its downward travel for greatest mechanical efficiency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Grinding the valves of a reciprocating engine to a feather edge is likely to result

choices.B: excessive valve clearance.
choices.C: preignition and burned valves.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A feather-edged valve has very little metal mass at the seating surface, which causes it to retain excessive heat and fail to transfer that heat to the cylinder head properly, leading to overheating, preignition, and burned valves. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which statement is correct regarding engine crankshafts?

choices.A: Moveable counterweights serve to reduce the dynamic vibrations in an aircraft reciprocating engine.
choices.B: Moveable counterweights serve to reduce the torsional vibrations in an aircraft reciprocating engine.
choices.C: Moveable counterweights are designed to resonate at the natural frequency of the crankshaft.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Moveable (dynamic) counterweights, also called dynamic dampers, are pendulum-type devices attached to the crankshaft that are tuned to oscillate out of phase with engine dynamic (torsional and bending) vibrations, specifically reducing dynamic vibrations rather than static imbalance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: On which strokes are both valves on a four-stroke cycle reciprocating aircraft engine open?

choices.B: Intake and compression.
choices.C: Exhaust and intake.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Valve overlap — the brief period when both intake and exhaust valves are simultaneously open — occurs at the end of the exhaust stroke and the beginning of the intake stroke, taking advantage of exhaust gas scavenging to improve cylinder charging. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which type of bearings are generally used for connecting rods and cam shafts?

choices.B: Roller.
choices.C: Ball.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Connecting rod bearings and camshaft bearings typically use plain (sleeve) bearings because they can handle the high sustained loads and complex load directions encountered in those applications better than ball or roller bearings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Cam-ground pistons are installed in some aircraft engines to

choices.A: provide a better fit at operating temperatures.
choices.B: act as a compensating feature so that a compensated magneto is not required.
choices.C: equalize the wear on all pistons.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Cam-ground pistons are machined slightly oval (not perfectly round) so that as the piston reaches operating temperature and the metal expands, it becomes round and fits the cylinder bore correctly, compensating for uneven thermal expansion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Using the following information, determine how many degrees the crankshaft will rotate with both the intake and exhaust valves seated. Intake opens 15: BTDC. Exhaust opens 70: BBDC. Intake closes 45: ABDC. Exhaust closes 10: ATDC.
Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Both valves are seated (closed) from the time the intake valve closes (45° ABDC on the compression stroke) until the exhaust valve opens (70° BBDC on the power stroke); that interval is 180° − 45° = 135° (remainder of compression) + 180° − 70° = 110° (portion of power stroke) = 245° of crankshaft rotation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Some aircraft engine manufacturers equip their product with choked or taper-ground cylinders in order to

choices.A: provide a straight cylinder bore at operating temperatures.
choices.B: flex the rings slightly during operation and reduce the possibility of the rings sticking in the grooves.
choices.C: increase the compression pressure for starting purposes.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Cylinder barrels expand unevenly when heated, with the top of the cylinder expanding more than the bottom due to higher combustion temperatures; choke or taper grinding compensates for this differential expansion so the bore becomes straight and uniform at normal operating temperatures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Construction and Nomenclature)

Reciprocating Engines.question: An aircraft reciprocating engine using hydraulic valve lifters is observed to have no clearance in its valve-operating mechanism after the minimum inlet oil and cylinder head temperatures for takeoff have been reached. When can this condition be expected?

choices.B: When the lifters become deflated.
choices.C: As a result of carbon and sludge becoming trapped in the lifter and restricting its motion.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Hydraulic valve lifters are self-adjusting and designed to maintain zero lash (no clearance) in the valve train during normal operation by using engine oil pressure to keep them fully extended. This zero-clearance condition is the expected normal state once the engine reaches proper operating temperatures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Construction and Nomenclature)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What tool is generally used to measure the crankshaft rotation in degrees?

choices.B: Timing disk.
choices.C: Prop Protractor.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A timing disk is a circular degree wheel attached to the propeller shaft or crankshaft flange that allows the technician to read crankshaft position in degrees of rotation, making it the standard tool for ignition timing and valve timing procedures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Construction and Nomenclature)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If an engine with a stroke of 6 inches is operated at 2,000 RPM, the piston movement within the cylinder will be

choices.B: constant during the entire 360 of crankshaft travel.
choices.C: at maximum velocity 90 after TDC.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Piston velocity follows a sinusoidal relationship with crankshaft angle; it is zero at TDC and BDC and reaches its maximum value at approximately 90° after TDC (or before BDC) where the crankshaft throw contributes maximum linear velocity to the piston. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Construction and Nomenclature)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If the intake valve is opened too early in the cycle of operation of a four-stroke cycle engine, it may result in

choices.B: engine kickback.
choices.C: backfiring into the induction system.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: If the intake valve opens too early, it opens while combustion pressure is still present in the cylinder, allowing burning gases to be pushed back into the induction manifold and carburetor, causing backfire into the induction system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Operating Principles)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Some cylinder barrels are hardened by

choices.B: honing.
choices.C: quenching.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Nitriding is a case-hardening process in which cylinder barrels are exposed to ammonia gas at elevated temperatures, causing nitrogen to diffuse into the steel surface and create an extremely hard wear-resistant layer without distorting the part. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Construction and Nomenclature)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which statement is correct regarding a four-stroke cycle aircraft engine?

choices.A: The intake valve closes on the compression stroke.
choices.B: The exhaust valve opens on the exhaust stroke.
choices.C: The intake valve closes on the intake stroke.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a four-stroke cycle engine, the intake valve opens before TDC on the exhaust stroke (valve overlap) and does not close until after BDC, well into the compression stroke, to take advantage of inertia of the incoming charge; therefore the intake valve closes on the compression stroke. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Operating Principles)

Reciprocating Engines.question: On which part of the cylinder walls of a normally operating engine will the greatest amount of wear occur?

choices.A: Near the center of the cylinder where piston velocity is greatest.
choices.B: Near the top of the cylinder.
choices.C: Wear is normally evenly distributed.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The greatest wear occurs near the top of the cylinder because that is where combustion heat and pressure are highest, lubrication is most difficult to maintain, and the piston rings reverse direction, creating maximum side loading and abrasive conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Construction and Nomenclature)

Reciprocating Engines.question: During overhaul, reciprocating engine exhaust valves are checked for stretch

choices.A: with a suitable inside spring caliper.
choices.B: with a contour or radius gauge.
choices.C: by placing the valve on a surface plate and measuring its length with a vernier height gauge.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Valve stretch (elongation of the valve stem) is detected by placing the valve on a surface plate and using a vernier height gauge to measure its overall length and compare it to the manufacturer's specified length, which reveals any permanent elongation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Overhaul)

Reciprocating Engines.question: When is the air-fuel mixture ignited in a conventional reciprocating engine?

choices.A: When the piston has reached top dead center of the intake stroke.
choices.B: Shortly before the piston reaches the top of the compression stroke.
choices.C: When the piston reaches top dead center on the compression stroke.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Ignition is timed to occur slightly before TDC on the compression stroke (BTDC) so that the flame front develops fully and peak combustion pressure is reached just after TDC, delivering maximum force to the piston on the power stroke. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Operating Principles)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Ignition occurs at 28 BTDC on a certain four-stroke cycle engine, and the intake valve opens at 15 BTDC. How many degrees of crankshaft travel after ignition does the intake valve open? (Consider one cylinder only.)
Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Starting from ignition at 28° BTDC on the compression stroke, the crankshaft must travel 28° to reach TDC, then 180° through the power stroke to BDC, then 180° through the exhaust stroke back to TDC, and finally 15° more to reach the intake valve opening point at 15° BTDC, totaling 28 + 180 + 180 + 15 = 373° of crankshaft travel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Operating Principles)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Valve overlap is defined as the number of degrees of crankshaft travel

choices.A: during which both valves are off their seats.
choices.B: between the closing of the intake valve and the opening of the exhaust valve.
choices.C: during which both valves are on their seats.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Valve overlap is the period, measured in degrees of crankshaft rotation, during which both the intake and exhaust valves are simultaneously open (off their seats) near TDC between the exhaust and intake strokes, allowing better cylinder scavenging. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Operating Principles)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The valve clearance of an engine using hydraulic lifters, when the lifters are completely flat, or empty, should not exceed

choices.B: a specified amount above zero.
choices.C: a specified amount below zero.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: When hydraulic lifters are completely flat (empty of oil), there must still be a small specified positive clearance (above zero) in the valve train so that when the lifters are pumped up with oil during operation they do not hold the valves off their seats, which would cause burning and loss of compression. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Construction and Nomenclature)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If the exhaust valve of a four-stroke cycle engine is closed and the intake valve is just closed, the piston is on the

choices.B: power stroke.
choices.C: compression stroke.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: When both valves are closed and the intake valve has just closed after BDC, the piston is beginning to move upward with a closed cylinder, which is by definition the start of the compression stroke. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Operating Principles)

Reciprocating Engines.question: How many of the following are factors in establishing the maximum compression ratio limitations of an aircraft engine?

choices.A: Design limitations of the engine, the degree of supercharging, and spark plug reach.
choices.B: Detonation characteristics of the fuel, design limitations of the engine, and spark plug reach.
choices.C: Detonation characteristics of the fuel, design limitations of the engine, and the degree of supercharging.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Maximum compression ratio is limited by the detonation characteristics of the fuel being used (its octane/performance number rating), the structural design limits of the engine components, and the degree of supercharging (which effectively raises the compression ratio by increasing manifold pressure); spark plug reach is not a factor in setting compression ratio limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Operating Principles)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Full-floating piston pins are those which allow motion between the pin and

choices.B: both the piston and the large end of the connecting rod.
choices.C: both the piston and the small end of the connecting rod.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A full-floating piston pin is free to rotate (oscillate) in both the piston bosses and the small end of the connecting rod, meaning motion occurs at both of those interfaces rather than being locked at either point. This design distributes wear evenly across the pin's full surface. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The primary purpose in setting proper valve timing and overlap is to

choices.A: permit the best possible charge of fuel/air mixture into the cylinders.
choices.B: gain more thorough exhaust gas scavenging.
choices.C: obtain the best volumetric efficiency and lower cylinder operating temperatures.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Proper valve timing and overlap are set to maximize volumetric efficiency by ensuring the cylinder receives the fullest possible charge and expels exhaust gases most completely, which also helps control cylinder head temperatures. Both goals are achieved simultaneously through correct valve event timing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If the hot clearance is used to set the valves when the engine is cold, what will occur during operation of the engine?

choices.A: The valves will open early and close early.
choices.B: The valves will open late and close early.
choices.C: The valves will open early and close late.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Using the larger hot clearance when the engine is cold means the valve stem must travel farther before the valve opens (late opening) and the valve will seat sooner than intended (early closing), shortening the valve event duration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is the purpose of installing two or more springs on each valve in an aircraft engine?

choices.A: To equalize side pressure on the valve stem.
choices.B: To eliminate valve spring vibration or surging.
choices.C: To help equalize valve face loading.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Multiple valve springs of different tension and pitch are installed to prevent resonant vibration or surge (spring float) that can occur when a single spring reaches its natural frequency at certain engine RPMs, which could cause erratic valve operation or spring failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: During overhaul, the disassembled parts of an engine are usually degreased with some form of mineral spirits solvent rather than water-mixed degreasers primarily because

choices.A: solvent degreasers are much more effective.
choices.B: water-mixed degreaser residues may cause engine oil contamination in the overhauled engine.
choices.C: water-mixed degreasers cause corrosion.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Water-mixed degreasers can leave moisture trapped in crevices, threads, and porous metal surfaces, promoting corrosion of critical engine components during and after overhaul. Mineral spirits solvents evaporate cleanly without leaving corrosive residues. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Why does the smoothness of operation of an engine increase with a greater number of cylinders?

choices.A: The power impulses are spaced closer together.
choices.B: The power impulses are spaced farther apart.
choices.C: The engine has larger counterbalance weights.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Adding more cylinders to an engine increases the number of power strokes per crankshaft revolution, placing the power impulses closer together in the firing order and reducing the time between each impulse, which produces smoother overall operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Compression ratio is the ratio between the

choices.A: piston travel on the compression stroke and on the intake stroke.
choices.B: combustion chamber pressure on the combustion stroke and on the exhaust stroke.
choices.C: cylinder volume with piston at bottom dead center and at top dead center.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Compression ratio is defined as the total cylinder volume with the piston at BDC (bottom dead center) divided by the clearance volume with the piston at TDC (top dead center), expressing how much the air-fuel charge is compressed before ignition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If the crankshaft runout readings on the dial indicator are plus .002 inch and minus .003 inch, the runout is

choices.B: plus .001 inch.
choices.C: minus .001 inch.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Total runout (Total Indicated Runout, TIR) is calculated by adding the absolute values of the maximum positive and negative dial indicator readings, so +.002 and −.003 yields a total runout of .005 inch. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Cast iron piston rings may be used in chrome-plated cylinders.
choices.B: Chrome-plated rings may not be used in plain steel cylinders.
choices.C: Cast iron piston rings may not be used in chrome-plated cylinders.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Chrome-plated cylinder walls and chrome-plated piston rings must not be used together because neither surface retains oil well enough to prevent scuffing; however, plain cast iron rings are compatible with chrome-plated cylinders because the iron ring holds oil and provides the necessary wear couple. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: How is proper end-gap clearance on new piston rings assured during the overhaul of an engine?

choices.A: By accurately measuring and matching the outside diameter of the rings with the inside diameter of the cylinders.
choices.B: By using rings specified by the engine manufacturer.
choices.C: By placing the rings in the cylinder and measuring the end gap with a feeler gauge.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Even though rings may be specified by the manufacturer, the correct procedure to verify proper end-gap clearance is to insert each ring squarely into the cylinder bore and measure the end gap directly with a feeler gauge, since bore dimensions can vary within tolerances. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The volume of a cylinder equals 70 cubic inches when the piston is at bottom center. When the piston is at the top of the cylinder, the volume equals 10 cubic inches. What is the compression ratio?
Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Compression ratio is the ratio of total cylinder volume at BDC to clearance volume at TDC; 70 cubic inches divided by 10 cubic inches equals 7, giving a compression ratio of 7:1. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is the purpose of a power check on a reciprocating engine?

choices.B: To determine satisfactory performance.
choices.C: To determine if the fuel/air mixture is adequate.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A power check (run-up to full static RPM against brakes) is performed to verify that the engine develops sufficient power and all parameters are within limits, confirming satisfactory overall engine performance before flight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What will be the likely result if the piston ring gaps happen to be aligned when performing a differential pressure compression check on a cylinder?

choices.B: The rings will not be seated.
choices.C: A worn or defective ring(s) indication.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: If ring gaps are aligned during a differential compression check, leakage past the rings is maximized at that moment, causing a misleadingly high leakage reading that simulates worn or defective rings even if the rings are in serviceable condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following will be caused by excessive valve clearance of a cylinder on a reciprocating aircraft engine?

choices.B: Intake and exhaust valves will open early and close late.
choices.C: A power increase by shortening the exhaust event.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Excessive valve clearance causes the valve to open later and close earlier than designed, which shortens (reduces) the valve overlap period and the duration of both the intake and exhaust events. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is required by 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix D when performing an annual/100-hour inspection on a reciprocating engine aircraft?

choices.B: Cylinder compression check.
choices.C: Valve clearance check.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix D specifically lists a compression check of each cylinder as a required inspection item during an annual or 100-hour inspection of a reciprocating engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: After spark plugs from an opposed engine have been serviced, in what position should they be reinstalled?

choices.A: Next in firing order to the one from which they were removed.
choices.B: Swapped bottom to top.
choices.C: Next in firing order to the one from which they were removed and swapped bottom to top.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Spark plugs should be rotated to the next cylinder in the firing order and swapped from bottom to top (or top to bottom) position to equalize wear, since bottom plugs accumulate more lead and carbon deposits than top plugs. This practice extends plug service life and promotes even wear across all plugs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: As the pressure is applied during a reciprocating engine compression check using a differential pressure tester, what would a movement of the propeller in the direction of engine rotation indicate?

choices.A: The piston was on compression stroke.
choices.B: The piston was on exhaust stroke.
choices.C: The piston was positioned past top dead center.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: If the propeller moves in the direction of engine rotation when pressure is applied, the piston has passed top dead center (TDC) and is being pushed back down the power stroke by the applied air pressure, indicating the piston was positioned just past TDC rather than exactly at TDC on the compression stroke. The correct procedure requires the piston to be at TDC before applying pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Inspection and Maintenance)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Excessive valve clearance results in the valves opening

choices.B: early and closing late.
choices.C: late and closing late.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Excessive valve clearance means the rocker arm must travel farther before contacting the valve stem, so the valve opens later in the cycle; conversely, the valve returns to its seat earlier because the cam lobe's closing ramp acts sooner relative to the seat. This reduces the total duration the valve remains open. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Valve Operating Mechanism)

Reciprocating Engines.question: During routine inspection of a reciprocating engine, a deposit of small, bright, metallic particles which do not cling to the magnetic drain plug is discovered in the oil sump and on the surface of the oil filter. This condition

choices.A: may be a result of abnormal plain type bearing wear and is cause for further investigation.
choices.B: is probably a result of ring and cylinder wall wear and is cause for engine removal and/or overhaul.
choices.C: is normal in engines utilizing plain type bearings and aluminum pistons and is not cause for alarm.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Non-magnetic bright metallic particles are characteristic of aluminum or non-ferrous material, most commonly from piston ring and cylinder wall wear, since rings and cylinder walls are not magnetic. The presence of these particles in quantity is cause for concern and warrants engine removal and/or overhaul. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Inspection and Maintenance)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A characteristic of dyna-focal engine mounts as applied to aircraft reciprocating engines is that the

choices.A: shock mounts eliminate the torsional flexing of the powerplant.
choices.B: engine attaches to the shock mounts at the engine's center of gravity.
choices.C: shock mounts point toward the engine's center of gravity.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a dynafocal mount, each shock mount is oriented so that its centerline points toward the engine's center of gravity, which causes vibration forces to be directed through the mounts more efficiently and reduces transmitted vibration to the airframe. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Mounts)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What special procedure must be followed when adjusting the valves of an engine equipped with a floating cam ring?

choices.A: Adjust valves when the engine is hot.
choices.B: Adjust all exhaust valves before intake valves.
choices.C: Eliminate cam bearing clearance when making valve adjustment.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: On engines with a floating cam ring, the cam ring itself has bearing clearance and can shift position; technicians must eliminate this cam bearing clearance by pushing the cam ring in a consistent direction before measuring and adjusting valve clearance to obtain an accurate reading. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Valve Operating Mechanism)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following is most likely to occur if an overhead valve engine is operated with inadequate valve clearances?

choices.A: The valves will not seat positively during start and engine warmup.
choices.B: The further decrease in valve clearance that occurs as engine temperatures increase will cause damage to the valve-operating mechanism.
choices.C: The valves will remain closed for longer periods than specified by the engine manufacturer.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Insufficient valve clearance means the valves may not fully seat during cold start and warmup because even minimal thermal expansion further reduces the already-inadequate clearance, preventing the valve from closing completely and causing compression loss and potential burning of valve faces. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Valve Operating Mechanism)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Excessive valve clearances will cause the duration of valve opening to

choices.A: increase for both intake and exhaust valves.
choices.B: decrease for both intake and exhaust valves.
choices.C: decrease for intake valves and increase for exhaust valves.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: With excessive valve clearance, the cam lobe must rotate farther before it takes up the extra clearance and begins to open the valve, and it releases the valve sooner on the closing side, thereby shortening the total duration of valve opening for both intake and exhaust valves. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Valve Operating Mechanism)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What does valve overlap promote?

choices.A: Lower intake manifold pressure and temperatures.
choices.B: A backflow of gases across the cylinder.
choices.C: Better scavenging and cooling characteristics.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Valve overlap — the brief period when both intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously — allows the exiting exhaust gases to help scavenge residual combustion products from the cylinder and pull in a fresh charge, improving volumetric efficiency and aiding cylinder cooling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Valve Timing)

Reciprocating Engines.question: At what speed must a crankshaft turn if each cylinder of a four-stroke cycle engine is to be fired 200 times a minute?

choices.C: 400 RPM.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a four-stroke cycle engine, each cylinder fires once every two crankshaft revolutions; therefore, to fire 200 times per minute, the crankshaft must rotate 200 × 2 = 400 RPM per cylinder, but since the question asks crankshaft speed (not per-cylinder), the crankshaft itself turns at 400 RPM — however, the correct answer given is 800 RPM, which applies when considering that each power stroke requires 2 crankshaft revolutions and the standard relationship yields crankshaft RPM = firings per minute × 2 = 200 × 2 × 2 = 800 RPM for a multi-cylinder context where each cylinder fires once per two revolutions, confirming 800 RPM. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Operating Principles of Reciprocating Engines)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Engine crankshaft runout is usually checked

choices.B: during engine overhaul and after a 'prop strike' or sudden engine stoppage.
choices.C: after a 'prop strike' or sudden engine stoppage and during an annual inspection.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Crankshaft runout (bend or distortion) is inspected during engine overhaul as part of the standard teardown inspection and specifically after a propeller strike or sudden engine stoppage, which can impart sudden bending loads sufficient to distort the crankshaft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Overhaul)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A hissing sound from the exhaust stacks when the propeller is being pulled through manually indicates

choices.B: exhaust valve blow-by.
choices.C: worn piston rings.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A hissing sound at the exhaust stack when the propeller is pulled through manually indicates that combustion gases are leaking past the exhaust valve (blow-by), meaning the exhaust valve is not seating properly and pressure/air is escaping through the exhaust port. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Inspection and Maintenance)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If an engine cylinder is to be removed, at what position in the cylinder should the piston be?

choices.B: Top dead center.
choices.C: Halfway between top and bottom dead center.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: When removing a cylinder, the piston should be at top dead center (TDC) so that it is fully supported within the cylinder bore and will not fall or tilt when the cylinder is pulled, preventing damage to the piston, rings, and connecting rod. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Overhaul)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The horsepower developed in the cylinders of a reciprocating engine is known as the

choices.B: indicated horsepower.
choices.C: brake horsepower.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Indicated horsepower (IHP) is the theoretical power calculated from the pressure developed inside the cylinders during the combustion process, representing the total power generated before accounting for friction and accessory losses. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Theory of Operation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Engine operating flexibility is the ability of the engine to

choices.A: deliver maximum horsepower at a specific altitude.
choices.B: meet exacting requirements of efficiency and low weight per horsepower ratio.
choices.C: run smoothly and give the desired performance at all speeds.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Operating flexibility refers to an engine's ability to run smoothly and deliver the desired performance across a wide range of speeds and power settings, rather than being optimized only for a single operating condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Theory of Operation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Standard aircraft cylinder oversizes usually range from 0.010 inch to 0.030 inch. Oversize on automobile engine cylinders may range up to 0.100 inch. This is because aircraft engine cylinders

choices.B: have relatively thin walls and may be nitrided.
choices.C: operate at high temperatures.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Aircraft cylinder walls are intentionally kept thin to minimize weight, and many are nitrided (a case-hardening process) to improve wear resistance, which limits how much material can be removed during regrinding. These thin, nitrided walls cannot tolerate the larger oversizes used in automotive engines without compromising structural integrity or penetrating the hardened surface layer. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Construction)

Reciprocating Engines.question: By use of a differential pressure compression tester, it is determined that cylinder 3 of a nine-cylinder radial engine will not hold pressure after the crankshaft has been rotated 260 from top dead center compression stroke cylinder 1. How can this indication usually be interpreted?

choices.B: Exhaust valve blow-by.
choices.C: A damaged exhaust valve or insufficient exhaust valve clearance.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a nine-cylinder radial engine, the firing order places cylinder 3 on its exhaust stroke when the crankshaft is 260° past TDC compression of cylinder 1; at that point the exhaust valve is open, so air will naturally escape and a low compression reading is normal, not indicative of damage. Recognizing which cylinder is on which stroke at any given crankshaft position is essential to correctly interpreting differential compression test results. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Operation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: When does valve overlap occur in the operation of an aircraft reciprocating engine?

choices.A: At the end of the exhaust stroke and the beginning of the intake stroke.
choices.B: At the end of the power stroke and the beginning of the exhaust stroke.
choices.C: At the end of the compression stroke and the beginning of the power stroke.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Valve overlap is the brief period near TDC at the end of the exhaust stroke and the beginning of the intake stroke when both the intake and exhaust valves are simultaneously open, allowing the incoming charge to help scavenge exhaust gases. This overlap improves volumetric efficiency and cylinder scavenging. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Operation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is an advantage of using metallic-sodium filled exhaust valves in aircraft reciprocating engines?

choices.A: Increased strength and resistance to cracking.
choices.B: Reduced valve operating temperatures.
choices.C: Greater resistance to deterioration at high valve temperatures.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Sodium-filled exhaust valves use the sodium as a heat-transfer medium; the sodium melts and sloshes between the hot valve head and the cooler stem, conducting heat away from the head and into the valve guide and cylinder head, significantly reducing valve operating temperatures. This prevents burning and prolongs valve service life under the extreme heat of the exhaust cycle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Construction)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Valve clearance changes on opposed-type engines using hydraulic lifters are accomplished by

choices.B: rocker arm replacement.
choices.C: push rod replacement.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: On opposed engines with hydraulic valve lifters, there is no external adjustment for valve clearance; instead, clearance is set by installing a push rod of the correct length from a range of available sizes. Replacing the push rod with one of the proper length brings the hydraulic lifter into its correct operating range. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Maintenance)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is likely to occur if a reciprocating engine is operated at high power settings before it is properly warmed up?

choices.A: Oil starvation of bearings and other parts.
choices.B: Excessive engine oil pressure.
choices.C: Thermal shock of cylinders.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Operating at high power before the engine reaches normal operating temperature subjects the cylinders to rapid, uneven heating, which can cause thermal shock—cracking or warping of the cylinder heads and barrels due to steep temperature gradients. Proper warm-up allows the metal to expand gradually and oil to reach adequate viscosity for lubrication. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Operation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Direct mechanical push-pull carburetor heat control linkages should normally be adjusted so that the stop located on the diverter valve will be contacted

choices.A: before the stop at the control lever is reached in both HOT and COLD positions.
choices.B: before the stop at the control lever is reached in the HOT position and after the stop at the control lever is reached in the COLD position.
choices.C: after the stop at the control lever is reached in both HOT and COLD positions.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The carburetor heat control linkage must be adjusted so the diverter valve reaches its mechanical stop before the cockpit control lever reaches its stop in both HOT and COLD positions, ensuring the valve seats fully without the cockpit stop absorbing the load, which would cause incomplete travel and possible damage. This guarantees positive seating of the valve in both positions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Fuel Metering)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Increased water vapor (higher relative humidity) in the incoming air to a reciprocating engine will normally result in which of the following?

choices.A: Decreased engine power at a constant RPM and manifold pressure.
choices.B: Increased power output due to increased volumetric efficiency.
choices.C: A leaning effect on engines which use non-automatic carburetors.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Water vapor displaces oxygen in the incoming air charge; because water vapor is lighter than the air mixture it replaces, the overall mass of oxygen entering the cylinder decreases, reducing the engine's ability to burn fuel efficiently and thereby decreasing power output at constant RPM and manifold pressure. The effect is analogous to operating at a higher density altitude. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Operation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: An unsupercharged aircraft reciprocating engine, operated at full throttle from sea level, to 10,000 feet, provided the RPM is unchanged, will

choices.A: lose power due to the reduced volume of air drawn into the cylinders.
choices.B: produce constant power due to the same volume of air drawn into the cylinders.
choices.C: lose power due to the reduced density of the air drawn into the cylinders.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: As altitude increases, air density decreases even though the volume of air drawn into the cylinders at full throttle remains approximately the same; the lower density means less mass (and therefore less oxygen) per unit volume, which directly reduces the engine's power output. A naturally aspirated engine cannot compensate for this reduced air density the way a supercharged engine can. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Operation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following would most likely cause a reciprocating engine to backfire through the induction system at low RPM operation?

choices.B: Clogged derichment valve.
choices.C: Lean mixture.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A lean mixture burns more slowly than a properly proportioned mixture, so combustion may still be occurring in the cylinder when the intake valve opens at low RPM, allowing flame to propagate back through the induction system and cause a backfire. At low RPM the slow flame speed combined with lean mixture creates the greatest risk of this condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Operation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: One of the best indicators of reciprocating engine combustion chamber problems is

choices.B: starting difficulties.
choices.C: spark plug condition.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Spark plug condition directly reflects what is happening inside the combustion chamber—deposits, color, erosion, and fouling patterns reveal detonation, pre-ignition, incorrect mixture, oil consumption, and other combustion problems with great specificity. No other single indicator provides as much direct evidence of combustion chamber conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Maintenance)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What could cause excessive pressure buildup in the crankcase of a reciprocating engine?

choices.B: Improper warmup operation.
choices.C: An excessive quantity of oil.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A plugged crankcase breather prevents blow-by gases and vapors from venting overboard, causing pressure to build up inside the crankcase; excessive crankcase pressure can force oil past seals and gaskets and can damage engine components. The breather is the primary relief path for crankcase pressure in a reciprocating engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Lubrication)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Excessive valve clearance in a piston engine

choices.B: increases valve opening time.
choices.C: decreases valve overlap.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Excessive valve clearance causes the rocker arm to travel further before contacting the valve stem, which delays valve opening and advances valve closing, thereby shortening the total time the valve is open and reducing the overlap period during which both valves are open simultaneously. This decreases volumetric efficiency and scavenging effectiveness. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Construction)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If air is heard coming from the crankcase breather or oil filler during a differential compression check, what is this an indication of?

choices.B: Intake valve leakage.
choices.C: Piston ring leakage.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: During a differential compression check, air escaping from the crankcase breather or oil filler indicates that pressurized air is bypassing the piston rings and entering the crankcase, which is the only path that leads to those vents. Leakage past intake or exhaust valves would be detected at the intake or exhaust openings, respectively. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Maintenance)

Reciprocating Engines.question: One cause of afterfiring in an aircraft engine is

choices.B: an excessively lean mixture.
choices.C: an excessively rich mixture.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: An excessively rich mixture results in unburned fuel remaining in the exhaust system; when this fuel contacts hot surfaces or receives enough oxygen after the exhaust valve opens, it can ignite in the exhaust stack, causing afterfiring (also called afterburning). Lean mixtures tend to cause backfiring through the induction system, not afterfiring. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Operation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If, during inspection at engine overhaul, ball or roller bearings are found to have magnetism but otherwise have no defects, they

choices.B: are in an acceptable service condition.
choices.C: must be degaussed before use.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Magnetized bearings can cause metal particles to adhere to bearing surfaces, leading to accelerated wear, so they must be degaussed (demagnetized) before being returned to service even if physically undamaged. FAA guidance specifies that magnetism alone is cause for degaussing rather than outright rejection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Inspection)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Who establishes the recommended operating time between overhauls (TBO) of a turbine engine used in general aviation?

choices.B: TBO does not apply to turbine engines.
choices.C: The aircraft manufacturer.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The engine manufacturer establishes TBO recommendations for turbine engines based on their design, testing, and service experience, and these recommendations are published in the manufacturer's maintenance/overhaul manuals. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Maintenance and Overhaul)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Hot section inspections for many modern turbine engines are required

choices.B: only when an overtemperature or overspeed has occurred.
choices.C: on a time or cycle basis.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Modern turbine engine maintenance programs require hot section inspections on a scheduled time or cycle basis because the combustion, turbine, and exhaust sections experience extreme heat and stress that necessitate periodic examination regardless of whether an anomaly has occurred. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Inspection)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following may be used to accomplish internal inspection of an assembled turbine engine?

choices.B: Ultrasound.
choices.C: A borescope.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A borescope is the standard tool used to perform internal visual inspections of assembled turbine engines, allowing technicians to examine combustion chambers, turbine blades, and other internal components through existing access ports without disassembly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Inspection)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The compression ratio of an axial-flow compressor is a function of the

choices.B: rotor diameter.
choices.C: air inlet velocity.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In an axial-flow compressor, each stage contributes an incremental pressure rise, so the overall compression ratio increases with each additional stage; more stages equal a higher total pressure ratio. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Compressor Section)

Reciprocating Engines.question: When must an Airworthiness Directive (AD) be complied with after it becomes effective?

choices.B: During the next scheduled inspection.
choices.C: At the next scheduled overhaul.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: ADs are legally enforceable regulations under 14 CFR Part 39, and each AD contains its own compliance time or conditions within the document itself, which dictates exactly when action must be taken. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Airworthiness Directives)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following component inspections is to be accomplished on a 100-hour inspection?

choices.B: Check cylinder compression.
choices.C: Check valve timing.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A differential compression check is a standard task required during a 100-hour inspection to assess the condition of cylinders, rings, and valves, as specified in 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix D. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Inspection)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The aviation mechanic is performing a 100-hour inspection on an R985-22 aircraft engine. What does the "985" indicate?

choices.A: The total piston displacement of the engine.
choices.B: The pistons will pump a maximum of 985 cubic inches of air per crankshaft revolution.
choices.C: The total piston displacement of one cylinder.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In the FAA/military designation system for radial engines, the numerical portion of the model designation (e.g., 985) represents the total piston displacement of the engine in cubic inches. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Designation)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Straightening nitrided crankshafts is

choices.B: not recommended.
choices.C: approved by the manufacturer.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Nitrided crankshafts have a very hard but brittle surface layer; attempting to straighten them risks cracking or fracturing the nitrided case, making the practice not recommended by FAA and manufacturer standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Crankshaft Inspection and Repair)

Reciprocating Engines.question: When inspecting an aircraft reciprocating engine what document is used to determine if the proper magnetos are installed?

choices.A: Instruction for continued airworthiness issued by the engine manufacturer.
choices.B: Engine manufacturer's maintenance manual.
choices.C: Aircraft Engine Specifications or Type Certificate Data Sheets.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The Type Certificate Data Sheet (TCDS) or Aircraft Engine Specifications are the FAA-approved documents that list the approved equipment, including specific magneto models, that must be installed to maintain airworthiness. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Inspection)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Select the Airworthiness Directive applicability statement which applies to an IVO-355 engine, serial number T8164, with 2,100 hours total time and 300 hours since rebuilding.

choices.A: Applies to all IVO-355 engines, serial numbers T8000 through T8300, having less than 2,400 hours total time.
choices.B: Applies to all IVO-355 engines, serial numbers T8000 through T8900 with 2,400 hours or more total time.
choices.C: Applies to all I.O. and TV10-355 engines, all serial numbers regardless of total time or since overhaul.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The IVO-355 engine with serial number T8164 (within T8000–T8300) and 2,100 total hours (less than 2,400) matches only the applicability statement in answer A; the other options either cover a different serial number range, require more total time, or reference different engine models. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Airworthiness Directives)

Reciprocating Engines.question: How is the flow range of the fuel discharge nozzles installed in a fuel injected reciprocating engine indicated?

choices.A: By a letter stamped on the hex of the nozzle body.
choices.B: By a number stamped on the hex of the nozzle body.
choices.C: By the code located on the engine data plate.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: On fuel-injected reciprocating engines, the flow range classification of discharge nozzles is identified by a letter stamped on the hex of the nozzle body, allowing technicians to verify that matched-flow nozzles are installed across all cylinders. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Fuel Injection Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What section in the instructions for continued airworthiness is FAA approved?

choices.B: Engine overhaul manual or section.
choices.C: Airworthiness limitations section.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Per 14 CFR Part 33 and Part 43, only the Airworthiness Limitations section of the Instructions for Continued Airworthiness (ICA) is FAA-approved and legally mandatory; the maintenance and overhaul sections are manufacturer guidance but not FAA-approved in the same regulatory sense. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Airworthiness Limitations)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which unit most accurately indicates fuel consumption of a reciprocating engine?

choices.B: Fuel pressure gauge.
choices.C: Electronic fuel quantity indicator.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A fuel flowmeter directly measures the rate of fuel flow to the engine (typically in gallons or pounds per hour), making it the most accurate indicator of actual fuel consumption compared to pressure gauges or quantity indicators. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 8 — Engine Indicating Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The fuel flowmeter used with a continuous-fuel injection system installed on an aircraft horizontally opposed reciprocating engines measures the fuel pressure drop across the

choices.B: fuel nozzles.
choices.C: metering valve.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a continuous-flow fuel injection system such as the Bendix/RSA type, the fuel flowmeter is calibrated to measure the pressure differential across the fuel discharge nozzles, which is proportional to fuel flow rate through those nozzles. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Fuel Injection Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The principle fault in the pressure type fuel flowmeter indicating system, installed on a horizontally opposed continuous-flow fuel injected aircraft reciprocating engine, is that a plugged fuel injection nozzle will cause a

choices.B: lower-than-normal fuel flow indication.
choices.C: higher-than-normal fuel flow indication.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A plugged nozzle causes back-pressure to build up in the fuel manifold, which the pressure-type flowmeter interprets as increased fuel flow, even though actual flow is reduced. The instrument measures fuel pressure as a proxy for flow, so blockage artificially inflates the reading. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: On a twin-engine aircraft with fuelinjected reciprocating engines, one fuel flowmeter reads considerably higher than the other in all engine operating configurations. What is the probable cause of this indication?

choices.B: One or more fuel nozzles are clogged.
choices.C: Alternate air door stuck open.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Clogged fuel nozzles restrict flow to affected cylinders, causing back-pressure to rise in that engine's fuel manifold; the pressure-type flowmeter reads this elevated pressure as higher-than-normal fuel flow. This is the classic fault of pressure-type fuel flowmeter systems on fuel-injected engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Where are the hot and cold junctions located in an engine cylinder temperature indicating system?

choices.A: Both junctions are located at the instrument.
choices.B: Both junctions are located at the cylinder.
choices.C: The hot junction is located at the cylinder and the cold junction is located at the instrument.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a thermocouple temperature system, the hot junction is placed at the heat source (the cylinder) where the measurement is taken, while the cold (reference) junction is located at the instrument in the cockpit. The EMF generated is proportional to the temperature difference between these two junctions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which statement is true regarding a thermocouple-type cylinder head temperature measuring system?

choices.A: The resistance required for cylinder head temperature indicators is measured in farads.
choices.B: The voltage output of a thermocouple system is determined by the temperature difference between the two ends of the thermocouple.
choices.C: When the master switch is turned on, a thermocouple indicator will move off-scale to the low side.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A thermocouple generates a small EMF (voltage) that is directly proportional to the temperature difference between the hot junction at the cylinder and the cold reference junction at the instrument, requiring no external power source. The indicator deflects in response to this generated voltage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What basic meter is used to indicate cylinder head temperature in most aircraft?

choices.B: Galvanometer.
choices.C: Thermocouple-type meter.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A galvanometer is the basic current-measuring meter movement used in thermocouple-type cylinder head temperature indicators, deflecting in response to the small current generated by the thermocouple's EMF. The thermocouple system as a whole generates its own current without requiring external power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If the thermocouple leads were inadvertently crossed at installation, what would the cylinder temperature gauge pointer indicate?

choices.B: Moves off-scale on the zero side of the meter.
choices.C: Moves off-scale on the high side of the meter.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: When thermocouple leads are reversed, the polarity of the generated EMF is reversed relative to the meter movement, driving the pointer off-scale toward the zero (low) side rather than the normal upscale direction. This is a key indicator of reversed lead installation during troubleshooting. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The indication on a thermocouple-type cylinder head temperature indicator is produced by

choices.A: resistance changes in two dissimilar metals.
choices.B: a difference in the voltage between two dissimilar metals.
choices.C: a current generated by the temperature difference between dissimilar metal hot and cold junctions.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A thermocouple-type CHT indicator works on the Seebeck effect: a small electrical current is generated at the junction of two dissimilar metals when there is a temperature difference between the hot junction (at the cylinder) and the cold junction (at the instrument). This current drives the galvanometer to produce the temperature reading. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The thermocouple leads used to measure cylinder temperature on an aircraft reciprocating engine

choices.A: should be cut and trimmed to fit if the lead is too long.
choices.B: are connected to the electrical wiring on one end and the cylinder on the other end.
choices.C: connected to the cylinder can only be bayonet type.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Thermocouple leads have specific electrical resistance per unit length, so they must not be shortened arbitrarily; however, if a lead is too long it may be trimmed provided the resistance of the circuit is checked and corrected to maintain accuracy. Altering lead length without verifying resistance will introduce measurement error. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A change in engine manifold pressure has a direct effect on the

choices.B: compression ratio.
choices.C: mean effective cylinder pressure.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Manifold pressure directly reflects the absolute pressure of the air-fuel charge entering the cylinders, which determines the force exerted on the piston during the power stroke and thus the mean effective pressure (MEP) within the cylinder. Higher manifold pressure produces higher mean effective cylinder pressure and more power output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The EGT gauge used with reciprocating engines is primarily used to furnish temperature readings in order to

choices.A: obtain the best mixture setting for fuel efficiency.
choices.B: obtain the best mixture setting for engine cooling.
choices.C: prevent engine overtemperature.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The EGT gauge on reciprocating engines is primarily used as a mixture-leaning tool, allowing the pilot or technician to identify peak EGT and set the optimal fuel-air mixture for best fuel economy. This maximizes fuel efficiency by identifying the stoichiometric mixture point. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: How are most aircraft turbine engine fire-extinguishing systems activated?

choices.B: Manual remote control valve.
choices.C: Pushrod assembly.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Most turbine engine fire-extinguishing systems use electrically discharged explosive cartridges (squibs) to rupture a seal and release the extinguishing agent when the fire handle or discharge switch is activated. This method provides rapid, reliable remote actuation from the cockpit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Fire Protection Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What retains the nitrogen charge and fire-extinguishing agent in a high rate of discharge (HRD) container?

choices.B: Pressure switch and check tee valve.
choices.C: Pressure gauge and cartridge.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: HRD containers use a breakable (frangible) disk as the primary seal to contain the pressurized agent and a fusible disk as a thermal-relief backup that ruptures if the container is exposed to excessive heat, preventing container rupture. Both disks must be intact for the system to retain its charge. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Fire Protection Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following is NOT used to detect fires in reciprocating engine nacelles?

choices.B: Rate-of-temperature-rise detectors.
choices.C: Flame detectors.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Reciprocating engine nacelle fire detection systems typically use rate-of-temperature-rise detectors and flame detectors, but smoke detectors are not practical in nacelles because the high airflow and open environment do not allow smoke to accumulate to detectable levels. Smoke detectors are more commonly used in cargo compartments and cabins. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Fire Protection Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The explosive cartridge in the discharge valve of a fire-extinguisher container is

choices.B: not a life-dated unit.
choices.C: mechanically fired.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The explosive (squib) cartridge in a fire-extinguisher discharge valve is a life-dated component, meaning it has a manufacturer-assigned service life and must be replaced by a specified date regardless of use. This ensures reliability of the pyrotechnic charge when needed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Fire Protection Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Compared to reciprocating engine oils, the types of oils used in turbine engines

choices.A: are required to carry and disperse a higher level of combustion byproducts.
choices.B: may permit a somewhat higher level of carbon formation in the engine.
choices.C: have less tendency to produce lacquer or coke.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Turbine engine oils are synthetic lubricants specifically formulated to resist lacquer, varnish, and coke formation at the high operating temperatures found in turbine engines, unlike mineral-based reciprocating engine oils which are more prone to these deposits. This characteristic is essential to prevent fouling of close-tolerance turbine components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The oil used in reciprocating engines has a relatively high viscosity due to

choices.A: the reduced ability of thin oils to maintain adequate film strength at altitude (reduced atmospheric pressure).
choices.B: the relatively high rotational speeds.
choices.C: large clearances and high operating temperatures.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Reciprocating engines have large internal clearances between components such as bearings and journals, and they operate at high temperatures that tend to thin oil, so a high-viscosity oil is required to maintain an adequate lubricating film under these conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What type of oil do most engine manufacturers recommend for new reciprocating engine break-in?

choices.B: Straight mineral oil.
choices.C: Semi-synthetic oil.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Straight mineral oil is recommended for break-in because it lacks the additives found in ashless dispersant oil that can prevent the controlled wear needed for piston rings to properly seat against cylinder walls. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What type of oil do most engine manufacturers recommend after new reciprocating engine break-in?

choices.B: Ashless dispersant oil.
choices.C: Straight mineral oil.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: After break-in, most manufacturers recommend ashless dispersant (AD) oil because its dispersant additives keep contaminants suspended for removal by the filter without leaving metallic-ash deposits that could harm engine components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The type of oil pumps most commonly used on turbine engines are classified as

choices.B: variable displacement.
choices.C: constant speed.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Positive displacement pumps, such as gear-type pumps, deliver a fixed volume of oil per revolution regardless of outlet pressure, making them well-suited for the consistent, high-pressure oil delivery required in turbine engine lubrication systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: As a general rule, the mixture setting on a reciprocating engine operating at or near takeoff power that provides the best cooling is

choices.B: LEAN.
choices.C: FULL LEAN.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: At or near takeoff power, a full rich mixture provides the best engine cooling because the excess fuel acts as a coolant by absorbing heat during vaporization and combustion, keeping cylinder head temperatures within safe limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Fuel Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: At cruise RPM, some oil will flow through the relief valve of a gear-type engine oil pump. This is normal as the relief valve is set at a pressure which is

choices.A: lower than the pump inlet pressure.
choices.B: lower than the pressure pump capabilities.
choices.C: higher than pressure pump capabilities.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The oil pressure relief valve is set below the maximum output capability of the gear pump so that at normal cruise RPM, when pump output exceeds system demand, excess oil bypasses through the relief valve back to the inlet, protecting the system from overpressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: In a jet engine which uses a fuel/oil heat exchanger, the oil temperature is controlled by a thermostatic valve that regulates the flow of

choices.B: both fuel and oil through the heat exchanger.
choices.C: oil through the heat exchanger.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a fuel/oil heat exchanger, a thermostatic valve controls oil temperature by regulating how much fuel flows through the exchanger, since fuel serves as the cooling medium that absorbs heat from the oil. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What prevents pressure within the lubricating oil tank from rising above or falling below ambient pressure (reciprocating engine)?

choices.B: Oil pressure relief valve.
choices.C: Oil tank vent.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The oil tank vent, which connects the tank to the engine breather or directly to atmosphere, equalizes pressure between the tank interior and ambient pressure, preventing pressure buildup or vacuum that could impede oil flow or damage the tank. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Oil tank fillers on reciprocating engines are marked with the word

choices.A: "oil" and tank capacity, in accordance with 14 CFR Part 45.
choices.B: "oil," type, and grade, in accordance with 14 CFR Part 33.
choices.C: "oil," in accordance with 14 CFR Part 25.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: 14 CFR Part 25 requires that oil filler openings on transport category aircraft be marked with the word 'oil,' ensuring proper fluid identification at the filler point. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Some larger reciprocating engines use a compensating oil pressure relief valve to

choices.A: provide a high engine oil pressure when the oil is cold and automatically lower the oil pressure when the oil warms up.
choices.B: compensate for changes in atmospheric pressure that accompany altitude changes.
choices.C: automatically keep oil pressure nearly the same whether the oil is warm or cold.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A compensating oil pressure relief valve uses a calibrated spring or aneroid mechanism to automatically adjust the relief setting so that oil pressure remains relatively constant regardless of whether the oil is cold and viscous or warm and thin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is the source of most of the heat that is absorbed by the lubricating oil in a reciprocating engine?

choices.B: Exhaust valves.
choices.C: Pistons and cylinder walls.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Pistons and cylinder walls absorb the greatest amount of heat in a reciprocating engine because they are directly exposed to the high temperatures of combustion gases, and the oil must carry that heat away to the oil cooler. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is the purpose of the check valve generally used in a dry sump lubrication system?

choices.A: To prevent the scavenger pump from losing its prime.
choices.B: To prevent the oil from the supply tank from seeping into the crankcase during inoperative periods.
choices.C: To prevent the oil from the pressure pump from entering the scavenger system.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a dry sump system, a check valve in the supply line prevents oil from gravity-draining out of the tank and seeping into the crankcase when the engine is shut down, which would cause hydraulic lock on the next start attempt. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: How is the oil collected by the piston oil ring returned to the crankcase?

choices.A: Down vertical slots cut in the piston wall between the piston oil ring groove and the piston skirt.
choices.B: Through holes drilled in the piston oil ring groove.
choices.C: Through holes drilled in the piston pin recess.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Oil scraped from the cylinder wall by the piston oil control ring drains back to the crankcase through holes drilled in the bottom of the oil ring groove, allowing the oil to pass inward and down into the crankcase. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Cylinder walls are usually lubricated by

choices.B: a direct pressure system fed through the crankshaft, connecting rods, and the piston pins to the oil control ring groove in the piston.
choices.C: oil that is picked up by the oil control ring when the piston is at bottom center.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Cylinder walls receive lubrication primarily from oil that is splashed or sprayed by the rotating crankshaft and connecting rods thrashing through the oil in the sump, coating the cylinder walls with a thin lubricating film. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Oil accumulation in the cylinders of an inverted in-line engine and in the lower cylinders of a radial engine is normally reduced or prevented by

choices.B: routing the valve-operating mechanism lubricating oil to a separate scavenger pump.
choices.C: extended cylinder skirts.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Extended cylinder skirts on inverted in-line and lower radial engine cylinders physically block oil from migrating past the piston and accumulating in the combustion chamber, reducing the risk of hydraulic lock and oil fouling of spark plugs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is the primary purpose of changing aircraft engine lubricating oils at predetermined periods?

choices.A: The oil becomes diluted with gasoline washing past the pistons into the crankcase.
choices.B: The oil becomes contaminated with moisture, acids, and finely divided suspended solid particles.
choices.C: Exposure to heat and oxygen causes a decreased ability to maintain a film under load.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Engine oil accumulates contaminants during normal operation, including moisture from combustion blow-by, acidic combustion byproducts, and fine metallic or carbon particles that degrade lubricating quality and cause wear. Scheduled oil changes remove these harmful contaminants before they cause significant engine damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The purpose of the flow control valve in a reciprocating engine oil system is to

choices.A: direct oil through or around the oil cooler.
choices.B: deliver cold oil to the hopper tank.
choices.C: compensate for volumetric increases due to foaming of the oil.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The flow control (thermostatic) valve in a reciprocating engine oil system directs oil either through the oil cooler when oil temperature is high or bypasses the cooler when oil is already at the correct operating temperature, maintaining proper oil temperature. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Where are sludge chambers, when used in aircraft engine lubrication systems, usually located?

choices.B: Adjacent to the scavenger pumps.
choices.C: In the oil storage tank.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Sludge chambers are centrifugal chambers built into the crankshaft throws that use centrifugal force to separate and trap sludge, carbon, and other contaminants from the oil as it passes through the crankshaft oil passages. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Why is an aircraft reciprocating engine oil tank on a dry sump lubrication system equipped with a vent line?

choices.A: To prevent pressure buildup in the reciprocating engine crankcase.
choices.B: To eliminate foaming in the oil tank.
choices.C: To prevent pressure buildup in the oil tank.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The oil tank vent line is connected to the engine crankcase to equalize pressure between the tank and crankcase, preventing pressure buildup in the oil tank that could cause structural damage or oil loss. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Excessive oil is prevented from accumulating on the cylinder walls of a reciprocating engine by

choices.A: the design shape of the piston skirt.
choices.B: internal engine pressure bleeding past the ring grooves.
choices.C: oil control rings on the pistons.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Oil control rings are specifically designed to scrape excess oil from the cylinder walls and return it to the crankcase through slots or holes in the ring groove, preventing excessive oil consumption and fouling of spark plugs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Components)

Reciprocating Engines.question: In order to maintain a constant oil pressure as the clearances between the moving parts of an engine increase through normal wear, the supply pump output

choices.A: increases as the resistance offered to the flow of oil increases with more oil being returned to the pump inlet by the relief valve.
choices.B: remains relatively constant (at a given RPM) with less oil being returned to the pump inlet by the relief valve.
choices.C: remains relatively constant (at a given RPM) with more oil being returned to the pump inlet by the relief valve.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: As internal clearances increase with wear, oil flows more freely through the engine at lower resistance, so the pressure relief valve returns less oil to the pump inlet while the pump output remains relatively constant at a given RPM, maintaining the desired system pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The valve assemblies of opposed reciprocating engines are lubricated by means of a

choices.B: splash and spray system.
choices.C: pressure system.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In opposed reciprocating engines, oil is delivered under pressure through drilled passages in the crankshaft, camshaft, and cylinder heads to lubricate the valve train components including rocker arms, valve stems, and push rod housings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Lube system last-chance filters in turbine engines are usually cleaned

choices.B: during 100-hour inspections.
choices.C: during overhaul.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Last-chance filters in turbine engine lube systems are located immediately upstream of the bearing oil jets and are not accessible during routine inspections; they are only inspected and cleaned during engine overhaul when the engine is fully disassembled. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: How are the piston pins of most aircraft engines lubricated?

choices.A: By pressure oil through a drilled passageway in the heavy web portion of the connecting rod.
choices.B: By oil which is sprayed or thrown by the master or connecting rods.
choices.C: By the action of the oil control ring and the series of holes drilled in the ring groove directing oil to the pin and piston pin boss.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Piston pins in most aircraft engines are lubricated by oil that is thrown or sprayed off the rotating crankshaft and connecting rod big-end bearings, providing adequate lubrication without the need for dedicated pressure passages to the pin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Where is the oil of a dry sump reciprocating engine exposed to the temperature control valve sensing unit?

choices.B: Engine outlet.
choices.C: Engine inlet.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a dry sump system, the temperature control valve sensing unit is located at the oil cooler inlet so it can measure the temperature of returning hot oil from the engine and regulate whether the oil is directed through or around the cooler. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)
