Reciprocating Engines.question: Under which of the following conditions is the oil cooler flow control valve open on a reciprocating engine?

choices.A: When the temperature of the oil returning from the engine is too high.
choices.B: When the temperature of the oil returning from the engine is too low.
choices.C: When the scavenger pump output volume exceeds the engine pump input volume.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: When oil returning from the engine is too cold, the thermostatic flow control valve opens to allow oil to bypass the cooler, routing it directly back to the engine to allow faster warm-up and prevent congealing of cold oil in the cooler. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The primary source of oil contamination in a normally operating reciprocating engine is

choices.A: metallic deposits as a result of engine wear.
choices.B: atmospheric dust and pollution.
choices.C: combustion deposits due to combustion chamber blow-by and oil migration on the cylinder walls.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Combustion blow-by past the piston rings introduces combustion gases, carbon particles, and partially burned fuel products into the crankcase oil, making combustion deposits the primary source of contamination in a normally operating engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following prevents oil from entering the main accessory case when the engine is not running?

choices.B: Hydraulic fuse.
choices.C: Check valve.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A check valve prevents reverse oil flow from the accessory case back into the engine when it is shut down, stopping oil from pooling in the accessory case and causing hydraulic lock or bearing damage on the next start. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: How many secondary coils are required in a low-tension ignition system on an 18-cylinder engine?
Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a low-tension ignition system, each cylinder requires one secondary (high-voltage) coil located near the spark plug to step up voltage close to the point of ignition, so an 18-cylinder engine with one plug per coil requires 18 secondary coils. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The purpose of staggered ignition is to compensate for

choices.B: rich fuel/air mixture around exhaust valve.
choices.C: diluted fuel/air mixture around exhaust valve.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Staggered ignition timing offsets the firing of the two spark plugs in each cylinder so that the plug nearest the exhaust valve fires slightly later, compensating for the diluted, cooler fuel/air mixture in that area caused by residual exhaust gases. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A certain nine-cylinder radial engine used a noncompensated single-unit, dual-type magneto with a four-pole rotating magnet and separately mounted distributors. Which of the following will have the lowest RPM at any given engine speed?

choices.B: Engine crankshaft.
choices.C: Distributors.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a nine-cylinder radial engine with a four-pole rotating magnet, the cam rotates at one-eighth crankshaft speed (half the number of cam lobes relative to cylinders), making it slower than both the crankshaft and the distributor, which turns at one-half crankshaft speed. The breaker cam therefore has the lowest RPM at any given engine speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What likely effect would a cracked distributor rotor have on a magneto?

choices.A: Ground the secondary circuit through the crack.
choices.B: Fire two cylinders simultaneously.
choices.C: Ground the primary circuit through the crack.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A crack in the distributor rotor provides a path for the high-voltage secondary current to arc to ground rather than reaching the intended spark plug electrode, effectively grounding the secondary circuit through the crack and causing a misfire. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Thermocouples are usually inserted or installed on the

choices.B: rear cylinder of the engine.
choices.C: hottest cylinder of the engine.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Thermocouples used for cylinder head temperature (CHT) indication are installed on the hottest-running cylinder, which varies by engine model and is determined during engine development testing, ensuring the pilot monitors the most critical temperature point. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Indicating Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Why are turbine engine igniters less susceptible to fouling than reciprocating engine spark plugs?

choices.A: The high-intensity spark cleans the igniter.
choices.B: The frequency of the spark is less for igniters.
choices.C: Turbine igniters operate at cooler temperatures.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Turbine engine igniters produce an extremely high-intensity spark that burns away carbon and other deposits each time they fire, effectively self-cleaning the igniter tip and making fouling far less likely than with the comparatively lower-energy spark of a reciprocating engine spark plug. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A spark plug's heat range is the result of

choices.A: the area of the plug exposed to the cooling airstream.
choices.B: its ability to transfer heat from the firing end of the spark plug to the cylinder head.
choices.C: the heat intensity of the spark.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A spark plug's heat range describes how efficiently the firing end dissipates heat into the cylinder head; a plug that transfers heat rapidly runs cooler (cold plug), while one that retains heat runs hotter (hot plug), and this characteristic is determined by the design and length of the insulator nose. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plugs)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The numbers appearing on the ignition distributor block indicate the

choices.B: relation between distributor terminal numbers and cylinder numbers.
choices.C: firing order of the engine.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The numbers on a distributor block indicate the sparking order, which is the sequence in which the distributor delivers high-voltage impulses to each cylinder; this is not necessarily the same as the engine's firing order because the distributor numbering relates to terminal position, not cylinder number directly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Hot spark plugs are generally used in aircraft powerplants

choices.A: with comparatively high compression or high operating temperatures.
choices.B: with comparatively low operating temperatures.
choices.C: which produce high power per cubic inch displacement.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Hot spark plugs are used in engines with comparatively low operating temperatures because their longer insulator nose retains more heat, keeping the firing tip hot enough to prevent fouling from lead and carbon deposits in cooler-running cylinders. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plugs)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following statements most accurately describes spark plug heat range?

choices.A: The length of the threaded portion of the shell usually denotes the spark plug heat range.
choices.B: A hot plug is designed so that the insulator tip is reasonably short to hasten the rate of heat transfer from the tip through the spark plug shell to the cylinder head.
choices.C: A cold plug is designed so that the insulator tip is reasonably short to hasten the rate of heat transfer from the tip through the spark plug shell to the cylinder head.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A cold plug has a short insulator tip that provides a shorter heat-transfer path, allowing heat to dissipate quickly from the firing end into the cylinder head, making it suitable for high-temperature, high-performance engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plugs)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Spark plug heat range is determined by

choices.A: the reach of the spark plug.
choices.B: its ability to transfer heat to the cylinder head.
choices.C: the number of ground electrodes.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Spark plug heat range is determined by the plug's ability to transfer heat away from the firing tip to the cylinder head; the physical design of the insulator nose length controls this rate of heat transfer and thus defines whether the plug is hot or cold. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plugs)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Spark plug fouling caused by lead deposits occurs most often

choices.B: when cylinder head temperatures are relatively low.
choices.C: when cylinder head temperatures are high.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Lead fouling occurs most readily when cylinder head temperatures are relatively low because lead bromide compounds that would otherwise be scavenged as gases condense and deposit on the cooler insulator and electrodes, building up conductive lead deposits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plugs)

Reciprocating Engines.question: In a four-stroke cycle aircraft engine, when does the ignition event take place?

choices.A: Before the piston reaches TDC on compression stroke.
choices.B: After the piston reaches TDC on power stroke.
choices.C: After the piston reaches TDC on compression stroke.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Ignition is timed to occur before the piston reaches TDC on the compression stroke (spark advance) so that the fuel-air mixture has sufficient time to begin burning and develop maximum pressure just after TDC, producing the greatest force on the piston during the power stroke. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: When installing a magneto on an engine, the

choices.A: piston in cylinder 1 must be a prescribed number of degrees before top center on the compression stroke.
choices.B: magneto breaker points must be just closing.
choices.C: piston in cylinder 1 must be a prescribed number of degrees after top center on the intake stroke.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: When timing a magneto to an engine, the number-one piston must be positioned at a specific number of degrees before top dead center on the compression stroke so that the magneto fires at the correct moment corresponding to the engine's required ignition timing specification. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is the relationship between distributor and crankshaft speed of aircraft reciprocating engines?

choices.A: The distributor turns at one-half crankshaft speed.
choices.B: The distributor turns at one and one-half crankshaft speed.
choices.C: The crankshaft turns at one-half distributor speed.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a four-stroke cycle engine, each cylinder requires one spark per two crankshaft revolutions, so the distributor is geared to rotate at one-half crankshaft speed to deliver a spark to each cylinder at the correct interval. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: At what RPM is a reciprocating engine ignition switch check made?

choices.B: The slowest possible RPM.
choices.C: Full throttle RPM.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The magneto switch check is typically performed at approximately 1,500 RPM because this speed is high enough to provide a stable and meaningful RPM drop reading when switching from both to single magneto operation, while still being below takeoff power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: In the event a pneumatic start valve will not operate and the manual override must be used, the starter T-handle must be closed at scheduled starter drop out because

choices.B: the starter will overspeed at a given N(2).
choices.C: the starter oil will be blown overboard.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: When the manual override is used on a pneumatic start valve, the starter lacks the automatic cutout mechanism, so if it remains engaged beyond the scheduled dropout speed it will overspeed, potentially exceeding the starter's maximum design RPM and causing mechanical failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Starting Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The metered fuel pressure (chamber C) in an injection-type carburetor

choices.A: is held constant throughout the entire engine operating range.
choices.B: varies according to the position of the poppet valve located between chamber D (unmetered fuel) and chamber E (engine-driven fuel pump pressure).
choices.C: will be approximately equal to the pressure in chamber A (impact pressure).

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In an injection-type carburetor, metered fuel pressure in chamber C is regulated to approximate impact (ram) air pressure in chamber A, so that the pressure differential across the metering jet reflects only venturi suction, ensuring accurate fuel metering proportional to airflow. This balance between impact pressure and metered fuel pressure is fundamental to the injection carburetor's operating principle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Metering)

Reciprocating Engines.question: If fuel is found running from the carburetor with the engine not running, the likely cause is that the

choices.A: float needle valve is not seated properly.
choices.B: float level is adjusted too low.
choices.C: main air bleed is clogged.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Fuel flowing from the carburetor with the engine not running indicates the float needle valve has failed to seat, allowing fuel to pass continuously through the inlet regardless of float position. A low float level would actually lean the mixture rather than cause overflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The device that controls the ratio of the air-fuel mixture to the cylinders is called a

choices.B: mixture control.
choices.C: metering jet.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The mixture control adjusts the ratio of fuel to air entering the engine by varying the amount of fuel introduced relative to the airflow, allowing the pilot to lean or enrich the mixture as altitude and power conditions change. The throttle valve controls volume, not ratio. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Mixture Control)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The device that controls the volume of the air-fuel mixture to the cylinders is called a

choices.B: metering jet.
choices.C: throttle valve.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The throttle valve controls the volume (quantity) of the air-fuel charge delivered to the cylinders by restricting or opening the induction airflow path, which in turn governs engine power output. The mixture control governs ratio, not volume. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Throttle Valve)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which statement is correct regarding a continuous-flow fuel injection system used on many reciprocating engines?

choices.A: Fuel is injected directly into each cylinder.
choices.B: Fuel is injected at each cylinder intake port.
choices.C: Two injector nozzles are used in the injector fuel system for various speeds.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a continuous-flow fuel injection system, fuel is discharged continuously at each cylinder's intake port rather than directly into the cylinder or via a common point, allowing fuel to mix with incoming air just before it enters the cylinder. This distinguishes it from direct cylinder injection used in some systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Continuous-Flow Fuel Injection)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following causes a single diaphragm accelerator pump to discharge fuel?

choices.A: An increase in venturi suction when the throttle valve is open.
choices.B: An increase in manifold pressure that occurs when the throttle valve is opened.
choices.C: A decrease in manifold pressure that occurs when the throttle valve is opened.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: When the throttle valve is opened, manifold pressure rises (suction decreases), and this increase in pressure acts on the single diaphragm accelerator pump, compressing the spring-loaded diaphragm and discharging an extra shot of fuel to prevent a momentary lean condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Accelerating Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: An aircraft engine continuous cylinder fuel injection system normally discharges fuel during which stroke(s)?

choices.B: Intake and compression.
choices.C: All (continuously).

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A continuous-flow fuel injection system discharges fuel at all times during engine operation, not just on specific strokes, which is why it is termed 'continuous'; fuel is sprayed constantly at the intake port regardless of the piston's position in its cycle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Continuous-Flow Fuel Injection)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following is least likely to occur during operation of an engine equipped with a direct cylinder fuel injection system?

choices.B: Kickback during start.
choices.C: Backfiring.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Direct cylinder fuel injection delivers fuel precisely timed into the cylinder rather than into the intake manifold, virtually eliminating backfiring through the induction system because there is no combustible mixture present in the intake tract. Afterfiring and kickback can still occur due to ignition timing or starting issues. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Injection Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What carburetor component limits the maximum airflow into the engine at full throttle?

choices.B: Venturi.
choices.C: Main metering jet.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The venturi is a fixed restriction whose throat diameter establishes the maximum mass airflow the carburetor can pass at full throttle; once the airflow reaches critical velocity through the venturi, it limits total induction airflow into the engine regardless of throttle position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Carburetor Venturi)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A nine-cylinder radial engine, using a multiple-point priming system with a central spider, will prime which cylinders?

choices.B: All cylinders.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: On a nine-cylinder radial engine with a central priming spider, the primer lines are routed to the odd-numbered cylinders (1, 3, 5, and 7) because these are the first cylinders in the firing order to receive the intake charge, ensuring the engine catches quickly during start. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Priming Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Under which of the following conditions would an engine run lean even though there is a normal amount of fuel present?

choices.A: The use of too high an octane rating fuel.
choices.B: Incomplete fuel vaporization.
choices.C: The carburetor air heater valve in the HOT position.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: When fuel does not fully vaporize, the liquid droplets do not combust efficiently, effectively reducing the combustible fuel quantity in the charge and causing the engine to run lean despite the correct metered fuel volume being present. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Vaporization)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Reciprocating engine power will be decreased at all altitudes if the

choices.B: humidity is increased.
choices.C: manifold pressure is increased.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Water vapor (humidity) displaces oxygen molecules in the intake air, reducing air density and the mass of oxygen available for combustion, which decreases engine power at all altitudes; increased air density or manifold pressure would have the opposite effect. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Power Factors)

Reciprocating Engines.question: An excessively lean air-fuel mixture may cause

choices.A: an increase in cylinder head temperature.
choices.B: high oil pressure.
choices.C: backfiring through the exhaust.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: An excessively lean mixture burns slower and at a higher temperature, causing elevated cylinder head temperatures that can lead to detonation or engine damage; it does not typically cause backfiring through the exhaust, which is more associated with rich mixtures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Mixture and Engine Temperature)

Reciprocating Engines.question: In turbine engines that utilize a pressurization and dump valve, the dump portion of the valve

choices.A: cuts off fuel flow to the engine fuel manifold and dumps the manifold fuel into the combustor to burn just before the engine shuts down.
choices.B: drains the engine manifold lines to prevent fuel boiling and subsequent deposits in the lines as a result of residual engine heat (at engine shutdown).
choices.C: dumps extra fuel into the engine in order to provide for quick engine acceleration during rapid throttle advancement.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: At engine shutdown, the dump valve opens to drain residual fuel from the manifold lines to a drain or overboard, preventing the heat-soaked engine from vaporizing that fuel and leaving lacquer deposits inside the fuel lines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What are the positions of the pressurization valve and the dump valve in a jet engine fuel system when the engine is shut down?

choices.A: Pressurization valve closed, dump valve open.
choices.B: Pressurization valve open, dump valve open.
choices.C: Pressurization valve closed, dump valve closed.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: At shutdown, the pressurization valve closes to stop fuel flow to the manifold, while the dump valve opens simultaneously to drain the manifold of residual fuel, preventing vapor lock and fuel coking in the hot engine environment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Pressurization and Dump Valves)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What could cause a lean mixture and high cylinder head temperature at sea level or low altitudes?

choices.B: Defective accelerating system.
choices.C: Automatic mixture control stuck in the extended position.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: An automatic mixture control (AMC) uses an aneroid bellows that extends at altitude to enrichen the mixture; if it is stuck in the extended (altitude-enriched retracted) position at sea level, it holds the mixture needle in the lean position, causing a lean mixture and elevated CHT. This is the opposite of the intended ground-level enrichment function of the AMC. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Metering Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: During what period does the fuel pump bypass valve open and remain open?

choices.A: When the fuel pump pressure is greater than the demand of the engine.
choices.B: When the boost pump pressure is greater than fuel pump pressure.
choices.C: When the fuel pump output is greater than the demand of the carburetor.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The engine-driven fuel pump bypass valve opens when boost pump pressure exceeds the engine-driven pump's output pressure, allowing boost pump fuel to flow around the engine-driven pump and continue to the engine without restriction. This protects the system when the engine-driven pump is operating at lower pressure than the boost pump. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following statements concerning a centrifugal-type fuel boost pump located in a fuel supply tank is NOT true?

choices.A: Air and fuel vapors do not pass through a centrifugal-type pump.
choices.B: Fuel can be drawn through the impeller section of the pump when it is not in operation.
choices.C: The centrifugal-type pump is classified as a positive displacement pump.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A centrifugal-type boost pump is a non-positive-displacement pump, meaning its output varies with demand and it does not trap a fixed volume of fluid per revolution; positive displacement is a characteristic of vane-type and gear-type pumps. Centrifugal pumps are specifically classified as non-positive-displacement devices. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Where is the engine fuel shutoff valve usually located?

choices.B: Adjacent to the fuel pump.
choices.C: Downstream of the engine-driven fuel pump.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The engine fuel shutoff valve is located on the cockpit (airframe) side of the firewall so that fuel flow to the engine can be cut off in an emergency without requiring access to the engine compartment, keeping fuel lines forward of the firewall isolated from the cockpit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel System Components)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is the purpose of an engine-driven fuel pump bypass valve?

choices.A: To divert the excess fuel back to the main tank.
choices.B: To prevent a damaged or inoperative pump from blocking the fuel flow of another pump in series with it.
choices.C: To divert the excess fuel from the pressure side of the pump to the inlet side of the pump.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The bypass valve in an engine-driven fuel pump allows fuel to flow around the pump in the event of pump failure or damage, preventing the inoperative pump from becoming a blockage when another pump (such as the boost pump) is supplying pressure in series. Without this valve, a seized pump would stop all fuel flow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Most large aircraft reciprocating engines are equipped with which of the following types of engine-driven fuel pumps?

choices.B: Centrifugal-type fuel pump.
choices.C: Gear-type fuel pump.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Large reciprocating aircraft engines use rotary vane-type engine-driven fuel pumps because they are positive-displacement pumps capable of producing the consistent, controllable fuel pressure and flow volume required by high-powered engines. Their output is regulated by a relief valve to match engine demand. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The fuel pump relief valve directs excess fuel to the

choices.B: inlet side of the fuel pump.
choices.C: inlet side of the fuel strainer.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The fuel pump relief valve recirculates excess fuel back to the inlet side of the fuel pump rather than returning it to the tank, maintaining a stable output pressure by internally bypassing surplus fuel within the pump assembly. This prevents overpressure on the downstream fuel system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which type of pump is commonly used as a fuel pump on reciprocating engines?

choices.B: Impeller.
choices.C: Vane.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The vane-type (rotary vane) pump is the most commonly used engine-driven fuel pump on reciprocating engines because it is a reliable positive-displacement pump that delivers consistent fuel pressure across a range of engine speeds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is a characteristic of a centrifugal-type fuel boost pump?

choices.A: It separates air and vapor from the fuel.
choices.B: It has positive displacement.
choices.C: It requires a relief valve.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A centrifugal-type boost pump uses its impeller action to separate entrained air and vapors from the fuel, purging them overboard through a vapor separator vent before the fuel reaches the engine-driven pump. This vapor separation capability is a key design advantage of the centrifugal boost pump. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A pilot reports that the fuel pressure fluctuates and exceeds the upper limits whenever the throttle is advanced. The most likely cause of the trouble is

choices.A: a ruptured fuel pump relief valve diaphragm.
choices.B: a sticking fuel pump relief valve.
choices.C: an air leak at the fuel pump relief-valve body.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A sticking fuel pump relief valve may intermittently stick closed, causing fuel pressure to spike above limits when the throttle is advanced and fuel demand increases, then release erratically, producing fluctuating pressure readings. A properly functioning relief valve would smoothly regulate pressure within limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Fuel pump relief valves designed to compensate for atmospheric pressure variations are known as

choices.B: pressurized-relief valves.
choices.C: balanced-type relief valves.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Balanced-type relief valves use an aneroid or reference chamber vented to atmospheric pressure so that the regulated fuel pressure automatically compensates for changes in ambient pressure with altitude, maintaining a consistent pressure differential regardless of altitude. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A fuel pressure relief valve is required on

choices.B: engine-driven vane-type fuel pumps.
choices.C: centrifugal fuel boost pumps.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Engine-driven vane-type fuel pumps are positive-displacement pumps that will build pressure indefinitely if flow is restricted, so a pressure relief valve is required to prevent overpressure damage to the fuel system; centrifugal pumps are not positive-displacement and do not require relief valves. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A rotary-vane pump is best described as a

choices.B: variable-displacement pump.
choices.C: boost pump.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A rotary-vane pump traps a fixed volume of fluid between its vanes and housing with each revolution, delivering a constant volume of fluid per cycle regardless of outlet pressure, which is the defining characteristic of a positive-displacement pump. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Fuel pressure produced by the engine-driven fuel pump is adjusted by the

choices.B: relief valve adjusting screw.
choices.C: engine-driven fuel pump adjusting screw.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Fuel pressure output from an engine-driven fuel pump is set by adjusting the tension on the relief valve spring via the relief valve adjusting screw, which determines the pressure at which the valve opens to recirculate excess fuel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Fuel Pumps)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What causes the fuel divider valve to open in a turbine engine duplex fuel nozzle?

choices.B: Bleed air after the engine reaches idle RPM.
choices.C: An electrically operated solenoid.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a turbine engine duplex fuel nozzle, the fuel divider valve opens when fuel pressure rises to a set value, at which point primary-manifold pressure is sufficient to unseat the valve and allow fuel to also flow through the secondary (main) orifice for higher flow rates. The valve is purely pressure-operated with no electrical or pneumatic actuation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Fuel Nozzles)

Reciprocating Engines.question: How often should float carburetors be overhauled?

choices.B: Annually.
choices.C: At engine change.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Float carburetors are considered part of the engine accessory system and should be overhauled concurrently with the engine to ensure all components meet serviceability standards at the same time. Overhauling at engine overhaul intervals keeps the carburetor's condition consistent with the overall engine condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Carburetor Maintenance)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What is the final authority for the details of carburetor overhaul?

choices.B: The Type Certificate Data Sheets for the engine.
choices.C: The manufacturer's recommendations.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The manufacturer's recommendations serve as the definitive technical authority for overhaul procedures, tolerances, and specifications because they are developed by the engineers who designed and tested the component. FAA standards consistently defer to manufacturer data for detailed maintenance and overhaul guidance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Carburetor Maintenance)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Maximum power is normally considered to be developed in a reciprocating engine with a air-fuel mixture ratio of approximately
Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Maximum power is produced at a rich best-power mixture of approximately 12:1 air-fuel ratio, which provides the optimal fuel quantity to release the most heat energy per power stroke. A leaner ratio near stoichiometric (15:1) produces best economy, not maximum power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Mixture and Fuel/Air Ratio)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A method commonly used to prevent carburetor icing is to

choices.B: mix alcohol with the fuel.
choices.C: electrically heat the throttle valve.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Preheating the intake air raises its temperature above the freezing point, preventing ice crystal formation in the carburetor venturi and throttle body where temperature drops and moisture can freeze. This is the standard and most commonly employed method for carburetor ice prevention. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Carburetor Ice and Carburetor Heat Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Into what part of a reciprocating engine induction system is deicing alcohol normally injected?

choices.B: The airstream ahead of the carburetor.
choices.C: The low-pressure area ahead of the throttle valve.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Deicing alcohol is injected into the airstream ahead of the carburetor so it can mix with incoming air and lower the freezing point of any moisture before it reaches the carburetor venturi and throttle area. Injecting upstream ensures the alcohol is distributed throughout the induction airflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Carburetor Ice and Carburetor Heat Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: As manifold pressure increases in a reciprocating engine, the

choices.A: volume of air in the cylinder increases.
choices.B: weight of the fuel/air charge decreases.
choices.C: density of air in the cylinder increases.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Manifold pressure is a measure of the absolute pressure of the air/fuel charge entering the cylinders, so as manifold pressure increases, more air molecules are forced into the cylinder, increasing the density of the charge. Greater charge density means more mass of air available for combustion, which directly increases power output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Supercharging and Manifold Pressure)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The differential pressure controller in a turbocharger system

choices.B: positions the waste gate valve for maximum power.
choices.C: provides a constant fuel-to-air ratio.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The differential pressure controller maintains a constant pressure drop across the throttle valve by modulating the waste gate, which prevents the turbocharger from surging or self-accelerating (bootstrapping) during part-throttle operations. Without this control, small throttle movements would cause large, uncontrolled swings in turbocharger output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbocharger Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The pressure between the turbocharger and the throttle valve is called

choices.B: induction manifold pressure.
choices.C: upper deck pressure.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Upper deck pressure is the specific term for the compressed air pressure that exists in the induction system between the turbocharger compressor outlet and the engine throttle valve. This term distinguishes it from manifold pressure, which is measured downstream of the throttle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbocharger Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The application of carburetor heat during engine operation will

choices.A: decrease the weight of the fuel/air charge.
choices.B: decrease the volume of air in the cylinder.
choices.C: increase the density of air in the cylinder.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Applying carburetor heat introduces warm air into the induction system, which reduces air density and therefore decreases the weight (mass) of the fuel/air charge entering the cylinders. This results in a richer mixture and reduced engine power output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Carburetor Ice and Carburetor Heat Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: In addition to causing accelerated wear, dust or sand ingested by a reciprocating engine may also cause

choices.B: sludge formation.
choices.C: acid formation.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Silicon compounds contained in ingested dust and sand can deposit on spark plug electrodes and insulators, causing silicon fouling that leads to misfiring and ignition system degradation. This is a well-documented hazard of operating in dusty or sandy environments without proper air filtration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Induction Systems and Air Filters)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The primary purpose of baffles and deflectors installed around cylinders of air-cooled aircraft engines is to

choices.A: create a low pressure area aft of the cylinders.
choices.B: force cooling air into close contact with all parts of the cylinders.
choices.C: increase the volume of air used to cool the engine.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Baffles and deflectors are designed to channel and direct airflow into close, intimate contact with all cylinder surfaces, fins, and head areas to maximize heat transfer away from the engine. Simply increasing air volume without directing it properly would not achieve adequate or uniform cooling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Aircraft reciprocating engine cylinder baffles and deflectors should be repaired as required to prevent loss of

choices.B: fin area.
choices.C: cooling.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Damaged or missing baffles and deflectors allow cooling air to bypass cylinder surfaces, leading to localized overheating and potential engine damage or failure. Maintaining the integrity of the baffle system is therefore critical to preserving proper engine cooling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Cracks in cooling fins that do not extend into the cylinder head

choices.A: should be left alone and monitored.
choices.B: may be repaired by removing the affected area and contour filing within limits.
choices.C: may be repaired by adding a stiffener.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Cracks in cooling fins that do not extend into the cylinder head material may be repaired by carefully removing the cracked fin material and contour filing the area smooth within the limits specified by the manufacturer. This prevents crack propagation while preserving the structural integrity of the cylinder head. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following should a mechanic consult to determine the maximum amount of cylinder cooling fin that could be removed when cracks are found?

choices.B: Engine manufacturer's service or overhaul manual.
choices.C: Engine structure repair manual.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The engine manufacturer's service or overhaul manual contains the specific dimensional limits and allowable repair procedures for cooling fins, as these limits are determined by the engineering data unique to each engine model. General references like AC 43.13-1B provide guidance but do not supersede manufacturer-specific data. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A bent cooling fin on an aluminum cylinder head should be

choices.B: left alone if no crack has formed.
choices.C: straightened out as much as possible without breaking.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Bent aluminum cooling fins should be carefully straightened as much as possible without breaking them, because even a partially straightened fin restores some cooling area and structural continuity. Removing or sawing off fins reduces the total cooling fin area and should only be done when repair within manufacturer limits requires it. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Where are cooling fins usually located on air-cooled engines?

choices.A: Exhaust side of the cylinder head, inside the pistons, and connecting rods.
choices.B: Cylinder head, cylinder walls, and inside the piston.
choices.C: Cylinder head, cylinder barrel, and inside the piston.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Cooling fins are machined onto the cylinder head and cylinder barrel to maximize surface area for heat dissipation, and the inside of the piston also contains fins to transfer heat to the engine oil. These three locations collectively manage the thermal load of combustion in air-cooled engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: How do cowl flaps aid in cooling a horizontally opposed aircraft engine?

choices.A: Recirculates air through the engine cylinders.
choices.B: Directs air through the engine cylinders.
choices.C: Controls the amount of air flowing around the cylinders.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Cowl flaps are movable panels located at the exit of the engine cowling that regulate the volume of airflow passing around the cylinders, allowing the pilot to increase or decrease cooling as needed. By adjusting the cowl flap opening, the pilot controls how much cooling air exits the cowling, directly affecting cylinder head temperature. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Generally, a small crack just started in a cylinder baffle

choices.A: requires repair by reinforcing, such as installation of a doubler over the area.
choices.B: requires no action unless it grows or is branched into two cracks.
choices.C: may be stop drilled.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A small, freshly started crack in a cylinder baffle may be stop-drilled to arrest further propagation, which is standard sheet metal repair practice for preventing crack growth. This technique is acceptable provided it is within the manufacturer's approved repair limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following defects would likely cause a hot spot on a reciprocating engine cylinder?

choices.A: Too much cooling fin area broken off.
choices.B: A cracked cylinder baffle.
choices.C: Cowling air seal leakage.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Cooling fins provide the surface area necessary to transfer heat away from specific areas of the cylinder; when too much fin area is broken off, localized heat cannot be adequately dissipated, creating a hot spot. The other options affect overall airflow but do not cause the concentrated, localized overheating that missing fin area does. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What part of an air-cooled cylinder assembly has the greatest fin area per square inch?

choices.B: Rear of the cylinder head.
choices.C: Exhaust valve port.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The exhaust valve port area experiences the highest thermal load in the cylinder assembly because exhaust gases at peak temperature flow directly past it, so the greatest concentration of fin area per square inch is located there to compensate. This targeted fin density is necessary to prevent warping or burning of the exhaust valve seat area. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Reciprocating engines used in helicopters are cooled by

choices.A: the downdraft from the main rotor.
choices.B: a fan mounted on the engine.
choices.C: blast tubes on either side of the engine mount.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Helicopter reciprocating engines do not receive sufficient ram air for cooling due to their low-speed or hovering flight profiles, so an engine-driven cooling fan is used to force air across the cylinders. This fan provides consistent airflow independent of the aircraft's forward speed or rotor downwash. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The greatest portion of heat generated by combustion in a typical aircraft reciprocating engine is

choices.B: carried out with the exhaust gases.
choices.C: dissipated through the cylinder walls and heads.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a typical reciprocating engine, the largest single portion of heat energy generated by combustion is carried away by the exhaust gases, exceeding both the useful work output and the heat dissipated through cylinder walls. This is a fundamental thermodynamic characteristic of the reciprocating engine cycle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: A broken cooling fin on a cylinder head

choices.A: is cause for rejection of the head.
choices.B: may be filed to smooth contours if damage and/or repair limits are not exceeded.
choices.C: should be left alone.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Broken cooling fins on a cylinder head may be repaired by filing the edges to smooth, rounded contours to prevent further cracking, provided the total amount of damage does not exceed the manufacturer's specified limits. Rejection is only required when damage exceeds those limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Cylinder head temperatures are measured by means of an indicator and a

choices.B: wheatstone bridge sensing device.
choices.C: thermocouple sensing device.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Cylinder head temperature (CHT) is measured using a thermocouple, which generates a small voltage proportional to the temperature difference between the hot junction (at the cylinder) and the cold junction (at the instrument). This direct-reading thermocouple system is the FAA-standard method for CHT instrumentation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: High cylinder head temperatures are likely to result from

choices.A: a very lean mixture at high power settings.
choices.B: fouled spark plugs.
choices.C: a very rich mixture at high power settings.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: An excessively lean mixture at high power settings produces higher combustion temperatures because there is insufficient fuel to absorb combustion heat and the charge burns more slowly and completely, raising cylinder head temperatures significantly. Fouled plugs and rich mixtures generally result in rough running or reduced power rather than extreme CHT increases. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Prolonged idling of an engine will usually result in

choices.B: increased oil consumption.
choices.C: foreign material buildup on spark plugs.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Prolonged idling results in an overly rich mixture and incomplete combustion, which causes carbon, lead deposits, and other foreign material to accumulate on spark plug electrodes, leading to fouling. This is a well-established operational characteristic cited in engine maintenance guidance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The most common method and generally the best conduction of heat from the inside of a cylinder barrel to the cooling air is accomplished by

choices.A: machining fins directly on the outside of the barrel.
choices.B: shrinking on a jacket or muff of aluminum cooling fins around a steel cylinder sleeve.
choices.C: machining fins directly on the outside of the barrel and shrinking on a jacket or muff of aluminum cooling fins around a steel cylinder sleeve (on different areas of the barrel).

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Machining fins directly onto the outside of the cylinder barrel is the most common and most thermally efficient method because it eliminates any interface resistance between the barrel and the fin material, providing direct metal-to-metal conduction. Added aluminum jackets introduce a thermal interface that reduces conduction efficiency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: The undersides of pistons are frequently finned. The principal reason is to

choices.A: provide sludge chambers and sediment traps.
choices.B: provide for greater heat transfer to the engine oil.
choices.C: support ring grooves and piston pins.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Fins on the underside of pistons greatly increase the surface area in contact with the oil splash or spray inside the crankcase, improving heat transfer from the piston crown to the engine oil, which then carries heat to the oil cooler. This is particularly important because the piston cannot be cooled by external airflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Reciprocating engine exhaust system designs commonly used to provide for ease of installation and/or allow for expansion and contraction may include the use of

choices.A: slip joints and flexible metal tubing.
choices.B: spring loaded ball joints, slip joints, and flexible metal tubing.
choices.C: spring loaded ball joints, slip joints, and bellows.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Reciprocating engine exhaust systems use spring-loaded ball joints, slip joints, and bellows to accommodate thermal expansion and contraction of the exhaust components while also easing installation and removal. These design features prevent cracking and leakage caused by the large temperature differentials experienced during engine operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Exhaust Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Sodium-filled valves are advantageous to an aviation engine because they

choices.B: dampen valve impact shocks.
choices.C: dissipate heat well.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Sodium-filled exhaust valves use the liquid sodium inside the hollow stem as a heat transfer medium; as the valve reciprocates, the sodium shuttles heat from the hot valve head to the cooler valve stem and guide, effectively dissipating heat that would otherwise cause valve burning. This cooling property is the primary advantage of sodium-filled valves in high-performance aircraft engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What type of nuts are used to hold an exhaust system to the cylinders?

choices.B: High-temperature fiber self-locking nuts.
choices.C: High-temperature aluminum self-locking nuts.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Exhaust system attachments to cylinders require brass or heat-resistant nuts because they must withstand extreme temperatures without seizing or failing, and self-locking fiber or aluminum nuts would be destroyed by the heat. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Engine Exhaust Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Power recovery turbines used on some reciprocating engines are driven by the

choices.B: crankshaft.
choices.C: velocity of the exhaust gases.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Power recovery turbines (PRTs) are driven by exhaust gas pressure tapped from the exhaust system, using that energy to turn a turbine wheel connected back to the engine crankshaft to recover otherwise wasted energy. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Engine Exhaust Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Reciprocating engine exhaust systems that have repairs or sloppy weld beads which protrude internally are unacceptable because they cause

choices.B: localized cracks.
choices.C: local hot spots.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Internal protrusions such as sloppy weld beads create turbulence in the exhaust gas flow, which causes localized overheating (hot spots) that can lead to burning and structural failure of the exhaust system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Engine Exhaust Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Ball joints in reciprocating engine exhaust systems should be

choices.A: tight enough to prevent any movement.
choices.B: disassembled and the seals replaced every engine change.
choices.C: loose enough to permit some movement.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Ball joints in exhaust systems must have some freedom of movement to accommodate thermal expansion and contraction; if too tight, they will bind and cause stress cracking in the exhaust components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Engine Exhaust Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: All of the following are recommended markers for reciprocating engine exhaust systems except

choices.B: lead pencil.
choices.C: Prussian blue.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Lead pencils are not acceptable for marking exhaust system components because the graphite can cause intergranular corrosion and embrittlement in high-temperature alloys, whereas India ink and Prussian blue are approved markers. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Engine Exhaust Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Augmenter tubes are part of which reciprocating engine system?

choices.B: Exhaust.
choices.C: Fuel.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Augmenter tubes are part of the exhaust system and use the velocity of exhaust gases passing through them to create an ejector effect that increases airflow over the engine for additional cooling and thrust. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Engine Exhaust Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: When necessary, APU engine cooling before shutdown may be accomplished by

choices.B: closing the bleed air valve.
choices.C: opening the bleed air valve.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Unloading the generator(s) reduces the power demand on the APU, allowing the turbine to cool down before shutdown and preventing heat soak damage to the hot section components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Auxiliary Power Units)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Usually, most of the load placed on an APU occurs when

choices.A: an electrical load is placed on the generator(s).
choices.B: the bleed air valve is opened.
choices.C: the bleed air valve is closed.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: Opening the bleed air valve places the greatest pneumatic load on the APU because it must supply compressed air for aircraft systems such as air conditioning and engine starting, which represents a much larger energy demand than electrical loading alone. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Auxiliary Power Units)

Reciprocating Engines.question: During overhaul of an engine, a first step of inspection on a crankshaft should be

choices.A: perform a magnaflux inspection for cracks.
choices.B: perform a runout check.
choices.C: inspect the bearing surfaces for pitting.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: A magnaflux inspection is the first step because it detects cracks that would immediately condemn the crankshaft, making further dimensional inspections unnecessary if cracks are found. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Engine Overhaul)

Reciprocating Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: A positive displacement pump produces a continuous flow, but due to lack of a positive internal seal against slippage, its output will increase as pressure increases.
choices.B: In a positive displacement pump, slippage is substantial compared to the pump's volumetric output flow. If the output port were plugged, pressure would increase, and the pump pressure relief valve would close.
choices.C: In a positive displacement pump, slippage is negligible compared to the pump's volumetric output flow. If the output port were plugged, pressure would increase, and the pump pressure relief valve would open.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: In a positive displacement pump, the tight internal clearances mean slippage is negligible relative to total output; if the outlet is blocked, pressure builds until the pressure relief valve opens to protect the system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Fuel Systems)

Reciprocating Engines.question: What does the blue arc on a cylinder head temperature (CHT) gauge represent?

choices.A: The range within which operation is permitted in auto-lean.
choices.B: The desired operating range on a twin-engine aircraft when operating on one engine.
choices.C: The range within which operation must be in auto-rich.

Reciprocating Engines.explanation: The blue arc on a CHT gauge indicates the temperature range within which the mixture must be in auto-rich to prevent detonation and ensure adequate cylinder cooling during high heat conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Which condition would be the least likely to be caused by failed or failing engine bearings?

choices.B: High oil temperatures.
choices.C: Low oil temperatures.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Failed bearings typically cause elevated oil temperatures due to friction and increased oil consumption from oil escaping past damaged surfaces, but they would not cause abnormally low oil temperatures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: The floating control thermostat, used on some reciprocating engine installations, helps regulate oil temperature by

choices.A: controlling oil flow through the oil cooler.
choices.B: recirculating hot oil back through the sump.
choices.C: controlling air flow through the oil cooler.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The floating control thermostat regulates oil temperature by varying the proportion of oil routed through the oil cooler versus bypassing it, maintaining oil temperature within the desired operating range. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: If metallic particles are found in the oil filter during an inspection,

choices.A: it is an indication of normal engine wear unless the particles are nonferrous.
choices.B: the cause should be identified and corrected before the aircraft is released for flight.
choices.C: it is an indication of normal engine wear unless the deposit exceeds a specified amount.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Any metallic particles found in the oil filter are abnormal and may indicate internal engine damage; the source must be identified and corrected before flight to prevent catastrophic engine failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: If the oil pressure gauge fluctuates over a wide range from zero to normal operating pressure, the most likely cause is

choices.B: broken or weak pressure relief valve spring.
choices.C: air lock in the scavenge pump intake.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: A low oil supply causes the oil pump to intermittently pick up air along with oil, producing wide pressure fluctuations from zero to normal as the pump alternately moves oil and air through the system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: If the oil pressure of a cold engine is higher than at normal operating temperatures, the

choices.A: oil system relief valve should be readjusted.
choices.B: engine's lubrication system is probably operating normally.
choices.C: oil dilution system should be turned on immediately.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Cold oil is more viscous than warm oil, so it flows with greater resistance and produces higher pressure readings; as the engine warms to normal operating temperature, viscosity decreases and pressure drops to the normal range, indicating the system is functioning correctly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: If an engine operates with a low oil pressure and a high oil temperature, the problem may be caused by a

choices.B: sheared oil pump shaft.
choices.C: clogged oil cooler annular jacket.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: A sheared oil pump shaft means the pump cannot circulate oil, resulting in both low oil pressure (no pumping) and high oil temperature (no cooling flow through the system). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: What is the best indication of worn valve guides?

choices.B: Low compression.
choices.C: Low oil pressure.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Worn valve guides allow excessive oil to be drawn past the guides into the combustion chamber where it is burned, resulting in high oil consumption as the primary symptom. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: How may it be determined that a reciprocating engine with a dry sump is pre-oiled sufficiently?

choices.A: The engine oil pressure gauge will indicate normal oil pressure.
choices.B: Oil will flow from the engine return line or indicator port.
choices.C: When the quantity of oil specified by the manufacturer has been pumped into the engine.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Sufficient pre-oiling of a dry-sump engine is confirmed when oil flows from the engine return line or indicator port, demonstrating that oil has reached and filled the necessary passages and bearings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: What is the possible cause when a turbine engine indicates no change in power setting parameters, but oil temperature is high?

choices.B: Engine main bearing distress.
choices.C: Turbine damage and/or loss of turbine efficiency.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: When power parameters remain normal but oil temperature is elevated, the most likely cause is main bearing distress, because damaged bearings generate excessive heat through friction without affecting the gas path performance indicators. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Engine oil temperature gauges indicate the temperature of the oil

choices.B: entering the engine.
choices.C: in the oil storage tank.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Oil temperature gauges are positioned to sense oil entering the engine (at the pressure inlet), providing the most critical indication of the oil's condition before it lubricates engine components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: A common type of electrically operated oil temperature gauge utilizes

choices.A: either a wheatstone bridge or ratiometer circuit.
choices.B: a thermocouple type circuit.
choices.C: vapor pressure and pressure switches.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Electrically operated oil temperature gauges typically use a Wheatstone bridge or ratiometer circuit with a variable-resistance temperature bulb (thermistor), which changes resistance with temperature to drive the indicator. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Instruments that measure relatively high fluid pressures, such as oil pressure gauges, are usually what type?

choices.B: Bourdon tube.
choices.C: Diaphragm or bellows.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The Bourdon tube is the standard mechanism for measuring relatively high fluid pressures because its curved tube straightens proportionally under pressure, providing accurate readings across a wide high-pressure range. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: What will be the result of operating an engine in extremely high temperatures using a lubricant recommended by the manufacturer for a much lower temperature?

choices.A: The oil pressure will be higher than normal.
choices.B: The oil temperature and oil pressure will be higher than normal.
choices.C: The oil pressure will be lower than normal.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Oil recommended for lower temperatures has lower viscosity at high temperatures, causing it to thin excessively and reducing its ability to maintain adequate film pressure, resulting in lower-than-normal oil pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: An oil separator is generally associated with which of the following?

choices.B: Engine-driven vacuum pump.
choices.C: Cuno oil filter.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Engine-driven vacuum (dry-type) pumps use an oil separator to remove oil mist from the air/oil mixture discharged by the pump, preventing oil from entering the vacuum system or being vented overboard excessively. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: The time in seconds required for exactly 60 cubic centimeters of oil to flow through an accurately calibrated orifice at a specific temperature is recorded as a measurement of the oil's

choices.B: specific gravity.
choices.C: viscosity.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The Saybolt Universal Viscosity test measures the time in seconds for 60 cc of oil to flow through a calibrated orifice at a specific temperature, and that time value is the direct measure of the oil's viscosity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Upon what quality or characteristic of a lubricating oil is its viscosity index based?

choices.A: Its resistance to flow at a standard temperature as compared to high grade paraffin-base oil at the same temperature.
choices.B: Its rate of change in viscosity with temperature change.
choices.C: Its rate of flow through an orifice at a standard temperature.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The viscosity index (VI) is a numerical scale that expresses how much an oil's viscosity changes relative to temperature change; a higher VI means less change in viscosity as temperature varies. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Lubricating oils with high viscosity index ratings are oils

choices.A: in which the viscosity does not vary much with temperature change.
choices.B: in which the viscosity varies considerably with temperature change.
choices.C: which have high SAE numbers.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: A high viscosity index means the oil's viscosity remains relatively stable across a wide temperature range, so it does not thin out excessively at high temperatures or thicken excessively at low temperatures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: If all other requirements can be met, what type of oil should be used to achieve theoretically perfect engine lubrication?

choices.A: The thinnest oil that will stay in place and maintain a reasonable film strength.
choices.B: An oil that combines high viscosity and low demulsibility.
choices.C: An oil that combines a low viscosity index and a high neutralization number.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Theoretically perfect lubrication is achieved with the thinnest oil that can maintain adequate film strength and stay in place, because thin oil minimizes internal friction and power loss while still protecting bearing surfaces. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: The viscosity of a liquid is a measure of its

choices.B: rate of change.
choices.C: weight or density.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Viscosity is defined as a fluid's internal resistance to flow; a high-viscosity oil flows slowly, while a low-viscosity oil flows easily, and this property is fundamental to selecting the correct lubricant for a given engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: What type of oil system is usually found on turbine engines?

choices.B: Dry sump, dip, and splash.
choices.C: Wet sump, spray, and splash.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Turbine engines use dry sump systems because the engine is not designed to retain oil internally; oil is stored in an external tank and delivered under pressure, then sprayed onto bearings and gears before being scavenged back. This combination of dry sump, pressure delivery, and spray application is standard for turbine engine lubrication. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Which of the following has the greatest effect on the viscosity of lubricating oil?

choices.B: Engine RPM.
choices.C: Oil pressure.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Temperature has the greatest effect on oil viscosity because as temperature increases, oil becomes thinner (lower viscosity), and as temperature decreases, oil becomes thicker (higher viscosity), directly impacting its ability to lubricate effectively. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: The engine oil temperature regulator is usually located between which of the following on a dry sump reciprocating engine?

choices.A: The engine oil supply pump and the internal lubrication system.
choices.B: The scavenger pump outlet and the oil storage tank.
choices.C: The oil storage tank and the engine oil supply pump.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: On a dry sump reciprocating engine, the oil temperature regulator (oil cooler) is positioned between the oil storage tank and the engine supply pump so that oil temperature can be regulated before pressurized oil enters the engine's lubrication system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: What will happen to the return oil if the oil line between the scavenger pump and the oil cooler separates?

choices.A: Oil will accumulate in the engine.
choices.B: The return oil will be pumped overboard.
choices.C: The scavenger return line check valve will close and force the oil to bypass directly to the intake side of the pressure pump.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: If the line between the scavenger pump and the oil cooler separates, the scavenger pump will continue to operate and pump the return oil overboard through the broken line rather than returning it to the tank. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: In a reciprocating engine oil system, the temperature bulb senses oil temperature

choices.A: at a point after the oil has passed through the oil cooler.
choices.B: while the oil is in the hottest area of the engine.
choices.C: immediately before the oil enters the oil cooler.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The temperature bulb is positioned immediately before the oil enters the oil cooler so that it senses the hottest oil returning from the engine, providing an accurate reading of maximum oil temperature before cooling occurs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: What is the purpose of the last-chance oil filters?

choices.A: To prevent damage to the oil spray nozzle.
choices.B: To filter the oil immediately before it enters the main bearings.
choices.C: To assure a clean supply of oil to the lubrication system.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Last-chance filters are fine mesh screens located at the entrance of each main bearing oil passage to trap any remaining contaminants immediately before oil reaches the bearings, preventing damage from particles that bypassed earlier filtration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: What would be the probable result if the oil system pressure relief valve should stick in the open position on a turbine engine?

choices.B: Decreased oil temperature.
choices.C: Insufficient lubrication.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: If the pressure relief valve sticks open, oil pressure will be continuously relieved back to the inlet side of the pump, preventing adequate pressure from building up to deliver sufficient oil to engine components, resulting in insufficient lubrication and potential bearing failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: What unit in an aircraft engine lubrication system is adjusted to maintain the desired system pressure?

choices.B: Oil viscosity valve.
choices.C: Oil pump.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The oil pressure relief valve is the unit adjusted to maintain desired system pressure by controlling how much oil is bypassed back to the pump inlet when pressure exceeds the set value. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Low oil pressure can be detrimental to the internal engine components. However, high oil pressure

choices.A: should be limited to the engine manufacturer's recommendations.
choices.B: has a negligible effect.
choices.C: will not occur because of pressure losses around the bearings.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: High oil pressure can be just as damaging as low oil pressure by causing oil seal failures, burst lines, and over-pressurization of components, so it must be kept within the engine manufacturer's specified limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: In order to relieve excessive pump pressure in an engine's internal oil system, most engines are equipped with a

choices.B: bypass valve.
choices.C: relief valve.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: A relief valve is used to relieve excessive pump pressure in the internal oil system by allowing oil to bypass back to the inlet side of the pump when pressure exceeds the designed operating limit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Which of the following factors has the least effect on the oil consumption of a specific engine?

choices.B: Engine RPM.
choices.C: Lubricant characteristics.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Mechanical efficiency has the least effect on oil consumption compared to RPM and lubricant characteristics; oil consumption is primarily driven by how much oil is burned, lost past seals, or vaporized, which relates more directly to operating speed and oil properties. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Which of the following lubrication system components is never located between the pressure pump and the engine pressure system?

choices.B: Fuel line for oil dilution system.
choices.C: Check valve.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: A check valve is never placed between the pressure pump outlet and the engine pressure system because it could block oil flow to the bearings; check valves are used on scavenge or bypass lines, not in the main pressure delivery path. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: As an aid to cold-weather starting, the oil dilution system thins the oil with

choices.B: alcohol.
choices.C: gasoline.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The oil dilution system mixes gasoline (aviation fuel) with the engine oil before shutdown in cold weather, thinning the oil so the engine can be more easily started; the gasoline then evaporates after the engine warms up. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: The basic oil pressure relief valve setting for a newly overhauled engine is made

choices.A: within the first 30 seconds of engine operation.
choices.B: when the oil is at a higher than normal temperature to assure high oil pressure at normal oil temperature.
choices.C: in the overhaul shop.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The basic oil pressure relief valve setting is made in the overhaul shop during bench testing so that the engine arrives in the aircraft with a calibrated baseline setting that can be fine-tuned during initial run-up. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Where is the oil temperature bulb located on a dry sump reciprocating engine?

choices.B: Oil cooler.
choices.C: Oil outlet line.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: On a dry sump reciprocating engine, the oil temperature bulb is located in the oil inlet line (between the tank and the pump) to measure the temperature of oil entering the engine, which is the standard reference point for monitoring oil temperature. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: If an oil filter element becomes completely clogged, the

choices.A: oil flow to the engine will be restricted.
choices.B: oil will be bypassed back to the oil tank.
choices.C: bypass valve will open, and the oil pump will supply unfiltered oil.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: When an oil filter becomes completely clogged, the differential pressure across the filter opens the bypass valve, allowing the oil pump to continue supplying oil to the engine—albeit unfiltered—to prevent oil starvation. This safety mechanism ensures lubrication continues even with a failed filter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Wet sump oil systems are most commonly used in gas turbine engines.
choices.B: In most turbine engine oil tanks, a slight pressurization of the tank is not desired to ensure a positive flow of oil.
choices.C: Dry sump oil systems are most commonly used in gas turbine engines.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Gas turbine engines predominantly use dry sump lubrication systems because the engine design does not provide a suitable sump reservoir, and oil must be stored in a separate tank to handle the demands of turbine operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: The pumping capacity of the scavenger pump in a dry sump aircraft engine's lubrication system

choices.A: is greater than the capacity of the oil supply pump.
choices.B: is less than the capacity of the oil supply pump.
choices.C: is usually equal to the capacity of the oil supply pump in order to maintain constant oiling conditions.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The scavenger pump must have a greater pumping capacity than the pressure supply pump to ensure that oil and air/foam mixtures are efficiently returned from the engine sumps and do not accumulate, preventing oil pooling in the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: In which of the following situations will the oil cooler automatic bypass valve be open the greatest amount?

choices.A: Engine oil above normal operating temperature.
choices.B: Engine oil below normal operating temperature.
choices.C: Engine stopped with no oil flowing after runup.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: When engine oil is below normal operating temperature, it is too viscous and cold to benefit from cooling, so the automatic bypass valve opens to its greatest extent to route oil around the cooler core and allow it to warm up faster. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)
