Engine Lubrication Systems.question: What will result if an oil filter becomes completely blocked?

choices.A: Oil will flow at a reduced rate through the system.
choices.B: Oil flow to the engine will stop.
choices.C: Oil will flow at the normal rate through the system.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: A completely blocked oil filter causes the bypass valve to open fully, allowing oil to flow around the filter at the normal rate through the system, ensuring continuous lubrication at the expense of filtration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: A turbine engine dry sump lubrication system of the self-contained, high-pressure design

choices.B: consists of pressure, breather, and scavenge subsystems.
choices.C: stores oil in the engine crankcase.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: A turbine engine dry sump lubrication system of the self-contained, high-pressure design is divided into three subsystems—pressure, breather (vent), and scavenge—each performing a distinct function in supplying, venting, and returning oil throughout the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: An engine lubrication system pressure relief valve is usually located between the

choices.A: oil cooler and the scavenger pump.
choices.B: scavenger pump and the external oil system.
choices.C: pump and the internal oil system.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The pressure relief valve is located between the oil pump outlet and the internal oil system to limit maximum oil pressure delivered to the engine's internal components, protecting them from excessive pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: In a reciprocating engine, oil is directed from the pressure relief valve to the inlet side of the

choices.B: oil temperature regulator.
choices.C: pressure pump.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Oil relieved by the pressure relief valve is directed back to the inlet side of the pressure pump, creating a recirculation loop that limits system pressure without wasting oil back to the tank. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: If the oil in the oil cooler core and annular jacket becomes congealed, what unit prevents damage to the cooler?

choices.B: Airflow control valve.
choices.C: Surge protection valve.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: A surge protection (congealing relief) valve opens when congealed oil blocks the cooler core and annular jacket, bypassing the blocked cooler to prevent structural damage caused by excessive pressure buildup from the thickened oil. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: A drop in oil pressure may be caused by

choices.B: the bypass valve sticking open.
choices.C: foreign material under the relief valve.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Foreign material lodged under the pressure relief valve can hold it open or partially open, allowing oil to recirculate back to the pump inlet rather than being delivered to the engine at full pressure, resulting in a drop in oil pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: The main oil filters strain the oil at which point in the system?

choices.A: Immediately after it leaves the scavenger pump.
choices.B: Immediately before it enters the pressure pump.
choices.C: Just as it leaves the pressure pump.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: Main oil filters are positioned just downstream of the pressure pump so that oil is filtered immediately after being pressurized before it is distributed to the engine's internal components, removing contaminants at the most critical point. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Engine Lubrication Systems.question: The purpose of a dwell chamber in a turbine engine oil tank is to provide

choices.B: for a pressurized oil supply to the oil pump inlet.
choices.C: separation of entrained air from scavenged oil.

Engine Lubrication Systems.explanation: The dwell chamber in a turbine engine oil tank provides a residence time and space for entrained air bubbles to separate from the scavenged oil before it is recirculated, preventing aerated oil from being supplied to the pressure pump. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Propellers.question: What is the principal advantage of using propeller reduction gears?

choices.A: To enable the propeller RPM to be increased without an accompanying increase in engine RPM.
choices.B: To enable the engine RPM to be increased with an accompanying increase in power and allow the propeller to remain at a lower, more efficient RPM.
choices.C: To enable the engine RPM to be increased with an accompanying increase in propeller RPM.

Propellers.explanation: Reduction gears allow the engine to turn at a higher RPM—producing more power—while keeping propeller RPM at a lower, more aerodynamically efficient speed, since propeller tip speeds approaching or exceeding the speed of sound cause significant efficiency losses. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propellers)

Propellers.question: The actual power delivered to the propeller of an aircraft engine is called

choices.B: brake horsepower.
choices.C: indicated horsepower.

Propellers.explanation: Brake horsepower (BHP) is the actual usable power delivered to the propeller shaft as measured at the engine output, accounting for internal friction losses from indicated horsepower. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propellers)

Propellers.question: Which of the following would indicate a general weak-engine condition when operated with a fixed-pitch propeller or test club?

choices.A: Lower than normal static RPM, full throttle operation.
choices.B: Manifold pressure lower at idle RPM than at static RPM.
choices.C: Lower than normal manifold pressure for any given RPM.

Propellers.explanation: With a fixed-pitch propeller at full throttle, a lower-than-normal static RPM indicates the engine is not developing its rated power output, which is the standard indicator of a general weak-engine condition since the propeller load is fixed and RPM directly reflects engine performance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propellers)

Propellers.question: If the ignition switch is moved from BOTH to either LEFT or RIGHT during an engine ground check, normal operation is usually indicated by a slight

choices.B: bump in propeller RPM.
choices.C: drop in torque meter pressure indication.

Propellers.explanation: During a magneto check, switching from BOTH to a single magneto removes one ignition source, causing slightly less efficient combustion and a small reduction in power; this results in a slight drop in RPM, which the constant-speed propeller compensates for by reducing blade pitch to maintain RPM, thereby slightly increasing manifold pressure. The slight MAP increase is the normal indicator of the RPM governing response to reduced power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Governing and Engine Runup)

Propellers.question: What is the basic operational sequence for reducing the power output of an engine equipped with a constant-speed propeller?

choices.A: Reduce the RPM, then the manifold pressure.
choices.B: Reduce the manifold pressure, then retard the throttle to obtain the correct RPM.
choices.C: Reduce the manifold pressure, then the RPM.

Propellers.explanation: When reducing power with a constant-speed propeller, manifold pressure (throttle) is reduced first, then RPM (propeller control) is reduced to avoid over-square conditions that can cause engine damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propeller Operation)

Propellers.question: To reduce the power output of an engine equipped with a constant-speed propeller and operating near maximum BMEP, the

choices.A: manifold pressure is reduced with the throttle control before the RPM is reduced with the propeller control.
choices.B: manifold pressure is reduced with the propeller control before the RPM is reduced with the throttle control.
choices.C: RPM is reduced with the propeller control before the manifold pressure is reduced with the throttle control.

Propellers.explanation: Operating near maximum BMEP, reducing manifold pressure first with the throttle prevents an over-square condition, which could impose damaging loads on the engine; RPM is then reduced with the propeller control afterward. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propeller Operation)

Propellers.question: What is the function of the inlet guide vane assembly on an axial-flow compressor?

choices.A: Directs the air into the first stage rotor blades at the proper angle.
choices.B: Converts velocity energy into pressure energy.
choices.C: Converts pressure energy into velocity energy.

Propellers.explanation: Inlet guide vanes are stationary vanes positioned ahead of the first-stage rotor that direct incoming airflow at the correct angle of attack onto the rotor blades, optimizing compressor efficiency and preventing stall. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Propellers.question: The velocity of supersonic air as it flows through a divergent nozzle

choices.B: decreases.
choices.C: is inversely proportional to the temperature.

Propellers.explanation: For supersonic (greater than Mach 1) flow, a divergent nozzle acts as a supersonic nozzle where the increasing cross-sectional area causes the flow to accelerate further, unlike subsonic flow where a divergent shape decelerates the air. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Turbine Engine Inlet and Exhaust Systems)

Propellers.question: The pressure of supersonic air as it flows through a divergent nozzle

choices.B: decreases.
choices.C: is inversely proportional to the temperature.

Propellers.explanation: In supersonic flow through a divergent nozzle, as velocity increases the static pressure also increases due to the compressible flow relationships that govern supersonic conditions, which is the opposite behavior from subsonic flow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Turbine Engine Inlet and Exhaust Systems)

Propellers.question: Continued and/or excessive heat and centrifugal force on turbine engine rotor blades is likely to cause

choices.B: creep.
choices.C: galling.

Propellers.explanation: Creep is the slow, permanent elongation or deformation of turbine blades caused by sustained high temperatures combined with centrifugal stress, and is a primary life-limiting failure mode for turbine rotor blades. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Turbine Section)

Propellers.question: A Cessna 180 aircraft has a McCauley propeller Model No. 2A34C50/90A. The propeller is severely damaged in a ground accident, and this model propeller is not available for replacement. Which of the following should be used to find an approved alternate replacement?

choices.B: Aircraft Specifications/Type Certificate Data Sheets.
choices.C: Aircraft Engine and Propeller Specifications/Type Certificate Data Sheets.

Propellers.explanation: The Aircraft Type Certificate Data Sheet (TCDS) for the airframe lists all FAA-approved propeller models for that aircraft, making it the authoritative reference for selecting an approved alternate propeller replacement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Records and Certifications)

Propellers.question: Which of the following contains a table that lists the engines to which a given propeller is adaptable?

choices.B: Propeller Type Certificate Data Sheets.
choices.C: Engine Type Certificate Data Sheets.

Propellers.explanation: The Propeller Type Certificate Data Sheet lists the engines to which a given propeller is adaptable, along with installation limitations, blade models, and hub configurations approved for use with that propeller. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Records and Certifications)

Propellers.question: A ground incident that results in propeller sudden stoppage would require a crankshaft runout inspection. What publication would be used to obtain crankshaft runout tolerance?

choices.B: Type Certificate Data Sheet.
choices.C: AC 43.13-1, Acceptable Methods, Techniques, and Practices -- Aircraft Inspection and Repair.

Propellers.explanation: Crankshaft runout tolerances following propeller sudden stoppage must be obtained from the current manufacturer's maintenance instructions, as these specifications are engine-specific and may be updated by service bulletins or service instructions that supersede general advisory material. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Sudden Stoppage Inspection)

Propellers.question: Which statement is correct concerning a thermocouple-type temperature indicating instrument system?

choices.A: It is a balanced-type, variable resistor circuit.
choices.B: It requires no external power source.
choices.C: It usually contains a balancing circuit in the instrument case to prevent fluctuations of the system voltage from affecting the temperature reading.

Propellers.explanation: A thermocouple system generates its own small EMF (electromotive force) through the Seebeck effect at the junction of two dissimilar metals when heated, so it requires no external power source to operate. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Indicating Systems)

Propellers.question: A fire detection system operates on the principle of a buildup of gas pressure within a tube proportional to temperature. Which of the following systems does this statement define?

choices.B: Lindberg continuous-element system.
choices.C: Thermal switch system.

Propellers.explanation: The Lindberg (also called Fenwal) continuous-element system uses a tube filled with a material that releases gas as temperature rises, creating a pressure proportional to temperature that actuates the fire warning when a threshold is reached. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Fire Detection Systems)

Propellers.question: According to the electron theory of the flow of electricity, when a properly functioning DC alternator and voltage regulating system is charging an aircraft's battery, the direction of current flow through the battery

choices.A: is into the negative terminal and out the positive terminal.
choices.B: is into the positive terminal and out the negative terminal.
choices.C: cycles back and forth with the number of cycles per second being controlled by the rotational speed of the alternator.

Propellers.explanation: When charging a battery using conventional electron theory, current flows from the negative terminal of the charging source into the positive terminal of the battery, meaning electrons enter the positive terminal and exit the negative terminal of the battery during charging. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Propellers.question: The reason for flashing the field in a generator is to

choices.A: restore residual magnetism to the field frame.
choices.B: ensure proper operation of the capacitor.
choices.C: relieve the unit of any residual electrical energy.

Propellers.explanation: Flashing the field restores residual magnetism to the generator's field frame poles, which is necessary for the generator to begin producing voltage through self-excitation when residual magnetism has been lost. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — DC Generator Systems)

Propellers.question: The constant current method of charging a nickel-cadmium battery

choices.A: will bring it up to fully charged in the shortest amount of time.
choices.B: will lead to cell imbalance over a period of time.
choices.C: is the method most effective in maintaining cell balance.

Propellers.explanation: The constant current charging method applies a steady low-rate charge over an extended period, which allows each cell to reach full capacity individually and is the most effective method for maintaining cell balance in a nickel-cadmium battery. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Aircraft Battery Systems)

Propellers.question: The resistance of the current return path through the aircraft is always considered negligible, provided the

choices.A: voltage drop across the circuit is checked.
choices.B: generator is properly grounded.
choices.C: structure is adequately bonded.

Propellers.explanation: Adequate bonding ensures low-resistance electrical continuity throughout the airframe structure, allowing it to serve as an effective current return path; without proper bonding, resistance in the return path can cause voltage drops and system malfunctions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Aircraft Bonding and Shielding)

Propellers.question: Which of the following helps determine the proper grade of oil to use in a particular engine?

choices.A: Adequate lubrication in various attitudes of flight.
choices.B: Positive introduction of oil to the bearings.
choices.C: Operating speeds of bearings.

Propellers.explanation: The operating speeds of bearings directly influence viscosity requirements, because higher bearing speeds require oils with appropriate viscosity to maintain a stable lubricating film without breaking down or causing excessive drag. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Propellers.question: Why is expansion space required in an engine oil supply tank?

choices.B: For oil enlargement and collection of foam.
choices.C: For proper oil tank ventilation.

Propellers.explanation: Engine oil tanks require expansion space to accommodate the increase in oil volume as it heats up and to allow foam—created by oil churning in the engine—to collect and dissipate before oil is recirculated. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Lubrication Systems)

Propellers.question: What is the relationship between the pressure existing within the throat of a venturi and the velocity of the air passing through the venturi?

choices.A: There is no direct relationship between the pressure and the velocity.
choices.B: The pressure is directly proportional to the velocity.
choices.C: The pressure is inversely proportional to the velocity.

Propellers.explanation: Bernoulli's principle states that as the velocity of a fluid increases through a restriction such as a venturi throat, the static pressure decreases proportionally, making pressure and velocity inversely proportional. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Carburetion)

Propellers.question: Carburetor icing on an engine equipped with a constant-speed propeller can be detected by

choices.A: a decrease in power output with no change in manifold pressure or RPM.
choices.B: an increase in manifold pressure with a constant RPM.
choices.C: a decrease in manifold pressure with a constant RPM.

Propellers.explanation: On a constant-speed propeller installation, the governor maintains RPM by adjusting blade angle, so carburetor icing reduces manifold pressure (power) while RPM remains constant, unlike a fixed-pitch installation where RPM would also drop. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Induction Systems)

Propellers.question: What part of an aircraft in flight will begin to accumulate ice before any other?

choices.B: Propeller spinner or dome.
choices.C: Carburetor.

Propellers.explanation: The carburetor can accumulate ice (induction icing) even in above-freezing ambient temperatures due to the drop in temperature caused by fuel vaporization and the venturi effect, making it the first component to ice up in flight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Induction System Icing)

Propellers.question: What is the function of a blast tube as found on aircraft engines?

choices.A: A means of cooling the engine by utilizing the propeller backwash.
choices.B: A tube used to load a cartridge starter.
choices.C: A device to cool an engine accessory.

Propellers.explanation: A blast tube directs a stream of ram or propeller slipstream air directly onto an accessory (such as a magneto or generator) to provide cooling and prevent overheating in the engine compartment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 8 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Propellers.question: How is aircraft electrical power for propeller deicer systems transferred from the engine to the propeller hub assembly?

choices.A: By slip rings and segment plates.
choices.B: By slip rings and brushes.
choices.C: By flexible electrical connectors.

Propellers.explanation: Because the propeller rotates relative to the fixed airframe, electrical power must be transferred through stationary brushes that ride on rotating slip rings mounted on the propeller hub, maintaining continuous electrical contact. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Propeller Deicing and Anti-icing)

Propellers.question: How is anti-icing fluid ejected from the slinger ring on a propeller?

choices.B: By centripetal force.
choices.C: By centrifugal force.

Propellers.explanation: The slinger ring distributes anti-icing fluid outward along the propeller blades by centrifugal force generated by the rotation of the propeller, requiring no additional pump pressure to move the fluid toward the blade tips. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Propeller Anti-icing)

Propellers.question: On most reciprocating multiengine aircraft, automatic propeller synchronization is accomplished through the actuation of the

choices.B: propeller governors.
choices.C: propeller control levers.

Propellers.explanation: Automatic synchronization systems compare RPM signals between engines and make small corrections through the propeller governors to match speeds, reducing the beat-frequency vibration caused by propellers turning at slightly different RPMs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Propeller Synchronizing)

Propellers.question: Propeller fluid anti-icing systems generally use which of the following?

choices.B: Isopropyl alcohol.
choices.C: Ethyl alcohol.

Propellers.explanation: Isopropyl alcohol is the standard fluid used in propeller fluid anti-icing systems because it effectively lowers the freezing point of water, is compatible with aircraft materials, and readily spreads across the blade surface. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Propeller Anti-icing)

Propellers.question: What is a function of the automatic propeller synchronizing system on multiengine aircraft?

choices.A: To control the tip speed of all propellers.
choices.B: To control engine RPM and reduce vibration.
choices.C: To control the power output of all engines.

Propellers.explanation: Automatic synchronization systems match the RPM of all engines to a master engine, which eliminates the annoying and fatiguing beat-frequency vibration that occurs when propellers turn at slightly different speeds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Propeller Synchronizing)

Propellers.question: Ice formation on a propeller blade will

choices.B: increase thrust and drag.
choices.C: cause a change in blade angle.

Propellers.explanation: Ice accumulates unevenly on propeller blades, creating asymmetric mass distribution that produces unbalance and resultant vibration, which can damage engine mounts and airframe structures if not corrected. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Propeller Deicing and Anti-icing)

Propellers.question: What unit in the propeller anti-icing system controls the output of the pump?

choices.B: Rheostat.
choices.C: Cycling timer.

Propellers.explanation: The pressure relief valve regulates and limits pump output pressure in the propeller anti-icing system, ensuring a consistent and controlled flow of fluid to the slinger ring regardless of pump speed variations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Propeller Anti-icing)

Propellers.question: Proper operation of electric deicing boots on individual propeller blades may best be determined by

choices.A: feeling the sequence of boot heating and have an assistant observe the loadmeter indications.
choices.B: observing the ammeter or loadmeter for current flow.
choices.C: feeling the boots to see if they are heating.

Propellers.explanation: Observing the ammeter or loadmeter for the expected current draw is the most reliable method because it confirms that each boot element is actually receiving electrical current; feeling the boots is unreliable due to the short heating cycles and safety hazards near rotating components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Propeller Deicing Systems)

Propellers.question: A propeller synchrophasing system allows a pilot to reduce noise and vibration by

choices.A: adjusting the phase angle between the propellers on an aircraft's engines.
choices.B: adjusting the plane of rotation of all propellers.
choices.C: setting the pitch angle of all propellers exactly the same.

Propellers.explanation: Synchrophasing systems control the rotational phase angle between propellers on multi-engine aircraft, aligning blade positions relative to each other to cancel out noise and vibration harmonics. This is distinct from pitch synchronization, which only matches RPM. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Synchronization and Synchrophasing)

Propellers.question: Which of the following determines oil and grease specifications for lubrication of propellers?

choices.B: Engine manufacturer's instructions.
choices.C: Propeller manufacturer's instructions.

Propellers.explanation: The propeller manufacturer specifies the correct lubricants for their product because grease and oil requirements are determined by the propeller's internal design, materials, and tolerances. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Lubrication)

Propellers.question: Grease used in aircraft propellers reduces the frictional resistance of moving parts and is easily molded into any form under pressure. This statement defines

choices.A: antifriction and plasticity characteristics of grease.
choices.B: antifriction and chemical stability of grease.
choices.C: viscosity and melting point of grease.

Propellers.explanation: Antifriction describes the grease's ability to reduce frictional resistance between moving parts, and plasticity describes its ability to be deformed or molded under pressure without returning to its original shape. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Lubrication)

Propellers.question: What type of imbalance will cause a two-blade propeller to have a persistent tendency to come to rest in a horizontal position (with the blades parallel to the ground) while being checked on a propeller balancing beam?

choices.B: Horizontal.
choices.C: Harmonic.

Propellers.explanation: Horizontal imbalance means one blade is heavier than the other along the blade-to-blade (horizontal) axis, causing the propeller to persistently rotate until the heavier blade is at the bottom, which on a two-blade propeller results in a horizontal resting position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Balance)

Propellers.question: What is the purpose of an arbor used in balancing a propeller?

choices.A: To support the propeller on the balance knives.
choices.B: To level the balance stand.
choices.C: To mark the propeller blades where weights are to be attached.

Propellers.explanation: An arbor is a precision mandrel inserted through the propeller hub bore so the propeller can rest on the balance stand's knife edges, allowing free rotation to reveal any imbalance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Balance)

Propellers.question: If a blade of a particular metal propeller is shortened because of damage to the tip, the remaining blade(s) must be

choices.A: reset (blade angle) to compensate for the shortened blade.
choices.B: returned to the manufacturer for alteration.
choices.C: reduced to conform with the shortened blade.

Propellers.explanation: When a blade tip is damaged and must be shortened, all remaining blades must be reduced to the same length to restore propeller balance and symmetry, as mismatched blade lengths create aerodynamic and static imbalance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Metal Propeller Repair)

Propellers.question: The application of more protective coating on one blade than the other when refinishing a wood propeller

choices.A: has little or no effect on operating characteristics.
choices.B: should never be done.
choices.C: may be necessary to achieve final balancing.

Propellers.explanation: Differential application of protective coatings (such as varnish) between blades adds weight selectively and is an accepted method for achieving final static balance on a wood propeller. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Wood Propeller Repair and Refinishing)

Propellers.question: Apparent engine roughness is often a result of propeller unbalance. The effect of an unbalanced propeller will usually be

choices.A: approximately the same at all speeds.
choices.B: greater at low RPM.
choices.C: greater at high RPM.

Propellers.explanation: The vibratory forces produced by an out-of-balance propeller increase with the square of rotational speed, so unbalance effects are significantly greater at high RPM than at low RPM. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Balance)

Propellers.question: Which of the following is used to correct horizontal unbalance of a wood propeller?

choices.B: Shellac.
choices.C: Solder.

Propellers.explanation: Solder is applied to the lighter blade tip of a wood propeller to add small, precise amounts of weight to correct horizontal imbalance between the two blades. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Wood Propeller Balance)

Propellers.question: Propeller aerodynamic (thrust) imbalance can be largely eliminated by

choices.A: correct blade contouring and angle setting.
choices.B: static balancing.
choices.C: keeping the propeller blades within the same plane of rotation.

Propellers.explanation: Aerodynamic (thrust) imbalance results from blades producing unequal thrust, which is corrected by ensuring all blades have identical contour profiles and are set to the same blade angle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Balance)

Propellers.question: A powerplant using a hydraulically controlled constant-speed propeller is operating within the propeller's constant-speed range at a fixed throttle setting. If the tension of the propeller governor control spring (speeder spring) is reduced by movement of the flight deck propeller control, the propeller blade angle will

choices.A: increase, engine manifold pressure will increase, and engine RPM will decrease.
choices.B: decrease, engine manifold pressure will increase, and engine RPM will decrease.
choices.C: decrease, engine manifold pressure will decrease, and engine RPM will increase.

Propellers.explanation: Reducing speeder spring tension lowers the governor's reference RPM set point, causing the flyweights to sense overspeed and direct oil to decrease blade angle (toward low pitch); the lower pitch reduces the load on the engine, manifold pressure rises, but RPM decreases to the new lower governor set point. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Constant-Speed Propeller Governor Operation)

Propellers.question: Why is the pulley stop screw on a propeller governor adjustable?

choices.A: To limit the maximum engine speed during takeoff.
choices.B: To maintain the proper blade angle for cruising.
choices.C: To limit the maximum propeller pitch for takeoff.

Propellers.explanation: The pulley stop screw limits the travel of the governor control arm, which in turn limits the maximum RPM the governor will allow, ensuring the engine does not exceed its rated takeoff speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Governor)

Propellers.question: During engine operation at speeds lower than those for which the constant-speed propeller control can govern in the INCREASE RPM position, the propeller will

choices.A: remain in the full HIGH PITCH position.
choices.B: maintain engine RPM in the normal manner until the HIGH PITCH stop is reached.
choices.C: remain in the full LOW PITCH position.

Propellers.explanation: When engine speed is below the governor's controllable range with the control in the full INCREASE RPM (low pitch) position, oil pressure or the absence of governing action holds the blades at the full low pitch (minimum blade angle) stop. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Constant-Speed Propeller Operation)

Propellers.question: When engine power is increased, the constant-speed propeller tries to function so that it will

choices.A: maintain the RPM, decrease the blade angle, and maintain a low angle of attack.
choices.B: increase the RPM, decrease the blade angle, and maintain a low angle of attack.
choices.C: maintain the RPM, increase the blade angle, and maintain a low angle of attack.

Propellers.explanation: When power is increased, the governor senses an overspeed tendency and increases blade angle (coarsens pitch) to absorb the additional power while maintaining the selected RPM and keeping the blade angle of attack low and efficient. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Constant-Speed Propeller Operation)

Propellers.question: The propeller governor controls the

choices.A: oil to and from the pitch changing mechanism.
choices.B: spring tension on the boost pump speeder spring.
choices.C: linkage and counterweights from moving in and out.

Propellers.explanation: The propeller governor meters engine oil pressure to and from the propeller's pitch-changing mechanism (piston/cylinder), thereby controlling blade angle to maintain the selected RPM. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Governor)

Propellers.question: During the on-speed condition of a propeller, the

choices.A: centrifugal force acting on the governor flyweights is greater than the tension of the speeder spring.
choices.B: tension on the speeder spring is less than the centrifugal force acting on the governor flyweights.
choices.C: centrifugal force of the governor flyweights is equal to the speeder spring force.

Propellers.explanation: During the on-speed condition, the governor flyweights are in equilibrium with the speeder spring, meaning centrifugal force equals spring tension, so the pilot valve remains centered and no oil is directed to change blade angle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Governor Operation)

Propellers.question: What actuates the pilot valve in the governor of a constant-speed propeller?

choices.B: Governor flyweights.
choices.C: Governor pump oil pressure.

Propellers.explanation: The governor flyweights, responding to changes in engine RPM, move outward or inward and mechanically reposition the pilot valve to direct oil pressure toward or away from the propeller pitch-change mechanism. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Governor Operation)

Propellers.question: What action takes place when the flight deck control lever for a hydromatic, constant-speed propeller is actuated?

choices.A: Compression of the speeder spring is changed.
choices.B: The governor booster pump pressure is varied.
choices.C: The governor bypass valve is positioned to direct oil pressure to the propeller dome.

Propellers.explanation: Moving the cockpit propeller control lever changes the compression of the speeder spring, which alters the RPM at which the governor flyweights reach equilibrium, thereby selecting a new governed speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Governor Operation)

Propellers.question: What will happen to the propeller blade angle and the engine RPM if the tension on the propeller governor control spring (speeder spring) is increased?

choices.A: Blade angle will decrease and RPM will decrease.
choices.B: Blade angle will increase and RPM will decrease.
choices.C: Blade angle will decrease and RPM will increase.

Propellers.explanation: Increasing speeder spring tension raises the RPM required for flyweights to overcome it, so the governor adds oil to decrease blade angle (lower pitch), which allows RPM to rise to the new higher governed speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Governor Operation)

Propellers.question: How does the propeller overspeed governor on a turboprop engine decrease propeller RPM?

choices.A: When oil pressure is decreased, the return spring and counterweights force the oil out of the servo piston.
choices.B: As oil pressure increases, the servo piston is pushed forward, and the feather spring is compressed.
choices.C: When oil pressure is increased, the return spring and counterweights force the oil out of the servo piston.

Propellers.explanation: When the overspeed governor detects excessive RPM, it decreases oil pressure to the servo piston, allowing the return spring and blade counterweights to move the blades toward a higher pitch, reducing RPM. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turboprop Propeller Control Systems)

Propellers.question: How is the speed of a constant-speed propeller changed in flight?

choices.A: By varying the output of the governor booster pump.
choices.B: By advancing or retarding the throttle.
choices.C: By changing the load tension against the flyweights in the governor.

Propellers.explanation: The pilot selects a desired RPM by repositioning the propeller control lever, which changes the tension on the governor speeder spring (the load against the flyweights), causing the governor to adjust blade angle to maintain that new speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Governor Operation)

Propellers.question: When the centrifugal force acting on the propeller governor flyweights overcomes the tension on the speeder spring, a propeller is in what speed condition?

choices.B: Underspeed.
choices.C: Overspeed.

Propellers.explanation: When flyweight centrifugal force exceeds speeder spring tension, the pilot valve moves to direct oil in a direction that increases blade angle, reducing RPM — this is the overspeed condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Governor Operation)

Propellers.question: What operational force causes the greatest stress on a propeller?

choices.B: Centrifugal force.
choices.C: Thrust bending force.

Propellers.explanation: Centrifugal force, caused by the rotation of the propeller, produces the greatest stress on the blades and hub, far exceeding the magnitude of aerodynamic or thrust-related forces. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Forces)

Propellers.question: What operational force tends to increase propeller blade angle?

choices.B: Aerodynamic twisting force.
choices.C: Thrust bending force.

Propellers.explanation: Centrifugal twisting moment acts through the center of mass of each blade, which is forward of the blade's pitch-change axis, tending to rotate the blade toward a higher (coarser) blade angle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Forces)

Propellers.question: How is a propeller controlled in a large aircraft with a turboprop installation?

choices.B: By varying the engine RPM except for feathering and reversing.
choices.C: By the engine power lever.

Propellers.explanation: In turboprop installations, propeller speed and blade angle are controlled independently of engine power through a dedicated propeller control system, allowing separate management of thrust and engine torque. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turboprop Propeller Control Systems)

Propellers.question: How does the aerodynamic twisting force affect operating propeller blades?

choices.A: It tends to turn the blades to a high blade angle.
choices.B: It tends to bend the blades forward.
choices.C: It tends to turn the blades to a low blade angle.

Propellers.explanation: The aerodynamic twisting moment acts along the chord of the blade, and because the center of pressure is ahead of the pitch-change axis, lift forces tend to rotate the blade toward a lower (finer) blade angle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Forces)

Propellers.question: Which of the following best describes the blade movement of a propeller that is in the high RPM position when reversing action is begun?

choices.A: Low pitch directly to reverse pitch.
choices.B: Low pitch through high pitch to reverse pitch.
choices.C: Low pitch through feather position to reverse pitch.

Propellers.explanation: When a propeller is already at the high RPM (low pitch) position, reversing action moves the blades directly from low pitch through flat pitch to reverse pitch without passing through high pitch or feather. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reversing Propellers)

Propellers.question: Propellers exposed to salt spray should be flushed with

choices.B: fresh water.
choices.C: soapy water.

Propellers.explanation: FAA guidance specifies flushing propellers exposed to salt spray with fresh water to remove corrosive salt deposits before they can cause pitting or corrosion damage to the blade surface. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Inspection and Maintenance)

Propellers.question: How can a steel propeller hub be tested for cracks?

choices.B: By magnetic particle inspection.
choices.C: By etching.

Propellers.explanation: Steel propeller hubs are ferromagnetic and therefore suitable for magnetic particle inspection, which reliably detects surface and near-surface cracks that could lead to catastrophic failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Inspection and Maintenance)

Propellers.question: Which of the following functions requires the use of a propeller blade station?

choices.B: Indexing blades.
choices.C: Propeller balancing.

Propellers.explanation: Blade angle is measured at a specific radial distance from the center of the propeller hub known as the blade station, making station identification essential for accurate and repeatable angle measurements. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Propeller Blade Angle)

Propellers.question: The propeller blade angle is defined as the acute angle between the airfoil section chord line (at the blade reference station) and which of the following?

choices.B: The relative wind.
choices.C: The axis of blade rotation during pitch change.

Propellers.explanation: Blade angle (pitch angle) is defined as the acute angle between the chord line of the blade airfoil section at the reference station and the propeller's plane of rotation, not the relative wind (which defines angle of attack). This is the foundational geometric definition of propeller blade angle per FAA standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: During which of the following conditions of flight will the blade pitch angle of a constant-speed propeller be the greatest?

choices.B: Climb following takeoff.
choices.C: High-speed, high-altitude cruising flight.

Propellers.explanation: At high-speed, high-altitude cruise, a constant-speed propeller governor commands the greatest blade pitch angle to absorb the available power efficiently at reduced air density and higher true airspeed, keeping RPM constant. Lower-power conditions such as approach or climb require lesser pitch angles. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: The actual distance a propeller moves forward through the air during one revolution is known as the

choices.B: geometric pitch.
choices.C: relative pitch.

Propellers.explanation: Effective pitch is the actual distance a propeller advances through the air in one revolution, accounting for slip, whereas geometric pitch is the theoretical distance based solely on blade angle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: The pitch-changing mechanism of the hydromatic propeller is lubricated by

choices.B: using an approved-type grease in a grease gun at intervals prescribed by the propeller manufacturer.
choices.C: thoroughly greasing, necessary only during propeller overhaul.

Propellers.explanation: In the Hydromatic propeller, the pitch-changing mechanism is lubricated by the same oil used to actuate the pitch-change piston, eliminating the need for separate greasing of internal components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Hydromatic Propellers)

Propellers.question: What is the result of moving the throttle on a reciprocating engine when the propeller is in the constant-speed range with the engine developing cruise power?

choices.A: Opening the throttle will cause an increase in blade angle.
choices.B: The RPM will vary directly with any movement of the throttle.
choices.C: Movement of the throttle will not affect the blade angle.

Propellers.explanation: Within the constant-speed range, advancing the throttle increases manifold pressure and engine torque tendency, causing the governor to increase blade angle (higher pitch) to maintain the selected RPM rather than allowing RPM to rise. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: Propeller blade stations are measured from the

choices.A: index mark on the blade shank.
choices.B: hub centerline.
choices.C: blade base.

Propellers.explanation: Propeller blade stations are measured in inches from the centerline of the hub (center of rotation) outward along the blade, providing a standardized reference for blade angle and thickness measurements. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: The thrust produced by a rotating propeller is a result of

choices.A: an area of low pressure behind the propeller blades.
choices.B: an area of decreased pressure immediately in front of the propeller blades.
choices.C: the angle of relative wind and rotational velocity of the propeller.

Propellers.explanation: A rotating propeller blade generates thrust by creating a region of decreased (low) pressure immediately in front of (ahead of) the blade faces, analogous to the low-pressure upper surface of a wing generating lift. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: Why is a constant-speed flyweight propeller normally placed in full HIGH PITCH position before the engine is stopped?

choices.A: To prevent exposure and corrosion of the pitch changing mechanism.
choices.B: To prevent hydraulic lock of the piston when the oil cools.
choices.C: To prevent overheating of the engine during the next start.

Propellers.explanation: Placing the propeller in full high pitch (low RPM) before shutdown protects the internal pitch-changing mechanism and seals from exposure to moisture and corrosion, as the dome and actuating parts are retracted to their most covered position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: The low pitch stop on a constant-speed propeller is usually set so that

choices.A: the engine will turn at its rated takeoff RPM at sea level when the throttle is opened to allowable takeoff manifold pressure.
choices.B: maximum allowable engine RPM cannot be exceeded with any combination of manifold pressure, altitude, or forward speed.
choices.C: the limiting engine manifold pressure cannot be exceeded with any combination of throttle opening, altitude, or forward speed.

Propellers.explanation: The low pitch stop is set so that at full allowable takeoff manifold pressure at sea level, the engine reaches its rated takeoff RPM, preventing the engine from overspeeding when the blades are at their minimum angle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: The angle-of-attack of a rotating propeller blade is measured between the blade chord or face and which of the following?

choices.B: Full low-pitch blade angle.
choices.C: Relative airstream.

Propellers.explanation: Angle of attack is always measured between the chord line (or face) of the airfoil and the relative airstream (relative wind), which for a propeller blade is the vector sum of rotational velocity and aircraft forward velocity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: The centrifugal twisting moment of an operating propeller tends to

choices.B: reduce the pitch angle.
choices.C: bend the blades in the direction of rotation.

Propellers.explanation: Centrifugal twisting moment acts on each blade element's center of mass, which lies ahead of the pitch-change axis, tending to rotate the blade toward flat pitch (reduced pitch angle) during operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Forces)

Propellers.question: Which of the following is identified as the cambered or curved side of a propeller blade, corresponding to the upper surface of a wing airfoil section?

choices.B: Blade chord.
choices.C: Blade face.

Propellers.explanation: The blade back is the cambered (curved) side of the propeller blade that corresponds to the upper surface of a wing and faces forward in the direction of flight, producing the low-pressure thrust face. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: Which of the following best describes the blade movement of a full-feathering, constant-speed propeller that is in the LOW RPM position when the feathering action is begun?

choices.A: High pitch through low pitch to feather position.
choices.B: High pitch directly to feather position.
choices.C: Low pitch through high pitch to feather position.

Propellers.explanation: When feathering is initiated from the low RPM (already high pitch) position, the blades move directly from high pitch to the feather position without first passing through low pitch, since they are already near the feather end of travel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Feathering Propellers)

Propellers.question: The holding coil on a hydromatic propeller feathering button switch holds a solenoid relay closed that applies power to the propeller

choices.B: dome feathering mechanism.
choices.C: feathering pump motor.

Propellers.explanation: In the Hydromatic feathering system, the holding coil keeps the solenoid relay energized, which maintains power to the feathering pump motor so it continues pumping high-pressure oil to drive the blades to feather even after the pilot releases the feathering button. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Hydromatic Propellers)

Propellers.question: What is the primary purpose of the metal tipping which covers the blade tips and extends along the leading edge of each wood propeller blade?

choices.A: To increase the lateral strength of the blade.
choices.B: To prevent impact damage to the tip and leading edge of the blade.
choices.C: To increase the longitudinal strength of the blade.

Propellers.explanation: Metal tipping is installed on wood propeller blade tips and leading edges primarily to protect against impact damage from rain, debris, and normal operational wear, which would otherwise erode or split the wood. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Wood Propellers)

Propellers.question: Blade angle is an angle formed by a line perpendicular to the crankshaft and a line formed by the

choices.B: chord of the blade.
choices.C: blade face.

Propellers.explanation: Blade angle is defined as the angle between the chord line of the propeller blade and the plane of rotation (a line perpendicular to the crankshaft), making the chord of the blade the correct reference line. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: Propeller blade station numbers increase from

choices.B: tip to hub center line.
choices.C: blade shank butt to tip.

Propellers.explanation: Propeller blade stations are measured in inches from the center of the hub outward toward the tip, so station numbers increase from hub centerline to tip. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: The aerodynamic force acting on a rotating propeller blade operating at a normal pitch angle tends to

choices.B: increase the pitch angle.
choices.C: bend the blades rearward in the line of flight.

Propellers.explanation: Aerodynamic twisting force (ATF) acts on a rotating blade to increase the pitch angle because the center of pressure of the blade is ahead of the blade's pitch-change axis, creating a moment that twists the blade toward higher pitch. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Forces)

Propellers.question: Which of the following forces or combination of forces operates to move the blades of a constant-speed flyweight propeller to the HIGH PITCH position?

choices.A: Engine oil pressure acting on the propeller piston-cylinder arrangement and centrifugal force acting on the flyweights.
choices.B: Centrifugal force acting on the flyweights.
choices.C: Prop governor oil pressure acting on the propeller piston-cylinder arrangement.

Propellers.explanation: In a constant-speed flyweight propeller, centrifugal force acting on the flyweights is the force that drives the blades toward high pitch (low RPM), while governor oil pressure opposes this by driving blades toward low pitch. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: The purpose of permanently sealing and partially filling some models of McCauley propeller hubs with dyed oil is to

choices.A: provide an always clean separate lubrication of the internal parts.
choices.B: dampen pressure surges and prevent too rapid changes in propeller blade angle.
choices.C: make the location of cracks readily apparent.

Propellers.explanation: McCauley hubs are partially filled with dyed oil so that any crack developing in the hub will cause the colored oil to seep out, making cracks readily visible during inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Inspection and Maintenance)

Propellers.question: Which of the following best describes the blade movement of a feathering propeller that is in the HIGH RPM position when the feathering action is begun?

choices.A: High pitch through low pitch to feather position.
choices.B: Low pitch through reverse pitch to feather position.
choices.C: Low pitch through high pitch to feather position.

Propellers.explanation: When a feathering propeller is in the high RPM (low pitch) position and feathering is initiated, the blades must travel from low pitch through increasing pitch angles to the full feather position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Feathering Propellers)

Propellers.question: The blade angle of a fixed-pitch propeller

choices.B: is smallest at the tip.
choices.C: increases in proportion to the distance each section is from the hub.

Propellers.explanation: Because the tip of a fixed-pitch propeller travels a greater distance (higher velocity) than sections near the hub, a smaller blade angle at the tip is required to maintain a consistent angle of attack along the blade, resulting in a progressive decrease in blade angle from hub to tip. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: During operational check of an aircraft using hydromatic full-feathering propellers, the following observations are made: The feather button, after being pushed, remains depressed until the feather cycle is complete, then opens. When unfeathering, it is necessary to manually hold the button down until unfeathering is accomplished.

choices.A: Both feather cycle and unfeather cycle are functioning properly.
choices.B: Both feather and unfeather cycles indicate malfunctions.
choices.C: The feather cycle is correct. The unfeather cycle indicates a malfunction.

Propellers.explanation: On hydromatic full-feathering propellers, the feather button is designed to automatically pop out upon completion of the feather cycle, which is normal; however, during unfeathering the button should also release automatically once unfeathering is complete—requiring the pilot to manually hold it indicates a malfunction in the unfeather cycle only. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Feathering Propellers)

Propellers.question: Inspection of aluminum propeller blades by dye-penetrant inspection is accomplished to detect

choices.B: material de-bond.
choices.C: warpage.

Propellers.explanation: Dye-penetrant inspection is a surface crack detection method used to reveal fatigue cracks in aluminum propeller blades that may not be visible to the naked eye. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Inspection and Maintenance)

Propellers.question: What controls the constant-speed range of a constant-speed propeller?

choices.B: Angle of climb and descent with accompanying changes in airspeed.
choices.C: The mechanical limits in the propeller pitch range.

Propellers.explanation: The constant-speed range of a propeller is limited by the mechanical high and low pitch stops built into the propeller; once the blades reach either stop, the propeller can no longer maintain constant speed and acts as a fixed-pitch propeller. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: For takeoff, a constant-speed propeller is normally set in the

choices.B: HIGH PITCH, low RPM position.
choices.C: LOW PITCH, high RPM position.

Propellers.explanation: For takeoff, maximum power is required, so the propeller is set to low pitch (fine pitch) which allows the engine to turn at high RPM and develop maximum thrust. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: Where are the high and low pitch stops of a Hamilton Standard constant-speed or two-position flyweight propeller located?

choices.A: In the hub and blade assembly.
choices.B: In the flyweight assembly.
choices.C: In the dome assembly.

Propellers.explanation: On Hamilton Standard constant-speed and two-position flyweight propellers, the high and low pitch stops are located in the hub and blade assembly, physically limiting the range of blade angle travel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: Which of the following statements about constant-speed flyweight propellers is also true when referring to two-position flyweight propellers?

choices.A: Blade angle changes are accomplished by the use of two forces, one hydraulic and the other centrifugal.
choices.B: Since an infinite number of blade angle positions are possible during flight, propeller efficiency is greatly improved.
choices.C: The pilot selects the RPM and the propeller changes pitch to maintain the selected RPM.

Propellers.explanation: Both two-position and constant-speed flyweight propellers use two opposing forces to change blade angle: hydraulic (oil) pressure and centrifugal force acting on the flyweights, which is the fundamental operating principle shared by both designs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: Most engine-propeller combinations have one or more critical ranges within which continuous operation is not permitted. Critical ranges are established to avoid

choices.B: low or negative thrust conditions.
choices.C: inefficient propeller pitch angles.

Propellers.explanation: Critical RPM ranges are established to prevent resonant vibration frequencies that can cause severe structural damage to the propeller and engine; continuous operation in these ranges is prohibited. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Vibration)

Propellers.question: Which of the following defects is cause for rejection of wood propellers?

choices.A: Solder missing from screw heads securing metal tipping.
choices.B: An oversize hub or bolt hole, or elongated bolt holes.
choices.C: No protective coating on propeller.

Propellers.explanation: Oversize, elongated, or out-of-round hub or bolt holes compromise the structural integrity of the propeller attachment and are cause for rejection because they cannot safely transfer loads between the propeller and crankshaft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Wood Propeller Inspection and Repair)

Propellers.question: Which of the following defects is cause for rejection of wood propellers?

choices.B: Bonding or separation of the trailing edge of the propeller blade.
choices.C: Delamination found by conducting a tap test.

Propellers.explanation: Delamination detected by tap testing indicates internal separation of wood laminations, which is a structural defect requiring rejection of the propeller. Wood propellers must be free of delamination, and the tap test is the accepted method to identify this condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Wood Propellers)

Propellers.question: An aircraft's propeller system beta range

choices.A: is used to produce zero or negative thrust.
choices.B: is used to achieve maximum thrust during takeoff.
choices.C: refers to the most fuel efficient pitch range to use at a given engine RPM.

Propellers.explanation: The beta range refers to propeller blade pitch positions from flat pitch through reverse pitch, which produce zero or negative (reverse) thrust and are used primarily for ground maneuvering and deceleration after landing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Turboprop Propeller Systems)

Propellers.question: The primary purpose of a cuff on a propeller is to

choices.B: strengthen the propeller.
choices.C: increase the flow of cooling air to the engine nacelle.

Propellers.explanation: Propeller cuffs are airfoil-shaped fairings fitted to the shank of propeller blades that increase airflow velocity into the engine nacelle, thereby improving engine cooling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Components)

Propellers.question: The purpose of a three-way propeller valve is to

choices.A: direct oil from the engine oil system to the propeller cylinder.
choices.B: direct oil from the engine through the governor to the propeller.
choices.C: permit constant-speed operation of the propeller.

Propellers.explanation: A three-way propeller valve directs engine oil pressure to the propeller cylinder to change blade pitch, and also allows oil to return to the engine sump, making it the controlling element for oil-actuated propeller systems independent of the governor. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Governor and Controls)

Propellers.question: The primary purpose of a propeller is to

choices.A: create lift on the fixed airfoils of an aircraft.
choices.B: change engine horsepower to thrust.
choices.C: provide static and dynamic stability of an aircraft in flight.

Propellers.explanation: A propeller converts the rotating shaft power (horsepower) produced by the engine into thrust force, which propels the aircraft through the air. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: A constant-speed propeller provides maximum efficiency by

choices.A: increasing blade pitch as the aircraft speed decreases.
choices.B: adjusting blade angle for most conditions encountered in flight.
choices.C: increasing the lift coefficient of the blade.

Propellers.explanation: A constant-speed propeller maintains maximum efficiency by continuously adjusting blade angle to maintain the optimum angle of attack for the existing flight condition, keeping the engine at its most efficient RPM across a wide range of airspeeds and power settings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: The centrifugal twisting force acting on a propeller blade is

choices.A: greater than the aerodynamic twisting force and tends to move the blade to a higher angle.
choices.B: less than the aerodynamic twisting force and tends to move the blade to a lower angle.
choices.C: greater than the aerodynamic twisting force and tends to move the blade to a lower angle.

Propellers.explanation: Centrifugal twisting moment acts on the mass of the rotating blade and tends to rotate it toward flat (low) pitch, and this force exceeds the aerodynamic twisting moment, which tends to increase blade angle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Forces)

Propellers.question: Geometric pitch of a propeller is defined as the

choices.B: effective pitch plus slippage.
choices.C: angle between the blade chord and the plane of rotation.

Propellers.explanation: Geometric pitch is the theoretical distance a propeller would advance in one revolution with no slippage, while effective pitch is the actual distance advanced; therefore geometric pitch equals effective pitch plus the slippage that occurs in practice. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: Propeller blade angle is the angle between the

choices.A: chord of the blade and the relative wind.
choices.B: relative wind and the rotational plane of the propeller.
choices.C: chord of the blade and the rotational plane of the propeller.

Propellers.explanation: Blade angle (pitch angle) is defined as the acute angle between the chord line of the propeller blade and the plane of rotation (the plane perpendicular to the propeller shaft). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: What operational force causes propeller blade tips to lag in the opposite direction of rotation?

choices.B: Aerodynamic-twisting force.
choices.C: Torque-bending force.

Propellers.explanation: Torque-bending force is the aerodynamic drag acting on the rotating blades that causes the blade tips to lag behind the hub in the direction opposite to rotation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Forces)

Propellers.question: What operational force tends to bend the propeller blades forward at the tip?

choices.B: Centrifugal-twisting force.
choices.C: Thrust-bending force.

Propellers.explanation: Thrust-bending force is the thrust load acting on the propeller blades during operation, which tends to bend the blade tips forward in the direction of flight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Forces)

Propellers.question: What are the rotational speed and blade pitch angle requirements of a constant-speed propeller during takeoff?

choices.B: High speed and low-pitch angle.
choices.C: High speed and high-pitch angle.

Propellers.explanation: During takeoff, maximum engine power requires high RPM (high speed), and a low blade pitch angle is needed to maintain that RPM and produce maximum thrust at low forward airspeed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: A mechanic certificate with a powerplant rating authorizes the holder to repair deep scars, nicks, and dents on aluminum propeller blades.
choices.B: A mechanic certificate with a powerplant rating authorizes the holder to perform minor straightening of steel propeller blades.
choices.C: A mechanic certificate with a powerplant rating does not authorize the holder to perform major repairs on aluminum propeller blades.

Propellers.explanation: Major repairs to propellers, such as straightening blades or repairing deep damage, require FAA approval and are typically performed by certificated propeller repair stations; an A&P with a powerplant rating is limited to minor repairs only. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Maintenance and Repair)

Propellers.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: During takeoff, propeller thrust (pull) is greatest if the blade angle of attack is low and the engine power setting is high.
choices.B: With the aircraft stationary, propeller thrust is greatest if the blade angle of attack is high and the engine power setting is high.
choices.C: During takeoff, propeller thrust (pull) is greatest if the blade angle of attack is high and the engine power setting is low.

Propellers.explanation: With the aircraft stationary (static condition), thrust is maximized when blade angle of attack is high enough to generate maximum air mass acceleration combined with high power, since there is no forward velocity to reduce the effective angle of attack. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Fundamentals)

Propellers.question: Longitudinal (fore and aft) clearance of constant-speed propeller blades or cuffs must be at least 1/2 inch (12.7 mm) between propeller parts and stationary parts of the aircraft. This clearance is with the propeller blades

choices.B: feathered or in the most critical pitch configuration.
choices.C: at the lowest pitch angle.

Propellers.explanation: Clearance requirements must be verified with the propeller in the most critical pitch configuration (feathered or whichever angle brings parts closest to the airframe) to ensure adequate clearance under all operating conditions, per FAA regulations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Installation and Clearances)

Propellers.question: Constant-speed non-feathering McCauley, Hartzell, and other propellers of similar design without flyweights increase pitch angle using

choices.B: spring pressure.
choices.C: centrifugal twisting moment.

Propellers.explanation: In constant-speed non-feathering propellers without flyweights (such as McCauley and Hartzell designs), governor-directed oil pressure is directed into the propeller hub to move the blades toward high pitch (increased blade angle), while springs and/or counterweights return blades to low pitch when oil pressure is reduced. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: Flyweights on constant-speed propellers are generally used to aid in

choices.B: decreasing blade angle.
choices.C: unfeathering the propellers.

Propellers.explanation: Flyweights on constant-speed propellers are driven by centrifugal force as RPM increases, and their mechanical action assists in moving the blades toward a higher (increased) blade angle, working in conjunction with or against oil pressure depending on the system design. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Constant-Speed Propellers)

Propellers.question: When lubricating a Hartzell propeller blade with grease, to prevent damage to the blade seals, the service manual may recommend on some models to

choices.A: pump grease into both zerk fittings for the blade simultaneously.
choices.B: remove the seals prior to greasing and reinstall them afterwards.
choices.C: remove one of the two zerk fittings for the blade and grease the blade through the remaining fitting.

Propellers.explanation: On some Hartzell propeller models, removing one of the two zerk fittings per blade before greasing allows displaced air and excess grease to escape, preventing excessive pressure buildup that could damage the blade seals. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Lubrication)

Propellers.question: When lubricating a newly overhauled steel hub propeller, the aviation mechanic should lubricate the propeller

choices.B: at the next 100-hour inspection.
choices.C: after one to two hours of operation.

Propellers.explanation: After overhaul, a newly assembled steel hub propeller should be lubricated after one to two hours of operation because initial operation allows the components to seat and grease to redistribute, requiring early relubrication to ensure proper coverage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Lubrication)

Propellers.question: The primary purpose of a feathering propeller is to

choices.A: prevent further engine damage when an engine fails in flight.
choices.B: prevent propeller damage when an engine fails in flight.
choices.C: eliminate the drag created by a windmilling propeller when an engine fails in flight.

Propellers.explanation: Feathering a propeller streamlines the blades parallel to the airflow, which eliminates the significant parasite drag produced by a windmilling propeller after engine failure, thereby improving aircraft performance and glide capability. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Feathering Propellers)

Propellers.question: What normally prevents a Hartzell Compact propeller from going to feather when the engine is shut down on the ground?

choices.B: A latch mechanism composed of springs and lock pins.
choices.C: Accumulator provided oil pressure.

Propellers.explanation: Hartzell Compact feathering propellers incorporate a latch mechanism consisting of springs and lock pins that engage at low RPM (such as during ground shutdown) to hold the blades in a low-pitch position and prevent unintended feathering. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Hartzell Compact Propeller)

Propellers.question: When running-up an engine and testing a newly installed hydromatic propeller, it is necessary to exercise the propeller by moving the governor control through its entire travel several times to

choices.A: seat the blades fully against the low pitch stop.
choices.B: free the dome of any entrapped air.
choices.C: test the maximum RPM setting of the governor.

Propellers.explanation: Cycling a newly installed hydromatic propeller through its full governor travel several times circulates oil through the dome assembly and purges any entrapped air, which if left in the system could cause erratic or spongy propeller operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Hydromatic Propellers)

Propellers.question: Which of the following occurs to cause front cone bottoming during propeller installation?

choices.A: The front cone becomes bottomed in the front propeller hub cone seat before the rear propeller hub cone seat has engaged the rear cone.
choices.B: The front cone enters the front propeller hub cone seat at an angle causing the propeller retaining nut to appear tight when it is only partially tightened.
choices.C: The front cone contacts the ends of the shaft splines, preventing the front and rear cones from being tightened against the cone seats in the propeller hub.

Propellers.explanation: Front cone bottoming occurs when the front cone contacts the ends of the crankshaft splines rather than seating in the propeller hub cone seat, which prevents proper clamping of both cones and means the propeller is not correctly secured on the shaft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Installation on Splined Shafts)

Propellers.question: What is indicated when the front cone bottoms while installing a propeller?

choices.B: Blade angles are incorrect.
choices.C: Rear cone should be moved forward.

Propellers.explanation: When the front cone bottoms on the crankshaft spline ends, the rear cone has not fully engaged its hub seat; moving the rear cone forward on the shaft corrects the spacing so both cones can properly seat in their respective hub cone seats. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Installation on Splined Shafts)

Propellers.question: How is the oil pressure delivery on a hydromatic propeller normally stopped after the blades have reached their full-feathered position?

choices.A: Pulling out the feathering push button.
choices.B: Electric cutout pressure switch.
choices.C: Stop lugs in the teeth of the rotating cam.

Propellers.explanation: A pressure cutout switch automatically senses when the blades have reached the full-feathered position by detecting the resulting rise in oil pressure and de-energizes the feathering pump motor to stop further oil delivery. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Hydromatic Feathering Propellers)

Propellers.question: The primary purpose of the front and rear cones for propellers that are installed on splined shafts is to

choices.A: position the propeller hub on the splined shaft.
choices.B: prevent metal-to-metal contact between the propeller and the splined shaft.
choices.C: reduce stresses between the splines of the propeller and the splines of the shaft.

Propellers.explanation: The front and rear cones serve as centering devices that precisely locate and position the propeller hub concentrically on the splined crankshaft, ensuring proper alignment and load distribution. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Installation on Splined Shafts)

Propellers.question: Which of the following statements concerning the installation of a new fixed-pitch wood propeller is true?

choices.A: Inspect the bolts for proper torque after every 50 hours and annual inspection.
choices.B: Install and tighten the bolts to the proper torque during installation; no inspection interval after that.
choices.C: Inspect the bolts for proper torque after the first flight and after the first 25 hours of flying.

Propellers.explanation: For a newly installed fixed-pitch wood propeller, FAA guidance requires a torque check after the first flight and again after the first 25 hours of operation because wood can compress and settle under load, causing bolt torque to decrease. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Fixed-Pitch Wood Propeller Installation)

Propellers.question: If propeller cones or hub cone seats show evidence of galling and wear, the most likely cause is

choices.A: the pitch change stops were located incorrectly, causing the cone seats to act as the high pitch stop.
choices.B: the propeller retaining nut was not tight enough during previous operation.
choices.C: the front cone was not fully bottomed against the crankshaft splines during installation.

Propellers.explanation: Galling and wear on propeller cones or hub cone seats is most commonly caused by a loose propeller retaining nut, which allows slight movement between the cone and seat surfaces during operation, producing fretting and material transfer. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Propeller Installation on Splined Shafts)

Propellers.question: On aircraft equipped with hydraulically operated constant-speed propellers, all ignition and magneto checking is done with the propeller in which position?

choices.B: LOW RPM.
choices.C: High pitch range.

Propellers.explanation: Magneto and ignition checks on aircraft with hydraulically operated constant-speed propellers are performed at high RPM (low pitch) so the propeller is governing and engine RPM remains stable and comparable to fixed-pitch runup procedures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Constant-Speed Propeller Operation)

Propellers.question: Oil leakage around the rear cone of a hydromatic propeller usually indicates a defective

choices.B: spider-shaft oil seal.
choices.C: dome-barrel oil seal.

Propellers.explanation: Oil leakage at the rear cone area of a hydromatic propeller typically indicates a failed spider-shaft oil seal, which is the seal located between the engine shaft (spider) and the propeller assembly at that aft junction point. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Hydromatic Propeller Maintenance)

Propellers.question: Maximum taper contact between crankshaft and propeller hub is determined by using

choices.B: a micrometer.
choices.C: a surface gauge.

Propellers.explanation: Prussian blue (bearing blue) is applied to one mating surface, and when the parts are assembled and rotated, the transfer pattern reveals the actual contact area between the crankshaft taper and the propeller hub, identifying high and low spots that cannot be detected by dimensional measurement alone. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Installation and Removal)

Propellers.question: Propeller blade tracking is the process of determining

choices.A: the plane of rotation of the propeller with respect to the aircraft longitudinal axis.
choices.B: that the blade angles are within the specified tolerance of each other.
choices.C: the positions of the tips of the propeller blades relative to each other.

Propellers.explanation: Blade tracking is performed by measuring the tip position of each blade relative to a fixed reference point, confirming that all blade tips rotate in essentially the same plane and are within the allowable tolerance of each other. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Inspection and Maintenance)

Propellers.question: What is the basic purpose of the three small holes (No. 60 drill) in the tipping of wood propeller blades?

choices.A: To provide a means for inserting balancing shot when necessary.
choices.B: To provide a means for periodically impregnating the blade with preservation materials.
choices.C: To allow the moisture which may collect between the tipping and the wood to escape (vent the tipping).

Propellers.explanation: The small vent holes in the metal tipping of wood propeller blades allow any moisture that migrates between the metal tipping and the wood to escape, preventing delamination and deterioration of the bond. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 3 — Wood Propellers)

Propellers.question: A fixed-pitch wooden propeller that has been properly installed and the attachment bolts properly torqued exceeds the out-of-track allowance by 1/16 inch. The excessive out-of-track condition may be corrected by

choices.A: slightly overtightening the attachment bolts adjacent to the most forward blade.
choices.B: discarding the propeller since out-of-track conditions cannot be corrected.
choices.C: placing shims between the inner flange and the propeller.
