Propellers.explanation: When a properly torqued wood propeller is out of track, thin shims placed between the propeller hub and the mounting flange on the side of the blade that is farthest forward will bring the blades back into the allowable tracking tolerance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Installation and Removal)

Propellers.question: Manually feathering a hydromechanical propeller means to

choices.A: block governor oil pressure to the cylinder of the propeller.
choices.B: port governor oil pressure to the cylinder of the propeller.
choices.C: port governor oil pressure from the cylinder of the propeller.

Propellers.explanation: To manually feather a hydromechanical propeller, oil pressure from the governor is ported (directed) into the propeller cylinder, driving the blades toward the high-pitch feathered position against the counterweights or feathering spring. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Feathering Propellers)

Propellers.question: In what position is the constant-speed propeller control placed to check the magnetos?

choices.A: Full decrease, low propeller blade pitch angle.
choices.B: Full increase, high propeller blade pitch angle.
choices.C: Full increase, low propeller blade pitch angle.

Propellers.explanation: During a magneto check, the propeller control is placed at full increase RPM (full low pitch), which allows the engine to develop maximum RPM so that any drop caused by a faulty magneto is clearly detectable on the tachometer. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Inspection and Maintenance)

Propellers.question: If a flanged propeller shaft has dowel pins

choices.A: install the propeller so the blades are positioned for hand propping.
choices.B: the propeller can only be installed in a given position.
choices.C: the front cone should be checked for bottoming against the pins.

Propellers.explanation: Dowel pins on a flanged propeller shaft are a positive-locating feature that physically allow the propeller to be mounted in only one specific rotational position, ensuring correct blade indexing relative to the crankshaft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Installation and Removal)

Propellers.question: Repairs of aluminum alloy adjustable pitch propellers are not permitted to be made on which of the following propeller blade areas?

choices.B: Face.
choices.C: Back.

Propellers.explanation: The blade shank is a highly stressed area that is not approved for field repairs on aluminum alloy adjustable-pitch propeller blades; any damage in this region requires removal from service and return to the manufacturer or an approved propeller repair station. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Repair and Alterations)

Propellers.question: Which of the following methods is used to straighten a bent aluminum propeller blade that is within repairable limits?

choices.A: Careful heating to accomplish straightening, followed by heat treatment to restore original strength.
choices.B: Either hot or cold straightening, depending on the location and severity of damage.
choices.C: Never straighten a damaged propeller.

Propellers.explanation: FAA guidance permits straightening bent aluminum propeller blades using either hot or cold methods depending on the extent and location of the bend, provided the damage is within repairable limits and the work is performed by an approved facility. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Repair and Alterations)

Propellers.question: It is important that nicks in aluminum alloy propeller blades be repaired as soon as possible in order to

choices.A: maintain equal aerodynamic characteristics between the blades.
choices.B: eliminate stress concentration points.
choices.C: equalize the centrifugal loads between the blades.

Propellers.explanation: Nicks create stress concentration points where fatigue cracks can initiate and propagate under the high cyclic loads experienced by a rotating propeller blade, making prompt repair essential to prevent catastrophic failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Repair and Alterations)

Propellers.question: Generally, unless otherwise specified by the manufacturer, repairs of nicks, scratches, gouges, etc., on aluminum propeller blades must be made

choices.A: parallel to the length of the blade.
choices.B: perpendicular to the blade axis.
choices.C: so as to return the damaged area to the original dimensions.

Propellers.explanation: Repairs to nicks, scratches, and gouges on aluminum blades must be filed and blended parallel to the length (span) of the blade so that the resulting contour does not create a stress riser perpendicular to the centrifugal and bending loads that act along the blade. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Repair and Alterations)

Propellers.question: Minor surface damage located in a repairable area, but not on the leading or trailing edges of aluminum blades, may be repaired by first

choices.B: filing with a half round or flat file.
choices.C: rough sanding and applying a proper filler.

Propellers.explanation: Minor surface damage in repairable areas away from the edges is first dressed with a half-round or flat file to remove the damaged material and blend the area smoothly before final finishing, as this provides the controlled metal removal needed without introducing additional stress risers. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Repair and Alterations)

Propellers.question: After proper removal of aluminum blade damage, the affected surface should be polished with

choices.B: very fine sandpaper or crocus cloth.
choices.C: soapstone or wire brush.

Propellers.explanation: After filing to remove damage, the affected surface is polished with very fine sandpaper or crocus cloth to restore a smooth finish that minimizes stress concentrations and provides a proper base for corrosion-protective coatings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Repair and Alterations)

Propellers.question: When preparing a propeller blade for inspection it should be cleaned with

choices.B: steel wool.
choices.C: methyl ethyl ketone.

Propellers.explanation: Mild soap and water is the recommended cleaning agent for propeller blades because it effectively removes dirt and grease without attacking the aluminum surface or degrading any protective coatings, whereas solvents or abrasives can cause corrosion or surface damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Inspection and Maintenance)

Propellers.question: What method would be used to inspect an aluminum propeller blade when a crack is suspected?

choices.B: Magnetic particle.
choices.C: Dye-penetrant.

Propellers.explanation: Aluminum is a non-ferrous material and cannot be inspected by magnetic particle methods; dye-penetrant inspection is the correct non-destructive testing method for detecting surface cracks in aluminum propeller blades. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Inspection and Maintenance)

Propellers.question: Removal of propeller blade tips within Type Certificate Data Sheet limits when correcting a defect is

choices.B: a major repair.
choices.C: permitted under the privileges and limitations of a powerplant rating.

Propellers.explanation: Removing blade tips within TCDS limits is classified as a repair action permissible under a powerplant rating, not a major alteration or major repair, because it falls within manufacturer-approved limits and does not change the design basis of the propeller. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Maintenance)

Propellers.question: Surface treatment to counter the effects of dye-penetrant inspection on a propeller is accomplished by

choices.B: wiping with alcohol.
choices.C: rinse the blade in alodine solution.

Propellers.explanation: After dye-penetrant inspection, rinsing the blade in alodine solution both removes residual penetrant chemicals and restores the corrosion-protective oxide layer on the aluminum surface. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Maintenance)

Propellers.question: One of the advantages of inspecting an aluminum propeller utilizing dye-penetrant inspection procedure is that

choices.A: defects just below the surface are indicated.
choices.B: it shows whether visible lines and other marks are actually cracks rather than scratches.
choices.C: it indicates overspeed condition.

Propellers.explanation: Dye-penetrant inspection works by drawing liquid penetrant into surface-opening discontinuities, then revealing them with a developer, which confirms whether a visible mark is a true crack (open to the surface) rather than a harmless scratch. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Maintenance)

Propellers.question: The primary reason for careful inspection and prompt repairing of minor surface defects such as scratches, nicks, gouges, etc. on aluminum alloy propellers is to prevent

choices.B: unbalanced aerodynamics.
choices.C: fatigue failure.

Propellers.explanation: Surface defects such as nicks and scratches act as stress concentration points that initiate fatigue cracks under the cyclic loading experienced by propeller blades, making prompt repair critical to preventing catastrophic fatigue failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Maintenance)

Propellers.question: Which of the following generally renders an aluminum alloy propeller unrepairable?

choices.B: Leading or trailing edge damage.
choices.C: Transverse cracks of any size.

Propellers.explanation: Transverse cracks (running across the blade) are oriented perpendicular to the primary centrifugal and bending loads, making them extremely prone to propagation and blade separation, so any transverse crack of any size renders an aluminum propeller unrepairable. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Maintenance)

Propellers.question: Cold straightening a bent aluminum propeller blade may be accomplished by

choices.A: the holder of a mechanic certificate with a powerplant rating.
choices.B: an appropriately rated repair station or the manufacturer.
choices.C: a person working under the supervision of the holder of a mechanic certificate with both airframe and powerplant ratings.

Propellers.explanation: Cold straightening a bent aluminum propeller blade is considered a major repair requiring specialized equipment and expertise, and therefore may only be performed by an appropriately rated repair station or the original manufacturer. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Maintenance)

Propellers.question: On twin engine aircraft, the propeller autofeather system is activated when

choices.A: rudder forces increase beyond a set value during an engine-out condition.
choices.B: a propeller overspeed condition is detected.
choices.C: engine power loss results in a propeller thrust drop to a preset value.

Propellers.explanation: The autofeather system monitors engine torque or power output, and when a drop to a preset low-thrust value is detected—indicating engine failure—it automatically feathers the affected propeller to reduce drag before the pilot can react. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Propeller Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of a thrust bearing used in most radial engines?

choices.B: Double-row ball.
choices.C: Deep-groove ball.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Radial engines use a double-row ball bearing as the thrust bearing because this design can absorb the large thrust loads generated by the propeller in both the forward and rearward directions while handling radial loads simultaneously. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the purpose of the safety circlet installed on some valve stems?

choices.A: To hold the valve guide in position.
choices.B: To hold the valve spring retaining washer in position.
choices.C: To prevent valves from falling into the combustion chamber.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A safety circlet (snap ring) is installed in a groove on the valve stem below the spring retainer to prevent the valve from dropping into the combustion chamber if the keeper or retainer fails. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following engine servicing operations generally requires engine pre-oiling prior to starting the engine?

choices.B: Engine installation.
choices.C: Replacement of oil lines.

Turbine Engines.explanation: After engine installation, all oil passages and the new or dry oil system must be pre-oiled before initial start to ensure bearings and other critical components receive lubrication immediately upon startup and are not damaged by momentary oil starvation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: At what point in an axial-flow turbojet engine will the highest gas pressures occur?

choices.B: Within the burner section.
choices.C: At the compressor outlet.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In an axial-flow turbojet, air is progressively compressed through each compressor stage so that peak pressure is reached at the compressor outlet (diffuser exit), just before entering the combustion section where fuel is added and pressure begins to drop. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: One function of the nozzle diaphragm in a turbine engine is to

choices.A: Decrease the velocity of exhaust gases.
choices.B: Center the fuel spray in the combustion chamber.
choices.C: Direct the flow of gases to strike the turbine blades at the desired angle.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The nozzle diaphragm (turbine nozzle) converts a portion of the combustion gas pressure into velocity and directs that high-velocity gas at the precise angle needed to strike the first-stage turbine blades efficiently for maximum energy extraction. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the profile of a turbine engine compressor blade?

choices.A: The leading edge of the blade.
choices.B: A reduced blade tip thickness.
choices.C: The curvature of the blade root.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The profile of a compressor blade refers to the blade tip thickness, which is intentionally reduced (knife-edged) to minimize tip clearance losses and improve aerodynamic efficiency at the blade tip. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: The fan rotational speed of a dual axial compressor forward fan engine is the same as the

choices.B: forward turbine wheel.
choices.C: high-pressure compressor.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In a dual-spool (axial) forward fan engine, the fan is mechanically coupled to and driven by the low-pressure (N1) compressor spool, so both rotate at the same speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: The abbreviation Pt7 used in turbine engine terminology means

choices.B: pressure and temperature at station 7.
choices.C: the total pressure at station 7.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In turbine engine station notation, 'P' denotes total pressure, 't' denotes total (stagnation) condition, and the numeral identifies the engine station; therefore Pt7 means total pressure at station 7. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: The blending of blades and vanes in a turbine engine

choices.A: is usually accomplished only at engine overhaul.
choices.B: should be performed parallel to the length of the blade using smooth contours to minimize stress points.
choices.C: may sometimes be accomplished with the engine installed, ordinarily using power tools.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Blade blending must be performed parallel to the blade length using smooth, gradual contours to avoid stress concentrations that could lead to crack propagation and blade failure. Hand tools are preferred over power tools, and the work can sometimes be done on-wing depending on damage limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Engine Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: What turbine engine section provides for proper mixing of the fuel and air?

choices.B: Compressor section.
choices.C: Diffuser section.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The combustion section is where fuel is introduced and mixed with the compressed air from the compressor, and combustion takes place to release heat energy. The diffuser and compressor sections deal with air pressure and compression, not fuel-air mixing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: In a gas turbine engine, combustion occurs at a constant

choices.B: pressure.
choices.C: density.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Gas turbine engines operate on the Brayton cycle, in which combustion occurs at constant pressure, unlike reciprocating engines which combust at constant volume. This constant-pressure combustion is a defining thermodynamic characteristic of the gas turbine cycle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Gas Turbine Engine Fundamentals)

Turbine Engines.question: Which statement is true regarding jet engines?

choices.A: At the lower engine speeds, thrust increases rapidly with small increases in RPM.
choices.B: At the higher engine speeds, thrust increases rapidly with small increases in RPM.
choices.C: The thrust delivered per pound of air consumed is less at high altitude than at low altitude.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Jet engine thrust is proportional to the square of RPM, so at higher engine speeds, small increases in RPM produce disproportionately large increases in thrust due to this non-linear relationship. At lower RPM ranges, the same RPM increment yields comparatively little additional thrust. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Gas Turbine Engine Fundamentals)

Turbine Engines.question: Some high-volume turboprop and turbojet engines are equipped with two-spool or split compressors. When these engines are operated at high altitudes, the

choices.A: low-pressure rotor will increase in speed as the compressor load decreases in the lower density air.
choices.B: throttle must be retarded to prevent overspeeding of the high-pressure rotor due to the lower density air.
choices.C: low-pressure rotor will decrease in speed as the compressor load decreases in the lower density air.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In a twin-spool engine at high altitude, the lower air density reduces the aerodynamic load on the low-pressure (N1) compressor, allowing it to speed up since the turbine driving it is no longer working against the same resistance. The high-pressure spool self-regulates through its own turbine stage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: Turbine nozzle diaphragms located on the upstream side of each turbine wheel, are used in the gas turbine engine to

choices.A: decrease the velocity of the heated gases flowing past this point.
choices.B: direct the flow of gases parallel to the vertical line of the turbine blades.
choices.C: increase the velocity of the heated gases flowing past this point.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine nozzle diaphragms (nozzle guide vanes) are convergent in shape and act as nozzles to accelerate and direct the hot combustion gases onto the turbine rotor blades at the correct angle, converting pressure energy into velocity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: Where is the highest gas pressure in a turbojet engine?

choices.A: At the outlet of the tailpipe section.
choices.B: At the entrance of the turbine section.
choices.C: In the entrance of the burner section.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The highest gas pressure in a turbojet engine occurs at the outlet of the compressor and entrance to the burner (combustion) section, after the air has been fully compressed but before energy is added through combustion. Beyond this point, energy is converted to velocity rather than additional pressure rise. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Gas Turbine Engine Fundamentals)

Turbine Engines.question: An exhaust cone placed aft of the turbine in a jet engine will cause the pressure in the first part of the exhaust duct to

choices.A: increase and the velocity to decrease.
choices.B: increase and the velocity to increase.
choices.C: decrease and the velocity to increase.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The exhaust cone creates a divergent duct in the forward portion of the exhaust section, which decelerates the gas flow, causing velocity to decrease and static pressure to increase in accordance with Bernoulli's principle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the function of the stator vane assembly at the discharge end of a typical axial-flow compressor?

choices.A: To straighten airflow to eliminate turbulence.
choices.B: To direct the flow of gases into the combustion chambers.
choices.C: To increase air swirling motion into the combustion chambers.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The stator vanes at the discharge end of an axial-flow compressor (sometimes called straightening vanes) remove the swirl imparted by the last compressor rotor stage, straightening the airflow to reduce turbulence before it enters the combustion section. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: The turbine section of a jet engine

choices.A: increases air velocity to generate thrust forces.
choices.B: utilizes heat energy to expand and accelerate the incoming gas flow.
choices.C: drives the compressor section.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The primary function of the turbine section is to extract sufficient energy from the hot gas stream to drive the engine's compressor (and accessories), with the remaining energy used to produce thrust or shaft power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: In the dual axial-flow or twin spool compressor system, the first stage turbine drives the

choices.B: N(2) compressor.
choices.C: N(1) compressor.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In a dual-spool axial compressor, the first-stage (high-pressure) turbine directly drives the high-pressure (N2) compressor, while a separate low-pressure turbine stage drives the N1 compressor, keeping the two spools mechanically independent. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following may be used to mark turbine engine components exposed to high temperatures?

choices.B: Layout dye.
choices.C: Graphite lead pencil.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Layout dye is acceptable for marking turbine engine hot-section components because it does not introduce harmful materials into the metal. Graphite (carbon) pencils and grease or wax pencils are prohibited because their residues can cause intergranular corrosion or sulfidation in high-temperature alloys. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Engine Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: What are the two basic elements of the turbine section in a turbine engine?

choices.B: Hot and cold.
choices.C: Stator and rotor.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The turbine section consists of a stator (nozzle guide vanes) that directs and accelerates gas flow, and a rotor (turbine wheel and blades) that extracts energy from that flow to drive the compressor. These two elements work together in every turbine stage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following acts as a diffuser in a turbine engine and converts velocity to pressure?

choices.B: Manifold.
choices.C: Stators.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Stator vanes in the compressor act as diffusers between rotor stages by converting the high-velocity air leaving each rotor into higher static pressure, following the principle that a diverging passage reduces velocity and increases pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: What are the two functional elements in a centrifugal compressor?

choices.B: Bucket and expander.
choices.C: Impeller and diffuser.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A centrifugal compressor has two functional elements: the impeller, which imparts kinetic energy and centrifugal force to the air, and the diffuser, which converts that velocity into increased static pressure. Both elements are essential to the compression process. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Design and Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: What must be done after the fuel control unit has been replaced on an aircraft gas turbine engine?

choices.A: Perform a full power engine run to check fuel flow.
choices.B: Recalibrate the fuel nozzles.
choices.C: Retrim the engine.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Replacing the fuel control unit alters the fuel metering characteristics of the engine, so the engine must be retrimmed to restore the correct thrust output and ensure proper fuel scheduling at all power settings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 8 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: A turbine engine compressor which contains vanes on both sides of the impeller is a

choices.B: double entry axial-flow compressor.
choices.C: single entry axial-flow compressor.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A double entry centrifugal compressor has vanes (inducer vanes) on both sides of the impeller, allowing air to enter from both sides to increase airflow capacity while keeping the compressor diameter manageable. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Centrifugal Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: Some engine manufacturers of twin spool gas turbine engines identify turbine discharge pressure in their maintenance manuals as

choices.B: Pt2.
choices.C: Tt7.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In twin-spool turbine engines, the station numbering convention designates turbine discharge (exhaust) total pressure as Pt7, with 'P' indicating total pressure and the subscript '7' identifying the exhaust discharge station. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Pressure Ratio and Instrumentation)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding the basic gas turbine engine?

choices.A: The engine is divided into three sections: cold, neutral, and hot sections.
choices.B: The cold section includes the engine inlet, diffuser, and compressor sections.
choices.C: The hot section includes the combustor, diffuser, and exhaust sections.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The hot section of a gas turbine engine encompasses the combustor (burner section), the diffuser that directs high-pressure air into the combustor, and the exhaust section where hot gases are expelled, as these are all areas exposed to combustion temperatures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Gas Turbine Engine Construction)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding welding and straightening of turbine engine rotating airfoils?

choices.B: Neither procedure is typically authorized by the engine manufacturer.
choices.C: These procedures are commonly recommended by the manufacturer.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine engine rotating airfoils such as compressor and turbine blades operate under extreme stress and temperature conditions, so welding and straightening are generally not authorized by engine manufacturers because such repairs could introduce structural weaknesses or imbalance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Inspection and Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: Turbine engine components exposed to high temperatures generally may not be marked with

choices.A: layout dye, commercial felt tip marker, or chalk.
choices.B: wax, grease pencil, or a graphite lead pencil.
choices.C: commercial felt tip marker or grease pencil.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Materials such as wax, grease pencil, or graphite lead pencil contain carbon or sulfur compounds that can cause intergranular corrosion and stress corrosion cracking in high-temperature turbine alloys when subjected to heat, making them prohibited for marking hot-section components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Inspection and Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: Who establishes mandatory replacement times for critical components of turbine engines?

choices.B: The component manufacturer.
choices.C: The engine manufacturer.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The engine manufacturer establishes mandatory replacement times (life limits) for critical rotating and high-stress components through the engine type certificate data and the engine's maintenance/overhaul manual, which the FAA approves as part of the type certification process. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Inspection and Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: Main bearing oil seals used with turbine engines are usually what type(s)?

choices.B: Teflon and synthetic rubber.
choices.C: Labyrinth and/or silicone rubber.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine engine main bearing oil seals are typically labyrinth-type seals (which use close-clearance fins to restrict oil flow) and/or carbon rubbing seals (which provide a positive contact seal), as both designs withstand the high temperatures and rotational speeds found in turbine engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: How does a dual axial-flow compressor improve the efficiency of a turbojet engine?

choices.A: More turbine wheels can be used.
choices.B: Higher compression ratios can be obtained.
choices.C: The velocity of the air entering the combustion chamber is increased.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A dual (twin) axial-flow compressor staggers two compressor spools operating at different speeds, allowing far greater overall pressure ratios to be achieved than a single compressor could produce, which directly improves thermodynamic efficiency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: Three types of turbine blades are

choices.B: impulse, reaction, and impulse-reaction.
choices.C: impulse, diverging, and impulse-diverging.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine blades are classified as impulse, reaction, or impulse-reaction (combination) types based on how they extract energy from the gas stream—through pressure drop, velocity change, or both—and these are the three standard categories recognized in turbine engine theory. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Which statements are true regarding aircraft engine propulsion?

choices.A: An engine driven propeller imparts a relatively small amount of acceleration to a large mass of air.
choices.B: An engine driven propeller imparts a relatively large amount of acceleration to a smaller mass of air.
choices.C: In modern turboprop engines, nearly 50 percent of the exhaust gas energy is extracted by turbines to drive the propeller and compressor with the rest providing exhaust thrust.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A propeller-driven aircraft accelerates a large mass of air by a relatively small amount, which is the most efficient application of Newton's third law for propulsion at lower speeds, as opposed to a turbojet that accelerates a smaller mass of air to a very high velocity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Turbine Engine Theory of Operation)

Turbine Engines.question: An advantage of the axial-flow compressor is its

choices.B: low weight.
choices.C: high peak efficiency.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The axial-flow compressor is capable of achieving very high peak efficiency and high compression ratios due to its multi-stage design and low frontal area, making it the preferred compressor type for high-performance turbine engines despite its complexity and higher weight. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: What is one purpose of the stator blades in the compressor section of a turbine engine?

choices.A: Stabilize the pressure of the airflow.
choices.B: Control the direction of the airflow.
choices.C: Increase the velocity of the airflow.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Stator blades in the compressor section redirect and straighten the airflow between compressor stages, controlling the angle at which air enters the next rotor stage to maintain optimal aerodynamic efficiency and prevent compressor stall. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the purpose of the diffuser section in a turbine engine?

choices.A: To increase pressure and reduce velocity.
choices.B: To convert pressure to velocity.
choices.C: To reduce pressure and increase velocity.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The diffuser is a divergent-shaped section located between the compressor and combustion chamber that converts the high-velocity, lower-pressure compressor discharge air into lower-velocity, higher-pressure air before it enters the combustor. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Diffuser Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Where do stress rupture cracks usually appear on turbine blades?

choices.A: Across the blade root, parallel to the fir tree.
choices.B: Along the leading edge, parallel to the edge.
choices.C: Across the leading or trailing edge at a right angle to the edge length.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Stress rupture cracks caused by excessive heat and centrifugal loading typically appear across the leading or trailing edge of turbine blades at a right angle to the edge length, which is a characteristic pattern used during inspection to identify thermally overstressed blades. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Inspection and Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: In which type of turbine engine combustion chamber is the case and liner removed and installed as one unit during routine maintenance?

choices.B: Can-annular.
choices.C: Annular.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In an annular combustion chamber, the liner and case form a single integrated assembly surrounding the engine core, so they must be removed and installed together as one unit. This contrasts with can or can-annular designs where individual cans can be removed separately. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Engine Combustion Section)

Turbine Engines.question: The diffuser section of a jet engine is located between

choices.A: the burner section and the turbine section.
choices.B: station 7 and station 8.
choices.C: the compressor section and the burner section.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The diffuser is positioned immediately downstream of the compressor and upstream of the burner (combustion) section, where it slows the high-velocity compressor discharge air and converts velocity energy into increased pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: When the leading edge of a first-stage turbine blade is found to have stress rupture cracks, which of the following should be suspected?

choices.B: Overtemperature condition.
choices.C: Overspeed condition.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Stress rupture cracks on turbine blade leading edges are a classic indicator of an overtemperature condition, as excessive heat causes the blade material to lose strength and crack under normal operating stresses. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Section Inspection)

Turbine Engines.question: Turbine blades are generally more susceptible to operating damage than compressor blades because of

choices.A: the high probability of carbon deposits.
choices.B: exposure to high temperatures.
choices.C: less exposure to solution during engine pressure wash.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine blades operate in the engine's hottest zone, directly exposed to high-temperature combustion gases, which accelerates material fatigue, oxidation, and creep damage far more than compressor blades experience. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following is the ultimate limiting factor of turbine engine operation?

choices.B: Turbine-inlet temperature.
choices.C: Burner-can pressure.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine-inlet temperature (TIT) is the ultimate limiting factor because the turbine section materials have a finite heat tolerance, and exceeding TIT limits causes rapid degradation, stress rupture, and potential engine failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: The recurrent ingestion of dust or other fine airborne particulates into a turbine engine can result in

choices.A: foreign object damage to the compressor section.
choices.B: the need for less frequent abrasive grit cleaning of the engine.
choices.C: erosion damage to the compressor and turbine sections.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Continuous ingestion of fine particulates causes abrasive wear on both compressor and turbine blade airfoils, progressively eroding the blade profiles and reducing engine efficiency and component life. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Engine Inspection and Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following engine variables is the most critical during turbine engine operation?

choices.B: Compressor RPM.
choices.C: Turbine-inlet temperature.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine-inlet temperature is the most critical operating variable because it directly limits the structural integrity of turbine components; even brief exceedances can cause irreversible damage to turbine blades and vanes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Reduced blade vibration and improved airflow characteristics in gas turbines are brought about by

choices.B: impulse type blades.
choices.C: shrouded turbine rotor blades.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Shrouded turbine rotor blades interlock at their tips, dampening blade vibration and reducing aerodynamic losses by minimizing tip vortex formation, thereby improving overall airflow efficiency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Which turbine engine compressor offers the greatest advantages for both starting flexibility and improved high-altitude performance?

choices.B: Split-spool, axial-flow.
choices.C: Single-spool, axial-flow.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The split-spool (dual-spool) axial-flow compressor allows each spool to operate at its own optimum RPM, providing better starting flexibility and superior high-altitude performance by maintaining efficient compressor operation across a wider range of conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Jet engine turbine blades removed for detailed inspection must be reinstalled in

choices.B: a specified slot 90 away in the direction of rotation.
choices.C: the same slot.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine blades are balanced and matched to specific slots during manufacturing and balancing; reinstalling a blade in any slot other than its original position would upset the rotor balance and could alter tip clearances and stress distribution. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Section Inspection and Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: An advantage of the centrifugal-flow compressor is its high

choices.B: ram efficiency.
choices.C: peak efficiency.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The centrifugal-flow compressor achieves a high pressure rise in a single stage due to the centrifugal force imparted to the air by the impeller, making it simple and effective despite its larger frontal area. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: The highest heat-to-metal contact in a jet engine is the

choices.B: turbine inlet guide vanes.
choices.C: turbine blades.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine inlet guide vanes (nozzle guide vanes) are the first stationary components exposed to the full temperature of combustion gases exiting the burner, receiving higher heat-to-metal contact than any other engine component, including the rotating turbine blades that benefit from centrifugal cooling airflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Which two elements make up the axial-flow compressor assembly?

choices.B: Compressor and manifold.
choices.C: Stator and diffuser.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The axial-flow compressor consists of a rotor (rotating blades that impart energy to the air) and a stator (stationary vanes that diffuse and redirect airflow), and together these two elements make up the compressor assembly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: The two types of centrifugal compressor impellers are

choices.B: rotor and stator.
choices.C: impeller and diffuser.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Centrifugal compressor impellers are classified by the number of air inlets: single-entry impellers draw air from one side only, while double-entry impellers draw air from both sides to increase airflow capacity without increasing impeller diameter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Between each row of rotating blades in a turbine engine compressor, there is a row of stationary blades which act to diffuse the air. These stationary blades are called

choices.B: rotors.
choices.C: stators.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Stator vanes are the stationary blade rows positioned between rotating compressor stages; they diffuse the accelerated air from the preceding rotor stage, converting velocity into pressure and correctly directing airflow into the next rotor stage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: When aircraft turbine blades are subjected to excessive heat stress, what type of failures would the aviation mechanic expect?

choices.B: Torsion and tension.
choices.C: Stress rupture.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Excessive heat stress causes turbine blades to exceed their creep limit, resulting in stress rupture failures characterized by cracking or fracturing along grain boundaries without significant elongation. This is a classic high-temperature failure mode distinct from mechanical load-induced bending or torsion failures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: In an axial-flow compressor, one purpose of the stator vanes at the discharge end of the compressor is to

choices.A: straighten the airflow and eliminate turbulence.
choices.B: increase the velocity and prevent swirling and eddying.
choices.C: decrease the velocity, prevent swirling, and decrease pressure.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The stator vanes at the compressor discharge (diffuser section) straighten the helical airflow exiting the last rotor stage, eliminating swirl and turbulence before the air enters the combustion section. This ensures smooth, axial airflow delivery to the combustion chamber. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 3 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Compressor field cleaning on turbine engines is performed primarily in order to

choices.A: prevent engine oil contamination and subsequent engine bearing wear or damage.
choices.B: facilitate flight line inspection of engine inlet and compressor areas for defects or FOD.
choices.C: prevent engine performance degradation, increased fuel costs, and damage or corrosion to gas path surfaces.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Deposits of dirt, salt, and contaminants on compressor blades reduce aerodynamic efficiency, causing performance degradation and increased fuel consumption, while also promoting corrosion of gas path surfaces. Compressor washing restores blade profile integrity and protects hardware. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Cleaning)

Turbine Engines.question: A purpose of the shrouds on the turbine blades of an axial-flow engine is to

choices.B: increase tip speed.
choices.C: reduce air entrance.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Shrouds are interlocking tip extensions on turbine blades that contact adjacent blades, dampening resonant vibration that could otherwise cause fatigue failure during operation. They effectively stiffen the blade assembly and reduce vibratory stress. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: In a dual axial-flow compressor, the first stage turbine drives

choices.B: N(1) compressor.
choices.C: low pressure compressor.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In a dual-spool axial-flow compressor, the first-stage (high-pressure) turbine drives the N2 high-pressure compressor, while a separate low-pressure turbine drives the N1 low-pressure compressor, allowing each spool to operate at its own optimum speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 3 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: What should be done initially if a turbine engine catches fire when starting?

choices.A: Turn off the fuel and continue engine rotation with the starter.
choices.B: Continue engine start rotation and discharge a fire extinguisher into the intake.
choices.C: Continue starting attempt in order to blow out the fire.

Turbine Engines.explanation: If a turbine engine fire occurs during start, the fuel must be shut off immediately to eliminate the fuel source, while continuing to motor the engine with the starter purges the residual fuel and fire from the engine with airflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Engine Starting)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the proper starting sequence for a turbojet engine?

choices.B: Starter, ignition, fuel.
choices.C: Starter, fuel, ignition.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The correct turbojet start sequence is starter engagement first to bring the engine to a motoring speed, then ignition is activated, and finally fuel is introduced so the ignition system is already energized when the combustible mixture arrives. This sequence prevents a hot start or hung start. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Engine Starting)

Turbine Engines.question: A weak fuel to air mixture along with normal airflow through a turbine engine may result in

choices.B: a lean die-out.
choices.C: high EGT.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A lean die-out occurs when the fuel-to-air mixture falls below the lower flammability limit while airflow remains normal, causing the flame to extinguish rather than producing the high EGT associated with rich conditions or the surge associated with abnormal airflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Engine Malfunctions)

Turbine Engines.question: What is used in turbine engines to aid in stabilization of compressor airflow during low thrust engine operation?

choices.B: Variable guide vanes and/or compressor bleed valves.
choices.C: Pressurization and dump valves.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Variable inlet guide vanes (VIGVs) and compressor bleed valves work together to maintain stable, stall-free airflow through the compressor during low-power operation by adjusting the angle of attack on rotor blades and bleeding off excess air. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 3 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: In a turbine engine with a dual-spool compressor, the low speed compressor

choices.A: always turns at the same speed as the high speed compressor.
choices.B: is connected directly to the high speed compressor.
choices.C: seeks its own best operating speed.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In a dual-spool compressor, the N1 (low-pressure) compressor is mechanically independent from the N2 (high-pressure) compressor and is driven by its own turbine stage, allowing it to find its own aerodynamically optimum rotational speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 3 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following is a good indication of a malfunctioning fuel nozzle when inspecting the exhaust section of an aircraft turbine engine?

choices.B: Hotspots on the tail cone.
choices.C: Carbon build up in the exhaust.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A malfunctioning fuel nozzle that produces an improper spray pattern or excessive fuel flow creates localized hot streaks that appear as hot spots on the tail cone or exhaust duct, making this a reliable visual indicator during exhaust section inspection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Combustion Section)

Turbine Engines.question: The stator vanes in an axial-flow compressor

choices.A: convert velocity energy into pressure energy.
choices.B: convert pressure energy into velocity energy.
choices.C: direct air into the first stage rotor vanes at the proper angle.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Stator vanes in an axial-flow compressor act as diffusers, converting the high-velocity air accelerated by the rotor into increased static pressure by slowing the airflow, thereby accomplishing the compression cycle stage by stage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 3 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Anti-icing of jet engine air inlets is commonly accomplished by

choices.A: electrical heating elements inside the inlet guide vanes.
choices.B: engine bleed air ducted through the critical areas.
choices.C: electrical heating elements located within the engine air inlet cowling.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Hot compressor bleed air is the most common method for anti-icing jet engine inlets and inlet guide vanes because it provides a continuous, reliable heat source that prevents ice accumulation on critical aerodynamic surfaces. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Ice Control Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the primary advantage of an axial-flow compressor over a centrifugal compressor?

choices.B: Less expensive.
choices.C: Greater pressure ratio.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The axial-flow compressor's primary advantage is its ability to achieve much higher compression pressure ratios through multiple stages while maintaining a small frontal area, making it superior in overall compression efficiency compared to the centrifugal design. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 3 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: The purpose of a bleed valve, located in the beginning stages of the compressor, in an aircraft gas turbine engine is to

choices.A: vent some of the air overboard to prevent a compressor stall.
choices.B: control excessively high RPM to prevent a compressor stall.
choices.C: vent high ram air pressure overboard to prevent a compressor stall.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Bleed valves at early compressor stages open to vent excess compressed air overboard when the engine is operating at low RPM, preventing the compressor pressure ratio from exceeding what the downstream stages can accept and thus avoiding a compressor stall. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 3 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: What is meant by a double-entry centrifugal compressor?

choices.A: A compressor that has two intakes.
choices.B: A two-stage compressor independently connected to the main shaft.
choices.C: A compressor with vanes on both sides of the impeller.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A double-entry centrifugal compressor has vanes on both sides of the impeller, allowing air to enter from both sides simultaneously, effectively doubling the airflow capacity without increasing the impeller diameter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Centrifugal Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the major function of the turbine assembly in a turbojet engine?

choices.A: Directs the gases in the proper direction to the tailpipe.
choices.B: Supplies the power to turn the compressor.
choices.C: Increases the temperature of the exhaust gases.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The turbine extracts energy from the high-velocity, high-temperature exhaust gases to drive the compressor, which consumes the majority of the engine's power output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Stator blades in the compressor section of an axial-flow turbine engine

choices.A: increase the air velocity and prevent swirling.
choices.B: straighten the airflow and accelerate it.
choices.C: decrease the air velocity and prevent swirling.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Stator blades are stationary vanes that act as diffusers, converting the velocity energy added by the rotor blades into pressure while also straightening and preventing the swirling of airflow between stages. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: A gas turbine engine comprises which three main sections?

choices.B: Turbine, combustion, and stator.
choices.C: Turbine, compressor, and combustion.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A gas turbine engine is defined by its three primary functional sections — compressor, combustion, and turbine — which together perform the compression, burning, and energy-extraction processes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Gas Turbine Engine Components)

Turbine Engines.question: What type of turbine blade is most commonly used in aircraft jet engines?

choices.B: Impulse.
choices.C: Impulse-reaction.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Impulse-reaction turbine blades are most commonly used because they combine both impulse (pressure drop across the nozzle) and reaction (pressure drop across the rotor) principles, producing greater efficiency across a wide range of operating conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the primary factor which controls the pressure ratio of an axial-flow compressor?

choices.B: Compressor inlet pressure.
choices.C: Compressor inlet temperature.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The pressure ratio of an axial-flow compressor is primarily determined by the number of compressor stages, as each stage contributes an incremental pressure rise and additional stages are added to achieve the required overall ratio. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: The non-rotating axial-flow compressor airfoils in an aircraft gas turbine engine, are called

choices.B: stator vanes.
choices.C: disc rims.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The non-rotating airfoils in an axial-flow compressor are called stator vanes; they remain stationary while the rotor blades rotate, working together in stages to compress the airflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding a turbine engine axial-flow compressor?

choices.A: Each parallel pair of rotor blades and stator vanes constitutes a pressure stage.
choices.B: The number of rows of stages is determined by the amount of air and total pressure rise required.
choices.C: Each consecutive pair of rotor blades and stator vanes constitutes a pressure stage.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Each consecutive (not parallel) pairing of a row of rotor blades followed by a row of stator vanes constitutes one pressure stage, with each stage progressively increasing the air pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: The air passing through the combustion chamber of a turbine engine is

choices.A: used to support combustion and to cool the engine.
choices.B: entirely combined with fuel and burned.
choices.C: speeded up and heated by the action of the turbines.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Only a portion of the air passing through the combustion chamber is mixed with fuel and burned; the remaining air (primary and secondary) is used to cool the liner and dilute the hot gases to a temperature the turbine can withstand. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Combustion Section)

Turbine Engines.question: The stators in the turbine section of a gas turbine engine

choices.A: increase the velocity of the gas flow.
choices.B: decrease the velocity of the gas flow.
choices.C: increase the pressure of the gas flow.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine section stators (nozzle guide vanes) accelerate and direct the hot exhaust gases onto the turbine rotor blades at the correct angle, converting pressure energy into velocity to drive the turbine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: The compressor stators in a gas turbine engine act as diffusers to

choices.A: decrease the velocity of the gas flow.
choices.B: increase the velocity of the gas flow.
choices.C: increase the velocity and decrease the pressure of the gas.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Compressor stator vanes act as diffusers by converting the high-velocity airflow from the rotor blades into increased static pressure while simultaneously reducing air velocity. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: The procedure for removing the accumulation of dirt deposits on compressor blades is called

choices.B: field cleaning.
choices.C: the purging process.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The process of removing dirt and deposit accumulations from compressor blades using abrasive or fluid washing techniques performed on the aircraft is known as field cleaning. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Cleaning)

Turbine Engines.question: A turbine engine hot section is particularly susceptible to which kind of damage?

choices.B: Cracking.
choices.C: Elongation.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The hot section, including the combustion chamber and turbine, is subjected to extreme thermal cycling and temperature gradients, making it particularly susceptible to cracking from thermal fatigue. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Hot Section Inspection)

Turbine Engines.question: Dirt particles in the air being introduced into the compressor of a turbine engine will form a coating on all but which of the following?

choices.B: Casings.
choices.C: Inlet guide vanes.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Dirt particles carried into the compressor will coat stationary and slowly varying surfaces such as casings and inlet guide vanes, but the high centrifugal forces on the turbine blades prevent significant dirt deposits from forming there. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Cleaning)

Turbine Engines.question: Severe rubbing of turbine engine compressor blades will usually cause

choices.B: cracking.
choices.C: galling.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Severe rubbing of compressor blades against the casing causes galling, a form of adhesive wear where metal is transferred from one surface to another due to friction and heat generated by the contact. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Compressor Section Inspection)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following influences the operation of an automatic fuel control unit on a turbojet engine?

choices.B: Mixture control position.
choices.C: Exhaust gas temperature.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Burner (combustion chamber) pressure is one of the primary parameters sensed by the fuel control unit to meter fuel flow and maintain proper engine operation across varying conditions. Mixture control is a reciprocating engine concept, and EGT is monitored separately as a limiting parameter, not a primary FCU input. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: If a turbine engine is unable to reach takeoff EPR before its EGT limit is reached, this is an indication that the

choices.B: EGT controller is out of adjustment.
choices.C: compressor may be contaminated or damaged.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A contaminated or damaged compressor reduces aerodynamic efficiency, requiring more fuel (and thus higher EGT) to develop a given level of thrust/EPR, meaning the EGT redline is reached before the desired EPR is attained. This is a classic symptom of compressor fouling or FOD damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: If the RPM of an axial-flow compressor remains constant, the angle of attack of the rotor blades can be changed by

choices.A: changing the velocity of the airflow.
choices.B: changing the compressor diameter.
choices.C: increasing the pressure ratio.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Blade angle of attack is determined by the vector relationship between rotational velocity and axial (inlet) airflow velocity; if RPM is constant, reducing or increasing the axial velocity of incoming air changes the relative wind and therefore the angle of attack of the rotor blades. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Axial-Flow Compressors)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following variables affect the inlet air density of a turbine engine?

choices.A: Speed of the aircraft, compression ratio, and ambient temperature.
choices.B: Speed of the aircraft, altitude of the aircraft, and ambient temperature.
choices.C: Compression ratio, turbine inlet temperature, and turbine and compressor efficiency.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Inlet air density is a function of the ambient conditions presented to the engine inlet: aircraft speed (ram effect), altitude (ambient pressure), and ambient temperature all directly affect the mass of air entering the engine before any compression occurs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Performance)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following factors affect the thermal efficiency of a turbine engine?

choices.A: Ambient temperature, speed of the aircraft, and altitude of the aircraft.
choices.B: Turbine inlet temperature, compression ratio, and turbine and compressor efficiency.
choices.C: Turbine inlet temperature and ambient temperature.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Thermal efficiency of a gas turbine is governed by how effectively heat energy is converted to work, which depends on turbine inlet temperature, the compression ratio (pressure ratio), and the mechanical/aerodynamic efficiency of the compressor and turbine components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Performance)

Turbine Engines.question: Why do some turbine engines have more than one turbine wheel attached to a single shaft?

choices.A: To facilitate balancing of the turbine assembly.
choices.B: To help stabilize the pressure between the compressor and the turbine.
choices.C: To extract more power from the exhaust gases than a single wheel can absorb.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A single turbine stage can only extract a limited amount of energy from the high-velocity, high-temperature gas stream; multiple turbine wheels on the same shaft allow more energy to be extracted incrementally, providing greater power to drive the compressor and accessories. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following types of combustion sections are used in aircraft turbine engines?

choices.B: Can, multiple-can, and variable.
choices.C: Multiple-can, annular, and can-annular.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The three accepted combustion chamber designs used in aircraft turbine engines are the multiple-can (individual cans), annular (single continuous liner), and can-annular (hybrid combining both features); variable and cascade vane configurations are not combustor types. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Combustion Section)

Turbine Engines.question: A cool-off period prior to shutdown of a turbine engine is accomplished in order to

choices.A: allow the turbine wheel to cool before the case contracts around it.
choices.B: prevent vapor lock in the fuel control and/or fuel lines.
choices.C: prevent seizure of the engine bearings.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Running the engine at low power before shutdown allows the turbine case, which cools and contracts faster than the heavier turbine wheel, to equalize in temperature with the rotor so the case does not shrink around and contact the still-hot wheel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Operation)

Turbine Engines.question: What type igniter plug is used in the low tension ignition system of an aircraft turbofan engine?

choices.A: Low voltage, high amperage glow plug.
choices.B: Self-ionizing or shunted-gap type plug.
choices.C: Recessed surface gap plug.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Low-tension ignition systems use a self-ionizing (shunted-gap) igniter plug that contains a semiconducting material to create an initial ionization path, allowing the system to fire reliably at lower voltages than a conventional air-gap plug. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: What is meant by a shrouded turbine?

choices.A: The turbine blades are shaped so that their ends form a band or shroud.
choices.B: The turbine wheel is enclosed by a protective shroud to contain the blades in case of failure.
choices.C: The turbine wheel has a shroud or duct which provides cooling air to the turbine blades.

Turbine Engines.explanation: On a shrouded turbine, the outer tips of the turbine blades are cast or machined with integral tip extensions that interlock with adjacent blades to form a continuous band (shroud) around the periphery of the wheel, reducing blade vibration and tip losses. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: What term is used to describe a permanent and cumulative deformation of the turbine blades of a turbojet engine?

choices.B: Distortion.
choices.C: Creep.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Creep is the slow, permanent elongation or deformation of turbine blades under sustained high stress and high temperature over time; it is cumulative and irreversible, making it a critical life-limiting factor for turbine components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Inspection and Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the purpose of the dump valve used on aircraft gas turbine engines?

choices.A: The fuel is quickly cut off to the nozzles and the manifolds are drained preventing fuel boiling off as a result of residual engine heat.
choices.B: The valve controls compressor stall by dumping compressor bleed air from the compressor discharge port under certain conditions.
choices.C: Maintains minimum fuel pressure to the engine fuel control unit inlet and dumps excessive fuel back to the inlet of the engine-driven fuel pump.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The dump valve (pressurizing and dump valve) drains the fuel manifold and nozzles at shutdown, preventing residual heat from vaporizing fuel in the lines (which causes hot-start conditions) and avoiding coking deposits at the nozzle tips. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: At what stage in a turbine engine are gas pressures the greatest?

choices.B: Turbine outlet.
choices.C: Compressor outlet.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Gas pressure rises progressively through the compressor stages and reaches its peak at the compressor outlet (diffuser exit/combustor inlet) just before fuel is added and combustion occurs, after which energy is extracted by the turbine and pressure drops. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Theory of Operation)

Turbine Engines.question: In what section of a turbojet engine is the jet nozzle located?

choices.B: Turbine.
choices.C: Exhaust.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The exhaust section contains the exhaust cone, tailpipe, and the jet nozzle (thrust nozzle), which is the final component through which gases are accelerated to produce thrust; it is not part of the combustion or turbine sections. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Exhaust Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding contaminants in the compressor?

choices.A: Accumulation of contaminants in the compressor of a turbojet engine increases aerodynamic efficiency of the blades.
choices.B: Two common methods for removing dirt deposits from turbojet engine compressor blades are a fluid wash and an abrasive grit blast.
choices.C: The only way to remove dirt deposits is an abrasive grit blast.

Turbine Engines.explanation: FAA standards recognize both a fluid wash (using water or a cleaning solution introduced at the inlet) and an abrasive grit blast as accepted methods for removing compressor blade deposits that degrade aerodynamic performance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Turbine Engine Compressor Cleaning)

Turbine Engines.question: Hot spots in the combustion section of a turbojet engine are possible indicators of

choices.B: dirty compressor blades.
choices.C: malfunctioning fuel nozzles.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Malfunctioning fuel nozzles can cause uneven fuel distribution or improper atomization within the combustion section, creating localized areas of excessive heat known as hot spots. Faulty igniter plugs affect starting but not ongoing combustion patterns, and dirty compressor blades affect airflow rather than combustion heat distribution. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Engine Inspection)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following can cause fan blade shingling in a turbofan engine?

choices.B: Large, rapid throttle movements.
choices.C: Engine overspeed and/or FOD.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Fan blade shingling (the overlapping or displacement of fan blade tips) is caused by engine overspeed and/or foreign object damage (FOD), which imparts sudden mechanical forces that cause the blades to shift out of their normal positions. Throttle movements and overtemperature do not produce the specific mechanical shock loading associated with shingling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Engine Inspection)

Turbine Engines.question: Compressor stall is caused by

choices.A: a low angle of attack airflow through the first stages of compression.
choices.B: a high angle of attack airflow through the first stages of compression.
choices.C: rapid engine deceleration.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Compressor stall occurs when airflow meets the compressor blades at an excessively high angle of attack, causing the airfoil to lose lift and the smooth flow through the compressor to break down. A low angle of attack or deceleration does not produce the aerodynamic separation on the blade that defines a stall condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: A condition known as 'hot streaking' in turbine engines is caused by

choices.B: a misaligned combustion liner.
choices.C: excessive fuel flow.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A partially clogged fuel nozzle delivers an uneven, poorly atomized fuel stream that burns in a narrow streak rather than a uniform pattern, producing the phenomenon known as hot streaking through the turbine section. Proper atomization and even fuel distribution from all nozzles are required to prevent this localized overheating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Engine Inspection)

Turbine Engines.question: Determine which portion of the AD is applicable for Model O-690 series engine, serial No. 5863-40 with 283 hours' time in service. This is the compliance portion of an FAA Airworthiness Directive. Compliance required as indicated: (A) For model O-690 series engines, serial Nos. 101-40 through 5264-40 and IO-690 series engines, serial Nos. 101-48 through 423-48, compliance with (C) required within 25 hours' time in service after the effective date of this AD and every 100 hours' time in service thereafter. (B) For Model O-690 series engines, serial Nos. 5265-40 through 6129-40 and IO-690 series engines, serial Nos. 424-48 through 551-48, compliance with (C) required as follows: (1) Within 25 hours' time in service after the effective date of this AD and every 100 hours' time in service thereafter for engines with more than 275 hours' time in service on the effective date of this AD. (2) Prior to the accumulation of 300 hours total time in service and every 100 hours' time in service thereafter for engines with 275 hours or less time in service on the effective date of this AD. (C) Inspect the oil pump drive shaft (P/N 67512) on applicable engines in accordance with instructions contained in Connin Service Bulletin No. 295. Any shafts which are found to be damaged shall be replaced before further flight. These inspections shall be continued until Connin P/N 67512 (redesigned) or P/N 74641 oil pump drive shaft is installed at which time the inspections may be discontinued.
Turbine Engines.explanation: Serial No. 5863-40 falls within the range 5265-40 through 6129-40, placing it under paragraph (B); with 283 hours' time in service exceeding the 275-hour threshold on the effective date, compliance paragraph (B)(1) applies, requiring inspection within 25 hours and every 100 hours thereafter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Airworthiness Directives)

Turbine Engines.question: Where would one find type design information for an R1830-92 engine certificated under the Civil Air Regulations (CAR) and installed on a DC-3?

choices.A: The Aircraft Specifications and Type Certificate Data Sheet.
choices.B: The Aircraft Engine Specifications.
choices.C: The Aircraft Engine Type Certificate Handbook.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Engines certificated under the Civil Air Regulations (CAR), such as the R1830-92, have their type design data published in the Aircraft Engine Specifications (not a Type Certificate Data Sheet, which is the modern FAR-era equivalent). This document contains the official certification basis and operating limits for CAR-era engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Technical Publications)

Turbine Engines.question: Each powerplant installed on an airplane with a Standard Airworthiness Certificate must have been

choices.B: manufactured under the TSO system.
choices.C: originally certificated for that aircraft.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Under 14 CFR Part 21, every powerplant installed on an aircraft with a Standard Airworthiness Certificate must hold a Type Certificate, ensuring it has been evaluated and approved by the FAA for airworthiness. TSO authorization applies to components, and original certification for a specific airframe is not required as long as the engine is type certificated and compatible. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Regulatory Requirements)

Turbine Engines.question: What publication contains the mandatory replacement time for parts of a turbine engine?

choices.B: 14 CFR Part 43
choices.C: Engine manufacturer's maintenance manual.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Mandatory replacement times for life-limited parts of a turbine engine are specified in the engine manufacturer's FAA-approved maintenance manual, which contains the airworthiness limitations section required by 14 CFR Part 33. This section is FAA-approved and legally mandatory, distinguishing it from service instructions or Part 43. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Technical Publications)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following conditions is usually not acceptable to any extent in turbine blades?

choices.B: Pits.
choices.C: Dents.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Cracks in turbine blades are virtually never acceptable because the extreme stress and thermal environment of the turbine section cause cracks to propagate rapidly, risking catastrophic blade failure. Minor pits and dents may be within allowable limits per the maintenance manual, but cracks are cause for immediate blade rejection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Engine Inspection)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Serviceability limits for turbine blades are much more stringent than those for turbine nozzle vanes.
choices.B: A limited number of small nicks and dents can usually be permitted in any area of a turbine blade.
choices.C: Serviceability limits for turbine nozzle vanes are much more stringent than those for turbine blades.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine blades rotate at high speed under enormous centrifugal and thermal stress, so their serviceability limits are far more stringent than those for stationary nozzle vanes, which bear no centrifugal loading. Nozzle vanes can tolerate more surface damage because blade failure due to fatigue crack propagation poses a far greater safety risk. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Turbine Engine Inspection)

Turbine Engines.question: Powerplant instrument range markings

choices.A: show whether the current state of powerplant operation is abnormal, unacceptable for a limited time, or authorized.
choices.B: are based on installed engine operating limits, which may exceed those limits shown on the engine Type Certificate Data Sheet.
choices.C: show whether the current state of powerplant operation is normal, acceptable for a limited time, or unauthorized.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Powerplant instrument range markings use a color-coded arc system to show whether current operation is normal (green), acceptable for a limited time (yellow/amber), or unauthorized (red line), giving flight crews immediate visual guidance on engine operating status. The markings reflect installed limits approved for that specific installation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Powerplant Instruments)

Turbine Engines.question: Thermocouple leads

choices.A: may be installed with either lead to either post of the indicator.
choices.B: are designed for a specific installation and may not be altered.
choices.C: may be repaired using solderless connectors.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Thermocouple leads are made of specific dissimilar metals matched to the thermocouple type and calibrated for a precise installation length and routing; altering them changes the resistance and thermal characteristics, introducing measurement error. Each lead must be installed as designed, with the correct polarity maintained, and cannot be field-modified. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Powerplant Instruments)

Turbine Engines.question: The engine pressure ratio (EPR) is a ratio of the

choices.A: exhaust gas pressure to the engine inlet air pressure and indicates the thrust produced.
choices.B: exhaust gas pressure to the engine inlet air pressure and indicates volumetric efficiency.
choices.C: exhaust gas pressure to the engine exhaust pressure and indicates the thrust produced.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Engine Pressure Ratio (EPR) is the ratio of turbine exhaust total pressure (P_t7) to engine inlet total pressure (P_t2), and it serves as the primary indicator of thrust being produced by the engine. It reflects the total energy added to the airflow, making it a direct thrust parameter rather than an efficiency measure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Powerplant Instruments)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: When a turbine engine indicates low EGT for a particular EPR, it means that the engine is out of trim.
choices.B: Some turbine-powered aircraft use EGT as the primary indicator of thrust produced.
choices.C: When a turbine engine indicates high EGT for a particular EPR, it means that the engine is out of trim.

Turbine Engines.explanation: When a turbine engine shows a higher-than-normal EGT for a given EPR (thrust setting), it indicates the engine is out of trim—meaning it is burning more fuel and running hotter than it should to produce that thrust level, signaling deterioration or improper fuel control adjustment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Powerplant Instruments)

Turbine Engines.question: Turbine engine EGT thermocouples are constructed of

choices.B: copper and constantan.
choices.C: chromel and alumel.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine engine EGT (exhaust gas temperature) thermocouples use chromel (nickel-chromium) and alumel (nickel-aluminum) as the two dissimilar metals because this combination produces a reliable, accurate EMF output across the high temperature ranges encountered in turbine exhaust gases. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Powerplant Instruments)

Turbine Engines.question: What instrument on a gas turbine engine should be monitored to minimize the possibility of a 'hot' start?

choices.B: Turbine inlet temperature.
choices.C: Torquemeter.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine inlet temperature (TIT), also monitored via EGT instrumentation, directly reflects the thermal energy entering the turbine section; excessive temperature during start indicates a 'hot start' before it causes turbine damage.
