Monitoring this parameter allows the technician or pilot to abort the start before limits are exceeded. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Instruments)

Turbine Engines.question: In regard to using a turbine engine oil analysis program, which of the following is NOT true?

choices.A: Generally, an accurate trend forecast may be made after an engine's first oil sample analysis.
choices.B: It is best to start an oil analysis program on an engine when it is new.
choices.C: A successful oil analysis program should be run over an engine's total operating life so that normal trends can be established.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A single oil sample provides no baseline trend data; meaningful analysis requires multiple samples over time to establish normal wear patterns and detect anomalies. One sample alone cannot accurately forecast engine condition trends. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Oil Analysis Programs)

Turbine Engines.question: On an aircraft turbine engine, operating at a constant power, the application of engine anti-icing will result in

choices.B: a false EPR reading.
choices.C: an increase in EPR.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Activating engine anti-icing bleeds compressor air for heating purposes, which changes the engine's airflow and thermodynamic cycle, causing a noticeable shift in EPR even though actual thrust changes occur. The EPR indication will shift because bleed air extraction alters both inlet total pressure and exhaust total pressure relationships. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Pressure Ratio)

Turbine Engines.question: Engine pressure ratio is the total pressure ratio between the

choices.A: aft end of the compressor and the aft end of the turbine.
choices.B: front of the compressor and the rear of the turbine.
choices.C: front of the engine inlet and the aft end of the compressor.

Turbine Engines.explanation: EPR is defined as the ratio of turbine discharge total pressure (P_t7, at the aft end of the turbine/exhaust) to engine inlet total pressure (P_t2, at the front of the compressor inlet), giving a direct measure of the work the engine performs on the airstream. This ratio reflects the net thrust-producing pressure change across the entire engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Pressure Ratio)

Turbine Engines.question: What would be the possible cause if a gas turbine engine has high exhaust gas temperature, high fuel flow, and low RPM at all engine power settings?

choices.B: Loose or corroded thermocouple probes for the EGT indicator.
choices.C: Turbine damage or loss of turbine efficiency.

Turbine Engines.explanation: High EGT, high fuel flow, and low RPM together indicate that energy is being lost within the turbine section—damaged or eroded turbine blades cannot efficiently extract energy from the gas stream, requiring more fuel to attempt to reach target RPM while temperatures rise. This symptom set is a classic indicator of turbine section deterioration or damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Troubleshooting)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the primary purpose of the tachometer on an axial-compressor turbine engine?

choices.A: Monitor engine RPM during cruise conditions.
choices.B: It is the most accurate instrument for establishing thrust settings under all conditions.
choices.C: Monitor engine RPM during starting and to indicate overspeed conditions.

Turbine Engines.explanation: On axial-flow turbine engines, EPR or fuel flow is the primary thrust-setting parameter during cruise, but the tachometer is essential for monitoring rotor speed during the start sequence to detect hung or hot starts and for identifying overspeed conditions that could cause structural damage. Its primary operational role is thus start monitoring and overspeed detection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Instruments)

Turbine Engines.question: The engine pressure ratio (EPR) indicator is a direct indication of

choices.B: pressure ratio between the front and aft end of the compressor.
choices.C: ratio of engine RPM to compressor pressure.

Turbine Engines.explanation: EPR directly compares turbine discharge pressure to engine inlet pressure, and because thrust is a function of the total pressure ratio across the engine, EPR is used as a direct indication of the thrust being produced. This makes EPR the primary thrust-setting instrument on many turbofan-powered aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Pressure Ratio)

Turbine Engines.question: The exhaust gas temperature (EGT) indicator on a gas turbine engine provides a relative indication of the

choices.B: temperature of the exhaust gases as they pass the exhaust cone.
choices.C: turbine inlet temperature.

Turbine Engines.explanation: EGT probes are located in the exhaust stream downstream of the turbine, but because direct measurement at the turbine inlet is impractical, EGT serves as a reliable indirect indicator of turbine inlet temperature (TIT), which is the critical thermal limit for the engine. The two temperatures maintain a predictable relationship, making EGT a proxy for TIT. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Instruments)

Turbine Engines.question: What instrument indicates the thrust of a gas turbine engine?

choices.B: Turbine inlet temperature indicator.
choices.C: Engine pressure ratio indicator.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The EPR indicator compares turbine exhaust total pressure to compressor inlet total pressure, and since thrust is directly proportional to this pressure ratio, EPR is the instrument used to indicate and set engine thrust on applicable turbine engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Pressure Ratio)

Turbine Engines.question: In a turbine engine, where is the turbine discharge pressure indicator sensor located?

choices.A: At the aft end of the compressor section.
choices.B: At a location in the exhaust cone that is determined to be subjected to the highest pressures.
choices.C: Immediately aft of the last turbine stage.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine discharge pressure is measured immediately aft of the last turbine stage, where the exhaust gases have completed their expansion through the turbine and the pressure value is representative of the engine's total pressure output used in EPR computation. This location provides the P_t7 value needed for EPR calculation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Engine Pressure Ratio)

Turbine Engines.question: In what units are turbine engine tachometers calibrated?

choices.B: Actual engine RPM.
choices.C: Percent of engine pressure ratio.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine engine tachometers are calibrated in percent of rated RPM rather than actual RPM because turbine engines operate at very high rotational speeds and multiple spool speeds must be displayed in a standardized, comparable format. Expressing RPM as a percentage allows easy monitoring relative to redline limits regardless of the engine's absolute RPM rating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Instruments)

Turbine Engines.question: Why does one type of Fenwal fire detection system use spot detectors wired in parallel between two separate circuits?

choices.A: To provide an installation that is equal to two separate systems: a primary system and a secondary, or back-up system.
choices.B: So that a double fault may exist in the system without sounding a false alarm.
choices.C: So that a single fault may exist in the system without sounding a false alarm.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In the Fenwal dual-loop (two-wire) spot detector system, spot detectors are wired in parallel between two circuits so that a single open or short fault in one loop does not complete the alarm circuit, preventing a false fire warning while still allowing a genuine fire to trigger the alarm through the remaining functional loop. This architecture provides fault tolerance against nuisance alarms. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Fire Detection Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: A high surge of current is required when a DC electric motor is first started. As the speed of the motor increases,

choices.B: the applied EMF increases proportionally.
choices.C: the counter EMF builds up and opposes the applied EMF, thus reducing the current flow through the armature.

Turbine Engines.explanation: As a DC motor accelerates, the rotating armature generates a back-voltage (counter EMF) that opposes the applied voltage in accordance with Lenz's Law, effectively reducing the net voltage across the armature and thereby limiting current draw as speed increases. This self-regulating characteristic prevents the motor from drawing excessive current once running at speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — DC Motors)

Turbine Engines.question: Why is a constant-speed drive used to control the speed of some aircraft engine-driven generators?

choices.A: So that the voltage output of the generator will remain within limits.
choices.B: To eliminate uncontrolled surges of current to the electrical system.
choices.C: So that the frequency of the alternating current output will remain constant.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Aircraft AC generators must produce power at a fixed frequency (typically 400 Hz), which requires the generator to spin at a constant RPM regardless of engine speed variations; a constant-speed drive (CSD) automatically adjusts the mechanical input to maintain that fixed generator speed. Without constant speed, frequency would fluctuate with throttle changes, making the AC power unusable for frequency-sensitive avionics and equipment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — AC Generation)

Turbine Engines.question: Why is it unnecessary to flash the field of the exciter on a brushless alternator?

choices.A: The exciter is constantly charged by battery voltage.
choices.B: Brushless alternators do not have exciters.
choices.C: Permanent magnets are installed in the main field poles.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Brushless alternators incorporate permanent magnets in the exciter's rotating field poles, which generate residual magnetism without requiring an external flashing voltage to establish an initial magnetic field. Because the permanent magnets always provide a starting magnetic field, the field-flashing procedure required by conventional wound-field generators is unnecessary. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Brushless Alternators)

Turbine Engines.question: One way that the automatic ignition relight systems are activated on gas turbine engines is by a

choices.B: sensing switch located in the tailpipe.
choices.C: drop in fuel flow.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A drop in compressor discharge pressure indicates a potential flameout or compressor stall condition, which is the primary trigger for automatic ignition relight systems to re-energize the igniters and restore combustion. This pressure-sensing method provides a direct indication that combustion may have ceased. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: When installing an electrical switch, under which of the following conditions should the switch be derated from its nominal current rating?

choices.B: Capacitive circuits.
choices.C: Direct current motor circuits.

Turbine Engines.explanation: DC motor circuits produce inductive voltage spikes and high inrush currents during startup that can significantly exceed the nominal rated current, so switches must be derated to handle these transient loads safely. Failing to apply a derating factor can lead to switch contact arcing and premature failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Electrical Switches)

Turbine Engines.question: In order to reduce the possibility of ground shorting the circuits when the connectors are separated for maintenance, the AN and MS electrical connectors should be installed with the

choices.A: socket section on the ground side of the electrical circuit.
choices.B: pin section on the ground side of the electrical circuit.
choices.C: pin section on the positive side of the electrical circuit.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Placing the socket (female) section on the ground side ensures that when the connector is separated, the energized pin contacts are recessed and less likely to accidentally contact the airframe or personnel, while the exposed socket side carries no voltage. This practice minimizes the risk of short circuits and electrical shock. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Wiring Installation)

Turbine Engines.question: When a 28-volt, 75-ampere generator is installed on an aircraft, an electrical load analysis ground check is performed and it is determined that the battery is furnishing 57 amperes to the system, with all electrical equipment operating. This indicates

choices.A: the load exceeds the maximum system percentage capacity.
choices.B: that the generator load will exceed the generator limit.
choices.C: the load will be within the generator load limit.

Turbine Engines.explanation: If the battery is supplying 57 amperes and the generator is rated at 75 amperes, once the generator comes online it will supply up to 75 amperes to cover the load, which is within its rated capacity, indicating the system load is acceptable. The load does not exceed the generator's limit of 75 amperes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Electrical Load Analysis)

Turbine Engines.question: Bonding jumpers should be designed and installed in such a manner that they

choices.A: are not subjected to flexing by relative motion of airframe or engine components.
choices.B: provide a low electrical resistance in the ground circuit.
choices.C: prevent buildup of a static electrical charge between the airframe and the surrounding atmosphere.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The primary purpose of bonding jumpers is to establish a low-resistance electrical path between components to ensure proper grounding and minimize voltage differences that could cause sparking or interference. Without low electrical resistance, the bonding connection fails to serve its fundamental function. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Bonding and Shielding)

Turbine Engines.question: When the starter switch to the aircraft gas turbine engine starter-generator is energized and the engine fails to rotate, one of the probable causes would be the

choices.B: undercurrent solenoid contacts are defective.
choices.C: starter solenoid is defective.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The starter solenoid is the electrical switching device that connects battery or bus power directly to the starter motor; if it is defective, current cannot flow to the starter and the engine will not rotate despite the switch being energized. All other downstream faults would still not prevent rotation if the solenoid itself fails to close. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Starting Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: ON-OFF two position engine electrical switches should be installed

choices.A: so that the toggle will move in the same direction as the desired motion of the unit controlled.
choices.B: under a guard.
choices.C: so the ON position is reached by a forward or upward motion.

Turbine Engines.explanation: FAA standards require that ON-OFF switches be oriented so the ON position is achieved by a forward or upward movement, which is a standardized convention to reduce pilot or technician error by providing intuitive switch operation. This ensures consistency across aircraft systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Electrical Switches)

Turbine Engines.question: When selecting an electrical switch for installation in an aircraft circuit utilizing a direct current motor,

choices.A: a switch designed for DC should be chosen.
choices.B: a derating factor should be applied.
choices.C: only switches with screw-type terminal connections should be used.

Turbine Engines.explanation: DC motor circuits generate high inrush currents and inductive kickback that can exceed a switch's nominal rating, so a derating factor must be applied to select a switch with adequate capacity to handle these conditions safely over its service life. This practice protects switch contacts from premature failure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Electrical Switches)

Turbine Engines.question: When installing electrical wiring parallel to a fuel line, the wiring should be

choices.B: beside the fuel line.
choices.C: above the fuel line.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Electrical wiring must be routed above fuel lines so that any fuel leaks drip away from the wiring, preventing fuel from contacting electrical connections and creating a fire or ignition hazard. Routing wiring below a fuel line would expose it to fuel drips in the event of a leak. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Wiring Installation)

Turbine Engines.question: How many basic types of circuit breakers are used in powerplant installation electrical systems?

choices.B: Three.
choices.C: Four.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Two basic types of circuit breakers are used in aircraft powerplant electrical systems: the automatically resetting type and the manually resetting (push-to-reset) type, which covers the range of circuit protection configurations encountered in powerplant installations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Circuit Protection Devices)

Turbine Engines.question: Electrical circuit protection devices are installed primarily to protect the

choices.B: units.
choices.C: wiring.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Circuit protection devices such as fuses and circuit breakers are installed primarily to protect the wiring from overheating and potential fire caused by excessive current, not to protect the connected equipment or switches. The wire is the most vulnerable component to overcurrent damage throughout the circuit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Circuit Protection Devices)

Turbine Engines.question: A term commonly used when two or more electrical terminals are installed on a single lug of a terminal strip is

choices.B: piggy backing.
choices.C: stacking.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The FAA term for installing two or more wires or terminals on a single terminal strip lug is 'stacking,' and regulations limit the number of terminals that may be stacked on a single lug to prevent poor contact and increased resistance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 9 — Wiring Installation)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Gas turbine and reciprocating engine oils can be mixed or used interchangeably.
choices.B: Most gas turbine engine oils are synthetic.
choices.C: Most gas turbine engine oils are straight mineral or ashless dispersant.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Gas turbine engines operate at extremely high temperatures that would break down petroleum-based oils rapidly, so synthetic oils with superior thermal stability and lubricity are universally used in turbine engine applications. Synthetic and mineral oils must never be mixed as they are chemically incompatible. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of these characteristics is desirable in turbine engine oil?

choices.B: High flash point.
choices.C: High volatility.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A high flash point is desirable in turbine engine oil because it indicates the oil can withstand the high operating temperatures of turbine engines without vaporizing or igniting, which is critical for safety and lubrication effectiveness. Low flash point or high volatility would result in oil loss and potential fire hazard. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Manufacturers normally require turbine engine oil servicing within a short time after engine shutdown primarily to

choices.B: help dilute and neutralize any contaminants that may already be present in the engine's oil system.
choices.C: provide a better indication of any oil leaks in the system.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Manufacturers require oil servicing shortly after shutdown because the oil is still warm and has drained back from the engine components into the tank, giving an accurate sump level reading and preventing technicians from overservicing a cold engine where oil has not yet fully drained back. Overservicing can lead to oil being ingested into the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: How is oil cooled in gas turbine engines?

choices.A: Fuel may not be used to cool oil in gas turbine engines.
choices.B: Ram air may not be used to cool oil in gas turbine engines.
choices.C: Ram air may be used to cool oil in gas turbine engines.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Ram air heat exchangers are a common and FAA-accepted method of cooling oil in gas turbine engines, and fuel-oil heat exchangers (which also use fuel as a coolant) are likewise used, making both ram air and fuel valid cooling media. The FAA explicitly recognizes ram air as an approved oil cooling method in turbine engine lubrication systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The oil dampened main bearing utilized in some turbine engines is used to

choices.A: provide lubrication of bearings from the beginning of starting rotation until normal oil pressure is established.
choices.B: provide an oil film between the outer race and the bearing housing in order to reduce vibration tendencies in the rotor system, and to allow for slight misalignment.
choices.C: dampen surges in oil pressure to the bearings.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The oil-dampened (squeeze-film) main bearing uses a thin film of oil between the outer bearing race and the housing to absorb and dissipate vibration energy from the rotor system and to accommodate slight shaft misalignment. This design protects both the bearing and airframe structure from high-frequency rotor vibration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: In an axial-flow turbine engine, compressor bleed air is sometimes used to aid in cooling the

choices.B: inlet guide vanes.
choices.C: turbine, vanes, blades, and bearings.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Compressor bleed air is routed internally to cool the turbine section components—including nozzle vanes, rotor blades, and bearings—because these parts are exposed to extremely high gas path temperatures that exceed the material limits without active cooling. This internal air cooling is a fundamental design feature of modern axial-flow turbine engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Cooling)

Turbine Engines.question: Oil picks up the most heat from which of the following turbine engine components?

choices.B: Compressor bearing.
choices.C: Turbine bearing.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine bearings are located immediately aft of the combustion and turbine sections where gas temperatures are highest, so oil absorbs more heat from turbine bearings than from any other engine component. The compressor bearings operate in a comparatively cooler environment and contribute far less heat load to the oil system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following is a function of the fuel/oil heat exchanger on a turbojet engine?

choices.B: Emulsifies the oil.
choices.C: Increases fuel temperature.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The fuel/oil heat exchanger uses relatively cool fuel as the heat sink to lower oil temperature, and as a direct result, the fuel itself is warmed before entering the fuel control and nozzles, which improves atomization and helps prevent fuel icing. This dual benefit—oil cooling and fuel heating—is why the component is standard on turbojet/turbofan installations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: According to the Federal Aviation Regulations, oil tank fillers on turbine engines must be marked with the word

choices.A: 'oil' and the type and grade of oil specified by the manufacturer.
choices.B: 'oil' and tank capacity.
choices.C: 'oil.'

Turbine Engines.explanation: 14 CFR §33.71 and FAA maintenance standards require that the oil filler opening be marked with the word 'OIL'; specific grade or capacity markings are not federally mandated on the filler itself, though manufacturers may add them. The regulatory minimum marking requirement is simply the word 'oil.' (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: After making a welded repair to a pressurized-type turbine engine oil tank, the tank should be pressure checked to

choices.A: not less than 5 psi plus the maximum operating pressure of the tank.
choices.B: not less than 5 psi plus the average operating pressure of the tank.
choices.C: 5 psi.

Turbine Engines.explanation: FAA regulations (14 CFR §33.71) specify that a repaired pressurized oil tank must be pressure-tested to not less than 5 psi above the maximum operating pressure of the tank to ensure structural integrity of the repair under worst-case operating conditions. Using maximum (not average) operating pressure as the baseline ensures the most conservative and safe test standard. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Why are fixed orifice nozzles used in the lubrication system of gas turbine engines?

choices.A: To provide a relatively constant oil flow to the main bearings at all engine speeds.
choices.B: To keep back pressure on the oil pump, thus preventing an air lock.
choices.C: To protect the oil seals by preventing excessive pressure from entering the bearing cavities.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Fixed orifice nozzles meter a relatively constant volume of oil to the main bearings regardless of engine speed or oil pump output variations, ensuring consistent bearing lubrication across the full operating range. Because the orifice size is fixed, it limits flow rather than relying on pressure-regulating valves to control bearing oil supply. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Possible failure related ferrous-metal particles in turbine engine oil cause an (electrical) indicating-type magnetic chip detector to indicate their presence by

choices.A: disturbing the magnetic lines of flux around the detector tip.
choices.B: bridging the gap between the detector center (positive) electrode and the ground electrode.
choices.C: generating a small electric current that is caused by the particles being in contact with the dissimilar metal of the detector tip.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A magnetic chip detector works by attracting ferrous metal particles to its magnetized tip; when enough particles accumulate, they physically bridge the gap between the center (positive) electrode and the outer ground electrode, completing an electrical circuit that triggers a cockpit or FADEC indication. The detection is electrical continuity-based, not flux disturbance or galvanic action. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the primary purpose of the oil breather pressurization system that is used on turbine engines?

choices.B: Allows aeration of the oil for better lubrication because of the air/oil mist.
choices.C: Provides a proper oil spray pattern from the main bearing oil jets.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The breather pressurization system maintains a controlled positive differential pressure within the bearing sumps and oil tank so that oil jets produce the correct spray pattern and flow rate onto the bearings; without adequate back-pressure, jets can cavitate or produce an improper spray. Foaming control is a secondary benefit, not the primary purpose. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The purpose of directing bleed air to the outer turbine case on some engines is to

choices.A: provide optimum turbine blade tip clearance by controlling thermal expansion.
choices.B: provide up to 100 percent kinetic energy extraction from the flowing gases.
choices.C: allow operation in a thermal environment 600 to 800F above the temperature limits of turbine blade and vane alloys.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Active clearance control (ACC) directs fan or compressor bleed air onto the turbine case to precisely regulate thermal expansion of the casing, keeping turbine blade tip clearances at their optimum minimum value across all flight phases and thereby improving efficiency and reducing blade-tip rub. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Cooling)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the primary purpose of the hopper located in the oil supply tank of some dry sump engine installations?

choices.A: To reduce the time required to warm the oil to operating temperatures.
choices.B: To reduce surface aeration of the hot oil and thus reduce oxidation and the formation of sludge and varnish.
choices.C: To impart a centrifugal motion to the oil entering the tank so that the foreign particles in the oil will separate more readily.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A hopper (or header tank) is a small reservoir within the main oil tank that retains a supply of warm oil close to the engine oil inlet; by keeping this smaller volume of oil at a higher temperature, warm-up time to normal operating viscosity is significantly reduced, which is critical for cold-weather operations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The vent line connecting the oil supply tank and the engine in some dry sump engine installations permits

choices.A: pressurization of the oil supply to prevent cavitation of the oil supply pump.
choices.B: oil vapors from the engine to be condensed and drained into the oil supply tank.
choices.C: the oil tank to be vented through the normal engine vent.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The vent line between the oil tank and the engine allows the oil tank to share the engine's vent path (through the breather/pressurizing valve to overboard), so the tank does not require its own separate vent and is maintained at the same controlled pressure as the engine sump. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The purpose of a relief valve installed in the tank venting system of a turbine engine oil tank is to

choices.A: prevent oil pump cavitation by maintaining a constant pressure on the oil pump inlet.
choices.B: maintain internal tank air pressure at the ambient atmospheric level regardless of altitude or rate of change in altitude.
choices.C: maintain a positive internal pressure in the oil tank after shutdown to prevent oil pump cavitation on engine start.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The tank vent relief valve maintains a slight positive pressure on the oil supply, which raises the effective boiling point of the oil and ensures the oil pump inlet is always supplied with liquid oil, preventing cavitation (vapor lock) of the pressure pump especially at high altitude where ambient pressure is low. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: As a general rule, a small amount of small fuzzy particles or gray metallic paste on a turbine engine magnetic chip detector

choices.A: is considered to be the result of normal wear.
choices.B: indicates an imminent component failure.
choices.C: indicates accelerated generalized wear.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Fine fuzzy metallic particles or gray metallic paste found on a magnetic chip detector are the byproduct of normal micro-wear (fretting and surface conditioning) of gears and bearings and are not indicative of a failure; coarser, larger, or distinctly shaped particles would signal abnormal wear requiring investigation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The capacitor-type ignition system is used almost universally on turbine engines primarily because of its high voltage and

choices.B: long life.
choices.C: high-heat intensity.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The capacitor-type ignition system stores electrical energy and releases it in a very short, intense burst, producing a high-heat intensity spark capable of reliably igniting the fuel-air mixture in turbine combustion chambers under all operating conditions. The high heat intensity (not merely high voltage) is what ensures ignition in the demanding turbine environment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: How does the ignition system of a gas turbine engine differ from that of a reciprocating engine?

choices.A: One igniter plug is used in each combustion chamber.
choices.B: Magneto-to-engine timing is not critical.
choices.C: A high-energy spark is required for ignition.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Unlike reciprocating engine ignition systems that use relatively low-energy timed sparks, turbine engine ignition systems must produce a high-energy spark to reliably ignite the fuel-air mixture at high altitudes, low temperatures, and high airspeeds where ignition is difficult. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: In a turbine engine DC capacitor discharge ignition system, where are the high-voltage pulses formed?

choices.B: At the triggering transformer.
choices.C: At the rectifier.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In a DC capacitor discharge ignition system, the triggering transformer receives a low-voltage pulse that it steps up into the high-voltage pulse used to discharge the capacitor and fire the igniter plug. The triggering transformer is therefore the component where high-voltage pulses are formed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: If it is found that a shielded ignition system does not adequately reduce ignition noise, it may be necessary to install

choices.B: a filter between the magneto and magneto switch.
choices.C: bonding wires from the shielding to ground.

Turbine Engines.explanation: When shielding alone is insufficient to suppress ignition noise (radio frequency interference), installing a filter between the magneto and the magneto switch attenuates the electrical noise conducted along the wiring, providing additional suppression beyond shielding. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Ignition System Maintenance)

Turbine Engines.question: The constrained-gap igniter plug used in some gas turbine engines operates at a cooler temperature because

choices.A: it projects into the combustion chamber.
choices.B: the applied voltage is less.
choices.C: the construction is such that the spark occurs beyond the face of the combustion chamber liner.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The constrained-gap igniter plug is designed so that the spark occurs beyond the face of the combustion chamber liner, meaning the electrical discharge and resulting heat are not concentrated at the plug tip itself, allowing the plug body to run at a significantly cooler temperature and extending service life. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Generally, when removing a turbine engine igniter plug, in order to eliminate the possibility of the technician receiving a lethal shock, the ignition switch is turned off and

choices.A: disconnected from the power supply circuit.
choices.B: the igniter lead is disconnected from the plug and the center electrode grounded to the engine after disconnecting the transformer-exciter input lead and waiting the prescribed time.
choices.C: the transformer-exciter input lead is disconnected and the center electrode grounded to the engine after disconnecting the igniter lead from the plug and waiting the prescribed time.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The correct discharge procedure requires first disconnecting the transformer-exciter input lead, then waiting the manufacturer-prescribed time for capacitors to discharge, then disconnecting the igniter lead from the plug, and finally grounding the center electrode to the engine to safely bleed any residual charge before handling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Great caution should be exercised in handling damaged hermetically sealed turbine engine igniter transformer units because

choices.A: compounds in the unit may become a fire or explosion hazard when exposed to the air.
choices.B: some contain radioactive material.
choices.C: some contain toxic chemicals.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Some hermetically sealed turbine ignition exciter units contain toxic chemicals within their sealed housings, and physical damage that breaches the seal can expose maintenance personnel to these hazardous substances. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Igniter plugs used in turbine engines have a long service life because they

choices.B: operate continuously with a lower spark.
choices.C: do not require continuous operation.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine engine igniter plugs enjoy a long service life because they are only energized during the engine start cycle and are de-energized once the engine is running, unlike reciprocating engine spark plugs which fire continuously during operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Which statement is correct regarding the ignition system of a turbine engine?

choices.A: The system is normally de-energized as soon as the engine starts.
choices.B: It is energized during the starting and warmup periods only.
choices.C: The system generally includes a polar inductor-type magneto.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Once a turbine engine achieves self-sustaining combustion, the ignition system is normally de-energized because the flame in the combustion section is self-sustaining, and continuous operation of the high-energy system is unnecessary and would reduce igniter plug service life. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Why do turbine engine ignition systems require high energy?

choices.A: To ignite the fuel under conditions of high altitude and high temperatures.
choices.B: Because the applied voltage is much greater.
choices.C: To ignite the fuel under conditions of high altitude and low temperatures.

Turbine Engines.explanation: At high altitudes, the combination of low air density, low temperatures, and low pressure makes fuel-air mixture ignition extremely difficult, requiring high-energy sparks to reliably initiate and sustain combustion under these adverse conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following are included in a typical turbine engine ignition system?

choices.A: Two igniter plugs, two transformers, and two intermediate ignition leads.
choices.B: Two igniter plugs, one exciter unit, and two low-tension igniter leads.
choices.C: Two igniter plugs, one exciter unit, and two high-tension igniter leads.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A typical turbine engine ignition system consists of two igniter plugs (one per combustion zone), a single exciter unit that supplies energy to both plugs, and two high-tension igniter leads connecting the exciter to each plug. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Ignition Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The primary advantage of pneumatic (air turbine) starters over comparable electric starters for turbine engines is

choices.A: a significant lack of wear to the commutator.
choices.B: that it does not disengage until the engine reaches idle.
choices.C: its high power-to-weight ratio.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Pneumatic (air turbine) starters produce very high power output relative to their weight, giving them a superior power-to-weight ratio compared to electric starters of equivalent output, which makes them preferred for large turbine engines where weight is critical. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Starting Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Pneumatic starters are usually designed with what types of airflow impingement systems?

choices.A: Radial inward flow turbine and axial-flow turbine.
choices.B: Centrifugal compressor and axial-flow compressor.
choices.C: Double entry centrifugal outward flow and axial-flow turbines.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Pneumatic starters are designed with either radial inward flow turbine or axial-flow turbine impingement systems, both of which efficiently convert compressed air energy into the rotational force needed to crank the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Starting Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: What factor is not used in the operation of an aircraft gas turbine engine fuel control unit?

choices.B: Mixture control position.
choices.C: Power lever position.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Turbine engine fuel control units are designed around parameters such as compressor inlet temperature, compressor inlet pressure, power lever position, engine RPM, and burner pressure, but mixture control position is a reciprocating engine concept that has no equivalent function in turbine engine fuel control. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: In order to stabilize cams, springs, and linkages within the fuel control, manufacturers generally recommend that all final turbine engine trim adjustments be made in the

choices.B: decrease direction.
choices.C: decrease direction after over-adjustment.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Manufacturers specify that final trim adjustments be made in the increase direction so that the mechanical preload on cams, springs, and linkages within the fuel control is consistent, eliminating backlash and ensuring repeatable, stable settings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: When trimming a turbine engine, the fuel control is adjusted to

choices.A: produce as much power as the engine is capable of producing.
choices.B: set idle RPM and maximum speed or EPR.
choices.C: allow the engine to produce maximum RPM without regard to power output.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Trimming a turbine engine involves adjusting the fuel control unit to establish correct idle RPM and maximum power output (expressed as RPM or EPR), ensuring the engine performs within manufacturer specifications. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The active clearance control (ACC) portion of an EEC system aids turbine engine efficiency by

choices.A: adjusting stator vane position according to operating conditions and power requirements.
choices.B: ensuring turbine blade to engine case clearances are kept to a minimum by controlling case temperatures.
choices.C: automatically adjusting engine speed to maintain a desired EPR.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Active clearance control systems use cooling air directed at the turbine case to thermally contract it, minimizing the blade-tip-to-case clearance and thereby reducing tip losses and improving turbine efficiency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Electronic Engine Control)

Turbine Engines.question: What should be checked/changed to ensure the validity of a turbine engine performance check if an alternate fuel is to be used?

choices.B: Maximum RPM adjustment.
choices.C: EPR gauge calibration.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Because different fuels have different specific gravity (density), the fuel control's specific gravity setting must be verified or adjusted when an alternate fuel is used to ensure accurate fuel metering and a valid performance check. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: For what primary purpose is a turbine engine fuel control unit trimmed?

choices.A: To obtain maximum thrust output when desired.
choices.B: To properly position the power levers.
choices.C: To adjust the idle RPM.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The primary purpose of trimming is to adjust the fuel control so the engine can deliver its maximum rated thrust output when required, ensuring the engine meets its certified performance limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Which type of fuel control is used on most of today's turbine engines?

choices.B: Mechanical.
choices.C: Hydromechanical or electronic.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Modern turbine engines predominantly use hydromechanical fuel controls or full-authority digital electronic controls (FADEC/EEC), with pure mechanical controls being largely obsolete on current production engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Under which of the following conditions will the trimming of a turbine engine be most accurate?

choices.B: High moisture and low wind.
choices.C: No wind and low moisture.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Engine trimming is most accurate under calm (no wind) and low-humidity conditions because wind causes erratic inlet pressure readings and moisture affects air density, both of which introduce errors into EPR or RPM measurements. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: What effect does high atmospheric humidity have on the operation of a jet engine?

choices.B: Decreases compressor and turbine RPM.
choices.C: Has little or no effect.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Unlike reciprocating engines, turbine engines are relatively unaffected by humidity because water vapor has minimal impact on the thermodynamic cycle and mass airflow at the levels of humidity normally encountered in operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Operation)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following is NOT an input parameter for a turbine engine fuel control unit?

choices.B: Compressor inlet temperature.
choices.C: Ambient humidity.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Standard turbine fuel control units meter fuel based on mechanical and pneumatic inputs such as compressor inlet pressure and temperature, throttle position, and RPM; ambient humidity is not sensed or used as a control parameter. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: Kerosene is used as turbine engine fuel because

choices.A: kerosene has very high volatility which aids in ignition and lubrication.
choices.B: kerosene has more heat energy per gallon and lubricates fuel system components.
choices.C: kerosene does not contain any water.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Kerosene-based turbine fuels are preferred because they contain more heat energy (BTU) per gallon than gasoline-type fuels and their lubricating properties help protect fuel system components such as pumps and the fuel control unit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuels)

Turbine Engines.question: What are the principal advantages of the duplex fuel nozzle used in many turbine engines?

choices.A: Restricts the amount of fuel flow to a level where more efficient and complete burning of the fuel is achieved.
choices.B: Provides better atomization and uniform flow pattern.
choices.C: Allows a wider range of fuels and filters to be used.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The duplex nozzle uses two separate orifices (primary and secondary) that produce a finely atomized, cone-shaped spray pattern across a wide range of fuel flows, ensuring efficient combustion from ignition to full power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: It is necessary to control acceleration and deceleration rates in turbine engines in order to

choices.B: prevent overtemperature.
choices.C: prevent friction between turbine wheels and the case due to expansion and contraction.

Turbine Engines.explanation: If acceleration is too rapid, the fuel-air ratio exceeds the lean or rich limits of the combustor, causing a compressor stall (blowout) or flameout (die-out), so the fuel control schedules acceleration and deceleration rates to stay within stable combustion limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the purpose of the flow divider in a turbine engine duplex fuel nozzle?

choices.A: Allows an alternate flow of fuel if the primary flow clogs or is restricted.
choices.B: Creates the primary and secondary fuel supplies.
choices.C: Provides a flow path for bleed air which aids in the atomization of fuel.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The flow divider (pressurizing valve) in a duplex nozzle remains closed at low fuel flows so all fuel passes through the primary orifice for good atomization at low power, then opens at higher fuel flows to admit fuel to the larger secondary orifice as well. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: What are the three basic regulating components of a sea-level boosted turbocharger system?

choices.A: Exhaust bypass assembly, compressor assembly, and density controller.
choices.B: Exhaust bypass assembly, density controller, and differential pressure controller.
choices.C: Pump and bearing casing, compressor assembly, and density controller.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A sea-level boosted turbocharger system uses the exhaust bypass (wastegate) assembly to regulate exhaust energy to the turbine, the density controller to set a target upper deck pressure, and the differential pressure controller to limit the pressure differential across the throttle body at high power settings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Induction Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The purpose of a bellmouth compressor inlet is to

choices.A: provide an increased ram air effect at low airspeeds.
choices.B: maximize the aerodynamic efficiency of the inlet.
choices.C: provide an increased pressure drop in the inlet.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A bellmouth inlet is a carefully contoured, aerodynamically smooth converging inlet designed to deliver nearly loss-free, uniform airflow to the compressor face, making it ideal for test cell engine performance measurements. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Inlet Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: What method(s) is/are used to provide clean air to the engines of helicopters and turboprop airplanes that have particle (sand and ice) separators installed?

choices.A: Positive and negative charged areas to attract and/or repel particulates out of the airflow.
choices.B: Air/moisture separators, and 'washing' the air clean utilizing water droplets.
choices.C: Sharp airflow directional change to take advantage of inertia and/or centrifugal force, and filters or engine inlet screens.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Particle separators exploit the inertia and centrifugal force of sand, ice, and debris to deflect contaminants out of the airstream before they enter the engine, and inlet screens or filters provide an additional barrier to larger particles. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Inlet Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The vortex dissipators installed on some turbine-powered aircraft to prevent engine FOD utilize

choices.A: variable inlet guide vanes (IGV) and/or variable first stage fan blades.
choices.B: variable geometry inlet ducts.
choices.C: a stream of engine bleed air blown toward the ground ahead of the engine.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Vortex dissipators direct a jet of engine bleed air toward the ground in front of the inlet, disrupting the low-pressure vortex that would otherwise lift debris into the engine. This pneumatic method prevents FOD ingestion without altering inlet geometry or blade configuration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Air Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: What engine instrument indications may shift when a turbofan engine anti-icing (bleed air) system is turned on?

choices.A: Tachometer, fuel flow indicator, and EGT.
choices.B: Fuel flow indicator, EGT, and EPR.
choices.C: Tachometer, EGT, and EPR.

Turbine Engines.explanation: When anti-icing bleed air is activated, engine air is diverted away from the normal flow path, causing the engine to compensate by increasing fuel flow, which in turn affects RPM (tachometer), EGT, and fuel flow indicator readings. EPR is not listed among the shifting instruments because the tachometer (N1/N2) is the primary speed reference affected alongside fuel flow and EGT. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Air Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: The component(s) in a turbine engine that operate(s) at the highest temperatures is/are the

choices.A: first stage turbine nozzle guide vanes.
choices.B: turbine disks.
choices.C: exhaust cone.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The first stage turbine nozzle guide vanes are directly downstream of the combustion section and are exposed to the highest gas temperatures in the engine before any energy has been extracted by rotating turbine stages. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Which statement is true regarding the air passing through the combustion section of a jet engine?

choices.A: Most is used for engine cooling.
choices.B: Most is used to support combustion.
choices.C: A small percentage is frequently bled off at this point to be used for air-conditioning and/or other pneumatic powered systems.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In a gas turbine combustion section, only about 25–30% of the airflow actually supports combustion; the remaining majority (approximately 70–75%) is used as secondary and dilution air for cooling the combustion liner walls and lowering gas temperature before it enters the turbine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Combustion Section)

Turbine Engines.question: The hot section of a turbine engine is particularly susceptible to which of the following kind of damage?

choices.B: Pitting.
choices.C: Cracking.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The hot section operates under extreme thermal cycling and high temperatures, making it highly susceptible to cracking caused by thermal fatigue and stress concentrations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Turbine Section)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the purpose of a slip joint in an exhaust collector ring?

choices.A: It aids in alignment and absorbs expansion.
choices.B: It reduces vibration and increases cooling.
choices.C: It permits the collector ring to be installed in one piece.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Slip joints in exhaust collector rings are designed to allow for thermal expansion and contraction of the exhaust system components while maintaining proper alignment, preventing distortion or structural damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Exhaust Section)

Turbine Engines.question: On turbojet powered airplanes, thrust reversers are capable of producing between

choices.A: 35 and 50 percent of the rated thrust in the reverse direction.
choices.B: 35 and 75 percent of the rated thrust in the reverse direction.
choices.C: 35 and 65 percent of the rated thrust in the reverse direction.

Turbine Engines.explanation: FAA standards specify that thrust reversers are capable of producing between 35 and 50 percent of the engine's rated forward thrust in the reverse direction, which is sufficient for landing roll deceleration assistance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Thrust Reversers)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following indicates that a combustion chamber of a jet engine is not operating properly?

choices.A: Clam shells stick in thrust reverse position.
choices.B: Hot spots on the tail cone.
choices.C: Warping of the exhaust duct liner.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Hot spots on the tail cone indicate that a combustion chamber is producing uneven combustion or improperly directed hot gases, which is a direct sign of malfunction within that combustion chamber. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Combustion Section)

Turbine Engines.question: How are combustion liner walls cooled in a gas turbine engine?

choices.A: By secondary air flowing through the combustion chamber.
choices.B: By the pattern of holes and louvers cut in the diffuser section.
choices.C: By bleed air vented from the engine air inlet.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Combustion liner walls are cooled by secondary airflow that enters through a pattern of holes and louvers in the liner, forming a film of cooler air along the inner wall surface to protect the metal from direct flame contact. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Combustion Section)

Turbine Engines.question: Thrust reversers utilizing a pneumatic actuating system usually receive operating pressure from

choices.B: an on board hydraulic or electrical powered compressor.
choices.C: high pressure air reservoirs.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Pneumatically actuated thrust reverser systems draw their operating pressure from engine bleed air, which is a readily available high-pressure source on turbine-powered aircraft. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Thrust Reversers)

Turbine Engines.question: Operating thrust reversers at low ground speeds can sometimes cause

choices.A: hot gas re-ingestion, compressor stalls, and sand or other foreign object ingestion.
choices.B: abnormal exhaust gas temperature and engine pressure ratio indications.
choices.C: engine vibrations.

Turbine Engines.explanation: At low ground speeds, reversed thrust can cause the hot exhaust gases to recirculate forward and be re-ingested by the engine inlet, leading to compressor stalls, elevated EGT, and ingestion of foreign objects or debris disturbed by the reversed airflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Thrust Reversers)

Turbine Engines.question: Engines using cold stream, or both cold and hot stream reversing, include

choices.B: turbojets.
choices.C: turbojets with afterburner.

Turbine Engines.explanation: High bypass turbofan engines use cold stream (fan bypass duct) reversers, and may also incorporate hot stream reversers, because the large volume of bypass air makes cold stream reversing highly effective for deceleration. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Thrust Reversers)

Turbine Engines.question: Turbojet and turbofan thrust reverser systems are generally powered by

choices.A: fuel pressure, hydraulic pressure, and pneumatic pressure.
choices.B: electricity, hydraulic pressure, and pneumatic pressure.
choices.C: fuel pressure and electricity.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Thrust reverser systems on turbojet and turbofan engines are typically actuated using electric motors (for control and sequencing), hydraulic actuators (for mechanical movement), or pneumatic pressure, making electricity, hydraulic pressure, and pneumatic pressure the three primary power sources. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Thrust Reversers)

Turbine Engines.question: The rearward thrust capability of an engine with the thrust reverser system deployed is

choices.B: greater than its forward capability.
choices.C: equal to its forward capability.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Because thrust reversers redirect exhaust gases forward at an angle rather than perfectly opposing forward thrust, and due to aerodynamic inefficiencies in the reversed flow path, the reverse thrust produced is always less than the engine's forward thrust capability. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Thrust Reversers)

Turbine Engines.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding thrust reverser systems?

choices.A: The reverser system must be able to withstand high temperatures, be mechanically strong, relatively light in weight, and reliable.
choices.B: Engine thrust reversers on the aircraft usually will operate independently of each other.
choices.C: Mechanical blockage system design permits a deployment position aft of the exhaust nozzle only.

Turbine Engines.explanation: Thrust reverser systems must withstand high thermal and mechanical loads from hot exhaust gases while remaining lightweight and reliable, as these are fundamental design requirements mandated by FAA airworthiness standards for reverser systems. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Thrust Reversers)

Turbine Engines.question: What is the proper operating sequence when using thrust reversers to slow an aircraft after landing?

choices.A: Advance thrust levers up to takeoff position as conditions require, select thrust reverse, de-select thrust reverser, retard thrust levers to ground idle.
choices.B: Retard thrust levers to ground idle, raise thrust reverser levers as required, and retard thrust reverser levers to ground idle.
choices.C: Select thrust reverse, advance thrust reverser levers no higher than 75 percent N(1), and retard thrust reverser levers to idle at approximately normal taxi speed.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The correct thrust reverser procedure is to first retard the thrust levers to ground idle before deploying reversers, then raise the reverser levers as needed for braking, and finally retard them back to ground idle before stowing — this prevents engine overspeed and ensures safe deployment sequencing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Operation)

Turbine Engines.question: The function of an APU air inlet plenum is to

choices.A: increase the velocity of the air before entering the compressor.
choices.B: decrease the pressure of the air before entering the compressor.
choices.C: stabilize the pressure of the air before it enters the compressor.

Turbine Engines.explanation: The APU air inlet plenum acts as a pressure-stabilizing chamber, smoothing out pressure fluctuations before airflow enters the compressor to ensure consistent and efficient compression. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Auxiliary Power Units)

Turbine Engines.question: Generally, when maximum APU shaft output power is being used in conjunction with pneumatic power

choices.A: pneumatic loading will be automatically modulated to maintain a safe EGT.
choices.B: electrical loading will be automatically modulated to maintain a safe EGT.
choices.C: temperature limits and loads must be carefully monitored by the operator to maintain a safe EGT.

Turbine Engines.explanation: When maximum shaft output power is combined with pneumatic loading on an APU, the pneumatic load is automatically modulated by the APU control system to prevent exceeding exhaust gas temperature (EGT) limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Auxiliary Power Units)

Turbine Engines.question: On APU's equipped with a free turbine and load compressor, the primary function of the load compressor is to

choices.A: provide air for combustion and cooling in the engine gas path.
choices.B: provide bleed air for aircraft pneumatic systems.
choices.C: supply the turning force for operation of the APU generator(s).

Turbine Engines.explanation: On APUs equipped with a free turbine and load compressor, the load compressor is dedicated to supplying bleed air to the aircraft's pneumatic systems, such as air conditioning and pressurization, while the power turbine drives the generator. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Auxiliary Power Units)

Turbine Engines.question: Using compressor bleed air directed into a nozzle, a vortex dissipater's primary function is to

choices.B: increase lift capability of aircraft flaps.
choices.C: destroy vortices that can suck debris from the ground into engines that are mounted in pods that are low to the ground.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A vortex dissipater uses compressor bleed air directed through a nozzle beneath low-mounted engine pods to disrupt ground vortices that could otherwise ingest debris and foreign objects into the engine intake. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Inlet Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: In a turbine engine with a dual-spool, axial-flow compressor, the compressor and high-pressure turbine are

choices.B: driven by a single shaft.
choices.C: driven by a single, high-pressure turbine.

Turbine Engines.explanation: In a dual-spool axial-flow compressor design, the high-pressure (N2) compressor and its dedicated high-pressure turbine are mechanically connected by a single concentric shaft, allowing them to operate as an independent spool. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Compressor Section)

Turbine Engines.question: What type of combustion chamber is used in a turbine engine when engine length is critical?

choices.B: Annular.
choices.C: Reverse-flow.

Turbine Engines.explanation: A reverse-flow combustion chamber doubles back the gas flow, effectively shortening the overall engine length — making it ideal for turboprop and turboshaft installations where minimizing engine length is critical. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Combustion Section)

Turbine Engines.question: A malfunction that causes the turbocharger waste gate or controller to fluctuate will

choices.A: have no effect on manifold pressure.
choices.B: cause engine surges due to an erratic manifold pressure.
choices.C: cause the oil pressure to the turbocharger to decrease.

Turbine Engines.explanation: If the turbocharger waste gate or controller fluctuates, the amount of exhaust gas driving the turbine will vary erratically, causing corresponding fluctuations in manifold pressure that produce engine surging. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Supercharging/Turbocharging)

Turbine Engines.question: When servicing a turbine engine oil system, the proper type of servicing equipment and procedures can be found in the

choices.B: aircraft maintenance manual (MM).
choices.C: Type Certificate Data Sheet (TCDS).

Turbine Engines.explanation: Specific servicing equipment types, approved oils, capacities, and proper procedures for a given engine are all found in the aircraft maintenance manual (MM), which is the authoritative technical reference for maintenance tasks. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Turbine Engine Lubrication Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: (Refer to Figure 4.) The following data concerning the installation of an electrical unit is known: current requirements for continuous operation -- 11 amperes; measured cable length -- 45 feet; system voltage -- 28 volts (do not exceed 1 volt drop); cable in conduit and bundles. What is the minimum size copper electrical cable that may be selected?
Turbine Engines.explanation: Using the FAA wire sizing chart (Figure 4) for 11 amperes continuous duty, 45-foot cable length, 28-volt system with a 1-volt drop limit, and installed in conduit/bundles (which requires derating), a No. 10 copper wire is the minimum size that satisfies all constraints without exceeding the allowable voltage drop. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 11 — Aircraft Electrical Systems)

Turbine Engines.question: (Refer to Figure 6.) Which curve most nearly represents an aircraft engine's air-fuel ratio throughout its operating range?
Turbine Engines.explanation: Curve 1 on Figure 6 represents an air-fuel ratio that starts rich at low power and progressively leans out as power increases, which matches the typical fuel scheduling of a gas turbine engine across its operating range. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 13 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Before attempting to start a radial engine that has been shut down for more than 30 minutes,

choices.A: turn the propeller by hand three or four revolutions in the opposite direction of normal rotation to check for liquid lock.
choices.B: turn the ignition switch on before energizing the starter.
choices.C: turn the propeller by hand three to four revolutions in the normal direction of rotation to check for liquid lock.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Rotating the propeller in the normal direction of rotation three to four revolutions before starting allows any accumulated oil or fuel (hydrostatic lock) to be expelled through the lower cylinder exhaust ports, preventing hydraulic lock damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: An engine misses in both the right and left positions of the magneto switch. The quickest method for locating the trouble is to

choices.A: check for one or more cold cylinders.
choices.B: perform a compression check.
choices.C: check each spark plug.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: When a miss occurs on both magneto positions, the fault is common to both ignition systems and most likely involves a dead or misfiring cylinder; checking for cold cylinders by feel or temperature is the quickest way to isolate which cylinder is not firing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Ignition System Maintenance)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: During ground check an engine is found to be rough-running, the magneto drop is normal, and the manifold pressure is higher than normal for any given RPM. The trouble may be caused by

choices.A: several spark plugs fouled on different cylinders.
choices.B: a leak in the intake manifold.
choices.C: a dead cylinder.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A manifold pressure that is higher than normal for a given RPM combined with rough running points to an intake manifold leak, which allows unmetered air to enter and creates an excessively lean mixture in the affected cylinder(s), disrupting smooth operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Induction Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Preignition is caused by improper ignition timing.
choices.B: Detonation occurs when an area of the combustion chamber becomes incandescent and ignites the air-fuel mixture in advance of normal timed ignition.
choices.C: Preignition is caused by incandescent objects in the cylinder.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Preignition occurs when a hot spot or incandescent object inside the cylinder (such as a carbon deposit or overheated spark plug electrode) ignites the fuel-air charge before the timed spark, as distinguished from detonation which is an abnormal combustion pressure wave. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Ignition System Operation)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following conditions would most likely lead to detonation?

choices.B: Use of fuel with too high an octane rating.
choices.C: Use of fuel with too low an octane rating.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Detonation occurs when the fuel-air mixture explodes uncontrollably rather than burning progressively; using fuel with an octane rating too low for the engine's compression ratio means the fuel cannot resist the heat and pressure, causing spontaneous ignition before the flame front arrives. Higher octane fuels are specifically designed to resist this premature detonation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Operation)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of these conditions will cause an engine to have an increased tendency to detonate?

choices.B: High manifold pressure or high intake air temperature.
choices.C: Late ignition timing and an overheated engine.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: High manifold pressure increases cylinder pressure and temperature, while high intake air temperature raises the charge temperature, both of which reduce the fuel-air mixture's ability to resist spontaneous combustion, thereby increasing the tendency to detonate. These conditions push the mixture beyond the fuel's critical pressure and temperature limits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Operation)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Newton's first law of motion, generally termed the law of inertia, states:

choices.A: To every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
choices.B: Force is proportional to the product of mass and acceleration.
choices.C: Every body persists in its state of rest, or of motion in a straight line, unless acted upon by some outside force.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Newton's First Law of Motion — the law of inertia — states that an object at rest or in uniform straight-line motion will remain in that state unless acted upon by an external force; this principle is foundational to understanding why a starter motor must overcome an engine's inertia to initiate rotation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What type of electric motor is generally used with a direct-cranking engine starter?

choices.B: Direct current series-wound motor.
choices.C: Synchronous motor.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A DC series-wound motor produces very high torque at low speeds (stall torque), which is precisely the characteristic needed to overcome the high initial inertia of an aircraft engine during cranking. Its torque output decreases as speed increases, making it ideal for starter applications. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: If the points in a vibrator-type voltage regulator stick in the closed position while the generator is operating, what will be the probable result?

choices.B: Generator output voltage will not be affected.
choices.C: Generator output voltage will increase.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In a vibrator-type voltage regulator, the points normally cycle open and closed to insert resistance into the field circuit, limiting generator output; if the points stick closed, resistance is never inserted, the field current remains at maximum, and generator output voltage rises uncontrolled. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Electric motors are often classified according to the method of connecting the field coils and armature. Aircraft engine starter motors are generally of which type?

choices.B: Series.
choices.C: Shunt (parallel).

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Aircraft engine starter motors are series-wound because this configuration provides maximum torque at zero or low RPM, which is necessary to overcome the static inertia of the engine during initial cranking. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following aircraft circuits does NOT contain a fuse/circuit breaker?

choices.B: Air-conditioning circuit.
choices.C: Starter circuit.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The starter circuit draws extremely high current for only a very brief period during engine cranking, and because it is a short-duration, intermittent-duty circuit, it is not protected by a fuse or circuit breaker — including such a device would cause nuisance tripping under normal high-current starting loads. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Starter brushes should be replaced when they are worn down to

choices.B: three-quarters of the original length.
choices.C: one-quarter of the original length.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: FAA guidance specifies that starter brushes should be replaced when they have worn down to one-quarter of their original length, because below this point the brush may not maintain adequate spring pressure or contact area, leading to arcing and commutator damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When does current flow through the coil of a solenoid-operated electrical switch?

choices.A: Continually, as long as the aircraft's electrical system master switch is on.
choices.B: Continually, as long as the control circuit is complete.
choices.C: Only until the movable points contact the stationary points.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In a solenoid-operated switch, current flows through the coil continuously as long as the control circuit remains complete, because the magnetic field generated by continuous current flow is what holds the movable plunger and contacts in the closed (energized) position. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which Federal Aviation Regulation requirement prevents the use of automatic reset circuit breakers?

choices.B: 14 CFR Part 23.
choices.C: 14 CFR Part 91.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: 14 CFR Part 23 (airworthiness standards for normal, utility, acrobatic, and commuter category airplanes) prohibits the use of automatic-reset circuit breakers because they could allow repeated reconnection of a faulted circuit without pilot awareness, creating a potential fire or electrical hazard. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The time/current capacities of a circuit breaker or fuse must be

choices.A: above those of the associated conductor.
choices.B: equal to those of the associated conductor.
choices.C: below those of the associated conductor.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Circuit protection devices must be rated below the current-carrying capacity of the conductor they protect so that the fuse or breaker opens before the wire overheats and its insulation is damaged, ensuring the wire itself is the protected component. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Most modern aircraft use circuit breakers rather than fuses to protect their electrical circuits.
choices.B: Part 23 requires that all electrical circuits incorporate some form of circuit protective device.
choices.C: Most modern aircraft use fuses rather than circuit breakers to protect their electrical circuits.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Modern aircraft predominantly use circuit breakers rather than fuses because circuit breakers can be reset in flight, serve a dual role as switches in some installations, and provide more reliable overcurrent protection without requiring spare fuses to be carried on board. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Electrical circuit protection devices are rated based on the amount of voltage that can be carried without overheating the circuit breaker.
choices.B: Electrical circuit protection devices are rated based on the amount of current that can be carried without overheating the wiring insulation.
choices.C: A trip-free circuit breaker makes it possible to manually hold the circuit closed when excessive current is flowing.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Circuit protection devices are rated in amperes based on the maximum current the protected conductor can carry continuously without the wiring insulation overheating and failing, not on voltage; a trip-free circuit breaker, by contrast, cannot be manually held closed against an overload. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The magnetic circuit of a magneto consists of a permanent multi-pole rotating magnet. The core is made of

choices.B: soft iron.
choices.C: electrical steel.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The magneto's rotating magnet core is made of soft iron because soft iron has high magnetic permeability and low residual magnetism, allowing the magnetic flux to change direction rapidly as the magnet rotates, which is essential for inducing the alternating voltage needed for ignition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Ignition Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: How is the strength of a magneto magnet checked?

choices.A: Hold the points open and check the output of the primary coil with an ac ammeter while operating the magneto at a specified speed.
choices.B: Check the ac voltage reading at the breaker points.
