choices.C: Check the output of the secondary coil with an ac ammeter while operating the magneto at a specified speed.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Magnet strength is verified by holding the breaker points open (removing the shorting path) and measuring the AC output of the primary coil with an AC ammeter while spinning the magneto at a specified RPM; the measured current is compared to the manufacturer's minimum acceptable value. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Ignition Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The E-gap angle is usually defined as the number of degrees between the neutral position of the rotating magnet and the position

choices.B: where the contact points open.
choices.C: of greatest magnetic flux density.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The E-gap (efficiency gap) is defined as the number of degrees the rotating magnet has moved past the neutral position to the point where the breaker points open, which is where the rate of flux change—and thus the induced EMF in the primary coil—is at its maximum. Opening the points at this precise moment collapses the primary field and induces the highest possible secondary voltage for a strong spark. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The greatest density of flux lines in the magnetic circuit of a rotating magnet-type magneto occurs when the magnet is in what position?

choices.A: Full alignment with the field shoe faces.
choices.B: A certain angular displacement beyond the neutral position, referred to as E-gap angle or position.
choices.C: The position where the contact points open.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: When the rotating magnet is in full alignment with the field shoe faces, the magnetic flux through the core is at its greatest density because the reluctance of the magnetic circuit is at its minimum, allowing the maximum number of flux lines to pass through the coil core. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Magneto breaker point opening relative to the position of the rotating magnet and distributor rotor (internal timing) can be set most accurately

choices.B: during assembly of the magneto before installation on the engine.
choices.C: by setting the points roughly at the required clearance before installing the magneto and then making the fine breaker point adjustment after installation to compensate for wear in the magneto drive train.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Internal timing—setting the breaker points to open at the exact E-gap position relative to the rotating magnet and distributor rotor—is most accurately accomplished during bench assembly of the magneto before installation, where precision timing tools can be applied directly to the magneto components without drivetrain variables. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Why are high-tension ignition cables frequently routed from the distributors to the spark plugs in flexible metallic conduits?

choices.B: To reduce the formation of corona and nitric oxide on the cable insulation.
choices.C: To reduce the effect of the high-frequency electromagnetic waves emanated during operation.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: High-tension ignition cables are routed in flexible metallic conduits primarily to shield the surrounding aircraft systems from the high-frequency electromagnetic interference (radio frequency interference) generated by the high-voltage pulses traveling through the cables during operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What will be the result of increasing the gap of the breaker points in a magneto?

choices.A: Retard the spark and increase its intensity.
choices.B: Advance the spark and decrease its intensity.
choices.C: Retard the spark and decrease its intensity.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Increasing the breaker point gap causes the points to open earlier in the rotation cycle, advancing the spark timing; however, because the points open before the magnet reaches the optimal E-gap position (peak rate of flux change), the induced primary current—and thus spark intensity—is reduced. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What is the purpose of a safety gap in some magnetos?

choices.A: To discharge the secondary coil's voltage if an open occurs in the secondary circuit.
choices.B: To ground the magneto when the ignition switch is off.
choices.C: To prevent flashover in the distributor.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A safety gap provides an alternate discharge path for the high secondary voltage if an open circuit occurs in the secondary (e.g., a broken lead or failed spark plug), preventing insulation damage or arcing at unintended locations by allowing the energy to safely discharge across the gap. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When timing a magneto internally, the alignment of the timing marks indicates that the

choices.B: magnets are in the neutral position.
choices.C: magnets are in the E-gap position.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: During internal timing, the alignment of the timing marks on the magneto indicates that the rotating magnet has reached the E-gap position—the precise angular displacement past neutral where the breaker points are set to open and the rate of flux change is greatest. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When internally timing a magneto, the breaker points begin to open when the rotating magnet is

choices.A: fully aligned with the pole shoes.
choices.B: a few degrees past full alignment with the pole shoes.
choices.C: a few degrees past the neutral position.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The breaker points are timed to open when the rotating magnet is a few degrees past the neutral position (the E-gap position), because it is at this point—not at full alignment—that the rate of change of magnetic flux is greatest, producing the maximum induced EMF in the primary coil. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Magneto timing drift is caused by erosion of the breaker points and

choices.B: wear of the cam followers.
choices.C: loss of magnetism in the rotor.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: As the cam follower (rubbing block) wears down over time, the breaker point gap decreases, causing the points to open later and retarding the ignition timing—this progressive drift in timing is a primary maintenance concern requiring periodic adjustment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What is the electrical location of the primary capacitor in a high-tension magneto?

choices.A: In parallel with the breaker points.
choices.B: In series with the breaker points.
choices.C: In series with the primary and secondary winding.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The primary capacitor is connected in parallel with the breaker points so that when the points begin to open, the capacitor absorbs the surge of primary current, preventing arcing across the points and allowing a rapid, clean collapse of the primary magnetic field to induce maximum secondary voltage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: In a high-tension ignition system, the current in the magneto secondary winding is

choices.A: conducted from the primary winding via the discharge of the capacitor.
choices.B: induced when the primary circuit is interrupted.
choices.C: induced when the primary circuit discharges via the breaker points.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: When the primary circuit is interrupted by the opening of the breaker points, the rapid collapse of the primary magnetic field induces a very high voltage in the secondary winding through mutual induction, which is what drives current through the secondary circuit to fire the spark plug. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When a 'Shower of Sparks' ignition system is activated at an engine start, a spark plug fires

choices.A: as soon as the advance breaker points open.
choices.B: only while both the retard and advance breaker points are closed.
choices.C: only while both the retard and advance breaker points are open.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In a Shower of Sparks system, the vibrator circuit produces a series of sparks beginning the moment the advance breaker points open, providing a retarded and repeated spark to aid starting; the firing sequence initiates as soon as those advance points open, not when both sets of points are in any particular combined state. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What is the radial location of the two north poles of a four-pole rotating magnet in a high-tension magneto?

choices.B: 270 apart.
choices.C: 90 apart.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In a four-pole rotating magnet, the poles alternate N-S-N-S around the rotor; with four poles equally spaced at 90° intervals, the two north poles are located 90° apart from each other (not 180°, which would describe a two-pole arrangement). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Magneto pole shoes are generally made of

choices.B: laminations of high-grade Alnico.
choices.C: pieces of high-carbon iron.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Pole shoes are laminated from high-grade soft iron because soft iron has high magnetic permeability and low retentivity, minimizing eddy current losses and hysteresis while efficiently channeling the rotating magnet's flux through the coil core. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Capacitance afterfiring in most modern spark plugs is reduced by the use of

choices.B: a built-in resistor in each plug.
choices.C: aluminum oxide insulation.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A built-in resistor in each spark plug suppresses the high-frequency oscillations (capacitance afterfiring) that occur after the main discharge by dissipating the residual electrical energy, which also reduces radio frequency interference and electrode erosion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What components make up the magnetic system of a magneto?

choices.A: Pole shoes, the pole shoe extensions, and the primary coil.
choices.B: Primary and secondary coils.
choices.C: Rotating magnet, the pole shoes, the pole shoe extensions, and the coil core.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The magnetic system of a magneto consists of the rotating permanent magnet, pole shoes, pole shoe extensions, and the coil core, which together form the magnetic circuit that generates the changing flux required for ignition. The primary and secondary coils are part of the electrical system, not the magnetic system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: In an aircraft ignition system, one of the functions of the capacitor is to

choices.A: regulate the flow of current between the primary and secondary coil.
choices.B: facilitate a more rapid collapse of the magnetic field in the primary coil.
choices.C: stop the flow of magnetic lines of force when the points open.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The capacitor (condenser) absorbs the surge of current when the breaker points open, preventing arcing and allowing the magnetic field in the primary coil to collapse rapidly, which induces a high-voltage pulse in the secondary winding. Without this rapid collapse, the secondary voltage would be too low to fire the spark plugs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When will the voltage in the secondary winding of a magneto, installed on a normally operating engine, be at its highest value?

choices.A: Just prior to spark plug firing.
choices.B: Toward the latter part of the spark duration when the flame front reaches its maximum velocity.
choices.C: Immediately after the breaker points close.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Secondary voltage reaches its highest value just prior to spark plug firing, at the moment the breaker points open and the primary magnetic field collapses most rapidly, inducing maximum EMF in the secondary winding. Once the plug fires, voltage drops as the energy is discharged across the gap. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: As an aircraft engine's speed is increased, the voltage induced in the primary coil of the magneto

choices.B: increases.
choices.C: varies with the setting of the voltage regulator.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: As engine speed increases, the rotating magnet turns faster, causing the magnetic flux to change more rapidly through the coil core, which induces a higher voltage in the primary winding per Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When internally timing a magneto, the breaker points begin to open when

choices.A: the piston has just passed TDC at the end of the compression stroke.
choices.B: the magnet poles are a few degrees beyond the neutral position.
choices.C: the magnet poles are fully aligned with the pole shoes.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: During internal timing, the breaker points are set to begin opening when the rotating magnet is a few degrees past magnetic neutral (the E-gap position), because this is the point of greatest rate of flux change and thus maximum primary current, ensuring optimum spark energy. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The purpose of a safety gap in a magneto is to

choices.A: prevent burning out the primary winding.
choices.B: protect the high-voltage winding from damage.
choices.C: prevent burning of contact points.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A safety gap provides an alternate discharge path to ground if the high-tension circuit becomes open-circuited, preventing excessive voltage buildup that would damage the secondary winding insulation. It essentially acts as a voltage-limiting protective device for the coil. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: A defective primary capacitor in a magneto is indicated by

choices.A: a fine-grained frosted appearance of the breaker points.
choices.B: burned and pitted breaker points.
choices.C: a weak spark.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A defective or failed primary capacitor cannot suppress arcing across the breaker points when they open, causing excessive arcing that burns and pits the contact surfaces. A fine-grained frosted appearance is associated with normal wear or a weak capacitor, not a fully defective one. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: A magneto ignition switch is connected

choices.A: in series with the breaker points.
choices.B: parallel to the breaker points.
choices.C: in series with the primary capacitor and parallel to the breaker points.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The magneto ignition switch is wired in parallel with the breaker points so that when the switch is placed in the OFF position, it grounds the primary circuit, preventing the points from interrupting current flow and thus stopping spark production. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The spark is produced in a magneto ignition system when the breaker points are

choices.B: beginning to open.
choices.C: fully closed.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The spark is produced at the moment the breaker points begin to open, because this interrupts primary current flow, causing the magnetic field to collapse rapidly and inducing the high-voltage pulse in the secondary winding that fires the spark plug. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Shielding is used on spark plug and ignition wires to

choices.A: protect the wires from short circuits as a result of chafing or rubbing.
choices.B: prevent outside electromagnetic emissions from disrupting the operation of the ignition system.
choices.C: prevent interference with radio reception.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Shielding on ignition wires and spark plugs contains the high-frequency electromagnetic emissions generated by the ignition system, preventing them from interfering with aircraft radio and navigation equipment reception. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What is the purpose of using an impulse coupling with a magneto?

choices.A: To absorb impulse vibrations between the magneto and the engine.
choices.B: To compensate for backlash in the magneto and the engine gears.
choices.C: To produce a momentary high rotational speed of the magneto.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: An impulse coupling temporarily holds back the magneto rotor during engine cranking, then releases it suddenly to produce a brief high rotational speed, generating a strong spark at low cranking RPM and at the correct retarded timing for starting. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Aircraft magneto housings are usually ventilated in order to

choices.A: prevent the entrance of outside air which may contain moisture.
choices.B: allow heated air from the accessory compartment to keep the internal parts of the magneto dry.
choices.C: provide cooling and remove corrosive gases produced by normal arcing.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Magneto housings are ventilated to allow cooling airflow through the unit and to expel the corrosive gases produced by normal arcing at the distributor and breaker points, which would otherwise degrade internal components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Failure of an engine to cease firing after turning the magneto switch off is an indication of

choices.B: an open P-lead to ground.
choices.C: a grounded magneto switch.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: If the P-lead (primary lead) between the magneto and the ignition switch is open, the switch cannot ground the primary circuit, so the magneto continues to fire regardless of switch position. This is a critical safety concern during engine shutdown. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Alignment of the marks provided for internal timing of a magneto indicates that the

choices.A: breaker points are just beginning to close for cylinder 1.
choices.B: magneto is in E-gap position.
choices.C: Cylinder 1 is on TDC of compression stroke.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The internal timing marks on a magneto align when the rotating magnet is at the E-gap position, which is the point of maximum rate of flux change and where the breaker points are set to begin opening for optimum spark energy output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When using a timing light to time a magneto to an aircraft engine, the magneto switch should be placed in the

choices.B: OFF position.
choices.C: LEFT or RIGHT position (either one).

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: When using a timing light to time a magneto to the engine, the magneto switch must be in the OFF position so the timing light can be connected to the P-lead circuit and detect breaker point opening without the system actually firing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What is the difference between a low-tension and a high-tension engine ignition system?

choices.A: A low-tension system produces relatively low voltage at the spark plug as compared to a high-tension system.
choices.B: A high-tension system is designed for high-altitude aircraft, while a low-tension system is for low- to medium-altitude aircraft.
choices.C: A low-tension system uses a transformer coil near the spark plugs to boost voltage, while the high-tension system voltage is constant from the magneto to the spark plugs.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In a low-tension ignition system, relatively low voltage is carried from the magneto through the harness, and a transformer coil located near each spark plug steps up the voltage just before firing; in a high-tension system, the magneto itself generates the full high voltage that travels directly through the harness to the spark plugs without any boost. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What test instrument could be used to test an ignition harness for suspected leakage?

choices.B: A high voltage dc voltmeter.
choices.C: A high amperage dc ammeter.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A high-tension lead tester applies a high voltage to the ignition harness and detects current leakage through degraded insulation, making it the correct instrument for diagnosing harness leakage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The amount of voltage generated in any magneto secondary coil is determined by the number of windings and by the

choices.A: rate of buildup of the magnetic field around the primary coil.
choices.B: rate of collapse of the magnetic field around the primary coil.
choices.C: amount of charge released by the capacitor.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The high voltage induced in the magneto secondary coil is a function of the turns ratio and the rate at which the magnetic field collapses around the primary coil when the breaker points open, because a rapid collapse produces a large rate of flux change (dΦ/dt) and thus maximum induced EMF. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Magneto breaker points must be timed to open when the

choices.A: rotating magnet is positioned a few degrees before neutral.
choices.B: greatest magnetic field stress exists in the magnetic circuit.
choices.C: rotating magnet is in the full register position.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Breaker points must open at the position of greatest magnetic stress (E-gap), which is a few degrees past the neutral position of the rotating magnet, because this is where the rate of flux change is greatest and the highest secondary voltage is induced. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: In reference to a 'Shower of Sparks' ignition system,

choices.A: the retard breaker points are designed to keep the affected ignition system operating if the advance breaker points should fail during normal engine operation (after start).
choices.B: the timed opening of the retard breaker points is designed to prevent engine "kickback" during start.
choices.C: the timed opening of the retard breaker points is designed to allow engine "kickback" during start.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The retard breaker points in a Shower of Sparks system are timed to fire later (retarded) in the compression stroke during engine start, which prevents the engine from kicking back against the starter or the person hand-propping it. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: In a low-tension ignition system, each spark plug requires an individual

choices.B: breaker assembly.
choices.C: secondary coil.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Because a low-tension system transmits low voltage through the harness, each spark plug requires its own individual secondary (transformer) coil mounted nearby to step the voltage up to the level needed to fire the plug. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What will be the effect if the spark plugs are gapped too wide?

choices.B: Hard starting.
choices.C: Lead damage.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Excessively wide spark plug gaps require a higher voltage to ionize the gap and initiate a spark; if the magneto cannot reliably supply that voltage, the result is a misfire and hard starting. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When removing a shielded spark plug, which of the following is most likely to be damaged?

choices.B: Shell section.
choices.C: Core insulator.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: When removing a shielded spark plug, the ceramic core insulator is the most vulnerable component because it can crack or chip if the plug is cross-threaded, over-torqued, or impacted during removal. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following breaker point characteristics is associated with a faulty capacitor?

choices.B: Fine grained.
choices.C: Coarse grained.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A faulty (open or weak) capacitor fails to absorb the energy of the collapsing primary field, allowing excessive arcing across the breaker points that erodes and roughens the contact surfaces, producing a coarse-grained appearance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: How are most radial engine spark plug wires connected to the distributor block?

choices.B: By use of self-locking cable ferrules.
choices.C: By use of terminal sleeves and retaining nuts.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: On most radial engines, the high-tension ignition leads are secured to the distributor block using terminal sleeves inserted into the block and retained by nuts, providing a reliable high-voltage connection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Capacitance afterfiring of a spark plug is caused by

choices.A: the stored energy in the ignition shielded lead unloading after normal timed ignition.
choices.B: excessive center electrode erosion.
choices.C: constant polarity firing.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Capacitance afterfiring occurs because energy stored in the capacitance of the shielded ignition lead discharges after the primary spark event, creating a secondary, unintended spark that can ignite residual fuel-air mixture at the wrong time. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When a magneto is operating, what is the probable cause for a shift in internal timing?

choices.A: The rotating magnet loses its magnetism.
choices.B: The distributor gear teeth are wearing on the rotor gear teeth.
choices.C: The cam follower wear and/or the breaker points wear.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: As the cam follower rubbing block and breaker point contacts wear during normal operation, the effective point opening position shifts relative to the cam lobe, causing a change in the E-gap timing and altering when the points open. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What should be used to clean grease or carbon tracks from capacitors or coils that are used in magnetos?

choices.B: Acetone.
choices.C: Naphtha.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: FAA guidance specifies that approved solvents (not acetone or naphtha, which can damage certain materials or leave residues) should be used to clean grease or carbon tracks from magneto capacitors and coils to avoid damaging insulating materials. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The electrical circuit from the spark plug back to the magneto is completed by grounding through the

choices.B: P-lead.
choices.C: cockpit switch.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The spark plug firing circuit is completed by the return path flowing from the spark plug shell through the engine structure (cylinder head, crankcase) back to the magneto housing ground, making the engine structure the return conductor. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Spark plugs are considered worn out when the

choices.A: electrodes have worn away to about one-half of their original dimensions.
choices.B: center electrode edges have become rounded.
choices.C: electrodes have worn away to about two-thirds of their original dimensions.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: FAA standards establish that spark plugs are considered worn out and must be replaced when electrode material has eroded to approximately one-half of the original electrode dimensions, as further use risks misfiring and engine damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following could cause damage to the nose ceramic or to the electrode of an aircraft spark plug?

choices.A: Plug installed without a copper gasket.
choices.B: Improper gapping procedure.
choices.C: Excessive magneto voltage.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Using improper gapping tools or technique—such as bending the center electrode or using a feeler gauge that pries against the ceramic insulator—can crack the nose ceramic or damage the electrode tip. The correct method requires bending only the ground electrode using an approved gapping tool. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition System Maintenance)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Sharp bends should be avoided in ignition leads primarily because

choices.A: weak points may develop in the insulation through which high tension current can leak.
choices.B: ignition lead wire conductor material is brittle and may break.
choices.C: ignition lead shielding effectiveness will be reduced.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: High-tension ignition leads carry very high voltages, and sharp bends stress the insulating material, creating weak spots where this high-voltage current can arc through or leak to ground, causing misfires and potential damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Harness)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: In a high-tension ignition system, a primary capacitor of too low a capacity will cause

choices.B: excessively high secondary voltage.
choices.C: the breaker contacts to burn.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The primary capacitor (condenser) absorbs the energy spike when the breaker points open; if its capacitance is too low, it cannot adequately suppress the arc, causing the points to burn excessively due to the sustained arcing across them. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Theory of Operation)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following, obtained during magneto check at 1,700 RPM, indicates a short (grounded) circuit between the right magneto primary and the ignition switch?

choices.B: BOTH -- 1,700 RPM, R -- 0 RPM, L -- 1,700 RPM, OFF -- 0 RPM.
choices.C: BOTH -- 1,700 RPM, R -- 0 RPM, L -- 1,675 RPM, OFF -- 0 RPM.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A grounded circuit between the right magneto primary and the ignition switch means the right magneto is permanently grounded (disabled) regardless of switch position, so selecting RIGHT or OFF both produce the same reduced RPM, while LEFT and BOTH show full RPM—matching option A's pattern. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Inspection and Troubleshooting)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: If an aircraft ignition switch is turned off and the engine continues to run normally, the trouble is probably caused by

choices.A: an open ground lead in the magneto.
choices.B: arcing magneto breaker points.
choices.C: primary lead grounding.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Turning the ignition switch OFF grounds the magneto primary circuit to stop firing; if the ground lead (P-lead) is open (broken or disconnected), the magneto primary cannot be grounded by the switch, so the engine continues to run normally and the propeller remains dangerous even with the switch off. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Switch and Grounding)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When the ignition switch of a single (reciprocating) engine aircraft is turned to the OFF position,

choices.A: the primary circuits of both magnetos are grounded.
choices.B: the secondary circuits of both magnetos are opened.
choices.C: all circuits are automatically opened.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The ignition switch controls the magneto by grounding the primary circuit; in the OFF position, the switch connects the primary circuits of both magnetos to ground, which prevents the buildup of the magnetic field needed to generate a spark. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Switch and Grounding)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: If staggered ignition timing is used, the

choices.A: spark plug nearest the exhaust valve will fire first.
choices.B: spark will be automatically advanced as engine speed increases.
choices.C: spark plug nearest the intake valve will fire first.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In staggered ignition timing, the spark plug closest to the exhaust valve fires first because combustion gases near the exhaust valve are hotter and ignite more readily, allowing the flame front to travel efficiently across the cylinder toward the cooler intake side. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Timing)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The term 'reach,' as applied to spark plug design and/or type, indicates the

choices.A: linear distance from the shell gasket seat to the end of the threads on the shell skirt.
choices.B: length of center electrode exposed to the flame of combustion.
choices.C: length of the shielded barrel.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The 'reach' of a spark plug is defined as the linear distance from the shell gasket seat to the bottom of the threaded shell skirt, which determines how far the plug protrudes into the combustion chamber. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plug Design and Construction)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When testing a magneto distributor block for electrical leakage, which of the following pieces of test equipment should be used?

choices.B: A continuity tester.
choices.C: A high-range ammeter.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Distributor blocks must be tested for high-voltage electrical leakage because they handle high-tension current; a high-tension harness tester applies the appropriate high voltage needed to reveal carbon tracking or insulation breakdown that a continuity or ammeter test would not detect. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Inspection and Troubleshooting)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: The platinum and iridium ground electrodes used on fine wire spark plugs are extremely brittle and can be broken if they are improperly handled or adjusted.
choices.B: When gapping massive-electrode spark plugs, a wire gauge should be inserted between the center and ground electrodes while moving the ground electrode to avoid setting the gap too close.
choices.C: The platinum and iridium ground electrodes used on fine wire spark plugs are extremely strong and not easily broken when handled or adjusted.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Fine wire spark plugs use platinum or iridium electrodes that are extremely brittle and can fracture if improperly bent or adjusted, which is why special care and approved gapping tools must be used when servicing them. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plug Maintenance)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: If a spark plug lead becomes grounded, the

choices.B: distributor rotor finger will discharge to the next closest electrode within the distributor.
choices.C: capacitor will break down.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: When a spark plug lead is grounded, the high-tension voltage in the distributor cannot reach that plug, so the rotor finger discharges to the next closest electrode within the distributor cap, potentially causing cross-firing or distributor damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Harness and Distribution)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following statements regarding magneto switch circuits is NOT true?

choices.A: In the BOTH position, the right and left magneto circuits are grounded.
choices.B: In the OFF position, neither the right nor left magneto circuits are open.
choices.C: In the RIGHT position, the right magneto circuit is open and the left magneto circuit is grounded.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In the BOTH position, both magneto circuits are left open (active/firing), not grounded; grounding a magneto's primary circuit disables it, so grounding both in the BOTH position would shut the engine down, making statement A false and therefore the NOT true answer. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Switch and Grounding)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When does battery current flow through the primary circuit of a battery ignition coil?

choices.A: Only when the breaker points are open.
choices.B: At all times when the ignition switch is on.
choices.C: When the breaker points are closed and the ignition switch is on.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In a battery ignition system, current flows through the primary coil circuit only when the breaker points are closed and the ignition switch is on, completing the circuit from the battery through the coil primary winding to ground. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Battery Ignition System Operation)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: In order to turn a magneto off, the primary circuit must be

choices.B: opened.
choices.C: shorted.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A magneto is a self-contained generator that cannot simply be 'turned off' by opening its circuit; it must be disabled by grounding (shorting) the primary circuit, which prevents the collapse of the magnetic field from inducing high-tension voltage in the secondary. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Switch and Grounding)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When performing a magneto ground check on an engine, correct operation is indicated

choices.B: no drop in RPM.
choices.C: a slight drop in RPM.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: During a magneto ground check, when the switch is moved to ground one magneto, the engine should show a slight RPM drop because it transitions from dual-ignition efficiency to single-ignition operation; no drop indicates the magneto is already grounded (P-lead fault), and excessive drop indicates a magneto or timing problem. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Inspection and Troubleshooting)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Defective spark plugs will cause the engine to run rough

choices.B: during cruise.
choices.C: at all speeds.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A defective spark plug fails to fire consistently regardless of engine power setting, causing misfires and rough running across the entire RPM range, not just at specific speeds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition System Maintenance)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: A spark plug is fouled when

choices.A: its spark grounds by jumping electrodes.
choices.B: it causes preignition.
choices.C: its spark grounds without jumping electrodes.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A fouled spark plug has its spark path grounded through conductive deposits (carbon, lead, oil) on the insulator or electrode before the spark can jump the gap, meaning it grounds without jumping electrodes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plug Inspection and Maintenance)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following would be cause for rejection of a spark plug?

choices.A: Carbon fouling of the electrode and insulator.
choices.B: Insulator tip cracked.
choices.C: Lead fouling of the electrode and insulator.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A cracked insulator tip is cause for immediate rejection because it compromises the dielectric integrity of the plug, which can lead to internal arcing, misfires, or engine damage; fouling conditions such as carbon or lead deposits may be cleaned and the plug returned to service. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plug Inspection and Maintenance)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What will be the result of using too hot a spark plug?

choices.B: Preignition.
choices.C: Burned capacitor.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A spark plug that is too hot retains excessive heat in the combustion chamber, raising temperatures enough to ignite the fuel-air mixture before the timed spark, resulting in preignition and potential engine damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plug Heat Range)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Ignition check during engine runup indicates excessive RPM drop during operation on the right magneto. The major portion of the RPM loss occurs rapidly after switching to the right magneto position (fast drop). The most likely cause is

choices.B: incorrect ignition timing on both magnetos.
choices.C: one or more dead cylinders.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A rapid, large RPM drop immediately upon switching to one magneto indicates that one or more cylinders are receiving no firing at all on that magneto, consistent with dead cylinders rather than the gradual loss associated with fouled plugs or timing issues. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition System Troubleshooting)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: If new breaker points are installed in a magneto on an engine, it will be necessary to time the

choices.A: magneto internally and the magneto to the engine.
choices.B: breaker points to the cylinder 1.
choices.C: magneto drive to the engine.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Replacing breaker points changes the internal timing relationship (E-gap position) within the magneto, so both the internal timing of the magneto (point opening relative to magnet position) and the external timing of the magneto to the engine (spark delivery relative to TDC) must be reset. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Timing)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Using a cold spark plug in a high-compression aircraft engine would probably result

choices.B: a fouled plug.
choices.C: detonation.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A cold spark plug dissipates heat too rapidly, causing deposits to accumulate on the cooler insulator tip and leading to fouling; cold plugs are intended for low-compression engines and are not suited for the higher heat environment of high-compression engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Spark Plug Heat Range)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The spark occurs at the spark plug when the ignition's

choices.B: primary circuit is completed.
choices.C: primary circuit is broken.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In a magneto ignition system, the spark at the plug occurs when the primary circuit is broken; the sudden collapse of the primary magnetic field induces a high-voltage surge in the secondary winding that fires the plug. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Operating Principles)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Ignition check during engine runup indicates a slow drop in RPM. This is usually caused by

choices.B: a defective high-tension lead.
choices.C: incorrect ignition timing or valve adjustment.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A gradual, slow RPM drop during magneto check is characteristic of incorrect ignition timing or valve adjustment, which reduces combustion efficiency progressively rather than causing the abrupt loss associated with a failed plug or lead. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition System Troubleshooting)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: If the ground wire of a magneto is disconnected at the ignition switch, the result will be the

choices.A: affected magneto will be isolated and the engine will run on the opposite magneto.
choices.B: engine will stop running.
choices.C: engine will not stop running when the ignition switch is turned off.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The magneto P-lead (ground wire) is the only means by which the ignition switch grounds and disables the magneto; if that wire is disconnected, the magneto remains live and the engine will continue to fire even with the switch in the OFF position, creating a serious safety hazard. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Grounding Circuit)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Advantages of dual ignition in aircraft engines are a more complete and quick combustion of the fuel, provides a backup magneto system, and

choices.A: permitting the use of lower grade fuels.
choices.B: increasing the intensity of the spark at the spark plugs.
choices.C: increasing the output power of the engine.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Dual ignition ignites the fuel-air charge from two points simultaneously, producing a more rapid and complete burn that increases combustion pressure and engine power output, in addition to improving reliability. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Dual Ignition Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: How does high-tension ignition shielding tend to reduce radio interference?

choices.A: Prevents ignition flashover at high altitudes.
choices.B: Reduces voltage drop in the transmission of high-tension current.
choices.C: Receives and grounds high-frequency waves coming from the magneto and high-tension ignition leads.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Ignition shielding is a continuous metallic enclosure around the magneto and high-tension leads that intercepts and grounds the high-frequency electromagnetic radiation generated by the ignition system, preventing it from radiating and interfering with radio equipment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition Shielding)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Which of the following are distinct circuits of a high tension magneto?

choices.B: Magnetic, E-gap, and P lead.
choices.C: Primary, P lead, and secondary.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A high-tension magneto contains three distinct circuits: the magnetic circuit (rotating magnet and pole pieces), the primary circuit (breaker points, capacitor, and primary coil winding), and the secondary circuit (high-voltage winding, distributor, and spark plug leads). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Operating Principles)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What is a result of 'flashover' in a distributor?

choices.A: Intense voltage at the spark plug.
choices.B: Reversal of current flow.
choices.C: Conductive carbon trail.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Flashover in a distributor occurs when high voltage arcs across the distributor block surface rather than through the intended path, leaving a carbonized conductive trail that can cause persistent misfiring and cross-firing between cylinders. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — High-Tension Ignition System Maintenance)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What is the approximate position of the rotating magnet in a high-tension magneto when the points first close?

choices.B: Neutral.
choices.C: A few degrees after neutral.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The breaker points close when the rotating magnet is at or near the neutral position, where the rate of flux change through the coil is greatest and the magnetic field is building, allowing maximum current to flow in the primary circuit before the points open at E-gap. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Magneto Operating Principles)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What component of a dual magneto is shared by both ignition systems?

choices.B: Rotating magnet.
choices.C: Capacitor.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: In a dual magneto, both ignition systems share a single rotating magnet (permanent magnet rotor) as the common magnetic flux source, while each system maintains its own separate distributor, coil, and points. This shared component reduces weight and complexity while providing two independent ignition circuits. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: What would be the result if a magneto breaker point mainspring did not have sufficient tension?

choices.B: The points will not open to the specified gap.
choices.C: The points will float or bounce.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Insufficient mainspring tension allows the breaker points to follow the cam lobe at low speeds but fail to track it at higher speeds, causing the points to float or bounce and resulting in misfiring or erratic ignition timing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The secondary coil of a magneto is grounded through the

choices.B: primary coil.
choices.C: ground side of the breaker points.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The secondary coil is electrically connected to the primary coil at one end and relies on the primary circuit's ground path through the breaker points to complete the secondary circuit; the secondary winding shares the primary coil as its ground reference within the magneto. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: In the aircraft magneto system, if the P-lead is disconnected, the magneto will be

choices.A: on regardless of ignition switch position.
choices.B: grounded regardless of ignition switch position.
choices.C: open regardless of ignition switch position.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The P-lead is the grounding wire that connects the magneto primary circuit to the ignition switch; if it is disconnected or broken, the magneto cannot be grounded by the switch and will remain in a 'hot' (live) condition regardless of switch position, creating a serious safety hazard. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: The purpose of an under current relay in a starter-generator system is to

choices.A: provide a backup for the starter relay.
choices.B: disconnect power from the starter-generator and ignition when sufficient engine speed is reached.
choices.C: keep current flow to the starter-generator under the circuit capacity maximum.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: An undercurrent relay monitors current draw to the starter-generator; when engine speed reaches self-sustaining levels, current draw drops below the relay's threshold, causing it to open and automatically disconnect starter power and ignition protection, ending the start cycle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: In a typical starter-generator system, under which of the following starting circumstances may it be necessary to use the start stop button?

choices.B: Hot start.
choices.C: Contacts stick open.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A hung start occurs when the engine lights off but fails to accelerate to idle speed, and the start-stop button may need to be activated to terminate the start attempt and prevent engine damage from prolonged sub-idle fuel flow and heat accumulation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When using an electric starter motor, current usage

choices.A: is highest at the start of motor rotation.
choices.B: remains relatively constant throughout the starting cycle.
choices.C: is highest just before starter cutoff (at highest RPM).

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: At the moment rotation begins, the starter motor is at a stall condition with no back-EMF to oppose current flow, so current draw is at its maximum; as the motor accelerates and back-EMF develops, current draw decreases. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: When using an electric starter motor, the current flow through it

choices.A: is highest just before starter cutoff (at highest RPM).
choices.B: remains relatively constant throughout the starting cycle.
choices.C: is highest at the start of motor rotation.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Because a DC electric motor produces no counter-electromotive force (back-EMF) when stationary, current is highest at initial motor rotation when rotor speed and back-EMF are zero, then decreases as the motor accelerates. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: A clicking sound heard at engine coast-down in a pneumatic starter incorporating a sprag clutch ratchet assembly is an indication of

choices.B: one or more broken pawl springs.
choices.C: the pawls re-contacting and riding on the ratchet gear.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: During engine coast-down, as starter turbine speed decreases, the pawls re-engage and ride on the ratchet gear teeth, producing a characteristic clicking sound that is considered normal operation of the sprag clutch ratchet assembly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Inspection of pneumatic starters by maintenance technicians usually includes checking the

choices.A: oil level and magnetic drain plug condition.
choices.B: stator and rotor blades for FOD.
choices.C: rotor alignment.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Routine maintenance inspection of pneumatic (air turbine) starters typically includes checking the oil level in the reduction gearbox and inspecting the magnetic drain plug for metal particle accumulation, which can indicate internal gear or bearing wear. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Air turbine starters are generally designed so that reduction gear distress or damage may be detected by

choices.A: characteristic sounds from the starter assembly during engine start.
choices.B: breakage of a shear section on the starter drive shaft.
choices.C: inspection of a magnetic chip detector.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Air turbine starters incorporate a magnetic chip detector in the reduction gearbox oil system; metallic particles collected on the detector indicate gear distress or damage, allowing maintenance technicians to identify problems before catastrophic failure occurs. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: Airflow to the pneumatic starter from a ground unit is normally prevented from causing starter overspeed during engine start by

choices.A: stator nozzle design that chokes airflow and stabilizes turbine wheel speed.
choices.B: activation of a flyweight cutout switch.
choices.C: a preset timed cutoff of the airflow at the source.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The stator nozzle in a pneumatic starter is designed to choke (reach critical/sonic flow) at a specific airflow rate, which inherently limits the mass flow and prevents turbine wheel overspeed regardless of supply pressure variations from the ground unit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: A safety feature usually employed in pneumatic starters that is used if the clutch does not release from the engine drive at the proper time during start is the

choices.B: spring coupling release.
choices.C: drive shaft shear point.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: A flyweight cutout switch is used as a safety device in pneumatic starters; if the clutch fails to disengage at the proper engine speed, the flyweights respond to excessive rotational speed and activate the switch to cut off airflow to the starter, preventing overspeed damage. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: A safety feature usually employed in direct-cranking starters that is used to prevent the starter from reaching burst speed is the

choices.B: stator nozzle design that chokes airflow and stabilizes turbine wheel speed.
choices.C: spring coupling release.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Direct-cranking electric starters use a stator nozzle design that chokes airflow to stabilize turbine speed, ensuring that even with maximum air supply pressure the turbine wheel cannot accelerate beyond its design limit and reach a destructive burst speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: An APU is usually rotated during start by

choices.B: a pneumatic starter.
choices.C: an electric starter.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: APUs are typically self-contained units located where bleed air or hydraulic power may not be available for starting, so an electric starter motor powered by the aircraft battery or ground power unit is the standard means of initial APU rotation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 5.) With power applied to the bus bar, what wire supplies standby power to the starter relay contact?
Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Wire 4 connects the bus bar directly to the starter relay contact, providing standby power so the relay can be energized when the start switch is activated. This is read directly from the circuit logic in Figure 5, where wire 4 is the feed path from the bus to the starter relay contact. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 5.) With power applied to the bus bar, what switch changes will allow the ignition exciters test switch to function?

choices.A: Engine master switch, battery switch, and power lever switch.
choices.B: Engine master switch, start switch, and test switch.
choices.C: Engine master switch and ignition switch.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: The ignition exciter test switch requires the engine master switch (to power the ignition circuit), the battery switch (to supply bus power), and the power lever switch (to confirm the lever is in the correct position) to all be in the appropriate positions before the test switch circuit is complete. This logical sequence is depicted in Figure 5's schematic for the exciter test circuit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 5.) If wire 8 is broken or disconnected after starter rotation is initiated, and the power lever is advanced, the

choices.B: starter will shut down, but the igniters will continue to fire.
choices.C: starting sequence will discontinue.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: Wire 8 carries the signal that holds the starter relay energized during the starting sequence, but once the power lever is advanced past a certain position, an alternate ground/hold path is established through the power lever switch, allowing the starting sequence to continue normally even if wire 8 is broken. This bypass logic is shown in Figure 5's starting circuit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Ignition and Starting Systems.question: (Refer to Figure 5.) When an external power source is connected to the aircraft,

choices.A: the battery cannot be connected to the bus.
choices.B: both battery power and external power are available to the bus.
choices.C: the starter relay coil has a path to ground.

Ignition and Starting Systems.explanation: When external power is connected, the external power relay closes and provides a ground path for the starter relay coil, enabling the starting circuit to function from the external source. Figure 5 shows that external power connection completes the starter relay coil ground circuit. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Ignition and Starting Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Which air-fuel mixture will result in the highest engine temperature (all other factors remaining constant)?

choices.A: A mixture leaner than a rich best-power mixture of .085.
choices.B: A mixture richer than a full-rich mixture of .087.
choices.C: A mixture leaner than a manual lean mixture of .060.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A mixture slightly leaner than the rich best-power ratio (approximately 0.085 fuel-to-air) places combustion temperatures at their peak because the mixture is closest to stoichiometric while still supporting maximum heat release; richer or significantly leaner mixtures both reduce peak cylinder temperatures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Reduced air density at high altitude has a decided effect on carburetion, resulting in a reduction of engine power by

choices.B: excessively leaning the fuel/air mixture.
choices.C: reducing fuel vaporization.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: At high altitude, reduced air density means less air mass enters the carburetor venturi, but the fuel metering system still delivers fuel proportional to the low-density airflow volume, causing the mixture to become excessively rich and reducing engine power output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Which statement pertaining to air-fuel ratios is true?

choices.A: The mixture ratio which gives the best power is richer than the mixture ratio which gives maximum economy.
choices.B: A rich mixture is faster burning than a normal mixture.
choices.C: The mixture ratio which gives maximum economy may also be designated as best power mixture.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Best-power mixture requires a richer fuel-to-air ratio (approximately 0.085) than best-economy (maximum economy) mixture (approximately 0.060–0.065) because extra fuel provides maximum heat energy release for power, while economy mixture is leaner to achieve the most work per unit of fuel. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Backfiring through the carburetor generally results from the use of

choices.B: excessively atomized fuel.
choices.C: an excessively rich mixture.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: An excessively lean mixture burns slowly and incompletely, and the flame front may still be active when the intake valve opens for the next cycle, causing the flame to propagate back through the induction system, which is the definition of a carburetor backfire. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Generally, when starting a turbine engine, the starter should be disengaged

choices.A: after the engine has reached self-accelerating speed.
choices.B: only after the engine has reached full idle RPM.
choices.C: when the ignition and fuel system are activated.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The starter should be disengaged once the engine reaches self-accelerating speed because at that point the engine's own combustion energy is sufficient to continue accelerating to idle RPM without motoring assistance, preventing starter overheating and wear. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: In an aircraft equipped with a pressure-drop type fuel flow indicating system, if one of the injector nozzles becomes restricted, this would cause a decrease in fuel flow with

choices.A: a decreased fuel flow indication on the gauge.
choices.B: an increased fuel flow indication on the gauge.
choices.C: no change in fuel flow indication on the gauge.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: In a pressure-drop fuel flow indicating system, the gauge reads the pressure differential across the injector nozzles; a restricted nozzle increases the pressure drop across the nozzle, which the gauge interprets as higher fuel flow even though actual flow has decreased. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Upon inspection of the spark plugs in an aircraft engine, the plugs were found caked with a heavy black soot. This indicates

choices.B: a rich mixture.
choices.C: a lean mixture.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Heavy black sooty deposits on spark plugs are a classic indicator of an excessively rich mixture, where unburned carbon from surplus fuel accumulates on the plug electrodes and insulator nose. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: An electronic engine control (EEC) is a system that receives engine operating information and

choices.A: adjusts a standard hydromechanical fuel control unit to obtain the most effective engine operation.
choices.B: develops the commands to various actuators to control engine parameters.
choices.C: controls engine operation according to ambient temperature, pressure, and humidity.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A standard (supervisory) EEC receives engine operating data and trims or adjusts the outputs of an underlying hydromechanical fuel control unit rather than replacing it, optimizing engine performance while retaining the hydromechanical unit as the primary control. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: A full-authority electronic engine control (EEC) is a system that receives all the necessary data for engine operation and

choices.A: adjusts a standard hydromechanical fuel control unit to obtain the most effective engine operation.
choices.B: develops the commands to various actuators to control engine parameters.
choices.C: controls engine operation according to ambient temperature, pressure, and humidity.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A full-authority EEC (FADEC) has complete authority over all engine parameters and directly issues commands to fuel metering valves, variable stator actuators, and other actuators without relying on a separate hydromechanical fuel control as the primary regulator. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: In a supervisory EEC system, any fault in the EEC that adversely affects engine operation

choices.A: causes redundant or backup units to take over and continue normal operation.
choices.B: usually degrades performance to the extent that continued operation can cause damage to the engine.
choices.C: causes an immediate reversion to control by the hydromechanical fuel control unit.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: In a supervisory EEC arrangement, the EEC only trims the hydromechanical fuel control unit; therefore, if the EEC fails or malfunctions, the system automatically reverts to the hydromechanical unit for primary fuel control, maintaining a safe operating condition. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Which of the following best describes the function of an automatic mixture control (AMC)?

choices.A: It compensates for change in the air density due to temperature and altitude.
choices.B: It compensates for the air pressure above the fuel in the float chamber.
choices.C: It compensates for the air pressure in the venturi of a float-type carburetor.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The automatic mixture control (AMC) uses an aneroid bellows that senses changes in air density caused by altitude and temperature variations, automatically adjusting the fuel metering to maintain the correct fuel-to-air ratio as conditions change. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 6 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: On a float-type carburetor, the purpose of the economizer valve is to

choices.A: provide extra fuel for sudden acceleration of the engine.
choices.B: maintain the leanest mixture possible during cruising best power.
choices.C: provide a richer mixture and cooling at maximum power output.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The economizer (power enrichment) valve enriches the mixture above cruise power settings to prevent detonation and provide charge cooling at maximum power output, where the lean best-economy cruise mixture would be insufficient. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The fuel metering force of a conventional float-type carburetor in its normal operating range is the difference between the pressure acting on the discharge nozzle located within the venturi and the pressure

choices.A: acting on the fuel in the float chamber.
choices.B: of the fuel as it enters the carburetor.
choices.C: of the air as it enters the venturi (impact pressure).

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The metering force in a float-type carburetor is the differential between the low pressure at the discharge nozzle in the venturi throat and the higher (near-atmospheric) pressure acting on the fuel surface in the float chamber, which pushes fuel out of the nozzle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: If the main air bleed of a float-type carburetor becomes clogged, the engine will run

choices.B: rich at rated power.
choices.C: rich at idling.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The main air bleed introduces air into the fuel stream to emulsify it and reduce the richening effect at higher airspeeds; if it is clogged, no air is bled in and the fuel discharge increases, causing an excessively rich mixture at rated power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Which method is commonly used to adjust the level of a float in a float-type carburetor?

choices.A: Lengthening or shortening the float shaft.
choices.B: Add or remove shims under the needle-valve seat.
choices.C: Change the angle of the float arm pivot.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Float level is adjusted by adding or removing shims (or gaskets) beneath the needle-valve seat, which raises or lowers the seat and therefore changes the point at which the float shuts off fuel flow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What is the possible cause of an engine running rich at full throttle if it is equipped with a float-type carburetor?

choices.B: Clogged main air bleed.
choices.C: Clogged atmospheric vent.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A clogged main air bleed prevents air from emulsifying the fuel, eliminating the leaning effect of the bleed and causing an excessively rich mixture at full throttle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: One of the things a metering orifice in a main air bleed helps to accomplish (at a given altitude) in a carburetor is

choices.A: pressure in the float chamber to increase as airflow through the carburetor increases.
choices.B: a progressively richer mixture as airflow through the carburetor increases.
choices.C: better fuel vaporization and control of fuel discharge, especially at lower engine speeds.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The main air bleed orifice introduces air bubbles into the fuel column, which improves atomization and vaporization and provides better metering control, particularly at lower engine speeds where venturi depression is relatively weak. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: A punctured float in a float-type carburetor will cause the fuel level to

choices.B: rise, and enrich the mixture.
choices.C: rise, and lean the mixture.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A punctured float takes on fuel, becomes heavier, and sinks lower in the float chamber, allowing the fuel level to rise above the normal setting; the higher fuel level reduces the distance between the fuel surface and the discharge nozzle tip, increasing fuel flow and enriching the mixture. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The back-suction mixture control system operates by

choices.A: varying the pressure within the venturi section.
choices.B: varying the pressure acting on the fuel in the float chamber.
choices.C: changing the effective cross-sectional area of the main metering orifice (jet).

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The back-suction mixture control connects the float chamber vent to a low-pressure (suction) region upstream of the throttle, reducing the pressure acting on the fuel surface in the float chamber and thereby reducing the metering pressure differential and fuel flow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: If an aircraft engine is equipped with a carburetor that is not compensated for altitude and temperature variations, the air-fuel mixture will become

choices.A: leaner as either the altitude or temperature increases.
choices.B: richer as the altitude increases and leaner as the temperature increases.
choices.C: richer as either the altitude or temperature increases.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: As altitude increases, air density decreases, so the same volume of air weighs less, delivering less oxygen while the fuel metering remains relatively constant, resulting in a richer mixture; similarly, higher temperatures reduce air density and produce the same richening effect. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Float-type carburetors which are equipped with economizers are normally set for

choices.A: their richest mixture delivery and leaned by means of the economizer system.
choices.B: the economizer system to supplement the main system supply at all engine speeds above idling.
choices.C: their leanest practical mixture delivery at cruising speeds and enriched by means of the economizer system at higher power settings.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Float-type carburetors with economizer systems are calibrated to deliver the leanest practical (best-economy) mixture at cruise power, and the economizer valve automatically enriches the mixture only when power settings rise above the cruise range. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: If a float-type carburetor becomes flooded, the condition is most likely caused by

choices.A: a leaking needle valve and seat assembly.
choices.B: the accelerating pump shaft being stuck.
choices.C: a clogged back-suction line.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A leaking needle valve and seat assembly allows fuel to continue entering the float chamber even after the correct level is reached, causing the chamber to overflow and flood the carburetor. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: If an engine is equipped with a float-type carburetor and the engine runs excessively rich at full throttle, a possible cause of the trouble is a clogged

choices.B: back-suction line.
choices.C: atmospheric vent line.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A clogged main air bleed eliminates the air-emulsification effect that normally leans the mixture at higher power settings, resulting in an excessively rich fuel discharge at full throttle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What occurs when a back-suction type mixture control is placed in IDLE CUTOFF?

choices.A: The fuel passages to the main and idle jets will be closed by a valve.
choices.B: The float chamber will be vented to a negative pressure area.
choices.C: The fuel passage to the idle jet will be closed by a valve.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: In the back-suction system, moving the control to IDLE CUTOFF vents the float chamber to the low-pressure (suction) side of the carburetor rather than to atmosphere, equalizing or reversing the pressure differential so no fuel is discharged and the engine stops. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Which of the following best describes the function of an altitude mixture control?

choices.A: Regulates the richness of the fuel/air charge entering the engine.
choices.B: Regulates the air pressure above the fuel in the float chamber.
choices.C: Regulates the air pressure in the venturi.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The altitude mixture control compensates for decreasing air density with increasing altitude by reducing fuel flow to maintain the correct fuel-to-air ratio, thereby regulating the richness of the mixture entering the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Select the correct statement concerning the idle system of a conventional float-type carburetor.

choices.A: The low-pressure area created in the throat of the venturi pulls the fuel from the idle passage.
choices.B: Climatic conditions have very little effect on idle mixture requirements.
choices.C: The low pressure between the edges of the throttle valve and the throttle body pulls the fuel from the idle passage.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: At idle, the throttle valve is nearly closed and the highest pressure drop occurs at the edge of the throttle plate where it meets the throttle body wall; this localized low pressure pulls fuel from the idle discharge port located just downstream of the throttle edge. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: On an engine equipped with a pressure-type carburetor, fuel supply in the idling range is ensured by the inclusion in the carburetor of

choices.A: a spring in the unmetered fuel chamber to supplement the action of normal metering forces.
