choices.B: an idle metering jet that bypasses the carburetor in the idle range.
choices.C: a separate boost venturi that is sensitive to the reduced airflow at start and idle speeds.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: At idle, airflow through the venturi is too low to create sufficient metering forces, so a spring in the unmetered fuel chamber of a pressure-type carburetor supplements those forces to maintain adequate fuel delivery. This spring ensures the fuel control valve opens enough to supply fuel even at very low engine speeds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Pressure-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The economizer system of a float-type carburetor performs which of the following functions?

choices.A: It supplies and regulates the fuel required for all engine speeds.
choices.B: It supplies and regulates the additional fuel required for all engine speeds above cruising.
choices.C: It regulates the fuel required for all engine speeds and all altitudes.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The economizer (power enrichment) system in a float-type carburetor is designed to enrich the mixture only above cruise power settings, providing additional fuel needed for cooling and power at high engine speeds. It remains closed during normal cruise and below, so it does not regulate fuel for all engine speeds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: How will the mixture of an engine be affected if the bellows of the automatic mixture control (AMC) in a pressure carburetor ruptures while the engine is operating at altitude?

choices.B: No change will occur until the altitude changes.
choices.C: It will become richer.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The AMC bellows is filled with a calibrated gas pressure; if it ruptures at altitude, the bellows collapses as though at sea level, causing the mixture control to allow more fuel than needed for the lower air density at altitude, resulting in an excessively rich mixture. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Pressure-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The fuel level within the float chamber of a properly adjusted float-type carburetor will be

choices.A: slightly higher than the discharge nozzle outlet.
choices.B: slightly lower than the discharge nozzle outlet.
choices.C: at the same level as the discharge nozzle outlet.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The fuel level in the float chamber must be slightly below the discharge nozzle outlet to prevent siphoning or gravity-fed fuel flow when the engine is not producing suction, which would cause an overly rich mixture or flooding. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Select the statement which is correct relating to a fuel level check of a float-type carburetor.

choices.A: Use 5 pounds fuel pressure for the test if the carburetor is to be used in a gravity fuel feed system.
choices.B: Block off the main and idle jets to prevent a continuous flow of fuel through the jets.
choices.C: Do not measure the level at the edge of the float chamber.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: When checking float level, the measurement must not be taken at the edge of the float chamber because fuel surface tension and meniscus effects at the wall can give an inaccurate reading; the measurement should be taken away from the chamber walls. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What carburetor component measures the amount of air delivered to the engine?

choices.B: Automatic mixture control.
choices.C: Venturi.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The venturi is the primary metering component that measures airflow by converting velocity into a pressure drop; this pressure differential is what drives fuel from the float chamber and signals how much air is entering the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Fuel is discharged for idling speeds on a float-type carburetor

choices.B: in the venturi.
choices.C: through the idle discharge air bleed.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: At idle speeds, the throttle valve is nearly closed and the venturi effect is insufficient to draw fuel from the main nozzle, so fuel is instead discharged from the idle discharge nozzle located downstream of the throttle valve where low pressure exists. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: When air passes through the venturi of a carburetor, what three changes occur?

choices.A: Velocity increases, temperature increases, and pressure decreases.
choices.B: Velocity decreases, temperature increases, and pressure increases.
choices.C: Velocity increases, temperature decreases, and pressure decreases.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: As air accelerates through the restricted throat of a venturi, its velocity increases while static pressure decreases; this process also causes adiabatic cooling, lowering the air temperature, which is why venturis can contribute to carburetor icing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Where is the throttle valve located on a float-type carburetor?

choices.A: Between the venturi and the discharge nozzle.
choices.B: After the main discharge nozzle and venturi.
choices.C: After the venturi and just before the main discharge nozzle.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: In a float-type carburetor, the throttle valve is positioned after (downstream of) both the venturi and the main discharge nozzle, so that when the throttle is nearly closed, the low pressure created at the throttle valve edge can be used to draw fuel from the idle discharge ports. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: An aircraft carburetor is equipped with a mixture control in order to prevent the mixture from becoming too

choices.B: rich at high altitudes.
choices.C: rich at high speeds.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: As altitude increases, air density decreases while the volume of fuel metered remains relatively constant, causing the mixture to become progressively richer; the mixture control allows the pilot or AMC to lean the mixture and restore the proper fuel-to-air ratio. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the carburetor venturi?

choices.B: Regulates the idle system.
choices.C: Limits the airflow at full throttle.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The venturi proportions the fuel/air mixture by creating a pressure differential that controls fuel flow, and it does limit maximum airflow, but idle system regulation is handled by the idle jet and idle discharge ports, not by the venturi. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Idle cutoff is accomplished on a carburetor equipped with a back-suction mixture control by

choices.A: introducing low pressure (intake manifold) air into the float chamber.
choices.B: turning the fuel selector valve to OFF.
choices.C: the positive closing of a needle and seat.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A back-suction mixture control routes low-pressure air from the intake manifold into the float chamber, reducing the pressure differential between the float chamber and the discharge nozzle to zero, which stops fuel flow and cuts off the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: One purpose of an air bleed in a float-type carburetor is to

choices.B: meter air to adjust the mixture.
choices.C: decrease fuel density and destroy surface tension.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The air bleed introduces air bubbles into the fuel stream before it reaches the discharge nozzle, which reduces fuel density and breaks down surface tension, allowing the fuel to atomize more readily and improving mixture quality across the power range. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: To determine the float level in a float-type carburetor, a measurement is usually made from the top of the fuel in the float chamber to the

choices.B: top of the float.
choices.C: centerline of the main discharge nozzle.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Float level is standardly measured from the parting surface (gasket surface) of the carburetor body down to the fuel level in the float chamber, providing a repeatable reference point for consistent and accurate adjustment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The throttle valve of float-type aircraft carburetors is located

choices.A: ahead of the venturi and main discharge nozzle.
choices.B: after the main discharge nozzle and ahead of the venturi.
choices.C: between the venturi and the engine.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: In a float-type carburetor, the throttle valve is located between the venturi and the engine (downstream of the venturi and main discharge nozzle), controlling the volume of the fuel/air mixture that flows into the intake manifold. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Float-Type Carburetors)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Why must a float-type carburetor supply a rich mixture during idle?

choices.A: Engine operation at idle results in higher than normal volumetric efficiency.
choices.B: Because at idling speeds the engine may not have enough airflow around the cylinders to provide proper cooling.
choices.C: Because of reduced mechanical efficiency during idle.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: At idle, airflow around the cylinders is minimal, reducing cooling effectiveness, so a richer mixture is required to help absorb heat and prevent overheating of engine components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What component is used to ensure fuel delivery during periods of rapid engine acceleration?

choices.B: Engine-driven fuel pump.
choices.C: Power enrichment unit.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: When the throttle is opened rapidly, airflow increases faster than fuel flow, causing a momentary lean condition; the acceleration pump mechanically injects an additional shot of fuel to compensate and prevent stumbling or hesitation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: During the operation of an aircraft engine, the pressure drop in the carburetor venturi depends primarily upon the

choices.B: barometric pressure.
choices.C: air velocity.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Per Bernoulli's principle, the pressure drop at the venturi throat is a direct function of air velocity; as velocity increases, pressure decreases proportionally, which is what draws fuel into the airstream. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: At what engine speed does the main metering jet actually function as a metering jet in a float-type carburetor?

choices.B: Cruising RPM only.
choices.C: All RPM's above idle range.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Below idle range, the main metering jet is bypassed by the idle circuit, so the main jet only begins to function as the primary metering device once engine speed rises above the idle range. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What is the purpose of the carburetor accelerating system?

choices.A: Supply and regulate the fuel required for engine speeds above idle.
choices.B: Temporarily enrich the mixture when the throttle is suddenly opened.
choices.C: Supply and regulate additional fuel required for engine speeds above cruising.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The accelerating system temporarily enriches the mixture during sudden throttle opening to prevent a lean surge or stumble, compensating for the lag between increased airflow and increased fuel delivery. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: When troubleshooting an engine that will not idle, what would be a probable cause?

choices.B: Mixture setting too rich.
choices.C: Manifold valve is not operating properly.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The manifold valve (idle valve) controls fuel flow to the idle circuit, and if it is not operating properly it can prevent adequate fuel delivery at idle speeds, causing the engine to not idle correctly. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What is the relationship between the accelerating pump and the enrichment valve in a pressure injection carburetor?

choices.A: No relationship since they operate independently.
choices.B: Unmetered fuel pressure affects both units.
choices.C: The accelerating pump actuates the enrichment valve.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: In a pressure injection carburetor, unmetered fuel pressure is the common operating pressure that acts on both the accelerating pump and the enrichment valve, linking their operation through the same fuel pressure reference. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: On a carburetor without an automatic mixture control as the aircraft ascends to altitude, the mixture will

choices.B: be leaned.
choices.C: not be affected.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: As altitude increases, air density decreases while the carburetor continues to meter fuel based on volume rather than mass, resulting in a progressively richer mixture because the air-to-fuel ratio decreases. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: During engine operation, if carburetor heat is applied, it will

choices.B: increase engine RPM.
choices.C: decrease the air density to the carburetor.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Carburetor heat introduces warm air into the induction system, which reduces air density, thereby decreasing the mass of air entering the carburetor and typically enriching the mixture while reducing engine performance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The desired engine idle speed and mixture setting

choices.A: is adjusted with engine warmed up and operating.
choices.B: should give minimum RPM with maximum manifold pressure.
choices.C: is usually adjusted in the following sequence; speed first, then mixture.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Idle speed and mixture must be set with the engine fully warmed up and running to account for normal operating temperatures and stable engine parameters, ensuring accurate and repeatable adjustments. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What is a function of the idling air bleed in a float-type carburetor?

choices.A: It provides a means for adjusting the mixture at idle speeds.
choices.B: It vaporizes the fuel at idling speeds.
choices.C: It aids in emulsifying/vaporizing the fuel at idle speeds.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The idling air bleed introduces air into the fuel stream in the idle circuit, which helps emulsify and vaporize the fuel, improving atomization and combustion quality at low engine speeds. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: If the volume of air passing through a carburetor venturi is reduced, the pressure at the venturi throat will

choices.B: be equal to the pressure at the venturi outlet.
choices.C: increase.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Per Bernoulli's principle, lower air velocity through the venturi results in less pressure drop, meaning the throat pressure rises (increases) toward ambient pressure when airflow is reduced. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What will occur if the vapor vent float in a pressure carburetor loses its buoyancy?

choices.A: The amount of fuel returning to the fuel tank from the carburetor will be increased.
choices.B: The engine will continue to run after the mixture control is placed in IDLE CUTOFF.
choices.C: A rich mixture will occur at all engine speeds.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: If the vapor vent float loses buoyancy it sinks, holding the vapor vent valve open, which causes excess fuel to be continuously returned to the tank rather than being retained for engine use. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What method is ordinarily used to make idle speed adjustments on a float-type carburetor?

choices.A: An adjustable throttle stop or linkage.
choices.B: An orifice and adjustable tapered needle.
choices.C: An adjustable needle in the drilled passageway which connects the airspace of the float chamber and the carburetor venturi.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Idle speed on a float-type carburetor is controlled by limiting how far the throttle valve can close, which is accomplished via an adjustable throttle stop or throttle linkage adjustment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: The mixture used at rated power in air cooled reciprocating engines is leaner than the mixture used through the normal cruising range.
choices.B: The mixture used at idle in air cooled reciprocating engines is leaner than the mixture used at rated power.
choices.C: The mixture used at idle in air cooled reciprocating engines is richer than the mixture used at rated power.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: At idle, combustion temperatures are low and cooling is poor, so a rich mixture is required to ensure reliable combustion and cooling; at rated power a rich mixture is also used but the idle mixture is comparatively richer than the cruise range mixture. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: During idle mixture adjustments, which of the following is normally observed to determine when the correct mixture has been achieved?

choices.B: Fuel flowmeter.
choices.C: Changes in RPM or manifold pressure.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: During idle mixture adjustment, the technician monitors RPM or manifold pressure changes as the mixture control is slowly moved toward idle cutoff; a slight rise in RPM (or drop in manifold pressure) before the engine dies indicates the correct idle mixture has been achieved. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: An indication that the optimum idle mixture has been obtained occurs when the mixture control is moved to IDLE CUTOFF and manifold pressure

choices.A: decreases momentarily and RPM drops slightly before the engine ceases to fire.
choices.B: increases momentarily and RPM drops slightly before the engine ceases to fire.
choices.C: decreases and RPM increases momentarily before the engine ceases to fire.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: When the mixture control is moved to idle cutoff on a properly adjusted carburetor, the momentary leaning of an overly rich idle mixture causes a brief increase in manifold pressure and a slight RPM rise before the engine ceases to fire, confirming the idle mixture was slightly rich as desired. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The use of less than normal throttle opening during starting will cause

choices.B: a lean mixture.
choices.C: backfire due to lean fuel/air ratio.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Using less than normal throttle opening during starting reduces airflow through the carburetor venturi, which also reduces the fuel discharge from the main nozzle, resulting in a lean mixture rather than the enriched mixture needed for starting. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: When checking the idle mixture on a carburetor, the engine should be idling normally, then pull the mixture control toward the IDLE CUTOFF position. A correct idling mixture will be indicated by

choices.B: a decrease of 20 to 30 RPM before quitting.
choices.C: an increase of 10 to 50 RPM before decreasing.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A correctly adjusted idle mixture will cause a small but noticeable rise of 10 to 50 RPM as the mixture control is moved toward idle cutoff, because the slightly rich idle mixture is momentarily leaned to best power before the fuel is completely cut off. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: When a new carburetor is installed on an engine,

choices.A: warm up the engine and adjust the float level.
choices.B: do not adjust the idle mixture setting; this was accomplished on the flow bench.
choices.C: and the engine is warmed up to normal temperatures, adjust the idle mixture, then the idle speed.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: After a new carburetor installation, the engine must be warmed to normal operating temperatures before idle mixture and then idle speed are adjusted in that order, ensuring accurate adjustments under representative operating conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The purpose of the back-suction mixture control in a float-type carburetor is to adjust the mixture by

choices.A: regulating the pressure drop at the venturi.
choices.B: regulating the pressure on the fuel in the float chamber.
choices.C: regulating the suction on the mixture from behind the throttle valve.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The back-suction mixture control varies the pressure above the fuel in the float chamber by connecting the chamber to a point of low pressure downstream of the throttle valve, effectively leaning the mixture by reducing the pressure differential that drives fuel through the discharge nozzle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: If the idling jet becomes clogged in a float-type carburetor, the

choices.A: engine operation will not be affected at any RPM.
choices.B: engine will not idle.
choices.C: idle mixture becomes richer.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The idling jet is the primary fuel metering component at low RPM; if it becomes clogged, insufficient fuel is delivered at idle speeds and the engine will not sustain idle operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: An aircraft engine equipped with a pressure-type carburetor is started with the

choices.A: primer while the mixture control is positioned at IDLE CUTOFF.
choices.B: mixture control in the FULL-RICH position.
choices.C: primer while the mixture control is positioned at the FULL-LEAN position.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Pressure-type carburetors require the mixture control to be in the full-rich position for starting because the metering system depends on mixture control positioning to allow fuel flow, unlike float carburetors that use a primer with the mixture at idle cutoff. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: One of the best ways to increase engine power and control detonation and preignition is to

choices.B: use water injection.
choices.C: lean the fuel/air mixture.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Enriching the fuel/air mixture increases evaporative cooling within the cylinder and reduces peak combustion temperatures, which directly increases power output and suppresses detonation and preignition tendencies. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: A mixture ratio of 11:1 normally refers to

choices.B: 1 part air to 11 parts fuel.
choices.C: 1 part fuel to 11 parts air.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Mixture ratios are expressed as parts of air to parts of fuel by weight; an 11:1 ratio means 11 parts air to 1 part fuel, which is a rich mixture used for high-power operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The economizer system in a float-type carburetor

choices.B: functions only at cruise and idle speeds.
choices.C: increases the fuel/air ratio at high power settings.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The economizer (power enrichment) system enriches the fuel/air mixture at high power settings to provide additional cooling and prevent detonation, supplying extra fuel above the amount delivered by the main metering system when the throttle is near or at full open. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: A carburetor is prevented from leaning out during quick acceleration by the

choices.B: mixture control system.
choices.C: accelerating system.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The accelerating system provides a momentary extra charge of fuel when the throttle is rapidly opened, preventing the lean surge that would otherwise occur because airflow increases faster than fuel flow through the main metering system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Detonation occurs when the air-fuel mixture

choices.B: ignites before the time of normal ignition.
choices.C: is too rich.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Detonation is an abnormally rapid, explosive burning of the fuel/air charge in the cylinder rather than a controlled flame front propagation; this uncontrolled rapid combustion produces damaging pressure spikes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What corrective action should be taken when a carburetor is found to be leaking fuel from the discharge nozzle?

choices.A: Replace the needle valve and seat.
choices.B: Adjust the mixture control.
choices.C: Adjust throttle linkage.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Fuel leaking from the carburetor discharge nozzle with the engine off indicates the float-operated needle valve is not seating properly and allowing fuel to flow past it, so the needle valve and seat must be inspected and replaced. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: A major difference between the Teledyne-Continental and RSA (Precision Airmotive or Bendix) continuous flow fuel injection systems in fuel metering is that the

choices.A: RSA system uses air pressure only as a metering force.
choices.B: Continental system utilizes airflow as a metering force.
choices.C: Continental system uses fuel pressure only as a metering force.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The Teledyne-Continental fuel injection system meters fuel solely based on fuel pressure differential across a metering valve without using airflow as a direct metering force, whereas the RSA (Bendix/Precision Airmotive) system uses the pressure differential created by airflow through a venturi as the primary metering force. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The function of the altitude compensating, or aneroid valve used with the Teledyne-Continental fuel injection system on many turbocharged engines is to

choices.A: prevent an overly rich mixture during sudden acceleration.
choices.B: prevent detonation at high altitudes.
choices.C: provide a means of enriching the mixture during sudden acceleration.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The aneroid (altitude-compensating) valve senses upper-deck pressure on turbocharged engines and reduces fuel flow to prevent the mixture from becoming excessively rich as manifold pressure rises during sudden acceleration or altitude changes. Without it, the fixed enrichment circuit would over-fuel the engine under boosted conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The primary purpose of the air bleed openings used with continuous flow fuel injector nozzles is to

choices.B: maintain constant fuel pressure.
choices.C: aid in proper fuel vaporization.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Air bleed openings in continuous-flow fuel injector nozzles introduce air into the fuel stream just before it exits the nozzle, atomizing and vaporizing the fuel for more complete and even combustion. This aeration breaks the fuel into finer droplets rather than controlling mixture ratio or fuel pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Where is the main fuel strainer located in the aircraft fuel system?

choices.A: At the highest point in the fuel system.
choices.B: At the lowest point in the fuel system.
choices.C: At the inlet chamber of the carburetor.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The main fuel strainer is located at the lowest point in the aircraft fuel system so that water and sediment, which sink to the bottom by gravity, are captured and can be drained during preflight checks. Positioning it at the lowest point ensures contaminants are intercepted before reaching the fuel metering device. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: A fuel strainer or filter must be located between the

choices.B: tank outlet and the fuel metering device.
choices.C: boost pump and engine-driven fuel pump.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: FAA standards require a fuel strainer or filter to be installed between the tank outlet and the fuel metering device (carburetor or fuel-injection system) to protect the metering components from contaminants that could cause malfunction or damage. This is the critical protection point for the precision fuel metering equipment. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: If an engine equipped with a float-type carburetor backfires or misses when the throttle is advanced, a likely cause is that the

choices.B: main air bleed is clogged.
choices.C: accelerating pump is not operating properly.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: When the throttle is rapidly advanced, the accelerating pump is designed to inject an extra charge of fuel to temporarily enrich the mixture and prevent a lean stumble, backfire, or miss; a malfunctioning pump fails to supply this enrichment, causing the engine to hesitate or backfire. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Excessively rich or lean idle mixtures result in

choices.B: incomplete combustion.
choices.C: incomplete cylinder scavenging.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Both excessively rich and excessively lean idle mixtures prevent the air-fuel charge from burning completely, resulting in incomplete combustion, rough idling, and possible fouling of spark plugs. The correct idle mixture is necessary for complete, stable combustion at low power settings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Which statement is true regarding proper throttle rigging of an airplane?

choices.A: The throttle stop on the carburetor must be contacted before the stop in the cockpit.
choices.B: The stop in the cockpit must be contacted before the stop on the carburetor.
choices.C: The throttle control is properly adjusted when neither stop makes contact.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Proper throttle rigging requires that the carburetor throttle stop (on the engine) be contacted before the cockpit stop so that full throttle is guaranteed at the carburetor; if the cockpit stop were reached first, the carburetor would never reach its full-open position, limiting power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What precaution should be taken when putting thread lubricant on a tapered pipe plug in a carburetor float bowl?

choices.A: Put the thread lubricant only on the first thread.
choices.B: Do not use thread lubricant on any carburetor fitting.
choices.C: Engage the first thread of the plug, then put a small amount of lubricant on the second thread and screw the plug in.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Thread lubricant (such as thread-locking compound or sealant) must be kept off the first exposed thread so it cannot be displaced into the fuel system and contaminate carburetor passages; starting lubricant on the second thread after engaging the first provides sealing without contamination risk. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Carburetor icing is most severe at

choices.B: high altitudes.
choices.C: low engine temperatures.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Carburetor icing is most severe when ambient air temperatures are between 30 °F and 40 °F combined with high relative humidity, because evaporative cooling of fuel and the pressure drop at the venturi can drop temperatures below freezing even when outside air is above freezing. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Carburetor icing may be eliminated by which of the following methods?

choices.A: Alcohol spray and electrically heated induction duct.
choices.B: Ethylene glycol spray and heated induction air.
choices.C: Alcohol spray and heated induction air.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Carburetor icing is eliminated by introducing heated induction air (raises air temperature above freezing) and by alcohol spray (which lowers the freezing point of water and melts existing ice); ethylene glycol is not used in aircraft carburetor anti-icing systems, making option C the correct combination. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Where would a carburetor air heater be located in a fuel injection system?

choices.B: None is required.
choices.C: Between the air intake and the venturi.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Fuel-injected engines do not have a carburetor venturi where ice can form, but induction system ice can still block the air intake, so an alternate air source or air heater is located at the air intake entrance to provide warm air if the primary intake becomes blocked. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: An increase in manifold pressure when carburetor heat is applied indicates

choices.A: ice was forming in the carburetor.
choices.B: mixture was too lean.
choices.C: overheating of cylinder heads.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: When carburetor heat is applied and manifold pressure increases, it indicates that ice was restricting airflow through the carburetor; as the heat melts the ice, the restriction is removed, restoring airflow and raising manifold pressure back toward normal. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: During full power output of an unsupercharged engine equipped with a float-type carburetor, in which of the following areas will the highest pressure exist?

choices.B: Intake manifold.
choices.C: Carburetor air scoop.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: During full power operation, the highest pressure in the induction system of a normally aspirated engine is at the carburetor air scoop (ram air inlet), because pressure progressively decreases through the venturi, carburetor, and into the intake manifold due to flow restrictions and the pumping action of the pistons. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The use of the carburetor air heater when it is not needed causes

choices.B: excessive increase in manifold pressure.
choices.C: a decrease in power and possibly detonation.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Applying carburetor heat unnecessarily introduces warm, less-dense air, which reduces volumetric efficiency and engine power output; the hotter, lower-density air also enriches the mixture and can raise cylinder temperatures to the point where detonation may occur. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Which of the following would be a factor in the failure of an engine to develop full power at takeoff?

choices.A: Improper adjustment of carburetor heat valve control linkage.
choices.B: Excessively rich setting on the idle mixture adjustment.
choices.C: Failure of the economizer valve to remain closed at takeoff throttle setting.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: If the carburetor heat valve control linkage is improperly adjusted so that the heat valve is not fully closed at takeoff, warm (less dense) air enters the induction system, reducing power output and richening the mixture; the engine cannot develop full rated power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 2 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: What is the purpose of the density controller in a turbocharger system?

choices.A: Limits the maximum manifold pressure that can be produced at other than full throttle conditions.
choices.B: Limits the maximum manifold pressure that can be produced by the turbocharger at full throttle.
choices.C: Maintains constant air velocity at the carburetor inlet.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The density controller limits the maximum manifold pressure the turbocharger can produce at full throttle by regulating the waste gate to prevent overboosting at altitude under wide-open throttle conditions. The rate-of-change controller (not the density controller) manages partial-throttle operations. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: If carburetor or induction system icing is not present when carburetor heat is applied with no change in the throttle setting, the

choices.B: manifold pressure will increase.
choices.C: engine RPM will increase.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Applying carburetor heat introduces heated air, which is less dense, reducing the mass of air entering the carburetor and thereby enriching the fuel-air mixture without a corresponding increase in fuel flow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: When starting an engine equipped with a carburetor air heater, in what position should the heater be placed?

choices.B: COLD.
choices.C: NEUTRAL.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: During engine start, the carburetor air heater should be in the COLD (off) position to allow maximum air density for starting and to avoid ingesting hot air that could cause backfire or reduce power during start. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The application of carburetor heat will have which of the following effects?

choices.A: The manifold pressure will be increased.
choices.B: The mixture will become leaner.
choices.C: The mixture will become richer.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Carburetor heat reduces the density of incoming air, so the same fuel metering delivers a richer mixture because more fuel is present relative to the reduced mass of air. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: When operating an engine, the application of carburetor heat will have what effect on the air-fuel mixture?

choices.A: Enriching the mixture because the AMC cannot make a correction for increased temperature.
choices.B: Enriching the mixture until the AMC can make a compensation.
choices.C: Leaning the mixture until the AMC can make a compensation.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: The automatic mixture control (AMC) is calibrated to compensate for changes in air density with altitude but cannot correct for temperature increases caused by carburetor heat, so the mixture remains enriched regardless of AMC function. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: On small aircraft engines, fuel vaporization may be increased by

choices.A: cooling the air before it enters the engine.
choices.B: circulating the fuel and air mixture through passages in the oil sump.
choices.C: heating the fuel before it enters the carburetor.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Routing the fuel-air mixture through passages in the oil sump allows heat from the oil to vaporize fuel more completely, improving combustion efficiency on small engines. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: The action of a carburetor airscoop is to supply air to the carburetor, but it may also

choices.B: keep fuel lines cool and prevent vapor lock.
choices.C: increase the pressure of the incoming air by ram effect.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: A carburetor airscoop is primarily designed to supply air to the carburetor, but its forward-facing design also uses ram effect from the aircraft's forward motion to slightly increase the pressure of incoming air, improving volumetric efficiency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: A carburetor air pre-heater is not generally used on takeoff unless absolutely necessary because of the

choices.A: loss of power and possible detonation.
choices.B: possibility of induction system overboost.
choices.C: inability of the engine to supply enough heat to make a significant difference.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Using carburetor preheat on takeoff reduces air density entering the engine, resulting in a loss of power and a richer mixture that can lead to detonation under high-power conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: An engine becomes overheated due to excessive taxiing or improper ground runup. Prior to shutdown, operation must continue until cylinders have cooled, by running engine at

choices.A: low RPM with oil dilution system activated.
choices.B: idle RPM.
choices.C: high RPM with mixture control in RICH position.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: Running the engine at idle RPM after excessive heating allows the engine to cool gradually and uniformly without thermal shock, while minimizing the heat load from combustion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: When in operation, the speed of an APU

choices.A: is controlled by a cockpit power lever.
choices.B: remains at idle and automatically accelerates to rated speed when placed under load.
choices.C: remains at or near rated speed regardless of the load condition.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: APUs are designed to operate as constant-speed units; their fuel control system automatically adjusts fuel flow to maintain rated speed regardless of varying electrical or pneumatic load demands. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.question: Fuel scheduling during APU start and under varying pneumatic bleed and electrical loads is maintained

choices.A: manually through power control lever position.
choices.B: automatically by the APU fuel control system.
choices.C: automatically by an aircraft main engine fuel control unit.

Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems.explanation: APU fuel scheduling is handled automatically by the APU's dedicated fuel control system, which manages fuel flow during start and adjusts for varying bleed air and electrical loads without pilot intervention. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 7 — Engine Fuel and Fuel Metering Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: When will small induction system air leaks have the most noticeable effect on engine operation?

choices.B: At maximum continuous and takeoff power settings.
choices.C: At low RPM.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: At low RPM (idle), the throttle plate is nearly closed and manifold pressure is very low, so even a small induction air leak represents a proportionally large increase in air mass flow, significantly leaning the mixture and causing rough operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 8 — Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: A manifold pressure gauge is designed to

choices.A: maintain constant pressure in the intake manifold.
choices.B: indicate differential pressure between the intake manifold and atmospheric pressure.
choices.C: indicate absolute pressure in the intake manifold.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: A manifold pressure gauge is an absolute pressure instrument calibrated in inches of mercury (in. Hg), measuring the actual absolute pressure within the intake manifold rather than a differential relative to atmospheric pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 8 — Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: A complete break in the line between the manifold pressure gauge and the induction system will be indicated by the gauge registering

choices.B: zero.
choices.C: lower than normal for conditions prevailing.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: If the line between the manifold pressure gauge and the induction system is completely broken, the gauge is exposed to the ambient atmosphere and will read prevailing atmospheric pressure (approximately 29.92 in. Hg at sea level). (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 8 — Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: The most satisfactory extinguishing agent for a tailpipe or intake fire is

choices.B: dry chemical.
choices.C: methyl bromide.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is the most satisfactory extinguishing agent for tailpipe or intake fires because it displaces oxygen without leaving residue or causing damage to engine components, and it is safe to use around aircraft structures. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 8 — Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: How are the teeth of the gears in the accessory section of an engine normally lubricated?

choices.B: By submerging the load-bearing portions in oil.
choices.C: By surrounding the load-bearing portions with baffles or housings within which oil pressure can be maintained.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: Accessory section gears operate at relatively low loads and speeds, making splash or spray lubrication sufficient; oil is flung or directed onto the gear teeth as it circulates through the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Lubrication Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: Which of the following statements regarding volumetric efficiency of an engine is true?

choices.A: The volumetric efficiency of an engine will remain the same regardless of the amount of throttle opening.
choices.B: It is impossible to exceed 100 percent volumetric efficiency of any engine regardless of the type of supercharger used.
choices.C: It is possible to exceed 100 percent volumetric efficiency of some engines by the use of superchargers of the proper type.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: A supercharger can force a denser charge into the cylinder than the cylinder could naturally induct, meaning the mass of air/fuel exceeds what the displacement alone would allow, enabling volumetric efficiency to exceed 100 percent. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Induction Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: What is the purpose of the rate-of-change controller in a turbocharger system?

choices.A: Limits the maximum manifold pressure that can be produced by the turbocharger at full throttle conditions.
choices.B: Controls the rate at which the turbocharger discharge pressure will increase.
choices.C: Controls the position of the waste gate after the aircraft has reached its critical altitude.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: The rate-of-change controller prevents overboosting by limiting how rapidly turbocharger discharge pressure can build up during throttle advancement, protecting the engine from sudden pressure spikes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Turbocharger Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: What directly regulates the speed of a turbocharger?

choices.B: Waste gate.
choices.C: Throttle.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: The waste gate bypasses exhaust gases around the turbine wheel; by modulating the waste gate position, the amount of exhaust energy driving the turbine is controlled, thereby directly regulating turbocharger speed and output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Turbocharger Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: What is the purpose of a turbocharger system for a small reciprocating aircraft engine?

choices.A: Compresses the air to hold the cabin pressure constant after the aircraft has reached its critical altitude.
choices.B: Maintains constant air velocity in the intake manifold.
choices.C: Compresses air to maintain manifold pressure constant from sea level to the critical altitude of the engine.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: A turbocharger compresses induction air to compensate for decreasing air density at altitude, maintaining sea-level-equivalent manifold pressure up to the engine's critical altitude. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Turbocharger Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: Which of the following is a function of the differential pressure controller?

choices.A: It limits the maximum manifold pressure that can be produced by the turbocharger at full throttle conditions.
choices.B: It controls all positions of the waste gate except at fully open position.
choices.C: It controls the position of the waste gate after the aircraft has reached its critical altitude.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: The differential pressure controller senses the pressure difference between upper deck pressure and manifold pressure and modulates the waste gate to maintain the desired differential, controlling all waste gate positions except fully open. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Turbocharger Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: The purpose of a sonic venturi on a turbocharged engine is to

choices.A: limit the amount of air that can flow from the turbocharger into the cabin for pressurization.
choices.B: increase the amount of air that can flow from the turbocharger into the cabin for pressurization.
choices.C: increase the velocity of the fuel/air charge.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: A sonic venturi (flow limiter) chokes airflow at the speed of sound, thereby limiting the maximum volume of compressed air that can be bled from the turbocharger into the cabin pressurization system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Turbocharger Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: In an airplane equipped with an alternate air system, if the main air duct air filter becomes blocked or clogged, the

choices.A: system will automatically allow warm, unfiltered air to be drawn into the engine.
choices.B: flow of air into the engine will be slowed or cut off unless alternate air is selected.
choices.C: system will automatically allow warm, filtered alternate air to be drawn into the engine.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: Most alternate air doors are spring-loaded and will open automatically when a blockage creates sufficient suction, allowing warm unfiltered air from inside the cowling to bypass the clogged filter and maintain engine airflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Induction Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: If a fire starts in the induction system during the engine starting procedure, what should the operator do?

choices.A: Turn off the fuel pump switches.
choices.B: Continue cranking the engine.
choices.C: Turn off the magneto switches.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: Continuing to crank the engine draws the fire back into the induction system and into the engine, effectively extinguishing the induction fire by ingesting it; stopping cranking leaves the fire burning in the intake. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Induction Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: The position of the cowl flaps during normal cruise flight conditions is

choices.B: open.
choices.C: neutral.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: During normal cruise flight, engine temperatures are within acceptable limits and aerodynamic drag should be minimized, so cowl flaps are closed to reduce drag while still providing adequate cooling airflow. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: Which of the following assists in removing heat from the metal walls and fins of an air-cooled cylinder assembly?

choices.B: A baffle and cowl arrangement.
choices.C: An engine induction system.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: Baffles and cowling direct high-velocity ram air over the cylinder fins, forcing a pressure differential that drives cooling air across the hot metal surfaces and carries heat away from the cylinders. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: During ground operation of an engine, the cowl flaps should be in what position?

choices.B: Fully open.
choices.C: Opened according to ambient conditions.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: During ground operation, airflow over the cylinders is greatly reduced compared to flight, so cowl flaps must be fully open to maximize cooling airflow and prevent overheating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: During an operational check of an electrically powered aircraft engine cowl flap system, the motor fails to operate. Which of the following is the first to be checked?

choices.B: Flap actuator control switch.
choices.C: Flap actuator motor.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: When troubleshooting any electrically powered system, the circuit breaker or fuse should be checked first because it is the most common and easiest-to-verify cause of a motor failing to operate. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Engine Cooling Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: The purpose of an intercooler when used with a turbocharger is to cool the

choices.A: exhaust gases before they come in contact with the turbo drive.
choices.B: turbocharger bearings.
choices.C: air entering the carburetor from the turbocharger.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: Compressing air in a turbocharger raises its temperature, which reduces its density; an intercooler (aftercooler) removes that heat from the compressed air before it enters the carburetor, restoring density and preventing detonation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Turbocharger Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: Which of the following results in a decrease in volumetric efficiency?

choices.B: Part-throttle operation.
choices.C: Short intake pipes of large diameter.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: Partially closing the throttle restricts the mass of air/fuel charge that can enter the cylinder, directly reducing volumetric efficiency below what the engine could achieve at wide-open throttle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 1 — Reciprocating Engine Induction Systems)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: What is the position of the cowl flaps during engine starting and warmup operations under normal conditions?

choices.B: Full closed at all times.
choices.C: Open for starting, closed for warmup.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: Cowl flaps must be fully open during engine starting and warmup to allow maximum airflow for cooling, since the engine generates significant heat while the aircraft is stationary with little ram air available. Restricting airflow during this phase risks overheating the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooling)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: One source commonly used for carburetor air heat is

choices.B: alternate air heat.
choices.C: exhaust gases.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: Carburetor heat systems use a shroud or muff around the exhaust manifold or collector to capture heat from exhaust gases, which is then routed to warm the induction air and prevent or remove carburetor ice. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooling)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: If an engine air filter element is not routinely cleaned and becomes excessively dirty, it can cause

choices.B: a loss of engine power.
choices.C: both A and B are true.

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: A severely clogged air filter restricts induction airflow, causing an excessively rich mixture that can both prevent starting and significantly reduce engine power output during operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooling)

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.question: When a fireguard is standing by during an engine start, they should be watching for potential fires that could start

choices.A: close to the battery compartment or starter.
choices.B: in the induction system or the exhaust system.
choices.C: at the ground power receptacle (if connected to a ground power unit).

Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooli.explanation: During engine start, the areas most susceptible to fire are the induction system (due to backfires) and the exhaust system (due to unburned fuel igniting), so the fireguard focuses attention on these zones. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 4 — Reciprocating Engine Induction and Cooling)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: When starting a turbine engine,

choices.A: a hot start is indicated if the exhaust gas temperature exceeds specified limits.
choices.B: an excessively lean mixture is likely to cause a hot start.
choices.C: release the starter switch as soon as indication of light-off occurs.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: A hot start occurs when the exhaust gas temperature exceeds the maximum allowable limit during the start sequence, typically caused by an excessively rich mixture or delayed ignition, and requires immediate shutdown per FAA standards. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: When starting a turbine engine, a hung start is indicated if the engine

choices.A: exhaust gas temperature exceeds specified limits.
choices.B: fails to reach idle RPM.
choices.C: N(1) is normal, but N(2) is low.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: A hung start (also called a false start) is characterized by the engine lighting off and EGT rising normally, but the engine failing to accelerate to idle RPM, indicating insufficient energy from the starter or a fuel control issue. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: The function of the exhaust cone assembly of a turbine engine is to

choices.A: collect the exhaust gases and act as a noise suppressor.
choices.B: swirl and collect the exhaust gases into a single exhaust jet.
choices.C: straighten and collect the exhaust gases into a solid exhaust jet.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: The exhaust cone straightens the residual swirl imparted by the turbine blades and collects the exhaust gases into a solid, unified exhaust jet to improve propulsive efficiency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: What is the first engine instrument indication of a successful start of a turbine engine?

choices.B: A rise in oil temperature.
choices.C: A rise in the exhaust gas temperature.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: The first instrument indication of a successful turbine engine light-off is a rise in exhaust gas temperature (EGT), confirming that combustion has begun before RPM has had time to increase appreciably. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: The exhaust section of a turbine engine is designed to

choices.A: impart a high exit velocity to the exhaust gases.
choices.B: increase temperature, therefore increasing velocity.
choices.C: decrease temperature, therefore decreasing pressure.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: The exhaust section is designed to convert the remaining thermal energy of the exhaust gases into a high-velocity exhaust jet, which contributes directly to the thrust produced by the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: Which of the following is used to monitor the mechanical integrity of the turbines, as well as to check engine operating conditions of a turbine engine?

choices.B: Exhaust gas temperature.
choices.C: Engine pressure ratio.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: EGT is a direct indicator of the thermal conditions within the turbine section, making it the primary parameter used to assess turbine mechanical integrity and verify proper engine operating conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: On a reciprocating engine aircraft using a shrouded exhaust muffler system as a source for cabin heat, the exhaust system should be

choices.A: visually inspected for any indication of cracks or an operational carbon monoxide detection test should be done.
choices.B: replaced at each reciprocating engine overhaul by a new or overhauled exhaust system or an hydrostatic test should be accomplished.
choices.C: removed and the exhaust muffler checked for cracks by using magnetic particle inspection method or an hydrostatic test should be done on the exhaust muffler.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: FAA standards require that exhaust systems used as a cabin heat source be visually inspected for cracks and that a carbon monoxide detection test be performed, since even small cracks can allow deadly CO to enter the cabin. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: The purpose of an exhaust gas analyzer is to indicate the

choices.B: fuel/air ratio being burned in the cylinders.
choices.C: temperature of the exhaust gases in the exhaust manifold.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: An exhaust gas analyzer measures the oxygen content of exhaust gases to indicate whether the fuel/air mixture being burned is rich or lean, allowing the pilot or technician to optimize mixture settings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: What is the primary purpose of the fuel/oil heat exchanger?

choices.B: Cool the oil.
choices.C: De-aerate the oil.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: The fuel/oil heat exchanger uses the relatively cool incoming fuel to absorb heat from the hot engine oil, thereby cooling the oil and simultaneously warming the fuel to prevent ice crystal formation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: An aircraft should be facing into the wind when trimming an engine. However, if the velocity of the wind blowing into the intake is excessive, it is likely to cause a

choices.A: false low exhaust gas temperature reading.
choices.B: trim setting resulting in engine overspeed.
choices.C: false high compression and turbine discharge pressure, and a subsequent low trim.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Excessive headwind ram pressure into the engine intake artificially raises compressor discharge and turbine discharge pressure readings, causing the fuel control to trim the engine leaner than required, resulting in a false high pressure indication and a subsequent low trim setting. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: If the turbocharger waste gate is completely closed,

choices.A: none of the exhaust gases are directed through the turbine.
choices.B: the turbocharger is in the OFF position.
choices.C: all the exhaust gases are directed through the turbine.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: When the waste gate is fully closed, all exhaust gases are forced through the turbocharger turbine wheel, delivering maximum energy to drive the compressor and providing maximum boost to the induction system. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 5 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: What is used to drive a supercharger?

choices.B: Gear train from the crankshaft.
choices.C: Belt drive through a pulley arrangement.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: A supercharger is mechanically driven directly by the engine via a gear train connected to the crankshaft, distinguishing it from a turbocharger which is driven by exhaust gases. This mechanical connection means the supercharger output is directly tied to engine speed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: What is the purpose of an augmenter used in some reciprocating engine exhaust systems?

choices.B: To aid in cooling the engine.
choices.C: To assist in displacing the exhaust gases.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Augmenter tubes use the velocity of exhaust gases to draw additional cooling air through the engine compartment, thereby aiding in engine cooling by creating a low-pressure area that pulls air across the engine. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: Which of the following statements is true?

choices.A: Some aircraft exhaust systems include an augmenter system to remove air over the engine for cooling.
choices.B: Augmenter systems are used to create a low-pressure area at the lower rear of the aircraft engine cowling.
choices.C: Augmenter systems are used to create a high-pressure area at the lower rear of the aircraft engine cowling.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Augmenter systems channel exhaust gases through tubes that induce airflow over and around the engine for cooling purposes, making the statement that they remove air over the engine for cooling the accurate description of their function. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: Why is high nickel chromium steel used in many exhaust systems?

choices.B: Corrosion resistance and low expansion coefficient.
choices.C: Corrosion resistance and high heat conductivity.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: High nickel-chromium steel is selected for exhaust systems because it resists oxidation and corrosion at elevated temperatures and also conducts heat efficiently, both critical properties for exhaust system longevity and performance. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: Repair of exhaust system components

choices.A: is impossible because the material cannot be identified.
choices.B: must be accomplished by the component manufacturer.
choices.C: is not recommended to be accomplished in the field.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Due to the safety-critical nature of exhaust components and the difficulty of properly identifying materials and ensuring weld integrity in the field, FAA guidance does not recommend field repair of exhaust system components. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: On an aircraft that utilizes an exhaust heat exchanger as a source of cabin heat, how should the exhaust system be inspected?

choices.B: Hydrostatically tested.
choices.C: With the heater air shroud removed.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Removing the heater air shroud allows the technician to directly inspect the exhaust system components for cracks or leaks that could allow carbon monoxide to enter the cabin heating air supply, which is the most thorough and recommended inspection method. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: How should ceramic-coated exhaust components be cleaned?

choices.B: By degreasing.
choices.C: By mechanical means.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Ceramic-coated exhaust components must be cleaned by degreasing rather than mechanical or abrasive methods, because mechanical cleaning would damage or remove the protective ceramic coating. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: Select a characteristic of a good weld on exhaust stacks.

choices.A: The weld should be built up 1/8 inch.
choices.B: Porousness or projecting globules should show in the weld.
choices.C: The weld should taper off smoothly into the base metal.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: A quality weld on exhaust stacks should blend smoothly into the base metal without abrupt transitions, porosity, or protruding globules, which could act as stress concentration points and lead to cracking under thermal cycling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: How do the turbines which are driven by the exhaust gases of a turbo-compound engine contribute to total engine power output?

choices.A: By driving the crankshaft through suitable couplings.
choices.B: By driving the supercharger, thus relieving the engine of the supercharging load.
choices.C: By converting the latent heat energy of the exhaust gases into thrust by collecting and accelerating them.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: In a turbo-compound engine, power recovery turbines extract energy from exhaust gases and return it to the crankshaft through fluid couplings or gearing, directly increasing the engine's total shaft power output. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: How should corrosion-resistant steel parts such as exhaust collectors be blast cleaned?

choices.A: Use steel grit which has not previously been used on soft iron.
choices.B: Use super fine granite grit.
choices.C: Use sand which has not previously been used on iron or steel.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Sand that has not previously been used on iron or steel must be used when blast cleaning corrosion-resistant steel exhaust parts to prevent contamination with ferrous particles that would embed in the surface and cause corrosion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: Dislodged internal muffler baffles on a small reciprocating engine may cause

choices.B: an engine over-speed problem.
choices.C: high oil consumption.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: If internal muffler baffles break loose and partially or fully block the exhaust outlet, they create excessive back pressure in the exhaust system, which restricts exhaust gas flow and reduces engine power. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: What is the purpose of an exhaust outlet guard on a small reciprocating engine?

choices.A: To prevent dislodged muffler baffles from obstructing the muffler outlet.
choices.B: To reduce spark exit.
choices.C: To shield adjacent components from excessive heat.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: An exhaust outlet guard is installed to catch any dislodged internal muffler baffles before they can block the muffler outlet, which would otherwise cause dangerous exhaust back pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: What could be a result of undetected exhaust system leaks in a reciprocating engine powered airplane?

choices.A: Pilot/passenger incapacitation caused by carbon monoxide entering the cabin.
choices.B: A rough-running engine with increased fuel consumption.
choices.C: Too low exhaust back pressure resulting in the desired power settings not being attained.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Exhaust leaks upstream of the cabin heat exchanger can allow carbon monoxide, a colorless and odorless byproduct of combustion, to enter the cabin heating system and incapacitate the pilot and passengers. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: How may reciprocating engine exhaust system leaks be detected?

choices.A: An exhaust trail aft of the tailpipe on the airplane exterior.
choices.B: Fluctuating manifold pressure indication.
choices.C: Signs of exhaust soot inside cowling and on adjacent components.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Exhaust leaks are most commonly detected visually by finding soot deposits inside the engine cowling and on components adjacent to the exhaust system, indicating hot exhaust gases are escaping from a point other than the tailpipe. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: Compared to normally aspirated engines, turbocharged engine exhaust systems operate

choices.B: higher temperatures and higher pressures.
choices.C: similar temperatures and pressures.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Turbocharged engine exhaust systems operate at both higher temperatures and higher pressures than normally aspirated systems because the turbocharger imposes backpressure on the exhaust and the increased power output generates more heat. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 12 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: Most exhaust system failures result from thermal fatigue cracking in the areas of stress concentration. This condition is usually caused by

choices.A: the drastic temperature change which is encountered at altitude.
choices.B: improper welding techniques during manufacture.
choices.C: the high temperatures at which the exhaust system operates.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: The extreme and sustained high temperatures at which exhaust systems operate cause the metal to repeatedly expand and contract, leading to thermal fatigue cracks, particularly at stress concentration points such as welds, flanges, and bends. This is the primary cause of exhaust system failure, not altitude temperature changes or manufacturing defects alone. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: The purpose of cascade vanes in a thrust reversing system is to

choices.A: form a solid blocking door in the jet exhaust path.
choices.B: turn the exhaust gases forward just after exiting the exhaust nozzle.
choices.C: turn to a forward direction the fan and/or hot exhaust gases that have been blocked from exiting through the exhaust nozzle.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: Cascade vanes are stationary aerodynamic turning vanes that redirect blocked exhaust and/or fan air in a forward and outward direction to produce reverse thrust, rather than forming a blocking door themselves. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.question: If a turbocharger waste gate is not able to close fully,

choices.A: the aircraft will not be able to reach its critical altitude.
choices.B: the engine is more likely to over boost.
choices.C: none of the exhaust gases will flow through the turbine.

Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems.explanation: The waste gate controls how much exhaust gas bypasses the turbocharger turbine; if it cannot fully close, maximum exhaust energy is not directed through the turbine, resulting in insufficient boost to maintain critical altitude pressure. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Engine Exhaust and Reverser Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: Motor-driven impeller and turbine fuel flow transmitters are designed to transmit data

choices.B: mechanically.
choices.C: by fuel pressure.
