Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: Motor-driven impeller and turbine fuel flow transmitters use aircraft electrical system power to drive the impeller motor, and the resulting signal is transmitted electrically to the cockpit indicator. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: The fuel flowmeter indicator rotor and needle for a motor-impeller and turbine indicating system is driven by

choices.B: direct coupling to the motor shaft.
choices.C: a mechanical gear train.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: In a motor-impeller and turbine fuel flow system, the turbine in the fuel stream generates an electrical signal whose characteristics correspond to flow rate, and this signal drives the indicator rotor and needle in the cockpit instrument. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: The fuel flow indication system used with many fuel-injected opposed engine airplanes utilizes a measure of

choices.B: fuel pressure.
choices.C: fuel flow mass.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: Fuel-injected opposed engines use a fuel flow indicator that measures fuel pressure at the injector inlet, since pressure in these systems is proportional to fuel flow rate and provides a practical indirect measurement. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: The fuel flow indication data sent from motor driven impeller and turbine, and motorless type fuel flow transmitters is a measure of

choices.B: fuel volume flow.
choices.C: engine burner pressure drop.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: Both motor-driven impeller/turbine and motorless fuel flow transmitters measure mass flow rather than volume flow, because mass flow more accurately represents the actual energy content of fuel delivered to the engine regardless of temperature or density changes. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: Which of the following types of electric motors are commonly used in electric tachometers?

choices.B: Synchronous motors.
choices.C: Direct current, shunt-wound motors.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: Electric tachometers use synchronous AC motors in the indicator because the motor rotates in precise synchronization with the frequency of the AC signal generated by the tachometer generator, making RPM directly proportional to frequency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: Basically, the indicator of a tachometer system is responsive to change in

choices.B: frequency.
choices.C: voltage.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: The tachometer generator produces an AC output whose frequency is directly proportional to engine RPM, and the synchronous motor indicator responds to that frequency to display the correct RPM reading. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: Which of the following is a primary engine instrument?

choices.B: Fuel flowmeter.
choices.C: Airspeed indicator.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: The tachometer is a primary engine instrument because it directly indicates engine operating speed (RPM), which is essential for monitoring engine performance and safety during all phases of operation. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: Why do helicopters require a minimum of two synchronous tachometer systems?

choices.A: One indicates engine RPM and the other tail rotor RPM.
choices.B: One indicates main rotor RPM and the other tail rotor RPM.
choices.C: One indicates engine RPM and the other main rotor RPM.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: In a helicopter, the engine and main rotor are connected through a transmission and freewheeling unit, so their RPMs can differ; separate tachometer systems are required to independently monitor each to detect conditions such as rotor overspeed or engine underspeed. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: What unit in a tachometer system sends information to the indicator?

choices.B: The two-phase ac generator.
choices.C: The synchronous motor.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: The tachometer system uses a three-phase AC generator driven by the engine to send frequency-based electrical signals to the indicator, where a synchronous motor converts those signals into RPM readings. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: Instruments that provide readings of low or negative pressure, such as manifold pressure gauges, are usually what type?

choices.B: Bourdon tube.
choices.C: Diaphragm or bellows.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: Diaphragm or bellows mechanisms are sensitive to small pressure differentials and can accurately measure low or sub-ambient pressures, making them the appropriate sensing element for manifold pressure gauges and similar low-pressure instruments. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: The RPM indication of a synchronous AC motor-tachometer is governed by the generator

choices.B: current.
choices.C: frequency.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: A synchronous AC motor rotates at a speed determined solely by the frequency of the AC power supplied to it; since the tachometer generator frequency is proportional to engine RPM, the indicator directly reflects engine speed via frequency. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: A red triangle, dot, or diamond mark on an engine instrument face or glass indicates

choices.A: the maximum operating limit for all normal operations.
choices.B: the maximum limit for high transients such as starting.
choices.C: a restricted operating range.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: Per FAA instrument marking standards, a red triangle, dot, or diamond indicates a restricted operating range where continuous operation is prohibited, such as a propeller critical speed range, to avoid structural resonance or other hazardous conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Engine Instrument Systems)

Engine Instrument Systems.question: The Federal Aviation Regulations require the fuel flow rate for gravity systems (main and reserve) to be

choices.A: 125 percent of the takeoff fuel consumption of the engine.
choices.B: 125 percent of the maximum, except takeoff, fuel consumption of the engine.
choices.C: 150 percent of the takeoff fuel consumption of the engine.

Engine Instrument Systems.explanation: FAR 23.955 and FAR 25.955 require that gravity fuel systems (both main and reserve) deliver fuel at a rate of at least 125 percent of the takeoff fuel consumption of the engine, ensuring an adequate safety margin under worst-case flow conditions. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 14 — Fuel Systems)

Engine Fire Protection Systems.question: Which of the following fire detection systems are commonly used in an engine nacelle?

choices.B: Thermocouple detector.
choices.C: Kidde continuous-loop.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.explanation: The Kidde continuous-loop detector system is the most widely used fire detection method in engine nacelles because it provides reliable, continuous sensing along the entire length of the detector element rather than at discrete points. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Fire Protection Systems)

Engine Fire Protection Systems.question: How does carbon dioxide (CO(2)) extinguish an aircraft engine fire?

choices.A: Contact with the air converts the liquid into snow and gas which smothers the flame.
choices.B: By lowering the temperature to a point where combustion will not take place.
choices.C: The high pressure spray lowers the temperature and blows out the fire.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.explanation: When CO₂ is discharged, the liquid converts into snow and gas upon contact with air, and this mixture smothers the fire by displacing the oxygen surrounding the flame rather than primarily acting through cooling. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Fire Protection Systems)

Engine Fire Protection Systems.question: The pulling out (or down) of an illuminated fire handle in a typical large jet aircraft fire protection system commonly accomplishes what events?

choices.A: Closes all firewall shutoff valves, disconnects the generator, and discharges a fire bottle.
choices.B: Closes fuel shutoff, closes hydraulic shutoff, disconnects the generator field, and arms the fire-extinguishing system.
choices.C: Closes fuel shutoff, closes hydraulic shutoff, closes the oxygen shutoff, disconnects the generator field, and arms the fire-extinguishing system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.explanation: Pulling the illuminated fire handle on a large jet isolates the engine by closing the fuel and hydraulic shutoff valves and disconnecting the generator field, and it arms the fire-extinguishing system so the agent can be directed to the affected engine; oxygen shutoff is not a standard function of the fire handle. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Fire Protection Systems)

Engine Fire Protection Systems.question: The fire detection system that uses a single wire surrounded by a continuous string of ceramic beads in a tube is the

choices.B: Kidde system.
choices.C: thermocouple system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.explanation: The Fenwal continuous-loop system uses a single center wire surrounded by a string of ceramic beads (eutectic salt) inside a stainless steel tube; when heat causes the beads to become conductive, a circuit is completed and an alarm is triggered. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Fire Protection Systems)

Engine Fire Protection Systems.question: The fire detection system that uses two wires imbedded in a ceramic core within a tube is the

choices.B: Lindberg system.
choices.C: Kidde system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.explanation: The Kidde continuous-loop system uses two wires embedded in a thermistor-type ceramic core inside a tube; at elevated temperatures the resistance of the core drops, allowing current to flow between the two conductors and trigger an alarm. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Fire Protection Systems)

Engine Fire Protection Systems.question: Two continuous-loop fire detection systems that will not test due to a broken detector element are the

choices.A: Kidde system and the Lindberg system.
choices.B: Kidde system and the Fenwal system.
choices.C: thermocouple system and the Lindberg system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.explanation: Both the Kidde and Lindberg systems rely on a complete, unbroken detector loop to function; a broken element in either system will prevent the test circuit from completing, so neither system will respond to the test signal, though Kidde can still alarm through the intact portion. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Fire Protection Systems)

Engine Fire Protection Systems.question: Which of the following fire detection systems will detect a fire when an element is inoperative but will not test when the test circuit is energized?

choices.A: The Kidde system and the thermocouple system.
choices.B: The Kidde system and the Fenwal system.
choices.C: The thermocouple system and the Lindberg system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.explanation: The Kidde and Fenwal systems both depend on electrical continuity through the detector element to pass the self-test; a broken element prevents the test from succeeding, yet a genuine fire condition can still reduce resistance or complete a circuit through the remaining intact sections, allowing actual fire detection. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Fire Protection Systems)

Engine Fire Protection Systems.question: Which of the following fire detection systems uses heat in the normal testing of the system?

choices.A: The thermocouple system and the Lindberg system.
choices.B: The Kidde system and the Fenwal system.
choices.C: The thermocouple system and the Fenwal system.

Engine Fire Protection Systems.explanation: The thermocouple system is tested by applying heat to the detector to generate the required differential temperature that triggers the alarm, and the Lindberg system similarly requires heat application to test its bimetallic sensing element, unlike electrical resistance-type systems that use an electrical test signal. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 15 — Fire Protection Systems)

Turbine Engine Air Systems.question: Generally, the practice when trimming an engine is to

choices.A: turn all accessory bleed air off.
choices.B: turn all accessory bleed air on.
choices.C: make adjustments (as necessary) for all engines on the same aircraft with accessory bleed air settings the same -- either on or off.

Turbine Engine Air Systems.explanation: During engine trimming (adjusting fuel control to achieve accurate thrust settings), all accessory bleed air must be turned off to eliminate variables that would affect compressor discharge pressure and produce inaccurate trim results. (Ref: FAA-H-8083-32B, Ch 10 — Turbine Engine Fuel Systems)
